title: Can't someone help me??????
body: I have had an extremely frustrating interaction with your chat team, which is not the degree of customer service that I have come to expect from Amazon. Please see a transcript of this chat, and please address this issue. I have wasted over an hour of my time, and have become very frustrated over this. Please contact me about replacing my fire, as the app is working perfectly on numerous other devices!You are now connected to Wayne from Amazon.comMe:I have 2 kindle fires with 2 free time profiles for each of my children. i have the family plan. I purchased the minecraft app and was able to successfully load it to each of my children's profiles, and they were able to successfully play it at the same time. Today, the minecraft app disappeared form my daughter's profile. I attempted to re add it, and it appears to be successfully added when I use the "manage profile" feature, but it will not show up under her profile. I attempted to delete her profile and re load it, and It still will not work. I also powered the device off and on with no results.Wayne:Hello, my name is Wayne from kindle customer care. I'm here to help you today.I am sorry to hear that you have problem with the apps on your Kid's profiles.Let us check the Kindle and see what we can do.To verify that I have the correct account, Please verify the complete name that shows on your account, Email address, and your complete billing address.Me:Maura SammonXXXXXXXXXXXXWayne:Thanks. Is there a direction on your address?Me:WWayne:Perfect! Thank you.May I know the name of the Kindle listed on amazon account please?Me:It is Maura's 3rd fire. The account that is not loading minecraft is Lina'sWayne:Got it, Thanks Maura.Do you have it handy? Ideally I love to troubleshoot the Kindle to see what seems to be the problem.Me:Sure. Interestingly, when I load Lina's profile to Maura's 2nd fire (which is my son's) Minecraft loads on her profile.Wayne:I see. So the app sis working on your 2nd Kindle.Me:but not the 3rdWayne:Anyways, Let us try to restart the Kindle first. Please make sure that your Kindle is not connected on any power source.Then press the power button for full 40 seconds and no matter what you see on your screen just keep pressing.Thank you.Once restarted, please let me know.Me:just checking nowstill not thereWayne:I see.Could you make sure the device is connected to Wi-Fi?Me:it is.Wayne:Great. Let us now try to remove the app on your device and redownload, then add it back to her freetime profile. Press and hold the title, and select Remove from Device.Me:So you want me to go back to my profile, delete the app on my profile and then re download it?Wayne:Correct. All contents you've purchased are stored on your Amazon Cloud and can redownload via wireless connection.Me:Worked on my profile, not on LinasWayne:Okay, so you have added it back on her profile, right?Me:yepWayne:I appreciate that. Hang on while I check my reference further about it. Thank you!Me:Are you still there?Wayne:I am still here, Sorry for that long wait.Me:No problemWayne:I am double checking my reference about it and it seems this app has an in app purchase. Can you please confirm?I am afraid that any app from amazon appstore that has an in app purchase cannot access on freetime profiles.Also are you able to double check if the app is updated.Me:But it works perfectly fine on my son's free time account. It also works completely fine on Maura's 2nd fire, and it was working completely fine on Maura's 3rd kindle up until today, so that cannot be the problem.Yes the app is updated, I uninstalled it, then re installed it, remember?Wayne:I understand Maura and I appreciate that.Can you confirm if this app has an in app purchase option?Me:How do I do that?Wayne:Well, I've checked it from Amazon and it shows that it has an in app purchases available. That requires an internet connect and restrictions to purchase. I am afraid that is not allowed inside freetime profile.Me:So can you kindly explain to me why it is working on both my other device, my other free time profile, as well as my friend's free time profiles as well????????Sorry, but this does not seem to be a good explanation, or a good solution.Wayne:I know how you feel and you have a good point actually.Me:If you cannot fix this problem, perhaps you should bump it up to someone who canRather than just trying to placate meand say that it can't be fixedWayne:I am trying my best to help Maura. Well, allow me to double check all my reference about it to make sure I have the correct informations.Me:Just please don't give me an obviously incorrect answer again.especially if it is one that basically says "Sorry, can't help you"Wayne:Will you be able to remove the app on your 2nd fire and then add it back on her profile if that is still same.Me:Not sure what you meanWayne:I am sorry if that is how you feel, but I don't mean I can't help you.On your 2nd Fire, where the Minecraft is working, may you please try to remove it and then re-add if that will work still?Me:works fine.Wayne:Thank you. I appreciate that.Me:Also, I just noticed that it will not show up on Kaes's profile on Maura's 3rd fire, so the problem seems to be with the fire itself rather than the profile.Wayne:Thank you for the info.I've doubled check my reference and my lead about it and I can confirm that Apps with in-app purchases cannot be accessed within a FreeTime profile. I am not sure why you are able to get it work on your other Fire tablet. I do not have an information about that. I can't do anything about making that happen right now but I will definitely pass it along to our developers so they can consider that.Me:I would like to speak with a supervisor please. This explanation simply does not make sense, and numerous other children that we know with this device can. I would also like a copy of this conversation sent to them first, so I do not need to re address this whole issue.It seems that the problem is with the device, and if you cannot fix it, it should be replaced.Wayne:I truly understand how you feel and your point. Anyways,Let me connect you to my lead.Please hang on while I inform her so that you no need to repeat yourself.Thank you.Thank you for waiting. I am afraid that she may tell you the same thing.However, here you go.A Customer Service Associate will be with you in a moment.You are now connected to Esther from Amazon.comEsther:Hello, my name is Esther one of the supervisor from Kindle Customer Care. I'll be happy to help you today.Me:Please read thru the entire transcript before we begin so I don't need to repeat myself. I am not going to be happy with the previous explanation. It works on my other device, as well as my friend's devices. If you cannot fix this device, I expect it to be replaced. This is the first time I have not had excellent customer service from amazon.Esther:I'm sorry to know that you are having trouble trying to access the minecraft on the Kindle.Let me try to check what happened.I've tried to checked on my resources here, Maura. I'm sorry, as of this moment in-app purchases are not working inside Freetime.Me:I'm sorry. but this explanation does not make sense, as it is working on numerous other devices. We are not trying to make in app purchases, just run the basic game which is working on numerous other devices. The problem is the device. If you can't fix my device, please replace it.Esther:Just to confirm is there an in-app purchase on the Minecraft?Me:I have no idea how to do this. Your guy just told me that there is, and I simply believed him.Because he is supposed to be the expert hereEsther:I'm really sorry, Maura. However once there is an in-app purchase on that application. It will not work perfectly on Freetime.Me:We did not make an in app purchase!!!!!!Wayne said that the application supports them, but we did not make oneI don't even know for sure if the application actually supports them, that is just what wayne saidClearly, the device is the problem, and you are unable to fix it. Please replace the device!!!!!!!!!Esther:Is there an update with that app?Me:No. And if you had read the context of this conversation, you would know that. I am getting really frustrated here. If you are unable to authorize this, please bump this conversation, along with the full transcript if this conversation to someone who can.Esther:I'm sorry, however as of this moment I am the highest point of escalation that is available, Maura.Are the other apps working perfectly on that Kindle?Me:When will there be someone available who can fix my problem? We don't use other apps. I purchased these devises solely so they could play minecraftEsther:Oh I'm sorry, however it seems that the problem is with the app itself and not the Kindle, Maura.Me:I have already wasted a ridiculous amount of time here. This needs to be escalated to someone who can replace my device.THEN WHY IS IT WORKING WITH NO PROBLEM ON EVERY OTHER DEVICE????????? Please escalate. I am very frustrated and about to write a formal complaint letter.Esther:I'm really sorry for the inconvenience this has caused you, Maura. However as much as we wanted to help you with this, minecraft doesn't work perfectly inside Freetime.Me:I am writing a formal complaint letter. I expect this device to be replaced. I am also very upset that you refuse to escalate this complaint.Esther:I'm really sorry, Maura. However as of this moment we are unable to transfer this chat since I am the available highest point of escalation.Me:And I asked when you would, and you refused to answer.Esther:If you want, we can try to troubleshoot the device further.Me:I want it replaced.I have spent over an hour on this, and I am done.You, yourself, have already told me that there is nothing else that can be done.Esther:I'm really sorry, Maura. However since this issue is regarding an application we are unable to replace the device.If you want, you can return the device back to us for a refund.Me:I would like to return it and simply recieve another.I would like for the replacement to be sent first while awaiting my return, as is always done by amazon on defective devices!!!!!Esther:I'm really sorry, Maura. However since there is no specific issue regarding the Kindle like defects such as charging, won't turn on and etc. we are unable to replace the Kindle.What we can only do is to process a refund for this.Me:I will repeat once more. I expect this to be escalated. How can I do that? If no one is available now, when will someone be available????

title: This device is all about the spam/ads business model, through the entire device.
body: This device is all about the spam/ads business model. It's not user friendly. I've been into tech pretty hard core for many, many years. If you like, here is my review from Power ON to usage with some updates in between. My Mother bought it for the Winter Solstice. I'm the tech of the Family. I figured I'd review this. It arrived before the Winter Solstice (what Christmas comes from), and as I'm looking at it and starting the Setup I notice it has one ugly interface. The on-screen Keyboard is a really poor version of the Apple Keyboard invented by Steve Jobs and Woz. The newer update for iOS 8 and 9 became worse. There aren't any boarders for the characters. It's like they've gone the flat, bland and ugly way like Apple (past Mavericks) and Microsoft with that Windows 8 and 10 thing. It's very functional which is sweet but looks, Amazon has done poorly with it. I also notice Amazon seems to have forgotten the capitalize "A" for the company name or the "F" fore Fire. Hmm...very lazy and unprofessional. My Mother bought this because we're both not happy with Apple's Copy Protection (Digital Rights Management) so I've had to Jailbreak everything to free our devices, BUT...they also are a closed system, so you're not able to transfer anything without using iTunes. Unless you're an advanced user. I am in hopes this isn't the case with the "fire" which should be "Fire". Given that this device is the extremely generic version, I understand why it doesn't connect to faster Wi-Fi when available, but I had hoped it would. All is good. Buddha/Zen calmness is very cool. It wants an update as soon as you start it and it connects to Wi-Fi. You're not able to bypass this as you're able to with any Apple device. Uncool, Amazon. I'd rather be able to choose to use it right away, or allow the update. Update has finished downloading and the "fire" not "Fire" logo is now displayed. I wonder if it'll do the poor grammar thing that Windows does?? "Welcome" without any punctuation. Shall know in a moment. I really like that Apple products do greet properly. As I'm watching this process I'm noticing it's not speedy, for those speed freaks out there. Haha. The USB cable is short!! Be warned. Unlike the USB cable when the iPad 2 was released, a good length cable. The "fire" has finished with the update, and is now "Optimizing system storage and programs." - What that means is not what the text inform you of. It's now installing the OS and programs that are stored in a chip in case ever have to factory reset. But they word it for the general unaware masses.So far, if you DO NOT have Internet access you're not going to be able to use this. Not everyone has 'Net access and will be able to use their "Fire" right away regardless of I.S.P. access. BONUS points to Apple!! Haha. It does come with your Account Info installed (unless you do a factory reset). It took several attempts at pressing "Timezone incorrect?" for it to use the G.P.S. or Wi-Fi to get the correct information. Next is the "Family Setup", and they use "can" where "may" should be and they're redundant in their text (I noticed this a lot throughout the device--using it some later..))!! "Each member of your family has a personalized experience, just for them, ....yada yada,, Gee, is that what "Personalized experience means??" I wonder! Haha...Poking a LOT of fun at the "Fire" programmers here. Haha. It's cool that it has a profile setup for Small people at this point. Here comes something I really don't like. Without calling it "Cloud" service it's exactly what it is! "Backup & Auto-Save". I do not use Cloud, and I tell all of my family to stay away from it! Apple has already been hacked and so has the I.R.S. and others. This is Computer.. Any Cloud service, for people whom don't know, means your PERSONAL information is now stored on SOMEONE ELSE'S computer. As cool as SSL (HTTPS://) so, it's still a computer Program and thus hackable. If you wonder how Personal I.D. theft is possible, this is one of the many ways. DISABLE it all. Yes, I know, it's convenient. I have trust in The Gods, my Soul, and people I know whom are close to me, I don't trust and have total faith in companies. Not everyone has my best interest. I'm also not into conspiracy theories, I like good logical common sense. Either disable the 3 settings or tap the Setup Options to fine-tune.. I'm disabling it for now, and will check into it once the "fire" is usable. Next is Social Media.. I have a Facebook account but I rarely use it. Personally, I prefer face to face meetings and conversations with friends. I also don't give a hoot what "Snookie" is doing. Too much drama, and other stuff there.Ahh...next is the Advertisement for Prime. I knew that would be there somewhere. Nice blue "Continue without FREE 2-day shipping." Nice guilt trip there Amazon. Laughing, eh..not a big deal. It took 3 taps for it to recognize I pressed it. This is becoming a big deal with this machine. I think there Touch Recognition Software or Hardware isn't as functional as it could be. Apple nailed it, but Amazon, past Setup, I'm noting it doesn't always recognize taps from fingers and pens made for this. AHH!!! A proper "Welcome!"...Nicely done Amazon. It is nice that through all the text they capitalize the "F" for Fire they got the punctuation right.A Not so cool note: I got a call at this point via iPhone. I set the Fire aside, and the screen blanked when not in use...okay, that's cool, but when I press the Power Button to unblank the screen there was SPAM, a commercial AD as the Lock Screen. That should be changeable in the prefs but that isn't the point!! SPAMMED!! THIS PRODUCT HAS BEEN BOUGHT, and is NOW Owned! The Lock Screen is an Amazon program, why in is there a Toyota Car Sale Advertisement on the "Fire" Lock Screen!?!? EXTREMELY UNCOOL, Amazon!! Piss Poor Form! We all get spammed constantly with Ads through Web Pages, and in Programs we bought or are free. NOW you're allowing Spam on a Lock Screen before it's even USED!? Is this the new Business Model????? This is not a very cool Introduction to the "Fire" in my opinion. If I were new to this stuff I'd also wonder. I rarely every go to movie Theaters anymore because as okay as I am with Trailers and I expect them, I am absolutely not OKAY with commercials! I'm not typing about the Ads for the Theater, I can handle that. I'm typing about actual commercials before a movie begins. What other intrusive stuff will I notice? Update: I've since learned after some checking around that this IS the intended business idea! Their point being, "We aren't making enough money from these and so we want YOU to pay 15$ (Plus applicable taxes.) to disable ALL advertising. After using it a bit, and my Mother using it a bit...it's all over the place!! We are both completely turned off by this practice. Amazon, I don't begrudge you wanting to make money. I don't call you greedy, but THIS business idea SUCKS!! You're attempting to compete against Apple, and Microstink (Windows 10..), but....this....this isn't it. I'm totally turned off. As is my Mother. I'll soon do a factory reset after I check 2 things about it, and it'll be on the way back. I DO NOT SUPPORT SPAMMING PEOPLE and then making a Computer and making the OS into a Shareware OS. Poor, poor, POOR form. I hope many others read this and agree. I very much enjoy the Website and buying from Amazon, but....not this business model.Continuing on... I tapped the Unlock button, and then prompted to Swipe. I'm attempting to swipe just under the "spam"...but it's not working. Sooo, I move my finger to the left edge of the screen and swipe. Now I'm seeing "Selected offers". What?? Wow, more spam. Okay, there's a little "O" or a Cycle at the bottom. I'll tap that. Hmm..Back to the Scam Lock Screen. Okay, do it again...I'm swiping over the lock, around the lock, and now ON the Ads!! Apple, you all made it correct. Sheesh! Ahhh, the text is so small I missed the word "up". Wow....Okay. My eyes.. "The Home page displays a list of all your programs, yada, yada..." "You MAY (not can) navigate by swipping....yada, yada..." They use a lot of "can" instead of "may".. Interesting... Anyway...yay, now I'm looking at the very flat and bland user interface. It looks a lot like Apple's iOS 8 and 9 flat and bland Interface with some differences. After all, to copy Apple would cause a "Lawsuit". HEHE! So, bypassing the fact that all the Icons are bland and flat...let's check function. First...to get to Settings and disable that Spam junk. I quickly found Settings and am going to check to disable Wi-Fi for now, as it SHOULD be usable without it as well as disable spam. Update: This isn't possible since they charge money for it...in case you skimmed while reading.(This "Fire" came well charged, about 80%, I think...but it was about 45 minutes before I was able to see the Spam/Lock Screen. So you know; it's NOT an Apple Device!...Hahah!)I found the Restore to Factory Defaults so I might use my iPad 2 to record a video to compliment this text. Update: I'm not going to make a video. It's not worth it to me. I'm so completely turned OFF by their business model and the excuse for using spam..any many others are going to notice it anyway. I bet sales for iPad's and MacBook Pros, and Mac Pro's continue to rise. I thought I would consider a larger screen version of this, but now.....nope. Thanks for reading, now I'm going to use my iPad 2 that I'm sticking with iOS 6.1.3 with, because it functions very well and looks pretty and bold. Thanks to my Mom for the "Fire" experience. Bummer.

title: DO NOT BUY! Way too many issues if you want this device for a non-toddler child
body: Do not, I repeat, DO NOT buy the Kindle Fire (HD or otherwise) if your intent is to give it to a child, older than the age of 7 (and definitely not for a child over the age of 10). Why do you ask?Because the ONLY things your child will be able to do with their FreeTime child account are: read books shared on the family account, play apps, and watch PURCHASED, downloaded to device videos. If you pay for the Freetime Unlimited, they WILL be able to watch "kid-friendly tv shows and movies (though I cannot find a list of what is available without buying it) BUT you are required to have a wifi connection because your child cannot watch a rented or prime video download. You cannot load any photos onto a child's account (there is no option to share them like there is apps) without taking a photo of the photo. You cannot share any music with a child's FreeTime account. The photos and music can be ON the device, under the parent's account, but NONE of it is selectable to share with a child account.It is one thing to restrict a child's access to the outside (restricting email, social media, in-app purchases, etc), and quite another make it impossible for the intended user (the child) to access half of the features.(The backstory)I recently purchased two Kindle Fires because our car's DVD player is dying and our extended family lives far enough away we often have an 11-hour car ride (that's one day, more the 2nd day) when we go visit. It is a day we lift the normal movie-watching restrictions. Getting my young daughter and older son to agree on a show/movie to watch is getting more challenging as they have very different interests. Both kids love to read. They rarely play games/apps but I loved that the Freetime app would let you set time limits. No more arguing for 5 more minutes, the Kindle would take care of it for you. Plus my kids love to dance and often have songs they want to listen to over and over again (driving me crazy). I thought the Fire was the perfect solution.I had no idea how wrong I was. After hours of setting it up, picking out my son's favorite photos, downloading a few apps, and downloading some Prime videos to watch offline, I finally entered my child's Freetime account.To my dawning horror, there was no Amazon video option to stream. None of the downloaded Prime videos, minus the one we owned (because we could actually redeem the digital copy when we bought the DVD), were available to even share with him. NONE of the photos were available to share.My child could TAKE a picture but after I googled and hunted under all the settings, I discovered there is no way to import any photo to a kid's account. None of the music is available to share, whether from Prime music or downloaded from your computer.ALL of these things ARE available under the main account, the adult who has the Prime membership. I can see photos, play and download music, watch downloaded prime movies. If I turn on the parental controls I have to type a code every single time I do anything (practically) but I can do them (if the settings are correct).But I didn't buy the Fire for me. I bought two of them, one for each of my elementary-aged children. My access means nothing. Being able to share my books with them means nothing (we don't read the same material). But not being able to use it to watch Prime movies, especially downloaded ones? Not able to share a photo so my kids could show their friends or teacher about the trip they love? Not able to have them listen to any music at all? That matters. The inability to do those things renders the Fire into a useless piece of junk, especially if your child is older (if you have a toddler, it's probably great, assuming you didn't want your toddler watching anything on Amazon Video... ever).I want to restrict my children's access to the outside (digital) world. I want to restrict how much time they spend on the device (minus the long car rides). I should have the ability to lock down outside access while possessing the ability to SHARE EVERYTHING ON THE DEVICE OR CLOUD with my children. I should be able to share photos, music and Prime videos with them. Things I SELECTED they could watch. Things the main user can watch and do.The FreeTime thing is not wonderful, though it has so much potential. It's a joke. A pathetic joke that turns your Fire into a glorified ebook sharing/app player. If you want it for the adult, fine, it seems to be good. But if you're buying this for a child (especially an older one), the stop now and DO NOT PURCHASE. The child Freetime setting is basically identical to the teen setting (the only things that really change are the background color and fonts). For some reason if you do select Books, Games or Apps in the FreeTime menu screen (even in teen mode) it can ONLY show you that tab in landscape, not portrait (even if the Fire was/is in portrait position). Really?Unless you do what you do with your Phone or iPad and just let the child use your device without any precautions at all, then do NOT buy this for a kid. It's a bit ironic that in making the device so restrictive to where you can't share most of your content, you're forced to avoid using FreeTime at all and avoiding the kid accounts all together.And it's been this way for YEARS. Apparently. I have to wonder if they have any plans to fix it.Yet, you see none of this mentioned on the main page, extolling the virtues of the Fire. There is even a young girl shown LISTENING TO MUSIC (or rather, she has ear buds in and it's in the "Listen" section which talks about using Prime Music... (see attached photos and the lack of music options... this is on the "teen" setting of Freetime).(ETA on 10-5-16) Other problems discovered:- Apps (my experience is with Minecraft, a paid app that is the one game my children like to play) will randomly stop working sometimes when the WIFI is off. As the main reason for my children having these is for travel and occasional use for "fun day" at school, where is NOT wifi, this renders the app pointless. NOT a problem on an iPad.-There is ZERO ability to turn off updates on the FIRE. It will be turned completely off (shut down) and then randomly update without your knowledge whenever it wants to. Then, it will NOT turn the device off so when you go to use it, instead of being almost fully charged because you shut it down, have zero battery left (the battery, when the device is not fully shut down, appears to drain very quickly).What the )(*&%@ Amazon? How could you go so wrong with a good idea?I recommend calling into Amazon customer service and telling them all that is wrong with Freetime, updates, and apps for the Fire. Because this is ridiculous. I only wish I'd learned of all of these issues when I could still have returned the devices and cases.

title: Overall, not an enjoyable experience
body: UPDATE TO THE UPDATE: One more thing that Amazon and Bezos forgot to mention about Fire OS 5: Encryption? Yeah, not so much. Seems that, while no one was much looking, they decided that your data wasn't worth safekeeping. [...]Not only is this device a piece of crap, this device is unsafe. Bezos probably needs to start wearing a hat because hjis exposed skull is evidently affecting his brain. His decision-making, at least, because while Apple is fighting to protect your privacy from hackers, Bezos is saying "come and get it, bad guys". Don't know about you, but that's intolerable to me.UPDATE: Now that I've had this device for another several days, I've discovered several hidden gotchas; ways in which Amazon neutered this device without mentioning that they have done so. Had Amazon been honest enough to mention these items in their promotional advertising -- each is quite surprising, in fact -- they wouldn't be down to 1 star in my review.The fact is, I have seen references on discussion forums that BOTH of these features are present on the device. They aren't and customers should be aware of their absence before they buy. Apparently, Amazon is counting on the low price dissuading those who are surprised by their absence from returning them. Memo to Amazon: dishonesty is NEVER a good policy, my friends and, yes, since both of these features are staples of *all* Amazon tablets, the lack of reference to them being missing IS dishonesty from Amazon; at least in any reasonable interpretation of the word "honesty".The first missing feature is quite surprising since it appears on virtually all Amazon devices except this one: Smart sleep. You know how, when you close the cover of your case, the device goes to sleep? Not with the Fire 7. Since I've seen references from customers *swearing* that this feature exists, it's something that Amazon should make clear.The second missing feature is one that really hurts: You know that feature on the keyboard where you press the microphone and dictate to the keyboard? The one that is present on ALL recent Amazon devices with a microphone (which this one has)? Nope. Not on the Fire 7. That's because it's missing the Nuance voice-to-text engine. So, maybe they wanted to keep the cost of the device low by not including this. I understand that and its absence would be disappointing but not worthy of a 1 star review. No, the 1 star is for Amazon's deception in not mentioning the lack of this feature.The bottom-line: You should be aware of what you are getting (and not getting) with this device.--- End of Update ----- Original Review --So you see this apparently great deal on a tablet ... $40 - $50 and you say ... why not? Before you order, though, here are some things to consider.1. Size: I was coming from a Fire HD 6. 7 inches is definitely better. That's a good thing.2. SD-Card: Again, a good thing because you'll have plenty of storage for your ebook library (IF you use a 3rd-party reading app like FBReader, Moon+, etc.), music, side-loaded video content, etc. BUT there's a problem that most reviews miss as I note in #3.3. Internal Storage: The 8 GB internal storage is way, way too limited. That's because, even if you have an sd-card, the OS itself must be installed on the internal storage -- leaving you with a paltry 5 GB free -- AND MANY apps that you'll install insist on being installed to internal storage only. There are 3rd-party solutions to move *some* of these to the sd-card but not all and it is LIKELY THAT YOU'LL RUN OUT OF SPACE on this device for apps. THAT is a very big problem.4. Resolution: The 171 ppi on this device is -- in a word -- terrible. Text is difficult to read and the quality of the video is very poor. Closed captions are also a victim of resolution because they appear distorted on the device. So much so, in fact, that I usually just turn them off now. Is text readable at all? YES. Is video tolerable? YES. Is it enjoyable in either case? Not really.5 Audio: Again, barely passable. Is it good enough? YES. Is it enjoyable? Not really6. Speed: I'm finding myself continually annoyed at the length of time it takes to *begin* downloading and then installing content, or accessing streaming content. Once the content is acquired, it plays just fine. This is NOT a fast device. In fact, it is annoyingly slow.7. Build Quality: Again, barely passable. It feels as inexpensive as the price tag suggests. Very cheap back, very cheap front glass. Is it good enough? YES. Is it enjoyable? Not really.8. Google Play Store: One of the most frequent complaints about all Amazon devices is Amazon's war with Google which extends to barring Google Play Store. That's not a problem with this device because there is a very easy fix for this (there are several videos on YouTube showing how to do this.) Of course, this displeases Amazon and we can expect that Amazon will attempt to prevent this so what works today might not work tomorrow.9: Interface: Another frequent complaint about Fire devices is that Amazon attaches their own interface to it, so that it doesn't look or behave like other Android devices. Again, you can fix that with a 3rd-party solution if you choose. Again, too, Amazon seems most displeased by this and is continually updating the device to prevent this so what's possible today might not be possible tomorrow.10. Root: Rooting is the process of gaining access to the OS and getting a device rooted allows all sorts of interesting things. Rooting is a good thing and it is quite easy on this device, unlike most Amazon devices. Again, Amazon doesn't apparently like this and is continually trying to stop it so what's possible today might not be possible tomorrow.So: The device is a nice size you'll have plenty of space for content but not necessarily apps. In addition -- unless Amazon prevents you -- the device is probably THE most customizable of the Amazon tablet offerings. Those are the good things about this device, along with cost. Everything else -- screen resolution, audio, speed, build quality -- make using this tablet less than enjoyable.Personally, I've had this for a couple of days and I've not decided whether I'm keeping it. The Fire HD 6 is smaller and more limited in content storage but it is a *much* more enjoyable experience for not much more than the cost of this plus an sd-card. If I had it to do over again, that's the Amazon device that I would choose.

title: WARNING: Riddled with problems; an embarrassment of a product.
body: I'm giving this low of a review because I see so many reviewers who've given 4 & 5 stars without having really put this device through its paces. After five months of use now, I have had more headaches than I can count. If I'd known how much time would be devoted to what should be (but is as far from it as technologically possible) a very simple process of clearing off free space--not to have more room to download new apps, or content as one might think--but just to get the WiFi to turn back on, as it automatically disables when the device approaches internal storage capacity (along with much of the functionality needed to remedy the storage problem in the first place). And you would think it would be as simple as transferring content to an SD card, problem solved. But it's nowhere near simple, or easy, or user-friendly, and least of all "quick."Here's the rundown: let's say you have a kid set up as a second profile, which uses freetime. You meticulously download the apps, and then set the permissions. That doesn't matter. When the kid uses their profile they just have to download the app again, so you end up with twice the storage used. So you delete the one from your profile, no biggie. Except the OS won't let you uninstall. So, assuming you're wifi hasn't been cut off, you download a third party file management app. After four or five crashes, you delete the apps entirely then re-download them just to the kid's profile. Okay, lesson learned. Only, you still have the same storage problem crop up once a week for some reason or another. You check to make sure the settings have everything installing to the SD card, but some apps are persistently downloading to internal storage despite the settings. So you go to look up a solution. Of course, you can't use the device itself because the WiFi is disabled, so let's just say you use your phone. You find an android app that says it can manage your storage. But wait, you can't download it because, oh yeah, no wifi. So you do a factory reset and spend hours setting the device up again. Finally, it's just right, but your SD card isn't registering. Hang on a sec, you downloaded all those movies, books, and albums to that sd card, not to mention the apps from the last time you set this accursed thing up. So you look up how to get the SD card working on the same service you set it up with. No luck of course, so after a few frustrating hours you realize it would have been quicker to just re-download everything, which you decide to do now. You reformat the SD card, and start all over again.NOW everything is prefect again. For another week. When the problem repeats. And while you now know what to do, and have the right third party apps (since the OS won't cooperate with storage management), but you still have his of work ahead of you, counting the for our five crashes when you use the file management app you need to fix the storage.So your experience thus far had been a nightmare, just wait till you cave and sign up for freetime unlimited. You wouldn't normally, since you have already had such an awful experience with the storage, but your kid loves Thomas the Train, and is on Prime, but since freetime doesn't allow prime videos, he/she has no way to stream Thomas (Yes, you read that right, kids can't use your prime content. Why? So you will have no choice but to buy the "free" content, or do the "sensible" thing and sign up for freetime unlimited.) you get your 1-month-free-trial and figure you can just cancel when it starts to bill (because one month of free Thomas the Train is better than no Thomas at all...or so you think). Little do you realize that you have just given away the control of what content your kid can see unless you go through thousands of titles to pick and choose after the fact. Sure, Amazon sticks with kids content, but you have no way of knowing if that includes the children's illustrated "Susie has two mommies," not that you're homophobic, but that's a conversation good rather come from you, not amazon. But after about a week, that will be the least of your concerns, because your kid will have downloaded a couple dozen freetime unlimited apps or books, and almost all of them went straight to internal storage despite your SD card settings. So you spend another hour or three trying to move the content to your SD card, which has tons of space side you had to reformat it to get it to work again. But the OS won't let you move the freetime unlimited apps. It says it will, but when you try (after about 5 minutes of waiting) it says it can't move the application. So you try to just delete it, but the option is greyed out. Why? Good question. Even Google doesn't seem to know why though. So it's back to your third party app to free up enough space just to get the WiFi back. After your usual crashes, it finally deletes a few apps. You go to see how much storage you feed up, since the "gigantic" apps were a whopping 300mb, but it hardly put a dint in your free space. You get angry and decide to cancel freetime unlimited to stop the problem from occurring again, but until you free up enough space to get your wifi back you can't manage your subscriptions. You barely restrain yourself from throwing the unit, but only just.After a half hour spent looking through content to see what is taking up all your free space, you find that will over half the storage is taken up by "app data," which didn't go down at all, even once the app was removed. So you go back to Google (from your phone, if you still couldn't free enough space for the WiFi to turn back on) only to find time and again people telling you the only fix is to restore to factory settings. It's been 4 months of this, your kid is crying, you've gone grey, and your blood pressure has reached dangerous levels, but since you were to cheap to shell out the money for a real tablet, this is the best you've got, so you reformat and the cycle begins anew.Seriously, this is just a glimpse of the frustration I've experienced with this unit. I cold have bought twenty of these things if I'd just spent the same amount of time on a side job. You're better off working a little more and buying an iPad. Just trust me; it'll cost far less than the time, effort, and frustration fooling with this worthless paper weight for months on end will.

title: Parental Controls: Fail, Fail, Fail
body: I was looking for a tablet for my kids to play educational games that would work, and that wouldn't break the bank. Well, this isn't it. I'm ready to send both of them back. My three big beefs:1. Memory. The tablet itself comes with very little memory and it is very difficult to manage. Instead, it just keep trying to sell you more memory. It's like a selling a bicycle without wheels. It doesn't operate with the memory is comes with. This problem will rear its head again in #2, problems with Freetime Unlimited.2.Parental Controls. I bought this because Amazon claims to have "best parental controls" with free time unlimited. If these are best the industry can offer, give up now. If you just want good quality educational apps and books, and nothing else, you're in trouble. The way the controls are set up, you can click "Block Entertainment content until all educational goals are met," and then chose time goals for videos, apps, and books. (Please note that Amazon decided all books are educational. I would differ on that.) How about simply, "block entertainment content," as in, period. You can disable videos, sort of, by setting the time limit to zero. You have to use "Time by activity" to scroll videos all the way back to nothing. Since I only want educational apps, I have "Time By Activity" for Apps set at a 3 hour limit, and then my goal for educational apps as a 3 hour goal. So in theory, the kids can only do educational apps, but wouldn't it be nice if that were just a setting?Despite these controls, there is still 'video' option on the home screen. So your kids can click on it and get mad at you for disabling it. Thanks, Amazon, I really wanted the tablet to make my job as a parent harder -- that was my goal (*sarcasm*). There are also videos hidden in apps. The PBS app, somehow, classed as "educational" is just unlimited access to their TV shows. If I wanted me kids to watch TV, I'd turn it on. I wanted a tablet for them to interact with and play educational games on. That's the setting I chose -- why am I getting TV shows? Now, I can go and block individual apps. Great, just what I want to do with my free time. But wait, not only does that take a lot of time, but it's really hard to do. The apps in the kids section don't have names, just little images. So you can scroll through 10,000 pictures and try to remember the bad ones you saw, or you can try to guess/figure out the name and use search. And when you block them, the pictures still appear in the kids interface, forever reminding them of PBS kids. And, it gets better, they sometimes come back. That PBS app with the hidden videos, well, I've blocked it three times and it keeps coming back. I blocked it just now, then logged in as my child and it was still there! This content frustration goes both ways. My son had found an app he liked and my daughter wanted it on hers. I went to all the trouble to downloading it to her device, but then once you're logged in the kids profile, you can't find it. It's a wasteland of content.3. Freetime Unlimited. Unlimited needs limits. Remember the tiny memory? My son has locked up his Fire several times because he touches the app, it starts downloading, his attention span runs out (he's a preschooler), and he touches another one. Next thing you know there's 10 apps trying to download, the memory is full, and you have to do a factory reset. Again, thanks for making mommy and daddy's life easy, Kindle Fire. Then there's the books. I told the Kindle my kids age, why are there books on there for tweens? He can't even read, and when he's ready for Cat in the Hat, I really don't want him downloading a graphic novel about zombies with, you know, graphic graphics. Why Amazon have a preselected group of books appropriate for different age groups? That would be nice for apps, books, everything. Even my first grader, whose patience is more developed, ends up loading too much content.Free time Unlimited interface needs a redesign, generally. There are four options at the top: Books (none that are good for pre-readers), Videos (which you can't get rid of even though you can disable videos), Apps (where the read-to-you books are, as well as hidden "appisode" videos), and Characters, which is another place where you can find blocked content (like videos, age-inappropriate books and apps, etc.). The main screen will keep the picture of content that's been removed. Example, while logged in as a child, you can press and hold the image and you get a dialogue box. One option is to 'remove from device.' Not sure what this actually does because it does not free up memory or take the picture off your valuable main screen "ribbon" display.The last gripe I have with Freetime Unlimited is several of the "free" "unlimited" apps have in-app purchases. You can disable it, so your kids can't spend you whole paycheck on treats for some app's pet rabbit, but it also leads to frustration. Why? Because there's options they can see, but can't select. There's an ABC song app that only plays one letter of alphabet. You need to buy the other 25. Since I am paying for a subscription, I would much rather have less quantity of content, but better quality.The good things: the Fire itself works well. When the kids click on something (provided it's already downloaded), it responds. We used to have a DaVinci that had great content, but terrible hardware so it was basically nonfunctional. But, the Fire's speed does not make up for the bad content, bad software design, inadequate parental controls and minuscule memory. If you are looking for a device for mindless entertainment and hidden TV programs, and don't mind being nickel and dimed for in-app purchases and adding memory, then you might like the Fire with Unlimited Freetime.

title: good hardware made cheap by DATA-MINING and BLOATWARE
body: I bought this on prime day for only $33.33. It has great hardware for the price, a nice screen, and seems to have decent build quality. So, why the the three star? Well, one word, DATA-MINING. The reason this is cheap is because just as song "Every breath you take" by the Police goes, its watchin' you. Amazon collects literally everything you do to sell it to advertisers. Your personal information is automatically taken and sold for profit without most people's knowledge of it. You are able to disable this nefarious practice, but it take going through every menu to thwart the DATA-MINING. The general consensus of the oblivious is "why should I care, I have nothing to hide." You might not (or you might not sure what kind of person you are...), but the issue is that your hardware gets bogged down trying to send Amazon every morsel of information so that you are left with a far slower device. So yes, you should care. Not just because of your privacy, but because what you have paid for is no longer designed for you, its designed for the corporation to make money. The other reason for the lower review is the limited amount of apps available. Amazon competes with google, so they are unwilling to put the google play store on the device. This prevents the user from getting firefox or any other decent browser. With this being said, for someone like me, a student entering college next year, I don't care for too much else. Amazon Underground does a great job providing all of the practical applications I need to be productive. I was amazed at how many quality apps were free that are actually useful. Pandora is available, which for me is a big plus as I mainly bought this to play music and do light research. However, for others who wish to download popular mindless games or find some other way to waste their future potential such as walking into a busy intersection while playing pokemon go, this may not be the right device. I would next like to address the layout of the operating system. It is somewhat of an android which is nice. However, the layout is largely designed for monetization rather than functionality. The other issue is the BLOATWARE. Again, this tablet is cheap because of the money Amazon makes after selling it to you, somewhat like HP's practice of selling cheap printers with negative seven millimeter ink cartridges so you have to buy them constantly for exorbitant prices. The standard version comes with 8gb of internal storage, however almost half is eaten up largely by apps you don't want, don't care for and can't uninstall. To summarize, this device has awesome hardware, but is let down by DATA-MINING and BLOATWARE. With more use I will determine if I should root it or not, but for the common person, this isn't practical. Rooting an android requires a decent computer knowledge and in my opinion a linux or mac operating system to avoid viruses if a download contains malicious software. However, about 98% of people either don't know, what I'm even talking about or don't have the technical knowledge to really know what it is they are doing. So the bottom line is, yes, this is great for the price, no you should not buy it unless you are the very minimum will go extensively through the settings to optimize your device.As a final piece of advice, going for the 16gb would be like paying $100,000 for a chevy cruze (proportionately). SD cards are far cheaper than the upgrade cost and can hold much more.Hopefully this helpsUpdate:After turning off tracking, I try to use some of the Amazon Underground apps and it tells me that in order to use them, I must have data tracking turned on. Well, thats the final straw, time to root. Because of this, my review has went from 3 stars to 1. The only that Amazon has not destroyed is the hardware. I'll update when I root it. I won't change my 1 star, but I'll let you, the prospective buyer know whether the device is worth your money and time.Update 2: So I went ahead and put the google play store on it, this is actually very easy and i recommend to anyone with at least average computing skills. I am by no means a genius with computers and this took me 5 minutes. However, the tablet was still handicapped by amazon's horrid excuse for an android operating system. So, I rooted it and put the google nexus operating system on it, installed ad-away and put up a firewall. It literally is about 5 times faster and has an actually good interface. With the exception of not having bluetooth, its like a $300 tablet for $33.33 and a decent amount of work. It took me about 6 hours or so to learn everything, but someone who already knows could do everything I did in less than an hour. The only thing left associated with Amazon is the hardware, which as I've said is actually good. However, it still gets one star. You wouldn't say a ford focus is really fast, except if it was one that a rally team had modified, in which case it is almost nothing like the original. The same goes for this tablet, its a piece of trash stock, but its an awesome deal with the right modifications.

title: DO NOT BUY- constant errors make this tablet non-functional
body: If I could give these Fire tablets zero stars or negative stars I absolutely would because all of the whining, frustration and trouble shooting that goes with having them is worse than having nothing at all.We purchased two of these Fire devices in October 2016 exclusively for use during long road trips or plane travel by our 2 and 4 year old. This means that when we use them we REALLY need them to work and with minimal input from us parents. We ended up with these tablets because that is exactly as they were marketed- as "kid safe" and "kid friendly" with features built in to allow the kids to navigate their profiles safely by themselves.We have had CONSTANT, unending, ever changing issues with using the apps and viewing the videos on these tablets. Every time we use them there are new complications requiring significant parent input to work around. And a problem that presents itself in the same way on both tablets needs to be solved in a different way on each. So much frustration.Our biggest issue is with maintaining access to the content we've bought and downloaded, particularly access via the kids' profiles.When trying to access apps from the kid's profiles we will get messages saying that an app is not on the SD card ("App Unavailable - SD card not found. Please insert the SD card which contains this app.") when it absolutely IS on the SD card, will play from the adult profile and has been downloaded from the adult profile. This happens both when the initial download of the app was started from that device and when it was initiated from the other Fire device.Intermittently the video tab will just completely disappear from the kid's profile making the content we have paid for and laboriously downloaded inaccessible.We bought and downloaded a whole season of episodes of a TV show to the SD card and in the middle of our first trip the videos went from working fine to being unable to be accessed from the kid's profile. All of these videos also now showed as not downloaded on the adult profile and had to be re-downloaded-- this is a waste of HOURS with our slow internet.After that happened I was terrified of having to download the videos for a third time so I put the tablets in airplane mode and left them in that state hoping that the tablets could just maintain their current status in that way.No such luck. The next time we tried to use the tablets, all apps came up with notifications that the licenses were expired. On one tablet fixing this just required connecting the tablet to the internet. On the other tablet ALL of the apps had to be re-downloaded as they no longer showed as being present on the SD card.The second trip for which we used the tablets, the audio volume when using headphones was no longer loud enough for kids to hear although it had worked fine the first trip. I thought it might be the decibel limiting feature on the kids' headphones but the same thing happened when regular adult headphones were used.The video player in the kids profile doesn't allow the screen to be pinned, meaning wandering fingers can constantly interrupt play. I resolved this by downloading the MX Player app, but really expected better from a stock video player on an interface intended for kids.The app used the most shows up in the app library, shows up as downloaded to the SD card and can be played from the adult profile. It does not show up on the kid profile list of apps anymore. Trying to re-add the content from the adult profile to the kid profile is useless as this app doesn't show up as an option to be added when in the profile management part of settings. Where has it gone?Final straw? In the midst of our second trip the kids' profiles just completely emptied of all apps and all videos. The only thing that shows up in them now is the Camera Roll. This requires us to play all apps and videos from the adult profile which completely defeats the purpose of buying this tablet for its "kid friendly" interface. I don't even want to troubleshoot getting the apps and videos back onto their profiles and risk having to re-download all of these things to the SD cards AGAIN.I have never used a device that is so terrible. Integrity of data and memory is non-existent. If I have purchased a season of videos or an app and downloaded it to the SD card it should be there and be recognizable by the tablet EVERY time I use it. Where is this data going? Having a video screen to be able to access a video from an SD card should be such basic technology by this point that the fact that these tablets can't even do that is just pathetic and feels like I've been completely scammed.The complete lack of quality control on these Fire devices would never be acceptable on a handheld mobile device and I cannot understand how Amazon can possibly justify SELLING people such a flawed item, even if it is at an ever decreasing price.

title: Kindle--An iPod for Books without Books
body: Given that the Kindle, Amazon's new electronic reader, rolled out just before Christmas, I thought it might have some buzz about it, despite its $399 price tag.I haven't seen much (any) and as an early adopter, I thought I'd pass on some feedback.Imagine the iPOD (or remember when it was) being sold with limited downloadable songs available. In the early days of iTunes, that was the case, but the iPod also had the capability to let you rip your CDs into the player, making your existing collection portable. Without this feature, the early iPods would have been expensive paperweights.And, speaking of expensive paperweights, I bring you the Kindle. The Kindle relies on the ability to download e-books and claims a library of nearly 100,000 titles. The usability factor gets a zero out of 100,000.First, though, let me say that the unit is cool enough. I think everything about it technically is fine and the concept of building the wireless into the price of the unit and the downloads is pretty forward-thinking. Amazon, after all, realized that shipping is built into the price of anything you buy at retail stores, so a comparable bottom line price that includes shipping is attractive to online buyers. I think they've kind of done the same thing with the wireless service in the Kindle. I have no complaints about the device itself.It seems to me that the value of the Kindle is when its mobile. Say, on a business trip you see a new release in the airport for $24.99. You whip out your lightweight Kindle, type in the title, and download it for $9.99, just as advertised. No lugging of the book and you saved money, too. Further, I would think that business readers would be a prime first-mover segment for the Kindle. Let's face it--smart phones and gadgets are the business traveler's manly version of the Coach handbag.After charging my Kindle, I typed in a current release (an October release from Harvard Business Press). Umm, no, not available. I typed in another. Nope. A third, no. A fourth, no again.I thought maybe I was wrong, that these titles weren't yet available. I'd been stockpiling some titles in anticipation of the Kindle so I could load up for an upcoming trip.I went to Amazon.com and all four titles were available in their store. I typed in the title of an elections-related book, out in 2006, into the Kindle and got nothing. Again, it was at the Amazon store.So, I went back and re-watched the video on amazon.com and I verify that they have more than 80,000 books, BLOGS, and NEWSPAPERS, and I have to assume right now that that's 0 books, and 80,000 blogs and newspapers. Or, maybe 1 book and 79,999 blogs and newspapers.The video goes on to say that New York Times Bestsellers and most new releases are $9.99 or less. Well, new releases were a strike out, so I go for a book issued in 2004. Aptly titled, "Seeing What's Next," it sells for $26.36. GREAT! That's only $4.61 more than I can buy it with free shipping from Amazon's website.Jeff Bezos proudly showed off the Kindle in a video on the website. He's a smart guy and I bet he does a bit of reading. I really have to wonder if he actually used the Kindle before he shot the video. I just can't believe he would have beamed like the 2007 version of the iPod-toting Steve Jobs if he knew how useless the device was.I emailed the kindle feeback address at Amazon and got the typical response you would expect....we are adding more titles daily, tell us specific title recommendations (which I did in the initial email), etc. All in all, the Kindle, I'm sure, will go down as a stupid thing for which I spent money. It's sad that for every Slingbox, you have to kiss a few Kindles, but that's what has happened.I think the whole experience is, well, bad, but I thought the post was worth relaying for a couple of other reasons. First, I haven't seen much written about the Kindle except in the Wall Street Journal and a couple of other columns and I thought you might want some insight. Second, I think this experience speaks volumes (pun intended, because there aren't many volumes with the Kindle) about a tremendous distribution gaffe by a company that kind of invented Internet retail distribution in the 21st century.Amazon seems more intent on distributing the Kindle than someone actually using it. That seems so unlike Amazon. There will be some post-mortens written about the E-Readers (Amazon's, Sony's, and others) and I'm sure Amazon's will go down as a dud. The epitaph may speak to poor technology but, like companies find in the non-Internet world, it really will be because of poor execution.[[ASIN:B000FI73MA Kindle: Amazon's New Wireless Reading Device]]

title: Absolutely hate it, for many reasons
body: I had a Kindle 3G Keyboard (new in 2011) and the master control button stopped working. After trying in vain to find a software fix or work-around, or anyone who could repair it, I purchased this Fire tablet as a replacement. I chose this model mainly because it has text-to-speech, and I also wanted a camera, and internet connection for when my desktop crashes. I did not intend to play games, watch movies, use email, text, or shop.First I found out that its requirements are mixed with its features, so I was misled that it HAD built-in Wi-fi like my 3G. For instance “Kindle Fire Tablet, 7” Display, Wi-fi, 8GB, ...” It HAS a 7" display, and COMES with 8 GB, why would I think I have to BUY the Wi-Fi that's part of the same list, apparently of features? I finally had to ask a relative why I couldn't turn it on. More time & money buying a wireless router ($40), on top of the extra $40 for the screen saver (just 2 sheets of plastic) and case.It's hard to know where to begin listing the problems with this model. The screen rotation is WAAAY too sensitive – if you want to be perpetually dizzy, dare to breathe while using it. I turned that feature off. When the tablet was turned off, it kept playing a few notes now & then. I found out how to turn that off, only to find it turns off ALL the sounds. One sound that's necessary is the “click” of the camera shutter button, as touching, tapping, pressing, swiping, or cursing doesn't help the intermittent nature of the response of this button. And, I was never able to find out WHAT it was notifying me of. Since it can read an entire book to me, how about having it say “text” or “e-mail”??Two months of practice have not allowed me to be able to standardize the tapping or swiping. (And with 50+ years of fine needlework, I don't think it's my ineptitude or clumsiness.) Book pages turn when they aren't intended to, sometimes up to 4 at once; at other times, they don't turn when you try 5, 6, or 7 times, although they “jiggle”, annoying the eyes. When you swipe for a drop down menu, how hard you swipe (or how long?) brings up different menus. And you must be very insistent and persistent if you want the dictionary for a particular word in a book. Often, although I have my print size set larger than average, it centers on a different, nearby word. By the time the dictionary comes up (with super-tiny, fine, print) I've completely lost the train of thought in the sentence.I haven't found a “collection” or folder feature for my library, as the 3G had. The feature of showing 9 book covers instead of a title list is impossible – imagine that less than 1/9 th of the screen is a book cover, then 1/3 of that space has a title – only an average of 4 short words appear, and so tiny I can't read some even with a magnifying glass! A friend was able to show me that I could have a title list instead, but that change disappeared as soon as it powered down, and he can't remember how he did it, nor can I find it.I paid extra for “no ads”, but when 1/3 to 1/2 my screen is taken up by “recommended for you”, I consider that advertising even though it's not inside a book I'm trying to read.I like to listen to stories when I go to bed. When text-to-speech is activated, nearly ANY movement whatsoever causes it to turn back off, such as ever so slowly and carefully putting the tablet down. (No, I'm not accidentally touching the screen, in fact I closed the cover to prevent that.) If it plays for a while (I haven't pinpointed the actual amount of time), speech can only be turned off by powering down to reboot!But the most disappointing feature by far is the fact that the highly reflective screen can't be used outside at ALL, even in deep shade, OR, even less than 1 foot from a window! I did most of my reading on the enclosed patio, but now all I can see out there is my own face – you can't even determine if the tablet is on or off. Likewise the camera is useless outside, you have NO clue whether you've managed to center your subject.So, what started out as a reasonable $75 for a hand-held computer / camera / e-reader ended up being $150+ and I still can't read or listen to a book without a ton of frustration, or take a photo outside. After two months spent getting additional required equipment, learning, and “practicing”, it's too late to return it, so it will just be sitting here gathering dust. It is not a pleasure or a reward to use, it's a PUNISHMENT! And $150 was not a small amount for me. It's back to paperbacks from the swap shop for my summer reading. I don't recommend this model for anyone.

title: Do not gift this! It ships with full account information and credit card access wide open! No password required! Jerks!
body: I purchased this tablet for a friend of a friend two years ago. I traveled from Montana to Nevada to visit my granddaughter on her birthday. While there I stayed with a friend, her boyfriend and his son. I had never met the boyfriend or son before or since but purchased the tablet as thanks for their hospitality. I found out today, unbelievably, that although I gifted this unopened-completely untouched Amazon shipped it to me "registered to me" attached to my account AND Amazon credit card. Full access, no password required! My friend has passed away sometime ago. So for two years, someone I barely know has been ordering from Amazon on my account without ever even having a password. Amazon made absolute no mention of this when I purchased the tablet. In fact when I discovered it today and tried to rectify the situation I was told that with the "SET UP" instructions it says "you may need to sign out of registered account and sign on to your own Amazon account". I was also told by Neil with exclamatory punctuation that it was "my fault, you wouldn't sell a phone without resetting it right? NO!" Well first NEIL, I've never had reason to sell a phone, I'm older, not all that tech savvy and had ZERO expectation of this policy-why would I? I never actually touched the tablet,, I wrapped it and gifted it without a clue. Also I have bought tablets for my grand kids from Walmart-they sure didn't attach my Walmart credit card number, wide open, no password required for any random person to start ordering from my account. I still do not know the extent of the damage. What I found buried in my statement today were two monthly charges (TWO YEARS WORTH) for access to digital content. I've had credits for digital content for using no rush shipping and not used them. Never have I purchased digital content. My other credit card companies prudently watch for unusual activity and advise me when they see something like that. Not Amazon-they are apparently so huge and successful they have no interest in looking out for their customers. I'm a prime member, with an Amazon credit card (bears repeating) and have been a loyal and active Amazon account holder since 2011! I had a two hour standoff with customer service today! They remain steadfast in their insistence on holding me financially responsible for information I was never given. It's not like I missed it in the fine print, no, it is simply an obscure policy they expect everyone to be aware of. I told them this is the most outrageous retail experience I have ever been victim to in my life. I am not exaggerating now, they once shipped me a recliner with the back missing and offered to give me a 40% discount as they figured the seat comprised appx. 60% of the total chair! Even THAT did not top this. UP YOURS AMAZON!I do need edit my remarks, I've been to hell and back over this issue but feel assured two later the matter has been put to rest. So 5/4 during my initial contact with Amazon customer service I was transferred several times. One CSR assured that the tablet in question had been deregistered, an hour later another CSR urged me twice to change my password as it had NOT been deregistered. Yesterday when I tried to open an app on my personal Fire tablet I found IT had been deregistered. I knew I had to have another go at customer service but was absolute loathe to do so. SO, this morning I FORCED down the bitter pill, contacted customer service again and WOW! was treated fairly, promptly, professionally. My many concerns were reinvestigated, (turns out the stranger still had access today) BUT its certainly deregistered now, digital content apps have been disabled, I received a refund for 5 months of PBS kids, and 7 months of Curiosity Stream-the remainder of the expense applied as an account credit (yet to be confirmed), I still have the tedious task of searching two years of order history, but with account secured-much better Amazon. I confirmed it does automatically ship attached to your account and with open store card access-it will be called my your name. Mine is named Kippis fire, as was the gifted tablet. So if you do buy this deregister, or don't leave it laying around, OR if you plan to give it as a gift, know that without ever opening the box the tablet is programmed. So open the box-examine the set up instructions follow those so you don't end up in this position. THE MAIN REASON FOR THE EDIT WAS TO ASSURE THAT AMAZON HAS TAKEN RESPONSIBLITY AND RECTIFIED THE SITUATION I WAS IN :) Good day everyone!

title: I wanted to love it...
body: I requested and received a Kindle from my wife as a birthday gift. I wanted to love this device; conceptually it is something I have desired for a long time. It turned out to be a disappointment to both myself (because the device didn't live up to expectations) and to my wife (because I ended up wanting to return the gift that I asked her for). I've read many reviews that complained about button placement and the overall shape of the device. I had expected that these complaints were execrations. Wrong! If anything they fail to properly illuminate how problematic the design is. It is physically impossible to hold the device without repeatedly mashing the page forward/back buttons. If you hold the reader at the bottom, in the right hand (as one would hold a paperback) the point at the lower right of the device pushes painfully in to your palm. Secondly the display... I spend my life working in front of PC displays. I love reading but dread recreational reading on a monitor. E-Ink is a superb answer or should be. I was under the impression that the kindle used the exact same sourced display as the Sony reader. Now I am not so sure. The kindle seems to have a far grayer background vs. what I remember the Sony had. This of course leads to a diminishment of the available contrast between the background and the text. With my eyes this is problematic, within half an hour of reading on this device I had a blazing eye strain headache. I did however read far longer on a Sony reader in a local bookstore with no ill effects. Wispernet... The promise of untethered access, to an endless supply of reading material. WHEN IT WORKS... Let me explain. I own a Sprint EVDO smart phone. My smart phone in my left hand had an EVDO connection with a full 5 bars of signal strength. The Kindle in my right hand was bouncing between 1 bar of 1x (1xRTT Data: Much slower connectivity then EVDO) and NOTHING. I would get constant error dialogues that no connection was available while browsing the Kindle store. Meanwhile I could play a streaming video on the EVDO smart phone, no signal issues at all. By the way I am on Long Island in New York, where we are completely saturated with wireless, the issue is NOT the wireless provider. I could overlook all of these shortcomings, I want a device like this so badly I could but not at the current price. This is a two-fold issue. Firstly the device itself... The most expensive parts of the device would be the display, the cellular radio and the battery, combined and in bulk together cost far less than $100. Yes I understand that currently it's a niche product and there are design costs to recoup. However the sale cost vs. the production cost just doesn't seem realistic. That combined with the second price issue, the cost of books it is downright abusive. Printing, Binding, Shipping, Cataloging and Inventorying physical books is an expensive process. ALL of that is eliminated in the electronic process. Lookup almost any title that is available in the Kindle format vs. the physical, in most cases the e-book is within 80 to 90% of the cost of the physical book. So almost none of the expense saved by the publisher, is enjoyed by the consumer. Plus of course with a physical book I have an almost indestructible (with care) object that that will always have some intrinsic value. I can lend a book to someone, I could give it away or sell it. Not so with the e-book. The draconian DRM won't allow that. I challenge anyone at Amazon or representing the publishers of e-books to justify their price. Any book more than 6 months old should not cost more than $3 to 5 dollars, even at that price the profit margin would be grossly higher then what they earn on a paperback. So you have an awkward uncomfortable device, at an unfathomably high price. The cost of media is also high; it costs nearly the same as a physical book but is loaded with DRM that strips you of the freedom that a regular book inherently grants you. Yet I still want one. If any one of these objections were corrected, I could live with the rest. As it stands now, my Kindle is re-boxed and ready to be handed to UPS for a return. My greatest regret is I never had the opportunity to see one in person before I told my wife I wanted one, if I had, I wouldn't have had to disappoint her, by returning what she thought was a perfect birthday gift.

title: Not going there.  No way.
body: First, the positive: saving trees and paper is a wonderful thing. I hope someday someone somewhere finally, at long last, catches a clue and makes a reader the consumer actually wants.Now, the negatives:1. The thing is monstrously expensive, and for no reason. All it is is an e-reader. It has no other features. For $400 I can buy another five or six bookcases from Craigslist - recycling is the way to go - and be able to double my already very sizable library of wonderful, wonderful books.2. Proprietary format. When are you guys going to learn? DRM and proprietary format is never, ever, ever going to work on a wide scale. The recording industry and people like Apple have been battling this for a long time, because consumers are NOT happy with these restrictions. I have flatly refused to buy anything like the iPod or the iPhone and similar equipment because of their highly restrictive proprietary nonsense, and this drives a huge number of consumers away. The outcry is there for even the deafest ears to hear - but apparently the publishing, music, and movie industries are still convinced they can make it work. You can't. It won't work, and the tide is slowly changing.I want to read every book I have in any format I want. There is simply no way I will ever pay any money at all for a reader that forces me to use a proprietary format. If I could read anything from the multitudinous web sources for online books, if this thing supported the Audible format, if it was open-source and user-friendly, I might consider paying about $100 for it. But probably not, because there's no way to connect it to my computer. Whose idea was this?3. WiFi is fantastic, but I want PC connectivity to store my books when the memory runs out or when I want to rotate my library, or if I simply want to read the books I buy on my computer instead of the Kindle reader.4. No backlight option. I love the idea of reading from a more natural page, but at least give me the option for backlighting. An external light is not good enough.5. $10 per ebook? With $10 and a couple of weekends I can buy 20-30 actual books - classic and contemporary - from the local Salvation Army or Goodwill stores, all of them in great condition, all of them recycled. Your pricing overall is way out of hand. Not a smart move. A greedy one, but not a smart one. You think you're being clever, comparing $10 to the cost of an average hardcover, but most people don't buy hardcovers because they're huge, bulky, and expensive. Even brand-new paperbacks cost less than your ebooks. And let's be frank here: out of that $10 almost NOTHING is going to the writer. Terrible.6. Textbooks. The textbook industry is out of control, sucking the blood out of the average college student by charging INSANE prices for their books. The bookseller industry needs to have a sharp sit-down and talking-to with the textbook people. Regardless of whether or not one is in school, textbooks are criminally overpriced.They need to be on board and EVERY textbook publisher needs to make ALL their textbooks available in reasonably-priced e-formats, both to save the student the multitudes of cash they are forced to spend and to save them from having to lug around large numbers of very heavy books all over the place. As an on-again, off-again student myself, I know I would jump at this. I can't even imagine how a full-time student manages that kind of load on a daily basis.I would ADORE being able to finally get a decent ebook reader. I'm waiting to spend my money on one. So far, nobody on Amazon's side of the industry has managed to understand that doing the exact same thing as everyone else has already tried - and failed - to do is simply not going to work. Expensive, proprietary-format-only hardware and expensive, proprietary-format-only reading material is never going to work. For $400, I expect to get at least a couple hundred ebooks OF MY OWN CHOOSING for no further charge.Until then, you can keep your expensive, largely useless toy for yourself, and I'll spend my $400 on real books I can read anywhere, any time, without having to worry about the battery running out.Too bad. I expected more from you guys. I guess I'll just have to keep waiting.
