title: Disappointed in Amazon
body: I would give this a zero if I could. For the price and for the first several months, we loved the device. We couldn't believe what a great tablet we got for less than $50. Well, it turns out we didn't pay $50 for a tablet that would last for a couple of years; we paid $50 for a tablet that would only last a few months. After about 8 months of light usage, my mother in law started to have issues where the Fire would no longer charge. She asked me for help. I was able to charge it no problem, just assumed it was her, and walked her through how to charge it. A couple of weeks later, she's back with the same problem. I've been a technology profession for almost 25 years (with 15 years of direct tech support and troubleshooting experience). When she brought it back to me, I quickly learned it wasn't her. After I had the same issue with the charger and usb cable that came with the Fire, I tried another usb cable and Amazon PowerStep cable that we use for our Kindle Paperwhites. It charged perfectly with those. I thought the original cable and charger must have gone bad; however, I tried them on my Paperwhite, and they worked fine. A week or so later, my mother in law is back with the same issue again. I tried another cable, and it wouldn't charge. I had a busy week at work, so I forgot about it for a couple of days, and then when I tried to charge it again with the original cable and charger, it charged fine. Rinse and repeat the same scenario multiple times over the past few weeks. I followed the troubleshooting steps I found on Amazon (hold the power button for 40 seconds to reset, etc); nothing helped, so I emailed Amazon customer support. They assured me they would get it resolved but I needed to contact their tech support via chat or phone. I opted for chat. What a worthless, frustrating experiencing. I spelled out exactly what was happening with the device as well as the troubleshooting steps I had taken. The first tech (Rohit) wasn't interested in working with me; he just wanted to walk through the steps. When I explained that based on what it was doing was pretty much the exact same issue that I've read online is pretty common with Amazon Fire tablets (weak micro soldering of the USB micro port causes the contacts to come loose) and I wasn't sure how is troubleshooting steps would help, Rohit told me he needed to leave the conversation and elevate it to another tech. The next tech, Abhishek, pretty much repeated the same scenario. I finally stopped him and explained that I am an IT professional with 25 years of experience and that I've done the basic troubleshooting (and that I had done the troubleshooting with the previous tech in the same chat that this tech had claimed he read). After about 45 minutes of my time wasted, Abhishek finally admitted that my Fire needed to be replaced by Amazon. Now if I would have just gone through the above and then gotten the device replaced, I'd be a little annoyed, but still a happy Amazon customer. Not at all. Abhishek offered to take care of the issue for me with a replacement for the low price of $44.99. Exactly $5 less than I had paid for a new one when the issue is clearly the result of poor workmanship of the product. Abhishek explained that I was out of the 3 month warranty. When I expressed frustration with that he said they could replace it for me for $39.99. When I still expressed disappointment in Amazon, he offered to instead replace it with Fire HD 6 for $59.99 that I might be happier with because it comes with a one year warranty. While appreciate Abhishek's desire to please the customer with the only options he had, I am extremely disappointed that Amazon has this as a known issue (do a Google search if you'e not sure if this is a known issue) and is unwilling to make it right with a device that I've owned for 9 months. Since it's out of warranty, I would have been willing to pay something (I don't know; half price) for a replacement unit. I feel like the $5 off and then $10 off offers were slaps in the face by Amazon. I have been a loyal Prime customer for over 10 years (I've been signed up since the beginning). It's disappointing to see that they are willing to peddle junk products like this without any desire to make a common issue right.

title: Returned it and buying another Sony Reader
body: I replaced my Sony Reader with a hipper new Amazon Kindle, and I'm totally disappointed. The Kindle has a few good ideas, but it is terribly executed. I'm returning it today (something Amazon is making pleasant enough) and buying another Reader.Read on for more detail...About 6 months ago, I bought a used Sony Portable Reader from a friend of a friend. I travel a lot, and have a lot of open-format stuff I wanted to read (pdfs, html manuals, etc). I've always through electronic ink was interesting technology, and buying used appealed to my cheaper inclinations.Fast forward to a a month ago, and having an electronic book has changed my life. The Sony fits in my bag nicely, reading it is quite pleasant, and I can easily carry 50 books with me at any time. It has changed the way I read, allowing me to have several books going at once and switch between them based on my mood, something I used to do in high school. libprs500 (now Calibre) is pleasant to use on the mac and open source. I even used the Sony software to buy a few closed-format bestsellers at reasonable prices.Unfortunately, my Reader broke, possibly due to it's previous owner or just due to being dropped. It will no longer hold a charge or boot properly. I took it apart, following helpful Flickr directions for prs-505 disassembly, but to no avail. If anyone has a project that would benefit from the eink components, which seem undamaged, let me know.Since I have a vacation coming up, and I'd so enjoyed the experience of having one, I decided to replace it. And being a consummate gadget shopper, I went looking for other options. I settled on the Kindle as the most advanced option, attracted by it having internet access and a way to buy and load books that would require Windows. So I went ahead and primed one.I received it the morning I left for a trip to New York on Acela. This gave me some time to get started using it. My first reaction is that it is really bulky. It barely fits into the pouch of my laptop bag, while the Reader is about the same size as the Moleskine I keep there all the time. The Kindle came with a leather cover, but it doesn't really fit into it well, and that just ads more bulk. The cover has a big elastic to hold it closed The on/off switch is on the back, where it is blocked by the cover. In contrast, the Reader has a cover that clips on, is held closed by magnets, and a switch on the top that is easy to access. Unlike the Sony, the Kindle doesn't really charge of USB, so you have to carry a silly power brick.The Kindle could survive all these misfeatures, if the reading experience were good. It is not. I don't know how they tested this thing, but anyone with normal sized hands can't find a way to hold it without accidentally hitting buttons. They claim it can be held with both hands, but I have to rest it on a surface, or hold it gingerly with my right hand only. The big fear of the designers seems to have been that people couldn't find the next page button, so they made it take up one whole side. A second next page button and a previous page button consume the other side. Which means if you want to hold the book naturally in one hand, you are going to accidentally palm those buttons, and lose your place.The keyboard, which is necessary to make the buying and web experience possible, also takes up a lot of space on the device and risks accidental key presses. There is really only one point on the front of the device you can safely rest your thumb. And holding the same position for long periods of reading isn't any fun.On the bright side, the EVDO is a cool feature, and I like the idea of devices that come with infinite bandwidth. The scroll-wheel menu interface is also good, certainly better than the Sony ten-button menu interface. But these don't make up for the difficult reading experience. And with a new iPhone coming, I'll have a portable web browser anyway. I just need my electronic book to be a good book, not a digital convergence device.I'd love to see a better E-Ink book than the Sony Reader. But the first generation Kindle is certainly not it. Don't buy one.

title: This device is HORRIBLE. I had the original Fire
body: Ugh... This device is HORRIBLE. I had the original Fire, which I loved, and used mostly for reading. It was nice that you could watch some movies on it, but it was getting old and I saw that this new Fire was on sale, so I thought I'd splurge for a new toy. What a waste!* The video doesn't work. You watch for a few minutes and then it tanks and just stalls at "buffering." It's not my internet connection because when I try the exact same thing on my phone a minute later, I have no problem.* The ads are relentless. I bought the version with ads, because that was what was on sale. OMG - you are practically attacked by ads. The lock screen has ads. Each "section" (i.e., video, apps, books, etc.) is covered in ads. I have an extensive book library, but only one-quarter of the screen shows any of my books. The other three-quarters of the screen is covered in "other things you might be interested in." You literally have to click through twice to get to your own library. When you finally get there, it's clear Amazon gave no thought to it because the interface is ugly. I think the savings between the no-ads and the ads version was only $12 -- spend the money!! This bombardment of ads isn't worth it...* Did I mention how annoying and relentless the ads are? Let me just reiterate -- OMG! Some are videos, so when you swipe up to get off the lock screen to type in your password, the video auto-launches, so you are subjected to some ridiculous ad for 30 seconds before you can even get to your home screen. And how are they choosing the ads? Clearly, their algorithm is messed up because the last couple of days the ad has been showing those rover wheelchair things marketed to the elderly. Not sure what in my purchasing profile makes amazon think I'd be the right target market for that. Sports equipment or baby diapers, maybe, but mobility chairs for the elderly? Not hardly...* The one-click is ridiculous. In addition to the bombardment of ads, the screen sensitivity is screwy. The whole right-hand side can't scroll. It just thinks you're clicking. So when you're trying to move away from some relentless ad, it takes you TO the ad instead. And since the kids' profile and parental controls are screwy, my kids have purchased a whole bunch of junk on accident. Thankfully, I can get a refund from Amazon if I notice and tell them quickly. It'd be better if they just fixed the system so it wasn't so easy to make an accidental purchase.* The screen sensitivity is off. The right-hand side of the screen is too sensitive, so when you try to scroll, it thinks you're clicking, so I've opened the same book repeatedly, just trying to scroll down. You end up having to find some creative scrolling strategies just to see your library.* The family profiles are useless. You can set up a kids profile, but you can't stream music from the kids' profile. They can access it from my profile, but then that defeats the whole purpose of parental controls on a kids' profile. I can set up additional parental controls on my profile, but then anytime I want to access my own stuff, I have to go into the settings and change everything, then remember to change them back before the kids grab it again.* The kids' profile still doesn't block all accidental purchasing, even when you add parental controls. There are certain items they can still click on and download without permission. The only thing that seems to work consistently is the email I get when someone purchases something. I've spent a ridiculous amount of time with Amazon getting refunds for junk my kids accidentally purchased because their settings are ridiculous.* The kids' profile only works in horizontal/landscape mode, rather than vertical/portrait mode. This isn't a terribly big deal, but, why? So when you're reading, rather than being able to hold it like a normal book, you have to hold it like you're watching a movie. Clearly, amazon designed that assuming kids will only play games and watch movies. Guess amazon doesn't think children read.

title: . . . a baby step in the right direction
body: I don't own a Kindle. But I'm a tech nerd and a voracious consumer of books. A few concerns about the Kindle that I have:- - no touch screen/wacom tablet? The breakthrough feature of the iPhone was it's interactivity. The Kindle looks like a menu/clicker driven device. Nowadays, that seems like a cumbersome interface.- - on-screen annotations or notes? I don't see this mentioned in the promo material. (Of course, it could be that notes are added via keypad and physical buttons.) The instability of electronic text is one of its greatest features - - ability to modify, markup, annotate, highlight etc. This seems like a pretty simple presentation technology - - not a major shift in bookreading. (iRex's Iliad is a nifty device that sports some of these features.)- - no images? If anything, the internet has promoted a new visual culture where text and image work in close proximity to communicate meaning. Can you really "read" a newspaper without photos or layout? And, what is a magazine without graphics and page display? These kinds of texts use the pagespace of text and image to construct meaning and afford ways of reading.- - interoperability. That's a big, hairy word - - but what it basically means is that the Kindle doesn't appear to be able to handle the many kinds of text formats that we work with all day and night - - .pdf, .html, .doc, .rtf - - to name a few. Why go halfway? If you're going to change the technology of the book - - change it to expand its ability to handle the whole new world of textuality, to exploit the new document environments that we swim through.- - the codex book is a great technology - - not only for its efficiency but for its social aspects. I.e. I've just re-read Don De Lillo's White Noise for the third or fourth time, and each time I pass it on to a friend. Friends loan me books all the time. Looks like I wouldn't be able to share books with friends or colleagues with the Kindle.- - pricing. The device is way overpriced. I can get an iPhone now for almost half the price of a Kindle - - granted they are two different devices and assemblages - - but when I think about my "technology" or "gadget" budget - - which device am I going to spring for?- - access structure. Someone else pointed out how cheap it is to digitize a book. And, free online versions of newspapers, blogs, and other texts are already available. Look at it this way: am I willing to pay ten bucks (plus cost of device) to experience the codex book (or other texts) in a new way? If the experience of the e-book is worthwhile, maybe. But the Kindle doesn't seem to offer much in the way of a better, richer experience. More portable - - yes. But the past couple of decades have shown that most people, myself included, will need a very convincing price point to abandon one technology (codex book) for another (e-book).With all this in mind - - I think the most valuable market for the Kindle isn't folks who read NY Times bestsellers in airport lounges, but college (and maybe high school) students. Another reviewer noted how many pound of books he carts around for his classes. The Kindle could be a great device to simplify and expand the use of the book in the classroom. For instance, students downloading course texts, rather than waiting in endless lines and buying wrong editions etc. Allowing professors and students to work together on texts, even perhaps allowing all to work (annotating, noting, etc.) one "master-text" to be shared. Saving countless trees lost to publishers' drive to change editions to recharge falling sales. Allowing for really customizable anthologies. Etc. Speaking as a professor and higher educator, I know there is some serious gold in those hills. But the Kindle will have to adapt a bit more to get a crack at it.All in all, looks like an interesting device. Limited, but interesting. Hopefully it's the first step and there will be many more to come.

title: Skip it and save your money for something better. You get what you pay for.
body: ***EDIT 4-26-2017*** I have used this Kindle Fire maybe 5-10 times since its purchase. It now refuses to charge because the charge port has come loose. How many defective units did Amazon ship? I got on with support, and they said they will be unable to repair or exchange the item. Instead, they are refunding me 100% of my purchase price. Pretty amazing!---I was perhaps naively optimistic when I ordered my six-pack of these for a paltry $263 after tax. My intention was to keep one for myself and give away the other five for the holidays. I'm still on the fence about whether I can give away something so fantastically lackluster and keep a good conscience.Now, I have used many, many devices since the mobile revolution came around. Every iPhone, dozens of Android phones, a bunch of Android tablets, and a bunch of iPads as well. I feel like I have a pretty good grip on what should be expected for a modern-day device. However, what I expected from Amazon this time around - which was to break the mold of what constitutes a $50 device - was shattered upon arrival.Let's start with quality control. This is actually the biggest problem and I want to get it out of the way first. I opened my "me" device out of the six I had coming, and upon turning it on, I immediately noticed dead pixels on the screen. It was like a hole in the LCD where the backlight shone through. I counted FIVE of them on the device I just turned on. Amazon has promised a replacement, but it shows in my order list as a 'refurbished' model. I will see today whether it comes in retail packaging or not... I hope it does, since I should be getting same-for-same exchanges for something defective right out of the box. Lastly, I went online to post about my issue and found at least two other people on a popular message board who also got Kindle Fire 7" with defective displays. Not boding well here.Secondly, let's talk about how slow the device is. Especially on first use. Remember receiving that PS3 for Christmas, only to find that you had to update not only the system software, but every single game that came with it, before you could even play it? That's pretty much the experience I had out-of-box with the Fire 7". It was at least 15 minutes of updating and ambiguous load screens before I could do anything with the device at all. What a buzzkill. I wonder if Amazon caught something in beta testing that just *had* to be patched on brand new devices. On "Android" devices, or Android-based devices, I'm used to the device needing to "settle" before it can be used without UI lag. Unfortunately, the UI lag on the device persists through use, despite the quad-core 1.3ghz processor.Next, let's talk about the screen. I've almost never seen a muddier, dimmer screen on a mobile device. I know this was only $50, but you really expect me to enjoy a game, movie, or book on a screen that practically gives me a headache each time I use it? The low resolution was expected, but the quality of the color and backlighting really leaves something to be desired. Want to get a clue about how to manufacture good screens, even low-res ones? Look toward Apple.I wouldn't say all of this based on the use of one device. But, once I found one defective device and got confirmation of a replacement, I immediately opened the next one to test it out. No screen defects, but the same issues of screen quality and slowness persist.I have to say, I took a chance with these and came out on the raw end of it. The family members I was planning on giving these to, suffice to say, will have a higher expectation for quality and usability than I will be gifting them with this device. I hate to say that we've been spoiled by higher-end Android and iDevices, but this device feels like a step back in time, by about 5 years. Pass.

title: If you want to keep feeling good about Amazon.com don't purchase the Kindle!
body: I love to read and I thought, "How Green is this?" I was so excited. I loved Amazon.com. I had been with them since their inception as a bookstore. But four hundred dollars was a lot of money for me. And I never buy the first version of anything. So I waited.Then Oprah did a show on the Kindle in October 2008. She talked about all of the features and function and offered fifty dollars off if you bought it that weekend. My birthday was coming up and so, I took the plunge.My Kindle arrived at the beginning of the next week, which was the last week in Oct.I pulled it out of the box and poured over it like it was heaven sent. It was going to be the object that went everywhere with me, that I trusted to hold my most favorite reading materials. I was so ready for it to change my life.But then I began to use it. Reality seeped in and she wasn't dressed for a party.1. First of all: Whispernet - Our Sprint coverage is fine in this area, and sometimes whispernet works and sometimes it doesn't. You takes your chances and you rolls the dice....and if it does work it is only occasionally within the sixty seconds they tell you about. I have tried this all over my house. On occasion I have had to download a book in order to get it onto the Kindle.2. The cover is romantic looking with the little band that closes over it like a journal to protect the Kindle and it does keep it from falling out. But for reading purposes, its very clumsy and the reader does not disappear in your hands.3. My Kindle has a problem in that you have to jiggle the wire to get the reader to start charging. Jiggle the wire, the light finally comes on and you are in business. I called the tech dept on this and they told me to keep jiggling. I called them recently and they told me to send it in and they would send me a refurbished one at their discretion. Which means I guess how much jiggling is required to get the thing to charge, because they do not repair their Kindles from what they told me. The techs are also very rude and condescending. The first one made me feel like I was wasting his time.4. I keep accidently flipping pages and so I lose patience and I have yet to make it through one whole book.5. This reader is a device that keeps making money for Amazon and therefore they should have a better support team and method for handling unhappy customers. The Boston Globe has stated that The Kindle II and Kindle downloads will make the company a billion dollars by 2010.6. If you dare to express your opinion about the Kindle I on any of these forums you will be eaten up and spit out by "other" posters. I wouldn't be surprised if you clicked on comments below this and found out exactly what I mean. How cool would that be if writing this actually stopped them from doing it?But honestly, if you want to keep your faith in Amazon.com don't buy the Kindle in whatever incarnation it shows up in. I loved this company, spent all my money with them in the past and even had a link for them on my website. I am sincerely grieving over my "innocence lost" with Amazon.comP.S. 7. If you are going to have a brand spanking new version of a product in three months, why do you have an icon like Oprah build a show about the first one that indicates the Kindle I will be around a long time. I wasn't upset about the Kindle II. I was upset about the way it was handled as I don't have money just sitting around nor the time to have a whole "auction" or sale for a product I just purchased that won't sell because its faulty in the first place.That's it..You decide. But I wouldn't invite anyone to this h-e-double hockey sticks, not fun and even less book reading gets done here.

title: Awesome price, terrible technology
body: I really wanted to love this tablet. The price is right, I have a serious loyalty to Amazon, and it seemed perfect for my needs.... but seriously I don't trust this tablet as far as I can throw it.If you are intending on reading books and doing very light use for other tasks, it might be perfect. I planned to do light internet browsing for myself, and having it for easy quick entertainment for my son while we're out and about (spare me the guilt of screen time - modern times bring modern challenges and modern solutions).On a single adult profile, the tablet is sufficient. It has a slow processor, clunky interface, and is not really user friendly; there are sub menus and pages to swipe through, you cant just make a home screen with everything you need a la an android phone.Adding on a child's profile throws a whole new wrench in the system and is what earns the whopping 1 star. I've been plagued with problems ever since creating my kiddo a profile on this dang thing. Here are a couple issues I've encountered: - It shuts off randomly. In the middle of my kiddo playing an app, it just shuts off. No power button press, no overload of RAM, no asking to restart.... it in the middle of the alphabet song it just shuts off. And it is a slllowwww reboot to get back up and running. that 23 seconds might be no big deal to some people, but when a 20 month old is sitting on the edge of their seat after singing along "H, I, J, K...." thats a long long long 23 seconds. Insert meltdown while waiting on the check at a restaurant!- If you close an app while a video is loading on the kids profile, the video will still load after the app is closed, and then it uninstalls that app from the kids profile. This happens every single time we use the PBS Kids app and any Fisher Price kids app that plays music. Kiddo accidentally exits to home screen while a video is loading, then the audio plays, but wait, the app is no longer installed and you have to switch back to adult to re-add the app to the kids profile - meanwhile, Curious George is playing but the imagery is no where and non-openable, across ALL profiles on the tablet. You think "oh, I'll just play a movie real quick and it'll stop that process and then I'll pause the movie and we'll be good" No. Then you have audio for Jurassic World and Curious George playing simultaneously. So then we have to shut the whole thing down. 23 seconds for reboot, meltdown, etc etc etc.I find it concerning that somehow my kiddo can uninstall apps on his side, but I have no idea how he does it because I (the adult!) can't figure out how he does it. The kids profile should *only* be editable from the adult's, but that is simply not what I experience, and I certainly couldn't trust an older kiddo since they would have the ability to circumvent using only the apps I chose.Worth mentioning, I've actually tried to work with Amazon Support to resolve my issues, but all they do is ask a few questions and transfer you on without anyone who has a clue on how to resolve it "Oh, this is definitely app support's department" "This is app support, but that is absolutely a kindle software issue" "Yes, you've reached kindle software, but this sounds more like a developer concern issue..." etc etc etc. Just pass the buck until the customer disconnects....This was a gift last year, so sadly I can't return it, but if I had purchased it myself it would be looooong gone. My HP touchpad from the fire sale, while completely unsupported and several years out of date technologically, is far superior to this tablet. I really truly love Amazon, but dang is this is a crappy piece of tech.

I'm here to help you today.Let me connect you to a member of our kindle team. It will only take a moment.A Customer Service Associate will be with you in a moment.You are now connected to Akshay from Amazon.comMe: I would like my kindle replaced or fixed.Akshay: HelloMay I know are you referring toMe: yesAkshay: May I know what is the issue exactly with the kindle?Me: My new kindle won't shut down. It will only take a moment.A Customer Service Associate will be with you in a moment.You are now connected to Amazon from Amazon.comAmazon: Hello, my name is Sowmith.I understand that your Fire tablet named "" won't shut down although you're selecting the shut button on the screen.Me:My new kindle won't shut down. It doesn't respond to my touching the shut down button. My new kindle won't shut down. It doesn't respond to my touching the shut down button. the screen is not frozen. When I press the shut down button on the touch screen the device will not does not shut down.I have another kindle that shuts down. I know how the device is suppose to workCorrectAmazon: May I know if there are any other touch screen issues that you've experienced with this device apart from this shut down problem?Me: no other issues that I am aware ofAmazon: Okay. We'll try some troubleshooting steps on this deviceMe: Look I don't want to try trouble shooting. I want to know if Amazon is willing to replace or fix the device. It looks like you sold me a lemon. I can understand that when I mail it back if Amazon finds that somehow the issue is my fault, but I'm a good customer and I have been sold something that does not work. The device not being able to shut down effect the power and battery and how much time I am allowed to use the device. I never thought that Amazon would sell me junk, I didn't think that because the kindle is only $50 that Amazon would think that meant that it is okay to send me broken equipment advertised as new.Amazon: I'm sorry but it's not possible for us to replace the device without performing necessary troubleshooting steps on this device. If there's any issue with the device, we'd provide some troubleshooting steps to get the issue resolved. However, if the troubleshooting steps fail to acknowledge the issue, then we'll proceed with further possibilities in this case accordingly. Also, please mind that, devices which are bought from and delivered by Amazon are brand new ones and not are used ones. The device is prone to technical glitches and hardware problems. Hence we're providing a free device support to ensure that our customer's inquires are resolved.

title: $50 tablet won't get google play store or my bank app that i got it for
body: additional comments:I am modifying this review and it reflects my overall dissatisfaction with all android and Apple tablet products because they are all essentially copies of each other with the same shortcomings: don't believe the claims about sharing all your stuff with your tablet, that's from it, but not to it. If i connect it to my computer, the Amazon tablet has it so all I can get off it are pictures &I can't transfer anything to it that way like I could with a computer! And the transfer methods they provide don't seem to work, such as the various "clouds". It takes like days and days and then to upload 8 gigs of music and then days and days of being tied up to download it making the operation unfeasible. I tried the Windows Cloud and amazingly there is no pause and if I stop it, there is no continuing it. So I cannot get my music from my PC to my tablet. I have my music on my PC in Itunes, but on my tablet I cannot get Itunes. I cannot bank with my tablet because there is not an amazon app from my bank. amazon and apple are all copied versions of each other creating the ultimate wallled (for profit) gardens for themselves instead of a usable, customer friendly device like they all advertise. So this rating is for all Android based tablets which apparently I find extremely less wonderful than most less discerning, less demanding consumers who invariably argue with me and defend this garbage and ultimately refuse to listen or acknowledge my experience as if their life depends on them blindly defending corporations and their products at all costs..& THERE IS STILL NOT A FORWARD BUTTON on Lollipop like is found on ALL PCs and PC internet browsers since the beginning of multicolor windowed GUIs and I ask WTFudge is up with that? Well this is what is up: It is the ultimate proof of this Android and Apple crap is 1st foremost devised to imprison our electronic lives and make these corporations tons of money.Original review:As they said, how can you go wrong with a $50 tablet? Well one thing is to get rid of advertising screens, they want $15 more. Do the ads bother me much. No they don't. So it's worth the $15 savings. One thing though, forget Goggle Play apps. With it's tablets, Amazon uses a heavily modified android operating system that eliminates EVERYTHING Google and replaces it with Amazon versions. so ,like Google and Apple before it, Amazon, with it's tablets, seeks to corral users in a walled garden reminiscent of the old America On Line (AOL) & make money every single time we click on anything on the tablet with a total, Amazon experience with email, a play store, etc. But you can access your gmail on it from the Silk browser. Overall good performance for the money, no complaints, except, personally, I got it specifically for depositing pay checks in my bank account, which doesn't have an Amazon version of it's app. So since I cold not deposit my checks with it, I initiated a return, got a refund and was not required to return the tablet and so wound up getting it for free. The reason I put on the form for the refund (it could be anything) was "doesn't have the google play store." it is claimed on the internet that one can hack the tablets and get google play, but I tried it and google play installed but doesn't start. title: The honeymoon is over
body: While I started out delighted, the honeymoon is over and now I'm annoyed.Primarily, I'm offended at the shockingly crappy customer service I've received from Amazon. This is the same company that that has sent me personal messages and gone to great lengths to be sure I get my book on time or my music download glitch is resolved. I had some early-on technical issues with the Kindle that were not made clear in the documentation that shipped with the unit (which I read). When I asked these questions, I was only sent long pages from some other technical document, and I had to read through it in detail to find the answer to my specific question.Then I started running afoul Amazon's fanatic devotion to its Whispernet wireless broadband. We are currently out of the country for my husband's academic sabbatical. Before we left the country, I used Whispernet enough to see that it was a killer ap. It was part of the reason I chose a Kindle, even though we'd be out of the country where it doesn't work, because we'd be home in only 10 months. And when I bought the Kindle, everything I read or could see from Amazon indicated that with a bit less convenience, we'd be able to get everything we needed in file format to transfer to the Kindle via USB cable. Since then, despite having a lovely page on its site for the management of Kindle downloads, Amazon has started deliberately offering Whispernet-only downloads. When I first encountered this (free book samples), I sent a confused inquiry to customer service. I was sent another long document about book samples that seemed to indicate there was no regular download option. I wrote back saying that I couldn't use this out of the country and expressed frustration, and I was flat-out ignored. I can't even see reason Amazon has for offering Whispernet-only files in the first place. They already have the infrastructure for normal downloads, and sending a file wirelessly offers no extra copy-protection because I can just download the file from the Kindle back to my computer. The whole thing seems senseless.Now, admittedly we're a fringe case. But even though Amazon doesn't sell these things outside the US, one of its primary user demographics is frequent travelers. And believe it or not, there are still parts of the US where you can't get good Sprint coverage. I can't imagine we're the only users who benefit from non-Whispernet options. Offering an innovative new feature doesn't mean cutting users off from using the old way when they can't use the new way.And then there's the big picture. Amazon is offering a US-only product, and its lock into Whispernet on the Sprint mobile network means that it stays in the US or it radically changes its platform. (Maybe it will with Kindle 2 rumors that are now circulating.) Despite this, it's moving aggressively to enforce a proprietary lock on in-copyright, DRM-protected ebooks. Like Apple's iTunes before the DRM-free move, Amazon ebooks only read on the Kindle. Most insultingly, their proprietary format is just the licensable and well-accepted Mobipocket DRM format with a Kindle-only flag in the file header. So if you own a Kindle, you have all the software capability to purchase and read ebooks from a dozen established vendors, but Amazon is deliberately locking you into buying only from them. The fact that Sony has now abandoned its proprietary dreams to back a publisher-endorsed, licensable DRM format suddenly makes them into the angels and Amazon into the demons in this war.Now I plan to put the Kindle on eBay when we get home and sit back to see how the reader war unfolds.

title: Makes my fingers hurt a lot! Electrical issues?
body: One problem, and then another. Initially this review was titled "NO WAY TO GET OUT OF USER GUIDE??? UNUSABLE." The first paragraph below reflects this issue. Go to the last paragraph for the finger pain issue.Unbelievable. My first kindle finally arrived, and there are no written instructions--that part would be okay if the screens took you through start up successfully.. Working through the screens takes one through about four until we reach an absolute dead end. I can get between the "fire kids edition--setup your child's account" (I don't have a child and have no "no thanks" or exit option available on the kindle) and from "learn more" I can get into the user guide. But move around the user guide all I want, I can't exit it except to go backwards to the screens I've been to and they have no exits either. Did amazon sell me a kindle meant only for kids without telling me? I can't get to home or anywhere else to actually USE the kindle or download content. HUH?The day I wrote that, my initial review, was a very frustrating day. Finally I began googling, and found there was tech support which I called. It took a long time, but the person on the other end kept trying to figure it out, and apparently asking others, and eventually was able to remove the programming that he agreed shouldn't have been on there. He was helpful and kind. After he was done, the kindle worked properly in the sense of usability.The limited information that came with the kindle, a 4 in x 5 in card folded in half to make 4 pages, did not give any customer service or tech support numbers. The first 3 pages are pictures (no words) diagramming the ports, the swipe up finger gesture to unlock, and plugging in for recharging. Presumably one would check these things out on one's new kindle while looking at the pictures. On the back page in text too small for me to read without serious difficulty, it says to go online to read the terms and conditions of use BEFORE USE. If one were supposed to read that FIRST, it should have been on the front page and labeled "read first" rather than "terms and policies." So if one doesn't have eyes for very small print maybe one violates the warranty or return option, I don't know. I DO now see the word "devicesupport" in the terms and policy url, so I perhaps in this avenue one might have located support, it just wasn't given in that context, and it was given in the kind of tiny print, as opposed to large clear icons on the pictures that by comparison, suggest the back matter of far lessor importance, if any.After the day of help with it and posting my 1-star review, I did get a user guide by email, or anyway an email labeled such. I haven't actually referred to it, since I got started with the tech person. The problem I have with the kindle now, is that there is tingling my fingers get while handling, and then after a bit the pain my joints and fingers hurt experience from holding and after holding the kindle, and this doesn't readily go away. I tried reading about 6 hours ago, and my fingers are still hurting. If it weren't for this issue, I would love the kindle. But arthritis-like pain isn't a fair exchange for the privilege of reading. I will keep it to see if I can devise a kind of holder and stylus that will prevent that, but my biggest reason for keeping it is so that I, who really want a book reader, won't forget about this issue and end up ordering a more expensive one down the road only to be out some much greater expense for something not usable without health costs.

title: It's a Low Priced Tablet
body: I have 2 existing Kindle units. An HDX 7 and a HDX 8.9. Both are great and I must state that I set up and registered each without ANY difficulty. I had an earlier Kindle E-Reader second generation for about 4-5 years that I had to eventually dispose of due to it's ability not to hold a charge or to be able to charge it. That is a fair statement as the unit lasted a long time and was used a lot.My 2 existing HDX tablets are at this point great, but I thought that I would get the tablet that I am reviewing to be able to just use as an e-book and take wear and tear off of the 2 units I am now using.Such is not the case as it didn't work out too great. It came earlier today but I decided to wait a few hours as there were other things to do. It did not work out too good. It came with a 72 percent battery charge which should be more than enough to set up the unit.. Everything went down hill from there. It would not accept my password, etc. I was able to get it hooked up to my router and wireless connection. All I had to do was to touch, actually it took a lot more than a touch and the screen would go blank. I use a stylus and it works great on my other two HDX's at the moment.Then it started to get real hot! The battery level was down to about 50 percent. I reset my password on the Amazon website for all units and my desktop. It still did not work. It just kept stating that I had to reenter all the info.I phoned Amazon tech service, who I am beginning to lose faith in due to a major problem that I had a few months ago and I loss all of my documents and family photo's that I had stored on my hard drive on my HDX-8.9. Like a fool I had never backed up the lost items to a storage unit or cloud.Anyway, the tech service rep could not understand what was happening and what the problem was and I told the rep that I wanted to return it for a full credit, plus all of my award points that I had used.I was now switched over to his supervisor after being on the hold button for an excessive amount of time. The same drill. I was finally told that I would be able to return it but I was concerned about my personal information that I had both entered and other info that I made an attempt to enter. No problem, so I was told. But now the battery was at 0, zero, percent, all within 1 hour or so and I had the unit plugged into a good charging device and it stated that the device could not be reset to the factory default until at least a charge level of 30 percent was on the unit. Now it is at least 2 hours, it is still charging and I must state that I have never had such a slow charge on my other units. And it is still warm, not hot, and I have to go to a UPS store to return it and it is really a few hours and total aggravation as well as a trip to UPS because of the lithium battery.I am of the conviction that newer Amazon tablets are not of the quality and build of their earlier units.My big question is 'Why did Amazon stop the sale of HDX tablets?". They were great.All of this aggravation just to get an e-book? I have seen and tried them as well and they are not great as far as I am concerned.When a customer has AMD, aka poor vision, a good back lit unit is essential. Even the largest font (yes I was able to set that) is too small.I must also suggest that Amazon, and other corporations, pay attention to what the customer is asking for.I am being very kind in writing this review, but if I was able to give it 0 stars that is what I would do.Now it's back to see if there is enough of a charge to do a factory re-set.

title: The worst kindle I've ever had.
body: Let me begin by stating that I own the original Kindle Fire, the Kindle Fire HD, and the Fire Phone. All of which were wonderful.Though my HD was getting full, and I wanted a device that I could use for a specific purpose.I was attracted to this one due to it's low price, and it's SD card capabilities.Now I wish I had just used the money on another HD.The software itself resembles Androids too much in my opinion, which I personally don't like. (I am aware that kindle software is based off of Android, but I personally like the style that Kindle used, as well as the fact that theres no Google services. And it's a lot easier to use.) I specifically opt for kindle because they usually have a more distinct style, and I love the carousel feature.Until now.This device does not have carousel. Instead it has a bottom icon that shows a list of your last used apps, which is not what I personally like.I decided to ignore it because at first it only seemed like a slight inconvenience for me.But after noticing everything else wrong with the device, I can't seem to ignore something that I would actively use.There are pages to scroll sideways where it organizes books and excess things like video, and games, news ect. All of which I don't use. And cannot remove for space.I understand that some people may use them, but not everyone will, especially if they purchased the device for a specific reason. These things should be optional due to the space they take up.The search bar at the top is only really there for show, considering whenever I go to use it, it takes several minutes to load.The apps all take longer to load than they should.There are so many glitches, I will be typing something, and next thing you know it will highlight and then delete all of it.The screen is too sensitive when you don't need it to be, but then it will freeze.The device updates automatically, which a consumer should have the choice whether or not to update, and sometimes it will take ten minutes to power on for that reason.The photo editor tools are mediocre, which is something I cannot ignore because I use them for blending when I make artwork.The autocorrect doesn't make any sense. It rarely does a spell check in many apps that I use, but it will autocorrect a word like "and" to "AMD" never do I need such an acronym, nor do I need it replacing a part of speech. There is no way to remove such words from dictionary that I could find.I find the autocorrect replacing every couple of words I type with something irrelevant that doesn't make sense.The battery is terrible. It has a "battery saver" to keep it from dying longer, but that doesn't work. It will take a couple hours to charge to one hundred, and it will run out faster than it charged.My HD lasts very long, and the battery doesn't take more than an hour to charge all the way. My original Kindle took a long time to charge and the battery didn't last (which was normal for devices like that, when they first came out) but for both of those devices, they could charge faster, and save battery energy significantly by reducing the brightness. That doesn't work for this device. I find the battery getting low several times a day.Overall, there are more cons than pros for me. You get what you pay for, and I wish I saved the money and got a different kindle.

title: These seriously need a quality check!
body: My husband ordered one of these tablets for me during the Black Friday sale. Since I already had a Kindle Fire from 3 years ago and I loved it, I thought I would love this one too. The day the package arrived I was excited, but as soon as I turn the tablet on I didn't realize I was going to go through a month of hell with this thing (and its replacements).Fire #1: It arrived December 8. I turn it on and my happiness dwindled as I saw something that looked like a tiny hair caught inside the screen. It could have been dead pixels, I really couldn't tell. I called Amazon and I had to talk to someone who could barely speak English and I had difficulty trying to get him to understand what was wrong with the device. He took me through the reset process, problem was still there, and he transferred me over to someone who could understand me better. This person told me I can get a replacement, but if I wanted it immediately I would have to get a refurbished one, or I could wait until December 28 when a shipment of new Kindles would arrive. I told him I would wait. He says he set a reminder for himself for the 28th, and sent me a confirmation email. The 28th came and went without a call. So January 1 I call and get the replacement going.Fire #2: The second one arrived on January 3. I open the packaging and turn it on. This one had a bunch of dead pixels! It was worse than the first one! I call for another replacement.Fire #3: This one arrived on the 4th. I inspected it for dead pixels/debris under the screen. At first I didn't notice any, but after a few more seconds of searching I finally did. It was frustrating, but this wasn't as bad as the first two. I decided I could live with it. The next day I decide to charge it for the first time. As I plugged it in, I saw that the port was a bit damaged as if someone had used this tablet before. Fearing they had probably sent me a refurbished instead of a new one like I had wanted, I had my husband call for a refund since my throat was sore and couldn't speak. He asked for a refund, but I guess the Amazon people really wanted not to give one, so they said they would replace it, and for the new one they will open the box and inspect it before shipping it to us. The process would start as soon as they see the previous tablet in the mail. I sent all the tablets back on the 6th.Fire #4: i just got it today on the 8th. I opened it a few minutes ago. On the emails I received it described this as a refurbished Kindle. I did not want a refurbished Kindle, I wanted a new one. But I thought that maybe they needed to say it was refurbished if they opened the box to check it out. I gave them the benefit of the doubt. I opened the package and noticed that the original packaging was not included, so it was just the tablet by itself. I take it out of its plastic bag and notice that the screen is dirty. I inspect the outside without turning it on. It has obvious signs of wear. Thise bastards sent me a used one! Then I turn it on. This is the biggest figurative slap in the face I've ever received. Not only did it have dead pixels, it also had a chunk of debris stuck under the screen as if it had been assembled in an environment full of dust particles. This thing is so obvious, there was no way in hell anyone inspected it before shipping it to me. Amazon will be receiving an angry call from my husband when he gets home.I think I'll use my money to buy a Samsung tablet.

title: Not for educational purposes at all..
body: Not for education purposes at all. I'm sure amazon will delete this review but I am going to be completely 100% honest. We purchased 2 of these for our kids we were sold on the Free time parental controls and learning apps. Fast forward one year later the device is almost out of memory 8gb the operating system takes most of that and while you request for many of the apps to download to the sd card they go on the device anyway using up your memory and you cannot move them later trust me I tried. The FreeTime unlimited is not what you want for educational purposes for your child yes you have countless and I mean countless access to movies, books and apps but that is just the problem. While your kid is supposed to be doing a learning app he can sit there and stream movies and play non educational games that you don't want. Yes you can adjust this with time constraints and tasks but then Amazon has this annoying practice of adding recommended videos and apps for the kids which they click on and they end up right back on the device again without you knowing, you turn around and they are listening to Disney radio and you just removed it 2 days ago. My biggest beef with this tablet and I am actually livid with Amazon customer service and support that nobody told me this when I first got this tablet for the kids I never had this issue. I was able to download learning apps for my kids and then share them to their profile in FreeTime I paid for the app to be unlocked once and that was it. Just recently I don't know if rules have changed but I can no longer do this.I was told by customer service that the app needed to be downloaded to each tablet and unlocked on each tablet so I paid for the app to be unlocked 3 times, my tablet, my son's and my daughter's and still they could not unlock their app in FreeTime. After countless calls to customer support I learned that in order for kids to use the extended paid apps their parent's had purchased for them they needed to have their very own Amazon account it would not unlock in FreeTime or as a teen account. I don't even know what to say to this, how this makes any sense at all but I was suddenly thrust into the situation of having to be forced to create and adult account for my toddler so he could access his data. Does this make any sense to you? I was told by customer service at Amazon that the kids each needed to have their own account but yet when I checked this out on the device setting and help for Family library I noticed you can only have one other adult account to share with so I would be able to create a fake adult account for one of my kids and not the other. Is this making any sense to anybody? So now, we have two kindles that the kids can barely use, the apps only go so far and then freeze up due to lack of memory, nothing gets stored on the sd card and their paid content is not accessible to them even though mom paid for it 3 times. This is false advertising at its worse, none of this is revealed to parents when they purchase none of this is told to them. I feel completely cheated and I feel worse for my kids who used to use the educational apps and now cannot use them at all. Amazon is not only cheating parents they are cheating kids too. And there you have it, that is why the Fire tablet is worthless for kids as an educational tool. It can entertain them all day but if you want them to learn you are out of gas.

title: DO NOT BUY THIS PRODUCT.  DESCRIPTION PAGE MISLEADING ABOUT CAPABILITIES, STORAGE, ETC.
body: DO NOT BUY THIS! "Touch" screen only works sometimes - tap does not work, pressing does not work, my fingers were aching after just a few minutes. What I bought in Kindle store CANNOT BE READ on this device! And it is a book about the fire and how to use it!!! Says DRM protected and cannot use on fire - its solution: BUY the book for another device! When I purchase books for Kindle they download themselves as soon as I connect via wifi or 3G - if the title is there, you've got the book. Not so with fire. It lists the books but they do not download unless you call up the book while on wifi connection. They also cannot be read without a wifi connection!! Device is useless if you are not connected to wifi, that's IF you can get it to work AT ALL.Please note, after my utter disappointment with the product, I read MORE reviews and found out that the fire description page IS NOT UPFRONT about things. They claim you can add storage to the device with an sd card. Sure you can - you just can't use it for storing anything of amazon's on it!! No books, no audible books, etc. So, the tiny 8gb storage on the fire gives you nothing to work with. You must constantly delete one thing and download another to use it. By the way, the sd card they pair with it costs more than the EXACT SAME thing you can find on amazon's website if you just look for it. $1 is not much, but it helps!Note also that the fire apparently cannot handle the free games - some are not compatible with fire at all, which you won't know until you buy it!. The camera is worse than my cheap cell phone - grainy, unviewable pictures. There are NO instructions available to help with this piece of junk. You cannot access HELP without wifi! No manual is on the fire like you can find on your Kindle so there is no help but to keep on trying - which cannot be done with the impossible "touch" screen! Got this piece of junk fire on 2/13 - wanted to return it within 15 minutes. Tried. Amazon website would not let me return it on the website. Would not let me chat about it. Emailed them - it takes 12-24 hours to get a reply. Get people who do not speak proper English-leave out words, twist phrases, have to reread and translate into English to understand the email. Finally got an email with a link to print out a return lable. Will be getting rid of this worthless piece of junk as fast as possible. I had been so excited to get a fire for $39.99 - now I would not advise spending one CENT on this thing. While trying to be fair and give it a chance, I did download two games: one cannot be played on fire (!), the other - well don't bother with even a simple game like pac-man! Books cannot be brought up, games cannot be played, there is NO instruction manual, the camera sucks, there is nothing good to say about this product!After giving it 48 hours and honestly trying, my fingers hurt so bad I can barely type, I have been frustrated enough to want to PAY to return it. This thing is such a piece of junk even two librarians could not help me get it to work or download or anything. That's saying a lot, because the librarians are constantly updating their skills on devices and software and they can help with just about anything! Not with the fire....I DO NOT ADVISE ANYONE TO PURCHASE THIS PRODUCT!!!

title: Not Even Worth the $50
body: I've updated this review three times, as the longer I have the device, the more issues I come across. I once said that all the Fire was good for was interacting with other Amazon devices and apps. I've found out even that's not true! The Shop Amazon app on the phone does not recognize PRIME memberships to allow you to add PRIME music to your library! What a waste. Please save your money and understand you get what you pay for! Save up for a real tablet.See additional comments (Pros and Cons) below.*UPDATED*I recently downgraded this from three stars to two for two reasons. In the last two days I've spent over an hour on the phone with Amazon customer service. First, while the Fire advertises the external hard drive, it is difficult to use. Music (mp3s) are comparatively easy to store on the SD card when compared to videos (mp4s). To store a video you must first record a video using the Fire's camera; then you can transfer your videos to the newly created folder. HOWEVER since the video is now on the camera roll, you must open the camera, then open the roll. This doesn't allow you to name the videos, which make them difficult to find. Secondly, the device's unreliability; when attempting to download a picture from a website or app (Bing, Tumblr, etc) there seems to be about a 50/50 shot the image will actually be saved. I spent over half an hour on the phone with customer service with no result.Third, there is no way to organize the bookmarks into categories.See below for original review.****************************************************First, the good news: The screen is nice, it integrates SEAMLESSLY with your other Amazon devices (i.e. Echo/Alexa, Fire TV, etc), and it offers many of your favorite apps. It also has EXCELLENT battery life. And the accessories available are fantastic (see Amazon's Fire Case and Mr. Shield screen protectors). Additionally, the expandable memory is a fantastic option some of the more expensive tablets don't offer.Now the bad: There is a pop up ad whenever you do ANYTHING! Turn on, pop up. Open an app, pop up... Also, the media library is hard to navigate, especially the photos. Speaking of photos, the photo library does not support gifs. And because it's the Amazon App store and not one of the larger competitors, there are quite a few apps you're used to/would like that are unavailable (Instagram for one). And if your a big social media user, and appreciate getting app notifications (Facebook, Tumblr, eBay) you won't be getting any with the Amazon App store versions of your apps. And although the Fire instructions say to pull down the main menu for Bluetooth, in reality you have to pull down the main, then go to settings, then connectivity, then Bluetooth, then on/off. While this isn't a major issue, it's another drop in the bucket of nuances. Perhaps the most frustrating issue is that Silk, the Amazon Web browser pales in comparison to other options like Firefox or Chrome; and doesn't even have an option to import favorites like the others. Lastly, and this is a minor issue, the charging port is on the top of the device instead of the side or bottom, which simply makes it awkward to use when charging.Overall, it's worth the low $50 price point, but understand that you are getting what you pay for.

title: Sub-par for even a $50 tablet
body: I rarely take time to write reviews because generally I'm happy with my purchases. The 7" Kindle Fire at $50 is exactly what you image a $50 tablet would be and less.My criticism falls into three main categories:1 - Built Quality. This feels sub-par for even a $50 tablet. It's cheaply made and the responsiveness of the screen is questionable at best. The way the button layout and bezel are set it's incredibly challenging to use. This was clearly never designed with human use in mind. The form factor is just bizarre and holding for reading requires acrobatics or double-jointed prowess. Further, the build quality is substantially lower from my FIRST generation Kindle Fire. It's a $50 tablet and, honestly, I could probably live with this, but for the other issues that are simply killers.2 - Stability. I have had to simply give up on using this device because built-in apps cause the tablet to become unresponsive and even built in Amazon apps to crash. A good example is my reading of The Martian. I read it on my phone, and my iPad. I wanted to read it on this new device and upon sync'ing to the latest page read I couldn't get the page to turn. I couldn't exit the book. The app crashed every single time. I restarted the device, I deleted and reloaded the book, I tried it with the WiFi off, and on a separate WiFi network in my house. I tried it with a full charge and just about every other way without any success. The Kindle Books app just crashes and is unstable...that's pathetic since it's a first party app for the hardware! Please keep in mind I have loaded no 3rd party apps on my tablet...so it's not some odd memory issue because I packed it full of lousy malware infested Android apps.3 - Screen. I get that it's $50 and accommodations must be made. I also get that a $50 tablet isn't going to be an iPad Mini or another quality Android device. I would expect, however, that archaic resolution would be mated to similarly sized icons and system design. It's not sin to use a 1280x800 screen if it brings the pricepoint down this low! In fact, the accessibility of the pricepoint is very cool! The problem is that the system was never designed. This product feels like an afterthought as a way to rope customers into the Amazon Fire Tablet product line. The system icons aren't chunky enough, the touch-screen is hard to use with the 16x16 pixel gear icon and hit zones for things like swiping are a nightmare.The bottom line is this: If you have someone you hate, this would be a great gift. On the surface it will seem generous and kind, but the reality is that it will drive them slowly insane as they try to even make the basic features of the device work. Selling this as a 6-pack is laughable. They must realize how disposable these are and that the build quality is trash. Finally, putting an ad in the setup to buy a warranty care plan for this device is just plain cruel. The first time you get into the device and it's crashing constantly, you'll realize you just bought a guarantee on a product you'd have no interest in using if it actually did break.Steer clear.

title: Getting the run around from Amazon. Won't replace defective Kindle Fire!
body: I've sent this Kindle tablet back twice now for repair. It won't stay connected to my WiFi network. It has a bad pixel on the screen.
