title: Awful
body: In about 10 years of using Amazon, I don't think I have ever given a 1 star to any product I've purchased, even ones that I was just "ok" with.I loved the idea of a '50' dollar tablet that was backed by the Amazon brand because Amazon has a good reputation overall and I thought that would be reflected on all their products. Sadly, that's not the case with the Fire 7."I charged it right away after I opened the box and immediately noticed stuck pixels. Stuck pixels aren't the same as dead pixels, stuck pixels still light up, but all you see is a white light, dead pixels don't light up and are black. These stuck pixels are all in one area though, so its noticeable- very. Strike one.After it download it's newest system updates, I then went on to try out Amazons 'Silk Browser.' I tested it with Youtube, video quality was ok, but I knew that before purchasing. Videos aren't in high def yet they still took a while to load. After looking at a few videos its player stopped working, I closed the browser, made sure I completely shut it down, re-opened it and tried again, nothing, videos weren't playing. It worked again a few days later but after that I really don't use that browser anymore. Strike two.After it charged 100% I unplugged it and started to play around with it, checking out it's settings menus, and checking out all it's the pre- installed apps that came with it. That took me a few minutes then I noticed the battery life was at like 90%, at first I thought that maybe I was at fault and I didn't let it charge completely. I took note of it though, let it recharge later on during the day. Same thing, battery drains extremely fast to the point where I wouldn't recommend traveling with it unless you have access to power outlets or some sort of external battery that can charge it when on the go. Strike three.Finally, it's connection to my wifi. Horrible, it disconnects randomly but only for a few seconds, so if you're on an app that needs to be connected for it to work properly it's going to be a nightmare. I use it to play Game of War and it's not a fun experience on it. Its an MMO strategy game, having a solid connection to the game and it's server is a must because it could literally cost you money if you're disconnected even if it is for a few seconds. It's also problematic if you have any kind of VOIP app on it because if you get disconnected, even for a few seconds you will get cut off then you will have to open up the dial pad and make the call again. Sonic CD works awesome on it but that's because it's completely downloaded to your Fire. Strike four.My idea was to test it out and if it worked the way it was promised, I was thinking of getting one for my dad and one for my mom, and maybe even for some family members overseas so we could communicate via Skype or VOIP's with U.S numbers. I think I would be better off getting cheap/ entry level Android phones and sending those to my family instead.Bottom line; It's a really bad tablet and I would not recommend it to anyone. Instead, be patient, save up a few extra dollars and get a better tablet.

title: If you're buying for your kids, beware of impending disappointment.
body: I bought 3 of these for my kids for Xmas. All of them have been a nightmare to set up. I've wasted literally hours installing, reinstalling, configuring, resetting, over and over again. An issue with one tablet out of three could be explained as a dud. But having issues with all of them seems to be an issue with the general software design.Pros:- Amazon Freetime is exactly what I want to be able to display to my kids and have them access only what I allow.- The screens, device speed, and expanded storage ability are fine for the cost.- For $50, you expect basic. But you expect the basics to work.Cons:- Personal videos, like kids' movies using MP4s from your own DVDs, cannot be added to the videos section of Freetime. You must use another app like VLC and give the kids' profiles access to that app to access movies.- You must use the adult profile to download apps/games, and then individually give each kid's profile access to those apps. That is expected. However, once inside any given kid profile (3 in my case), you must re-download/install EACH app within the profile. If you want to have each tablet as a backup to another, making them all exactly the same, then you need to log into each profile on each tablet and download/install each app.... and wait... because downloading/installing is incredibly slow to begin with.- Each time you get a new game, you have to repeat the above steps on any table and profile you want to include.- All 3 of my tablets have issues retaining apps within the kids' profiles. Randomly, the app inside a profile will need to be downloaded/installed all over again. Sometimes, ALL of the apps inside a profile require the download/install process all over again, all at once. Hours wasted.- Once an app is loaded into a profile, regardless if it actually works, it seemingly cannot ever be removed. You can try, there are options for that action. But even deleting the app in the Amazon account on a laptop, removing it from the tablet (and cloud) in the adult's profile, and doing the same action inside the kids' profile won't remove it from the supposedly available apps in their view. I even reset the tablet altogether. No avail.Bottom line: all I needed it to do was run Amazon Freetime, provide workable access to the apps available in Amazon's appstore, and play video's I already own. It struggles to do these basics tasks. I'll likely return all of them.In the attached pic: the Amazon Freetime interface is shown. Each of the apps are in the process of downloading/installing within my son's profile; notice the small "x" in the bottom left corner, the greyed icon, and the spinning "processing" symbol. The same apps are already installed and can be run from the device under the main adult profile. I also had already installed them in my son's profile on the same tablet earlier in the day, and had gone through exactly what it is doing in that picture. I experienced the same thing for my other kids' tablets in their profiles, multiple times.

title: Terrible Product, spend a little more, and Horrible Customer Service
body: Ordered this tablet on a promotion of Fire1999 and even paid the extra $15 dollars to have it without ads. As soon as I got the tablet I could tell that two pixels were dead. So I contacted Amazon and they shipped out a new one. I tried to ask them to just let me get the Amazon Fire HD 8 which was at promotion at the time I purchased the Fire 7" but the operator said "No, That is not our policy to give people promotions" All I was trying to say is that I ordered the Fire 7" and realized the cheapness of quality and sluggishness of the device. Not to mention TERRIBLE LIGHT BLEED THROUGH and TWO DEAD PIXELS. The operator kept refusing to give me the HD 8 on promotion so I just had them send a replacement out. Thinking that how could this possibly happen twice... well... It happened again....This new tablet I received from Amazon I really wanted it to work like it was supposed to. Even though in the back of my head I knew I should have originally sprung for the more expensive option, in hopes that you spend a little more you get a little more.The new tablet had bad light bleed through, this time it was just on one side and not three of the sides like the first one. There were two big pieces of dust trapped underneath the screen though, and it was very annoying. You could move them around a little, but I didn't want to be dealing with that every time I was gonna use the tablet.So I called Amazon back in hopes that they would see their mistake which has cost not only me time and money, but them more more in just returns. The operator, her name was "Hazel", and even supervisor were no help. I kept asking them that if I just return this devices because after reading more reviews and actually seeing the terrible products they put out I should have just originally bought the HD 8 on promotion for $99, but they kept saying no it isn't on promotion anymore so we can't give it to you at that price, but the only reason it isn't on promotion is because I've been wasting my time with these 7 inch tablets that are more or less a paperweight. I was explaining to him that if I knew what I know now about the Fire 7inch tablets I would have just purchased the HD8. Ohh also which I thought was extremely rude is that after I talked to the supervisor I told him to just put me back on with the girl and we can figure out the return process.... so instead she just hung up on me. Probably one of the first times I have actually had to complain about Amazon's customer service, because usually Amazon has great customer service and is on top of doing whatever it takes to keep the customer happy and coming back.Long Story short.. somewhat... lolDon't order either the Fire 7 inch or Fire HD 8 because they are junkAfter doing a little more research they have lots of light bleed problems and I would be saving you a few phone calls and trips UPS to make returns.Personally I'm gonna go with the ASUS zen pad, newest edition. A little more expensive than the HD 8, but it has 2k resolution and is not locked down by the Fire OS.

title: Why I hate my kindle...
body: I was given a kindle as a present. All I can say is that this is a completely underwhelming product. It was a total disappointment. There is nothing about the kindle that I like. It is nothing like the experience of reading a nicely printed book. And there is nothing fun or interesting about the experience of holding it or reading from it. I would actually rather read from my Mac Book. Apple products are so nicely crafted. This looks and feels like something from KMart. Cheap hard cold plastic. The wireless thing is a joke. The navigation is non-intuitive and really oddly designed. About the only thing I can see this being good for are text books - but honestly it would be better to have pdfs of text books on a laptop - namely because that is where you are going to need content and robust applications. Reading the newspaper off the kindle is nasty too. I'm planning to sell this thing on craigslist or ebay. For all Mac users - beware - if you love apple design you will hate the kindle.I probably would not be so adamant in my anti-kindle stance if it were not for all the enormously overblown build up about this thing. If you read reviews from a few misguided tech heads - or listen to the patently self congratulatory hyperbole coming from Amazon - you would thing this was the best thing since the wheel. The reality is that the kindle is a major let down and an industrial design blunder of enormous proportion.Hey Amazon !! Listen to me - redesign this thing!! Get some real industrial designers to make a nice high quality device... something at least as nice as the iphone. I don't know - may be good designers were hired and the heads at Amazon just have bad taste and told them to build something for the TrailerPark crowd. Wake up - we expect better. Sony can make a nice e-reader why can't you?UPDATE: I posted this review a few months ago never expecting that it would garner so many comments. I've enjoyed reading the comments so much and being able to respond to some of the questions that have been raised by my original critique of the kindle. I wanted to update you all and share my experiences.For all those who wonder, I happily sold my kindle to some one who for some reason likes it. Several friends of mine were also given Kindles. They too have put them up for sale and are glad to be rid of them.My philosophy about negative reviews is that they should be over the top. What's the point of being restrained. Reviews should be informative, but they should be theatrical too. Why be middle of the road. Love it or hate it. But do it with zest.Too many people take their gear and themselves too seriously. They equate their self-worth with the gadgets they own. They don't like to admit that they have trailer park sensibilities even if they do ;) So if you lack a sense of humor, or you get outraged and defensive because someone does not like your toys. Just grow up please. I'm looking forward to more of your hate mail - its always a treat to see how nasty and humorless people can be. Enjoy those kindles ;)

title: but none of them were terribly serious. Considering that it has lasted me four ...
body: I bought the original Kindle Fire when it came out several years ago. With the number of improvements that have been released since then, I have always contemplated buying an updated one, but ultimately thought, "Well, this one is still working perfectly, I see no reason to change." The original Fire had some issues for me, but none of them were terribly serious. Considering that it has lasted me four years without a case, and that I have not been terribly careful with it (put it in pants pockets, backpacks, dropped on the floor...), speaks a lot for the quality of its construction.The new Kindle Fire feels cheap. Rather than the solid feel of the first generation, it feels easily breakable. The home screen is extremely cluttered with icons I will never use, but cannot remove, only relocate. One of the things I used my Fire for the most was listening to music; being connected with Amazon made it convenient to download albums I had purchased, and listen to them at work without WiFi. The new Fire has made that more difficult, because the Music icon on the home screen takes you to the store, not the library. You can access the library from the tab list on the home screen, but unlike the first generation with the tab set at the top (Newsstand - Books - Music - Video - Docs - Apps - Web), the new tablet has essentially the same bar, but now requires you to scroll through to find the thing you want, and introduces some new selections (splitting Apps and Games, and adding Shop and Audiobooks tabs), mostly because they chose a much larger font size.One of the most attractive features was the option to expand the internal storage with an SD card. As my old Fire was constantly at capacity, and I was constantly trying to decide which albums to delete to download a new purchase, I was excited for an extra 64 Gb of space. Unfortunately, while you can have videos and apps downloaded/installed onto the SD card, this has no effect on music downloads; they still go into internal storage until it is full, and then the Fire disables WiFi until you have cleaned it up. Even with ES File Explorer, I could not move the files onto the SD card, requiring it to be connected to my desktop to manually move files from my computer to the SD card (which the Kindle can then play perfectly fine, making me wonder why it was so difficult to begin with). The layout of the Music library has also changed in such a way as to make it less user-friendly, and now you cannot add a song to "Now Playing", a very useful feature when tapping a song plays every song after it on whatever screen you are viewing.For the price, it's hard to complain. But in almost every regard, I have found the new Kindle Fire to be less user-friendly and more cluttered than the original, and have yet to see any real improvements beyond the addition of the SD slot, which itself is poorly used by the device.Update: Now Amazon has apparently decided to randomly send notifications. Shop Amazon for Holiday Deals! Listen to a holiday playlist on Amazon Music! Great.

title: Stay away from it.
body: I have always loved Amazon branded stuff (Echo, tap, Amazon Basic) for their decent quality and durability with yet the amazingly affordable price. However there is no use for anything that's just cheap, but not well built. This $50 tablet is unfortunately one of the failures from Amazon.Before I purchased it, I read the comments and did my homework. I thought I know what I was expecting from a $50 tablet:1. It's slow, but it'll get me there eventually.2. Hardware specs will suck but it'll be okay for kindle reading, non-HD movie streaming and some music.3. Sometimes it may fail (reboot by itself, apps won't launch, etc) but that only happens once in a while.But when I actually received the tablet and played with it for a whole day, I found out what I actually got from my money is this:1. Being slow is not the issue here. LOL. Quite often it'll never get me there.2. Hardware specs suck, and none of the reading, movie, music is going to happen.3. It fails every other 30 minutes. (reboots itself, app won't launch unless reboot, hard resets itself).Here's a more detailed description of what I run into:First thing I noticed was that when I tried to turn it on for the first time, the tablet initializes itself and sets itself up for use, supposedly. Yet surprisingly this process failed. It froze over 30 minutes (where it says on screen approx. 10 mins) so I had to manually turn it off and re-do it again. Not long after it actually turns itself on for the first time after initialization, I run into the problem of not being ablt to register my tablet with Amazon, because everytime I hit continue, it restarts. I still don't know how I fixed that but after about and hour or so and after couple re-initializations, it finally let me get pass the registration part. So I went ahead and opened kindle. Surprise, surprise. Kindle store won't load. I ended up calling Amazon and after about 30 mins of waiting they told me there's something wrong with my account's associated region (or something like that). Luckily they fixed that on their end so I can finally see available books from kindle store. I bought a couple books and decided to see how they look on a tablet, then guess what, the app crashed and won't let me open it again. Sigh. So I googled and followed the dull instruction to reset my kindle..... You get the idea. Among the whole day I've been playing with it, my video app crashed twice, my kindle app crashed many times, my music app won't launch for about an hour, the device rebooted itself at least 10 times, and hard reset itself 10 times as well.In conclusion, this product is cheap, but is not made with quality. I don't know what the hell Amazon is doing but there it is, a piece of worthless plastic board. I would happily give it a five star should it be at least barable to use (not having to restart every other 10 mins and hard reset every other 3 hours). But no. It's the least well spent $50 and I had to sadly say that Amazon suck on this one.

title: I don't want to buy another Amazon device after this.
body: Short Version- Got a tablet. Cord sucks. Charge port sucks. Asked for simple directions on how get repair. Told to call them instead of giving directions. [a lot of time passes] Then told that it's out of warranty, even though it was still in warranty when I first made contact to tell them it was broken.Long Version- This is one of the worst experiences with tehnology that I have had in my life. My son received this for a Christmas gift and managed to break the charging cord within a week. He's a kid, so I half expected that to happen at some point, but upon examination it's obvious the cords are extremely poor quality. We bought another cord. Fast forward to mid-February. He comes to me and tells me the tablet is not charging. I examine it to find that the tab inside the charging port is bent so that the charger won't connect. I tired to delicately straighten it and *SNAP*, it broke right off.I'm frustrated at this point, but I figure Amazon can help me. They always do. I email the customer service "contact us" and give a detailed account of all of this. I ask them to contact me back to tell me how I go about getting this repaired. Later in the day, they sent me a brief dismissive email that says call us for troubleshooting. ???? Last time I checked you can't troubleshoot repairing hardware unless they are going to tell me how to solder it over the phone. Instead of providing customer service, they put the onus back on me. I filed the email for the time being and set the tablet aside, because I don't like to talk to people when I'm upset. A little time passes and the tablet got lost in my nightstand (out of sight = out of mind) and I forget all about it.I finally thought about it today, when discussing buying a tablet for my daughter. So, I contacted customer service again. Imagine my surprise when they told me that the tablet was out of warranty so they couldn't help me now. I was informed that the warranty of 90 days has since passed. Awfully convenient that they didn't bother putting that in their short email back in a February, don't you think? They would not do anything other than offer me a 15% discount on the purchase of a new tablet. They literally still would not answer the question about how I get it repaired. Finally I spoke to a manager who agreed that the email they sent should have had the information I asked for, but they still couldn't do anything now, since I waited so long to call. Then I was told perhaps a third party could repair it, but they don't offer repairs. At least I finally got an answer.I was a big fan of Amazon, and I'll still use them to buy stuff, but I will not buy their poorly crafted technology. I had another tablet in my Amazon shopping cart to buy for my daughter. I deleted it. I previously held their customer service in the highest regard, but that is now in the gutter. I went ahead and cancelled my Prime membership. No sense paying for perks with a company that doesn't value me as a customer.

title: Not satisfied
body: http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00TSUGXKE?redirect=true&ref_=cm_cr_ryp_prd_ttl_sol_0The main problem I've had with this is that the 8 GB of internal memory is quickly depleted to 5B G as the operating system uses this also. I added a 32 GB scandisk, but it turns out that it will not store books at all. I hadn't had the Kindle very long when I got a message saying I was out of internal storage. I had no apps, no videos, no music, no photos, etc. downloaded to the Kindle just 4 short books and all my internal memory was gone! When that happens the wireless automatically disconnects and the tablet cannot be used for streaming. I looked for other items to delete, but you can't delete Kindle apps you never use from the internal storage to free up space.I checked my internal storage. I have 43.28 MB of memory left and can't connect to Wi-Fi to change any settings, etc. There is a drop-down menu under it offering the choices of 1) System (which includes all the items that make the Kindle work, taking up 5.57 GB of space. 2) "Movies" which consists of a file called "others" ?????? taking up 256KB of space and 3) "Miscellaneous" with 2 files - ".imagecache" (6.48 MB0 and "kindle" 8.00 KB. The "others" file under "Movies" does not offer any way to delete it (no box to check). The items under miscellaneous does have the boxes to check to delete but since I have no idea what they are and the Amazon call center could not tell me I don't know if I should! If I do will it render my Kindle useless? I tried to move the "Movies" to the SD card but there was no option for that either. It stayed in the internal memory. So I have an SD card in, what is my opinion, a very poorly designed device. I'm guess I'll go back to another Kindle, another brand of e-reader or a PC again. I had high hopes - even bought one for a gift. What a joke! I've had 4 new Kindle Fires and out of all of them I was happiest with the one bought for me in 2012. I was hopeful, and still am, that Amazon would design a device where storage (for everything) was really expandable.Searching Amazon's website was not helpful so I contacted them by phone and spoke to a rep at a call center in the Philippines. I have reset to factory defaults as suggested but it hasn't helped. I get the same results - same files, same lack of internal storage. (If you have to call Amazon ask for a Kindle specialist who is American if you're from the U.S. - trying to get communicate with a person who seemed to have very little English language comprehension was frustrating and I wasted 15 minutes of my time before I was sent to an American Kindle specialist) who could only suggest resetting the Kindle) I'm very frustrated!I ordered a Kindle Paperwhite and will send back the the extra Fire I bought for a gift. If the Paperwhite doesn't meet my expectations I'm done with Kindles and will try a new Nook or other e-reader!

title: Tablet Probably Amazing. But Amazon Sunday Shipping Blows.
body: Firstly I love the Kindle Fire. What we don't love is USPS Sunday delivery. We ordered a Kindle on the 19th when it was still on waitlist knowing we would have to wait for it. Low and behold on the 24th it kicked to shipping. HOORAY... Will be delivered on Sunday... Okay. At least we're home and that gives a full day to play with it before taking it to work... or so I thought. Kindle didn't show up. We waited. My living room faces the front of our house where our front door is. I checked. The husband checked. We left the front door open. I haphazardly refreshed the shipping info and low and behold it flipped to delivery attempted NO SECURE LOCATION."NOTICE LEFT" That's bulls***. We llive in a house. We have a fenced yard where the guys usually "kindly" throw the packages over the gate. and I find them waiting there in the AM. The nice guys walk it up to the porch. I had shoes delivered from Amazon on Saturday. Got up at 10 am and they were waiting there. SO. We went out to look for the notice... was it in the maillbox? Nope. Tucked into the fence where they sometimes like to put it? Nope. There was no notice. We were unable to verify where the hell the fire was or what post office was in possession of it on Monday to even attempt to pick it up. I spent an hour calling the sort facility on Monday trying to make contact to ask why they are actively lying about deliveries. Finally on Tuesday morning after checking the tracking and still seeing Delivery attempted Notice left and no movement toward my local Post Office and with no notice number to request a redelivery, I contacted Amazon to say send another one. So they agree to do that and rightfully so. Turns out the Rando sort facility decided to deliver it today and I find it after I get home from work and open it. Log immediately onto amazon to cancel the other one. Too late. Okay fine. I'll just refuse the other when it gets here. No problem. Open the Kindle go to activate it. Won't activate. Get on Amazon chat. It's marked as lost and stolen. And NOW I'll have to wait another 24 hours to activate it. I get it. This is a smart procedure for when people are lying or trying to cheat. But we're not doing that. I just want to use the tablet we payed for. I had been waiting to get a Kindle that we could afford for a long time. I'm riding a nook color that I've had for years. So what's another 24-48 hours right? I would love to be able to write a great review of the tablet. But seeing as it's basically a toaster right now what's the point? I'm very dissatisfied with the Sunday Delivery process and the fact that it's not being run thru the local POs but the sort facilities with part time folks who obviously could give a rats ass if you get the item you ordered. In the future I hope amazon considers shipping more responsibly and with a way that people can actually contact the shipper directly when issues like this happen. It would have saved me a lot of aggravation and disappointment.

title: Buyer beware - Amazon can randomly reset device and you'll lose all data
body: This seems like a great tablet for the price. I bought a couple, including one for a kid. But after the events of yesterday, I cannot recommend this device, nor any device in the Amazon "ecosystem."Long story short: Amazon retains control over your tablet. They make it difficult to hack by implementing a number of features which restrict your ability to manage your own device and customize things. I had some misgivings about that, but I accepted it because I believed that ultimately on a cheap child's tablet it would be better to just protect things.Except Amazon itself has become the greatest danger to the data on the device. They push updates and exercise remote control over various elements of the tablet -- and yesterday, the device essentially did an unprompted reset and deregistered itself. If you go over to the Amazon Kindle forums, you'll see a thread -- I'm not alone. So far, there are reports of over 60 owners and 100+ affected devices, and that's only the ones who bothered to post to the thread on the Kindle Forum. And this is not the first time something like this has happened (last time it was a different model).Sorry, Amazon, I know you advertised this great deal, but the price is still too high for a device which could automatically delete all of the user's data and reset itself. (In my case, it actually didn't do a proper reset, but placed itself in an ambiguous state where it no longer recognized my username or the parental controls password, so I couldn't actually even access my account to try to fix it. Others have had other random results.)Sure, I was lucky enough to turn on automatic cloud backups for the kid's photos, etc. They will be recoverable. Assuming Amazon can allow me to reset the device completely, hopefully I'll be able to reinstall all the apps. But progress on educational games, etc.? Lost. Go to the Kindle Forums. There are many MANY really disappointed kids out there. I cannot fathom how Amazon pushed an update (or whatever it was) onto remote devices without realizing this sort of damage could occur... and frankly it should NEVER be able to occur. I don't want a company that can do something remotely which will effectively disable my device and destroy my data any time it wants.Unless Amazon does something REALLY nice to make up for this horrible lapse (and hopefully prevent such incidents in the future), I'd strongly recommend everyone to think hard before considering an Amazon tablet.EDIT: There seems to be some confirmation from other people who have talked to customer support that this was related to an automatic Freetime update sent out by Amazon, so it probably only affects tablets used by kids. But previous incidents (as can be seen in the forums) affected a broader group of users due to automatic updates causing deregistration.

title: Don't Buy This, More Problems Than It's Worth!!
body: My last Kindle fire was a first gen and I got four years out of it before the charging port came loose and made it difficult to charge. I really liked that Kindle. I was hoping to get something like that with this device but there's a lot to be desired with this new gen "upgrade." My main problem is that I have vision issues and I can't make the fonts in the books large enough to read comfortably, they only give you three options, small, medium and huge. And "huge" in books and documents that I load in myself isn't that huge. I really dislike the Word Runner program, I don't read to race myself or other people, I read for enjoyment. Having the bottom of the screen tell me how many more minutes is in a chapter is distracting and I wish I could turn it off but can't find out how to. I really don't like all the advertising on it, but didn't want to spend the extra $15 to have it off because I'm low-income. I also don't like how the store is right there on the home page with you, instead of being accessible by a couple of touches on the screen, it feels cluttered with "recommendations" that I'm not interested in. I'm also really disappointed that I can't delete icons from the home screen; I don't use IMDB for instance but can't remove the icon. And the Washington Post keeps trying to get me to install it by sneakily offering itself at every opportunity; and, well, if there's anything I dislike more than a device that falls short of expectations, it's infotainment that passes as "news." I guess it's a fair value for $50 but there's a lot not to like. I really wish I had had the extra $100 to go up to the next model. Maybe as I get more familiar with it, it won't be so annoying.Editing to downgrade to 2 stars: Recently I haven't been able to delete folders from the local storage, even after two resets. I have to plug this device into my computer in order to get things deleted properly. The device should allow you to delete items and folders easily, I should not have to plug it into my laptop in order to remove things. This is completely unacceptable. I really regret buying this device.Editing again to downgrade to 1 star: This device has started locking me out of books I bought and downloaded from the Kindle store, with a message that the book is locked by DRM and I need to delete it from the device and re-download it. This happens even on books I had been reading and had book marks in. I have tried all the solutions offered on the help forum to no avail. Problem persists after recent OS update. I found a business on eBay that will replace the charging port on my first gen Fire for $25 and I'm going that route. I will pass this piece of junk on to someone else if I can find someone to take it. Otherwise I'm going to trash it.I highly recommend NOT buying this device, it's just a waste of $50.

title: Flaming bag of turds!
body: Someone rings your doorbell so you go to the porch and someone has left you a package in a small paper bag. OMG!, It's on fire! With all the excitement, you don't have time to think, instinct kicks in and you stomp out the fire. Oh no, it wasn't a present, it was a bag of turds! By the time you realize it, it's too late. It's all over you and the stench won't go away. Now everything reeks of flaming turds and your don't know how you could have been so stupid.That is exactly what this tablet is like. You get caught up in the excitement of finding a new Android tablet for under $50. It's quad core and expandable memory, can you believe it! Without thinking you order it, how could you not! When the package shows up at your door it even says Fire on it, but without thinking, you tear into it only to discover very quickly that you now have the stench of flaming turds all over you.Start with the resolution, it looks like your peering through a wore out screen door. You can see but it's ugly. The feel of the screen is horrible, your fingers don't slide on it instead they grip like it's almost tacky. The balance of the tablet is wrong, it's uneven and unbalanced and the back of the case is tacky feeling.Now let's talk about the OS. I knew that Amazon was going to overlay Android with their own skin that directed you to their services first. What I didn't realize was that they would completely block off any useful functions and cut off any way to work around it without booting the OS and loading your own. You have no access to the Google play store, no ability to load any Google apps except for YouTube. You can't even sync with a gmail account, Apple let's you do that! All of your apps have to come from Amazon app store and if you have a game that you like to play well to bad because it's not available. Not to mention the constant ads that are built in to the tablet, they never go away!Almost none of the basic functionality that you expect from a tablet is available. Of course. I've had a tablet for years and I'm used to using Google based apps, WHICH YOU CAN'T WITH THIS "TABLET."I gave up very quickly and announced that I'm sending back the flaming bag of turds or "Fire" tablet. My wife thought I was being overly dramatic and had the same thought that sucked me in. " It's only $49, how bad could it be." She was sure that she could use it for the three things that she does on her phone or with my old tablet. So she wanted to try it and now she smells of flaming turds too, it won't stop spreading everywhere! In my wife's words, " it's unusable and doesn't even have the Instagram app, send it back. "Don't fall for it, it's not a usable tablet. You will regret it and if you buy this as a gift for someone then they will know that you don't love them and wonder what they did to upset you.

title: BETTER OFF WITH A DOLLAR STORE TABLET!!!
body: Here's the deal... My Twin 9 yr old sons had a limit of $60 each for Xmas, all they have been asking for all year was there own tablets, as they have been playing all their video games on two old 4-inch cell phones, one of which has a cracked screen. From the moment they opened their tablets their has been nothing but issues with these things!!! First off, my 2 sons favorite games are Minecraft and Pokemon go. Inorder to download these games you need to download them through Google Playstore. These Amazon tablets are not loaded with Google Play, but i did find a video posted on YouTube that showed how to get GooglePlay with some trickery. So we go through the steps tricking these Fire tablets into downloading GooglePlay (which took about 15min each tablets). Once downloaded, my 2 sons proceed to download their 2 favorite games (which they play non-stop). The games downloaded just fine, but almost immediately there was an issue. While playing Minecraft (which i purchased for them for $7 each from the playstore) the tablets kept freezing or crashing, they cannot play for long enough for them to get any fun out of it. As far as Pokemon Go, you better forget about it!!! Inorder to play Pokemon Go the tablet needs a GPS transmitter (which basically every phone and tablet has these days, well except for this clunky tablet of course!!!). I did read online that you can teather your cell phones GPS to the tablet and this would allow you to play Pokemon Go, but once again with some trickery!!! My son James, who is actually very good for his age figuring out electronics, just gave up on his tablet after weeks of on again off again trickery, crashing, and freezing during game play! I aswel did my best waisting hours away at times researching and trying to get these things to work properly, to NO LUCK!!! My son Jesse broke down crying in frustration at the torture his tablet put him through, and actually asked for his 4-inch cracked phone back, James was quick to follow! Right now these tablets are collecting dust on our book shelf, while my 2 sons are back on their tiny 5-6 year old 4-inch cracked phones. I just feel absolutely horrible for my 2 sons, i honestly feel as though they never got a Christmas, as they only received 2 gifts each this year, these tablets being one of them. Seriously, what good is a tablet if you constantly have to trick the thing into doing what any normal tablet does right out the box?!!!! I honestly don't wish these tablets on anybody, not even North Korea!!!! No one deserve to be tortured as we have by these cup coasters, because thats what im about to turn them into! PLEASE PLEASE PLEASE do yourself a favor and just get a NORMAL ANDROID TABLET! They are all over Amazons site, and some just as cheap as this sorry excuse of these soon to be Cup Coaster!!! :(

title: I HATE THIS THING!!!!!
body: I super hate this device! I wanted a product that would function like a tablet and got an Amazon commercial maker /shopping tool instead. NEVER can I EVER use this device without being spammed by a bajillion Amazon ads - which I know I can 'unsubscribe' to but they charge me $15 for that. Not only do you deal with their CONSTANT irritating ads for crap I'm not interested in and will never buy every time I want to read one of the books I own I'm prompted with a bunch more 'recommendations' for more books Amazon thinks I should buy, as if every time I want to read a book I want to be hassled by some smarmy sales pitch to buy more crap. I've had to rig my daughter's identical device to make it impossible for her to access the internet because Amazon has the whole kit and every access point tethered to my account with 1-click purchasing and I don't trust either her or Amazon not to buy up the internet on this thing and charge it too me. So every time she wants to buy a book I have to add it to her device and she's unable to use it for anything but read books, which is not what we bought it for. I tried to add our music files to both devices and it copied a random assortment of about a tenth of the songs, and yes there is still a ton of memory space. I'm a vegetarian and somehow every f-ing ad that pops up is a huge picture of some disgusting meat thing..... the pictures are vivid and gross. And ironically, the camera on this device SUCKS, but we expected that.... I tried adding my anti-virus protection to this and that doesn't really work, so that even if I'm trying to use the internet which I nearly never do from here but am otherwise always working online, I get spammed there too. I work online and go to school online so having an even more mobile device like a tablet, versus only my laptop was what I was after... one that could take decent quality snapshots, so I don't have to take my pro-camera out for every image and this device fails completely. In EVERY. POSSIBLE. WAY. I HHHHHHAAAAATTTE it. And I might hate it less if it hadn't been such a difficult task to get them delivered to us in Central America by a traveling friend who brought them down to prevent them from being stolen by customs. Yes, there will be a thousand recommendations about how to deactivate some of these things, but all in all, there are just too many points of connection to Amazon's sales post purchase, too many things it can't do that I'd like it to, and all it hat I've used my device now this month for the first time since I received it in January, because I HAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAATEEEEEEE it. I'd sell it, but I could never be sure that my Amazon account info/email was completely removed so it sits.........charged, just in case every other device we have in the house fails, and this is my only option............it's horrible and I SUPER hate it.

title: Review of Fire OS 5
body: Fire OS 5 ReviewThis review is aimed specifically at the major software update we just received, not at the particular Kindle Fire model. I myself use an HDX 8.9 which I love in terms of its hardware, and until the system overhaul that OS 5 brought had loved for its software as well. Since this review will be rather lengthy, you can just read the headings that seam pertinent to you. I will be reviewing this in terms of comparison and contrast with how it has improved or devolved since Fire OS 4.Topics:LOCK SCREENPULL-DOWN MENUMAIN USER INTERFACE (UI)BUILT-IN APPSBUGS1. LOCK SCREENPros: The lock screen has added a really nice feature allowing us to directly access the quick pull-down menu with wifi, airplane mode, etc. I will talk about the development of the pull-down menu in the next part of this review. Another nice feature is the ability to directly access the camera app from the lock screen with a swipe from the bottom right corner.Cons: As is the case with almost all the changes for OS 5, the cons far outweigh the pros. First, the biggest problem with the lock screen is the loss of the direct ability to pause, skip, or go back 30 seconds when listening to an audiobook or to music as we had before. Even with access to the pull-down menu on the unlock screen we are not able to control playback. We must unlock the screen first; and THEN access the pull-down menu to access the playback controls. Ridiculous.The other major nuisance of the lock screen is the fact that the unlock swipe has been changed from a right-to-left swipe in the middle of the screen (which allows for one-handed operation of a large tablet) to a down-to-up swipe from the middle of the bottom of the screen, forcing two-hand operation to unlock the tablet. To add insult to injury, this time fro the gesture to actual unlock seems to take longer than before.Finally, I find it obnoxious that Amazon added a quick-swipe into shopping on the lockscreen, as though accessing the Amazon store is as essential as accessing the camera quickly. Seems to me there are many other apps that deserve such a useful piece of lock screen real estate (say, e-mail).2. PULL-DOWN MENUPros: This is one of the aspects of OS 5 that is done pretty well overall. It provides all of the most essential features: Wifi, Airplane Mode, Blue Shade*, Do Not Disturb, Settings, Auto-Rotate toggle, Mayday (help), and Firefly (to catch the name of that song on the radio quickly). Blue Shade is an excellent feature addition which allows us to use the Fire before bedtime without the sleep-preventing overuse of blue light. The blue shade removes the blue light, turning the screen a reddish color, and dimming it, though you can easily adjust the red-yellow balance and brightness to your liking.Cons: The biggest Con has to do with the implementation of Audiobook playback controls. Music playback controls are largely unchanged. If you play a song, then playback controls are added into the native pull-down menu. However, for audiobooks, the playback controls are added as a white notification below the native pull-down menu. This has two disadvantages. First, the controls are ultra-small requiring super-accurate touches. Second, I have not figured out what triggers its disappearance, but the playback controls seem to randomly disappear from the notification section. The fact that I can even remove them from the notification while the audiobook is still playing is also weird. They should be there permanently unless I actually quit the audiobook app, as they were with OS 4.Non-Pro-Con: This depends on preference. I like less options; but others like more. If you own a Kindle Fire, like owning an iPad, you understand that you are losing customization choices with the OS. Nevertheless, some might be annoyed that there is no customizability with the actual pull-down menu’s native items, though all of the notifications and priorities can be controlled through the App Settings menu.3. MAIN USER INTERFACE (UI)Perhaps, I’m using the wrong wording, but when I say the main UI, I simply refer to how the whole Home Screen, Tabs, animations, etc. have been tweaked (or more accurately, mangled) with OS 5.Pros:Probably the best new feature that was added to the interface is the addition of a small box icon next to the home circle and back arrow icons. When the box is tapped you will get a carousel view of all running apps. In fact this view has revealed either a new problem with the OS or perhaps a problem that remained hidden in previous versions since there was no “App Reveal” button. I am including a screenshot of the problem. As you will see, the system seems to run duplicates once apps are opened. I am only including an example that shows two of these (Music and Settings), but the problem persists across all built-in apps. An app exists for Settings as well as menus accessible from Settings—Wi-Fi Settings, E-mail Settings, Parental Controls, etc. Music artists each take up an app tab, the music library requires an App tab, etc. Thus, the system appears to be wasting a lot of resources running so many separate apps. The other possibility is that this is always the case in the OS with the aim of speeding up launches, but the end result is still an extremely cluttered app carousel. We only need one Settings tab, one music tab, etc.Another possible Pro for some is the ability to change the Home Screen wallpaper. I prefer the simplicity of the original dark gray screen of OS 4 (less distracting when searching for a colorful app that could be lost in the colorful background), but I was easily able to change the wallpaper to a simple gray to suit my needs. Others may like the ability to put their own pictures or scenic backgrounds to more personalize the home page.Cons:Perhaps the most egregious of the UI problems is the removal of a main tab for Photos and Docs. Some might argue that the Docs tab gets less mainstream use, but Photos is undeniably one of the most frequently used apps for most users. Nevertheless, both Photos and Docs have been removed from the major tabs in the banner across the main screen WHILE KEEPING A TAB CALLED “SHOP”!!! That is to say, in order to push for impulse buying, Amazon has removed the instant accessibility to photos in favor of keeping an entire tab dedicated to shopping.Another major change with the OS is that instead of accessing tabs within the main screen, each tab has been broken into separate apps. Photos and Docs have been removed from the tabs and exist only as separate apps that we can open from the Home Screen or from the Apps tab. The result is more Home Screen clutter. What is crazy is that all of the apps are stuck on the Home Screen…they can no longer be removed for a neater Home Screen. The only workaround I have found is that I can combine them into their own “Folder” so that instead of taking up 50 App spaces, I can cull them into 1 App Folder space.While on the topic of the Home Screen, it is also worth noting that we can no longer save our favorite books as an app-like icon to the home screen. For example, I read my Bible every day. Previously this could just be seen immediately on my Home Screen next to my Silk browser app. Now I must go to the inconvenient Recent tab (discussed later).Regarding the Tabs (Music, Apps, Shop, etc.), these have become less useful. Previously, you were brought directly to your library for the selected tab; now the screen wastes resources animating a movement to a tab which shows you your most recently accessed apps, but you must additionally tap “Library” in order to see all of it, which is exactly the same Library interface we had on OS 4. Thus, two taps are required to do what was previously one tap. This is a small insignificant amount of time; but the digression in usability is the bigger problem. What happens when OS 6 requires three taps, then four?When Accessing the Apps tab, the results are far more problematic. Seeing only five apps in the Tab section, we almost always have to view the Library, but the apps library has become horrible. The apps are not viewable as icons. Instead they are in a list form with their icon, name, download/cloud status, and a context menu. First of all, this view makes it impossible to see more than 15 apps at a time. Second of all, you can’t tap the App name or icon to open it. Tapping these will bring you to the app information page in the Store! Ridiculous! This makes no sense from a UI perspective. Instead, the only way to open it is by tapping the tiny “Open” button on the right side.I’m not sure how many felt about the Carousel that previously dominated the Home Screen. I loved it. Instead this has been replaced by a Recent tab. I have two problems with this. One, it now requires two taps instead of one to access the most recently used 5 apps. Two, the apps are organized in a vertical pane instead of a carousel (though I know this is more habit/visual preference than a Con with the system UI). I think that we should either have the option to activate a carousel into the Home Screen or the carousel should be added as a permanent strip between the Tab titles and the apps on the Home Screen. I think this is much more efficient than forcing me to either tap Recent tab, the box/app-reveal icon, or the other associated tab (i.e., Books for my Bible).This is a small UI problem and easily fixable. Previously, we had sliders for turning things like On or Off (e.g., the Wi-Fi). However, they did not give a visual cue that they needed to be slid. They have changed these sliders to now look like sliders! Excellent, right? The “sliders” are shown as small floating dot inside of an oval that is orange when the dot is on the left to indicate On and gray when the dot is on the right for Off. HOWEVER, a sliding motion does not move this dot! Instead of sliding, we must TAP them. It makes no sense. If a tap is required, the icon should not be shown as a slider, it should be shown as a tap-switch; otherwise the sliding motion we used in OS 4 should be preserved to go with the much better visual slider cue.4. BUILT-IN APPSI will only speak about the system Apps I use most frequently, as I cannot comment on Apps that I didn’t really use in OS 4.Calculator: This app has a huge new problem. They got rid of the “C” and “CE” buttons! Thus, as you tap out “5 + 5” you can see the answer “10” below it on the screen like any normal smartphone calculator. No need to tap “=“ to find the answer. But how do you delete the numbers? No “C” button. You must first tap “=.” The “DEL” (which had only functioned as a backspace) will then change to “CLR” and then a really slow animation erases the answer so that you can begin a new problem. There is in fact space on the calculator to keep both the DEL and CLR buttons visible at the same time without squeezing, shrinking, or removing any other keys.Clock: The clock changes LOOK nice, but they don’t work nicer. When you want to set an alarm clock, for example, you no longer get a big number keypad where you can tap out the time you want. Instead, you get a tiny little clock-like circle of numbers 1 - 12 to choose the hour to wake. For selecting the minutes, you get the same clock circle for numbers 5 - 60. These numbers are so small and squeezed that it is not easy to tap out the right time on first touch. Worse, if you want a time like 5:22pm you have to do a really difficult tap on :25, then slowly slide your finger until the :22 appears. By the time you lift your finger, you may accidentally change it to :23 or :21 from the movement during the lift of your fingertip. This is an easily fixable problem. If they insist on keeping this interface because it is more attractive then a number pad, then blow up the clock…use more screen real estate. Then add little dots to represent the minutes between the 5-minute increments currently shown.Camera: I like how the camera added the ability to tap either a photograph shutter button or a video record button from the same menu without having to switch between Camera and Video mode. This is an excellent end-user consideration to make when adding/changing a feature. Also, I’m not sure if we had HDR previously; if not, I’m glad this was added. At the very least, the camera adds a “HDR recommended” text to the screen during camera mode if you do not have it activated but it might help the shot be better.E-mail: This has become slightly streamlined, making deletion of messages easier, and making the composing icon more meaningful by changing it to an always-visible pen in the bottom right corner. I like the e-mail client on here and I’m glad they didn’t tweak it too much.Calendar: I haven’t used the calendar very much in the past or even now. The reason: it is useless when you set a reminder but the reminder does not remind you. I want my calendar reminders to go off with their alarm and turn on my screen just like the alarm clock does. Instead, you won’t see the reminder until you take the time to turn on your Fire and look at your notifications. It works nicely in terms of its Cloud connectedness and its interface (if your requirements are relatively basic), but its inability to wake the device up and actually remind me of events leaves its purposes moot at this point.5. BUGSI have encountered several bugs, but cannot remember all of them at the moment. The only one I can remember at the moment happens when you have a Folder of apps on the Home Screen and open that folder, then open an app inside the folder. Once in the app, if you tap the Home circle at the bottom of the screen, it will bring you back to the Home Screen, BUT it will automatically enter the Keyboard mode trying to make you edit the name of the Folder containing the app you just accessed.CONCLUSIONI am aware of how long this review of OS 5 is, but I hope that bears witness to how much I love Amazon and how much I had loved my Fire HDX 8.9 before this attractive but non-user-friendly OS 5 was automatically downloaded and added to my device. I think that many user’s who read this will notice that they share in my dislike for many of the changes. I am not against change, I am simply against changes that do one or more of the following:Favor visual attractiveness over user friendliness (animations just to animate)Replace simplicity with unnecessary complexity (two taps instead of one)Overlook simple design issues (small buttons on large screens)I think that Amazon has made all three of these mistakes with the release of OS 5. Moreover, I am really unhappy with how hard Amazon has pushed shopping with the added swipe on the lock screen as well as with favoring a Shop tab over a much more widely used tab like Photos. I feel like I signed up for having the ads on the lock screen when I bought this; I knew, accepted, and sometimes even liked these. It keeps the lock screen from being boring. However, I feel like Amazon is favoring its bank account more than the end user with these choices, and it does not seem very Amazon-like to me.As a result, I give 4.5 stars for the HDX 8.9 tablet itself, but 2 stars for OS 5, which makes the 4.5 star tablet work like a 3-star tablet.Amazon has always been fantastic with customer service; it is why I have remained a customer since its humble beginnings. And I believe in Amazon’s ability to fix these issues. They have always done a great job responding to customer feedback. I really hope that by posting this review across all devices that at least one developer sees it and responds with actions that get me back to loving my Kindle Fire.Thanks for reading and hope this helps you make an informed decision about the OS ecosystem you may or may not want to enter.

title: You get what you pay for.
body: After living with three of these with three children, I simply cannot recommend this device. We have three of these, and we use Kindle Free Time with a subscription.Despite buying a 64GB SD card and telling everything to install to the SD card, the 8GB internal storage basically cripples this device.About once every other week one of the children comes to me with their Kindle not functioning because it has run out of space. Usually this manifests as the WiFi not working. I am not sure who thought it was a good idea to disable the WiFi when it runs out of it's tiny internal 8GB memory, but that's what it does - I guess to keep you from downloading more stuff?When the device runs out of space, which is frequent, your only option is to use the 'Manage Storage' feature. A slow, buggy, crash ridden application that most decidedly doesn't allow you to manage the space on the device. It will frequently report large amounts of space as "Other" with no way to manage or delete it - once I had to do a factory reset to get rid of a large block of space. Currently, again despite telling the device to install everything to the SD card, my son's tablet is out of space.When you open the "Manage Storage" list, be prepared to wait. It can take several minutes until it is usable, in the meantime items will be disabled and say "Refreshing", and while they are refreshing quite possibly report the incorrect size. Currently it says my son's got 2GB of Apps and Games.If you click on the list of apps to try to delete something, you are presented a massive list of everything installed, including system items like "Proxy Handler" and "HTML Viewer". None of this is sorted by where it is installed, or even by size - at least at first. Get this - it starts out sorted randomly, as the sizes are loading, and then, once ALL of the sizes are loaded, it sorts by size. Hope you are patient, it took me awhile to figure this out.So finally you can see the largest apps at top, and click on them. You are presented a technical looking screen, with everything grayed out 'Uninstall', 'Force Stop', 'Move to SD card', 'Clear Data' - all disabled. There is a little three dot button in the upper right, and when you click that you get "Uninstall for all users", and oddly it's not disabled, it works. How does this differ from the disabled 'Uninstall' button, not a clue.Frequently you will find apps that have the "Move to Tablet" button enabled, indicating that they were installed to the SD card, but they still report 50-100MB of space taken in the "App on Device" column. How does this happen? Not a clue.So you spend a nice half an hour being confused by this very technical, clunky "Manage Storage" application, and hopefully you can get the device enough free space that it starts functioning again. But rest assured, your kid will just go off and start installing applications that will eventually fill up the internal space yet again. It's inevitable. There are no 'don't show my kids apps that won't install to the SD card', or no 'don't use internal storage for application storage' settings. If your kid uses the device and downloads apps, even with everything set to use the SD card, you will eventually run out of internal storage space.Oh, and there's a nice "Archive" option, which offers to archive less frequently used items. Every time I've clicked it I get "No content available for archiving". Helpful.Next up, offline play. So you take the Kindle on the road, you are tooling down Route 66, and suddenly your kid starts complaining their apps don't work. You check, and sure enough, apps that are downloaded and installed on the Kindle are saying they need WiFi to continue. It turns out apps have to phone home periodically over the Internet, and if they go long enough without checking in, they won't work without WiFi. Isn't that wonderful? There's no way to tell all the apps to checkin before you go on the road, it appears to just happen randomly via usage. So, if there's a favorite app your kid hasn't used in awhile, make sure to open it and use it before you go on the road.Then there's the times when they just lock up and become mostly unresponsive. I just had to restart my daughter's because some app or background process made it mostly unresponsive. We've actually taught our kids how to hard reset the Kindle, because this happens so frequently. Sad.Overall I'd say that this is frustrating, clunky, underpowered device with too little internal space and no good ways to manage that space. The app DRM is frustrating and frequently locks your kids out of applications when offline. If your kids can get into their apps because there's WiFi, and if the WiFi is working because there's enough free internal space, well then it's just an OK device.Original Review: What can I say, it's a freakin $50 tablet that performs a hell of a lot better than the Kindle Fire 1.0 I bought a long time ago for quite a bit more money. Does it have an amazing screen? No, not really. Is it "HD"? No not really. Is it the best tablet you can buy for $50, unequivocally yes. Is this a thinly disguised attempt to sell you the razor real cheap in hopes you'll be hooked on the subscription for the blades? Yep - unabashedly this is what Bezos is trying to do. I am 100% aware of it, and I still bought it.For the kids, it's really a no brainer, you can buy 3-5 of these things for the price of an iPad, and my kids love their kindles. Just make sure to buy an extra SD card (32GB is nice and will run you ~$11, 64GB is really nice and should run you < $20). Sign up for a "Free Time" subscription and your kids get unlimited access to a curated app garden - highly recommended Download Amazon movies to that spacious SD card and take them on road with you kids, who needs a DVD player in the car.Even if you're not a kid it's a good little device. It's snappy enough for lightweight web browsing. The email client is actually usable, and the new Fire OS is a huge improvement. Though it makes a great book reader, if all you're into is books, get a Paper White.The speaker (that's right, there's only one) sucks, hard. Get some head phones for the thing if you care about sound quality.

title: You get what you pay for... lacking useful apps, no useful BROWSER!
body: PLEASE EXCUSE THE LENGTH OF THIS… When I found out that the Amazon Fire was only $50, I was stoked! Even if I only used it for reading, it would be worth it! (Regular new Kindles are way more expensive!) So, I clicked my order thru Amazon Prime... I've had it now, for a little over two weeks and have given it a good college try. Today, I'm returning it - and here goes my story:The interface is okay and the graphics are really good. Everything seems really well thought out. Like, my Grandparents could even figure this out... I love how small and lightweight it is. But lemme start from the beginning: I'm pretty techy - and at any given moment, my carry-on/bag contains these things: MacBook Pro, iPad, Old Kindle (Keyboard generation), iPhone and my Amazon Basics headphones (that are uber awesome, and only $15 btw.. Link here: [[ASIN:B00NBEWB4U AmazonBasics Lightweight On-Ear Headphones - Black]] ).I'm also an avid music junkie and I read like crazy. I also marathon watch TV shows like OITNB & Walking Dead. I also am constantly on Trulia/Redfin because I enjoy dream shopping for houses for fun. I enjoy things that offer instant gratification - like googling. Which you can't do. Because Amazon hates Google I've discovered. But whatever, I also like downloading music.On this, the interface is actually pretty nice at first glance. It's basically a searchbar, and when you type something into it, it brings up your result (or something similar to your result if they don't have it... (Like TS 1989.) It's not as good as iTunes, and def not as smart, but it gives Amazon Prime suggestions based on what you're looking for and albums that you can listen to for free, which is really cool. It's cool because it's free. I'm not trying to be a jerk or anything, but WHERE'S THE NEW TAYLOR SWIFT ALBUM!?! Seriously guys...Okay, so I will Google it... Hmn... No Google.. Why? After pulling out my iPhone to figure out how to get to a browser on my Fire tablet (I should have known then...), I discovered Silk. Firefox and all other browsers seem to be blocked from Amazons App store (which is the only place you can get apps) Silk claims to use Amazon magic cloud fluff to make your browsing super fast. THIS IS A LIE. I tried both ways (with cloud assist stuff turned on, and turned off...) and it was the SLOWEST browser ever! It was like Netscape on dial-up! (I'm really not that old..). I tested my wireless connection to make sure my wifi was okay - it was just the Fire tablet! It was like it was being throttled! I would try and look something up - and I would set the tablet down and walk away and wait for it to load! It was terrible! NEW DISCOVERY: Every website you go to is recorded in the Amazon cloud and then sent back to you and your tablet (not. fast. at. all.) -- but think of the privacy issues with this! Lets say, for example, I decide to look up "monkey balls" out of curiosity. My monkey balls search goes from my tablet, to my own wifi, to amazon, then to the actual search, then back to amazon, and then they sends the monkey balls to my tablet. Why does Amazon need to know all of my browsing??? Leave my balls alone! (I promise, I'm not some weird hermit conspiracy theorist - but this is pretty blatant)SPEED TEST: SEE PHOTOS. I went to a website (speedof.me) thru Silk, and while it was loading, I went onto Chrome on my laptop and did the speed test at the same time. As of writing this, the test is still processing on the Fire tablet (5 minutes later, the screen went to sleep - without ever being able to complete the test...) - I am giving up.The Amazon App store is really lacking. When I searched for Redfin or Trulia, I was given a result of Realtors.com or Zillow. These are the only apps downloadable in the App store. REALLY!?! This is getting ridiculous.Moving on to the Kindle part. So, I thought that this would be better than my Kindle keyboard and less dangerous then holding my big ipad when I'm reading in bed. Seriously. I almost lost a tooth a few times when I would fall asleep and it would come crashing down onto my chin. So... at night, I dim my bedside table light onto it's lowest setting and a turn whatever I'm reading also down to the lowest setting. On my iPad Kindle App, it can be dimmed with the black background to the point where it almost disappears. I know about BLUE LIGHT (google it, it's real.) and I try not to light up my face at night, because I know that it will keep me awake all night long.The Fire has a BLUE LIGHT setting, which basically keeps everything at the same brightness, but changes the backlight to a dull orange color. It is helpful when browsing at night (even with it's distorted color), but in the READING APP - where I think, it is MOST IMPORTANT - it fails miserably. SO, the way to use this is to turn off the BLUE light filter, and to adjust the background color to black with white letters - and to dim it to the lowest setting. Okay, but EVEN turned down to it's DARKEST setting, the Fire tablet was still SO EFFING BRIGHT that it lit up my face and the headboard behind me. My husband even woke up and asked me to turn down the brightness because it was bothering him! It was like I was laying in bed with a flippin spotlight on my face.That was the last straw. I understand, you get what you pay for... I shouldn't have expected more - but I did! I expected that I could at least use this to read with.. peacefully.. in the dark... without looking like I was shining a flashlight in my face. BTW: I was super into my book, and the blue light kept me awake until almost 3AM. On the plus side, the battery appears to last forever.

title: a solid "eh."
body: I would never have purchased one of these myself, for reasons I'll go into later, but it so happens I was given one at work to evaluate for free. Never one to turn down a bargain, I accepted eagerly.The device itself rates a solid "eh." The design is not terrible, but it could have been better thought out. The plcement and organization of the controls befuddles me. For example, the beveled edges are almost entirely taken up with two sets of next and previous buttons. Why do we need two sets of the same button? And why are they placed at the edges, where I am constantly pressing them accidentally when I hold the device? Why are the chicklet-sized keys so small, when there is room on the bottom of the device for them to be bigger? And why are they all angled diagonally? Why are the power and wireless switches located on the back, where they are covered when the device is in its leather case? Why is the headphone jack on the bottom, and where in gods name is the SD card slot? I can't even *find* it. None of these is a deal breaker, but they are annoying. Also, the plastic case feels cheap and it's not very attractive.Thankfully, reading E-books is not as difficult as the poor design would indicate. Select a book and use your huge previous and next buttons to page through, that's it. The E-ink display is clear and readable, and it's relatively easy to search, place bookmarks, and look up words. Leaving notes is a bit harder due to the odd keyboard design-you're not going to want to type anything lengthy.Whispernet and the kindle store work extremely well-this is easily the best-thought out feature on the device (I wonder why...). It is easy to connect to the kindle store, and once there, you can read reviews and even download sample chapters for free. It's a shame I won't ever use it. Why not? Kindle uses a proprietary e-book format that isn't readable on any other device, which makes the kindle store and the kindle ebook reader inextricably coupled. It can read files in other formats (text and Mobi), but anything you buy from Amazon will only be readable on the kindle. What the consumer ebook market needs is an ebook version of the MP3 file format: something that's supported by the majority of the ebook reader hardware. Until that happens, you effectively don't own the content you pay for-you can only use it so long as you use the service and the device it's designed to display on, and only so long as that platform and format are supported.One of the reasons I like buying electronic music is because the selection is better than in any physical store. Ebooks, however, have a long way to go in that regard. The Kindle store has better selection than any online ebook store I've been to yet, but there are still titles I can't get in electronic format. So even if I had the money to convert my seven plus bookcases worth of physical books to ebooks, it would be impossible to do so completely.The Kindles other features sounded cool until I really examined them. Sure, it sounds great to get newspapers delivered wirelessly to your kindle...until I remembered that a lot of that stuff is free on the web, and I can already access it on my handheld. It's also worth noting that the e-ink display can't really handle images. You'll either get a grainy greyscale image, or nothing at all. So it's actually better to read newspapers online, where you can see color images and charts. This goes double for magazines, which have lots of glossy photographs. And why, oh why would I pay to read blogs when I can get the content for free via RSS? This is just silly.The device also has a web browser, but trying to surf the web over a slow wireless connection, in greyscale, using a scroll wheel and next and back buttons, in an exercise for a masochist. While it technically has a music player, it lacks even basic functionality such as pause, forward, and back buttons. This is essentially a single-use device-it reads ebooks, and that's all it does. In my opinion, that makes the $400 price tag very hard to justify. Typically, a device that expensive will do lots of things....like an xbox 360 or an iPhone, both of which can be had for that amount.My feeling on the kindle before I had one was "eh," and now that I've used it, I see nothing to make me change my stance. I still have a problem with the proprietary, DRMed-out-the-wazoo file format. I'm unimpressed with the physical design. I see no use for the newspaper and magazine subscriptions, and I consider the blogreading feature to be highway robbery. Don't get me wrong, it's nice to have-I loaded it up with some free ebook content from project gutenberg and am currently working my way through the collected works of Dickens. But at $400, I would never have spent my own money on it.

title: If you're on a mission to replace your Ipod Classic, please read this before you buy.
body: The major points of concern in this review are the following;1. Online registration is required before virtually any features of this device are accessible; even those that don't require or benefit from online content.2. The user interface is clunky and hard to customize, frequently popping up with unwanted messages. In general, it's designed more like an online store than a mobile media device.3. The device's packaging is dishonest about the total storage space that it offers.4. The device does not allow proper sorting of music, pictures or video into categories, and I have yet to find any app that fixes this.Technology replacement war journal 10/06/16.Just received this device today, after reading, in its description, that it could be expanded to a storage capacity sufficient to replace the Ipod Classic. Having asked many direct questions about this online, and receiving few straight answers, I decided that the low price made it viable to experiment with.I have bad experiences with non-Apple mobile players (specifically Samsung, which just lumped all my songs together into one colossal list in alphabetical order,) making it impossible to divide songs into categories or to select what order to listen to them in, and therefore, such players were inferior to my old CD and cassette players.My first warning sign was when this device required me to register online. I have never connected a video player to the internet in my life, but I somehow found a way to do this. Had I failed to access a WiFi network of some type, nearly all of the features of this device would be inaccessible; even many of those, like playing music stored on the device, which are not dependent on online connectivity, nor do they even benefit from it.The next problem that I ran into was a lack of documentation included with the device. This device doesn't come with a booklet or anything like that, and while it has a brief "tutorial" of sorts, this only outlines very general functions of the device in a very general sense, and only once the device is connected to a WiFi Network and registered. Even once it is registered, the "home page" on the device displays only a number of pre-installed programs for general functions of the device; a clock, a calculator, camera, links to online content (NOT content contained in storage; you need to go looking for that,) and so on. There are tabs for general categories of media, but again, the default displays in each tab (which I have yet to find a way to customize) start with online stuff I don't want, rather than my own media, and what's the point of a high storage capacity if you can't play your own media?Speaking of a high storage capacity, the baseline capacity written on the box is 8 gigs, and it's a bald-faced lie. According to the "storage" setting of the device, the total storage capacity of the device is 5.63 gigs, over a gig of which is taken up by internal programs that come with the device.However, I could take the hard-to-navigate interface, the low base storage (provided it's expandable,) and the setup requiring WiFi access if the device just did for me what the 15-year-old, smaller, less-powerful device called the Ipod did; namely, allow you to arrange media into categories for viewing. I, again, have found that it does not allow you to do this. You can create individual "playlists," and sort by genre, artist, etc, but if you want to subdivide playlists into playlist categories, or "folders" of playlists, this is impossible on this device, even if it allows you to use your own media, and it seems to have a problem with this too.I create and edit video and audio files for online use, and I listen to some of them while I'm at work. If I had to depend on this device, I would never be able to listen to any of my own creations again. This is because it has a tendency to make certain files unplayable on the device, if it finds them different from what's in its online catalog. I put this point last, because by this point, the device has already failed to meet my needs, and adding one more piece of idiocy to the pile doesn't make things any worse. The whole ethos around which this device is designed is contrary to the purpose for which I use mobile devices, and I hope that more people will become aware of its crippling limitations soon.In the meantime, I'm going back into the battlefield, in search of a 120+gb, media-playing, mobile device with Ipod Classic or Classic-like media management capabilities, and I hope you'll join me in this fight.

title: Returned it!
body: I am anxiously awaiting Kindle 2.0 and I'll tell you why - ergonomics. I really, really wanted to like the Kindle. The concept of having your entire library in a small, searchable electronics device that didn't melt your eyes like staring at a computer screen does really appealed to me. I'm an avid reader of novels, computer books, and lots of other things. An electronic reader with an E-Ink screen made sense. The Sony E-Reader looked good, but lacks the content that Amazon's Kindle offers. I think that almost everything about the Kindle is great, but - the ergonomics are terrible!How are you supposed to HOLD this thing in your hands?
