I spent the last 24 hours reading books, newspaper, and magazines on it. The keyboard surprisingly stays out of the way; I never accidentally hit a key when I was using it. BUT - those large Previous/Next keys on the side make it impossible to hold easily. You basically have two choices - hold it by the bottom/middle or hold it very gently by the sides, so as not to activate the buttons. I hold my books by the sides when I read them because it's very comfortable and natural to do so. You can't do this on a Kindle because you'll accidentally switch pages if you do. The design is terrible! It was very frustrating to use because I had to pick it up a special way so as not to activate the page turning buttons - I couldn't just pick it up like a book because I might lose my page by hitting the Previous or Next button.I can't emphasize this enough...those large side buttons are the reason I'm returning my Kindle. It's very frustrating to use because you press the keys on accident all the time and it makes it not very comfortable to hold. Amazon, if you're reading this, please take some of the following ideas to your drawing board for version 2.0:1. Put comfortable hand grips on the sides - I want to be able to hold my book easily! I'm not exactly sure why there's a rubber grip on the back of the book but not the sides - my fingers aren't designed to hold a flat surface like the back of the book!2. Move the Previous/Next buttons somewhere else that is still easily accessible. It's not easy to operate a Kindle with one hand and it definitely should be. Also, my fingers want to grip the sides of the screen but since the buttons are there, I can't. This was annoying to the point that I returned mine.3. Why isn't there a backlight or built-in light? Books have been around for hundreds of years and Amazon's goal is to replace them with the presumably better Kindle E-Reader...so why doesn't it have a light so I can read it in the dark?4. Why is there a physical Wireless On/Off switch? I forgot to turn it off and it drained my battery. It should be a software switch that activates when it needs to access the Internet, unless you're on an airplane or something in which case you can put it into Airplane Mode.5. Why is it white? Mine was dirty less than an hour after I got it. A darker color would be appreciated!6. Why is the Kindle oriented vertically? When I read, I read in widescreen, i.e. two pages open in my novel. When I tilted the Kindle and held it horizontally, it was MUCH more comfortable to hold. The Sony E-Reader has this feature and I think it's great.7. I want good document conversion software for PDF, DOC, TXT, etc. I have a ton of material on my computer that I'd highly prefer reading on an E-reader, but it gets kind of mangled when I import it. If I could read my own content with good formatting as well as books, that'd be awesome.8. Why do I have to pay nearly full price for an electronic book when I'm not paying for ink, paper, or distribution costs? I know the free EVDO wireless is costing Amazon money, but if you sold your books at 50% off people would be all over this thing, despite it's high price tag.9. Please offer a service to trade in paper books for their electronic versions. I have bookshelves upon bookshelves of books I've purchased, but I don't want to buy them again just so that I can have a digital copy of them - I've already paid for them!10. PLEASE put a store button on the Kindle! I don't want to have to go into a sub-menu just to access the Kindle store - I want to hit a button and be taken directly in!Other than those gripes, the Kindle is a fantastic piece of gadgetry. I hope that Amazon gets their entire library converted soon because it would be great to have access to any content I want, whenever I want it. Amazon, please listen to your users because you have the potential to make the Kindle great with just a few hardware modifications.

title: Owner, Long Time ebook reader
body: I have always been interested in reading periodicals and downloaded web articles on a portable device. I have progressed through a Palm, various notebooks, a treo, a Blackberry, and now on the Sony Reader and the Kindle. The main reason for using an ebook reader is the eink is significantly easier to read than any other digital screen. This is one area where the Kindle falls significantly short. The 2nd generation Sony Reader has an uncannily sharp screen. When you look at it it almost looks like the letters are little black stickers stuck on an off-white screen. When I first started the Kindle I immediately wanted to turn up the contrast (not possible). The letters are not quite black and the screen is not quite white so there is considerably more strain in reading the Kindle than the Sony Reader.The second reason to use an ereader is that the battery life is so long that is not really an issue, eink screens do not use power except to turn the page so they last about a week. The Sony and Kindle are equivalent if you turn off the largely-unnecessary wireless on the Kindle.The Kindle is larger and heavier than the Sony Reader although not noticeably. The real problem lies in the Kindle design; every edge of the device is surrounded with long easily pressed buttons so that there is ultimately only one way to hold it (both thumbs by the front logo) any other way to relieve stress by shifting positions while reading generally results in a button press. The Sony is smaller, lacks the obtrusive keyboard and has small convenient buttons that allow you multiple ways of holding the device.Getting media on both systems is identical although the Kindle doesn't seem to have any convenient way to organize files, resulting in a long list of titles that must be sorted through. The Sony has a far superior and more intuitive interface. The Kindle also has a strange secondary lcd which you must click multiple time to take any action in the user interface. I suppose they did this because the screen redraws are slow with eink. Sony's solution with multiple buttons down one side is more intuitive.The Kindle offers no way to rename or edit the file info of files on the system to improve organization. The Sony software and third party software does this nicely on the Sony Reader. As a result, Gutenberg books and some content from Fictionwise gets labeled with strange unchangable confusing labels. I currently subscribe to three drm-free magazines from Fictionwise. I also subscribe to two newspapers from Amazon. The Amazon papers are expensive and fairly incomplete versions of the respective papers and I can't load them on any other device or my computer. The Fictionwise magazines may be loaded on any device or read on the computer. They are also complete versions.The Sony device provides a far superior reading experience in terms of comfort, weight, product design, and easy of reading. The Kindle seems of far less build quality and no effort seems to have gone into the design or user interface. Where Amazon did seem to put all its effort is into the store/business model. It is far easier to purchase material for the Kindle and Amazon has a far greater selection, but the single restrictive format means that you will never really own your books, you are just renting them for the life of the Kindle business or until a better device comes out. Of course then you will need to repurchase all your books. This isn't so painful when referring to one-off periodicals such as newspapers, which most people don't archive. Unacceptable for books.The Sony covers many more formats than the Kindle although they have to be generated to the specific screen size of the device. You can convert formats to the Kindle using Amazon's staggeringly inconvenient email conversion, which has yet to convert a document correctly.All total I prefer the Sony, I read material from Fictionwise, Gutenberg Manybook, and the web. The screen is better. I have not bought books from the Sony store and don't recommend buying them from the Amazon store. Perhaps when the quality of the hardware improves to the level of the Sony and the business model changes to allow conversion and the right of resale (which Amazon previously said was critical for books) then I will be able to rate the Kindle more highly than two stars. Right now it feels like a rushed-to-market attempt to impose a close drm system rather than a carefully designed means to provide convenient content to the customer.

title: Not what I thought it would be but idea is brilliant
body: Ok so some people are getting caught up on the same things I did and found very few useful things on the Kindle Discussion boards. Let me address a few things...Pros1. If you already have a sleuth of eBooks then how do you get them to the Kindle? Albeit there are instructions in the Kindle user guide and discussion boards, I found that I still could not get this to work. I emailed my PDF to my account@free.kindle.com and sure enough it came back within minutes to my hotmail account with the supposed files but when I put them on the Kindle it all looked like garbage. Just a bunch of dots at the bottom of my Kindle screen. I noticed that my files were named filename.pdf and were returned from Amazon as filename.pdf.azw. I tried removing the .pdf and just leaving the .azw in the filename and it still did not work. Lo and behold I've discovered MobiPocket Creator. It converts your PDF documents into eBooks. It's final results are .PRC files in which a Kindle and any other eBook reader can read. You'll have to google this one. It's pretty easy to do.2. I am a software developer so the books I need are usually 1000 pages or more and they are all technical. Imagine trying to lug around a shelf of books all day long. The Kindle store indeed has technical books as well!Now here comes the cons...1. I couldn't organize my content. I was so proud that I bought a Kindle that I showed everyone my Kindle. But everytime I did I found that my esoterical books were showing and there were some people I didn't want to know that I was reading those kinds of books who saw them on my Kindle. They never asked about it but I quickly tried to figure out how to get it so it would hide such content. There is a Content Manager on the Kindle but it's only for moving content between the Kindle and the memory card. The book titles still showed up in the listing. Regardless of how much I connect the Kindle to my PC and organize its folders, the Kindle still just searches the folders and lists any files it finds.2. If I couldn't find what I needed then I thought I could get an account on Safari books online and download the books from there to the Kindle. But I found that Safari books online has it fair share of problems too. Like it's more expensive for each book than Amazon. (Don't let the tokens concept fool you. You may as well pay the $40 for the printed version since it takes 20 tokens to buy a book which costs $40 to buy the 20 tokens.) Once you download a book you can't just do with it as you please. It has license management crap attached to it that prevents me from converting it to a Kindle ebook using the aforementioned freeware.3. Which leads me to con 3 but for some people it's a pro. Amazon works daily, I believe, to try to get more books available on the Kindle. This may involve getting publishers and distributors to agree to something that may hurt their profits or require them to digitize every book which may add extra expenses, not to mention all the legal stuff that goes into it. But it's a slow process and it seems like they are adding only hundreds of books each day and they seem to be more in the categories that I don't care for like mystery and scary novels. I have 271 esoterical and miscellaneous books of which only 33 are available in the Kindle store. I have over 100 technical books of which 0 are available in the Kindle store.4. In addition to con 3, I've already paid $522 for books that would cost me $269 to put onto my Kindle. This is more a gripe with publishers than Kindle. And it's more of a problem with all companies who are trying to offer books in an electronic format. I think it's unfair that I have to spend more money to get the same books I already have into a digital format. Yes this may cost publishers and distributors more money, but it's still unfair to those who have already purchased printed versions. Now I know it's unreasonable to expect publishers and distributors to look into the future and know what format books will be offered in, but it's also unfair to not offer those who have purchased old formats - at a higher cost than the new formatted version - a free upgrade to the new format.5. Also the Kindle discussion board here on amazon.com is somewhat useless if you can't do a search on it. Time is money for some people and they don't want to spend time sifting through hundreds of threads and thousands of posts trying to find answers to their questions.

title: Kindle FreeTime isn't ready for PrimeTime for a modern family with tweens (great for toddlers!)
body: I've really tried to learn to like this device a lot...I just can't seem to make it do what I want. If you're an adult user, with your own Amazon account it is a really good device at a very big discount when compared to other Android tablets. However, my review won't be about it's efficacy as an adult device. My review is from the perspective of a parent of two children ages 9 & 10. I purchased these devices with the intent to allow my children access to the kindle book store and lending library. With the occasional reward of playing games or watching movies for good reading habits. All the marketing sounds great, and makes it sound as though this device will do exactly what I want...well that's completely wrong.So here it is in a nutshell. I want to give my kids their own profile on the device and let them peruse the book store/library, pick out books and read them, and even look for apps but ultimately seek my approval for purchases. This all seems quite straight forward...however, it's not to be so.I first tried Kindle FreeTime as it sounded exactly like what I wanted. However, I quickly discovered that FreeTime lacks the type of videos and books that are appropriate for children reading above Grade 4. There's a wealth of videos, books, and apps presented, but they are simple "bob the builder" or "see spot run" type books. My children are far beyond the pre-school reading level at this point. So I then tried converting their Kindle FreeTime Unlimited profiles to Teen profiles. Immediately all the apps, videos, and books vanished and there was no store for them to peruse on their own. I was to become the person who had to hunt down things I'd think they'd like and then deploy it to their device remotely. Which this feature works great, but is not at all viable in a modern working family. I simply don't have the time to curate all their books for them. I want the liberty of setting a ratings limit or password for purchase and let them do the shopping and then come to me with "can i download this?" and at that point I can put in the pin code and away they go. **Important note: you cannot use sideloaded ebooks, or apps on FreeTime**So this takes me to what is step next, since FreeTime is a wash....There's two choices I suppose. 1) I let them use my account with Parental Controls turned on or 2) I create them their own adult Amazon account. The big problem with number 2 is that managing content on another persons device becomes problematic and time consuming. Not to mention i'd have to set it up with its own forms of payment, etc. Just simply not ideal. So this takes me to option 1 - I set it up using my account and locked it down pretty tight with Parental Controls. So now they can use sideloaded books from the public library or apps from the Android marketplace. That's Great! However, here's where it all goes wonky again...Sideloaded ebooks don't appear in books, they appear in Docs...well that's just dumb! And they don't pull the metadata showing the book title, they show the filename. This is ok for supersmart kids, but might prove a problem for kids who aren't so tech savvy.Ok. So we can sideload and let them see downloaded ebooks and the like which is good...it's a step in the right direction. But here's where it all goes to crap. If you block the store they cannot shop for books, if you unblock the store they can see all your purchases in the Amazon shop app. This is a huge bummer because who wants your kids snooping on your purchases of laundry detergent or their birthday presents. C'mon Amazon!?!? Really?! They can't shop the Kindle book store without having full access to my shopping history?! This is just dumb. And I fully expected that in it's 5th generation this product would be mature enough to handle households better than this. This just simply isn't ready for PrimeTime with a family that wants to give kids access to a device like this but maintain a little control on buying habits and content. In truth I would have had a better experience on a pure Android device, but alas the price tag on a Nexus is far higher than that of a Kindle Fire.The FreeTime product needs to mature very quickly...This is just sloppy now and really only ideal for parents of toddlers.

title: Child's profile purposefully limited in a blatant money grab by Amazon
body: If you are looking for a little tablet to stream Amazon Prime videos and connect to Facebook, you cannot beat this tablet for the price. However, it also touts itself as being great for kids because of the feature that allows you to set up a child's profile and restrict content. This feature would be great except it is very poorly executed, and has been very frustrating for me (and my child), considering that I bought this cheap tablet primarily for use by my child. Therefore, I cannot give this tablet more than 2 stars because of how bad the child's profile feature is. My review will focus primarily on using this tablet with children.First, a note on battery life: it sucks. With the screen turned off and not plugged in, the battery drains at least 25% overnight. So in 2 days or less of no use, your tablet will be dead. Therefore you really need to shut the tablet completely off when you are done with it, and it takes a long time to boot back up -- already a downside for an impatient child.Now, the child's profile. Theoretically, you are supposed to be able to curate your child's tablet content by adding content to your child's profile. However, when it comes to videos, you can only add content one of two ways: either by owning it (purchasing it through Amazon), or by paying an additional $5/month ($3/month for Prime members) for "FreeTime Unlimited," which is essentially Amazon Prime for kids. As far as I can tell, there is absolutely no difference in content between "FreeTime Unlimited" streaming and "Amazon Prime" streaming -- the only difference is whether I can put it on my child's profile or not. My kid can already watch Daniel Tiger etc. with my Amazon Prime account. Yet, I cannot provide him access to Daniel Tiger on his child's profile unless I pay an additional $3/month? This is a blatant money grab, and it pissed me off enough that I wrote to Amazon customer service about it (and received the watery reply "sorry you're disappointed and we'll pass on your concerns.")The second serious problem with the child's profile is with regard to apps. I already cannot add videos to my child's profile because I refuse to pay another monthly subscription fee for content I already have access to through my Prime account. So instead, I tried downloading some children's games and adding them to my child's profile. So far, so good. Except that, when you reboot the Fire (which, because of the bad battery life, you will have to do on a regular basis, see above), for some reason the child's profile demands that you must be connected to WiFi when launching the apps for the first time after reboot, even though those same apps are STILL DOWNLOADED TO MY FIRE. If the apps are already downloaded, and accessible through my adult profile, why on earth would we need WiFi to launch them through the kids' profile? This is a huge problem considering that my child uses the tablet most often when we are traveling (flying or on long car trips) when we do not have access to WiFi. On our most recent plane trip, I just had to let the kid play his games through my adult profile.In short, the child's profile has pointless limitations on it that serve no purpose that I can see except to manipulate you into paying Amazon more money, and these limitations render the tablet's child profile basically unusable. Not cool, Amazon.Note: This same tablet is available at double the price ($100) as a "Fire Kids Edition" which is basically this tablet plus a rubber case, a 2-year warranty, and a year of FreeTime Unlimited included. You can buy the rubber case separately for $25, and the already low price of this tablet means the warranty is probably not worth it (you are better off taking the gamble that your kids won't break the tablet with the case on it, unless your kids are especially destructive). Based on this calculation, and the fact that I already pay for Prime so I already have unlimited streaming of children's TV content, I decided to buy the tablet for $50 rather than the "kids" tablet for $100.

title: Why I want to Kindle my Kindle
body: I really wanted to like the Kindle. And in fact, until an hour ago, I did. I was able to overlook most of the issues that other people have with it (such as the DRM, or the design, or the pricing). I'm convinced that Amazon is on to something, and soon we will all read books this way. In fact, in the time I've had it, I've undergone something of a reading renaissance. Its just so easy to discover new books, and they're so easy to buy.But today, as I sit here on a plane to Brazil, having just spent something like $30 on books and magazines, I've decided that I'm done. Once I get back from my trip, I am packing it up and shipping it back.Why? No not because the buttons are too easy to press or the case is lame. Although the case is lame. Rather, because the Kindle has horrible power management. It's terrible, and for the second consecutive time, it's totally out of batteries just when I want to use it. I'm staring at 9 more hours on a plane, and I am stuck carrying around a useless brick and have nothing to read. I find it hard to imagine a worse user experience.The first time was soon after I first got the device. I had been carrying it around for a few days and didn't realize then that the batteries drain, even in sleep mode. To keep it from killing the batteries, you have to switch off the wireless and power on the back, which I didn't do. I picked it up to use it after 3-4 days or so, and it wouldn't start up. It was a brick. At the time I thought, no problem! I'll just plug it into a USB port. It has a mini usb port on the bottom, and the charging light even lights up when you plug it into USB. No dice. Apparently, even though they are able to charge all sorts of devices, including Blackberries, MP3 players and coffee mug heaters using USB, there is no way to power the Kindle using USB. Well that's not entirely true. According to Amazon customer support, if you plug a kindle into a usb port for 24 hours, it MIGHT charge. Well, that's a terrible solution, but oh well. Its a first gen device, and now I know.Cut to today. In preparation for a trip I am taking, I charged up the Kindle, and made sure to turn off the power and wireless switches on the back. While sitting in the airport, I spent an hour or so downloading some books, magazines, etc. While I was doing this, we were called for boarding, and I threw the kindle in my bag. Just now, about 1-2 hours after takeoff, I pull out the Kindle, and of course its totally bricked. After 1-2 hours without a radio signal.So here's what I've gathered. The Kindle is an insufferable power hog. It battery drains very quickly, especially if you're on a plane. It can't be powered by convenient and common USB chargers or plugging into a computer, but instead requires a proprietary power adapter. And because of this, you the user are required to monitor its power situation like a nurse in a neo-natal ICU unit. After all, I can't always remember to turn off the wireless.Needless to say, I think this is totally unacceptable. I mean, how hard would it be to put in some logic or circuitry that shuts off the wireless when the battery is low, or only turns on the wireless every hour or something? There must be a way. Also, they have to figure out a way to charge and operate via USB. I'm not an EE, but I really find it hard to believe that they couldn't figure out how to use USB power better.All this is especially unacceptable on a device, that by all rights, should be at least half the price. I mean really. I've been spending money on my Kindle like crazy. Why should the early adopters like me foot the bill and subsidize Amazon as they build up what is now a pretty shoddy inventory of content. I bet most Kindle users spend a good chunk of change on content. If Amazon were smart, they'd drop the price to get more devices in the hands of users, so that they can build up their library faster.Anyway it soon won't be my problem, because I'm returning this thing.

title: I so much wish this did what it implies it does: Work (well) without WiFi. With Wi-Fi, I assume it works very well indeed.
body: This is advertised as for Wi-Fi and non Wi-Fi users alike.The Fire does not perform as advertised for non W-Fi users. Not even with all the warranty-legal work-rounds the reps here. and internet searches, can offer.As a bare start of the challenge list for Non W-Fi users, know that you will HAVE to find and connect to a Wi-Fi network ... even though you've already got the device showing up on your Amazon account, the device ITSELF thinks its unregistered until this step is complete). Else you have nothing past a paperweight. As in blank screen.The problem we found first is that for some reason this 2015" product comes with its date (and thus certificate acceptance) set Back To The Past by year "- 5". 2010, in our case. This on a device shipped as "new 2015" from Amazon And, you can't fix that 'refusal to function owing to invalid dated certificates' online even if you have online i-net access. Instead you have to find a source of free WiF (initially, it has to be one that doesn't require a log in screen for guests, since you can't get to that in new format) to use to get to the place to correct it.I've read of some pretty hard-core hacks that will fix this, but most of use prefer not to rely on messing with our brand new units based on a heavy duty hackers' suggestion.The unit is supposed to offer the option to play Wi-Fi free videos that you own (and load onto it using Wi-Fi, as there is NO other way to get a video on) in a series, auto-playing one episode after another. There IS a setting for that, and I set it to do so. Unfortunately, connecting to WiFi, after selecting that setting, auo-tosses you back to default land - which is where only 'one episode-at-a-time' of a series you totally paid for and transferred into the device places it. Again.You can indeed play Prime membership video content (as long as you WiFi-guest at a business that allows that, to place the material onto your Fire in the first place) - one episode at a time. AND for only 30 days. It takes hours. Occe done, one episode at a time, binge watching, here I come - the clocks ticking. Not what I was led to believe the Fire would do. Bleh.You CAN NOT drag and drop Amazon purchased videos into your Fire as you can Amazon purchased books. This is a decision by the manufacturers that I personally think costs them customers.If you can accept the reduced functionality for non Wi-Fi users, and can find a Wi-Fi to borrow for the absolutely necessary set-up and long video loading hours at a guest-Wi-Fi Supplying store, its OK. I don't enjoy hours sitting waiting for something I bought to load, but others may be more forgiving. After all, a lot of folks have Wi-Fi themselves. So, 2/5 stars for the Fire.I'd share my prodigiously lengthy attempts to fix these issues, via Chats and calls and e-mails with Amazon's reps into this review, but I feel that more than enough time has already gone into those futile pursuits. If you decide to give reps a try, bring a book, and some chamomile tea.Summary:I would not advise a friend who prefers to avoid Wi-Fi to be credulous about the claims made in the online ad.I want to, but can't, recommend this device to non Wi-Fi users. I would advise that any would-be buyer who doesn't have Wi-Fi, but does has a burning desire to pick one of these up, accept sharply limited, inconvenient video use and gracefully accept that their new toy will have surprisingly limited functionality. Also, any buyer should be aware that they must to have at least occasional access to guest WiFi - it is absolutely essential at the start.I have no doubt that the Fire works very well for those who have unlimited WiFi. The picture is good for the format, and the size is handy. I accept mine for the limited function is has, with hope for alternate decision from Amazon to better support the Non Wi-Fi community in the future.

title: Does what it says, but disappointed in quality
body: I own one of the first-generation Fires, from 2010, and have been thinking for a year or more that it's time to upgrade. I purchased this Fire as a early Christmas gift to myself.Although there are some things I like, overall I'm extremely disappointed. I loved my old Kindle Fire. Really adored it, it was perhaps my favorite object and I took it everywhere with me. The new one feels less like a better version of a great reader and more like a cheap Chinese tablet knockoff I would buy for a child.The good: I love the improvements in internet connectivity. The old Fire was great for many things, but not browsing the internet. It could - sometimes - do it, but not easily. In the new Fire, email is always at the touch of a button. I love the Silk browser. I really love having my Goodreads account easily accessible on my reader. Every thing internet-related is faster and smoother.I like the idea of having multiple accounts (say, for children) on one device. I dislike the way it turned out, but this is a great idea.I like having a volume toggle.Highlighting, bookmarking and word lookup while reading are significantly improved from the old style. You still can't zoom to see tiny images or tables, though.I would say the new Fire is both lighter and faster than my old one.The bad:I really dislike that they switched the carousel layout to a tablet icon style. This is probably just a preference, but I find the new layout ugly and harder to use.When I navigate to "books" or "apps" or whatever, I am immediately shown the store, not my own library. It adds another step before I can get to my stuff.The Fire comes with a horde of useless apps that you cannot remove from the device. They clutter up the main screen. (The best solution I have found for this, thanks to someone on another site, is to make a "junk" folder to contain them. Click and drag the icons onto each other.)One of the most awful things about the new Fire is that you are slammed with ads when you start up. It makes the whole experience less about reading a favorite book and more like cheap commercialism. It's intrusive and garish. I have heard from other sources that this is how Amazon lowered the price of the Fire, but if I had known bout this I would gladly have saved up for another few months to get a reader I could access without ads in my face.My kids were excited to have their own personalized account on the Kindle, as we read about in the description. Unfortunately Amazon left this benefit halfway finished. Kids accounts are not able to customize anything, they show nothing but a black blank screen with the shared apps. And even though I have a "teen" account for them they are unable to play many of their games because Amazon blocks ones with leaderboards for safety. Look, I get that internet safety for teens is important, but I can't imagine that seeing who made a high score on Candy Crush is going to lead them straight to hell. We deleted the separate account and are back to all sharing one.Both my kids and I find the new Kindle to be cheaper and less responsive than the old one. It requires firmer, more direct pressure on the screen to register.I don't have a cell phone. So I was really, really excited to have a reader (that I carry with me) with a camera! So exciting. I planned on taking it on our Thanksgiving trip to take photos. Unfortunately the quality of the camera is so, so bad that it is essentially useless. Pictures are so grainy you could feed a village. Lighting and focus is terrible. I'll add a few I took to show the quality.Overall? I'm kind of sorry I purchased this. Maybe if I had saved up for longer and gotten a more expensive Fire instead, I would feel better about things. But basically now I'm just wishing I had saved my $79 and kept my old Fire.

title: HUGE disapointment.  Not a device to give your child, to receive your Prime benefits and have their own user account on it.
body: I am not real happy with the Kindle Fire. First, it seems to be built really well and the CPU performance appears to be great. However, the limitations the Kindle Fire have when purchasing for a child family member almost caused me to return it in person! I grew more and more aggravated, going back and forth with the setup configurations.First off, in order to use this tablet, you HAVE TO have an Amazon account. No problem if this was for me. However, I intended to give it to my 12 year old son. He likes to play various games as well as communicate with his cousins and friends using apps such as Hangouts. There are 2 options for configuration for a child:1) Register the Fire using my Amazon account and set the Parental Controls. I thought this option would work fine because I was able to add his email account. However, the Parental Controls are inadequate. There is an option to block Amazon "Stores", but not an option to choose which store (App store, Shopping, etc..). If I block Amazon Store, he cannot use the shopping app, which is great. But the App store is blocked as well. I would have to constantly download apps for him. If I unblock the stores, he has access to the Shopping app. You do have the option to require a password to purchase however, there is still access to the 1-click purchasing setup in the shopping app. P Also, the user remains to be me as the user is determined by the Amazon Account the Fire was registered under. This really is not cool as a 12 year old does wants to be the user and put his own picture on the profile.2) Register the Fire using my Amazon account and add a family member user to the tablet. The only way for the user to receive me Prime benefits is for the user to be a child. Amazon only allows one adult family member to have access to your Prime benefits. Therefore, the user must be a child family member user account. This option brings all sorts of limitations. Once the switch is made to the child user, most of the tablet's options disappear. What you are left with is a VERY basic and unappealing user interface. No option to change the wallpaper, no way to use the browser, much less any chat apps. It is really hard to explain on here unless you have seen it for yourself. Quite simply, the kid's user option really is terrible. There is an option for a Free Time subscription, but I am not going to pay a monthly fee for something a 12 year old does not need!3) Create your child their own Amazon Account and register the Fire using it. However, a credit card, or pre-paid visa, etc is required. Also, they won't have access to your Prime benefits.Instead of returning it, I am attempting to set it up as a home automation tablet where I will use it to turn control my SmartThings devices as well as monitor my security cameras. The SmartThings wasn't easy either as I had to install an apk downloaded from the internet. The app is not available in the Amazon app store.Finally, I am a HUGE Amazon fan and have been a Prime customer for over 5 years. I am all "Amazoned" out having an Echo, Tap, and Dot, as well as using the video streaming and music service. I get my money's worth out of my Prime membership, believe me! But the Kindle Fire is obviously simply a tool for Amazon to make it easier for people to buy even more from them. To have the limitations on it as it does and to advertise that it is "kid friendly" is almost unethical. The Kindle Fire is NOT a kid device, no matter how they market it or whatever color they make it. Amazon, you really fail with this and should refrain from advertising this as a "tablet". It is NOT a tablet but merely a shopping device.Thank you for your time

title: I thought this was a steal - disappointed
body: I'm editing my review 5 months from purchase. I am an avid reader. I have a lot of books in my library. This reader is so slow in opening books or even popping up information on the books. It closes the book I'm reading if I back out of the app. So if I had gone down to the depths of my library for something to read, I have to scroll back up to the top to find the book I had opened to read, sometimes only minutes before. (Sure, that doesn't sound like a pain, but when I say I have a lot of books in my library waiting for me to read, I'm talking hundreds of books. The shared library is magnificent for bibliophiles.) I mentioned this before, but it even does it if I minimize the app. It doesn't stream well. I know that my internet is not the best, but it worked great with my previous tablet and this one performs poorly.Let's talk about charging. The cord provided is too short to work with any kind of power outlet unless you sit next to it on the floor. Sure, I can connected it to my computer, but that doesn't work for me while I read in bed, or even on the couch sometimes. If I use another charger, say for my phone and they're compatible, it falls out. I literally have to hold it in. Amazon does sell a longer charger cable separately.Applications are slow to react to your commands, especially messenger while you're reading. It's easier to set the kindle aside, pull out your laptop, wake it up, and read the message.If you download a swype keyboard, it works in most places, except search in every application. I'm upset that I purchased this device and I probably will not purchase another dedicated kindle device again.This is my first official kindle. I had been using the app on my nexus, but the touch screen would stop responding in the middle of things I was doing, the keyboard was acting crazy on at any given time, and powercycling the device wasn't helping. I needed something to replace it and decided to try this out.Here's what I liked: So far the touchscreen is behaving like you would expect it to. It responds smoothly and I've no frustration trying to work it. The screen also doesn't seem bothered by cold fingers. I have high hopes. The night time, bed function that changes the color to a sort of sepia tone. I was able to find and replace 98% of apps that I use on a daily basis. As with the kindle app on my android device, it is easy to use and read on and easy to make any adjustments you'd see fit. It's easy to use.Here's what I don't like: In the kindle app itself it doesn't list the audio upgrades that are available to me. There doesn't appear to be a lock screen orientation for the specific app. (However you can lock it in the drop down menu but it affects the entire device, not just the app. I'll have to change that when I want to watch a video.) If I should close the app, which I do often, it doesn't keep the book I'm currently reading open. So if I happen to download a considerable amount of books (Because I had to when I noticed not all of them had downloaded) I have to search for the book I'm currently reading.Outside of the kindle app - I don't like how cluttered my home screen is. I won't use 80% of those apps on a regular basis, I don't want to see them. So far I have not yet bothered to google how to fix this or if I can. If I can't I know I will eventually get used to it.Silk browser sucks. It's like a step-sibling to IE, but it gets the job done-slowly.I also want to point out that the color of the device has a matte finish. It isn't shiny like I thought, but I'm indifferent to that and it didn't affect my review.Overall I'm pleased with my purchase and hope that it will continue to behave for me for many years to come.

title: poor screen quality control: dead pixels and back-light bleed
body: I have purchased 4 of these tablets. When the device was first released, I purchased a black, magenta, and blue. The quality on that first batch was good. I noticed no issues with the screen. However, the blue one quit powering on after 4 months. Notice that these devices only have a 90 day warranty. So we still have that dead blue one.I bought another blue one to replace the one that died after 120 days. That tablet had multiple dead pixel clusters spread around one corner of the tablet. Areas of the screen that were stuck displaying white. When watching any kind of video with black in it, those white areas with dead pixels draw your focus and attention, taking it away from the video. There was also noticeable back-light leakage (i.e. areas of the screen around the edges with white light bleeding through. when the screen is black, those areas shine like a flashlight, turning the black to a shade of gray).So I exchanged it. The one they sent back was from the Warehouse Deals crew. That one had an even more pronounced area of dead pixels. Maybe the same number of dead pixels, but all gathered up together. It was even more distracting. Not only that, this one had even more significant back-light leakage. Just as distracting and unacceptable as the dead pixels. So, that one was exchanged as well.When I got the next one in, the dead pixels were just as bad and the back-light leakage was significantly worse. The dead pixels look like someone's hair and dirt got dropped into the screen as it was being assembled. They were all lined up in a curly pattern. The back-light leakage lights up a whole side of the tablet. Amazon customer service said they would send me another one, and this time they would check it first to make sure it didn't have these issues.Well I just received the replacement in the mail. Lo and behold, not only does this one have white dead pixels, but it also has a spot of black dead pixels. So now, whether the screen is white or black, there are noticeable areas of dead pixels. They are not as bad as on the previous tablets, but they are certainly noticeable. Not only that, but the back-light leakage on this one is the worst of all 7 tablets I have had the pleasure of working with. This one was brand new, not from Warehouse Deals, and shipped out so fast there's no way they checked it before shipping.If this level of screen quality is what we are supposed to expect, it needs to be made clear to people before they purchase it. It's as if that first batch of tablets were made to meet the quality control standards, and they have been slipping and getting worse as time goes on. I'm debating if I want to just keep this one and cut my losses, or return it for a refund and be done with it. Either way, even on a budget tablet, these kind of screen defects are unacceptable.Customers need to expect that there WILL be screen defects. Don't look very closely, maybe you won't notice. Perhaps my background in manufacturing and quality control lends me to notice these things more than the average consumer. But be aware, if you're buying these after Q4 2016, the quality of the screen is not very good at all. Everything else on the tablet seems to be fine for what it is, but the screen is what you interact with. Thus my 2 star review.I wish I had taken pictures of the previous tablets with dead pixels, but I was not thinking about documenting them at the time. But just look at the back-light bleed on this tablet. That is an accurate representation of what black looks like on this screen. If you can deal with those shades of gray and even approaching white, perhaps this tablet is for you.

title: NOT! or at least not yet
body: The obvious vision for the Kindle is laudable - a quantum leap in information sharing and a marketing bonaza for Amazon similar to what iTunes did for Apple. The execution falls well short - a pity, really. I bought it with high hopes and have used every feature.First, the hardware. The visual display IS NOT competitive with paper. It is gray and although the font is well shaped, the contrast is poor. It doesn't even beat newsprint. (Note that one of the most popular accessories is a clip on lamp - I wonder why. The font size can be increased effortlessly, but cold it be for the same reason?)Next, one cannot hold the thing normally without accidentally turning the page. I cant say enough about this obvious design flaw. Lying in bed on my side, I can't prop it on a pillow without accidentially turning page after page. The aim proclaimed by Bezos is for the Kindle to disappeard, not to get in the way of the content. But it does, in a big way. The paging function is a key design element and I cant believe it escaped beta testing with this flaws intact.Of lesser concern is the nifty little reflective cursor some reviewers seem to like. Unfortunately it is not visible under some lighting conditions. For example, if it reflects a white surface, like a ceiling or wall, it disappears. Also, it does not have an infinite loop - when you are at the page bottom you must scroll all the way to the top, you can't simply scroll down a bit more and have the cursor jump to the top.Now for the software. Most digital software suffers from an inability provide the user with a relative frame of reference. Kindle's software is no different. Although the programmers tried a few things, they failed. Thumbnails are one option that the designers did not utilize, but thumbnails work in many applications. The NYTimes is very difficult to navigate and needs some work in this regard. Jumping back and forth between books and chapters in the same book is not effortless.Clippings are easy to make but they cannot be deleted without the use of a computer hookup.Downloading free ebooks from the likes of Guttenburg can be done easily online but the only format acceptable to Kindle is Text. Text opens in Kindle with the formatting all screwed up. Lines of text are all truncated prematurely. Besides the obvious anoyance this causes with a book like Moby Dick, you can forget obtaining the original structure of a poem.The music player is a joke. With all the MP3 players around, I don't know why they bothered adding this feature.The good stuff.I live in a metropolan area with good cellphone coverage. The whispernet works very well. Connecting with the Amazon store is a snap and having Amazon's incredible selection of titles 60 seconds away is mind blowing. On the negative side, searching online is a little clunky. Buying books is easy although a couple of free samples provided contained only the preface and table of contents.The lookup feature for definitions is a treat. I find that I actually use the feature at least once an hour. The ability to search wikipedia and the web is in its infancy. Great idea but even with the system optimized for speed, its very clunky (a bit slow, poorly formated, inaccurate, difficult to read).The USB connectivity worked very well, transferring and deleting files worked, the simple file folder organization was intuitive.All in all, I love the IDEA of this little machine but it has lots of design flaws. If Amazon could only steal from Apple their design team, Im sure Bezos would have a runaway winner for the Kindle's next incarnation.

title: A deeply flawed attempt
body: I do instructional technology work for a small college, and recently got a Kindle to see just how useful it might be in education. After using it for several weeks, I passed it on to another member of my group with only slight thought. It's an interesting attempt, but simply fails in so many ways compared to what is truly needed. At best, it would work very well for someone commuting on a train who wanted to read a newspaper on their way to work, but as a book replacement it just isn't there.The Good:1) Book/magazine purchase is amazingly simple and quick. Any future ebook readers need to aspire to this level of ease-of-use.2) The screen is quite easy to read even on the smallest typeface. E-ink is fine without color and it's a lot nicer to read from than a LCD screen, even with the black flash.3) The user interface, while a bit odd initially, becomes quite easy to use after a few minutes.4) A couple of folks commented on how nice the box was. (Seriously)And now for the much longer list; The Bad:1) The screen size is *much* too small for a device this size. It's a book reader, yet very little of the actual book can be seen on the page, even at the smallest typeface. Ditch the keyboard, ditch the big side buttons, replace with either a slide-out keyboard or a touchscreen. Page control should be through touch gestures- swipe left for next page, swipe right for back, swipe up for table of contents, etc2) No PDF support. This is a crippling flaw. I'm a member of a committee on campus that generates a true blizzard of paperwork. I tried using the Kindle to save paper, but the converters needed are just too clunky. The Mobi converter failed to convert straight DOC to ebook, so I had to go from Doc->PDF->ebook, one-by-one since there's no batch conversion. It's far easier to just read them off of my smartphone which handles both DOC and PDF without problems, even if the screen is nowhere near as nice.3) Lack of serious tools to handle footnotes and indexes. I know for most people this isn't an issue, but for academics it's critical. The Kindle could be an amazing textbook reader with some work: add footnote following, search by index topic/footnote/concept and a few others. Textbooks are an area where ebooks could really take hold- they are expensive, very heavy and go out of date rapidly.4) Not all that easy to hold. Amazon needs to hire some top-rate designers here since the ones they have managed to make a device that is very awkward to handle without bumping the huge side buttons. For me at least the bottom left corner dug into the ball of my index finger in the only position I could find where I didn't bump them.5) Power management is very poor and battery life is not all that great. A hard switch to turn the radio off and on? That's just absurd- I know it drains power, but how hard is it to put a soft power switch and just have the thing wake up every hour or so, send a ping asking "Any content for me?" and shut down when done? The battery life was surprisingly bad- I took it home on a Wednesday, read the Post every day for 30 minutes and was out of power by Saturday. Shutting the radio down seems to help.6) DRM'd content. I'm sorry, but I keep my books for years and will often go back and read something I last touched in 1997. What's the chance I'll be able to do that with a Kindle book?It's not a terrible product, but it's very clearly a 1.0 beta with a long road to real usability in front of it. Still, I'll be looking for future revisions since it really does have a nugget of potential buried deep within the problems.

title: Looks Like Kindling
body: Yet another dedicated ebook reader, yet another failure to understand what is wrong with the current ebook market so it'll probably go nowhere.1. It's NOT the screen quality that's the problem with ebooks. People use regular LED screens to read on every day, millions of them on smart phones, MP3 players, laptops and desktops. They're not perfect but they're good enough. While the eink screens used by Sony and now Amazon's readers are very impressive for text they currently have severe limitations - text only, no colour, slow response. Thus they can't be used for a truly multipurpose device (that might justify spending hundreds of dollars).2. It's the limited choice of titles, price, multiple and incompatible DRM schemes and file formats used by each vendor/ebook reader that are the major problems with current ebooks. And the mania for expensive yet limited function dedicated ebook readers like Kindle. Instead of properly supporting the devices most people *already* have: laptops, smart phones, MP3 players, PDAs, etc.3. Spending vast effort and money on making your dedicated ebook reader "just like a real book" or "disappear" in the hope it will appeal to the older (45+) readers who are used to dead tree books is a waste of time, 90% of them will *never* give up regular books. And they're dying off anyway... The future, and the challenge for book, magazine and newspaper publishing is getting the younger generation, the screen-generation, who already do most things online and via their mobile phones, MP3 players, laptops, etc. to buy and read their products. This generation don't care a hoot about how "bookish" it is, they are in fact put off by that old fashioned stuff, they want something that looks and works similar to the DVDs, video games and web sites they already use and read every day. Books should be just another form of (electronic) media to them. Dead trees are what their parents were into and therefore totally boring and weird.4. It seems Kindle ebooks can only be purchased via a Kindle reader, you can't download them to your PC and then transfer them to your reader (particularly the reader of your choice)? While a nice add-on facility to have it's foolish to ignore the huge pool of online devices that already exist on the open and accessible world-wide internet - desktop and laptop PCs plus internet connected smart phones and PDAs, etc. People don't want yet another (expensive, with limited functionality) device to lug around. Can you spell Blackberry, iPhone, iPod, Treo, Nokia, etc.? They're the devices publishers and booksellers need to target.5. I already own scores of ebooks (in at least three different file formats and using different DRM schemes) on my Palm PDA and Mac laptop. Both can connect to the real internet, and to each other, wirelessly and using wires. They manage my appointments, keep my addressbook, calculate prices, browse the full web, can be used to jot down notes and store whole databases of information. My laptop can also make phone calls via VoIP and play music, videos and podcasts, and stores my iPod's library of such content too. They're *multi-function* devices that happen to enable me to read books too, why would I consider spending $400 on a device that can really only read books/magazines and old fashioned newspapers (when newspapers are all available for free online anyway! I stopped reading print newspapers years ago and get almost all my news online or via TV now, and I'm over 45!), all of which content I have to buy from Amazon (and Amazon only it appears) and which is (apparently) unable to handle the ebooks I already own? D'oh!

title: Not a good kids device if you want it to actually work.
body: I don't often give poor reviews, but if anything deserves it, it is this Fire 7" tablet. We bought FOUR of these for our recent road trip so our kids could each have a tablet and use the Plex app to watch movies on a streaming media center inside the car. The kids profiles were OK for my 7 year old and 10 year old (when they worked), but the only difference my teens got with the teen profile was a black background instead of blue, and they could rotate it to show either portrait or landscape orientation. I only found out how worthless and aggravating the kids profiles were by looking it up after my husband bought these tablets. Kids profiles don't support movies or music. If you want the teens to have any control at all, then they need the full adult profile with parental controls set. This means a lot of password typing as a parent for everything they want to do. But I choose to do this so they don't have power to control each others' profiles or have access to anything my credit card is linked to. (If they want to browse the app store, they also have access to the real amazon shopping site. You can't give access to one without the other.)After dealing with this on all four devices and reading forum posts, it's obvious the kids profiles are pretty worthless except for young children. Using the kids profiles created all kinds of glitches on the device. It would randomly uninstall apps from the kid profile, so it had to be reinstalled when they wanted to play a game, even if they had just played it two minutes before. Internet is required to reinstall, plus installation time (1-10 minutes). If you want to use the device for your kids in a car without internet, FORGET IT! This is why we ended up hiding all the kids profiles and running them from my own profile for the long car rides. Once we had internet again at our destination, I had to lock it up tight with parental controls and/or re-enable the kids profiles. Turns out, that deletes all their former game progress, which gets lots of tears from a 7-year old.Finally, the extendable memory with an SD card is a joke. I set it to put everything it could directly to the SD storage, but apparently 90% of every app has to go on internal memory anyways. The device still filled up its internal memory with a moderate amount of apps, with the SD card practically empty. My teen found a way to put a lot of data onto the SD card, but it did not remove it from internal memory--just duplicated it to take up space on both sides. The worst part, which shows up on all four devices, is that it randomly but frequently won't recognize the SD card so the little bit that does go on to the SD card for an app prevents you from using that app. ("App unavailable. SD card not found.") If I went and looked at the SD card storage, it would show it was there and holding data, and then the app would sometimes work again, sometimes not.Oh, and the camera is a lousy quality. Fine for the kids (assuming you will delete them all), but any decent phone will get you much better pictures.I would give this tablet only one star, but it the end we did get about 25 hours of near silence while driving on our road trip while they were busy with working apps and movies via Plex. That's worth another star, even if it had to be done on the standard owner profile after messing with it for hours and hours to come to that conclusion.Bottom line: it may be a decent inexpensive device for adults, but for kids it is a ridiculous amount of parent involvement and frustration.

title: Disappointing
body: Dear Amazon,I'm sorely disappointed in your latest budget Kindle Fire 7 5th generation. I have 3 Kindles I love: the 2012 Kindle Fire HD 7, the 2013 Kindle Fire HDX 7 (my all time favorite), and the Kindle Fire HD 6 4th gen. I'm an avid fan: I spend 8-16 hours per day on my Kindles, sometimes 2-3 Kindle screens at a time when I'm gaming, and I'm a walking talking advertisement for Kindles when tablet discussions come up. (I have even converted several friends from iPad fans to Kindle.)That being said, I despise this Kindle Fire with a passion, and I'll tell you why: whatever you changed on the display (the lighting, the resolution, the brightness, contrast, the poorly saturated colors, maybe even the refresh rate) physically hurts my eyes, makes me dizzy, and gives me an instant headache. I realize I may be one in a thousand who have trouble with some of those minute details, but any device that causes physical discomfort or worse is not a device I'll ever use (I'll cough up the cash for an iPad mini before I'll suffer headaches and dizzy spells for a tablet). Even if I could dim the brightness of the screen to a comfortable level, there is something fuzzy about the display that reminds me of looking at the spot my cat's laser pointer makes on the floor; it's seems as though the pixels are subtly moving and it hurts my eyes. I haven't had this problem on any of the other 3 Kindles I own even when I use 3 of them at a time! The colors and crispness are just ok, which is what I expected on a $50 tablet.In addition you changed the dimension of the screen so that it's now a tall skinny rectangle that's displeasing: it squeezes my books and makes games and videos look weird. The proportion of the previous Kindle Fires were much more pleasing. Another minor frustration was the change in keyboard layout. It doesn't make any sense, and I couldn't adjust it. For the keyboard alone, this device would have been relegated to a read only device, but you should reconsider your keyboard layout on future revisions.I also don't care for the new user interface. I'll adjust because I don't have a choice, but it's cluttered and chaotic. The beauty of Amazon Kindle was the simplicity of the carousel for recent items and menus for specific areas such as books or newsstand. When I start this Kindle Fire up there are icons I don't recognize that I don't want and no way to turn off the recommendations. If you want to go full Android, go full Android and let me choose which icons I want on my desktop. You do get points for giving me a choice in wallpaper, that was an improvement.You get one star for adding expandable memory! Yay! Thank you very much for a much welcomed improvement! Now all of my thousands of books can be one memory chip away, and I can still have room for games videos and music. Bravo!You also get one star for the price. After all, that's what lured me into trying this device out. A $40 sale price was well worth the experiment, even if $40 is not worth keeping a device that makes me dizzy and hurts my eyes.I'll close with the fact that I appreciate you trying to deliver a quality budget tablet to those of us that can't or don't want to spend a fortune on a tablet. And the improvements of additional memory and reduced cost might be worth the drawbacks if not for the headaches and dizziness the screen caused. I remain a loyal customer, just not loyal to this device.

title: Read this if you're thinking about sharing/giving this tablet with your kid(s)
body: I am retracting my previous 4-star review. Amazon's handling of child profiles is the worst. And I say this fully recognizing that Apple doesn't have anything at all, unless you count their Restrictions system. What makes Amazon worse is that it hints at the potential for allowing a rich experience for kids, but snatches defeat from the jaws of victory by failing to develop their system for the real world. Did they test it on real people? In real-life use scenarios? Or children? I am 99% the answer is no. We have had our Fire tablets for *two days* and already I want to throw them through a window.Here's what needs to be fixed to go from "At least a root canal would be done by now" to "Take my money now, Amazon!" from a parent's perspective.Make a teen profile that isn't insulting to teens that plays to their intellect, freedom, responsibility and personality. - The teen profile (11-18 years) is just the child profile (2-10) with a different background. Child profiles are designed with young kids in mind. That pretty much sums it up. - The teen profile still doesn't have access to the weather app, the calculator, the clock, the calendar, email, or any of the other useful productivity apps that come installed by default.Amazon Underground is great. Free apps with free in-app purchases. If only... - Amazon FreeTime (the child profile management system) doesn't allow kids to download content-appropriate FREE apps and take advantage of the FREE in-app purchases for those apps. This is the most asinine and frustrating failure of Amazon's system. So a teenager still has to ask mommy and daddy to download apps and add them to their profile. But there is no way, even with parent intervention, for a child profile to enjoy these lauded free in-app purchases.Child profiles don't allow access to Amazon Prime Instant Videos. - Let me say that again. Kids can't procure *Amazon* videos on the *Amazon* tablet. - Parents can, however, manually share titles that have been bought or rented. Wait...Hold up... I have to spend money to buy or rent video content that I get for free as a Prime subscriber in order to share with my kids? Why wouldn't I just let them watch it on my iPad? Exactly; I would.Child profiles don't allow access to the Amazon E-book store. - I'm going to repeat that also. Kids can't procure E-books from the *Amazon* store on the *Amazon Kindle*. They can't download free book samples, they can't download free classics. Nada. - Parents can, however, manually go in and download books and share them (manually) with *each* child separately. You know, because we all have time to do that, in addition to guessing what each of our kids is interested in reading at the moment.I get what Amazon was aiming for with this ecosystem but they need to go back and finish the job, because they implemented a half-baked plan that's only and half-done. At $40, this tablet is not worth the hassle.TL:DRCould be great for kids. Instead, is full of frustrations and broken dreams.Amazon needs to allow older kids freedom to learn and explore safely or not bother with teen profiles at all.

title: Not bad, but I wish there was more on the ...
body: Not bad, but I wish there was more on the App Store and last I checked rooting is still illegal for tablets (seriously, thanks Supreme Court).I really don't know what to say because anything I review would more likely be Amazon itself. I feel like the tablet is Amazon's right hand and I am a bit disappointed with them lately, because, things that used to be available for prime are now prime pantry; which want me to pay them $5.99 for a box that has items priced comparably to WM. I think I would just drive to WM. I would love to have a few small items here and there for prime shipping. I like prime music because it has unreleased music (Adele's new foray into soulful bliss is superb). I think that given the choice again I would buy it over the more expensive models because they offer near nothing more except Dolby and mine sounds fine enough.I would also say Amazons new delivery service should be in like 200 cities so we could try this stuff. I mean they have all these warehouses in like every state just so they can deliver faster to non-prime members why not just have drivers? I think the future is bright, but implementation is slow for the Bezos.If I had to make a suggestion on one thing though it would be $119 unlimited-&-forever 4g lte across several bands.Good:cheapReasonable battery for what it isGotta love that X-Ray on device and scene by scene Id (it's magic for real and I am fairly sure by using it an angel gets her wings)Access to kindle lending library (this is still a let down though they should at least give you half off of the unlimited service and just do away with this unorganized and frankly misguided at this point service). That's right Amazon I have a suggestion for you😀Access to Amazon underground with actually free apps and games (the strange thing is though I have an iPad and Amazon game studios has good iPad games but they aren't on the kindle devices? Seriously!?)Apparently you can back things up online, but it sort of lies about unlimited photo and video (it told me I had to pay for video beyond a certain amount).Durable?Do you notice how some of the goods are kinda bad?Easily fits in your murse or "messenger bag"The OS is simple just scroll left or right and you have everything you can do on it almost, so chew on that JackIt loves to cuddle on the beach with me and it never losses my number (or anyone else's)Anti glare coating was reasonable and brightness as well.It held me host... While writing this pa.Bad:Low res and I don't have good visionCan be fast or slow it just dependsNO 4g SUPPORTLives and dies by its father lolAlmost an entirely consumptive based device even the silk browser on it won't allow flash (Apple anyone?)Does not include anything like Echo/Alexa. It should be standard even if not quite as functional.After programming/OS there is only like 5GB of available memoryIf you think you like salty food it Eats your soul and crushes your dreams of a spectacular $50 tablet. It uses your tears for salt and I have to say that pretty smart for a machine (oh wait it's not waterproof).

title: Worst Kindle I've ever owned
body: I've owned several Kindles, starting with the 1st gens. This is the 4th Fire Kindle I've own, and even at the cheap price, it's easily the worst.-- The model I got was suppose to have 8 GB of memory. When I hook it up to my PC to read the internal storage, it says it only has 5.62 GB, and at time of purchase around 4 GB of that was already used up by whatever Amazon has installed, including apps I can't seem to uninstall, like the Amazon Freetime, Shopping and Offers apps. I can't seem to free up any memory so I'm left with less that 2 GB. This leaves exceptionally little shortage for me to use for music and audiobooks that I can use offline (i am unable to be online at my work, and one reason I mainly bought this to for use during my downtime there)-- It doesn't have to the ease of organizing my content as other Kindles have. My other Kindles would put downloaded media into corresponding files--photos into photos, docs into docs. There is not file for these, so I have to access downloads through the awful Silk browser every time.-- It is very sluggish and slow compared to older Fire models I have used and the touch screen is not very responsive compared to just about anything on the market right now. It makes using the virtual keyboard very frustrating as I have to go slowly and often double- and triple- touch the letters. My $20 cellphone has a better touchscreen than this thing.-- The Blue Shade feature is very disappointing. There must be some way for they to implement this feature without losing so much image definition,.-- Terrible battery life. Up to 7 hours advertised, but in truth, just using apps and ebooks, I get maybe 5 hours before I have to plug it in to recharge, and the USB cable that came with it is ridiculously short, so you can't use your Kindle while it's pulled in unless you pull the charger into an extension cord. And of course you have to use the cable and adapter that came with it. (You have to use as it will charge excessively slowly with any other, including the chargers for my previous Kindles.) And as usual, the input on the Kindle for the USB cable is wonky and probably isn't going to last, like nearly all my Fires (seriously, Amazon, is this that hard?). Also if you do try to use it while it's charging, the side with the battery tends to get hot and a little uncomfortable to hold.-- At the time I bought this I could only find Fire with special offers available on Amazon. I hope when I have money again to get a better one that the ones without Special Offers are back because I hate the Special Offers. It makes me feel like I'm using a public device that I don't actually own. (Plus I'm sure it's contributing to whatever is hogging up my piddly 5.62 GB of memory)Pros? It works and I guess it's better than not having a mobile device to use my ebooks, music and audiobooks that I've already paid for. I bought this only because I was desperate for a Fire-like device after my gen 4 Fire's touchscreen got busted and I'm currently very strapped for money. I don't see myself using this for long. If you have money to get something better, do yourself a favor and do so.

title: What does it do? Really. Why is it so ineffective at teaching how to do stuff with it?
body: Gee, maybe I'll change this review as time goes by. I'm sure this thing is good for something, but I really wish I could get the two hours back that I've spent tapping around since getting my first kindle in the mail. Really, I'm still clawing my way past the ads, disguised as "content." For free I automatically get 6 months of some magazine that only shows me 7 articles that aren't that interesting. I allowed Kindle to connect to F-Book. I don't understand why I had to give Amazon the right to post pretty much anything they want in my name, but that's just the way any F-Book app works. It really just means I get to post, but that F-Book sees it as Amazon's posting for me. What I don't understand is, "Since I have to provide wi-fi access, and since this thing clearly has a CPU capable of handling internet connectivity, why does Amazon not just 'step out of the way,' and let me connect to sites on the Internet?" It has to be harder to produce a device which interferes with me. I obviously have a learning curve ahead of me. But Amazon gets zero points for teaching how to use this thing.But let's look at its function as an e-book reader. It really wants to steer me to where I can buy stuff. I have a lot of Kindle books I have already bought. I can't get it to show me that menu from my cloud library. When I declined to waste my life by rating books for it so it could give me recommendations, it gave me the option to accept some "samples," partial books I don't want. So when I ask to see all my titles, I see 3 random books from my cloud library plus these 4 samples. I don't know if these are taking up my memory or if they are being read from the cloud, but when I get on my real computer, I see these "samples" have been added to my cloud library. What really burns me up is that I bought a Kindle book on how to use the Kindle Fire. I read about half of it in the cloud reader while I was waiting for my Kindle Fire to arrive. I guess I haven't read the part that tells me how to get this particular book onto the device it's teaching me to use.I guess the card I added is working ok, because as soon as I plugged it in, Kindle Fire offered to save memory by "archiving" to the card things I have not used for a while. I thought it was suggesting that it might fill up the card with its own choice of things from my cloud library, but I've read other reviews that say they can't even get the Kindle Fire to load purchases into the card, so maybe it's something else. I was going to check this again, but while I was looking away, my brand new Kindle Fire decided to "update." But really, the point is that I get no choice. I give it one click, and it decides what it wants to fill up my card with. I'm used to being able to choose. I will pre-load the card if I have to, cursing Amazon for their evil ways the whole time I screw around with this.Bottom line - people love their Kindles. I just haven't had time to figure out why, and I have really hated trying to figure it out. I'm giving an extra star because it really is beautiful. If I could make it do anything, it would be awesome.

title: Gen 5 replacing a Gen 1, its orange and there for is a ginger and there for is soulless.
body: My Gen 1 Fire died, first the charge port had a cold solder joint go, but battery still going strong after 6 yrs. So in repairing my kindle, the touch screen decided it would no longer work...and I decided forget it the first one was 199 and its 6 yrs old you can replace it for 50 bucks go for it. So I did. Comes in a far flimsier box then Gen 1. Charger, meh, I like my old wall wart style charger though that's more the cords longer then 2 feet then anything else. Gen 5 is LIGHTER then Gen 1, the kindle fire itself seems flimsier...speaker is in a stupid location on the back(and I thought on the top edge was dumb) it has a camera...why I don't know as it doesn't have a very good camera. Then theres an annoying updated feature to the reading of books. I didn't have to swipe the old kindle you could just TAP the edge of the page and gen 1 would turn your page. Gen 5 goes all whacko on ya and pops up your page and the page before and after. Tap a word, the dictionary function on this one is horrid, propbably because its powered by Bing(seriously who let Bing! as a web search engine???) I miss the carousel and FAVORITES on my home screen. I have a crap ton of APPS hogging up space on this thing that you can't DELETE. I don't want SILK browser, I don't WANT that weather app as that's not much good either. Audibles can't be deleted...in short my entire home screen is WORTHLESS, I can't change it. Youtube has failed me, which is shocking.I get that Kindle is competing with Apple as a Tablet pc/reader to match up with the iPads...but the thing Kindle did well was the book reading and use...it makes a cruddy tablet pc, even gen 1 made a cruddy tablet pc. So, I'm looking at youtube for screen issues, maybe buy a non functioning battery one off of Ebay and swap out. Heck the battery on this gen 5 doesn't even make 5 hours, the gen 1 will get me 8 hrs and 10 minutes...and I've not replaced the battery at all in the 6 yrs I owned it, first cracked it open 3 days before I ordered the replacement. Oh and Alexa...is the redheaded stepchild of Cortana and Siri. I have gone to great lengths to delete Cortana off a windows 10 device, and I don't care for Siri and Alexa is no better. Actually I'm still hung up on Bing! being allowed on this thing, seriously the only other worse choices would have been Microsoft Edge(Internet Explorer re-packaged) or glitchy firefox. Its droid based, make it google. I was prepared to LOVE this little reader, having grown to like the first one...Its orange...as a ginger it has no soul. There's lots of other stuff I don't like in the updated version, maybe if I had updated all along from gen 1 to 2 to 3 to 4 to 5 I would like it, alas, I did not. Is it a great reader, probably. Do I still think its the best reader out there, yep. I'm just not thrilled. Had I paid 199 for this one it would be going back. 50 bucks...I can always let the dog eat it and not feel bad about it. Can you beat the price, heck no. Maybe I shoulda been leary about the reading thing when I had the kindle app on my win 10 tablet PC...I didn't like that, and this is exactly like it(so there is consistency between platforms).

title: Adequate tablet for the price, but Netflix downloads are DISABLED!
body: SUMMARY: If all you want is a device that plays your Amazon-related content, and you don't care that playing non-Amazon content will be a pain if not impossible, then this is a dandy device for the price. But all other content sources, including free ebooks, Hulu, Netflix, Google Music, etc, will be difficult, crippled, or disabled altogether. Despite decent hardware specs for the price, you're probably better off getting a regular Android tablet and installing the Kindle app.At the $40 - $50 price point, the 7-inch Kindle Fire outperforms many similarly-priced Android tablets from a hardware point of view: - battery life has been 8-12 hours of moderate use, with days of standby time. - the quad-core processor is beefier than most other entries at this price-point. - the controls are well thought-out; I'm rarely accidentally changing the volume or turning the device off; even the micro sd card slot has a nice cover, so I've never accidentally spit the card out, unlike my other tablet, where I was doing that all the time.From a software point of view, starting up applications can feel pretty slow even compared to a phone with similar specs, though once they're running they tend to be pretty responsive.
