Reading ebooks is seamless when you acquire them through the Amazon store, but is painfully difficult when you don't. Download a public-domain ebook from Project Gutenberg, for example, and you'll need a file browser application to even find it; as far as I can tell, having it show up on the "Books" tab alongside titles purchased from Amazon or checked out of the public library is simply not possible. The same basic issue obtains with movies or other media you already own.In other words, the "Amazon Ecosystem" is simultaneously a nice feature and the device's biggest drawback; content you get from Amazon is generally easy, any other content is basically impossible. Don't think you're getting "An Android Tablet With Amazon" like I did; you are not: - The Amazon App Store features some popular games with some in-app purchases provided for free, but overall has a much smaller selection than the Google Play store, which is not included or available for the device without side-loading third party software. - Hulu rarely works at all on the device; I frequently have to uninstall it and reinstall it, sometimes within the viewing of one episode. It's bad enough I generally just don't try anymore. - Netflix's new Offline Viewing feature is disabled; it worked for me for about a week, and has been disabled since then. - Amazon Prime Video's offline viewer is terrible; it takes several hours to download one hour of TV over fiber optic high speed internet. The video quality when viewing offline is noticeably worse than viewing streaming, when the opposite should be true. My best guess is that there is some kind format transcoding that has to happen for downloaded videos, which makes it take forever and deteriorates the video quality.

title: Freetime is incompatible with audio books. Why?!?
body: I bought this Fire so my son can experience "immersive reading" by combining Audible audio books with Kindle books. Unfortunately, it turns out that this isn't possible within the Freetime app. Bummer.I love the idea of a child-safe space in Freetime, where content is tailored by me, where he can't access all my videos, my books, my apps. And I love the ability for him to read along as the audible book is being read. I'm excited at the huge library of audio books for children that Amazon offers. And I love the quality of the new Fire: it's much improved over my old version, and feels like an awesome tablet for the device.Unfortunately, my son won't be able to listen to audio books on it: Amazon has not enabled audiobooks on Freetime.Why oh why would they sell children's audio books, yet disable them in their children's app? It doesn't make any sense to me!I just called Amazon, spent an hour on the phone with them, only to learn that what I thought was a bug (the audible narration works just fine in my main account, just not in the Freetime set-up for my son), is actually a known "feature." They said it was for "privacy" reasons--but that doesn't make any sense to me. If you sell audio books for children, shouldn't they be able to listen to them in their app?I was told to just have him log into my main account, and use parental controls to disable the store and web browser--but that doesn't make sense, as my son would still be overwhelmed by my 400+ book library with lots of content that isn't appropriate for him. I cannot set another adult account up for him in my household, either, to customize for him (household sharing is limited to two adults)--and I'm not willing to set up an independent, separate Amazon account and repurchase all the books I already own, so he can listen to them.I asked the Amazon customer service person I spoke to if there was a way to contact the Product Management for Amazon Freetime, and was told the only way to do so was to send a snail mail letter to a corporate P.O. Box. Really?!?Anyway, I'm generally a HUGE Amazon fan; I have been a customer since the very early days in the late 1990's, and I own hundreds of Amazon books, as well as several Kindles. I've been very impressed with their customer service, across almost two decades and many customer service inquiries. Today was the first real disappointment.I do hope that someone from Amazon reads this, and forwards these comments to the Freetime Product Manager. I hope this is an easy fix in the software: I imagine that there are other parents who may want their children to be able to listen to audio books within Freetime!Are you monitoring this feedback, Amazon Fire product management? Please respond: I'd love to hear that a fix is in the works!

title: Great idea - poor delivery
body: I was extremely excited when I first saw Amazon's news about new e-reader. As a matter of fact, I immediately thought it would be a great Christmas gift for me. I am an avid book reader, and world traveler. I also read plenty of research (part of my job), so the concept of that device seemed to me revolutionary. I've considered before Sony eBook reader, but the hassle of downloads from proprietary website and limited selection of eBooks prevented me from buying the product. Kindle seemed to me like a great solution for a new way of reading books and research on the go.Unfortunately, there are several things which I don't like about this new device. It's only black and white (no problem with books), so subscription to magazines and reading some of my research with graphs and charts doesn't make sense. It does not support .PDF files without cumbersome conversion (95% of my research is written in that format). The wireless works only on EVDO (CDMA) network (I travel abroad once every 6 weeks for extended period of time) so wireless communication doesn't work abroad. The list of Kindle books is impressive but still limited. When I went through my own list of books I wanted to read over the next 12 months, only 10% of it was in Kindle format (I imagine over time there would be more conversions). When I buy a book, I usually share it at least with my family members (wife, brother, niece), who have a very similar taste in book reading like me, and my understanding is that even if one of them owned Kindle, I would not be able to transfer my books to their devices. There is also a risk with your Kindle library. Yes, I understand Amazon will be backing-up your library, but if something happens to your device, you need to buy a new one, if you want to utilize the library.And the last problem (but not the least) I see is the look and feel of Kindle device itself. It is thin and light but looks more like and old type plastic (PVC covered) calculator than sleek and modern or revolutionary device. The screen seems large enough but a large portion of the device is taken by `ugly' looking QWERTY. I am the iPhone owner, and if one compares just the look, the feel and ergonomics of both devices, one immediately discards the Kinlde device to the garbage. And it's not that I want Amazon to imitate Apple, but please, try to make your device more hip and fun (allow for different colors), and come up with different skins or leather book covers, or something to create device, which is not only useful but also nice to hold in your hand. So, Amazon, I will not buy Kindle now, but wait until you update/redesign your product and make more users friendly and less limited in terms of hardware and software choices. And by the way, I don't have a huge problem with price, but it will be nice if Kindle is at least $100 cheaper and eBooks are at the price level of paperbacks

title: DISAPPOINTING FOR LISTENING TO MUSIC!!!
body: I think they issued this item WITHOUT sufficient beta testing! I have now had three of them and none of them have adequate audio volume headroom.I like listening to classical music at concert hall volume. With these units, (all three of them), it's more like listening to the concert from OUTSIDE of the concert hall, or even OUTSIDE OF THE BUILDING. My old HD7 has FAR better sound than any of these.Also, none of the three would download music files to the microSD cards that I bought with them. Yes, I have activated all of the (three) preference switches in the "storage" area of the "Settings" menu. Still, whenever I download audio from my "Amazon Cloud Library", the files always go to the internal memory, NOT to the SDcards! Soon the internal memory is full and I get error messages stating that I am running out of memory, while my 64 GB MicroSD sits there EMPTY. One of the techs I talked with said, (I think), that they were working on that problem, but didn't have a solution yet. (I say, "I think", because everyone I talked with at their tech support site spoke "English" with a very strong accent, and some of what they said was very difficult to understand).There are a few other "bugs" and the "menu" architecture is VERY CONVOLUTED, DIFFICULT TO FIND WHATEVER YOU'RE LOOKING FOR. You can spend lots of time looking around in frustration. (For example, the "library" link doesn't show up in a lot of places where it should, (particularly in the "Music" area), so you often have to go back to the home page and follow a different track through the labyrinth to find access to the "Library". The instruction manual is definitely INCOMPLETE, (especially in the MUSIC department). There isn't anything at all about how to transfer audio files from the "internal memory" to the SD card. Their on-line help isn't much better.It's somewhat better for reading books, but the controls sometimes make it difficult to go back and forth WITHIN a volume that you're reading.My kids tell me that it's ok for playing games. My Netflix access generally works, but sometimes crashes and restarts.Basically, I like the idea behind this Kindle, it's got a promising "recipe", but they haven't finished baking it yet. It's still gooey in the middle.I love Amazon. They offered me a refund and gave me one replacement, (just as buggy as the other two). I turned down the refund hoping they would eventually exterminate the bugs and come up an operating system that works and doesn't just lead you around in unproductive circles.Until then, I'm still using my old HD7 and keeping my fingers crossed for better software for these new models.

title: Needs Page Numbers
body: I have had the Kindle for about a month now. It is a great concept and, with some improvements (discussed below) in future versions, could be a truly transformational technology.1. What I miss most compared to reading a print edition of a book, is knowing where I am in a chapter or in the book overall. For example, I have been using the Kindle to read a chapter book to my kids at bedtime. With a paper book, I can leaf forward to see how long the chapter is to decide whether we have time to finish the chapter or to read the next. Leafing around is much harder to do on the Kindle - I don't know if the next chapter is long or short so I can't judge whether we have time to read it. I have the same problem if I am reading in bed, and getting sleepy - I can't flip forward very easily to see if I am near a chapter break. All of this could be fixed if page numbers were inserted and some information where shown at the bottom indicating the page range of the current chapter. They could use the actual page numbers from a print edition (with page breaks shown as bracketed numbers in the text) or they could create their own electronic page numbers (it already has an electronic numbering system of sorts). It doesn't matter what page numbering system is used so long as it is understood how long a page is and, importantly, it displays the page at which the chapter ends, or the next chapter begins.2. Some of the Kindle Editions of books are poorly implemented - the formats should be standardized. But, here again, a page numbering system would help. For example, I downloaded a collection of Grimms Fairy Tales. The book consists of dozens of individual stories. These are all listed in the table of contents, but they are not linked to the table of contents (as are the chapters in most other Kindle Edition books). This means that I cannot select an individual Fairy Tale, but must turn, page-by-page to get to a story. This flaw makes this book (and any other anthology that shares this flaw) unusable. With a print edition you are much more in control; you can flip quickly through the book. Again, page numbers that can be shown on the table of contents, with a "go to page #" feature in the menu, would have resolved this issue.3. The Kindle Editions of some books have weird formatting glitches. Charts are reformated as unintelligable lists. Graphics and illustrations are generally omitted entirely. Kindle is capable of rendering monochrome graphics, but generally the Kindle Editions simply don't. I raise this in a product review of the Kindle itself because it is not an isolated issue - it is pervasive. In every Kindle Edition book and magazine I have downloaded, some or all of the illustrations and graphics are omitted.

title: Lukewarm feelings for Fire 7 (5th gen./No ads.)
body: I bought an 8gb model with no ads for my wife for Christmas.First off: It is a $40 tablet.So my expectations were not super high, even still we've been rather disappointed.The unit we kept was the third replacement.When my wife opened it on Christmas we noticed immediately that the screen had a tiny dead area. Amazon customer service was great and sent a replacement right away.... The replacement ALSO had dead pixels in the screen.Customer service again was responsive and sent a new unit. Unit #3 had a good screen and is the one we kept.As far as function goes, the quad core cpu does great on a low end spec device and reading her ebooks, watching Netflix, lite browsing, and lite gaming works.I was also able to install the Play store and critical play services without any fuss or voiding any warranties. (Shame that you have to side load, but whatever.)So now she has access to apps that she needs and are not in the Amazon App store.The FireOS is kind of a Train Wreck - that is if you need to do any trouble shooting or have any respect for AOSP. For a basic user it works, and my wife is generally happy with it.Generally.The January update (5.3.2.1) introduced several improvements ... AND several pretty awful bugs. Starting with the new update the tablet will lose 30-40% or more of it's battery over night in "sleep mode"Naturally, the battery usage app that is baked into all versions of Android to allow users to troubleshoot battery life ... Is removed in the FireOS fork. After digging around on the forums and downloading a battery data app it seems this is a widespread bug and the guilty program is "system apps"The most common recommended solution is either charge your tablet every night or power it down completely.To me, that's a pretty terrible solution and I hope they patch this soon, but the wife is kinda okay with it.Ultimately, as far as $50 ($40 when it's on sale) tablet goes, it's not terrible. It's obvious the manufacturing is not the best and the Android fork is Ham fisted at best, but for a cheap tablet it gets the job done and the quad core processor can manage the low RAM okay.My wife likes it and (aside from the frustration of returning 2 and battery issues) is happy with it.Personally, I'm glad I didn't splurge and get myself one too. I kind of hate it.UPDATE: Return #3 in progress. Charging issues became more apparent and battery life tanked at EXACTLY 90 days. Customer service was understanding and gave us a return after the 90 day warranty period had just expired.3 Duds. Waiting on Fire Tablet #4

title: Great idea, poor execution
body: I enjoy my Kindle and being able to carry my reading with me in my purse instead of a suitcase. Still, the current execution of the Kindle leaves a lot to be desired.First, the Kindle is an odd shape that is difficult to hold. I am constantly turning the page when I don't mean to because the next page button runs down the entire length of the right side. The power button and the wireless button are on the back of the unit where you have to remove the Kindle from it's cover to reach them. The Kindle won't stay in it's cover anyway, but it would be nice to be able to reach all the controls from the front. It would be a much better design to make a flat thin package with the buttons all on the front. They shouldn't be too big and the device should be designed so that it would stay in it's cover.If you leave on the wireless feature, it quickly depletes the battery. There is no indicator on the screen to notify you that wireless is on. Since the switch is on the back, I often leave it on without realizing it.The keyboard is extrainous. I would be much better served with a touch screen minus the tiny keys.I had hoped that the Kindle would be multipurpose. As it is, it is a $350 electronic book. With the linux operating system, it could be so much more. It could hold my calander, my contacts phone book, email and support simple programs that would make my life easier. Alas, none of this is supported.The processor is underpowered, even for the electronic book alone. It is certainly inadequate to do any serious web browsing. I like the electronic paper screen, but it is slow to refresh. Color would also be nice.My ideal kindle?Take the form factor of an I-pod touch and streatch it the the current screen size. Give it an OLED touch screen and eliminate the tiny keys. Put all the controls except on and off on the screen. Imporve the processor power and open up the menu's to allow us to add linux applications. Give us web access at an acceptable speed. Charge me a subscription for air time, it would be worth it to have a web pad that could be carried anywhere. Keep Whisper net as this is a great way to deliver books. Get more magazines on board. I would love to get all my magazines by electronic edition. (Color should help a lot in recruiting magazines).You have a real opportunity here. Give us a pad computer at a size that is easy to read and yet small enough to easily carry. You have the makings of a device to replace PDA's and laptop computers and sell a billion books to us to boot.I await Kindle 2. I hope it will meet my expectations. Frankly, I'm more than a little disappointed in Kindle 1.

title: Disappointed - but hopeful
body: My wife bought me a Kindle for my birthday - and I know that she was excited to give me such an innovative gift. I love gadgets and because I travel a lot - she thought that the Kindle was perfect for me. I could carry several books in a small space - download from the web - it sounded great!!I opened it yesterday and within about 10 minutes of playing with it I knew that it just wasn't ready for prime time.1. Ironically, like other reviewers, I found that reading the instructions that automatically load on the Kindle was harder than reading the printed instructions that came with it.....that was my first clue!!!2. The amazingly irritating screen "pop" that, although probably meant to simulate turning a page,is more like a blinking light, and as I flipped back and forth between pages it sometimes took a few seconds - which, given that at this point I had downloaded nothing into it - did not look promising for when it was fully loaded!!!3. As other reviewers pointed out - the design is cumbersome - the keys at the bottom are hard to read - and the "next page" buttons are VERY badly located - I turned the page three or four times by accident just picking up the Kindle!!! Getting back to the right page took a few moments - much longer than going forward - which seems ridiculous!!4. As I travel on planes a lot - it seemed like the compact design would be great. Then as I realized that the airlines are decades behind the real world in understanding that using electronic devices does not interfere with the plane's navigation system - it dawned on me that at times when I would most like to read from the Kindle - I would be reminded by the Flight Attendant that electronic devices must be switched off after the doors are closed!!!! So, I would literally be unable to use it during the extended wait times before take off!!!5. I like to read in the bath and while eating - the prospect of reading through a zip-lock bag did not appeal to me!!6. As it was a gift I was not aware of the price of the Kindle. At $359 it is too expensive - probably $100 too much. If the display was in color - note to Amazon - some good books are illustrated - not to mention magazines etc.7. The selection of books although extensive, over 10,000 titles - the list is missing some significant content. Other reviewers have discussed this also.So, I am disappointed - I had hoped that the Kindle would live up to its hype. It's current version (1.0) doesn't do that. BUT --- it has promise and I'll probably look seriously at Version 2 or 3.Amazon, nice try. The Kindle has a lot of potential - keep working on it.

title: The "Zune" of the text world
body: In Amazon's defense, this product looks like an attempt at making inroads into a potential market that nobody has been able to develop - that of eBooks. Unfortunately, it seems destined to a quick demise.Sure, Amazon probably has the connections with publishers to develop the necessary volume of titles for use on this device, which is something that very few other firms have right off the bat. But that's only part of the equation, and the only part that it looks like they got right.The device itself - well, it's outrageously expensive for nothing more than a display with some memory attached to it, overly complex, and lacking a few basic features. I know backlit screens are difficult to read for a long period of time, but shouldn't it have some provision for reading in darkness? The wireless delivery is very cool.That being said, what I think is the real killer here is the fact that all of the content is DRM protected and limited to storage on the Kindle. Sure, people are a lot more confident in buying digitally-delivered products than they used to be (example: people buy most of their music on iTunes with confidence), but I think that this premise only holds true when you can store the content on your computer, in a format you trust. I for one wouldn't spend much money developing a library that is constricted to an obscure new device that has no history and could lose its support a few years down the road.Also, while there may be a relatively large library of titles available, is it really large enough to justify a $400 price tag? For some readers who focus on bestsellers or are flexible about choosing from the available titles this device may be fine, but for that amount of money I want to be able to read pretty much anything on it.Furthermore, why would I tote around a new device when my notebook is already with me most of the time, and perfectly capable of displaying the same thing? The convenience of having a standalone bookreader doesn't outweigh the fact that it's another $400 item to carry just to read a book.Amazon is simply asking for too much of an investment of both money and trust from consumers in this device. I for one would love to have the ability to read books electronically... however, I won't be doing it until they are available on my computer. Even if it were in the form of an iTunes-like DRM protected software package... just not on a $400 device that has no backing of a known electronics manufacturer and no history of support. This thing really brings Microsoft's Zune music player to mind... it needed to be done, but it's poorly executed to the point that I don't even see why they bothered.

title: A Kindle in the Wind
body: As an avid reader and user of technology, it PAINS me to write that something so promising (and needed) as the Kindle has come as such a big disappointment...enough that it worries me this is a reflection of things to come from Amazon.While usually a trailblazer of innovative consumer-centered design, the Kindle looks more like what you would expect from a big telecom - designed more for them than for you.My big issues? Poor design and connectivity and the stunningly low-bar someone set for what could otherwise be a revolutionary product.Rather than follow in the footsteps of the iPod, iPhone or Blackberry (or even Amazon itself), the makers have created an unsexy device that doesn't even fit well into the human hand. It has a myriad of buttons more poorly placed than my old high school clock radio. Navigation is non-intuitive and page turns, book loads and searches are slow. Unfortunately the poor features and functions outweigh cool ones like the very readable screen technology.On a side note, it is amazing that someone associated with Amazon let the web based features even make the initial white boarding session. The first Kindle is a testimony that they have gotten away from what they know best.The connectivity issues are enough to turn me away. Not only have they made a poor choice in choosing cellular, but they have partnered with Sprint, which doesn't provide connectivity in 80% of our world. Looking at their coverage map is like looking at a U.S. map before the Lewis and Clark expedition. If you are outside a major city, your Kindle is extinguished.Why not let consumers do what we do with every other wireless device and connect via WiFi? At the very least this would ensure connectivity at the two places I would guess people read the most: at home and at work. It is also misleading to refer to any cellular device as truly wireless.Sure I can download books using my USB cable - but what about newspapers, magazine and blogs? Somebody didn't think this product out and you can tell by the marketing and support materials as they are both dismissive and defensive about these inadequacies. Why didn't they just wait and fix them?Hopefully, Amazon will make some big changes before version 2.0. Until then, I'm packing mine up and regretfully sending it back.I'm also reassessing the mindshare I've given to Amazon to lead us into a new era of publishing. This product just has too many fundamental flaws to be considered necessary, much less revolutionary...and it is not a harbinger of good things from a company that is out to redefine the publishing industry.

title: After using an iPad for several years I’ve been disappointed by my new Kindle Fire (7”
body: After using an iPad for several years I’ve been disappointed by my new Kindle Fire (7”, 5th gen.). When I got my Fire I had been thinking that it might be nice to have a smaller, lighter device as an option. I also thought that it would help extend the battery life of my aging iPad so that I might postpone a replacement purchase.My biggest disappointment with the Fire is the odd screen size and low resolution (171 ppi / 1024 x 600). After the iPad’s 2048 x 1536 display the Fire’s long, narrow, low-res screen is quite a letdown. The screen’s aspect ratio is optimized for video but I only occasionally use it for video. Instead, I use it mostly for reading Kindle books, Texture magazines and the Washington Post (free 6-mo. subscription included with the Fire). For reading books, it’s not bad but the small screen size means you spend a lot of time turning pages. And, with the screen’s poor resolution you don’t want to decrease the font size too much. (If you do plan on watching video note that the Google YouTube app is unavailable in the Amazon app store. There are third-party apps which are apparently designed to play YT videos but the standard Google app you are probably familiar with is not offered.)For reading magazines in Texture, the screen’s low resolution works even less well. With the tall, thin screen you have to do a lot of zooming and panning to get a decent size image—which is only a small portion of the magazine’s page. Of course, when zoomed in you lose the overall perspective on any photos or graphics. And the Fire’s Texture app lacks some features included with the iPad Texture app; e.g., the ability to rotate the display; or the animations seen in National Geographic on the iPad.I’ve found similar limitations in the handful of other apps I’ve downloaded. For example, you can’t search in the Fire's version of the TED app.Battery life is very poor. I accidentally left it turned on without using it for a couple of days and the next time I tried to use it the battery was dead. Just sitting idle it drains the battery.The device incessantly pushes ads. Every time you turn on the screen there’s a new ad at the lock screen. Notifications for ads are constantly appearing and there doesn’t seem to be any way to turn that off.Finally, as is well known, the Fire has a highly customized version of Android. Among other things, this means you can’t get apps from the Google Play Store. The only app store is Amazon’s. Apple’s walled garden approach has convinced me that my next serious tablet needs to be an Android—it just won’t be a Fire with its own walled garden.

title: Don't let it get stolen!!!
body: So, I purchased this tablet for my toddler, specifically for the educational apps that are available on it. I love the tablet itself in its simplicity and my daughter was very attached to it. I'm rating this product a 3 though because of the customer service. It's a wonderful tablet to own, unless it is stolen. My daughter's was stolen and when I contacted Amazon about it I was informed the only thing I could do was "Blacklist" it so that whoever stole it couldn't use it and to report it stolen to the police. It was explained to me that once the police called in that Amazon would release the information of who deregistered it from me and re-registered it to them self so that I could get the tablet back. I followed that advice only to be told by the detective that took the case that Amazon was refusing him the information. Upon calling Amazon a third time myself, I was again informed that if the detective had the serial number that he would be able to obtain the information of who had the tablet. After a second time of the detective being denied, I called again only to reach a new Amazon rep that I asked what else I needed to do or what procedure to follow and when I wouldn't give up on trying to find out she finally told me, "nobody will be given that information, not even the police and honestly you will not get the tablet back." At that point I requested a supervisor, held for almost 30 minutes, for the agent to get back on the phone and tell me that she would call back in 5-10 minutes because the supervisor was on another call, and she promptly hung up on me. This is after me stating countless times that I wanted to hold for the supervisor and didn't mind waiting for them to get on the line with me. When I called back and finally reached the correct department and had a supervisor on the phone, I also was informed that the agent had lied to me about her name (it was Brittany and she told me Kim, even spelled it out). The supervisor also told me that she had told me the truth, that they wouldn't give the police the information because they had to protect the other persons privacy and rights. I was given an email to request the information from and then ushered off the phone. The detective has yet to receive any response to the email at subpoena-criminal@Amazon.com (I'm pretty sure I got that accurate without going through all my notes) nor did I ever receive that call back from the agent named "Kim".I know that this tablet isn't terribly expensive in a monetary capacity, however it's got sentimental value. It was the first present I was ever able to buy my daughter on my own.

title: I like my prior "generation" Fire tablet better.
body: FIRST I want to remind anyone reading this, that this is simply my personal opinion on this product!After buying a few unbranded tablets that were a waste of money, I finally decided to buy a Fire tablet. My mother had one and after trying hers, I fell in love with it! So I got myself one. The first one I got was a Fire HD 6 (4th Generation). I loved every single thing about it. (Until it updated it's operating system and the whole look of it changed. Did NOT like that, but unless I wanted to reset it to factory settings and lose all my data, I really didn't have a choice about using it with it's updates. At Christmas time, I bought this new one, the Fire 7" 5th Generation. I bought a second one because in spite of the updates of the first Fire, I really ultimately liked it better than anything I'd had yet. I was excited waiting for it, but have been a bit disappointed with it ever since I received it.First of all, every time it goes into sleep mode, the screen saver is nothing but ads. So instead of an attractive screen to swipe to start it, it's advertisements every single time (yes, this lasts for only one second if you swipe it immediately, but the point is- it's the first thing you see). Completely annoying & cheap looking. Also, it has the operating system that my first one updated to, so that wasn't great. It's also pre-loaded with 12 Amazon apps. If I wanted the apps I would have downloaded them myself! Give me the choice. And when you go to the system settings to where it shows the apps that are downloaded, it doesn't show any of the pre-loaded Amazon apps, so no way to delete them. So the home page is cluttered with not your own apps, but the pre-installed ones. And finally, the quality. The quality is almost as if they were in a hurry to put this out for Christmas. Although my first Fire is only 6" and a 4th generation, it 'feels' better in quality, if that makes any sense. Also, this new tablet, after choosing an app while waiting for it to load, when the screen goes black, there are several places along the sides where white light is bleeding through- at least 3 on each side. It's not so obvious while playing a game or watching a show, because you are distracted and with the range of colors it's not so obvious, but it is when it's loading. My other one never does this.All in all, I'd have to say, I'd personally do not like this newest version. As far as the price goes- price isn't supposed to be part of a 'product' review- but I'll just add that obviously the price has NOW lowered on this product since it's arrival. It wasn't low when I bought mine.

title: Not good for kids, even with a Prime account
body: Amazon needs to tweak these tablets. They are fine for adults. They could be great for kids with a few small changes.We bought three of these for our kids for a road trip. We wanted the kids to be able to access apps and movies from the tablets and we liked that we could create profiles for kids using something called Free Time on the tablet. When the kids went into their profiles from the main "lock screen" (which was tricky to figure out at first) they had no access to our main Amazon account. We loved this. We could get to our Amazon account from the lock screen by entering a password, but they had no access to it. Also, we liked that we could customize each child profile with the apps we thought would be appropriate for them. This also took some effort to figure out, but for the price, it was worth figuring out.Here's the problem: when the kids are in their own profiles, the navigation is easy. It's a "carousel display" that was common to older versions of Fire tablets. However, you can't have this display with your own profile.So why is that important? Well, with a Prime account, you are able to download movies to the tablets, but kids cannot access any downloaded Prime movies or shows unless they are on YOUR profile. (So much for security.) Now, you can add movies you buy outside of Prime to their profiles, but you cannot share anything that you download from Prime. For example, our daughter can watch "Annie" for free if we pull it up on the tablet from our account. But she can't see it from her profile unless we spend 13 dollars to BUY the movie...if we do that, we can share it with her. Amazon allows you to give your kids access to PURCHASED movies, not anything downloaded through Prime.We considered eliminating their profiles altogether, so the kids could just access the movies from our main profile screen. The tablets aren't secure from there, but we planned on monitoring them from the trip. However, there is a problem with that as well. Remember that kid-friendly "carousel display" we mentioned? There is no way to get this display from the main profile screen so they would have to try and navigate a very confusing screen, and wouldn't be able to do it on their own (they are young).Amazon needs to either allow kids to access the Prime movies downloaded from their profiles or allow the main screen to be adjusted so it is kid friendly. That would make for a great tablet. Until then, these need to be adjusted. We suggest holding off from buying one of these until they work this out.

title: COMES WITH NO INSTRUCTIONS WHATSOEVER!!
body: What I don't like about this devise is that it shipped without ANY INSTRUCTIONS WHATSOEVER!!!! Nor does there appear to be any online tutorial for using it. You open it up and then you have to ask yourself, "Well, what do I do next? You hit the power button (which you only know about from a non-verbal diagram on a folded 3x5 card that did come with it) and nothing happens. They don't tell you that you have to charge the battery first. With it on charge, it will start. And then you find that IT has to download the OS (which takes around 50 min.) and other crap before it will open to the home page. There you do find the most rudimentary 5 step tutorial ("slide your finger up. Slide your finger down.") But then you're on your own. This is not a simple devise. But it could be mastered easily IF there was an adequate tutorial to go along with it. Without that though, you are left struggling to figure everything out. For example: There are several alternative keyboards. One for lower case letters (no shift key), one for upper case letters, one for numbers. But NOTHING there tells you about this. It is not intuitive. There are no instructions on how to use the cameras. Bla bla bla. You have to configure it to get email. But no instructions on how to do this. There are NO INSTRUCTIONS FOR ANYTHING!!!! I suppose they assumed that people buying it will have smart phone experience and will know how to operate it. But why would such people buy a devise like this when their smart phone does most of what this devise does? I bought it as an alternative to a smart phone. But if I have to spend hours trying to figure out how to make it work, my initial enthusiasm for it is diminishing exponentially. And this lack of instruction seems so pointless. I understand not including a printed one, cuz of cost. I mean it's only $50! But not to have an online tutorial seems to me to be inexcusable. It's overwhelmingly frustrating.Having carped about that, I will say that the picture quality is great. The battery life seems quite good. And I hope that, once I have spent hours figuring it out, I might very well enjoy it. But right now that is definitely NOT the case.I think it should also be said that, it quickly becomes obvious that the REAL point of the devise is to drive the user towards buying Amazon content. Of the many icons on the home page, more than half are links to Amazon products.All in all, my advise to potential buyers is be aware that you will be on your own getting it to function properly. Amazon will be of no help.

title: How could the same company make this, the Fire 7 HD and the Fire TV
body: I've tried so hard to like this tablet, because I'm a big fan of my Fire 7 HD Third Generation and my Fire TV. The hardware on this one seems fine, but what were they trying to do with the software? It's really terrible, even for a $35 tablet (what I paid). Probably the worst feature is the home screen, where the controls are part of a transparent layer which is then superimposed over an advertising image. How is the user supposed to deal with such visual clutter? It sure takes the fun out of using the tablet. What were they thinking?Next, instead of having a simple apps page showing all my apps (like every other tablet), selecting the "apps" tab gives me a selection of about 5 and I have to press yet another link to see "all my apps." You know, when I selected "apps" I really do want to see my apps -- yes, all of them! And I would like to see them in decent sized icons. How could you do this right on the 7 HD and fail so completely with the new Fire 7?News Flash: Amazon seems to have upped their game in keeping the user from augmenting the paltry selection of apps by side loading. Side loading made the Fire 7 HD really pretty useful, but now Amazon (in its infinite wisdom) has gotten nasty about it and I seem to be limited to their pathetic app store. I can side load all the Play Store files, but the device still refuses to play them. I'm sorry, but if you can't get a Google App, a You Tube App, a BBC News app and a Wall Street Journal video app in your app store, you just don't deserve to be in the app store business. No, CNN and USA Today aren't substitutes. They should know this.By the way, who are these people giving this 5 stars? Have they never used an iPad or an Android tablet? I'm giving this thing 2 stars, not compared to a regular 7 inch tablet, but even as a $35 tablet. I like cheap hardware as much as the next guy, but it has to have some level of usability. The Fire 7 HD has it; this thing doesn't. I wish I had returned it while I could, but I just couldn't believe it was as bad as it it apparently is. I figured "How bad can it be for $35? "Surely I can get $35 worth of use out of it." Well, let's just say I always reach for my Fire 7 HD and this one sits on the table next to the other devices that still work, but can't compete. I will give it one thing: it was definitely the cheapest of the devices in that "not good enough" category.Someday Amazon will make a terrific entry-level tablet again, but how will I know if the reviewers call this one a 5 star?

title: Disappointed - Don't expect Apple user experience
body: We were given this as a gift. My first impression was that the Fire is a steal for the price, but user experience has been sub par. I cannot speak to using the Fire within the Amazon infrastructure. We intended to use the Fire for one thing - let our kids watch movies on long car rides. I copied some files from our PC to the Fire internal memory and our own micro SD card which I installed in the Fire. Worked fine for a few rides, until it simply didn't. Three kids, two happily watching their iPads and when I try to start up Mickey for the third, I just get a spinning circle on the screen. I am pretty tech savvy so I tried to fix it in the driveway for five minutes before we leave for a three hour trip. No success, restart, turn airplane mode on and off, remove the SD card - nothing works and therefore screaming kid. I have been using various incarnations of the iPad since its launch - I have never had a problem like this. My experience with Apple products is that they are overpriced but they work.When we got back home, I deleted all the files off the Fire and reinstalled them. Great, Mickey was alive again. That is until we had another road trip. Confident that I fixed the problem we got in the car, and again spinning circle and video wouldn't start.Fool me once, shame on you.....That's it, the Fire failed at the most simple of tasks I required of it. For me its a zero or one star, but I gave it two since I didn't really put it through the paces and therefore cannot call it utter trash. Though, like I said, it failed at what I consider to be one of the most basic functions of a tablet. If you are looking for a cheap tablet, buy a used iPad. Maybe a little more money, but, in my opinion, the user experience is far superior.My other big problem was the lack of easy screen lock. The kindle does have a whole "child mode" function (I forget the actual name) where you can limit apps and usage time and a whole host of things, but all I was after was a simple lock so my kid couldn't keep shutting the movie off (when it actually played). No dice. There might be video apps that allow this, but honestly why do I have to download a 3rd party app to do something that should be native to the device.Final note: I am completely open to the possibility that there was somehow user error on my part, but I tried to research the issue and couldn't find any help. I am usually the person friends come to for help fixing their devices, it shouldn't be that hard.If my reviews are helpful, please click "yes" below.

title: The Hardware is great, the Operating System and Interface is a big, frustrating, confusing advertisement for Amazon
body: This boils down to a review of the OS, not the hardware itself, which is fine. I even love the fact that it includes an SD card slot. The problem is that in the past couple of years Amazon has transformed the Fire from an easy to use tablet to a confusing mess designed to get you to buy stuff from Amazon.You can go ahead and buy an Ad Free version of the Fire, but the entire tablet is essentially an Ad for Amazon, music, movies, apps, and anything else they want to try to sell you.Gone is the simple Carousel that made it easy to navigate through apps. Now you have a "home" screen that just throws all of your apps together over several pages with no hope of organizing anything so that it is easy to find. You can move icons around on individual pages, but not sort them in any meaningful way over all of the pages. You do not have a "favorites" list. There is a recently used list, but that is of limited use. When you are on your home screen you end up with several different screens such as your "library" and your installed apps and other stuff that is given front and center placement despite being something that should be accessed through the app store.So far this is not a deal breaker. The problem is that the home "screen" is one of several that clutters up the device. All of the other screens are basically App Stores for everything that Amazon wants you to buy, such as music, movies, tv episodes, books, etc. If you do buy these things good luck finding them, since it will ALWAYS be easier to find what Amazon wants you to buy next than to find out what you have already paid for.Amazon is really shooting themselves in the foot here. My family has bought 3 kindles for reading and are now on our 5th Fire, but it will be our last. When our son was under 2 he could easily use a 1st generation Kindle Fire to find his favorite apps, then go to the home screen and switch to a different one, and he never got stuck trying to figure out how to get out of something he did not want to be in. Now that he is 4 and much more technologically savvy he spends most of his time frustrated trying to get out of confirmations to buy things he does not want. We could buy the kids version, but the functionality of that is horrible, as is Kindle Free Time. The first kindle was so easy that literally a 1 year old could use it, the new ones are so horrible that my wife does not use hers anymore because it is too convoluted, and she has a technology related masters degree and has done her share of programming.

title: Disappointed - cameras and video camera options don't work. Waiting on a replacement...
body: I might change this/update this in the future, but for now it's a 2-star experience. Disappointing. I bought this for a few reasons - it was inexpensive, it was small, I need to video my dance practices, and I like that there's the option to use a micro SD memory card for up to 128 gb. I received the tablet, went through all the updates, connected in to my Amazon account... and tried the camera. First - no option or button or setting that would enable me to switch from the front-facing camera to the rear-facing camera. I looked everywhere, under every tab, multiple times. Nothing. It has a rear camera and that you should be able to use... but no way to get to it. We found an online manual that showed us what the icon should look like and where it should be - not there. Second, the video isn't working either (using front-facing, which is honestly what I need most of all). I touch the video camera icon and it goes from red to grey, then everything freezes. Front facing camera is bad quality as it is, but I knew that - it's only $50 afterall, not expecting too much. Another thing I don't like is the advertisements. They are ridiculous. Every time your screen locks or you turn it back on, it's a new ad. I can get past it - just irritating.So to deal with the camera issues, I jumped on Chat with Amazon. All that the chat helper was able to do was to ask me if it was charged (really?) and ask me to try to restart it by holding the power button down for a full 40 seconds. I did that, and it wouldn't turn on again. I was finally able to get it to turn back on by pressing the power button for different lengths of time, but it fixed nothing. All they could do to help was to offer a replacement, which I asked for the following day. This was all yesterday, October 7, and when they sent my confirmation email for the replacement, the expected delivery date is October 28th!! Online they're available again (must be sold out) October 26th... so they couldn't even spring for next-day shipping!!I'm not someone who is terribly hard to please. I wasn't expecting too much for $50, but the main reason I got it was for video/photos and the memory, and the photos and video options don't even work. Amazon support wasn't much help, as they didn't even know if this was normal or just my unit. I would honestly just upgrade to the $100 one, but there's no option for an additional memory card, so my other option would the the $150 - and then I might as well get an older Surface like my daughters have. At least I know those work.....

title: Not Ready for Prime Time
body: I will read anything, any time and any place. I wanted very much to like the Kindle, but it is simply not a very good device. I really think that it should be viewed as a beta: It has some problems, but it works. I have used my Palm Treo for reading for several years (first a 600, then a 650, a 700, a 750 & now a 755). I really appreciate that it's always in my pocket or on my belt and whenever I have a couple of spare minutes, I can pull it out and read a few pages. The Kindle is just another gadget to carry. It's too large to put in a pocket, the button layout just sucks and I have had serious problems with the device having to be re-set frequently. I finally tracked that down to an improperly formatted book that I had put on it. The battery life is actually quite good, which I expected, since the selling point of e-ink displays is that it only uses power when it updates like turning a page. The greatest part of the Kindle is the ability to buy books from Amazon with just a few clicks. It's like always being in a bookstore, which is dangerous for me as I could quickly run up a huge debt. It uses Verizon's EVDO network for wireless connectivity, which means that you are usually in coverage. The keyboard and User Interface are what makes this a don't buy right now recommendation. A touch screen or a mouse of some sort would really help. The User Interface is confusing and takes some time to get used to. To anyone who uses a computer frequently the cursor bar is confusing, but you quickly get used to it. The keyboard reminds me of a Sinclair Z80, it is very stiff and is difficult to use even though it is a QWERTY layout. Any review of the Kindle would be incomplete without mentioning the fact that it WILL NOT READ PDFs. PDFs have to be converted to be used on the Kindle. This is easily done with the Mobipocket Creator, which is free for download. But it really seems senseless to me that the Kindle won't natively read a PDF. PDF is a standard file format that is used in more and more programs and is accepted everywhere. Lastly, the formats that the Kindle accepts are limited and not commonly used outside of Palm products. It will accept .txt, .prc, .mobi, and Amazon's proprietary .azw formats. The books downloaded from Amazon are locked with Digital Rights Management. I am very disappointed in Amazon in producing a product of this poor design and with as many problems as it has. I will anxiously await Kindle 2.0 and see if they can get it right next time.

title: Too slow to be useful
body: Should have written this before Christmas, to save some headaches. This is almost a usable device. But due to its slow speed, i have to warn you against it. it's a shame, because it's a really good value at the price. I too read all the reviews that say it's slow, and having a 2nd Gen which is considerably slower than my iPad, i thought that's what they meant. No, this device isn't really practical for anyone. It reminds me of very early Sony blu-ray players that got confused and locked up when you went too fast for them. I still use and love my Kindle 2nd gen everyday. The battery no longer holds a charge, but keeping it plugged in to operate it, usually works. Should have sprung for the new battery, but, at the time, it was confused trying to figure out which was the exact replacement battery, and my wrong-battery-ordered drawer is already full with incorrect laptop and phone battery past orders. It seems to me that if you know you're product type, style and generation, ordering a new battery on amazon should be a breeze, but that hasn't been my experience.So, it's not just the snail pace of this device. The touchscreen is also very finicky. There are many times the touch location required just doesn't respond. Could be speed related, but even trying to use the X to clear the search bar usually fails. But it's not just that, I routinely can't select by touching the screen, because it just won't respond. Battery life is yet another issue. It can be fully charged in the morning, throw it in my bag and when I take it out on lunch hour or afternoon, it's down to 30 - 40 percent without having been used. And once the screen is activated it zooms down from there. The resting battery loss may be related to wi-fi but the usual culprit Facebook is not even installed on this device.Because of my battery issue with the 2nd gen, i still must use this but I find myself only using it to read books. It's just too slow for most game and youtube apps. Don't even email on it, because the touch screen is too wonky to write an email.Finally, I prefer the size of the 2nd gen, more square where this is one is decidedly a rectangle which makes finding the right case a bit tricky.Also, i miss the carousel on this model and haven't found a way to enable that.To summarize, if you have NEVER used an iPad. Or earlier Kindle Fire, you might be patient enough for this one to be a value tablet. But i would recommend you pay a little more for faster processing.

title: Sham Shared storage reduces value
body: I was pretty excited about this item. My son's have Samsung Galaxy Tabs, but there was no way to segregate the educational games from the general games. So even with purchasing multiple learning apps, we couldn't be sure the boys weren't just gaming. As a result, we just assumed the tablets were toys and limit their use accordingly. We were considering another tab, but learned that these were for sale and had child friendly profiles. Unfortunately for each child profile we create, the apps added consume storage space on every other tablet! Are you kidding me! We have 3 boys, purchased three tablets and cannot use the child friendly app space because it renders the advantage of having 3 tablets useless.I was able to add Google Play and use most of the boys' apps, however, they cannot be moved to the child protected space.This is a very cheap tablet. I didn't expect too much, but the shared storage space is a huge drawback. Additionally, a large chunk of data is consumed by Amazon video on each tablet, despite my never having downloaded a video and disabling Amazon video because I don't want the boys to have access to videos.So without being able to use the child profiles, I think that this wouldn't be too horrible of a deal if Amazon didn't force all of the various profiles to be downloaded onto each tablet. As the boys now have to use the adult profiles, I had to set up so many different purchase blocks it would almost be worth $100 extra dollars per tablet to have just buy each boy another Samsung Galaxy Tab.These are pretty nice for $50 if you don't need or intend to use the child profiles. It still wouldn't have been the worst thing in the world if I had simply been aware prior to my purchase that all of the profiles would consume space on each tablet. Each one has a 32GB SD card, but there are still storage issues that prevent downloads if the child profiles are I use and taking up space.Would I buy these again? No. I could have bought a single tab and the boys could have shared for the exact same price of the 3 Fire 7's I bought. I really don't understand why there is no way to stop the additional profiles from taking up space, even when their hidden. So disappointed because these could have been pretty cool.Great graphics. Using a Unicorn Beetle rugged case. Great battery life. Serious storage issuss. And 8GB is more like 5GB. And books cannot be moved to the SD card.

title: Not even decent.
body: For the price, I guess it is ok, but it isn't a polished product in the sense that it has several major flaws that detract from what would otherwise be a great device. First, battery life sucks. Even under moderate use, you can watch the percent charge numbers drop at a rate of about 1% every 3 to 5 minutes. I called amazon and their foreign call center staff, although nice in an artificial way, weren't all that knowledgable and their best response was to shut all power drains - wifi, location, screen brightness, apps, the usual - off. That doesn't make for a very satisfactory user experience. Next, the screen is not very good unless you crank up the brightness -- then it is nice, but you lose a lot of battery and quickly. Next, the touch screen is not nearly as good as my first generation iPad and nothing like my Samsung. The speakers are pathetic. The user interface takes some getting used to but isn't horrible. And when I tried to use it with FB messenger to video chat with my daughter, it wouldn't stay connected. No clue why, but that was disappointing.Now for the good. Access to a great array of games, videos, music and other stuff for "free" with prime membership. Because I get so many books and audible books from Amazon, it is nice to have easy access to them. The music library is amazing. It is like getting an iTunes store for free. Much better than my experiences with streaming sites because you can download the music for use without a connection. It has a micro sd slot. Its build quality is great. Far better than my Samsung Galaxy and as good as my first gen iPad. MUCH stronger than the iPad Air I have at work, the screen of which cracked from a minor - 6 inch - drop within a week of my receiving it.Final analysis. If it were not for the price and the prime stuff, it wouldn't be worth keeping. But with those two attributes it is a good knock around unit. If I had young kids, this would be my choice due to strength and, well, if it breaks, it was only $50 bucks. Still, it is a little disappointing. I sure hope I can fix the battery drain issue. If not, I might send it back for replacement. Maybe I just got a bad battery.UPDATE Battery stinks, drains overnight just sitting there with no apps active. Run out of memory even with a 32G sd added because apps have to be stored to the device and they eat the memory. I only have about 5 extra apps. The touch screen is horrible.

title: Cheap, small, heavy, prior-generation wifi tablet with bad battery life
body: Disclosure: I got this Fire Tablet for free as a promotion for signing up for Xfinity internet. With that said, I would be disappointed if I had paid money for the device, even at the low price that Amazon is charging.Build quality: Very heavy for its size, low screen resolution compared to other devices. The screen is really shiny, contributing a lot of glare.Hardware: No 5 ghz wifi. The 2.4 ghz wifi is extremely unreliable-- I constantly have to disconnect and re-connect. None of my 5 ghz devices have this problem. Even my old phone, which only has 2.4 ghz, is much more stable. The battery drains very quickly.Software: The Silk browser is sloooow. I prefer to browse the web on my phone (looking at mobile pages) or on a laptop (looking at full size pages). The Xfinity app is also slooow.Use cases: The main use I have found for this device is reading newspaper/magazines through their native apps. For example, the Washington Post is very nice and easy to read on this device, with full color pictures, and comes with a free 6 month subscription. It is an improvement over reading the paper on a phone or on a traditional Kindle. As for books, I would quite simply never choose this device over my traditional Kindle. The text is sharper and clearer with the Kindle, and the battery on the Kindle lasts forever. As for videos, the 7 inch screen isn't really that much of an improvement over a phone if watching by yourself, especially at low resolution, and the battery drain means you won't watch for very long. For watching with others, I much prefer to cast to a TV (a feature that as far as I can tell is not available on this device, and anyway I can do that from my phone).Summary: The main advantage of this device is that it is very inexpensive. However, the tradeoffs are numerous. It's cheaply built and slow, and the battery doesn't last long. Combined with its intermediate size-- not quite small enough to put in your pocket but not quite large enough to watch a movie-- it does not have very many use cases for which another device is better suited.If you need a cheap, small, heavy, prior-generation wifi tablet with bad battery life, this is for you.If you like to read books, steer clear and go for a Kindle paperwhite. For everyone else, I would suggest taking the money you save from not buying this device and putting it toward a current generation iPad.

title: Poor Design, Good Overall
body: According to the stats, over 5,000 people have weighed in on Amazon's Kindle prior to this review. I'd like to assume that all these people own one and speak (write) from first-hand experience. Unfortunately I know this isn't the case.I may be long-winded in this review, but rest assured my own Kindle is on the nightstand by my bed as I write this...an anniversary present from my wife. There will be those who disagree with my opinions, but for those of you who haven't bought yet, know that these are first-hand experiences gathered over nearly two weeks of constant reading and playing...not wild guesses made from cursory examination of photographs.First, the bad. I start with the negative because I feel overall that the Kindle was worth the money my wife spent and I'm very happy with it. I therefore want the positive to be the last impression I leave. For those of you wanting "the bottom line", there you have it. Stop reading and go buy one. If you want to know why, skip ahead to "The Good" below.Most if not all of the reviews have something to say about the Kindle's design. Most if not all are negative, and most if not all are dead-on correct in their rants.It is impossible to hold this device without inadvertantly hitting one of the navigation buttons located along the left and right edges of the device. Here's and experiment I tried: I opened a book, and then handed the device to someone else. Without fail, each of the ten people reaching for the device hit either the "Next Page" or "Previous Page" buttons.Why is this important? It illustrates a serious flaw in the overall design: You can't pick this thing up without losing your place. The buttons are not only badly placed--they're also ridiculously easy to press.I'm going to mention something here that most reviewers don't: The Kindle is designed to be used within its leather case. Here's a secret: It doesn't help much. I have large hands and I find the only "safe" way to hold this thing is to clamp it with my left hand, thumb on the empty space just below the screen and to the left of the Kindle logo and all four fingers of the same hand behind the device. It's literally the only way to hold it without pressing a button.Also on the subject of the case: It's very easy for the device to slip out. There's a plastic tongue on the inside back cover of the case, near the spine. It's supposed to fit in a recessed slot in the back of the Kindle, holding the device within the case. On my device it fails miserably...and in one noteworthy incident my Kindle almost kissed a Linoleum floor when my boss took it from me and it slipped out of the case (he also, naturally, pressed the "Next Page" and "Back" buttons in his successful attempts to save my Kindle).I think by now I've painted an adequate picture of how I feel about the overall design; suffice it to say I think someone thought it looked great on a CAD station and neglected to actually hold the finished prototype. If the placement of the navigation buttons isn't enough, the placement of the power and wireless buttons should erase all doubt: They're located on the back of the device, in the upper left-hand corner. This means that, if you're using the case (as the documentation stresses you should), you have lift the Kindle up off the backing of the case--which for me always pops the plastic tongue out of the slot in the back--by putting your thumb on the very top-right corner, in the quarter-inch of edge space between the "Next Page" button and the top edge of the device. I've tried every other combination...only this method prevents me indadvertently hitting the "Next Page" button just before I turn the device off, or power the wireless on or off.The placement of the power cord (and for that matter the headphone and USB jacks) are also poorly placed: On the bottom edge of the device nearest the spine. This precludes my holding the Kindle in the "preferred" way I mention above, because the cables get in the way of my hand.Navigation leaves a bit to be desired, but in fairness I can't say it's a serious negative (I wouldn't even deduct a star for it). It'd be great if the Kindle were a touch-screen device, but I give Amazon a big fat pass on this, given that e-Ink technology probably doesn't allow for such things yet. Overall I was able to get around fairly intuitively.The screen refresh is also something of a nuisance, but again I attribute this more to e-Ink requirements and not to any serious limitations of the Kindle itself. In my tests, it took about two seconds from the button press to actually seeing the pages turn, or the menu to come up. Two seconds is a long time, especially if you're expecting immediate action, but in "normal use" I can't say it's any different than, say, flipping the page of a conventional book.One of the big headaches for me is that I love to read in bed. My wife doesn't. What's more is that she can't fall asleep without the lights off. See where this is going? When I read a book, I have to use one of those "light wedges" or mini-lights, which are bulky. It would have been great if somehow the Kindle geniuses could have put in a light on this device. Sure, that probably would have added battery drain. Then again, I'd venture to guess that most people read in the dark when they're home...in bed...near an outlet. They also could have made it optional with a switch to turn the light on or off.A note about purchased eBooks: So far as I can tell, all purchases are DRM-protected (encrypted) and unreadable by any means other than the Kindle. You'd think this is common sense, but I think Amazon would do well to introduce a "soft" version of the Kindle free of charge, much like Apple does with iTunes. Folks who don't want to shell out $399 for the Kindle could still purchase eBooks from Amazon and read them on their laptop. The experience would not be the same, but there are times when I'd like to read some of my purchases when I'm away from my Kindle. It's a small rant, but in my opinion a valid one.Okay...so much for the ragging. Now "The Good".I bought three books thus far. The Amazon store is unobtrusive and fast, and the downloads never took more than a minute. One of my books was "The Stand" by Stephen King. It's the new edition, with over a thousand pages. Even all those bytes took less than sixty seconds to retrieve once purchased. 'Nuff said.The display: E-Ink technology is, in a word, amazing. For those people who say that eBooks in general are a waste of money because a good ultraportable notebook has the same capabilities, I say this: Try reading a novel on any liquid crystal display for more than two hours without a run for the Ibuprofen bottle and you'll have my respect. The Kindle display isn't just "sharp"...it's nearly identical to any printed page on a standard paperback. The screen itself isn't glaring white or overly dark. In short, it's perfect and comfortable. After a few minutes reading you completely forget you're staring at an electronic device.All purchases are listed in your "home page", and bringing up each displays the last page you were on. I have a 1GB SD card I inserted easily enough by removing the back cover of the Kindle. Through the "Content Manager", I can easily move documents and books from Kindle's on-board memory to the card and back...it's intuitive and fast. Incidentally, the three books I purchased (including "The Stand") took very little memory...I could put more novels on that SD card alone than I have in my extensive bookshelves...probably more than I could read in the next twenty years, and I'm a fast reader.I already knocked the navigation a bit, but I like the scroll wheel. Moving it up or down with your thumb moves a little mirrored e-Ink cursor up and down in a thin "track" parallel to the right edge of the Kindle. You move this cursor until it sits next to a menu item on the main screen and push the scroll wheel in. That's it. For me it was intuitive and simple.For those of you wondering about "The Bottom Line" and how I could recommend this device with such strong opinions about its design (and the mediocre rating of three stars), this is it: For the most part, the Kindle does what it's supposed to do.Many folks rag on the Kindle for not offering more social networking features (like, say, tracking what your friends are reading). I don't know about you, but as much as I like my friends, I don't care what they read and I think they'd say the same about me. Social networking is not something I miss about standard books, and I don't miss it on the Kindle.Converting documents is also easy. You can move text-only documents (.txt) directly to the Kindle via the USB port, or send the document to Amazon and have them convert it to Amazon's proprietary format for a nominal charge. There is, however, a better option: The Kindle is MOBI compatible. You can get an eBook creator free from MOBIPocket.com free of charge and convert text, HTML, DOC or PDF documents to a format readable by the Kindle. Move the converted file to the Kindle and voila! Instant home-made eBook.The networking on the Kindle is a strong plus, and in fact it's this feature that sets the Kindle apart from its competitors (which are usually the Sony Reader and iRex iLiad). You don't need a home network or a WiFi "hotspot" to access the Amazon store. The Kindle's "Whispernet" uses Sprint EVDO technology. Think cell phones. As long as you've got wireless coverage odds are you've got Whispernet. There's nothing to configure. When you buy the Kindle through Amazon it's already set up with your account information right out of the box. Pretty darn cool. You can also choose to change your account settings. Some of this can be done from the Kindle, but I'd use the standard home computer on Amazon's web page.There's a stripped down browser on the Kindle. It's not worth going into; suffice it to say a minute or two playing with it convinced me of all I needed to know: I wouldn't use it. It's painfully slow and e-Ink technology isn't very graphic friendly. Since it's not needed for any critical function of the Kindle, I don't rate this a pro or con. It's just there, and I ignore it.There's another element that's worth mentioning. It's not a positive or negative (I certainly don't have a problem with it) but some folks rag on it as a definite con: There's no page numbers. In its place there's something called a "Location". Although this introduces interesting problems if you want to reference a particular page with someone who has the printed edition of your book, there's a logical reason for this: The Kindle allows for font size changes, which means if you increase the size of the font (for senior or tired eyes), the amount of text on any given page changes, which means the number of pages also changes. The "Location" is actually a fairly clever solution to this. There are two numbers that represent the location range. The first represents the first word on the page and will not change no matter how small or large the selected font. The second number represents the last word on the same page, which will change based on how much text can fit given the font size.In conclusion: This is supposed to be an electronic book. No less, no more. It's supposed to give the owner the ability to have multiple...or hundreds...of book titles, periodicals and other documents in one easy-to-access place. It's supposed to allow the owner to read his/her purchases without eye-strain, and without the constant reminder that he/she is reading words on a device light years ahead of the printed page. It's supposed to scratch that itch of "immediate gratification", allowing the purchase of the latest books from a favorite author without caring what time it is or how far the nearest book store is from your house.So the "Bottom Line" is this: If you can get around the poor design and quirky placement of buttons (and I can), the Kindle fits the bill.

title: The good -The Amazon Kindle Fire HD 7 is faster than ...
body: The good -The Amazon Kindle Fire HD 7 is faster than last year's model and starts at an affordable $140 (£120). Amazon's Sangria OS is user-friendly and new features make the tablet easily shareable for families.The bad Its plastic redesign feels cheap and it lacks the Mayday instant customer service feature. Free space on the 8GB models runs out quickly and there's no expandable memory option. The Amazon app store is less extensive than the Google Play store.The bottom line For an inexpensive tablet, the Fire HD 7 satisfies with a practical, family-friendly operating system, but those interested in a 7-inch tablet can find models with better build quality in the same price range.A great tablet will cost you at least $200 and anything priced less than that can run the gamut from good to garbage. The Amazon Fire HD 7 is on the good end of that spectrum.Starting at $139 or £119, the 7-inch tablet features a faster processor with better graphics performance than its predecessor and ships with Amazon's latest Android-based Sangria operating system. The new OS now has better battery saving functions, allows individual profiles - great for sharing amongst families -- and permits content sharing between different Fire devices, though the feature has yet to roll out. It also comes in a cheaper 6-inch model for those with smaller hands -- or tighter wallets. Pricing and availability in Europe and Australia has yet to be announced, but the US price works out to about €90 or AU$160.The change in design is the most notable difference, but it's also the most unimpressive. In comparison to last year's model (now discontinued) the Fire HD 7 sports an angular design -- reminiscent of the HDX 7 line -- and comes in five fun different colors. Unfortunately, the plastic back panel feels flimsy and, for the first time ever, the Fire HD 7 tablet feels as cheap as it costs.The Fire HD 7's saving grace is ultimately Amazon's useful feature-rich operating system and family-friendly functions. If you can get past the inexpensive feel, its lackluster design can be forgiven at its bargain basement starting price, however the free space on the 8GB models fills up quickly and a jump to the $159 or £139 16GB version is a recommended move.Amazon Fire HD tablets are cheap -- in a good wayDesignAesthetically, the Fire HD 7 doesn't break the mold; chunky bezels thickly frame the sharp 7-inch screen and the angular design looks similar to the Fire HDX line. Yet, the materials that construct the tablet give it a cheap toy-like feel.The thin plastic back panel makes the tablet feel hollow, the way removable back covers do, and its smooth texture provides little grip support -- though the angular design helps fingertips securely wrap around the edges of the tablet. Indeed it's still comfy to hold and portable, but I was disappointed in the rinky-dink plastic design -- despite the five fun colors it comes in.The buttons on the Fire HD feature a more traditional placement and design in comparison to the concave controls on the Fire HDX tablets; the rectangular buttons slightly protrude from the top edge and right corner, instead of the left and right edges. The headphone jack, microphone pinhole, and micro-USB port can also be found on the top edge, while the back of the bottom is home to the solitary mono speaker.For the first time, the Fire tablets are available in a variety of colors.Amazon offers the tablet in a Kids Edition , which ships with a protective case and a two-year replacement plan. The tablet itself is simply the Fire HD 7 with the added bonus of a FreeTime Unlimited subscription. The service, free for a year, features curated kid-friendly apps, games and videos. Packaged into an interface children can easily navigate themselves, FreeTime also works as an extensive parental control function that can set a schedule or time limits for tablet use. The extras will cost you, at $189 for the 8GB model 7-inch model in the US; availability in the UK and Australia have yet to be announced for the Kids Edition, but that price converts to around £120 or AU$215.Pass the SangriaAmazon's latest operating system, Fire OS 4 Sangria, isn't much different than last year's Mojito, but it adds a few features to make sharing the tablet with family members simpler and extending the battery life easier. The Fire HD line lacks a few of the software perks that can be found on the HDX tablets, like the instant customer service feature Mayday, but the majority of features on the high-end line can be found on the Fire HD 7, too.Amazon recently introduced its FreeTime service, which helps parents manage how and when their children use the tablet, and the addition of user profile options help parents take that control a step further. With the Sangria OS, you can create individual user profiles for adults and children, so instead of going into the settings menu to turn on the kid-friendly tablet mode, you simply create a separate profile for your child. Android tablets have long had a similar feature, but Amazon's function is geared toward making the Fire tablets more sharable among families. Soon there will be an update to allow sharing content from one Amazon account across multiple Fire devices, but it has yet to roll out.Individual profiles make sharing the tablet easy.One of the other subtle differences new to Sangria includes the ability to manage power consumption when the tablet isn't in use.
