The SmartSuspend option is a new function that helps increase stand-by battery life by turning off the Wi-Fi when the tablet is sleeping. In automatic mode, the function's timing adjusts to when you typically use the tablet, but you can also manually schedule when you'd like the Wi-Fi turned off. There are a variety of battery-saving apps that can do this, but this new built-in function is a user-friendly addition for the less tech-savvy.In addition to the benefits of a Prime membership, the Fire tablets also help take easy advantage of Amazon's Kindle Unlimited subscription service. For $10 or £8 a month (and not yet available in Australia), you can access over 700,000 reads and audiobooks. For anyone interested in a tablet with e-reader sensibilities, the service functions as a high-end library where the latest magazines are always available and they're never torn to shreds.New operating system, same carouselOne of the downsides of the Amazon Fire tablets, including the Fire HD 7, is their lack of Google Play store access. The Fire tablets instead offer Amazon's app store, which still offers a great number of apps, but not as many and as varied as Google's. Sometimes, it also takes awhile for the latest games and apps to to hit the store, however a curated app store is less overwhelming in terms of options -- a relief for the non-tech-savvy -- and it matches the simplified operating system.HardwareThe Amazon Fire HD 7 houses an ARM Cortex-A15 1.21GHz quad-core processor, 1GB of RAM and 8GB or 16GB of internal storage.Other specs on the tablet include 802.11b/g/n Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, an accelerometer and a gyroscope. Unlike the HDX series, the Fire HD 7 is not offered in a 4G LTE versionThe Fire HD 7 sports a brighter screen than the Fire HD 6.PerformanceThe Fire HD 7 performs smoothly and its limitations as a budget tablet are expected. The biggest restriction comes with the 8GB entry model. After downloading a movie, some magazines, books and various apps, the free 5GB of internal storage was quickly spent. Amazon tablets are very cloud-centric -- in place of offering an expandable storage option -- and the Fire HD 7 is no different.For casual use like checking email, reading, and browsing the Web, the tablet performed effortlessly. Even when many apps were open in the background, the tablet ran without a hitch. Navigating between one app and another was also swift, and Web pages were speedy to load.SCREEN SPECS COMPAREDTested spec Amazon Fire HD 7 LG G Pad 7.0 (LTE) Samsung Galaxy Tab 4 7.0 Toshiba Excite Go Google Nexus 7Maximum brightness 448 cd/m2 317 cd/m2 (294 cd/m2) 314 cd/m2 380 cd/m2 570 cd/m2Maximum black level 0.42 cd/m2 0.29 cd/m2 (0.22 cd/m2) 0.47 cd/m2 0.25 cd/m2 0.44 cd/m2Maximum contrast ratio 1,066:1 1,093 (1,336):1 668:1 1,520:1 1,295:1Screen resolution (pixels) 1,280x800 1,280x800 1,280x800 1,024x600 1,920x1,200Density (pixels per inch) 215ppi 215ppi 215ppi 169ppi 323ppiDisplay type IPS IPS TFT LCD IPSHD video looks sharp on the 1,280x800-pixel resolution IPS LCD screen and the Fire HD 7's display is brighter than its 6-inch counterpart , making it easier to see outdoors. Colors appear true to life, but sometimes fall flat when watching cartoons or CGI-infused movies. Text is also clear and easy to read at the smallest and largest fonts. Reading e-books was smooth sailing, however the lack of an ambient light sensor requires manually adjusting the screen's brightness when changing environments.Touchscreen response was quick and accurate. If the tablet is in the process of downloading a large file, gesture response can become delayed or staggered -- a typical downside to tablets with less than 2GB of RAM.The angular design looks similar to the Fire HDX tablets.Gaming performance for simple mobile games was consistently stable. Large games fared slightly differently; loading times were sluggish for games like N.O.V.A 3, and each level took at least 30 seconds to load, slowing down the pace of any action-packed game. This is another typical performance downside to low-end slates.The Fire HD 7 has a front-facing 0.3-megapixel camera that's fine for video conferencing, but photos are fuzzy with dull colors. The 2-megapixel rear camera isn't much better, but offers an HDR and panoramic mode, as well as manual focus. The photos at full resolution carry a significant amount of digital noise and don't look very sharp. Aside from a bluish tint, colors tend to look lifelike. The good news is that, when reduced in size as seen below, the 2-megapixel photos don't look half bad.The rear camera doesn't offer any scene modes, but can take panoramic and HDR photos.Anecdotally, battery life for the Fire HD 7 was preemptively impressive. With casual to heavy use, the tablet lasted about two days on a full charge. Check back after we're done testing the tablet in the CNET Labs for results from our battery-drain testing.ConclusionAmazon Fire tablets are typically affordable, but not until the 2014 line of the Fire HD models have they actually felt cheap. The flimsy plastic back is somewhat forgivable on the charmingly chunky low-end Fire HD 6 , however on the 7-inch model, I expected something better from Amazon.The Dell Venue 7 and LG Pad 7.0 are two small tablets that run Android, offer less restrictive user interfaces and both look more appealing than the Fire HD 7. They're both affordably priced and make solid alternatives to the Fire HD 7. Of course you can splurge on a top-of-the-line model -- like Amazon's own Fire HDX 7 or Google's Nexus 7 -- for faster performance and sleeker design, but if simple is all you need and access to your Amazon content is key, the Fire HD 7 remains an attractively affordable small tablet.Conclusively, the value of the Fire HD 7 is on the inside. The Sangria OS is both user-friendly and packed to the brim with features. Families with children will enjoy the extensive parental controls and individual user-profiles, while Amazon Prime members can revel in the abundance of streaming video available, or trade in their library card for a Kindle Unlimited subscription.

title: No Gmail or Google, But good quality
body: I am in the midst of a project to install a tablet in-wall in each of my kids bedrooms.This started with the purchase two Rotor HD 7" tablet devices. You can read that review if you wonder why I sent them back. http://www.amazon.com/gp/customer-reviews/R2IY6AHLIA3UO9/ref=cm_cr_pr_rvw_ttl?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B010TMZ0L2But when I returned them, instead of just requesting a replacement, these had just been announced and I thought that they would be perfect--higher quality, etc.However, for my needs they are not very good at all, and if I could do it all again I would definitely just get a stock android device. So, this review will include a review of the device itself, and the functionality of the OS as I subjectively view it.First, the quality of the Kindle seems very high. It is very solid, a little thick--I also have an iPad mini, which is both thinner, lighter and WAY more expensive, ;-) so I'm OK with the thickness. The screen display is very good, compared to the Rotor is clearer and easier to read, especially off-axis. The front facing camera is pretty good, if not great for the price point. In fact, the increase in camera quality from the Rotor to this Kindle Fire is the only thing keeping me from returning it and going back to the no-name, lower quality but stock Android tablet--I use the camera to talk to my kids and with the other brand tablet the picture was dark and grainy, even in perfect conditions, I couldn't actually see my kids.First problem is that this exclusively uses the Amazon store, which I understand is not exactly a secret, but you need to know that this is not the same as the regular Amazon App Store, and it is quite likely you will have apps you want but can't get. In fact, if I could scream one thing to the world about this tablet is that despite the fact that this runs a version of Android OS, it's NOT AN ANDROID TABLET. I kind of feel better.The biggest app store absences are Google apps. There is no gMail, calendar Hangouts (which is what I use to talk to my kids when they are in their rooms and I am at work, etc.) or anything by Google. Its very misleading for the advertisement to say you have this great Underground App Store with access to the most popular apps but not let you know that no Google Apps are allowed. I feel like when I accidentally rented "Atlantic Rim" or "Apocalypse Z" from Redbox. Yeah, its ultimately my fault but these guys know what they are doing with their deception.So right there this Tablet becomes a lot less useful--worse than when I had a Windows phone because at least you knew they were trying to get the best apps...it was just that they had to create them from scratch for a new OS.So before someone rebuts this...this IS NOT the same as how Apple ties their products to the Apple Store...you can get all the Google products you want on the Apple Store. You can get Microsoft products on the Apple Store. This is Amazon locking Google Apps that they don't like, plain and simple and are afraid to complete with. It's not like a Windows phone where there were programming barriers. These apps work fine on the device as you'll learn at XDA. This is Amazon making a choice that hurts their customers on purpose to restrict access to one of their competitors and so I'll deduct a star for that. It says something about a company's faith in its products when it won't allow them to compete on the merits...However, there are many other apps that you can't install either--for no reason. They exist in the Amazon Android marketplace, they work after you hack the device to get the Google Store, it's just bad choices by Amazon.Anyway, another thing I noticed and am frustrated by is the lack of widgets. I don't know if this is normal for Kindle devices but unlike other Android device this does not support widgets.So as I alluded, I visited XDA and learned how to install the Google Play store to get the apps I wanted, but still no widgets.Other complaints about the SD card have been well documented, so I won't cover that, other than to say that you can't use the SD card to store your music or movies on for a road trip or something. Doesn't bother me for what I'm using it for though.Another problem for me is that sometimes the device is way too simple, other times it's over complicated. Example: volume controls. There is, under settings, sliders to set "media" volume and "system/notifications" volume. However, neither of those controls the alarm clock volume. To get to that, you have to go into the Alarm screen, choose settings, then set the alarm volume. However, you can't get the alarm to output through the headphone jack...so if you want to use powered speakers the alarm won't work through them. Also, there is no way to control the volume from the status bar "tray"...even though you can pull down the tray and adjust other settings like brightness. It's just poor UI design plus poor functionality.The sad thing is, I'm not expecting this to be a supercomputer or a high-end device. It's not like I'm expecting this to be a Surface Pro. I'm literally expecting it to do everything my $35 tablet did--and not much else. Its pretty sad that it can't.It's advertised as having this cool family profile feature, which really got me excited. My kids are 16 and 14, but I think this is geared toward really little kids. Best as I can tell, the profiles are really just subsets of your profile, so I wasn't able to (for instance) install an App on my sons profile that wasn't in my own. It's a pretty tedious process to go through to set up, and again functionality and options are just too limited.Example: If you have profiles enabled, you can't disable the screen lock PIN no matter what. I don't like lock screens with PINS, especially for devices that never leave the house. Profiles and lock-screens definitely go together conceptually, but why no option to disable? In the end I had to go to my computer browser and actually delete the family profiles from my Amazon account to get the option to disable the lock screen PIN.To be fair, this is going to be a great table for some people who don't need access to Google apps, who are content with a limited number of Apps from the Amazon Underground App Store and don't mind some of those other things I and others brought up. I think it's probably a better choice for streaming songs and videos or for little kids to stream movies to (but you can't store these on the SD card so streaming is your best option) than many other tablets that are more expensive. Build quality seems good. Screen is good. Camera is great for the price.I also think it's possible that I and maybe others know just enough about this device to bias ourselves against it. For instance, I know it runs a version of Android, so I expect it to do everything my android phone and tablet do...and I'm disappointed when it doesn't. But maybe someone who knows nothing about operating systems doesn't even process that information. To that person the Kindle OS is as different from Apple and Android as Apple is from Windows, and maybe that person goes into this device expecting less, so turns out more satisfied? Just a theory.In conclusion, if it wasn't possible to get access to the Google Play store I would send this right back. My family shares Calendars and Contacts through Google, so it just won't work for us without it. It's less functional than a generic $40 Chinese knock off, but higher quality--that's the tradeoff for me. Many other people will disagree and love it, so I'm trying to figure the best rating for it. For me personally, its a 2. Open it up to the Google Store, widgets, etc. and it jumps to a 4 (there are still some of those UI issues holding it back). I think for the "average" consumer it's probably a 3, so that's what I gave it.

title: For those who DON'T read a lot....check my review out.
body: Because I know you're out there...just like I am.I have hard books - a whole box full, the majority from the same author. The problem I currently face is the fact that I just don't feel right carrying my books where ever I go. Also some have started the edge yellowing and I can imagine what they'll look like in 10 years. I want to make it clear - I'm not one of those who buys a new book every week. I might buy one or three books a year, because it seems these days everyone is throwing a book out there and it's hard to really find one that works without sitting in a Barnes & Noble for three hours - which I wasn't inclined to do.So you might be saying, "Then why'd you bother??" I bothered for the convenience. The ease of access to books, free samples, etc. might just compel me to read more books. Plus, if I only bought 15 full price books, the device had already paid for itself. Also, the ability to get new books directly via the device without even needing a computer was rather appealing for me. I've been using the device for over a day straight, and I've got a lot to say. Some has already been said, some is nitpick, some is praise. I'll let you sort it out.First the negatives.APPEARANCE: I agree that the Sony E-Reader is WAY more attractive than the Kindle. But then, Sony is always well known for having good looking devices; reliability is of course another matter. But Amazon really needs to streamline this badboy a bit.SCREEN: The resolution is fine, but I would like the ability to increase the contrast. Sometimes it just doesn't look right, somewhat fuzzy. I'm just a stickler for high contrast when dealing with black and white. The lack of color doesn't bother me too much except when browsing the Amazon store (book covers). What DOES bother me is the lack of touchscreen. I know it's a symptom of the screen type needed for e-ink, but if this had a touch screen, it would increase its value exponentially.ERGONOMICS: I can hold the device fairly comfortably. In fact I hate holding it inside the case for some reason. Then again I've been a Star Trek watcher for years, and watching them hold the PADD, it's the same way with the Kindle. Thumb in the dead center below the screen - right on top of the logo, fingers spread out on the back rubber. The corner does not poke into my hand to where it's painful.FUNCTIONS: These are going to be nitpicks for some people, but I have to break it down. First, why is there no clock on the bottom bar? There's tons of real estate on the bar that could easily accomodate a clock, alas one is not to be seen (at least not from what I can see). If it's buried in another area, to me that is insufficient; it should be showing at all times so I can keep track while I'm reading. Second, and others have mentioned this, the lack of foldering is just appalling. Finally, you can't rename books to something more your liking - since some book titles are longer than the visible field on your book list, it's hard to distinguish books in a series if the series is named all the same with a small different subname. For example, Terry Brooks' Voyage series all starts with "Voyage of the Jerle Shannara" and then a word. In my list I can't tell which is which - very annoying.Also, you can sort by certain things, but you can't group. At least grouping or stacking options would have sufficed in lieu of foldering. Sample docs are bulkied in with books (which shouldn't happen) and I can't really organize what I've got.GENERAL DEVICE: The device's price, while it can pay for itself after 15 full price hardcovers, is still too high in my opinion. I would rather pay $200 for the device than its current price point, just because it is limited in some ways. Personal opinion. Also, if a book just came out, you're getting a deal. If it's an older one where there is a paperbook or used available for $5-6, you might not realize a savings on that book. Just something to keep in mind, as I don't feel that the Kindle prices adjust properly with the book's true price. It's really designed for early adopters/purchasers. On a smaller note, there is a minor delay for things to happen on screen after you press buttons. The processor must be the bare minimum allowable for the form factor. Great for battery life, terrible for processing and screen refresh speed.Nitpicks yes...but they add up quickly.Now for the good stuff:- The Amazon Store is quick and easy to search and find things. It's also convenient to be able to search for a book that someone just told you about, find it and download a sample, and if you enjoy it, buy the full book, all within a few minutes. Also, the amount of books available is amazing; even obscure books like "Investing in India for Dummies" and unexpected ones like "If I Did It" have Kindle editions. Some books that were expected were not to be found - like Brooks' Landover series- The screen does have a very good resolution to it. Text is very easy to read and not stressful on the eyes.- Playing music while reading; Some might not like this, but I do. There's nothing like listening to the likes of David Benoit, Joe Sample, Brian Culbertson, Rick Braun, Boney James, Kirk Whalum, etc...while reading a good book!- The built in storage is quite sufficient for all but the most frequent reader. I've got a number of books on my Kindle and I've barely cracked my space allocation.- The cool factor. I showed it to a guy at work who was curious about the Kindle, and he was like me: price turned him off, but after seeing it in action he's seriously considering getting one.- Battery life was quite good, even when playing music constantly. The key is to turn the wireless off if you're not browsing the store.The biggest turn offs are:- The form factor - too bulky compared to its closest competitors;- The price for the device, higher than competitors;- The lack of touchscreen capability;- Interface/processor speed, not good at multitasking;- Lack of foldering/grouping/stacking options to make it easier to organize your library;- Not every book is Kindle'd. You would think that an author would want every book they published to be Kindle'd if they allowed at least one to be, but sadly this is not the case.Some of those are personal nitpicks, as I said, but there are so many that I just have to knock two points off.In summary: Do I recommend it for the reader who doesn't read a lot of books? Maybe...but not at its current price point. Right now it's for early adopters for sure. Anyone who is a book veteran and spends their hours at Barnes and Noble should just keep on doing it. However this *could* open up doors of potential if they do it right. Schoolbooks, more widespread Kindling of books, faster processor performance, and a better web experience would help to justify the price tag.

title: Poor Customer Service, excellent product
body: Please read to the end, I have edited this now that I have finally recieved the Kindle.Ok, I have not recieved the product yet, and I assume I will love it, but who knows since it's nowhere to be seen. What I am upset about is that when I ordered it, it was supposedly in stock and would ship within 24 hours, in fact there was an option to overnight it for the next day. Somehow though, several days later, it is still not shipped. I called, they basically read off the screen that I saw on the internet.For me the problem has to do with poor customer communication, How can Amazon not know when a device that they own exclusively will ship? It's not like they have to deal with a supplier or other potential issues, they supposedly stock it in their warehouses. So, this is either not true, or they are trying to create the impression they have them when they don't.It's hard for me to know. Simple and honest communications would easily change my position. I can only hope the customer service after the item arrives (if it ever does) is better.Other small annoyances include the fact that is very difficult to get a telephone number when you are sick of the misinformation on the website. Once I did, I found that the Kindle customer support department is it's own division within Amazon, and apparently they can't transfer you. It seemed to me by the frustration I found on the other end of the phone that they are sick of getting Kindle calls.When I placed the order, since it was supposed just moments away from shipping, the status changed such that I can no longer modify (I.E. Cancel) the order. So, I have only one option if I get to that point, I have to return it. Who knows how long it will take to get a refund if I decide to do that.The last small annoyance is that there are apparently multiple phone numbers for support, and even within Amazon, they don't seem to know what they are. I was put on hold for at least five minutes while they looked up the Kindle customer support number. How can they not have that number? And why is that number not posted on their website?Anyway, I am excited about the Kindle, but every day I am loosing interest and beginning to second guess the purchase.If I ever get it and I decide to keep it, I will post another review of the actual unit.Here is my feedback now that I have the device.What I like:The Kindle is easy to use. It is intuitive, easy to read and generally performs well, though there are some problems that I will get to later.The fonts are adjustable, though I am not sure that for people with vision difficulties it gets big enough. For me, I can read just about everything on the lowest settings. The argument one could and should make is that the fact that you can adjust it is pretty nice. Try doing that with an off the shelf book!I really like the interface. It just works well most of the time. It is easy to search, easy to find your books and to bookmark pages or add notes. Getting used to the wheel took a little bit of time, but very shortly I was using it like old hat.I really enjoy the fact that it can play music. I often have to boot up my laptop or use an MP3 player when reading to be able to 'escape' into the content of what I am reading. With the Kindle, you can do this with one device. It makes is nice for travel.I love the fact that the Kindle can browse the internet, though it's not the best interface that I have every used. (See more about that later).Battery life is suprisingly long. I have only needed to charge it a few times, and I use it every day since I got it two weeks ago.The interaction with Amazons content delivery is amazing. You can easily find books and download them within minutes and start reading.You can convert your existing content to Kindle format for free or for a fairly low fee with very little effort. Finding the information about how to do it for free was a little tricky, though it was easy to figure out how to do it for a fee.On the whole this is a very well designed reader and one which I will likely be loyal to.What I don't likeI don't like the fact that your documents have to be in .DOC format to convert to Kindle format. It seems to be that the most common format is PDF, why can't I use that format to convert directly to Kindle format?I read many secure PDF's, which I can easily read on my PC, but not on my Kindle. I don't think this will every be possible due to the technology constraints of the security mechanisms and limitations of the platforms.Kindle doesn't render graphics very well. Since I am a technical reader, I nearly always read technical documents. These documents often make reference to diagrams, graphs, tables etc. They are very difficult to read on the Kindle, and I am not sure that for some books they are acceptable.If you buy a traditional book, you may get additional media (CD, DVD etc). With the Kindle versions, they do not exist. The books that you are reading still reference them though, so you may be left wondering how you can follow along the text when you don't have the media that came with the book.I am not sure that the price of the books and the Kindle are fairly adjusted for the sacrifices you make with loosing the paper/hard cover traditional books. In some cases it probably is fair, in others it seems that there is little discount, but a loss of functionality.The Basic Web interface is slow and barely useable. That said, the fact that its available is pretty amazing to me. I do really enjoy it none-the-less. I have had very intermittent coverage while travelling around the United States. Reminds me of why I don't have a Sprint phone for my personal cell phone.I don't like the battery connection. It looks very tenuous to me. I like to carry a spare battery for my devices (laptops, pda's etc) so that I can keep two fully charged. This is handy when you are not able to charge the connected battery for a while. I do not have a spare for this yet, but I did have to remove the battery when the interface locked up on me over the weekend. I was able to remove it, but I felt that doing that too many times would result in a broken wire. It seems like a very poor interface.Quick SummaryI have not used any other e-readers, so I am not the best reference for comparison. I have used many PDA's and laptops for this purpose though. As an e-reader, the kindle is head and shoulders above the devices that I have used. Is it worth it? I am not 100% sure that it is. There is a loss of features in some areas, while adding features that traditional books will never have. As a business travellor, this will likely become an indispensible companion, though I do not believe that it will be able to replace all my traditional books.

title: Memory issues kill this device even with external that only takes some of the app and the rest goes to the 4.5 g internal
body: I wanted to use this for a few days before giving it a review, right out of the box most things are great, it's when you use them for a while you find out the extras. This is one of those items. The expandable memory for starts sounds like a hell yeah deal, but soon you find out that it doe's not mean you nave the extra memory you paid for which is half the price of the tablet for a 64gig card or over the price of the tablet for 128gig card, I put 1,200 hundred songs on the sd card from my mp3 player and it would only take 700 or so songs for some reason even after a factory reset, don't know why since their all just mp3 format it took some but didn't take the others and didn't give me a message or warning saying they wouldn't work or were incompatable, I found out by checking my sd card memory under music and felt ripped for not getting the last 400 songs I wanted. Then there flixter the only way you view your movie library if you have one like me, which I've got a 160 movies or more on, it doesn't let you dowload these movies like most other devices do, it says not yet compatable, which means no movies for on the go without wifi. Then when you do stream the voice is constantly out of sync, it freezes a lot and is overall not as good an experience as with my Lenova 10 inch tablet. But back to the memory card, then of course now I've got tons of memory left on my 64 gig card, since it didn't take all my music, and wouldn't let me download movies for a better playback experience on the external card, so games like clash of clans, and tons of others that want all access to your life and accounts and camera and who knows what else, these games loaded to the sd card load fine but put up to a third of the game on you internal memory, which means thirty to sixty games and your internal memory is gone since when you get it it's already down to about 4.5 gigs from factory, so there is almost no internal memory to work with to start with, so you are forced to buy an sd card and then you have these issues I mentioned. I've got 500 mb left on my internal memory after installing everything I mentioned, so I guess a 32gig external card would have been enough and cheaper. So now what do I do with the 50 gigs I have left on my external memory card, I can't dl movies, it's not accepting new songs which is wierd as hell, I've got a thousand photos on it and just don't have anymore to give it, movies I don't know how to convert on my comp and then do a direct dl and that sounds like too much work anyway, why it doesn't have vudu and why flixter movies can't dl is bogus. You can dl to your internal memory but I've yet to figure out how to even with the instructions on screen right there for me, and then your four internal gigs would be halfed with just two movies since their about a gig each and that means only twenty to thirty games would fit (that required access games). Ok enough about memory, the touch sreen is very responsive, the whole thing feels cheap compared to the nexus 7 I owned and my Lenova tablet, but that's ok to me who cares it can take a drop or more I hear and is sturdy. I've noticed lately that since I did a factory restore it freezes up on menu screens then I have to wait till the screen goes blank and then turn it on, swipe up to get rid of the add and then it works fine for a couple of swipes or menu choices and doe's it again, but only in menu, not on apps or anywhere else. Kind of annoying. The camera 's I didn't use the pictures looked so bad I didn't even snap any to test, but hold on it's a nice winter day and I'm at my glass doors lets test it with a pic or two, but remember they say they always look better on the tablets, camera, or whatever than when their printed it the truest test. I enclosed the test pictures their pretty good in a high sunlight situation, which digital always requires good or better lighting, so camera's good. Oh and I looked I only got about thirty five games before I ran out of internal memory even with the 64gig external cards taking most of the game, so that's not much and still one of my big complaints. Then there's the bluetooth connectivity situation, I've got a cheeap Easy ACC bluetooth speaker, works fine with my lenove, Iphone and have never had any issues at all. With this it's constantly and I mean constantly connecting and disconnecting, the beeps of the speaker going on and off and the sound going on and off from the speaker back to the tablet make impossible to enjoy a movie on flixter, resetting by removing the easy acc from my tablets system fixed the problem and then adding it back with another search for bluetooth devices, but it's not permanant, and for music it works for a while then glitches up now and then. So very weak bluetooth signal or something that my other devices don't do. Oh and here's a wierd one it didn't load all my music so I was in external memory and cleared all the the music it had and tried to reistall thinking it would take it all, but it wouldn't take any music after I used the clear button, I had to do restore to factory settings then and then it took it again, but as I said not all of the music. I did find a finte black case for it for seven bucks or so with shipping, but otherwise the cases are fourteen to twenty five dollars for a fifty dollar tablet, ridiculous. So I got eighty five dollars or so invested and only thirty games, a thousand pics, seven hundred songs, and no movies. Tons of external memory left over and no internal memory, so it works out like both my other tablets did, having to constantly erase and add games instead of having a massive library which is what I wanted, and expected to be able to do tons of movies, music, pics. Oh and there's no you tube downloader app so you can forget about getting music and vids and movies off there to add to this, unlike my lenove a few clicks and I'm downloading anything off you tube I want. Overall I really like and would give it four stars for a fun little device, that's fairly ez to use, downloads quick, acceps and converts your music for you and quickly. Is very responsive, and for the most part is just fine for most anyones needs, the memory issue is the big killer for me though, they make these things with such small memories and charge through the roof for memory internal on these devices, and they don't make replaceable batteries for these or cellphones anymore forcing us to buy new stuff, when we all know rechargables only are good for couple of years at most then serious reduction in battery life occurs. For the kids this is a fine tablet and even adults but only buy a sixteen or thirty two gig memory card is my recommendation. Thanks

title: Kindle-Not Bad, Not Perfect Either
body: Well my new Kindle arrived yesterday and here are my impressions:1. The packaging was impressive- very Apple-ish. Elaborate (expensive) off-white box with elegant design. For example, the electrical plug on the charger had a form fitted plastic covering over the electrical prongs. Nice, but way over the top. And as I would soon find out, it represents an inconsistent degree of attention to detail in the overall customer experience. If everything about Kindle was done as well as the packaging and the electrical prong covers, this would be a perfect product. Alas, it is not.2. My Kindle wouldn't turn on at first. Eventually it came around, but I had a bead of sweat on my forehead before it finally coughed to life. I may have simply been impatient. This seems unimaginable. I know how long electronic devices take to turn on.3. All of the parts were present and accounted for. I found the owners manual to be skimpy, shallow and not exactly full of the answers I was already looking for (I'll give you an example soon). Like Apple products, this is begging for a "Kindle for Dummies" book. Hmmm.....maybe I could order one for my Kindle?? I want my products to be completely intuitive to operate and not require a manual. This one needs a manual.4. After charging it, the first order of business was to order a book over the built-in wireless system. It operates on the Sprint/Nextel EVDO system and I was concerned that I'd have no wireless service at my farm. My fears were well founded-I have zero signal here. Of the three major wireless networks that operate in the US, this one is a distant third in terms of coverage, so I was dumbfounded to learn that Amazon chose it. Why, I wondered? Consulting the book, I found nothing addressing what to do if you have no wireless service. Crap.5. Well, I reasoned, there MUST be a way to download a book over the internet and I went looking for an answer. Nothing in the owner's manual and nothing apparent on the web site. I finally found the tech support phone number (like many companies, they make you work to find this on the web site). My call was answered by a rep in India (oh no), but she quickly told me that Kindle device support was provided via a different number (thank God). I called that number and was quickly picked up by a North American rep (ok, I thought....). Her pleasant sounding voice was unable to conceal an attitude that was off-putting, to say the least. Her tone said "I am in a hurry, I don't want to help you, I will resist explaining anything to you, I will use every buzzword I can think of and if you're not careful, I will hang up on you." Not a world class customer support experience. Not by a mile. However, I did get a book to download and it loaded so fast, I was suspicious that I'd only received a part of it. When I asked the rep if there was a way to tell if I'd gotten the whole file. She answered, "No." I asked her if she could tell by the size of the file. She said, "Sir, all books are different size." I said, "Well of course they are, but on average, how big would a book file be?" Her answer was, "Sir, there is no average size book". So I asked, "How do I know if the whole file was downloaded?" She replied, "Read the book. If it's not all there, download it again." Wow- somebody is in the wrong line of work. (To the Millers: Reminds us of a certain waitress, no?)6. After I got comfortable buying a book on-line, I started actually reading the book. The font size is 6 way adjustable and that's nice. The ergonomic issue with accidental page turning is a real problem- can't tell you how many times I did it. SO you become fixated on how you are holding the device and that is pretty distracting. You find yourself thinking more about how you are holding the device than what you are reading. Putting the Kindle in it's leather cover (pretty ill-fitting actually) does help as it gives you added points with which you can make contact without turning the page. Eventually I learned to where to hold it and was able to melt into the story and forget the device. Sorta.7. I like the way Amazon lets you keep a copy of the book in your "account in the cloud". That way, if something happens to your device and you lose your books, you can download them for free from your account. Wish iTunes would do that.8. Page numbers don't exist (huh?????). The Kindle forum (one of the worst forums I've ever seen) was some help explaining this. Seems that with 6 different font sizes, numbering pages would be a problem. So, each time you page forward, the book shows a new "location" followed by numbers like 223-29 and then the next page would say "location 229-35". I could not understand this. But it turns out that they are actually saying location 223-229, followed by location 229-235. Saving "ink" by not printing the extra digit and confusing the hell out of a customer. Can't explain how location differs from page. The forum was full of people asking about this, so I know I'm not the only one who was confused. Bad, bad design- well at least confusing design.9. Once I got accustomed to the location issue and page turning, I started warming up to it. I was able to make the device "disappear" and focus on the story, which, after all, is the whole point of any book.10. When you page turn the whole screen goes black before delivering you next page (or should I say "location"). It is very distracting at first. Eventually, it becomes only slightly distracting. Fix it Amazon.Overall, I would score Kindle a C+. Nicely designed device, with exception of page turner, with huge potential. At $359.00, they have it priced right on the edge of what I would consider spending for it. Very good book selection (and you can play MP3's too) and at $9.99 per book, they seem attractively priced. First day customer experience was a C-, phone support was an F. I'm going to keep it as I think it's worth the money, if I only use it when I fly to board meetings.I no longer think this will replace all books and it surely does not replace having a beautiful book in your hands. But how many times have I bought a trashy novel or a magazine to read on an airplane and them wonder what to do with it when I'm done. Kindle solves that problem. No, it won't replace ALL books, at least not in it's current configuration. I'll still buy nice books for my library, but will not buy another paper version of a John Grisham novel. I can't see it replacing a text book either.

title: Still using it after a year...
body: It's been over a year since I first posted this review, and I'm still using the device (and indeed bought a second one for my wife during one of the discounted sales), so I guess that says something good about it. Also I'm pleased to report that none of the OS updates in that time have broken the side-loaded Google Play Store which was something I had been afraid of happening.Pro #1: the machine performs well, it's fast and streams video really well. Update: after installing a *few* apps (and I mean a few - I have not filled this full of junk like I did with my old Xoom - I knew how much it had slown down so I tried to avoid that problem on the fire), touch response and scrolling has become jerky. I've seen several complaints posted about the screen resolution but I have to say those people must have damned good eyesight. It looks really sharp to me and at around 150dpi seems quite sufficient. At arms length it is better angular resolution per pixel than my TV which is about 15 ft away.Pro #2: it came preconfigured with both my account and my wife's account on it - I was pleasantly surprised that I could view Amazon Prime videos, since my wife's account is our primary one that paid for Prime, and usually she has to log in for us to view videos on the PC. (Update: this may not be solely a Fire feature - we appear to be treated differently on the web page now too. I'm wondering if my acct has been given a free month of prime separate from my wife's as a result of buying the Fire? I'll look into that possibility before the end of the month just in case...)Pro #3: the price is excellent, and the free apps appear to include some that are not junk. Unlike the earlier release of Underground, having it built in to the machine on delivery means that it is not asking for a million permissions the way it did on the previous fire devices. (Update: after a few days, I'll downgrade that to 'meh'. I just have one free app that I've kept (a cam scanner app ostensibly sold at $5) and now I have Google Play Store running I rediscovered that I'd got the same app free from Google a couple of years back.Update: Pro #4: turns out it's not that difficult to add the Google Play Store following the advice at XDA Developers. I was worried about later versions and messing my machine up since their guide was for previous Fire versions, but it worked perfectly.Update: Pro #5: the machine comes with a nice process manager. I have had no crashes caused by killing running processes or uninstalling apps, which it does easily. There aren't too many extraneous apps running by default, and just about everything can be safely killed to free up RAM and get the response rate back to 'zippy' rather than 'jerky'.Con #1: the lock-in to the Amazon App Store is extremely annoying. Every single specific app I've wanted to install so far is missing from the store: Trax (a GPS tracker I own); Google Translate (the real app with camera and voice support, not the cut down web page); Ooma (IP phone client for my landline phone system). This is going to force me to install a workaround to use Google Play, which if it is even possible on this device is a lot of hassle, or sideload them from my current android where they are already installed (which is a dated Motorola Xoom that the Fire was meant to replace). Update: it was possible, it just took a couple of days to locate the info.Con #2: The only web browser in the store is Silk which sends all your data to Amazon to render pages (like Opera does). I'm not happy with any company knowing everything I browse. The feature can be turned off or you can sideload Firefox quite easily, but I just object on principle to the presumption.Con #3: Out of the box on the 1st of October, I had to download an OS update dated 30 Sep. It took about an hour combining the download and installation time. I was very lucky I was on a free wifi connection because usually I would have been on my Freedompop access point which is limited to 400 - 500Mb per month.Con #4: This is minor but really set the tone for the whole experience - on opening the box, the screen was covered in nasty looking streaks. OK, I was able to clean it with spit & polish and a lens cleaning cloth, but it bothered me and made me wonder where else Amazon is cutting corners - their quality assurance and packaging used to be top notch - this packaging looks like it belongs on the cheap equipment shelf at Big Lots.Update Con #5: Can't store music on the SD. Haven't seen such a useless SD since the Xoom.Update Con #6: The war with Google extends beyond the Play Store. You can't view Amazon's video on the Chromecast (and there's only limited Chromecast support via a few apps from the Google Play Store) - you have to buy Amazon's Fire TV stick. No thanks, I have three other brands of sticks already, there's no more HDMI sockets on my TV for another one.Update Con #7: No Flash support. I guess this is an Android problem rather than just an Amazon one, but they could do something about it if they wanted to.((((Update Con #8: Doesn't play well with Echo/Alexa. This is unforgivable. Turn on Bluetooth pairing with the Echo and (even if you're not streaming sound to the Fire) the Echo starts stuttering on the sound output (eg playing the news or weather). This is unforgivable, as is the requirement to pair using bluetooth when they could just as easily communicate over tcp/ip.))))NEW: The fire now has full Alexa support, It was added around the start of 2017.Update Con #9: Both front and back cameras are absolutely appalling. Low light sensitivity is terrible.PS I almost forgot: Con #10: While most manufacturers strive for expensive oleophobic glass, Amazon have somehow managed to corner the market in the much rarer and highly effective oleophilic glass.I'd drop my rating to 2.5 if I could; it has issues, but I won't go as far as "I don't like it". Although it took me a couple of days to work out how to safely put Google Play Store on the device without rooting it, having done so I'd say "It's Okay" and acceptable value for money - if you're comparing it to the cheap Chinese androids from places like Best Buy or CVS.However I would drop my rating to 1 in protest of Amazon's ridiculous war with Google which is hurting both companies' customers (who are often the same people) if I thought that it would have any effect on Amazon.

title: A solid 3 stars...or maybe 2 1/2. Great price, but weaknesses in display, storage and the Amazon App Store.
body: I have been using the Kindle Fire 7 inch for about a month now. Before that, I was regularly using a Kindle Fire HD 7 inch (4th Generation). The new Kindle Fire 7" is not an upgrade form the Fire HD 7 (4th Generation). It is somewhere between horizontal transition to a downgrade.It is very difficult to argue that the Kindle Fire 7" is a not a great price. Based on my usage of this Kindle Fire, I would say that it is priced just about right. If it had been priced more, it would be too much. Much of this review will sound very negative, but the fact is, a sub-$50 tablet will put a somewhat capable tablet in the hands of many who might not otherwise afford one. This point should not be taken too lightly. Many of the criticisms of this tablet seem to be due to compromises required to get down to this very low price-point. Readers should also keep in mind that there are other alternative tablets that go on sale periodically for a little more money, that are significantly more capable devices.My basis for this review is a long history with tablets, but I will primarily contrast this Fire to other Android tablets. The price difference between this device and any iPad historically places it outside the 'Apples to Apples' comparison. When Google started talking about producing an Android tablet, Samsung rushed one to market in the Samsung Galaxy Tab in 2010. I bought several for my company and used one extensively. I have also extensively used a Samsung Galaxy Tab 10.1, Galaxy Note Pro 12.1, several Windows tablets (from Windows XP through Windows 10), several iPads of various sizes shapes and models, as well as nearly every smartphone you can imagine.The shape of the new Kindle Fire 7" is a big improvement over the Fire HD 7 (4th Gen). The device is slightly smaller and the edges seem to roll the correct direction, towards the back. I always felt like the Fire HD 7 was shaped 'wrong', with the front being more narrow than the back of the device. The weight of the Kindle Fire 7" feels substantial, but not heavy. It feels more substantial and 'solid' than you would expect for a sub-$50 tablet. It also feels slightly heavier than a Lenovo and Asus 8 inch tablet that I compared it to. This was a totally unscientific 'feel' test, not based on actual weights of the devices. I was more concerned by usability and 'feel' than weight specs.The Fire 7" comes with 8GB of storage. Of this 8GB, close to 2 GB is taken by the Operating System and other necessary things. You will likely find that the remaining 6 GB is not going to be enough storage once you start installing apps and books. Thankfully, Amazon has included the capability of adding a Micro SD card. The Micro SD card will give you storage for Personal media, as well as space to download videos from Amazon Prime Video for offline viewing.The display is not an improvement. The new Fire 7" display seems to not be bright enough most of the time. There just doesn't seem to be enough contrast or brightness. The resolution is also reduced from the 4th gen. The most annoying thing about the display, to me, is the glare. In all of the devices I have used, this seems to have the worst reflectivity of any tablet I have ever used. It seems to pick up any light source in the room and reflect it back into your eyes. I don't know how they managed it, but they were successful in making this device work like a mirror.I have no criticism of the size of the display. It is a 7 inch tablet. If you want something bigger than 7 inches, this is not the device for you. This being a 7 inch display, makes the device small enough to slip into the back pocket of my blue jeans. You mileage (and pocket size) may vary.The new operating system on the new fire is an improvement over the 4th Gen. The new interface seems to be much more intuitive and more like other mobile operating systems (Android and iOS). I still switch back and forth between the new Fire 7" and my older Fire HD 7", and the old Fire OS just feels more clunky. I wish they would hurry and upgrade the older Fire devices to the new OS.With all of the good things and bad things about the Kindle Fire 7", the one that has the biggest impact is the Kindle App Store and the lack of Google Play Services. Switching between other Android devices and my Kindle Fire, I notice very quickly the impact this has on the usability of the device. There are many apps that I use that are not available in the Amazon app store. These apps are not available on the Kindle Fire because Amazon wants to lock you into their ecosystem. Even a bigger problem than apps just not being available, is the fact that Apps in the Amazon app store are almost never as up to date as the same app in the Google Play Store. It is not uncommon to have an app updated to fix bugs in the Google Play Store and spend months waiting for the same bug fixes in the Amazon App Store.<We can always hope that Amazon will sell 2 million of these and encourage App developers to more fully support the Amazon App store, or alternatively, open up the Google Play services to Amazon Fire devices.>Without Google Play services, these are some of the things that are not available:Android App StoreGoogle HangoutsYouTube applicationNative Gmail applicationGoogle MapsGoogle +Google DriveAny other apps that are in the Google Play store, but not in the Amazon App Store.Here is a quick summary of some of the Pros and Cons of the new Kindle Fire 7"Pro:Price!!!Smaller and shaped better to fit in the handMicro SD Card slot (Finally!!!)Con:Display: Low resolution, horribly reflective, not bright enough.8 GB storageAmazon eco-system (No Google Play services, no YouTube, no Google Hangouts, No Google Maps, etc.)In summary, I gave the Amazon Fire 7" a 3 star rating. I honestly went back and forth between 2 stars and 3 stars and settled on 3 because there was no 2 1/2 available. The tablet is $50, when you compare that to some other things that are $50, this tablet makes sense. If you are not looking for a flagship, powerhouse tablet, and a low end tablet that is dependable fits your needs, this will probably be the best $50 you could spend.

title: Not what I expected.
body: I have a Kindle Paperwhite. I love it. But I thought it would be nice to have color on the screen and a camera and all the stuff I'll use, too, right in my hand, and the price was right. The screen for reading books, which is my major use of this, is narrower than the paperwhite's. It's also a very heavy object in my hand as I read. The screen is clear and easy to read. Until I finally found what turns it off, everything I did was cluttered with recommendations and what other people have bought, all based on the tracking software installed and what I already had. I turned all that off (Maybe. I'm not sure I got it all!) and improved it greatly. I paid to have no ads on it, but I got ads, anyway, disguised as recommendations. That rather ticked me off. I bought the second one for my grandchild, and I planned to read books using mine to record me doing so, so she can listen to her grandmother read books I planned to buy for her and install on her Fire. Well, I can't put my books onto her Fire, and until it's set up for her, which I cannot do, I cannot put any books onto it. She needs her own account for all of that, and I don't know what her parents will use as her account. This bothers me. My expectation was to buy her books, put them onto her Fire, and put videos of me reading them on there for her, too. That can't happen. Disappointing. I hope I can figure out how I can buy her books and put them onto her fire so she can have them.I am also disturbed because I cannot remove links to apps, a newspaper you think I should want and I don't want, links to email, links to freetime, goodreads, newsstand, contacts, games. I do not use social media, and I have a computer for any online news I may want to read. I stuck them all into one folder just to get them off the screen, but I really would like them to go away. Nothing is intuitive about this device. Until I can figure out how to stop it from putting everything I do onto "the cloud", I can't do anything with it. I can't take photos or make videos until I know they will be mine alone, not out somewhere on some server that any idiot can hack into. The help is non-existent without having wi-fi connected, which tells me it's being transmitted somewhere for someone else to peruse what I look up. I did get the free kindle fire owner's manual, but it's not for this model. What's in that does not apply to this model. Everything has its own "settings" display, and I have to set everything individually. Maybe I'll get them all one day. I did contact Amazon using the chat forum, but because it's so new, they had a hard time figuring out what I was saying and why I didn't have the same buttons they have and couldn't click those links. That will improve over time, I'm sure, but right now, it wasn't as helpful as it could have been.When I turn it on, it boots slowly. I expect it to turn on immediately, but that only happens when it's "asleep", not turned off. Half the time, when I try to turn it off, it asks me if I want that and when I touch "OK", it does not turn off. It takes several tries to shut it down. I cannot figure out why it boots up saying "amazon", then "fire", and takes its good old time doing it all. I know it's from amazon, I know it's a fire.Overall, I'm almost sorry I bought it for myself, and I seriously doubt it will do well for my grandchild to learn to read and enjoy books as I do. She will most likely have games on it, but I'm sure they'll cost money that isn't to be spared for that. I got it as a teaching tool for a child to learn to read and enjoy books. I do hope it works for that, but I have serous doubts that it will. It's just too hard to use that way. It is my hope that because she is a child, she will find it intuitive. Children learn more easily than old folks do, after all.Because it's not intuitive and because I cannot get the owner's manual to tell me what to do with it, I have not yet figured out how to borrow movies from the library and watch them on it. I hope to get that figured out soon.I got the case from Amazon, not the cheaper ones, and it does work nicely. It stands it right up in front of me for reading hands-free when I am at a table that is the correct height. I do like that! It closes firmly. It looks nice, it feels nice in my hand. I ordered screen covers for my grandchild, but they are not here yet. Demand was too high, I suppose, and the supply did not meet it. I'll wait. This is a Christmas present, and the promise is for later in October, so I will be patient and wait for them.I'm sure that if I can ever find out how to shut down all the tracking and "cloud" issues, I will love it. The screen is much more clear than the one on the paperwhite and reading is easier. The battery does not really last as long as it is advertised to last. It went from 100% to 42% as I read one book over a period of 5 hours. Maybe it holds the charge while it waits for you, but not so much as you use it. If I sit and read all day in winter, I will have to plug it into an outlet to do so. I'm sure movies will make it drain even faster, which is the secondary purpose I have for it.Although this is not an HD screen, the photos already installed are crisp and clear. It is a good screen. There are good points to this device. The only really bad points I have are related to the fact that there is so much tracking software attached to it and so many things on it that I do not want and cannot be rid of. Would I recommend it to someone else? I would, with reservations and explanations about how much data it transmits to unknown places and people for unknown purposes. I keep it in airplane mode and transfer my books to it by USB. I will continue that until I know I have all the tracking software turned off. I will continue trying to figure out how it works and how to watch movies on it. I am certain that once I have it figured out without any written materials available to tell me how to do things I want to do, I will love it. Until then, to me, it's just "OK".

title: 24 Hours of Kindle
body: REVIEW: 24 Hours of KindleI wanted to share my views on the first 24 hours of Kindle ownership.Screen / Readability: I have to say I like the E-Ink screen. It is very easy to read and it is much easier on the eyes than a traditional screen. The ability to adjust the font size is easy to use. The one improvement I would like to see is the ability to adjust the contrast. I'm not exactly sure if this even possible with the E-Ink format, but it would be nice. The rendering of graphics is good, though limited to black and white. I downloaded the sample chapter of a mathematics text and the rendering of the symbols and tables came out well. Of course, if a color E-ink screen can be produced at an affordable cost, that would be the ultimate improvement.There is a brief screen-flash when moving from one page to the next that some might find annoying, but I found that I got used to it very quickly.Power: The on-off switch is on the back of the unit. I would like to see that placed in a more convenient location on the front. I'm sure some space could be freed up by reducing the size of the "Next Page" and "Prev Page" buttons (see Navigation below). Also, unless you are actively downloading content, I would turn off the wireless switch as keeping the wireless ability on drains the battery much faster.I would also like to see a time-out setting that will turn the unit off when no button has been pushed for a while. When I left the unit alone for a while, the unit showed a "hold" screen and requested I push the Alt button and text size button to unlock. This seems an unnecessary waste of power. Instead of a physical slide power switch, the power switch should be electronic, with a default power-off after 5 minutes of dormancy.Navigation: Navigation is accomplished through large Next Page buttons on the right (with a smaller version on the left) and a Prev Page button on the left. There is also a small Back button on the right for web navigation. Navigation can also be accomplished via the menu system. As mentioned by many reviewers, the buttons are easy to use - too easy (see Ergonomics below). I can think of several improvements. For instance, a touch-screen where you drag the page across to turn (in addition to Next/Prev Page buttons) would be a nice addition. Again, this may be a limitation of the E-Ink technology. I do have to say that occasionally, the unit pauses for a couple of seconds when a page button is hit. This delay is on top of the flash mentioned above.While menu-bar is fairly intuitive, there has to be a better way. The menus are somewhat slow to respond and don't add much to the experience.Ergonomics: This is perhaps the biggest flaw in the unit. The navigation buttons are so large that you tend to hit them accidentally quite often. There are only a couple ways to hold the unit comfortably. Also, the unit's angular design does not help. The navigation buttons to the right and left of the screen wrap around the sides. If you try to grip the unit by the sides, you WILL turn the page. Also, I'm not sure the white base color of the unit is the easiest on the eyes.I also have to comment on the included leather case. The case is nice and the elastic works well when the case is closed. However, when you try to read the book with case open, there needs to be something to hold the unit in the case on the right side. You are better off taking the unit out of the case to use it.I have to subtract one star for the Ergonomics.Content: I am mixed on the content. Amazon touts that they have 115k books available, but many of those are in the public domain. Since the Kindle can read .mobi and .prc files, you can get them for free from sites like manybooks.net, feedbooks.com and Project Gutenberg. Unless you think the formatting is better on the Amazon versions, I would not recommend spending the money on the public domain content on Amazon.Yet Amazon does have many modern books still protected by copyright available for purchase. As they say, 100 of the top 112 New York Times bestsellers are available. However, many books that I love are not available. As a fan of epic fantasy and science fiction, I want to download works by J.R.R Tolkien, J.K Rowling, Robert Jordan, Orson Scott Card, etc., but these are not yet available. In other cases, one or two titles are available from an author, but others that you might want are not available. You can't necessarily blame Amazon as the decision to release texts electronically is up to the publishers and authors, but lack of availability on these titles is a downer.I'm subtracting one star for content.Extras: I have not used most of the extras, but I don't see myself using the internet browsing on this unit. I'd rather use another platform for browsing the internet.The online dictionary has given me some strange results. You highlight a line on the page using the menu bar and select Lookup from the menu. A screen then appears with the lookup results - the definitions for the words on the line. On one line, the dictionary provided the following definition for the word "hill"Hill , Benny (192592), English comedian; born Alfred Hawthorne. His risqué humor, as seen in the series The Benny Hill Show (195766), had an international appeal.Funny, but hardly useful.Overall I have to give the Kindle three stars. I really like the concept and I am, for the most part, enjoying the product, but improvements can and should be made. If you enjoy the classics as I do and like the idea of carrying your library with you at all times, I recommend purchasing the Kindle. For those of you on the fence, I would wait for the next version of Kindle. I'm hoping the design flaws are corrected and that the content will eventually catch up to the market.

title: 3 stars because if you only expect a $40 to $50 tablet you won't be disappointed - but if you call amazon & they mislead well...
body: I purchased the 6-pack deal only after I called in to ask specific questions about the tablet.Currently our family owns (2) 2nd generation original Kindles / (1) Kindle Paper White / (4) 3rd generation Kindle Fire Tablets / and I am currently on my 4th Fire Phone. Aside from the Fire Phones - I generally have been very happy with my Amazon Electronics. (Fire phones - an entirely different problem, don't get me started!!!).My plan was to replace our existing 3rd Generation Kindle Fires that my Grandchildren use. I am very tech savvy. I understand these tablets are much cheaper than the 3rd generations - but I was willing to sacrifice a little to save a lot. Unfortunately there is way too much to sacrifice - so these babies are going back to Amazon ASAP.My phone call prior to purchasing was to clarify details about the Screen. I wanted to know if screen on the new tablets was comparable to the 3rd gens.I was viewing the details through the Amazon app on my AMAZON FIRE PHONE. The description lists 2 different screen specs in 2 separate paragraphs - I called to clarify which was correct.>>>Under the “Description” section the specs listed are: 7” Display 1280x800 252ppi // >>>Under the “Features & details” section the specs listed are: 7” Display 1024x600 171ppi.(((current 3rd generation tablets I own specs are: 7” Display 1280x800 216ppi)))The sales rep found the page I was on and said that the higher quality configuration was the correct set. Following the reps clarification, I bought the tablets using my Fire Phone and did not think to consult my desktop to review the tablets to see why the discrepancy was there. I figured calling in to talk to the rep that assured me that they are comparable if not better quality vs. the current 3rd gens I am using. WRONG - they arrived and the screen is smaller and the geometry for the viewing area screws up the Video viewing on Netflix (I did not go any further than testing Netflix before factory resetting the tablet to send it back). The Picture Quality is TERRIBLE and the people in the movies are all out of proportion and there is no way to change it.I did not think to check the speaker system to see what these tablets were coming with. My 3rd gens have decent loud STEREO speakers. The new tablets speaker is a piece of junk. It is Mono and very quiet compared to my 3rd gens. The sound quality is very poor as well.I used the tablet for about 30 minutes and it was considerably slower than the 3rd gens / it froze multiple times while trying to download apps. It is a 1.3ghz Quad Core 1gb Ram vs. the 3rd gens 1.5ghz Dual Core 1gb Ram. The new tablet has additional cores to help multi task – but the clock speed is noticeably slower for the day-to-day tasks. The 3rd gen is much quicker to load vs. the newer tablet. Not worth sacrificing the speed for multi tasking features on these tablets in my opinion.The table also weighs in much lighter than the current 3rd gens that I own – 12.2oz 3rd gen vs. 11oz new tablet. I asked if the difference in weight was at the expense of sacrificing durability – specifically thinner glass. The sales rep assured me it wasn't any less durable. After actually receiving the tablets and realizing the Rep simply did not know what they were talking about when it came to the display resolutions etc - I am not willing to find out if they are more fragile. The general feel of this tablet is that it is much more flimsy than the 3rd gens that I currently have. I have been using the 3rd gens without incident for several years - they are for my Grandkids ranging from 2-7 years old - and nothing has ever stopped working and the glass is very durable. They are excellent quality tablets to say the least, and can take a beating!The rep also hinted that the current price of $50 was most likely to increase in the near future. So I decided to get the 6 pack with the thought of selling 2 tablets to bring the price down even more. I figured that I would wait until the tablets on Amazon increased in value and then list them for sale. From what I have read and from actually seeing the tablets 1st hand - I would bet the $50 will be lowered in the future - not raised. The tablets are simply not worth more than $50.The other sinker for me was the lack of options available in the settings menu to customize it. If you compare the Settings Menu’s side by side - 3rd gen next to the new Tablet and choose any area (I started with the keyboard) there is a huge disparity between the 2. The Keyboard on the new tablet is missing so many features it would take too long to list. Here are just a few examples:>>Trace Type = Not Available>>Secondary Keys = Not Available on 2 of three font sizes - only available on the smallest font size - but it is so hard to read the primary keys it makes the even smaller secondary keys impossible to see at that font size>>Blue Tooth Keyboard = Not Available….and the list goes on and on.Prior to calling in for assistance I accessed the help pages on the tablet. It is glaringly obvious that they were too lazy to write new directions for this model - they are loaded with tons of options that simply DO NOT EXIST. I then called customer service to see if I was just not looking in the right place - but alas the help absolutely does not understand the equipment they are trying to help with. I ended up spending almost 5 minutes to get the rep to even understand what Trace Type(swype) was let alone that it is now missing. The call only went further down the rabbit hole from there. Waste of Time!!I will be returning these tablets tomorrow and then ordering brand new old stock 3rd generation tablets at $150 each. One would figure that things would improve 2 generations later - but sadly they are much worse.

title: Not a good tablet; but it doesn't cost enough to give you buyer's remorse
body: The 2015 Fire tablet is a mishmosh of really good ideas, a solid price, and disappointing performance and software. The whole package wraps up to be nothing more than an average device, but with a rock-bottom price point ($50 normally, $40 at time of writing) to make it not quite so much of a disappointment.The Pros: When reviewing a device I always seem to talk about RAM, but here goes: the Fire has 1GB of RAM. That's roughly double what most other players in the super-budget tablet market seem to include, which means multitasking is possible, though not really likely. 1GB is still a small amount, but it's really good for this price.The screen is really nice for the price, though there's a few unfortunate quirks (see below). IPS technology means stellar viewing angles, with no color inversion at any angle. It's just a really nice panel that's good for video and some light gaming.The SoC, called the MT8127, is one of MediaTek's better super-budget options to date, giving you four 1.3GHz Cortex-A7 cores. That said, once you get that low-priced you're bound to hit poor performance, and the Fire does struggle at times (again, see below). Surprisingly, though, the Fire plays a lot of midrange 3D games surprisingly well (usually around 30-45fps), and handles video without any jitters. Granted, the MT8127 is built to drive displays up to 1080p and is only driving a 600p one, so it's unsurprising that performance is adequate. That said, high-end 3D games will give you some stutters or not run at all.The Fire currently runs Fire OS 5.1.2 "Bellini," which is based on Android 5.1 "Lollipop." (It could be based on Marshmallow at this point, but it seems safe to say that it's still Lollipop.) It's not the latest version of Android by any means, but it's better than the old Android 4.4 "KitKat" even modern budget tablets seem so intent to keep using. I have no problem with KitKat, but as it's not supported even with security updates by Google anymore, it doesn't seem likely to expect that OEMs like Amazon will patch security flaws on their own in outdated OSes.Speaking of Fire OS "Bellini," it's easily the most pleasant version of Fire OS to use yet. Aside from the home screen and settings menu, most stuff looks like a reasonable approximation of stock Android. The navbar has back, home, and overview buttons in normal spots, the quick settings menu looks like stock Android, the overview screen has the 3D card effect; everything looks pretty normal.Battery life is adequate to good; standby time is really good and I haven't ever used it for long enough to be extremely low on battery while I'm using it.The Cons: The screen resolution is absolutely abysmal for reading. It's odd how 800x600 at 6 inches is fine (though not exceptional) for reading on a Kindle whereas a tablet with the widescreen version of that same resolution is horrible to read on. Chalk it up to differences between an eInk display and IPS LCD, but this just isn't a good advice to read Kindle ebooks on. I use a Nexus 10 as my main tablet and it's much nicer to read on (as well it should, it has six times the pixels and cost me over six times the price of one of these).Speaking of the screen: There's a lot of backlight bleed at some angles of the screen. It's not a huge deal for people who are used to IPS screens, but there are certain points where it's so noticeable to be distracting.There are jitters everywhere. You scroll the home screen, there are jitters, you scroll a Web page in Silk there are jitters, you scroll through Amazon Apps and it jitters. $50 doesn't pay for much hardware, but it just seems like a pretty lousy experience for Amazon to put their name on.Fire OS is still a really heavy skin, despite the praise I had for it above; you cannot change the home screen from the default one, which defeats a lot of customization that Android is known for. I'm also not a fan of the Material Design-meets-Holo theme Amazon has in Bellini; I think it looks hideous and it would've been better if they committed to a single design standard (preferably full-on Material Design, because that's where developers are going).The Fire is a huge step back in terms of comparison to the previous 7" Fire. The Fire HD 7 had superior specs in nearly every way. This is more a complaint of the Fire replacing the Fire HD 7 rather than expecting similar performance; it would've been better if Amazon kept the Fire HD 7 in stock as a higher-end version of this one.The design is strikingly boring; that would be fine if it was ergonomically good, but the curvature of the back to make it easier to hold in one hand is defeated by the incredibly slippery plastic. It's also pretty thick, exacerbating the problem.The Fire can't access Google Play (at least not officially). It's to be expected from an Amazon tablet, but it's important to know what you're getting into. The Fire should be able to access the stuff you might've downloaded from the Amazon Appstore on other devices, but that awesome app you bought on Google Play will either need to be purchased again or just won't work with the Fire if it's not available on Amazon Apps. That said, Amazon Underground is available, meaning a lot of apps that are paid on other stores are totally free on Amazon Apps.Ultimately, the Fire tablet is one I'd rate two stars at best if it was any more expensive. It's really not a good tablet; there's a lot to like here but it's difficult to love it taken as a whole. The price makes a lot of its shortcomings forgivable, but you're better off saving up for a slightly nicer tab unless you never will be able to afford better.

title: A pretty good bargain, if you can overlook the issues....
body: The old saying, "You get what you pay for.", couldn't be more true when talking about this tablet.Full disclosure: this is my first tablet, but I've owned an iphone the last four years, and I've used my mothers ipad extensively. Also, I don't speak tech, so this is a total layman's terms review. So, now that you know where I'm coming from.....My parents purchased these tablets for all the grand-kids this Christmas, and gifted one each to my sister and I as well. I was pretty excited to finally have a dedicated e-reader with some extras. The iphone 6 is great for games, and apps....but for binge watching hulu and netflix, and reading, it leaves a little to be desired. So I was thrilled to have a larger screen for viewing and reading. It's true that the resolution isn't "top notch", but it's not bad. There are worse. It does get pixley when the action seems fast moving (could be my internet....not sure.) but other than that, it's performed fine. As for reading, well...it's difficult to screw up the one thing you were designed to do. ;-) It does well and the Prime lending library is pretty decent.But...there are some bugs and it's really up to you to decide if they are worth the insanely cheap price tag. (anytime you can grab a decent tablet for less than a tank of gas, that's a pretty sweet deal in my book.)- It's slow. Truly. Coming from the iphone 6....this thing crawls. I might not notice it if I wasn't constantly switching between the two, but I am, so I do.- Silk is a joke. And it's beyond frustrating to have to deal with bing as the default search engine. I'm a google girl. Always have been, always will be. 9 times out of 10, if I have to access the internet for any reason... I do it on my phone (unless my laptop is nearby).- The appstore leaves a bit to be desired. I know there are ways to side load apps, but I haven't tried yet. I don't really use this tablet for games, so it's a non-issue for me. And my kids have found more than enough game apps to keep them happy, but if you are buying this for apps.....I would check amazons appstore and make sure they offer what you want/need, first.-The touch screen is "touchy" (or "not touchy"? lol) At first I thought it was just mine, but my children's tablets are the same. I sometimes have to tap an icon several times to get it to respond. Very annoying.- Storage is also a joke. Don't buy this without getting an SD card too. I still need to order them for all of our tablets. Once you reach a certain level of storage, the WiFi shuts down and will not turn back on until you delete some apps.
