This is most frustrating for my children, who reached max capacity within three hours of opening their tablets, thus disabling WiFi and in turn, rendering some of the apps and the video streaming useless.- They take fooooorrrrreeeevver to charge. For real. We had my daughters plugged in and it took over an hour to go up 10%. Insane. you can buy an "accelerated" charging adapter for $20, but IMO, that's something that should just be included.Speaking of my children and their tablets, we decided to take advantage of the Amazon FreeTime. On the surface, this is a wonderful tool that helps control their usage even when mom and dad aren't right there. It was actually the one big thing that swayed me from ipads to Fires. (that and the difference in price.) I love that I can set time limits on everything as well as goals for activities. The automatic shut-down and start-up feature is also nice to prevent sneaky children from getting on their tablets too early in the morning, or staying up late and playing on them past bedtime. (yes, I realize I can just "take them away", but kids are sneaky and where there's a will there's usually a way.) Moving on....The one HUGE complaint I have with FreeTime, is the inability to control it from other Fires. Since our families separate profiles automatically downloaded to each Fire as we registered them, I assumed that I could make changes to my children's FreeTime accounts via my Fire. I was wrong. For the first few days, I was under the impression I had set up time limits and reading goals, only to discover, merely by accident, that I had not. I had to go onto each tablet and change each child's goals and time limits. It may not sound like such a laborious activity, but with four tablets to adjust, it's annoying. Especially when they spent the night at their grandparents house and wanted some extra video streaming time and I had no way to adjust it. I know that seems like a #FirstWorldProblem....but it's still aggravating. There may very well be a way to sync the tablets, but I have yet to find it.The other issue I have with FreeTime is that it seems the apps on my children's profiles need to re-download each time they turn them back on. I've called Amazon Kindle tech support and they were of NO help. Meanwhile, I have four very irritated children over the issue. I have no clue how to fix it, or if it's due to the tablets themselves, or a tech issue. Regardless, its frustrating for sure. If you've downloaded an app,. I see no reason why you'd need to re-download every time you power the thing on. I hope it's a glitch I'll soon be able to fix.All in all, these are pretty good bargain.. As I said, you can barley fill your gas tank for $50, so to get a name brand tablet for that price is a good deal. Also, I'm not holding my breath each time my kid drops theirs, or mine. At $50...it's not too painful to replace if needs be. ;) The bugs are something I'm willing to deal with given the incredibly cheap price point.

title: Wowzer, what a gadget! - for several months, anyway
body: UPDATED on January 20, 2010: I love my Kindle. But after about 15 months of use - and not abuse - the scroll wheel has become very unstable. I know it's been more like 18 months since I bought it, but this problem surfaced as more than an occasional issue, a few months ago. I've realized I'm avoiding using my Kindle because of the problem. Now it's getting to the point where it's hit or miss as to where the cursor goes when I move the scroll wheel that's supposed to control it, meaning it's essentially unusable. The other controls are just fine.Naturally, we're past expiration of the original 1 year warranty, and I didn't buy an extended warranty for this item. So my options, unless I want to abandon Kindle reading, are (1) to send it back to amazon and buy a reconditioned Kindle 1 for $99, which comes with a 90 day warranty with no possibility of buying an extended warranty for it, or (2) buy a Kindle 2. I've just placed my order for a Kindle 2, and if I decide to keep it will no doubt buy the extended warranty while it's available for purchase. I'm disappointed that the Kindle 1 hasn't been more durable. I've had no other problems with it, although as I said, I have not subjected it to any hard treatment or had any accidents with it (drops, hits, etc.).Now, here's the original review:I bought myself a Kindle about 7 weeks ago, a splurge in honor of one of those "decade" birthdays. I wondered if this would be yet another gadget I bought on impulse, used for a little while, and then put on the shelf.So far, the answer is no. After nearly two months most of the novelty factor is gone from the experience, but the Kindle is still a constant companion. Main reasons:1. Super portability: I took the Kindle traveling with me just after I got it, and it was great to be carrying the content of more than 3000 pages of books in that one lightweight compact device. On the plane, in the airport, at the hotel - no muss, no fuss, just flip open the Kindle to read and close it up when finished. I estimated the 3000 pages by adding up the pages of the books I'd loaded on the Kindle at that time. Heaven knows how many pages' worth of reading material is on there by now.2. Wireless. Okay, this is still a little of the novelty factor, but it is sweet to pick up my Kindle at lunch and click on today's International Herald Tribune which appears without my doing more than turning on the Kindle (and the wireless function).3. Choice of content. For books, newspapers and magazines, it's amazon. And free samples too. Nuff said.4. The reading experience. I'm not sure when it happened, but not long after I started using the Kindle, the device "disappeared" the way a book "disappears" as I'm reading it. The act of clicking a "next page" bar on the Kindle became no more intrusive than the act of turning a page in a book. Navigating newspapers and magazines is still enough different that I'm conscious of it, but it's not a bother at all. I like the ability to skip articles with one click, or go right back to the articles/sections list from the beginning of just about any article.5. Documents. I'm just starting to put documents on the Kindle, but so far I like it. Today I paid ten cents to have a long pdf file converted by amazon and sent to my Kindle. The conversion made the table of contents look awkward, but the main text of the document is easily readable, including the footnotes which appear in readable but distinguishable font so they are not confused with the main text.6. Privacy. When I'm using my Kindle on a plane or a bus, in an airport or waiting room, or while eating lunch, nobody can tell (without asking) what I'm reading. I've answered questions about the gadget itself, but if I'm reading a nice bit of escape fiction - for instance, [[ASIN:B0015DTVVA The Roar of the Butterflies]] - well, that's my business and might not be anyone else's.The main downside so far is what to do with the books I've finished reading. I hang on to a lot of books, but also pass a lot of them along in different ways: lending or giving to friends, donating to charities/libraries, trading in at a used paperback store. Can't do any of those things with a Kindle book. I can see my use of the Kindle evolving to mostly books I'll want to keep for reference and documents I want to carry along with me, plus recreational reading to take when traveling.But so far, no buyer's regret for this purchase. It's a great gadget that rarely gets left at home.UPDATE on March 17, 2009: I still use my Kindle almost daily, and my only buyer's remorse is over the amount of money I sometimes spend on Kindle books. I bought a replacement battery for it recently - after a LONG time on the wait list for it, because after 6 months the original battery wasn't holding a charge as long as it did at first. No problems with the replacement. I note that Kindle 2 does not have a user-replaceable battery, so I'm hanging on to my Kindle 1. I'd hate to have to send it in just to have a battery replaced.

title: This is a step back for Amazon tablets
body: I recognize that this is supposed to be an entry level tablet, but Amazon missed the mark wide on this. Coming from a 7" Fire HDX, this tablet is largely a disappointment. My disappointment stems as much from Amazon's gutting of their line with high quality devices like the HDX and moving to lesser systems, thereby stripping us of a good balance between cost and quality features. None of the current tablets in the Fire line provide what the HDX/non-HDX options did, and it is supremely disappointing. If Customs hadn't caused my HDX to get cracked, I would have stayed on that platform indefinitely.I acknowledge that you get what you pay for, and the price point on this device is pretty phenomenal, which is why it gets 3 stars. The 7" HDX was a great device and one of my go to systems -- it had good resolution, was relatively fast, had a small bezel, was lightweight, used a highly contrasting back light, was just the right size in my hands, etc. Everything the new 7" is not. Going to an 8" class device just doesn't appeal, and looking at the only moderately different specs on the HD 8 don't make the price jump even remotely worth it. I have a couple of HD 6's for the kids, and they are like bricks in your hands, and again the minuscule spec bump and decrease in size simply don't justify the jump in price. That extra inch of the 8 becomes an even riskier proposition in a backpack going through TSA security all the time and shoved under an undersized airline seat. It makes a big difference.The new device is fatter, heavier, with a lower resolution, slower speed, a larger bezel (therefore a smaller screen which sucks for reading -- one of my primary use cases), adjusting the brightness has very little effect, and there is no auto-adjust of the brightness. This new blue shade feature is obnoxious, and there is no blue to it. I've used a tablet to read at night for years, and this doesn't help me to fall asleep after reading one bit. A nicely adjustable brightness setting is WAY more effective than this supposedly "scientific" approach. I stare at screens of various types and sizes all day long and have for years and years, so maybe it's just that I'm more adjusted to that than others so making the screen red or yellow doesn't do much for me.While I like the fact they've tried to go back to a more Android-esque view on the new OS (I'm an Android user, will never go back to iOS), they should either go all in or keep their highly proprietary way of presenting things. I personally liked the sliding carousel. It made the content-driven use of the Fire different than my other Android devices, but not obnoxiously so that I couldn't easily flip between them -- it was distinct, which actually made it easy to use. This new version has no real identity except one of "where the heck is that now?". It's not well done, it doesn't help me to get to content or the store quickly and easily, and it's a kludgy mix of "real" Android and FireOS. Again, I've used the HDX 7 since it came out, so I may just be used to it and in time might adjust, but I've been using this for a few weeks now and not found anything redeeming about the changes. Where I would use the HDX 7 frequently for more than just reading, with this I find myself only using it for reading due to it's largely useless interface changes. I'd have been better off buying a non-Amazon tablet and a regular Kindle e-reader. Especially since I use a number of other, more "pure" Android experiences on other devices, this just feels like a very bad attempt to appease Android users at the cost of their unique identity. The only thing that separates a Fire tablet from any of the dozens of generic and serviceable Android tablets out there is the unique tie into the Amazon ecosystem -- the primary point in using Fire tablets being content acquisition. That is ostensibly gone now, and will likely result in my acquiring a generic Android tablet of higher quality and putting the Amazon apps on it, since I'm not going to spend more money on a Kindle e-reader at this point and this will not serve as a generic content consumption device for me.If this were my first experience with an Android or Amazon tablet, it may not be a bad one. It is serviceable for anyone without experience with the superior previous HDX generation, or any other high level of expectations. Kids without other Fire table experiences should be fine with it, and anyone without serious Android "purist" tendencies. It will likely become a third or fourth device for me as a backup or one to share with the kids once I find a 7" tablet I like better. For the price, that may be a perfectly good place for the tablet to live in my menagerie of tech, but Amazon's going to have to do something spectacular to become my primary tablet provider again. I only wish I could find a reconditioned HDX 7 at a reasonable price.....

title: Pros and Cons from a first-time tablet buyer.
body: Fire 7" 2015 Review: It's okay for the price, but I have some serious buyer beware issues with the device that severely impacted my rating. I'll cover both the pros and cons, starting with the cons. Disclosure: this is the first tablet I've ever owned. CONS: Users can't access the Google Play store (sans doing some shady and potentially risky mods). This means no access to Google Drive apps. no Chrome etc... (not even via the Silk web browser). This wouldn't be as big of an issue for me if the Amazon Apps store offered comparable software, but I keep encountering instances where it doesn't. For example, I downloaded the tumblr. app from Amazon and I was informed by tumblr. that the version Amazon offers is outdated. Considering tumblr. is a relatively mainstream social media platform, this is inexcusable. (A search on the Amazon store show complaints about this version discrepancy dating back for months). Of course, the updated version is only available via Google Play, which Kindle owners don't have access to. The same thing happened when I downloaded the AMC app (the television station, not the movie chain). The other big issue I have is memory card management. I purchased a 64GB microSD card along with my Fire, yet I already filled most of the internal memory due to the device's poor memory management. When selecting the option to save to the external card, it still retains much of the content on the main drive. Some apps have to reside on the main card, others are distributed between the two cards. My cable provider (Xfinity) offers on-demand content. I downloaded the Xfinity app and streamed media just fine (looks great BTW). The app also gives one the option to download the content to the device for viewing offline, which I tried. It sent the file to the main memory card, despite having selected download movies and TV shows to your SD card in the Fire's options menu. Manual transfer of files between internal storage and external storage is not easily done. Considering there's only 8GB of internal memory, of which only about 5GB is actually available (due to system software), downloading big movie files will fill the device's built-in storage really quickly. Lastly, the device lacks a physical home button. This is kind of annoying, for as someone with BFGF (Big Friendly Giant Fingers), I've inadvertently activated the on-screen home menu on numerous occasions. Okay, now for the PROS: As aforementioned, it looks great. Yeah, it's only 1200x600 dpi, but the IPS display can be viewed fairly clearly at a wide range of angles. Every game I've thrown at it has worked so far, even some graphically intense games. Perhaps the relatively low end DPI display is actually an asset when it comes to game playback (I would assume playback on a low-res display is less taxing on the processors). I have a HULU account, so I downloaded the HULU app. Playback was smooth and sharp, even in the high quality mode (Note: a decent speed wi-fi connection is needed and sometimes the first few seconds of streamed video are kind of pixelated, but once the buffer fills, the video quickly becomes clear). Amazon's underground store has plenty of free content (a big plus). As someone who both listens to podcasts and hosts a podcast, I wanted to be able to download podcasts to my Fire and manage them. The Podcast Addict app on the Amazon App store seems to do this quite well. I also used the Kindle to check out an e-book from my local public library. I was really impressed at how well the Overdrive application I downloaded from the Amazon store integrates with the actual Amazon store. When I checked out the book from my local library's e-catalog, it took me to a link to the Amazon store where I was then able to borrow the title directly from Amazon for a three week loan. I have the option of checking the tile in electronically should I finish the book early. Public libraries that partake in this program usually have a limited number of copies of a given e-title available for checkout, if they're all checked out, one can place a hold just like one would do with a physical library book. While some files have to be stored on the main memory, this is not true of non-DRM files which I transferred from my computer via USB to the external flash drive using the Android File Transfer. If one has a lot of home videos, audio files, or photos, an external card is the way to go. Kindle's default video player recognized my videos with ease, though I found VLC's free app a bit better at navigating large libraries. Overall, this is a very capable device for the price, I just wish some of the apps on Amazon's App store would stay abreast with the versions offered on the Google Play store, and I wish I could access my online Google Drive spreadsheets for I use them quite often.

title: Not a success, not yet a failure.
body: I received this as a Christmas gift from my dad after having wanted one for quite some time. I was terribly excited to get it, but that excitement gradually dimmed. I'm going to go through the cons first, then the pros.THE CONS: STORAGE SPACE, APPSTORE, SCREEN, SIZE, LIGHTFirst of all, let me warn you that this does NOT come with 8GB of storage. It comes with 5, due to the many, many apps Amazon installs onto it automatically that it will not allow you to uninstall. Don't have kids? Doesn't matter, you have to keep the FreeTime (20MB) app installed anyway. Don't use Audible (8MB), Goodreads (13MB), WPS Office (66MB), or Games (9MB)? Doesn't matter, you have to keep those apps installed as well. It would be fantastic if we could uninstall apps like that which we aren't going to use, but you can't. Because of this, you don't have a lot of room left to store things and after about 3 apps are downloaded, you only have 2GB left. I constantly find myself clearing app caches, and the data of apps that I haven't even used, just to free up some space.The second biggest disappointment is the Amazon Appstore. They have a very small selection of apps compared to Google Play, and you'll be lucky if you get the apps to work. Many of them are not compatible with Amazon Fire or they stop working after an update. If you'd rather use Google Play apps, you could always waste storage space downloading a platform that allows you to do so, or you could just get an Android tablet or phone instead to avoid this altogether. However, most people are fine with the Amazon Appstore because they already have an android device in their home and can use it for the apps they want from Google.The very first thing I noticed about the Amazon Fire when taking it out of the package is that it's not smudge-resistant at all. It doesn't claim to be, but that doesn't mean it has to attract dirt and fingerprints like a magnet. While it's not noticeable while the device is turned on and being used, which is most important, you can see the damage while the screen is dormant and black. This may not bother some but if it does, I recommend getting a screen protector.Lastly, the size is a bit too big for me. Even the Fire HD 6, which is just one inch smaller, would make a huge difference compared to this one. Due to the size of the 7", I find it difficult to type using both hands which means it takes a long time to send someone a message.And as an added extra, I'd like to point out that the light on this thing hardly dims. If you put it to its lowest setting, it's still very bright which doesn't make it good for nighttime reading if your significant other is sensitive to light being on while they're sleeping.THE PROS: PERFORMANCE, EMAIL APP, MUSIC APP, DESIGN, KEYBOARDAs for the performance of the device, there's nothing to complain about here. It runs very fast and smooth and I've never had any problems with getting an app to open or getting it to run fast.I love the Email app that is already installed onto the device. I'm a business woman, so I have to be available throughout the day to check my email, and the Email app on the Amazon Fire makes it easy to do so. It's much simpler to be able to go through my emails with the swipe of a finger instead of relying on a clunky computer or my slow android phone. The music app is also a wonderful thing. It allows you to play music in the background while doing other stuff such as browsing the internet, and includes the song lyrics if you're viewing the song page while it's playing. However, if you don't want to take up space by downloading music onto your device then the Fire allows you to play music from your Cloud too.The design of the device is very sleek and attractive. It's thin, so it's able to fit in small places and isn't a hassle to carry around with you in your purse or luggage, unlike a laptop computer would be. Furthermore, the on-screen keyboard is just the right size. On all of the touch-screen android phones I've had, the keyboard has been way too small, causing me to constantly click on letters I didn't mean to and have to waste time changing things and eventually end up wasting space by downloading a new keyboard. None of those things have ever happened with the Fire.CONCLUSION: The 7" Amazon Fire is a great catch for a low price if you're not looking for something spectacular with lots of storage space. I think it deserves a 3.5 but that kind of rating system isn't available here. Personally, I'd rather have an android device with Google Play available, but I enjoy the Fire nonetheless.

title: Excellent V 1.0 effort, needs a few tweaks
body: I read my first novel on the Kindle over the weekend, and having owned it now for a week, thought I would write my review.The Good:- The format is immersive. I really did forget I was reading from an electronic device, and even pawed at imaginary pages to turn every now and then. Super easy on the eyes, and I really like the fact that it mirrors a paper reading experience - if the light is bad, the device does not compensate by way of a backlit screen.- The Kindle store's shopping experience is very smooth, very transparent. I have bought a few books, a magazine, and currently on a couple of free trial magazine and blog subscriptions. The latest editions of the New York Times and The Onion are waiting for me first thing in the morning, once you get the hang of how the "Back" button works (versus "Previous Page"), navigating is a snap.- The size of the device is just right - it is very easy to hold and read in bed, at a table, on the couch etc. More on this in the "Needs Work" section. But to be clear, my Kindle goes everywhere with me - the doctor's office, the park, the bus etc.- Pricing on Books is good - I would easily pay $9.99 on a title, especially as we run out of library space at home. I read my first novel in a long time thanks to the Kindle (the book - "A Thousand Splendid Suns", Khaled Hosseini is a trifle predictable and irritating after The Kite Runner ')- Text is very crisp and easy to read - as I said above, very immersive.- Love the quick charging and overall battery life.- Love the integrated web and Wikipedia search. I also checked my gmail account from time to time - horrible user experience, but had to be a true geek and say that I did it 'Needs Work (in no particular order of priority)- The case is a disaster. The device does not sit in it firmly, and while it looks nice when closed with the elastic band etc, what is really more practical is a sack of some sort, because you really need to take the device out of the case to read effectively and ergonomically. This has to fixed, hopefully through a rich third party accessory community.- As I read my novel, I often hit the side buttons accidentally and lost my place in the book (either fell asleep with my hand on the buttons, or was walking somewhere with the device without putting it to sleep, or kids got a hold of it etc etc). Bottom line, getting back to the right spot was very hard. I had to remember to bookmark every time I anticipated a disruption. Remembering location numbers is not too practical, since they can run into 4 digits in a long book. Accidentally pressing the wrong buttons is a also a problem when holding the book out of the case (which is more intuitive for reading)- One thing that is physically intuitive while reading a paper book is knowing exactly where you are, as measured by the pages completed/remaining. Making that same leap using location numbers and the indicator at the bottom is not (yet) that intuitive, so I was bit disoriented when people asked me questions like "how far are you into the book?"- The silvery indicator to the right (above the wheel) is not very visible in low light conditions or when there is some reflective directly in front of the device.- It would be nice to have an option (on the Home screen) to group all your content into Books, Magazines, Newspapers etc.- Charging for papers and blogs does not make too much sense - this content is available for free, with rich graphics and color, on the web. Over time, I see myself reading books and documents on the Kindle, not newspapers and blogs unless the format is made more rich on the device.- Graphics is very rudimentary. It's nice to see the device render pictures with stories in the Times etc, but the quality of the images harkens back to 1991 x486 computing.- Keyboard latency is a bit painful, but am sure this will get fixed soon.- Perhaps I have not had the device long enough, but it was not clear what the archival process is going to be for newspapers etc.- In mirroring actual paper/ink, I think the screen background needs to be a bit brighter, without being backlit. Right now it is gray-ish.- I am sure a million others have already said so, so let me be #1,000,001 - $399 for the reader does not make sense for broad penetration. And this SHOULD be broadly adopted - it is a no-brainer device in the classroom, for textbooks, course handouts, teacher communications etc. It's a brilliant device, and it needs to be $199 or less. People will pay for the books, and other content. It's all about the blades...

title: It's the average tablet.
body: I purchased 2 of these for my boys for Christmas. One child is 5 years old, the other is just about 11 years old.The PROS that I've found so far in the 4 days we've had them:- Great price! I got these on black Friday for $35!! That's a steal! I think this fire actually exceeds my expectations as far as performance. I have an older $60 android tablet that the boys played on before that was much slower than the Fire.- There are a variety of apps in the app store. I know a lot of people dislike Kindle's app store, but there are plenty of free games that my boys enjoy playing and that suits us just fine. There is even a nice selection of education games and books for them as well.- I can set up a separate profile for the kids. I have the main profile which allows me full access, and I have created a separate profile for my 5 year old which doesn't allow him to mess with anything on the device, except what I allow him to. I really appreciate this aspect because my 5 year old likes to turn on the Wi-Fi and download games without asking. With the separate profile, he can't do that and he would need a password to try to get back into my profile. Win for mom!- It's light weight and my kids have no problems holding on to it.- There are a variety of cases for this Fire just for the kids, which is a nice addition for us.Now, with all of that said - I find this tablet only "Okay" because of the annoyance level of the CONS:-I've had to restart the Kindle Fire probably about 12 times already in the 4 days we've had it. There is an issue with the profiles. I have a love/hate relationship with the profiles. When adding content to my son's profile, I have to log into his profile and allow the apps a moment to "upload" or "install"...I'm not even sure what it's doing, because it doesn't tell you. It just has an "x" to cancel in the lower left corner, an orange progress bar in the center, and a circle that cycles in the middle. This happens as soon as you attempt to use the app for the first time.When the app is ready a check mark will appear in the lower left corner of the app icon. Seems like something normal to encounter, except we have to do this every single time my son logs into his profile. He can't just log in and play. He has to log in, then wait for the games to cycle again, and IF the Kindle hasn't decided to stall out, he can play in about 5 mins when it's done cycling.- Sometimes, the kindle will stall and I have to restart it to allow the apps to load again properly within a child's profile (this goes along with above con). I've tried resetting the kindle back to factory and only putting one game at a time on the device, but it still does the same thing. I believe we have 5 games on the Fire currently. The games do run smooth once you can get into them to play.-Every app you download seems to go into the "cloud". I understand that some people enjoy their information being thrown into Internet space for convenience. I'm, personally, not one of those people. So, even though I have deleted an app from the device, it STILL shows up on the device via the cloud. It took me a few mins of "googling" to figure out how to solve this issue. I had to log onto my Amazon account on my computer, go to where I managed my apps/devices, and search around for a few mins to find my "cloud". From there, I could permanently delete the app.- I still cannot get the Washington Times app to stop showing up on my son's profile. I've deleted the app from both the device (for all users) and "the cloud" - It was an app that came with the Kindle. It doesn't appear to cause any issues, except that my 5 year old doesn't really need to see the Washington Times. It might a bug of some sorts that I am unaware of.Overall, it's a pretty decent tablet. You get what you pay for, and I think in this case, I got a little more since they were on sale! I should have purchased the kids version of the fire, but I was weary on spending more on a tablet I've not experienced yet. I'm not a tech professional, but I'm not a newbie either. This tablet took me some time to navigate around and I have spent a couple of hours trying to get things to load correctly and set up right with the cloud.

title: Eh
body: I've had a Kindle for about 3 months, and my overall impression is: Eh. It's okay.Pros:+ It's greener: no paper, no ink, no shipping.+ It's compact and lightweight: very easy to keep with you.+ It's very nice to have a selection of reading materials with you at all times, in case you lose interest in what you're currently reading. And it's great to be able download new content anytime, anywhere.+ It's very nice to be able to download large samples (often a whole chapter) prior to buying a book.+ I like being able to look up words in context. I've only used that feature a few times, but it's a handy feature to have when you need it.+ Changing the font size comes in handy: smaller when reading up close (e.g. in bed), larger when reading further away (e.g. with coffee).+ The battery lasts for a couple of weeks.Cons:+ It's Way Too Easy to push the next / prev page buttons. Making those buttons that large and that responsive was a preposterous decision. There is exactly one way to hold it without pushing some button or other. Hope your hand doesn't get tired. (And good luck reading while laying on your side, with the weight of the Kindle on those big, responsive buttons!)+ Speaking of which: No built in stand. How silly is that?+ The menus are poorly designed. When I launch a menu, most of the time the item I want is the farthest item. And the scroll wheel doesn't wrap around, so you have to scroll the whole way. (Usually the menu item that's closest is "Shop in Kindle Store" or "Buy Now" - I find that blatantly rude.)+ It's slow. "Turning" a page takes a full second or more, i.e. long enough that it breaks your flow. You have to learn to hit the button while you still have a bit left to read on the page. And if you hit the button by accident (which you'll do often - see above), you have to wait for it turn the page forward, then wait again to turn it back: Very Annoying. Pulling up the menu and selecting items is also too slow.+ It's so slow you cannot just flip through / browse the book and see what catches your eye. Also, you can't tell how close you are to the end of a chapter or other good stopping place.+ The content is inferior to print: A) Footnotes are virtually never linked; looking up a footnote is an ordeal. B) Books that rely on pictures or graphics to convey / clarify information: forget about them. The book I'm reading now, [[ASIN:B0010SKTRA Your Inner Fish: A Journey into the 3.5-Billion-Year History of the Human Body]], has diagrams that are almost illegible, and photographs that I wish I could make out. C) Words that were hyphenated in print are still hyphenated, even though lines don't break on the hyphen.+ Sometimes your "book" freezes - the Kindle has locked up on me twice in 3 months. And the reset button is recessed deep enough in a hole so small that you must have a paper clip with you to reset / un-freeze the Kindle. A PDA stylus, pencil, fork tine, etc. won't work. So if you don't have a paper clip, you can't read anymore. Ask me how much fun that is.+ It's expensive. The unit itself is $100 more than it should be. And the "books": Often the Kindle edition is the same price as the print edition, or cheaper by less than $1. (In one case, a Gold Box deal on a paperback made the paperback less expensive than the Kindle edition!) That's just offensive. The per-title cost is very low (especially since they almost never bother to link the footnotes or bookmark the sections / chapters), and the per-unit cost is nil. Kindle versions should be half the cost of the print versions at most, particularly since the Kindle edition is inferior.+ No vegan option for a case - I have to carry it around in dead animal flesh.For me, the biggest point, pro or con, is the green factor: I read so much that not using paper, ink or fuel to ship matters to me. So I'll keep the Kindle, and continue to overpay for (usually slightly) inferior content (I'm not paying any more than I would for print, but I should be paying significantly less). But overall:Eh. Nice idea, and a decent 1.0 implementation. Hopefully the 2.0 version will be better designed: smaller buttons / more options for holding it, faster performance, a built-in stand, a vegan case, and better content (in terms of links and images).

title: A long worth while wait......
body: I receieved this as a gift from my husband; however, in the meantime, I wasn't aware he was purchasing one so I ordered one myself. It turned out we both ordered one on the same day. I waited six weeks and received 2 Kindles on the same date! Amazon was great about reimbursing me for the return of the additional Kindle. The wait was long, but worth it.The Pros:I love the idea of shopping for books on my Kindle as well as online at Amazon.com. The speed in receiving samples to read of the books I select prior to purchasing is incredible. It really does get to me in about a minutes time. The samples that are sent to my Kindle are generous in portion and it helps me make the determination as to whether I am interested in purchasing the book or not.The prices are fair. Many of the books I do choose to purchase for my Kindle are between $5-$8, which the majority of the paperback books I read range from $7-$8 at Books a Million.It truly reads just like a book. Regardless if I am sunbathing by the pool or inside curled up on the couch, there is no glare and it looks just like the pages of a book.I love that I can change the font size. It seems that I am able to read the normal print during the day when there is natural light, but have a harder time reading in the evenings under a lamp. At this time, I increase the font size from 3 to 4, you can't do that with a book!It is lightweight and I now carry it in my purse so that if I am stuck somewhere, I always have something to read. I tend to be much more patient waiting for things when I am preoccupied with a book.Holds multiple books. The advertising indicates up to 200. I tend to read about 3 to 4 books at one time on a normal basis. It is nice that I can do this all in one location. I am currently going back and forth between three books right now on my Kindle.The Cons:Along with the majority of the reviews, I will reiterate that the buttons need improvement. I find that I continually accidentally bump the "page forward" button and find that I am constantly using the "page back" to get where I was.I wish there were actual page numbers instead of line numbers (I am thinking that is what is posted at the bottom of each page). Rather than going back to say, page 70, it is more like you have to remember to occasionally look at the bottom of the screen where you may be at lines 1265-1272. Last night I was maybe 1/4 of the way through a new book where I dozed off and must have had my hand on the page forward button because when I woke up, I was almost at the end of the book. Because I had dozed off, the page I was on wasn't bookmarked. I hadn't bothered to look down at the bottom to see where I was as far as the line numbers...so needless to say, I spent several minutes trying to find where I was in the book.If there is a way to categorize your books into folders, I am not aware of this and it would be a nice feature. For example, I read some "Biographies" and some "Romances" but they are all lumped together in the Home Page area.I primarily read True Crime and at this time, there isn't a large selection. I would love to see this expand greatly as the books are limited that are on Kindle.I like that the book is in black and white, but it would be a nice feature to have color screen capability when you are shopping for a book.It would be nice if the Kindle had a backlit screen for reading in the dark (although no eye doctor would recommend this)! Nintendo DS', Gameboys, etc. have a backlit screen to play video games in the dark. It would be nice for those times my husband is driving and it is dark outside, that I could pull out my Kindle and read it without having to purchase and then try and find a spot to put a reading light onto my Kindle.Of course, like everyone else has stated, the internet websites on the Kindle needs a lot of work.Overall, I love my Kindle. I gave it 3 stars as I feel it does leave a lot of room for improvement. I have no doubt there will be a newer, sleeker, more upgraded design in the future and I will one day own it as well! However, I am impressed and happy with what I waited six weeks for.

title: Why I (regretfully) returned my Kindle
body: At first the Kindle's price made me hesitate, but the idea of having a portable library that I could just toss into my backpack was too hard to resist. I waited about a month for my Kindle to arrive, and once I had it in hand, I took it just about everywhere. I read "The Great Gatsby" and "Cat's Cradle" on it, and I downloaded and sampled at least a score of first-chapter previews.After about two weeks of using the Kindle, however, I decided, rather reluctantly, to send it back. It wasn't that I didn't like the device -- for the most part, it's actually very well designed, and the fact that I was reading from a screen and not paper posed no obstacle whatsoever to my being able to immerse myself in my reading. No, I decided to return it based primarily on two reasons: First, the display, which of course is text-centric, is not sophisticated enough to render any but the crudest of maps, and being able to reference a given book's maps can be important when reading works of history and fantasy, among others. This first reason, however, was a fairly minor consideration relative to the second, which was, namely, that the Digital Rights Management (DRM) aspects bothered me much more than I had initially anticipated.First of all, I completely understand Amazon's need to have DRM on its Kindle books; without DRM, the publishers simply walk away from the table. I understand that. Also, seeing as how Amazon was a leader in DRM-free MP3 music, I believe that Amazon is actually on the consumer's side when it comes to these DRM issues.My objection to Kindle's DRM does NOT stem from any interest in copying books and making them available to perfect strangers. But the DRM does prevent me from doing the one thing that I am interested in doing, namely giving a book that I've finished reading to a friend or family member. What I envision as a fair application of DRM is one in which I can transfer my rights on a Kindle book to one (and only one) other person. Once I transfer my rights on a book, that book would be delivered to that person's Kindle and, at the same time, removed from my own. When that person was done with the book, they could give it back to me or choose to keep it or pass it along to someone else. I believe that this use is both fair and consistent with how we regard physical books.In my two weeks of owning a Kindle, I bought from Amazon just one book, "The Great Gatsby," for which I paid a little less than 4 dollars. Everything else was either a free preview or a legally obtained e-book (public domain works in most cases) that I had gotten from other sources and e-mailed to my device. Amazon does sell plenty of Kindle books that I was interested in reading, and a number of them I was quite tempted to buy. But when it came time to click that button -- to click that wheel, actually -- and close the deal, to tell Amazon, go ahead and charge my card and send me the complete e-book, I just couldn't do it. My awareness of the DRM on the book kept rattling around in my head and asking me, "What are you going to do with the book once you've finished reading it? If you really like the book, you can't let someone else read it without handing over your Kindle. You can't sell the book, you can't trade it or lend it, and you can't donate it. You're about to pay $7 to $10 for the mere privilege of reading that book ... and that doesn't sound very different from renting. So if you're not going to have any of the benefits of ownership, why rent what the library will lend you for free?"I couldn't get past thoughts like these, and so I packed up my Kindle -- not without quite a bit of regret I admit -- and returned it. I'd like to say that I haven't looked back, but truth be told I really did like reading on the Kindle, and I loved being able to quickly pull up a history book or a novel depending what I felt like reading at that particular moment. Unfortunately however, the 800-lb gorilla that is the Kindle's DRM model as it's currently implemented proved to be just too big for me to ignore.

title: A Mixed Review
body: I’ve had a new 7 inch Kindle Fire for a couple of weeks now and have formed an opinion about the device.First, some background. I had a first generation Kindle Fire tablet for three years. Due to declining battery performance and a loose power cable connector I began looking to repair or replace it. Since the new Fire was priced below what repairs would have cost — $49.99 for the new Fire direct from Amazon — a decision to purchase “new” was a no brainer.Second, my “use cases” for this device are fairly straightforward. I read Kindle books as well as documents I email from the web or my computer for later reading. I also watch an occasional Amazon Prime, Netflix, or HBO Go movie.As far as these use cases are concerned the new Kindle 7 inch Fire performs well and is even lighter than the old one. I like it especially given its low cost.But if someone really thinks this device can substitute for an iPhone or a higher performing Android tablet, some disappointments are going to emerge.Keep in mind my use cases when you read the following. I don’t expect this device to seriously replace a better tablet so it’s not really fair to evaluate it at that level. There are few things to keep in mind, though, as you contemplate slapping down your $49.99:App store. The Kindle app store is severely limited compared with Apple’s and Google Play. Amazon is making a huge collection of games available — many for free — but the numerous absences are frustrating. For example, there’s no native Instagram app in the Kindle app store, nor is there a separate Gmail or Google+ app there. For those you have to configure the native Silk browser via bookmarks. (see next comment).Browser. The Silk browser is much improved over its first rendition but it does experience an occasional stutter or lag. Performance-wise it’s adequate but in no way does it perform as smoothly or snappily as a Safari or Chrome on the iPhone or iPad.Cameras. The front and rear cameras are barely adequate. The less said about them the better.Display. The display itself is adequate. Resolution shows some pixilation of small text or tiny objects. Color and brightness are very good. I have no issues with reading or movie watching.Wireless. Wireless performance is noticeably better than the old Kindle especially in terms of locking on and keeping its signal connected.Touch screen performance may not be quite as good as the old Kindle Fire. It appears that “hot spots” for touch activation of links, buttons, and other active objects are not as sensitive or as well defined as with devices such as the iPhone or the iPad. It’s my impression that slightly more “hunting” around a spot is needed, and this leads to mistakes.I like using a variety of devices so I am sensitive when I go from one to another. I’m still surprised, for example, that the Kindle software on my iPhone is actually smoother and more responsive than the Kindle book software on this new device. Not by much, granted, but it is noticeable.Still, when you think about it getting a tablet computer like this for $50.00 it’s an amazing deal. 10 years ago such a low cost and versatile device would have been a miracle.But it is 2015 now. Being “adequate” might be a meaningful business strategy but the inclusion of so many underwhelming “standard” tablet features inevitably could attract comparison with (better performing and more expensive) competitors.A good question is, who is this device designed for? I’m sure the market is huge as a media consumption device which is why I have it and happily use it. But those who seriously want to use it as a more general purpose device may want to look elsewhere given the limitations of the Amazon infrastructure when it comes to general purpose mobile computing.

title: Changes the way I approach reading, with caveats
body: I got my Kindle on Christmas Eve, which was a surprise because Amazon had marked it as shipping after Christmas. It came almost fully charged, and already had the several books, blogs and New York Times subscriptions loaded on it by the time I got to it a few minutes after turning it on.The good:I think it's a fabulous text reader. I have already read more in several days than I would in a month, besides online content. It really does get out of your way, provided you hold it in the right place. The picture to my right called "Item being reviewed" right now is of a kindle, handheld with the left hand, pinching it with a thumb on the top and the palm on the rubberized back. That's how I read with it.I love the text resizing feature. Very nice.The articles are downloaded automatically, and I have read the NY Times every day now, and find myself becoming more connected with the real, non cyberspace world a bit more. Time, Slate and Salon come as well, and I am finding myself reading content I would pass over online.The screen is absolutely beautiful and fabulous, with no eye strain so far. And as a USB device, it mounts on my Mac and I just drop files into the e-book folder and away I go.The bad:Why couldn't we have shipped this thing with 16 shades of grey? I'm reading Steve Martin's standup comedy memoirs, and although I can do without the images, they really DO look horrible in four-shade black and white.We need folders, or some sort of organization mechanism on the main screen. I will use the Kindle for personal, professional and kid content, as well as periodicals, etc. I want to place those things in folders. Why they haven't gotten to that does not make sense to me.The thing is very strange to grip, as the buttons are really poorly placed. I can live with it due to the fact that it replaces my need to carry around everything I might read, but I think they need to hire an Apple designer.The dial's clicker is a bit hard to push, and I find myself scrolling by accident now and then.The ugly:The case. It is ugly. But I really don't mind it one bit.It can't handle content formatted for anything other than e-book text format well. I have several hundred presentations in PDF format that I would love to take along with me in the Kindle, but when I put them in PDF and email them to my kindle account, they come up very badly formatted (the background of each slide shows up above the slide, and the Kindle does not honor text bullet formatting).Yes, all bestsellers are $9.95, but why do the tech publishers still think it makes sense to sell a Java book at $35.00 to Kindle owners when it costs next to nothing to distribute? I would think the publishers would make a WHOLE lot more money if they dropped tech book Kindle prices to the same $9.95 rate.And finally, Jeff Bezos should really consider the whole lending concept. Since the files are DRM managed, why NOT allow them to be transferrable? Couldn't we set up a 'lend this book' button, and select another person's account? When they are done, they could send it back. Seems to me that, since we already know who a file belongs to, we can certainly set up transfer options.Anemic memory in this device. I can see putting a 2GB SD card in there.Search is slow, almost to the point of unusability. I would really love to limit my searches to the current book.Overall: a C+ almost B. For what I need it for, it's excellent, but some of its' shortcomings should be solvable by firmware updates. I hope Amazon listens, as this could do for books what the iPod did for music (hey, I'm thinking the publishers DON'T think that is a Good Thing).

title: insane value but large holes in day to day usage, frequent rebooting to solve ecosystem, hardware, app issues
body: There were other value Android based tablets before the Amazon Fire but this is the first to hit the $50 mark and carry a respectable brand name.Expectations were tempered by the price but also quite high given the obvious Amazon brand attached to it. I specifically wanted one to use for online music streaming and Plex video viewing. While the resolution is lower than 720P, it's still sharp enough to be serviceable watching 1080P movies from my Plex server with no complaints. Popped in a 64 gb microsd card I had laying around and going through a few settings to allow sd card storage at the system level and once again within the plex app and after some days I now have ~40 gb of content synced up via plex sync! each movie compresses from the server down to ~2.5 gb at 4mbps/720p settings which suit the Fire tablet just fine. Since this may be a common use scenario for others I will describe the overall experience a bit more.Plex sync works! that's the good news, the mediocre news is that sync sometimes stops when the tablet goes to sleep so I found myself babysitting it from time to make sure it was running. Sometimes the plex app gets confused between what's saved locally vs the server and won't play anything. A forced app close doesn't solve it, a tablet reboot does. There also seems to be issues with reliably reading from the SD card, again a reboot fixed it. Streaming music from Plex proved frustrating, it would play a few songs in order and then just stop. Reboot the app/tablet and on we go again. Amazon Prime music streaming was equally troublesome where either a whole song just would not play or playback stops after a while. Really made me miss Google Music where I have my library stored in the cloud.Other issue I encountered with watching movies stored locally with Plex, no sound! guess what, reboot and it's working again. Connecting to my car's bluetooth also exposed another flaw in the tablet, there's a noticeable ~0.5 second delay between audio and video which is not a big deal for music playback but makes any kind of video watching via bluetooth untenable as the audio and video are always out of sync no matter which app I used.So much for the mixed bag of plex experience. How about some other streaming? The Wifi just will not stay connected to my apple airport router, I have IP cameras, tablets, iphones, pc's, etc. that have no problem maintaining a connection except this tablet. For no reason the wifi symbol will have an exclamation mark that effectively takes me off line; turn wifi on/off and back we go. it's also noticeably weaker wifi than even my old iphone 5 running in the same N band.I also already have 3 stuck pixels on the edge border, so much for Amazon brand level quality control.The kid friendly profile is also not ready for primetime, try as I might I could not get it to display the apps I had shared and more importantly the Plex profile is considered separate so whatever I had already synced up under my profile does not carry over. Between not seeing the apps I had shared and the plex issue, it was enough for me to write off the Freetime profile mode.Not sure how many of these issues are with the plex app itself, hardware of the tablet, or the Fire OS but take them all together and you get a mixed bag for the end user.The upsides of this tablet have already been recounted by others; good screen, external storage, and that amazing price of course. The price is what will drive volume and hopefully cyanogen support soon so for those inclined set forth a path to root and install custom roms. There are already ways to sideload google services and i've used google music to stream music which works much better and more RELIABLE than streaming through plex or amazon prime music.

title: Good, but room for improvement
body: I've had my Kindle for a couple of weeks now, but held off on reviewing to get a better understanding of its high points and low points. I'm sure most of my thoughts have already been covered by the 1000+ reviews posted, but wanted to add my drop of water to this growing stream of feedback.Overall, I've enjoyed the Kindle experience, hence three stars. However, there is definitely room for improvement as one would expect w/ any 1.0 release. In particular, from my point of view, the Kindle is great if you fit the following profile: 1) Read multiple books simultaneously, 2) Read books that tend to be lengthy, 3) Travel frequently, and 4) Are comfortable being an early adopter. If you fall into one or more of these categories, then you'll probably be okay w/ the Kindle, but the device itself is not going to blow anyone away.In particular the the physical design is a bit on the clunky side. The now infamous "turn page" buttons are the primary shortcoming I've run into. They are very easy to hit and unlike a paper book, it's often cumbersome to "flip" around to find where you were when you set the Kindle down and inadvertently move to a new page. Short of some firmware upgrade that changes the way the button clicks execute, this issue won't be addressed until a full redesign takes place. In addition, the included case is a bit chintzy. It can be a chore to get the Kindle lodged just so to avoid it falling out. Once you get it situated it's not too bad, but it should be much easier. Hopefully this will be addressed by after market accessory makers.Another aspect of the Kindle some may find annoying is the screen refresh. Every time you turn a page, the screen flashes black momentarily, then renders the text on the new page. I personally haven't found this to be distracting, but others that I've allowed to play w/ the device have commented on it. On the upside, the display is very crisp and easy to read as one would expect from an eInk device.Speaking of positives, there are quite a few.Far and away is the wireless capability and online store component. It's very well put together and easy to navigate to available content. Maybe too easy...I can see how you could blow through some serious cash with no effort. Some have concerns about coverage based on their location and that's valid. If you're in a large metro area, you're probably good to go. If not, check the coverage map on the Kindle page to figure out if you'll be driving to the nearest Interstate to download a book.Battery life w/ the wireless turned off is fantastic. I've can go about a week w/ fairly regular reading before having to charge. You really only need the wireless to shop and search Wiki / online content, so not critical to the experience most of the time.Obviously the size when compared to most hardcovers is a bonus. It's much smaller and lighter, making it a boon to frequent travelers. Most of the books I read tend to be long and that usually means leaving them at home on road trips, which means it takes me about a year to finish a book.On balance I'm happy w/ the Kindle. However, the Kindle is best suited to books and it's experience is built around them. The blog / newspaper content doesn't seem as well suited to the format.So, if you want an alternative to lugging around bulky hardcovers and the ability to access new reads from most locations, the Kindle's for you. If you want an iPod like device that does a bunch of other stuff, you'll want to pass. The Kindle is a reading device first and foremost.I give it five stars for convenience, but until the design flaws are addressed, I can't give it five overall. Three stars for now, but w/ tons of upside potential.

title: An actual owner!
body: Well, since many of these reviews are from people who don't even appear to own a Kindle, I'll preface this by saying I have one in my hands, and have been using for a day or so now. I also have a Bookeen Cybook Gen3, so it should be fairly easy to make a nice comparison between the two.Setup was almost non-existent. The device was set up at the factory with all my details, and was even pre-charged. When you open the package, the Kindle is wrapped in a plastic wrapper which explains that you just plug it in and turn it on. I did plug it in, to charge, but in a very short while, the charging light went out, indicating full charge. When I powered it up, after an initial boot sequence, the Kindle owners manual opened. I read thru it, casually skimming the more obvious entries, and paid attention to some of the Kindle unique features.The most striking feature is the scroll bar and cursor wheel. The scroll bar lets you quickly scroll up and down, selecting menus or choosing a position within the book. When you have the menu item selected, you press the cursor wheel, just like a mouse. Navigation was a breeze and took only about 10 minutes to really become proficient.The screen is as expected. E-ink devices are superior displays for these kind of applications. For the nay-sayers who mock lack of backlight or color, I suspect they haven't even tried any e-ink device. Very readable. I haven't tried fonts or anything, because I'm happy with the default fonts for now. There's no eye-strain to speak of. After reading for about an hour, my eyes were no less or more tired than if I were reading a print book. For me, that's not possible on any computer (or PDA) device. The refresh is just too stressful for long periods of reading.The page buttons, on the side of the unit, are interestingly placed. It is easy to mistakenly press a button. While lying in bed (where I do most of my leisure reading), it can be a little awkward to hold the unit without pressing buttons. Coupled with the keyboard and all the buttons, the interface looks more busy than it really is. I hope that this is just part of the learning curve.Ordering a book was fairly easy. If anything, this is where Amazon will win the war. Over-the-air delivery in fast time. I searched for Steven Lawhead's "Merlin" (book 2 of the Pendragon cycle), ordered it, and within a very short time (less than a minute), I was reading my new book. When I was tired of reading, I set it in sleep mode. Sleep mode puts a nice image on the screen, but I wish it would just turn the whole screen off. It never seems to go from sleep to just plain shutdown.I won't go into the DRM issue much. But I do wonder if Amazon will reconsider their relationship with Mobi, or reconsider their decision to support their own DRM'ed format. It's been determined that the AZW format is basically DRM'ed Mobi, but at the moment, there's no way to get one to read the other.There is a USB port, so I tried to download a free (non-DRM) Mobi books. That went fine: download Treasure Island, and just copied to the Kindle. Disconnect the USB port (you can't use the Kindle while the USB is connected), and the book showed up in my content, and I was able to start reading it.The cover is almost useless. It seems to be made for storage, not for usage. The power switch of the Kindle is on the back, so you'd need to remove it from the cover to turn it off and on. There's also a strange plastic shim that appears to attempt to hold the Kindle in the cover. I figure that there will be after-market covers soon.Overall I think I am happy and won't have any bouts of buyers regret. I'll just have to keep careful on which and how many books I buy! This could easily be very costly.

title: Kindle v Books
body: Have you ever felt lonely? Outside, it's raining, and inside, the house feels cold. Not even the fireplace warms my bones. Either by want or circumstance, you feel alone. During these moments, I don't turn to wine or spirits but instead to a book.This [sensual feeling] leads me away to a different time and place, sometimes I know where and when, other times, I only know that I am leaving-temporarily. This journey begins with a trip into my library.There, I run my fingers along the shelves, wandering toward the book spines, some paper both old and new, others made of a solid stock, and I smell the paper, an unmistakable scent that reminds me that this room is not simply a library, but a passageway away from loneliness.I have rows and rows of books, and because I am a structured man, I separate the books into sections based less upon the Dewey decimal system but on the Terry system: Fiction in alphabetical order, nonfiction by subject, big books on the lower shelves (mostly), and books I don't like on shelves I can't reach. Call it being organized; call it me being anal. Whatever you call it, when I run my fingers across the books, I reaffirm my existence and that I am a reader and a writer for a greater purpose. My library is my church, and the words on every page remind me of my place.I love the ink, the letters, the words, the ideas, the poetry, the pages, the ideas, the story, and the feel of holding a book, as a book is my connection to the improbable world around me.Amazon recently added a new product. A Kindle. It is a small idol of technology capable of holding a gazillion pages of up to 200 books (for now) and is about the size of a paperback (7.5" x 5.3" x .7" at 10.3 ounces). Amazon describes it as:"...Revolutionary electronic-paper display [with a] high-resolution screen that looks and reads like real paper."Looks and reads like real paper. Wow. It doesn't say feels and smells like real paper.Obviously, if I were back in school, a Kindle would be a great idea to load up those out-of-date textbooks. (You know the ones.) To this day, I remember riding my bike to class while toting 50 pounds of books. Probably closer to a hundred pounds. A Kindle would have reduced my burden. All for the price of a few downloadable 1s and 0s and a few batteries.So why is it that I can't imagine tracing the edge of my Kindle and longing for another time and place? When it rains, will I feel inspired to press my Kindle to my chest? Will I learn to love the smell of a quirky gizmo and give up the need for forty- or eighty- or sixty-year-old paper? What will my 1926 copy of A Farewell to Arms smell like in electronic form? True, I won't have to worry about tearing a page. Just finding an internet connection. And true, I won't have to worry about having three different kinds of pens or highlighters and Stickies to mark pages. But what will I leave for the next generation-the ones who will buy my books at some future estate auction? I want to share my love of Story with those I love. I desire to demonstrate a need that propels me toward other worlds and loves; I want to be angry and fall in love and murder and forgive, to be greedy, and to be a part of another's redemption. I want to share my romantic side with people who know me and with those I have yet to meet.After I'm gone, a bibliophile perusing my library will know a part of me. A friend would say, "Terry loved his books." A lover would say, "This library is a small part of him." To me, a book holds words like a woman holds love.My library: a look inside my head for others. For me, a walk around my universe. Come with me. You. Are. Invited. terrybanker com

title: its OK!!! thats it
body: I bought these as gifts for my children so the little things they don't care about. so here is a review from an adult who is avidly into electronics and android in general.I did not expect much from these tablets, and I feel that Amazon believes that people will pick these up strictly for reading books and a secondary for gaming. but if you are looking to get this specific tablet for #1 gaming and social media, think again and spend the extra money to get one worthy of being called a tablet. this tablet should be called an E-Reader Plus... so I will just call it a Fire.this specific Fire, has expandable memory which on paper sounds great, but when it has to perform, the Fire is lacking. moving apps to an SD card is a great idea to save space on this small 8GB tablet (which is barely enough for a few movies and games) but when apps are moved to the SD card on this device, it takes a boat load more time to start up. the OS starts up normally, but the apps that are moved to the SD card take another 5 minutes or so to be able to use them. they remain greyed out on your screen for a while until the OS finishes reading the SD card, until then, apps are unusable.the Google plays store is not on the Fire anymore and being locked to just the android store is going out of the scope of what android stands for, open source and the freedom to do with it as I wish, with this tablet you are pretty much locked to the amazon market away from your friend Google. the Fire team is highly INCOMPATIBLE with Google apps, which is absurd considering Google has been leading the Android game for quite some time now, and is a big ballsy move to trail off on your own. it makes me think as if a team of people went hiking, and only 1 person didn't know how to survive in the wild, that person decided to break off from the group and have their own adventure. surely they will die, it may not be soon, but they will die if they don't know what they are doing. which is how it seems with Amazon.there are ways to get the Google play store on your Fire without rooting it, but even when you get it on, it does not play nice with Google specific apps, Youtube in particular. Youtube on the Fire is hit or miss as to whether it will load anything or not, and with the random logging me out and not playing content, its just easier to go strait to youtube and gave it work with no issues. if you search for Youtube in the Amazon app store, it provides you with a link that directs to the website and does not contain a stand alone app that is built for an android tablet.if I was to purchase the Amazon Fire specifically for myself, I would have returned it immediately and saved for a tablet and stayed away from the fire line forever because this one put a bad taste in my mouth, 100% because of the OS. the UI is cluttered with an unfamiliar feel and in my opinion is bulky and can be very confusing at first. its a tabbed system, and you cannot get rid of it unless you root the device and flash something like cyanogenmod. and even then, this tablet may not work 100% properly.my children seem to love it for what it is, because all they see is that they have a tablet, and they are at a phase where they believe that the internet in all its glory and its bottomless source of information is comprised of only 1 thing....MINECRAFT. to them, minecraft is the internet and the internet is minecraft. so when they start venturing out of that bubble, I will be right there to let them know that there is better out there.GREAT kid tablet 10 and under. terrible adult tablet. unless your a baller on a budget

title: I really wanted to love this device.  I had to send it back, but I do like it.
body: Here's what I would do differently for version 2.0 (or further down the line) to make me buy it again. (I returned mine after 20 days)1) the form factor doesn't feel right in my hands. It could weigh a bit more and feel a bit more solid (think iphone but at the larger size) in my hands.2) the cover doesn't attach well and easily detaches when manipulating the device. The cover is a good idea but needs better material and better attachment to the device3) as for the functioning of the device it is way to easy to hit the "next page" button. So for the next version it seems like this should be on the front surface not extending to the side or just on the side (without the slanting surface) I like the fact that those buttons were so big however. this was useful.4) the keyboard one would use relatively little and I would hide it behind a bigger screen in version 2.0 so that you get more reading area and then a slide out keyboard of the same variety would be accessible for web search, book purchase etc.5) refresh rate page to page is slow. I'm not the fastest reader in the world so to me it matters less than the real speed demons but it was noticeable.6) I liked the no-subscription, instantaneous wireless downloading of ebooks. This worked very nicely and seamlessly without trouble. We should be able to access this content on a Kindle emulator on a PC or Mac in the future (right now the 3 books I bought are sitting on my account and can't be accessed since I don't have the kindle anymore)7) I would like to see better navigation through the books with a better idea of where you are in a book besides the filled dots on the bottom of the screen. A bigger screen may allow for chapter marks and section marks and interval marks to be present and usable to navigate directly and quickly to particular sections.8) in an ideal world the device would have a color screen that is 8.5X 11 in size with ability to render pdfs (I am a a scientist and thrive on reading papers in PDF format) If I could carry my scientific bookshelf with me in the form of a "clipboard" size device that has Kindle functionality, I would pay $600-$800 for it (in reality by the time all of these improvements come around, the price point should be back down to $300).9) I probably wouldn't need all of the improvements listed above to pull the trigger again and actually keep the device, but in sum, its got to feel better in my hands and the navigation could be easily and quickly improved and then I may reconsider.All in all, my reading experience of Snow Crash, the 4 hour Work week, and The Last Lecture were all pretty good though figures didn't render ideally (charts in the 4 hour work week were illegible) This is a very good 1.0 product. I love the idea of clearing space in my house of all the destroyed and processed trees. And I love the idea of carrying my bookshelf with me. Not sure most would be able to justify $359 for it, especially since it's a vehicle for paying for virtual content. The price point needs to be better for Amazon to get a larger market share I would think. If they want to sell more ebooks they should take the hit on the device a bit more. I would say as is the device resembles something that should run for around $179. Also it would be great to be able "lend" books to friends for short periods of time. Before I continue my stream of consciousness thoughts on the device I will stop. Those are just my biased opinions on the whole thing so take it for what it is.

title: Buy the warranty for this device/Wi Fi connection is a must have in order to work properly.
body: Had my Fire HDX 7" 64 GB die and contacted customer support. It could not be fixed. I decided to go with this cheap version with the knowledge that it was lower resolution and specs. Received it yesterday and upon starting the first thing I noticed there was a dead pixel on the Fire start up screen, in addition there is a glow from the middle bottom of the screen when starting up (this is normal as my old version did the same), that went away when booted up. The next issue I had was with an 8GB model I added a 32GB SD card. I downloaded two to three apps and it appears that it was added to the SD card, great no problem there. When I added the first app before the other ones and during play (a drag racing game) in the middle of a race, for no reason the device crashed. I had to hit the power button and it fired up fine except for the dead pixel. I played it extensively till the battery was down to 30%, Note it arrived with 70% and I charged it while it was updating to the new OS5 to 100% before doing anything with this tablet. Seeing that this only has a 90 day warranty I decided that I would contact customer service who will be replacing this device because of the problems. I will be buying the extended warranty when I receive my replacement and hope the replacement does not have any problems. I will update this review once I receive the replacement. Several reviews have stated they have had this pixel problem with their device, I hope my replacement doesn't have this problem. Again buy the warranty! Update 2/5/16: Received my replacement, no pixel problem. I put in my blank 32GB micro card, downloaded my favorite apps to the card. Put it through intensive game play, device crashed one time and hasn't happened again. Now for the downside to this tablet, even though it does download the apps to your storage card it still uses up space on the paltry 8GB hard drive. Worst part is I was going out of town for business and downloaded about 100 songs to listen to, no it won't download songs to your storage card so I had to delete them. If connected to Wi Fi, you can always stream them but Wi Fi is not compatible on Boingo (Delta airlines) so even though I connected to the Wi Fi it would not let me stream my music. Even worse is because the WI Fi did not work, the majority of my apps would not work when in the air. If I wanted to watch delta studio it would not work even using the Silk browser, an error message "browser is not compatible". Very disappointed because I never had this problem with my old 7" HDX. For $50.00 it works great if you are connected to WI Fi. I have downloaded 39 apps to this device using the micro card. This is not including the apps pre loaded to this device. My internal storage is 3.42GB free of 5.62, so the system uses 2.38GB. In reading about the storage to micro SD in app purchases are stored to the internal storage, so much for the underground apps with free in app purchases. It states movies and music can be downloaded to your card if it is your profile. Using my profile I downloaded one TV show and one movie but my internal storage dropped from 3.42GB to 3.37GB? When checking my storage card it shows 1.81GB for apps and games (39 apps downloaded to card) and one Movie downloaded under Movies and TV shows 1 item with 756MB used and listed under Others I think this is where the TV episode loaded showed 839MB. Bottom line if you like a lot of movies, and TV shows, this device may hold up. Music is questionable at best. This Fire tablet is what it is, an inexpensive tablet with several issues.

title: Great to read, but problems with features
body: Overall, I think it shows that eBooks have the potential to be my choice for the only way to read. If the Kindle had all the books I want, and the freedom to take them with me, I'd have no serious complaints.Big PlusesIt's very readable. Since I got it a few weeks ago, I've been reading it a lot, and I find it at least as readable as the average paperback. I'm over 50, and read for hours at a time, and feel no strain.Wireless download. This is extremely convenient, as having to dock the device to some sort of network would be a pain. Personally, I'd be happy with WiFi, but I can see the value of this solution. However, if they start charging for it, I'll certainly want to see a WiFi solution.You can download all the free books from Project Gutenberg (there are 25,000). I've read some on a computer screen, which is painful: this is fantastic.Big MinusesSelection. There are 5.8 million "Books in Print." Amazon probably has more than this available, with used books. The Kindle has 130,000, so on looking for a specific book, figure on a less than 1 in 50 chance of finding it. And for me, the ideal use of the Kindle would be to load up series of books that I've reread over the years, like Rex Stout's Nero Wolf, Dorothy Sayers' Peter Wimsey. But they're largely unavailable. There are only 4 of 60+ Wolf, and 1 of 10 or so Wimsey books. No Lord of the Rings. Of course, your luck may be different.Pricing/Rights. Pick a hardcover release, like David Sedaris's new book. It's $14.29 in hardcover. The Kindle edition is $9.99, which sounds okay. But then you have to factor the dreaded Digital Rights Management (DRM) into the equation. If I buy the hardcover release for $14.29, or wait a while and buy the paperback for $7.99, I can keep it if I buy a new bookshelf. I can even sell it to a used bookshop. But once I've spent $9.99 on a Kindle book, I'll have to have a Kindle as long as I want to read that book. This is a great deal for Amazon, not so good for me. I'll also mention that it's a little irritating that the Amazon site shows the Kindle price, "$9.99, you save $16," even though you can flip to the hardcover edition with one click and get it for $14.29, which is $2.30 more. The "save $16" is versus some imaginary price.Small PlusesLookup. I use lookup all the time: it could be a trifle more convenient, but being able to just click a couple of clicks and look up a word is fantastic.
