I probably use it every 15 minutes or so, usually because I'm reading something maybe a little old, and I wonder about the use of some word in a specific context. I love it.Small MinusesThe wireless network is either poor, or gives lowest priority to Kindle users. I often have plenty of bars of signal, but get a message, "Can't connect at this time, please try again later."It's easy to subscribe to a magazine right from the Kindle for the "14 day free trial," but as far as I can tell, the only way to cancel is to log in to amazon.com via computer.The "screen saver" pops up with whatever nonsense the developers have decided is cute, or helps make the Kindle look desirable. I can control the screen saver on my computer, even my mobile: why not on the Kindle.The User's Guide says you can change your dictionary (for Lookup), but there are no other dictionaries available from Amazon, and I haven't been able to get it to accept Project Gutenberg's Webster as a dictionary.

title: A hopeful look at the future
body: The Kindle is a great device. I use it all the time. But I'm looking forward to Kindle.2 and beyond because it needs a lot of tweaking. Here's my critique.It is hard to hold. The pages are always getting turned by accident. Such large page turners probably aren't necessary or advisable. And the way the fastener is designed on the supplied book cover is so primitive and design-flawed, it's a joke. Pick it up upside down and it falls out. Not good.By far my biggest complaint is that it is not backlit. So you have to be prepared in any low light situation to haul around a cumbersome lighting device. And good luck fastening the lamp just so, so it doesn't fall off as you move around. And the lighting device causes almost completely unavoidable glare. The designers should take a tip from the PDA e-reader's. It can't be that difficult to figure out how to build it with a self contained light system, so you can, say, read in bed without bothering your spouse by either lighting up the whole room or fumbling around with an awkward mini-light in the dark. This is a major complaint. I would not consider purchasing the next model if this problem is not addressed, whereas if it were fixed I might go out tomorrow and get one just for this single improvement.The keyboard is a good deal less than ergonomically designed.It is, to be blunt, ugly. Why so drab? I'd like my next one in red or maybe yellow.It could take a few pointers, again, from e-reader compatible devices, like PDA's, and start working on changing more than just the font size, but the color and background, as hard as that may be with paperless ink technology. I have been reading gold type on black background for years and personally find it preferable to the black on drab grey.And it won't "scroll", which also may be hard to accomplish, but it is great to have a hands free device with adjustable scroll speeds.The dedicated dictionary is somewhat limited. A really good unabridged dictionary would be nice. And if you want to look up a word that isn't part of the text you are reading, as near as I can tell, you can't. That's not good.The location numbers, as opposed to page numbers, are totally indecipherable and as near as I can tell completely useless. If they are reference points, how do you get back to the point of reference? If they tell you where you are in the book, how do you translate them? Why not just have page numbers at a chosen font size, then you can tell with precision where you are at in the book? Of course that would require knowing the total number of pages, also not now available.Will I be able to get books in Spanish, German, French, etc.? Will there be a dedicated dictionary available as in English? Right now the answer seems to be "no." Hopefully this will be addressed.On the plus side, the device is a good size, the screen size is perfect, the font selections are quite adequatewhispernet is terrific, the selection of materials is quite good and getting better, and the prices are pretty good. Not having to download or otherwise be tethered to a PC is also a distinct advantage.Despite my criticisms the Kindle has become my constant companion. On balance and with the above caveats, I would recommend it to anyone, with the exception perhaps of those who just want to look at the color photographs - because there won't be any. I hope some of these issues are addressed, most particularly the lighting issue.

title: I hope you speak Chinese to set things up
body: First, it came, and started up in, Chinese charters/symbols. It is hard to set up something when you can not read the questions and instructions. To help out, swipe a finger down from the top, and select the gear that appears in the lower right hand corner of the menu that opens up. Then look for the globe icon on the left hand side - it is a light gray icon on a black background, and it is the last selection in the the section before the end.Next, the calendar, contacts, and email, are all linked together. I prefer setting each up independently, as I have an email set up for junk and spam, yet it is my calendar. And since these apps are bundled with the tablet you can't just remove them. Rest assured, I know at some point someone will post instructions on how to root the device, and remove the bloatware.The USB charging/syncing cord is very short. Because of this I have tried using another USB cord, and found out that the tablet doesn't like it. I plugged one in to my computer's USB 3.0 port, and nothing. After grabbing the custom cable that came with it, and then I was able to set up the tablet as a media device, and transfer files.This brings up another issue - you can not move downloaded content from a web browser's download folder on the device to the MicroSD card directly. You would have to move it to a computer, or anther device, then back again, to get the content onto the SD card. It would be easier, and quicker, just to download the content on your computer, plug in the included USB cable, and transfer files that way..Ok, so much for the shortcomings. I have a Google Nexus 7 (first generation), and like it, yet have noticed it's shortcomings. The biggest one is memory, or lack of the ability to expand it. This Fire Tablet can be expanded with a 128 GB MicroSD card - sweet music to my ears. If you want to read a 128 GB MicroSD card you might need a new reader - the one I purchased a couple years ago can only see 23 GB, yet I think the newer ones will see the full 128 GB (will update this in a couple days).I don't have to download and install the Kindle app on my Nexus, and it automatically updates my library. It has a number of main home screens, such as Recent, Home, Books, Apps, Newsstand, etc.So far it is doing mostly what I want it to do. And now that I can move apps, or at least a large part of each one, to a MicroSD card so I don't have to worry about the bloat included (very much). And I am sure that at some point I'll be ripping that out. And yes, the idea of being able to use a 128 MicroSD card in this is wonderful - I have already transferred a number of videos to sdcard1. It works nicely, is quicker than my first generation Google Nexus 7" tablet. This seems to be a natural progression, and at a lower price. Yes, I did pay $65 so I don't get bombarded with advertisements. If I wanted to I could retire my Nexus 7, and use the case with the Fire Tablet.Pros: Can handle a 128 GB MicroSD card, Amazon's name, PriceCons: Email, contacts, and calendar, are all tied together, Can't readily managed your own personal content, such as downloads, by moving from tablet to external storage, Need to use the included USB cord if you want to charge the tablet properly, and connect to a computer as a media device.

title: The truth lies elsewhere
body: I will preface this review by saying that I do not have one yet, so I cannot (and will not) address the issues that require me to use it. It seems like most of the reviews here either love it or hate and they both seem remarkably ignorant. Many of the people who have used it and written about how much they love it have obviously never used the Sony Reader, or they wouldn't be saying such stupid things. No, the ability to read books this way is not revolutionary; it's been around a few years now. They remind me of the yokels back in 1990 who said Windows 3 was a "wonder" because you double-click to open files (yeah, right--Mac user had been doing that for five years already). Yes, it is great, but I want to here from someone who already has and uses a Sony Reader or similar device for an honest comparison with those devices that are already available.Even more idiotic are the folks who object to it because "you have to carry another device." Let's see if I can explain this in terms you'll understand. Do you carry around books with you? If the answer is yes, then this is an alternative that lightens your load. If the answer is no, then you don't really read anyway, so why are you bothering to even talk about this device?The same goes for the people who want an LCD screen, presumably so they can read in a dark room. Anyone who said that also obviously does not read much. If you did, I wouldn't need to tell you how hard on the eyes reading from an LCD screen can be when compared to reading from a reflective screen (or paper).Now, for my personal observations:Screen: Same as the new Sony Reader PRS-505, i.e., much nicer than previous readers. For the people who want color, right now the only way I could see that working is if the display operated like the ones on the OLPC PCs where, when you turn the brightness all the way down, it converts to non-backlit grayscale (hey, that's not a bad idea come to think of it).Design: Butt ugly. There are many aspects to the design that smack of creeping elegance, so I feel for the people who had to design it.Formats: This is where it gets tricky. It is mandatory that a reader can read text files. PDF files I also consider mandatory (and here is where the Sony Reader falls down--you can open them, but if they are not formatted for smaller pages, you sure won't be able to read them). It looks like the Kindle needs more compatibility to me, but I will wait until I use it to give my final opinion on this.Price: Still too high. I agree with the others here that say that the price point for an ereader needs to hit $99 before they'll become popular. Even at $299, you will see the item picking up steam.Connectivity: The wireless feature has some distinct advantages. Yes, there is a proprietary aspect to it, but at least it doesn't limit you to a Windows environment like the Sony reader does.Keyboard: I can see this is actually a useful feature for two reasons. The first is the ability to use a dictionary easily. You need a keyboard for that, and a dictionary is useful when reading certain authors. The second reason is that it may allow the addition of things like crossword puzzles and sudoku to the device. On the downside, the keyboard certainly contributes to the ugliness factor.

title: A Real Review of The Amazon Tablet 7 (not HD)
body: So after a year+ owning this tablet, I have come to the conclusion that is a solid 3 star compared to the 4 star i gave it. For the price, its well more than worth it. You cannot get a 7in tablet anywhere that works as well as this one for the price range. But, here comes the bad part.-With the low price point Amazon makes up the rest in advertisements on your display screen and other various apps. It gets very annoying when you accidentally click on this ads and it sends the tablet searching for these products you dont care about.-The screen feels really sticky all the time making it extremely hard to swipe the right way at times. This can be a good and a bad for different people.-The tablet will randomly turn off and on during a self update. I have the self update turned off, yet it still updates by itself.-The Wi-fi isnt as strong as I expected to be. My wifi box is no more than 30 feet away and it struggles to keep the connection. My cell gets my wifi connection on the street of my house which is a good 70-100 feet give or take.-Amazon installs bloatware into you tablet with or without your permission, the worst thing is you cannot delete them. I only use 3 apps from amazon and that is the music (rarely use) Amazon Video and Amazon underground. I have about 4 gigs of unnecessary apps that i don't ever use or need. Thats 4 gigs of videos. pictures, and songs i could store.-Has a hard time reading my SD card (30 gig with less that 2gb of used data) upon restart. Sometimes it randomly desync the memory card causing me to either restart the tablet or itself will restart on its own.-There is too man home screens to look at. You have your recent, home, music, book, apps, and its a clutter after that of god knows what.-The tablet will not let you download anything google which makes it hard for me to exchange files. I have to download different types of apps and find new ways to transfer a file size less than a MB.Aside from all the bad things i dislike about the tablet, there are things I do like:-IF you have Amazon Prime, this tablet shines a little more (though that is roughly 30-60 dollars every 6 months).-All Amazon apps is optimized the tablet. that means there is no hiccups, very little delay (dated hardware), and quite enjoyable experience.-When all tablets get an upgrade, you get one too. This means that a Fire 7 will get the same patch as Fire HD 10, putting you on the same playing field.-The battery life is very fair and last a long time. Ive went months without charging this tablet and when i charged it up, it still had the same reliability as before. Goes about 2 weeks one 1 charge connected to the wifi untouched and about 6 hours of hard usage (literally wont let it sleep). I use this tablet for no more than 4 hours a week, which lasts me about a good 3-4 days without charge.-Quite durable. I dropped it from about 3-5 feet at items. no damage yet.This tablet is Fair. For $50 it is a good price for replacements, gifts, and the basic needs of a smart tablet. However if you use gmail on a daily basis, i think getting a regular tablet that supports Google would be better. If anything you are still on a budget, you can jailbreak this tablet to run google apps, however you void the warranty.

title: Great tablet for the price. But remember, you get what you pay for!!!
body: First let me say that I have owned, and still own, every Kindle Fire tablet! I've had the new Fire tablet for about 24 hours now. So, here are my first impressions:Pros:The price!!! You are getting a fully functioning Android tablet with full benefits of Amazon for $50. That is Amazing!!!The other thing I want to point out is, the tablet does not feel like a cheap tablet. You can go online right now and purchase many Android tablets for $50 and under, but all of these will feel like a toy, rather then an actual portable device. Amazon Fire, actually feels like a quality mobile device.Finally, there is an expansion card slot. Now, you can have all the space your heart desires.Misses (These are not cons or pros)The new OS is that: new. It is easy to navigate, and I do not have any specific complaints, it is just different. I guess for a new Kindle user, it will be easy to get used to. But for someone who is used to the previous version of their OS, the new interface feels strange. To me, Amazon is getting closer what other Android tablets look like. And I, personally, liked (enjoyed) Amazon's custom OS interface.ConsRemember, you get what you pay for: simply scrolling through the home screen, the tablet would hang for several seconds. It is not as smooth as their more expensive versions. (Again, this is exactly what I would have expected from a $50 tablet). But with that said, Minecraft played without any lag or lockups. I was extremely impressed with the quality of game play on it.Installing apps: So, if you are planning on installing anything on this tablet, expect to wait a long long time. Simple apps, such as Facebook messenger, took 10 minutes to install. Not download, but to actually install. It is very slow to do any kind of installation.System updates; just like with app installation, system update took forever! What I found interesting was how quickly this process worked on my previous generation of Fire.Screen: I put my previous 6" Fire next to this one and it is like night and day. This Kindle Fire is a lot dimmer. The screen is not as vibrant. (Again, this is exactly what I would expect for $50)Sound: I have no words!!! The sound quality is just plain terrible! If you are planning to use this tablet to listen to music, either invest in headphones or just don't buy it!!! The sound if very quiet. Even on the loudest setting, it is very hard to hear the sound.Conclusion:You get what you pay for: For a $50, this is a great tablet!!! Worth every penny!!! The reason, I have many tablet was because I have young kids, who I want reading. But, they are kids and they are not at that age, where they are careful with electronics. So, Kindle Fire tablets are a lot cheaper than iPads. And for $50, Amazon is doing a heck of a job to accommodate parents like myself. With that said, I will say that if you are planning to use this tablet for anything more than reading and basic web surfing, spend a little more and purchase a little more expensive model. As much as I want to think that the cons are acceptable, they are not! I, myself, use Kindle Fire HDX and there is a huge difference between the two,Overall, for $50, this tablet makes a great gift and somewhat of a disposable device.

title: Two in three years ain't bad.... righ?
body: I got mine on Christmas from my family. I've always been a techie but never liked tablets due to the limitations and spending $300-500 to "try" one out didn't really fit with me. So when I got this and I knew it was $49.99 I was skeptical. However after using it for a week I an completely impressed. I've used tablets before and I have to say for the price this feels every bit as good and well built as higher end expensive tablets.Pros:PriceAndroid OSEase of use right out of the boxCons:No google playBattery life when in use can be shortMost apps are useless or fakes or they costSilk browserYes it has some cons but those are more personal preference outside of battery life. It's still a 5 start product because at $49.99 something like this should feel cheap and operate cheap, but it doesn't It feels and operates like I spent $250. That to me is an extremely good value.To those reviewing and giving bad rates. Learn to use the product entirely first. Many of the reviews that claim it to be crappy or not working is because the user isn't using it properly. Take time to use the device. Use the internet to search for tips and tricks. It's easy to spot the reviews of people who just used it for 5 minutes.**update 01-07-17**I'm on my second one since I got this for xmas 2015. About May or June it stopped working completely and they send me a new one via the warranty. It was a quick and easy process. Now right after xmas 2016 I started having the same issue. The charging port didn't always charge or have contact with the charging cable. I believe the issue is the very stiff and short data/charging cable that is supplied. So after talking with support I was informed that I only had a 90 day warranty on the new tablet and that a new one didn't carry over to the new tablet. To me that makes no sense. Needless to say I can't get a new one for free even though it's the same issue. Not good service on that end and not good quality of the tablet. I gave it 5 stars originally because I didn't have issues but that is dropping to 3 stars due to everything that's happened in 1 year of using the tablet.**update 11-11-18**So here we are again.... I sit here now with a dead tablet for no real reason that I can understand or gather. Kept a charge like new. Wasn't unexpectedly just shutting off like my last one. But for some reason when I woke up it was dead. I did use it to play music via the iheartradio app. I understand it's a $40 device but come on things should last if taken care of. Dropped down to 2 stars because of this.**update 01-06-19**Got another one for xmas of 2018. Seems to work well so far. They did make the charger cable better and it feels like it's of better quality. Hopefully it lasts.**update 02-19-20**It's over a year now and it's still going strong. No issues or warnings signs of failure. I am however upgrading to the Fire 8 HD due to it being super cheap. I did notice that they removed the 1 year warranty in place for a 90 day limit warranty(unless I read it wrong). If this is true that's a little disappointing.

title: As a streaming only device 5 stars, but 3 stars due to SD card issues.
body: Update April 2016This tablet has issues with SD cards, even if you are using the official ones sold by Amazon as I am. Based on what I have read online it is an issue with many if not all of these tablets. Sometimes the tablet will not recognize the sd card and you have to reset the tablet. Then once reset it takes several minutes to find the sd card. This is a big deal because the whole reason I bought this tablet is to add an SD card so I could store content for long car rides and plane trips. - Thus the 3 starsHowever as a streaming tablet I would still give this 5 stars for the price. Battery life is very good and would still consider the screen good enoughOld ReviewSome of this review will compare this tablet to an Ipad mini 2. I actually sold my ipad mini 2 and bought this tablet because I never used it and it did not have enough storage space for movies which I wanted to load for my kid on long car trips and airplane trips.Screen- The screen is not as good as an Ipad Retena its more on par with the original ipad mini. However, it is good enough for my young kids.Interface- You have access to the same andriod apps that a phone has as well as pre-loaded amazon apps. I downloaded several kids apps and movies in the amazon app store all of which worked fine.Overall this is a great tablet for young kids and cheep enough that if it only last 1 or 2 years who really cares. If I were still a tablet user myself I would not recommend this tablet. However, I bought an ipad and never really used in anyway so I was never really the tablet audience.Negatives (and the 4 star reviews)- I had a 3 year old 32Gb memory card left over from an older android tablet I had which did not work very well in this tablet. I have since bought the official one from amazon which was not expensive. I have not yet used it so I will up date this review later.- The interface can be slow and buggy, especially when you first get the tablet. Its annoying enough that I would not want to use this tablet personally. However kids apps and the amazon movie app work fine once you get past the initial slowness.- The disney app will not let you download movies to the device as it will on the ipad. This is annoying as I own a bunch of movies on there I was hoping to download for my kid to watch on an airplane ride. Movies on the amazon video app download just fine however and you can still stream videos on the disney app.- As a whole amazon has way too much loaded on this device. There are all menues and apps built in for all of the things amazon offers. Like shopping, movies, books, music etc. This has the advantage of making it easy to access your amazon content, but it without a doubt slows this tablet down.Overall this is a great table for kids, and I am happy with my purchase. I would also say that its worth up top 50 bucks (I got it for 35 bucks which is a steel.) However its no Ipad (which is an amazing tablet but far too expensive for one with a decent amount of storage space. 16 Gb is worthless these days).

title: Updated - Using as home automation controllers
body: Update:Unfortunately, I am sending them all back. Pretty much everything I said below is still true, but for the apps I use, it's not working out quite like I need. I need to be able to wake up the device and select an app, like my thermostat app, and have the app open without having to log in each time - I can currently do this on my phone as well as my other Samsung Tabs. The Kindle not only logs out of the apps, but when I do try to log back in it will not connect and gives an error message. It does this with most of the home automation/surveillance apps and the only way to get them to work is to restart the device. I found I had to do this every couple of days.I need it to operate sort of like a remote - press a button, make something happen, in seconds not minutes. But having to restart the device and enter in user and password information every couple of days is not useful for me.Original:It's not too bad.I ordered the special - buy 5 get one free.I bought them specifically to control automation devices in my home, not really for anything else. They seemed to fit the bill because they were super cheap and ran android. I currently have two Samsung tablets that I use, but needed something cheaper to have one in every room. I am using them to run Insteon devices through Mobilinc app, my thermostat through Ecobee app, view my surveillance cameras through Meye and Samsung Smartcam app, monitor my weather stations through Acculink app, and specialize functions using Tasker app. Most of these apps are only on the Google play store, but I knew that ahead of time. When I got the Kindle I quickly sideloaded the apps I needed. The only one I haven't figured out yet is Pushbullet because it requires Google Play Services to actually run. Also, Mobilinc needs an APK file specific to the Kindle, so I had to go back to the app developer to request it. The Kindles work fine for what I use them for and for the price. You really can't beat the price. I know the Samsung tabs are in a different price bracket, but if you have used them before, you may be a little disappointed in the look of the screen. It's not quite as crisp and bright as the Samsung, but it is not that bad. The Kindle is also compact and mounts nicely to the wall.Even though I knew I would have to sideload most of the apps since they weren't available in the Amazon store, I would be a little frustrated if I were using this tablet for general use with not having access to the Google play store. One item thing I don't like is the home screen. It has bunch of Amazon apps that are preloaded on the device and I haven't figured out how to remove them. I'm not sure yet if you can or cannot. I have my Samsung tabs set up with only the automation apps I use on the home screen. This way it looks like a custom touchscreen home remote. It would be nice to do this with the Kindles.All in all, I am satisfied with the Kindle for my purposes. The low price allows me to have these throughout the house.

title: Impressions without trying the device
body: I currently use both mobipocket reader and MSreader on a pocket PC to read ebooks. The Kindle looks interesting, not sure if I will try it or not, but here are a few thoughts.1. My problem with ebook readers in general is content availability. Like a PC or a gaming platform, it is content that matters- what software/ebooks are available is critical since it is what you can read that makes the device useable. I continue to be disappointed when I go to the Award winning book sections of online book stores and find that a small minority of highly rated books are available in ebook format. Sometimes when they are, they are at hard cover prices even after the paperback version is available for a fraction of the cost. The Kindle seems to be trying to address this with the New York Times best seller list available at paperback prices. Now add the past 20 years of prize winners and runner ups...2. What I like about my pocketPC reading experience is that I can read in bed without any other light source, and I can carry it in my pocket so I always have it with me. This means that when I have to wait for a plane, appointment, etc, I can pull it out and read. The Kindle looks like it will need another light source to read and is too big to fit in a pocket. The advantage of being bigger is the text size, the disadvantage is portability and probably an increase chance that you will leave it somewhere by accident. With a $10 paperback book that is not a big deal, with a $400 device...3. I am dreaming of a Kindle like device, with a rollup screen that would be both more portable and have the advantage of the larger size. Wonder if we will see that in the next 10 years...4. I wonder if it will be able to keep a few issues of the magazines/papers in the device or whether it overwrites the current one... I would like to keep at least a month of the weekly magazines online, and probably a week of a daily paper. The ability to save an issue or article from these would be nice as well, in case you do not have time to read it right now. Adding the New Yorker Magazine might be enough for me to take the plunge if it could keep a few issues online. Also, I would want it to allow me to set an auto delete date for downloadable magazines/newspapers so that I do not have to spend time deleting the ones over a month old.5. The last item may be uncommon enough not to be important for sales, but I use Avantgo to supply reading material for continuing education, which comes with questions that I respond to, which is then tracked. That works on the PocketPC fine, I wonder if Kindle will support this type of use?I will be closing watching content availability for this. Still worried that the size will mean it is mostly a use at home device or it will be a pain to keep track of on the road unless I am on a trip with a travel bag.Does it fit in a suit coat pocket?PDF support, as mentioned in other posts should be added as well.Ross

title: This is less a rating than a wish list
body: I have a confession to make. I have not yet used the kindle. But I can tell from the online information that I have a wish list of items for Amazon to consider.Item 1 - I probably have hundreds of documents written in Microsoft Word that I would wish to convert. I really don't want to go through Amazon for every conversion in order to store it on my kindle. There are things that I don't want them to save on their servers or even see. So I am sugggesting that they offer the tools for me to sutomatically convert Word documents to Kindle format and a download line, perhaps via USB for me to load my own document.Item 2 - I have a massive library of technical books that I have already bought from Amazon that I would dearly love to have in this format. But paying even a quarter of their original purchase price in order for the convenience would be cost prohibitive. It is not just popular reading that people would like to have in this format. I would like Amazon to come up with a way to convert previous purchases from Amazon into Kindle Format at a reasonable price so that I could keep my library in Kindle format.Item 3 - You mention add-in memory cards as a way to store more books. I sure hope this works, because my technical library alone could take up that room. I'd like to be able to switch in and out memory cards holding different libraries of books or genres. That is likely how I would use the device.Item 4 - Kindle is electronic paper, but have you made provisions for vision impaired persons, e.g. could you add a book reader? I know that this would shorten battery time, but it would make it more widely useable.Item 5 - You mention the ability to mark passages in books that you want to remember. I could envision using this as a research tool. But to make it a viable research tool it would be necessary for a person to download the marked passages to their personal computer complete with footnotes, to be used as reference material.Item 6 - I would love to see you make deals with text book manufacturers to put their textbooks in this format. I recently took an accounting course where the textbook was well ove two inches thick. It weighed so much that I could not hold it up to read it! I happen to have a back problem, caused by poor posture. Reading that textbook did not help because I was always slouched over to read it. I am not blaming the textbook manufacturer for my bck problems, but I can see how the Kindle could help to prevent this.Using Kindle or devices like it could save a lot of trees and lower the cost of books. The text books are expensive enough as it is. Any savings we could get would be a boon to education. You could create a whole new market in elementary, secondary, post secondary, and graduate education, as well as in professional books.I am not ready to buy just yet. If you address at least some of these items, I believe that you will have an even larger market share.

title: this isn't a BAD tablet, and for what I bought if for, it works (almost)
body: This is a tough one. I have bounced around with tablets for years, always returning to the iPad world due to the ease of use, quality of build, and simply because it worked. What keeps bringing me back to the Fire tablet world, however, is that is is simply more "fun" to use (I have even written my books [[ASIN:B0095IHGBY G. Eric Francis]] on iPads. However, I am a tech person, so when I heard that Amazon was coming out with a $50 7 inch tablet, I figured I'd give it a go.I have had the tablet for a week, and as my mother always told me, "You get what you pay for."Now before I begin, this isn't a BAD tablet, and for what I bought if for, it does serve its purpose well. But some of the same flaws that caused me to eventually move on from other versions of the Fire tablet brand still remain...especially the God-awful Silk browser, that no matter how often they "improve it", still hangs and is slower than Congress.The plusses:1. The price, of course. You are not going to get a non-China knockoff tablet that actually offers support for $50. Period.2. On the most part, it functions. Now I know that isn't a glowing review, but the tablet boots up in about 45 seconds or so, and so far my use of it (once I ported Chrome onto it to avoid Silk, and games, once they start up (more on that later) work fairly decently; not super smooth, but playable, surfing on a different browser is OK, and videos are sharp despite the low resolution on the tablet. 7 inches helps (forgive the pun).3. As far as I can tell, the build quality for $50 isn't bad. Doesn't feel like a kid's toy, but once again, see what my mom said once. It ain't the iPad, but for the price you shouldn't expect it to be. Looking at this in low light at different angles is OK as well.OK, now the bad news....THIS THING, AND FROM WHAT I SEE, THE ENTIRE 2015 LINE IS GROSSLY UNDERPOWERED.When I had the 2014 Fire HD 7, that was a sweet tablet. It simply, as I have said about the iPad, WORKED. Yeah it hiccuped now and then, but beyond that, it remains my favorite tablet from Amazon. This thing, and once again I know this is a $50 tablet, strains if you push it even a little. Now my view of this could be simply based on the horrid Silk browser, but playing a game as simple as "Slotomania" and you can see it get intermittently stuck trying to load it. Even side-loading Chrome drags this along at a snails pace from time to time simply scrolling down the page. Now I guess this would be acceptable because of what was paid for the tablet, but it seems that Amazon put this CPU across the board, and that is a shame. What made these so popular as an iPad alternative (besides access to the massive Prime library) was even the low end ones actually worked well for the price.This will be a backup for when the iPad is charging, but sadly that is all it will be.

title: Several Big Drawbacks to This Device
body: I'm not all that happy with the Fire. I have the original Kindle (Gen One), a Paperwhite, and 5 Kindle Keyboards, along with the new Fire. I figured it would be a nice addition to the work horses. (I am a beta reader for various authors, and I usually devote a Kindle to just one or two of them, so that I can download their entire catalogue, and just be able to grab the one I need when I need it.)The regular Kindles just always seem to work, unless I do something like forget to charge them, or am out of the Country. Dependable and reliable.That's not the case with the Fire. I assumed, when I bought it, that it would use my W-Fi to access the net, and the regular old Whispernet to access the books, like with the others. That's not true. It's all Wi-Fi, and I have a hard time getting the Fire to connect, even when I'm practically sitting right on top of the router, and my husband is happily surfing away right next to me on his "Air". I can't even seem to get into the Kindle store itself, most of the time. I can have one of my Keyboards accessing the Store, while my Fire just keeps telling me that there is no connection, and I have to "retry", which usually never works, anyway.My husband (a Tech Geek) has double checked all my settings, and so forth, and found nothing out of line. So, it's the Fire itself that is the problem.What I also don't like is the battery life. I can deplete the battery in an hour or so, and it takes a long time to recharge. Since I can't just hang out with it on the charger all of the time, that's also a major drawback. The regular Kindle batteries last for hours and hours. I usually stop reading before they deplete.What I DO like about it is the screen. It's nicer than any of the other Kindles. It has rich color, sharp delineation, and, in general, a terrific pleasure to read on. When I CAN read on it. I don't even mind the touch screen tech, which I ordinarily don't care for. I have an iPad, and don't really like that. I use a stylus for everything. The "swiping" is, actually, annoying, because, with my other Kindles (except the Paperwhite) I can basically sit unmoving, while just my thumbs turn the pages. With the touch screen tech, I have to move my whole hand, and it gets annoying. (Small quibble, I suppose. I knew what it was when I bought it.)In retrospect, I probably wouldn't buy this, despite the fact that it is so cheap now. (My husband sagely points out that there is likely a good reason for that.)So, 3 Stars, and that much only because the screen is so nice, and the relative ease of function is good (as long as the connection is made). But, the fact that I can't just take off with the Fire, and must drag along another Kindle when I leave the house is a major drawback. I thought it would relieve me from having to have a Keyboard and my iPad with me, but that's not the case.

title: It's OK
body: This tablet is more under-powered and under-featured compared to the previous Fire than I realized, even though I tried to detect what differences there were from looking at Amazon's comparison charts.The most obvious difference is the screen size. I still don't understand how both this and the Fire HD 7" (which I have) from 2014 are both listed as having 7" displays. This screen is NOT 7" - The width is about 1/4" narrower, and that makes a big difference when we're talking about small tablets. The length is the same, but with the width narrower, you have a long skinny screen. An example of how this is a let down is how things display on Facebook. The edges of most posts are cut off - you constantly have to click an image for it to open up in a new screen in order to see the full image - then you click back to the FB page. On last year's Fire HD 7, no posts were cut off on the edges like that.This doesn't automatically go into Sleep mode when using a cover with a magnet in the flap. That's a great feature with the previous Fire - This one you have to turn off and on with the switch on top.The sound is monophonic - How long has it been since you've heard anything in Mono instead of Stereo? - Your ears are very disappointed.There is no built-in Voice to Text - I really dislike typing on these things, and have grown to rely on the excellent, built-in Voice to Text available on the 2014 Fire. Looking online for a solution, I saw many people saying it's impossible to get V2T on this,- but I finally found an app which sort of fixes the problem. Sort of. The microphone icon appears, and you can struggle along speaking your messages - but it's much less reliable than the one built in to the last Fire. AND it looks like after a month, I'll need to pay for this app - don't know the price yet - better not be much, since it's sub-par.From looking at the spec comparisons before buying this, I could see that it wasn't going to be quite as fast as the Fire HD 7. The difference turned out to be Much greater than I'd expected. It's a drag to go online to look for info - it can take Forever. Expect to be staring at a blank white screen much of the time while the thing struggles to open a web page.In this feedback I've been comparing this tablet, which was on sale for $35, to the Fire HD 7 which I paid $120 for, since I got that in the 16 GB version. That evens things out a bit - It's not really fair to expect this inexpensive tablet to compare more favorably to last year's more expensive model. All I can say is that the differences were Much more pronounced than I expected - Especially that annoyingly skinny width --- How can both tablets be advertised as being the same size when they're not? That remains a mystery.It's a so-so, OK fill-in as a backup tablet. But it's definitely nothing to get very excited about.

title: Kindle Review
body: I am very satisfied with the display, which was the main reason I bought this device. It is very easy on my eyes and the ability to change font sizes is welcome. I think the keyboard is also pretty good and easy to use. The word search and dictionary work excellently for the most part. More about this later. Battery life is quite acceptable and the leather case is neat.On the negative side, however, there are a number of annoying features that I find in using the device. First, the page forward and page backward controls are much too large. This makes handling the device tricky. You need to be careful not to inadvertently hit one of these controls while just manipulating the device. These controls should be much smaller, perhaps one quarter of their present size so that there are areas available for the user to hold the device without advancing the page. Second, the on/off control is on the back. It should be on the front. It's difficult to get to without hitting one of the page advance controls inadvertently.I am not in an area that can connect wirelessly to Amazon, so I need to download to my computer first and then download to the Kindle. This is not a problem, but getting the information from your website about how to do it was not easy. You need to make sure that the computer download option and instructions are more prominently displayed on your web pages so that users who have this problem don't have to hunt around among small print for instructions.Finally, I purchased Thomas Hardy's Complete Books. When I tried to use the lookup function for word definition it didn't work. When I tried to search the text for certain words, that didn't work either. I contacted your support people and was advised that the supplier of the book had not created the file according to your specifications so that these functions didn't work. I was also told that the problem would be fixed in a few days. It has been over five weeks and the file still has not been fixed. Your people then advised me that it was 'out of their hands' since they cannot control what the supplier sends. Well, it seems that there needs to be some quality control here, not only for the issues that I mentioned, but also for content. It turns out that in this particular file there are a significant number of typos and misspellings.The typos are legitimate English words so they won't be flagged by a spell checker, but it's annoying to encounter these so frequently.(Regarding the issue of word searches and definition lookups, I took a pdf file, created a simple text file of the content and downloaded that to the Kindle, and all of the search/lookup functions worked perfectly. Perhaps this should be looked into as well.)Overall, I'm satisfied with the product, but think it should be improved. I also hope that I don't have similar problems with content in future e-book purchases.

title: Likeable but terrible ergonomics
body: The Kindle is easier to adapt to than I thought it would be. It is a little tedious clicking buttons but after a while it becomes second nature just as turning pages. It's easy to read but does require room lighting - you cannot read it in the dark. The Kindle is nothing if not a conversation starter. Everywhere I go it attracts attention, especially among professional travelers. Convenience is the Kindle's strong suit. But ergonomics isn't.There are some serious design problems with this thing that I think would have been vetted had Amazon done a more thorough test marketing. The buttons are huge and mounted on the edge making it impossible to handle it without accidentally flipping pages. The power and Whisper Net buttons are on the back which is a hassle if you use the cover, and you have to use the cover to have something to hold without gripping the sides and hitting a page button. You can't even flip the thing over to turn it off without hitting a page button.I have suggestions for improvement. Remove the entire keyboard and move it to a touchscreen (like the iPhone) which would allow for a larger screen. Move the On/Off and Whisper Net buttons to the front and recess them, and most importantly put small rocker switches on the front so the unit can be gripped without hitting a page button. I would also take the rubber backing on the rear and put some on the front where the keyboard is now. I would have preferred a cover that used velcro to hold it and, and a flap door in the cover for access to the on/off buttons would have been nice too.Other than that, it is likeable, and you will get used to it. The download times are very good (30 seconds or so) and I have had my Kindle in a lot of locations and get good signal everywhere. The Kindle Store is easy to use and a minimum of hassle. I have been disappointed in the selection. The first 5 books I wanted to buy (all new releases) were not available when the books were released, but in the past 10 days or so a couple have appeared in Kindle format, and I suppose this only improves over time.I don't regret buying it at all, and if the ergonomics were better I would have given it a five star rating - I think the next version will deal with those problems so if you are not in a rush you might wait. If they do make these changes a lot of people will upgrade and you can pick up a used original model cheap. Or keep the original as a bit of history because this product is ground breaking. College kids can buy and carry every book they need in the palm of their hand. If I were Barnes and Noble I'd be worried because this is a book store killer. It won't win over the hearts and minds of the Boomer generation, but those who follow will adapt as readily as they did to cell phones.

title: Great for Prime members but not so good for teenagers
body: I bought two of these 7" tablets for my children and the 10" for me. They are fun to use but there are limitations.These tablets are very affordable and designed to be an easy way to use the benefits of prime. For adults they are a great way to access amazon prime videos and music. If you also subscribe to kindle unlimited these tablets give you great access to those benefits too. You can also read and respond to email and access internet through amazon's silk browser. And the app selection on Amazon underground seems plentiful and full of free apps.If you use the children's profiles be aware that access to prime videos and music is not available on these profiles. You'll need to allow access into your own profile if you want your children to watch a free prime video or listen to the free music - this solution works for me because I'm comfortable with my children seeing what I've been reading and watching and I just enable the parental controls to stop them from doing anything else while on my profile. Their own profiles (called freetime) allow them to play games, read books, watch purchased videos and access a few limited web sites.These tablets do not easily support anything google related other than gmail. If you're good with technology I think there are ways around this problem but if you're tech skills are limited and you want to use google chrome, your google account and the google app store you'll need a different tablet.These tablets are useless for teenagers!!!If give your child access to your adult profile and use the installed parental controls you must choose to either allow the web browser with absolutely no filters or completely deny access to the web browser. There is no middle ground which is really what you need for a teenager. For some reason Amazon has made it impossible or next to impossible to use a web filter with their fire tablets. I've tried accessing filters through the parental controls offered on my router (through openDNS) but not even the tech at the router support center could make these filters work on the fire tablets. And the one parental control app offered on Amazon's app store is not yet compatible with the 5th generation of fire tablets and gets poor reviews from kindle users.My home computer has a great internet filter offered through the security program, so my children can continue to access the internet on our home computer but definitely not on their tablets! And what teenager wants a tablet without some kind of internet access? My eleven year old will soon need an upgrade to a different android tablet.So if you are child-less or your children are young these are great affordable tablets especially if you are a prime subscriber and can live without google.

title: Kindle is a Swindle: The Good & Bad About Amazon Kindle
body: Amazon's new Kindle e-book reader has a lot of potential but a lot of room for improvement. I agree with fellow reviewer Alex P Keaton's review "The Good The Bad & The Ugly" about the pros and cons of the device. The Kindle by design looks like a 1980s style device -- a device that would be made back then -- and not just a 1980s device but a Texas Instruments based device.Now I'd like to mention the points Keaton used in his piece about the Kindle and my 2 cents after it:The Good:The screen is actually pretty nice on this device. This is one of those new style of paper screens that make it much easier to read on than an on your PC, iPhone, or PDA. +1 points.The Bad:- Price. $400 is way too much. -1- The books you buy from Amazon are DRMed. This means that 10 years from now, you will probably not be able to read the books you buy. -1The Ugly:- No PDF support which makes it completely useless for most people who would like a device to review documents while traveling. -1- This does not replace the book. What Amazon does not understand, is that I want something that could replace my *laptop* so I can review work related PDF files without printing them out. I do not want something to replace my paperback novel which is cheaper, more durable, has infinite battery life, and I'll be able to read 20 years from now. -1The above are all the good and bad factors about the Kindle. Hopefully, the emergence of the Kindle will lead others to make similar devices that work even better and if Amazon wants the Kindle to do well it will have to continue innovating the device and improving on its features.The statement about Kindle books being DRMed is an important point: If Amazon ever discontinued the Kindle then several years later if you were in the market for a new portable e-book reader and you have a Kindle DRMed book but get a non Kindle e-book player it won't even work with your digital books. Oh yeah and did I mention the Kindle is a swindle. Books provide people a way to share information and knowledge. It is every individual's right to have knowledge for free. Gutenberg invented a printing press and brought books to the masses. His generation looked forward to a new book every few months -- today a new webpage is created every few seconds. Thanks to technology we have access to lots of information and knowledge. We need a free society with a free flow of information. Books are meant to be shareable, you should be able to re-sell them if you want or share them.Electronic books are amazing but DRM is crippling. Say no to Kindle, Kindle books and any and all content with DRM.If Amazon wants Kindle to do well it has to improve it.

title: 🔥Detailed Fire Review🔥- Very worthwhile purchase, IF you know what you're getting into!
body: For any tablet, these are the things most important to consider. The Holy Trinity: CPU, storage (memory) and screen.After those three, the slightly less-important things are battery life, audio, device accessories, and anything which either makes it better or worse at doing its job.  (Like, WiFi and such)_______GOOD:✔✔✔ It's only $50! And, better than most $50 tablets you'll find.✔✔✔ Fifty dollars. Yes, this does count more than once. It's a good value, IF you know what you're getting into. (Pay attention to the rest of the review)✔✔ SCREEN - It is decently crisp and bright! With many other $50 tablets, you can expect their screens to be difficult to read outdoors. Amazon found a good-quality but financially cheap screen. Its resolution isn't too high, but you honestly aren't going to suffer for it. (On most devices,  the screen is a big part of the cost. So if you demand an ultra-high def display, for some specialized purpose,  why are you looking at Amazon's entry/bottom-tier device? This is also why the screen doesn't get three marks.) The screen isn't uber, but I'm so pleased about it that I'm putting it in the positive category.✔✔✔ MEMORY - This may sound boring, but it's really, really important!Usually, I tell people that, when they get a new tablet or phone, they NEED 16G or more of internal storage. If standard Android devices have a slot for a micro-SD card, that's nice, but the dirty little secret is that SD cards are largely worthless for most of these devices. Two thirds of the apps you'll find are *stuck* in your internal storage, and can't be moved to the SD card. (Unless, you have someone smart root the device and add some special software. The point is: with a Fire, you don't have to do that!)However, FireOS handles storage in a much smarter way than the typical Android OS does. It will store apps on the SD card, automatically! So, the 8G of internal storage really is enough! (Yay!)Technically, Google limits SD cards on normal Droid devices because of somewhat uncommon security concerns. I really wish Google would think about following Amazon's example. This limit is a serious handicap for Android devices. ✔✔ BATTERY - Admittedly, I've only had the Fire for less than two weeks, so it's difficult to give a full assessment. However, I like to use my devices pretty constantly when I'm not otherwise engaged. I've found that the battery keeps the Fire going for a pretty decently-long period of time. Charging time, on the other hand, can also be fairly long. However, I don't necessarily downmark for that, due to my personal philosophy about mobile devices. With the Fire, I'm mainly being "mobile" around my house, going from bedroom to bathroom to family room to kitchen; I may take it to church or to pass the time before an appointment. But, I'm probably not going to take the Fire camping! Even if I did, I personally keep a portable charger device with me when I leave home, mainly because I don't want my phone to die - EVER - while I'm away from home. (I have agoraphobia) A decent, portable charger device can be had for $20-40. Problem solved.✔✔ ACCESSORIES - You won't find more exotic or gimmicky items like with some other devices. However,  there are a lot of options out there for book-style cases, bumper-cases (if you want better protection) at affordable prices. The selection appears better than with more expensive tablets.__________THE BAD:❎ The CPU - I'd be surprised if the processor for a "bargain basement" device like this impressed me. Of course it ends up on this list, but you shouldn't necessarily pass the Fire by because of it. The Fire isn't made for mobile gaming; it's made for reading and similar uses.❎ AUDIO - Really not terrible, but not spectacularly. Just one speaker. And, that speaker is oddly placed at the back of the device, making it harder to listen to things with the tablet lying on a table. The audio quality won't be turning on any of you audiophiles. But, it actually produces adequate audio at a very nice, maximum volume. (Better than some higher-priced tablets, truthfully. Which, takes away one of the downmarks I might otherwise have given) This tablet is just fine for watching shows from Amazon Prime, Netflix or Hulu.❎ CHARGING CABLE - The USB cable packaged with this device is egregiously short. As if, you have a major power-outlet built into your bedpost, your kitchen table and any other place you plan to be. It's not THAT important - you can find a much longer, better-quality cable on Amazon for as cheap as $3-5. Make sure it has a Micro-USB connector, and I recommend you get a "braided" cable (to better withstand wear and tear) - they're easy to find at 6 or 10 foot lengths.❎❎❎ ECO-LOCKED (APP AVAILABILITY) - You are locked into Amazon's "ecosystem". (Doublespeak for, "We don't play nicely with others") This is the most ugly limitation for this Fire device.Amazon's App Store has lots of apps. They love to say they have lots and lots of apps, as part of their sales pitch. Numbers aren't everything. There are several great apps I've come to know and love, over the years, which simply aren't available inside the Amazon gulag.Or worse, maybe a few of them are "available" but they still won't work after you download them, because they are expecting a more standardized Android device to run on. (Including, needing certain, free things from Google Play Services, which Amazon STILL won't let you have. Because, again, Amazon doesn't play nicely with others.) Worst of all, you may THINK you'll be able to use these apps, if you're carefully checking before you buy a Fire, and then be disappointed.❎❎❎ ECO-LOCKED (THROWING BAD MONEY AFTER GOOD) - Yes, I intentionally said it that way. The Fire is a really great price. However, the cost of apps may be an unfortunate, hidden expense for some Droid users. You MUST get all of your apps from the limited selection on the Amazon app store. Even if you already paid full price for those apps on other Android devices before, you are going to have to buy them all over again, through Amazon. Amazon does not allow you access to the Google Play Store, or any other place.Yes, being eco-locked also counts twice. Partucularly, because I had to pay twice.  However, if money isn't as tight for you as it is for me, or this is your first foray into the Android universe,  this may not be as much of a problem for you. Regular, non-Amazon mobile devices are allowed to get apps from Amazon.❎❎ CUSTOMER SUPPORT - Three of the four times I've talked to anyone from their Fire Support department, they were amazingly ignorant about the most basic concepts related to tablets. (How can they provide support for a device they've never even used? Seriously - one of them didn't know what a "lock screen" was!)I was completely civil, I promise. But this person who didn't understand what I was talking about put me on hold and then hung up. Nice. (I wasn't impressed with how this "customer service representative" represented Amazon)However, the most recent time I talked to Amazon improved things a bit. (See below)_______________INTERESTING:🔘 FireOS & BUILT-IN SOFTWARE - Simple. Which isn't bad. I have to say that I like how the Fire backs up not just apps but photos to a [supposedly secure?] cloud server. You have a choice, too, if this is a concern and you don't want to take advantage of this ability.When I deactivated my old Fire and started up the replacement device (see below) I was back in action in half an hour. No sweat and no hassle!However, that simplicity also means that the Fire doesn't have certain features like WiFi Direct, Voice control, etc. The Fire also doesn't allow for more specific control of some things, as much as I'm used to with most devices. But? Most people won't miss these things at all. 🔘 "SPECIAL OFFERS" - If you don't like your lock-screen being one, giant billboard? You can call up Amazon Customer Service, and they will remove the ads – or, as Amazon calls them, "special offers" – from your lock screen for $15. You can ONLY do that by calling them; there isn't an option on the website. (Their rep confirmed this is the case) These are apparently the "special offers" in the "Fire Tablet with Special Offers" deal which Amazon has been offering this last Holiday season.You might consider $15 highway robbery, but think about this: all of people who have ads in their faces is one of the most important reasons why this tablet is a really, really good deal at $50. (Or, $65, after you pay to have ads removed - I personally think it's worth it. And now my lock screen shows a picture of my wife and kids) All of that advertising is worth a lot of money to them. All of that advertising makes this tablet cheaper. 🔘 MY PERSONAL EXPERIENCE - Lastly, I only had my Fire since Christmas (9 days) when a problem cropped up a few days ago. I've kept it in safe places. Never done anything remotely physical with it. But a few days ago, the display has a flaw in it. A weird, white mark. Even more weirdly, this flaw has been moving around my screen... O.o  So, it's not strictly just a problem with the screen, itself, but with the hardware controlling the screen.Either way, it's still a flaw. But? I'm happy to report that my conversation with "Kimisha A." was actually useful. She shipped out a replacement device within half an hour, and it arrived today. (3 days later) Kimisha also emailed me a label, so that I can ship the defective device back in the same packaging, for free.Replacing this device has been pretty painless. Kimisha was the one person who was helpful, out of four people in three phone calls. Not a great ratio, but she came through when it mattered most._______ _______ _______ _______So, in conclusion? The Fire isn't stellar. But, it is a very good bargain. For most people.I'm still reeeeeally annoyed with how Amazon fences its customers into the Amazon ecosystem so mercilessly. (It makes me think of East Berlin, during the Cold War) I'm more than annoyed at the limited selection for apps, and utterly pissed that I have to buy the same apps - ones they do have available - all over again. If not for the ecosystem issues, I'd consider the Fire to be a truly stellar bargain, and recommend it for pretty much anyone.I was pretty annoyed with the billboard-for-lock-screen, but Amazon offers an affordable fix for that. That matter is resolved.Make sure you pay attention to the trial-subscription programs you may sign up for, between Amazon Prime, Kindle Unlimited, and FreeTime (for kids). And, I hope that my review might help you to decide if the Fire is right for you. Happy 2016!p.s. - two last important tips any Fire user should know:1. In any app - especially, in fullscreen mode - in case you're ever wondering how to get out, or how to get to the basic control menu (◀ ⚫ ◼), swipe up from the bottom of the screen.2. To take screenshots:There are three buttons along the top of the device. Two for volume control, and the power/sleep button. For a screenshot, hold down the left volume button and the power button at the same time.(I can't call the left button "volume down", because the volume buttons actually change function according to whichever part of the Fire is pointed up at the present moment. It's so smart and convenient, you may not even notice!)You're welcome. ;-)

title: A great, but limited, budget tablet.
body: Summary: A great, but limited, budget tablet. But at $50 shipped, it’s hard to beat in terms of value; on sale at $35 shipped -- it’s an incredible proposition! Be sure to match expectations with what you paid.Background: I purchased this device on an impulse when Amazon launched what seemed like a fire-sale during Thanksgiving--$15 off this $50 tablet! That’s right: $35 delivered for the newest Fire 7 offering! I was originally never going purchase the new Fire 7, as I purchased two of the previous 4th generation (2014) Fire HD 7 tablets earlier in 2015. But when this sale hit I thought I could justify (to myself) having yet another device to tinker with (i.e. root) and would not feel so bad if I bricked it. And if rooting it worked out, I could turn it into a more desirable and usable Android tablet.Initial impression before turning it on for the first time: Out of the box, the 2015 Fire 7 looks like any generic tablet available: familiar all black face with relatively large bezels all around, and ubiquitous front-facing camera at the top of the tablet (held in portrait position). Both the power and volume buttons were also at the top (again, in portrait position). Also included were a power plug adapter, USB-to-micro-USB cable, and quick start instructions. Picking up the tablet, it felt lighter than I expected. The glass front felt smooth and slick so that your fingers glide across easily. The back is hard plastic with a satin-like finish. It’s fairly smooth to the touch with a fine texture, but still somewhat slippery to hold. When looking at the back of the Fire in portrait position, the single speaker is on the bottom left, the camera on the top left, and the Amazon logo centered in the top third of the tablet. Along the top side of the tablet (if held in portrait) are the power and volume rocker buttons at the top, the micro-USB input for charging and data transfer, a small hole for the microphone, and a headphone jack. On the right side, if looking at the front in portrait position, is the door for the micro-USB card. The rest of the sides are all featureless, save for a seam in the plastic backing.Build: Holding the tablet, I noticed it doesn’t feel as solid as other more expensive tablets. For example, when you push into the back of the back plastic cover, you can feel the plastic move inward ever so slightly. When you try to twist the tablet, you can hear slight noises…not quite creaking, not quite cracking. Looking at the front of the Fire, there is a noticeable seam where the glass ends and the plastic covering wraps up over the sides to meet the glass on the front. And although the glass and plastic appear to be flush visually, if you run your finger off the screen glass over the seam onto the plastic, you can definitely feel the transition. Now, all this isn’t to say the build quality is bad; my point is that I personally noticed a different “feel” in the build of this latest Fire compared to more premium devices such as your iPads or more expensive Android or Windows tablets. On the whole, however, I’d venture to the say the build quality is good, particularly at this price-point.Screen: Firing up the 2015 Fire, you notice that the screen is another area where the budget-mindedness of this table peeks out. Amazon lists the resolution as 1024 x 600, which is sub-HD quality; and it shows, particularly if you’ve become accustomed to viewing HD resolution and higher screens. It’s not as pixelated as 680 x 480 screens of times past, but you do notice the pixels and not-quite-so-smooth edges of graphics, particularly when reading text. When compared to the screen resolution and crispness of the previous 2014 HD 7, the lower quality resolution is noticeable. Color and brightness are decent; colors appear to be vibrant and not washed out, and the screen brightness is fairly bright at maximum. As with the build quality, this isn’t to say the screen is bad; it’s actually pretty good if you think about the cost of the tablet. However, if you’ve become to the Retina displays and other HD and HD+ screens, you may be a bit disappointed. But at $50 or less, there’s really no room to complain.Sound: Sound comes from a single rear-facing speaker on the bottom of the tablet (if held in portrait). The sound coming from the single speaker is reasonably loud and clear; even at maximum volume there wasn’t much, if any, distortion. However, because of the speaker’s location, all of the sound is directed away from you. This speaker placement may be another cost cutting measure to ensure the entry price of this tablet remains as low as it is, however, in today’s market of tablets and phones with front firing, or at least side firing speakers, it’s disappointing. With fairly large bezels all around on the front side, one might think the speaker could have been reoriented to be front firing.Performance: Performance of this latest Fire is great when considering the price point. Although the operating system (OS) is heavily burdened with Amazon’s overlay, I did not notice any lag or stutter when switching between screens or apps. Processor/GPU intensive games such as Riptide 2 and Real Racing played smoothly. Benchmark runs on Antutu v6 produced consistent scores in the 25,000 range. One area of lag I noticed was related to internet download speeds. For example, Facebook took quite a while to refresh data (even with a strong, 50Mbps+ connection), and web pages seemingly take an eternity to load or update. Not a show stopper, but when you’re used to near instantaneous refreshes on your various feeds and quick uploads of websites (even those loaded with ads), it’s first-word-problem annoying. On the whole, it’s no spec-sheet powerhouse, but the new Fire 7 has enough performance for the majority of users to use as a daily driver for web-surfing, Amazon browsing, trolling social media sites, and occasional gaming. UPDATE (after a month of owning): Playing CSR Racing, I did notice that there are some performance slowdowns. Occasionally, the game will stutter and lag, particularly after loading the game. Additionally, there's a delay in loading, such as when (in this game) you go to view your garage of cars. Also, after some game play, you start to notice that the back of the tablet area near the camera starts to get warm. Nothing too bad like other phones/tablets, but noticeable. Once you get going in the game, the stutter/lag seems to go away for the most part. Other games seem to run just fine, such as Plants vs. Zombies 2 and Monument Valley. But again, at this price point, there's no room to complain as it handles most everything pretty darn well.Interface/interaction: This is one area where I really am not a fan of Fire tablets. The Fire runs on Bellini OS, a heavily modified version of Android’s Lollipop OS. Out of the box, it came with v5.0, but as soon as I connected to the internet, the over the air updated initiated (well, I let it) to bring it up to Android 5.1.1. As most know, Amazon has heavily modified the OS user interface (UI) to maximize use of the Amazon ecosystem. If you’re ok with that, great; for those that like a more traditional tablet interface, it could prove to be annoying. This isn’t to say that Amazon’s modifications to the OS are bad, but when compared to the unadulterated version of Android (such as on Google Nexus devices), there seems to be a lot of extra layering, extra screens, and extra steps. It’s “busy” in a sense. At least all this extra that Amazon added doesn’t seem to impact performance in the way that Samsung’s Touchwiz UI cripples even the most powerful processors. Luckily, the Android community has already been able to root this latest Fire (even on the latest OS update) so that you can truly make this your own tablet -- which I have by the way lol! UPDATE (after 1 month of owning): The tablet is easily "rooted" to gain full access. With root, I was able to get rid of the Amazon home launcher and default it to Google Now launcher to make it more like a stock Android tablet. Also, installed the Play Store as well for the full inventory of Android apps (while retaining Amazon App store for your Underground Apps). By changing out the ridiculous Amazon home launcher (but retaining full Amazon services), installing the Google Play Store, and full Google Now capability, this tablet is probably the best-bang-for buck bargain out there!Cameras: Very disappointing; in this department Amazon's cost cutting efforts to keep price down comes through. Amazon lists a 2MP rear facing camera (no flash) and a VGA front facing camera. While both work, don't expect any high quality pictures or videos. The rear camera takes grainy photos, even in good lighting conditions; low light, forget it. The front-facing (i.e. selfie or Skyping camera) is just plain bad in all conditions. In this department, Amazon could have cut the rear facing camera and instead put a better front facing camera, as I imagine this tablet's camera would likely be used more for video chatting than true photography.Other thoughts: One thing to consider when purchasing this tablet is the dismally small amount of onboard storage; a measly 8GB, of which only about 5.5GB is actually available to the owner. Luckily, there is a provision to add a micro-SD card, and of course Cloud storage, should you need more space. However, that’s more for media content like music, pictures, videos, and other files--NOT apps. Despite Amazon’s claim of “no longer worrying about space” due to the micro-SD storage, apps WILL remain resident in your onboard storage, with only limited ability to move portions of an app to the SD card. In some cases, apps in their entirety are NOT moveable at all to the micro-SD card. In other words, your 8GB, which is really only less than 6GB, gets eaten up VERY quickly if you start downloading apps, particularly games that require a lot of additional data. With careful storage management, you can make due, but if you like to keep a lot of games on your device “just in case,” you’ll be wishing this device came with at least 16GB. Yes, you can root the tablet and do some magic to force apps to SD, but with storage memory so inexpensive these days, I’m sure this Amazon could have provided at least 16GB onboard storage with only a minor price bump. UPDATE (after 1 month of owning): Another notable plus for this tablet is the battery life--it's great! In standby mode, I've left the tablet on overnight and only lost ~1-2% battery life, if that. Also, even while playing games that normally drain the heck out of the battery in my other devices, I don't see nearly the percentage drop with this tablet. So, another plus for this already amazing value performer!Conclusion: This latest Fire 7 is a decent tablet. Despite its less-than-HD resolution, noticeable budget build, and puny storage capacity, it works well enough for the average user. At $50, this is a great tablet. At $35, this is an awesome tablet!

title: The iPod for books!
body: Just as the iPod and similar devices allow us 24/7 access to our personal music library, the Kindle allows us to read anywhere, anytime.It is light-weight -- about the weight of an average magazine, adding virtually no weight to your briefcase or bag.It is small enough to hold with one hand -- AND turn the pages. Try that with a magazine or newspaper or book.Granted, as most reviewers have pointed out - you have limited choice of thumb space on the front of the Kindle with which to hold the thing. I expect that the next version will shrink the keyboard and page turn keys, so that accidental hand-shifts don't create accidental page and menu changes.I have been pondering purchasing the Kindle for about 6 months. I have to admit that, even though I could easily afford it, the price put me off. I am an avid reader. It is nothing for me to take a trip to Barnes and Noble and walk out with 10 books -- and go back again 2 weeks later for more.An upcoming vacation brought me back to the Kindle. Well, it's not really a vacation. It's kinda complicated and quite private, but I will be in a not very interesting American city for 6 days with very little to occupy my days.I started contemplating a trip to B&N to stock up and then thought about the sheer weight of all the reading material I might bring, now that we must pay for checked bags (which is more annoying than a financial inconvenience, truth be told). I had one of those "eureka!" moments, when I realized I could take 100s of pounds of books, magazines, and newspapers with me -- in my carry-on luggage.I am now completely and utterly in love with my Kindle and I am thinking of all of the barriers in everyday life that conspire to limit one's reading:Recognize yourself in any of these scenarios?***Crowded subway or train.Too difficult to read more than one page of the paper or a magazine? Need one hand to hold on - can't turn pages? Can't spread out the front page of the newspaper to read?Kindle's solved that. Once you work out the mechanics, it is easy to hold the Kindle with one hand and turn the pages.I just finished a luxurious hour floating in my swimming pool, noodle under my arms, lightweight Kindle in my hands, reading a good book.Before the Kindle, I had stopped reading in the pool, because the mechanics of holding the book and turning pages without getting them wet got to be too difficult. I had to limit myself to reading material easily held in one hand - pretty much magazines; and there are only so many magazines I am interested in reading (well, other than professional journals, I am a New Yorker girl through and through; everything else is fluff, IMHO).***Almost done with a book - can't bother to carry the heavy thing with you because you are close to the end, but you really want to finish it?Old school (pre-Kindle) you would have 2 choices - take a different book with you and finish the nearly done one another time -- or carry 2 books with you (ugh, the weight!) so that you could finish the first and still have something to read for the ride home. Now, with a Kindle, you can take both (and 100s more) with you whereever you go.****Strangers checking out what you are reading and/or commenting/trying to create a "connection" through your choice of reading material?Kindle offers Complete Privacy!The only thing I will miss as the Kindle becomes ubiquitous is the delight of seeing what other people are reading.And the occasionally interesting conversations that come up on public conveyances as readers meet each other -- "I see you are reading __________, how do you like it? Have you read his/her other works?". "I see you are reading the Nation; are you going to the protest rally on Thursday?"Some of us do not enjoy such encounters/come-ons and would prefer that one's traveling and waiting-room companions not have such an easy conversation-starter.
