It really is a quality piece of hardware, with great parental control software to go with it. I'm fine with the price of $64.99. I'm NOT okay with being swindled. It was not advertised as $49.99 + $15 to remove ads. It was advertised as $49.99 + "special offers" (which is $0.00). It's guerrilla marketing. It's dirty and underhanded, and I'm VERY disappointed with Amazon for it.Was that worth it, Amazon? For all the billions you're making off of us all the time, was $15 really worth destroying the trust I had in you? I know every company is in it for the profits; that's the way it works, and I'm totally fine with that. However, until this happened, I trusted you to make your profits in an honest way. This was a dirty trick. Now, I don't just recommend this tablet to anyone with kids. I tell them, check it out, but bear in mind that it's not really the price advertised. Come to think of it, if they weren't honest about that, I wonder what else they're not being 100% honest about...Thanks a lot Amazon. Like the world really needed another company we can't trust to just be honest with us.

title: Good tablet, not great. Would be better with some basic features added in.
body: I have not used all the features yes, but so far, I am very impressed. I will not lie, there are better out there. the screen is pretty good, but not great. I watched some video on it though netflix, and it seemed decent enough. I would say it was about as good as my 720p TV. The video played seamlessly with no glitches, delays, or anything else. As for the memory, as soon as I put all my info in, it did automatically add on another program, so that did take slightly away from the memory I had to use. The touch screen seems pretty responsive. I have seen other reviewers stating about how the screen is glitchy, and not very responsive to the touch. Honestly, it reacted perfectly every time that I used it. I did not have to hit it hard, or many times. Just nice light touches was all it needed.As for the memory, like others have stated, I wish you could put more on to the memory cards, but you live with what you got. I got it during the black friday deals, so for that price, you really are not going to find any better. I had seem some android devices on sale, of the same size, etc, at the same time I bought this one, and they were barely cheaper then this was. But overall, they had the same specs... Memory, ram, processor, video quality/aspect, etc. Pretty much everything was the same, just with an android OS instead of what comes on these.Overall, it is pretty easy to use. The memory card was also easy to install. I was thinking about going up to the 6" one that was supposed to have the better video, sound, etc, but then when I realized that you could not use a memory card in that one, and it had the same memory as this one, I deceided to stay with this one as the few extras that you got with it was not worth loosing the MicroSD card ability, plus the extra cost on top of it.. Meaning to get more memory in it, it would have cost more yet. That made me stay with this one. The micro SD card that I bought was just a class 10 basic card. Meaning you do not need any kind of special card to use it. One bad thing that I can say about installing the MicroSD card is that you have to watch when putting it in. You have to push it in far enough for it to latch into place. If you can not do it with your finger nail, you may want to get something to push it in. It sits in so far so that you can put the door back over the slot. That is good that it is hidden, but a pain to get it in far enough to get it to latch, and lock into place.as for the camera. It is an OK camera. The pictures turn out very well in full light, but in dim lit rooms, the pictures start to get a bit grainy. But I did not buy it for the camera, so that is minor detail to me.The sound is OK too. It is pretty clear, and comes out loud enough to hear, with no problem, but do not expect any kind of high quality. It is better then mono, but not really great. Not much bass or treble in it. Still, I have heard worse. I had no problem with any kind of static or anything like that in the sound either.The OS does take some getting used to. I am used to an android OS, so it is a bit different, but has enough similarities to make it very easy to get used to comparing the similarities.I can not say much about the book reading feature, as I don't really read a lot of books. I got it for other features.As for the other drawbacks of it. I don't like the limited ability of connecting it to a TV. With this, Amazon is basically trying to force you into buying more of their stuff. Like their Fire TV. What I dislike about this is if you do not have a TV or device that does not have FireTV installed, then you can not hook this up to the TV at all. So that leaves the problem of either buying an adapter for each TV, or carrying one around. I liked the old kindle, where it had the microHDMI slot. With that, you could easily just plug the kindle into the cord, and play whatever. The cords cost a fraction of what the FireTV devices do to, so it is not that big of a deal to get one for each TV, just in case you wanted to switch TV's or rooms. I do like the idea that you can go wireless, with FireTV. But the lack of ability to connect it to a TV if you do not have it, is limiting.I have not used it for texting, or email, or anything like that, where I had to do a lot of texting yet, so I can not say as to the ease of doing it.Also, it might be me, but you would think that they could have included something in the basic package to go with, or put the kindle into. Heck, we went to the local store and picked up a cheap case for when I am carrying it around, for a dollar. If I can get one for that cheap, retail at a local store, you know it would have cost them next to nothing to add one in. I guess they are relying that you are going to buy one of their cases on top of the device.The charging cord that it comes with is long enough to use, but not a lot extra. Meaning, find to plug into an outlet, and set on a table by the outlet, but not long enough to use the device while it is charging.It would also have been nice if you could have added in some small extra features, like 4G support. I doubt it would have cost that much extra in design. Since the device is made so that you can watch movies, listen to music, etc, it would have been nice to be able to use it in an area where there is cell service, but no WIFI anywhere around.Overall, I understand that it has its limitations, and I am taking the price into considerations when I rate this. While it is missing a lot, like Ok sound, not good, no 4G abilities, no way to connect it to a TV with some kind of FireTV device, which is an added cost, and no case to at least store it in while moving around. I dropped one star due to it limitations, but only one because of the price.If I find anything note worthy to add, I will add it in later, but right now, I am very happy with my purchase.

title: Generally Good, but Mixed Results for Technical Books
body: I played with a friend's Kindle for about an hour. I've been reading ebooks on various PocketPCs for 3-4 years now and I also consume a lot of technical books and audio books.I really liked the cover that comes with the Kindle. When it's closed it's easy to mistake the Kindle for a medium sized book. There's an elastic band which holds the cover closed, similar to what you sometimes see on a journal style book.I also like the size of the Kindle. It's a good form factor for reading. It's small enough to be portable but big enough that you're not constantly flipping pages like when reading on a PDA with a 2.8 inch screen.The Kindle screen is very nice for reading and resulted in less eye strain than using a backlit handheld. My eyes aren't great, but I could still use the smallest font and it was fairly comfortable. When I first started playing with it the time it took to turn pages was really long, like 3-4 seconds. I never figured out what was causing that, maybe it was a warm-up issue? It snapped out of it after a couple of minutes and page turns started to take less than a second. The sluggishness never returned and page turns were pleasant after that.I only used the keyboard briefly while searching for books. It seemed to work well, but since it takes a second to refresh the screen, I didn't have the patience to watch every character I typed. I typed in full book titles and hit the go button. This was much faster, but it was annoying when I miss typed one and had to do it again.The instant download of free samples worked flawlessly. (I didn't actually buy anything.) I always buy more books than I get around to reading. I figure that if I always download the sample chapter first and resist purchasing the book until I've finished the sample, then I'll probably save a lot of money on books that I never get around to reading.Reading fiction and other long, continuous text works worked flawlessly. I think that's the Kindle's real strength. The newspapers were readable, but the pictures weren't very good. Still, I would use the newspapers since their content is primarily text and the Kindle format is a lot more portable than a physical newspaper. Besides, I hate the amount of paper I waste with physical newspapers, especially when I only read a few pages.Reading the paid subscriptions to free websites worked surprisingly well. I was reading Slashdot, which I've always liked but never got into consistently reading on a computer screen. I would actually pay for this to get the auto downloads and to get it on the Kindle. I think I would read more online material in this format.There were embedded links back to the online Slashdot site and these worked well. The browser was usable for basic reading. You can navigate to sites directly and get the content for free, but I would still pay for the subscription to eliminate page load delays and to not need the wireless coverage while reading.I didn't try using the browser for anything more interactive, like reading email. I'm thinking that by the time you read and delete a few messages, the screen refresh lag will drive you crazy. I'll keep my Windows Mobile smartphone for email, but I can imagine that people who only check their email only once daily might get by with just the Kindle.I was concerned about how the Kindle would do with technical books, especially diagrams. I downloaded the free sample for Effective Java. This one worked well. There were embedded links to code samples and you could click to see them or just skip them if you didn't want to bother. While looking at a code sample you could be three pages deep and click the back button once to return to the main text. (Or hit the previous page button to go back to the previous page of the code sample.)I still wanted to see a technical diagram, so I downloaded the sample for "Reliable Distribute Systems" by Kenneth P. Birman. I was amazed that they had this title available for the Kindle since it's essentially a senior level college text book which is otherwise only available in hardcover. Unfortunately, once I downloaded it, problems were immediately apparent.The text wasn't the usual crisp, clean text that I had seen in all the other Kindle material I had tried. I zoomed in by increasing the font size and it became apparent that this book was somehow optically scanned. There were small imperfections randomly scattered throughout the letters, like text was scanned into gif and then resized. I tried all the text sizes hoping to find one that might match the original size and be cleaner, but there were still imperfections with all sizes. I settled on the second to smallest size. I could still see the imperfections, but it was reasonably readable.I finally found some diagrams to try out. They were displayed in-line in the text. It looks like the scanned the diagrams in separately. Each one had a zoom option available which displayed the diagram in full screen landscape mode. They were usable, but text within the diagrams was a little hard to read. In the hardcover edition of this book the diagrams are only about a third of a page in size. I have my doubts about the usability of an intricate full page diagram if they just scan it in like this. On the bright side you can always download the free sample first to see how well a technical book was translated to the Kindle.I didn't bother with the audible books or the MP3 player. I'll continue using my Windows Mobile phone for those since it's more portable.All in all I liked the Kindle. I'll probably get one when I can spare the money. For technical books I'll always download the sample first and verify that it was translated decently.

title: A nice impulse purchase but not an iPad killer
body: IMPORTANT UPDATE (22 June 2016)--Recently, while trying to open a new e-book, my tablet prompted me to save content to the installed SD card. Upon agreeing to this change in settings, I began encountering error messages when attempting to download and open ebooks I had purchased. After a frustrating conversation with Amazon's customer service during which the operator didn't seem to comprehend but maybe half of what I was saying (I won't say where the call center was located, but the operator had a heavy south Asian accent--draw your own conclusions), I had to de-register and re-register my device and decline the prompt to save content to the SD card for it to work properly again. I asked the service rep several times why my tablet would prompt me to do something it wasn't able to do, and instead of answering the question, he simply ignored it by remaining silent. He did say the device would save music and video files to the SD card but I haven't tried that yet.ORIGINAL REVIEW:I have to confess; I bought the Amazon Fire 7-inch tablet (Amazon has dropped the 'Kindle' name from its tablet offerings to differentiate them from its e-reader line) more out of curiosity than necessity. Just what kind of a tablet can you get for $39.99 on sale? Having tried a cheapo tablet in the past, I wasn't expecting much, but it turned out to be not half bad and thus was a pretty good impulse purchase.What you get in the package—The device comes in a pretty bare bones package, more of an card stock envelope, really; I've seen cookies packaged much the same way. Just pull off a little zip closure, reach in and pull the tab. The entire contents, what little there is, slide right out on a little card stock tray. Those contents consist of the tablet itself and a standard micro USB charger. There's really nothing else—no manual, no warranty card, nothing except for a little quick setup guide on a card with almost no text on it.Setting it up—Setting the device up was almost frighteningly easy. It appears my Amazon account information was loaded onto it before shipping. The only data I had to enter was my WiFi password. Once it was connected, everything else was automatic.What's on the inside—The Fire runs on Fire OS, which is an Amazon-centric version of Android. The nice thing is if you're at all familiar with the Android interface, the learning curve on Fire is nonexistent. Like Barnes & Noble's Nook devices, the Fire is designed intended primarily as a gateway into the Amazon ecosystem. Just about every app loaded on by default is directed at facilitating the consumption of media—from Amazon. This isn't totally a bad thing, especially if you're fairly committed to the Amazon ecosystem, but if you're looking for something a bit more open, you may find it a bit confining.The Google Play store has been replaced with the Amazon Appstore, which has its plusses and minuses. On the plus side, many normally paid apps like OfficeSuite Pro, normally $14.95, are free on the Amazon Appstore as part of the “Amazon Underground”. This is a good thing since MS Office isn't available. Amazon claims their Appstore offers more than $10,000 worth of free apps; of course, you couldn't fit all that on the fire, which has only 8 GB of storage.For those who yearn for more choices, it is apparently not terribly difficult to sideload Google Play store and other Google apps that are not available from Amazon. This also limits users' access to alternative Web browsers like Chrome and Firefox as well as keeping MS Office apps, with the notable exception of OneNote and Outlook, out of most users' reach. The best part is you don't have to root your device to do it. I'll probably spend a little more time getting accustomed to my Fire tablet before I do this. Amazon apparently tolerates this as the instructions for doing so can be found on a customer's review of the device. So instead of totally jailbreaking your Fire tablet, you can just let it out on parole.The user experience—Being accustomed to a 10-inch iPad, the first thing to get used to with the 7-inch Fire tablet is its small size. Just a little larger than a large 'phablet' phone, the Fire tablet is almost exactly as long as the iPad is wide. That said, I can see where this device would be handy to slip into a coat pocket to use when a larger tablet would be inconvenient. The screen is plently sharp, although it does not have HD resolution, and its touch response is almost as good as my iPad and maybe a little better than my Moto-X smart phone. What few ports and controls the device has are found along the top edge, except for the MicroSD card slot, which is located on the right side, adjacent to the top. That slot is important because this budget-priced device only comes with 8GB of storage.By far, the Fire's most obnoxious feature is the ads that appear on the unlock screen, which would seem to be proof positive that Amazon sees the device as a remote sales tool and intend to use it to hawk its wares to you at every opportunity. Fortunately, those ads are gone in the flick of a finger. You can pay about $15 extra for an ad-free Fire or you can suppress them yourself while you're in developer mode to sideload the Google Play Store.The Bottom Line—So is this device an iPad killer? Absolutely not, but it at less than $50 (or less than $40, in my case) one wouldn't expect it to be, either. While the insanely low price tag is truly one of its best features, it's not a shabby performer and more than worth the low price of admission. More than a toy tablet, it's an excellent value for what it is and a worthy impulse purchase if you happen to catch one on sale.

title: Living with the Kindle
body: Update:I've owned a Kindle since December of 2007. While it's not a perfect device, it is an excellent one.I could pick on the design, especially the power and radio button location, but I'd rather focus on "living with" the Kindle.The absolute best thing is the ability to get a book at any time and almost anywhere. So, when a new book was released in the Twilight Saga series, my daughter downloaded it at midnight and spent the whole night reading it. When I was reading a series of Harry Bosch novels, and came to the end of one novel, I immediately download the next novel and keep reading. My newspaper shows up every morning, my Reader's Digest every month - before it hits the news stands. I've downloaded new books while riding in the car, while on vacation, and while in bed.If I want to try a new author or new book, I download a free sample and see if I really do want the book. And, while I have a good vocabulary, if I don't know the definition of a word, the built in dictionary is a great feature.The Internet browser isn't great, but it's free and handy in a pinch. I didn't buy the Kindle to replace my laptop anyhow. And, yes, the leather cover slips off, and I do sometimes mistakenly hit the "Next Page" button. But those are very minor annoyances.My kids love the Kindle - - if it were a bit cheaper, I'd buy each of them one.I understand (from cnet.com's BUZZ Report) that the Kindle 2 is due out in February- or at least a press conference 2/9, which cnet reports will show a more attractive Kindle that "fixes some of the problems."Overall, the Kindle is one of the best tech purchases I have ever made. I absolutely love the device.As far as my old review is concerned - I have found some books that don't have Kindle editions - but most every book I've wanted has been available.My old review:This rating is an analysis of the content available. I've read many reviews critizing available content, so let's see. First, a caveat - I have not yet received my Kindle. I am anxiously waiting for its arrival, or even the e-mail saying they'll Amazon will send it soon (12/16/07 order).Assuming most people want to read books on the NYTimes Bestseller list (otherwise, they wouldn't be on the list), let's do an objective analysis of the top 10 NYTime hardcovers. I have limited this to Kindle editions available on Amazon. I haven't checked other sources.Today (1/13/08), the following books are on the New York Times Bestseller list. I marked with (Yes/No) whether the Kindle edition is available TODAY on AMAZON, and number of weeks on the book has been on the NYTimes Bestseller list.HARDCOVER FICTION1. A THOUSAND SPLENDID SUNS, by Khaled Hosseini (Yes) 332. DOUBLE CROSS, by James Patterson (Yes) 83. THE SHOOTERS, by W. E. B. Griffin (No) 14. WORLD WITHOUT END, by Ken Follett (Yes) 135. T IS FOR TRESPASS, by Sue Grafton (Yes) 56. THE DARKEST EVENING OF THE YEAR, by Dean Koontz. (Yes) 67 PEOPLE OF THE BOOK, by Geraldine Brooks. (No) 18 SHADOW MUSIC, by Julie Garwood. (Yes) 29 BLOOD DREAMS, by Kay Hooper. (Yes) 110 THE CHOICE, by Nicholas Sparks. (Yes) 15Result as of Today: 80% of Top Ten NonFiction, the two that are not have only been on the bestseller list for one week.HARDCOVER NONFICTION1. IN DEFENSE OF FOOD, by Michael Pollan (Yes) 12. I AM AMERICA (AND SO CAN YOU!), by Stephen Colbert, Richard Dahm, Paul Dinello, Allison Silverman et al. (Yes) 133. BORN STANDING UP, by Steve Martin (Yes) 74. AN INCONVENIENT BOOK, by Glenn Beck and Kevin Balfe (Yes) 75. BOOM!, by Tom Brokaw (Yes) 96. THE NINE, by Jeffrey Toobin (Yes) 137. QUIET STRENGTH by Tony Dungy with Nathan Whitaker (Yes) 268. CLAPTON, by Eric Clapton (Yes) 139. FREE LUNCH, by David Cay Johnston (No) 110.RESCUING SPRITE, by Mark R. Levin (yes) 9Result: 90% of Top Ten hardcover nonfiction. Again, the one not on Kindle has only been on the list for one week.HARDCOVER ADVICE1. YOU: STAYING YOUNG, by Michael F. Roizen and Mehmet C. Oz et al. (Yes) 102. THE SECRET, by Rhonda Byrne (Yes) 523. BECOME A BETTER YOU, by Joel Osteen (Yes) 124. THE DARING BOOK FOR GIRLS, by Andrea J. Buchanan and Miriam Peskowitz (Yes) 105. THE DANGEROUS BOOK FOR BOYS, by Conn Iggulden and Hal Iggulden (No) 366. DECEPTIVELY DELICIOUS, by Jessica Seinfeld (Yes) 137. THE 12 SECOND SEQUENCE, by Jorge Cruise (No) 18. HOW NOT TO LOOK OLD, by Charla Krupp (No) 19. JIM CRAMER'S STAY MAD FOR LIFE, by James J. Cramer with Cliff Mason (Yes) 110.LIVING WELL, by Montel Williams with William Doyle (No) 1Result - 60% of the Advice books - but 3/4 of those not on the list have only been on the list for one week and the 4th is a kids book anyhow.CHILDREN'S BOOKS - I'm going to assume that you aren't giving a $400 Kindle to a child.. so I stopped at 5 after 0/0.1. HIGH SCHOOL MUSICAL: ALL ACCESS, by N. B. Grace (No)2. GALLOP!, written and illustrated by Rufus Butler Seder (No)3. STAR WARS POP-UP GUIDE TO THE GALAXY, by Matthew Reinhart (No; but a pop-up book on Kindle would be quite a technological achievement!)4. THE CHRONICLES OF NARNIA, by C. S. Lewis (No)5. THE NIGHT BEFORE CHRISTMAS, by Clement C. Moore (No)My overall impression - Kindle has the books I am most likely to read.

title: Best Value, Especially with Prime, but beware: mine has 2 dead pixels, Works with GH4 + Antutu Benchmarked!
body: [[VIDEOID:ba853d217a79f1be1a24e04786022f08]]$50? Despite the dirt cheap price, i find it to be great for casual use. Videos play nice, games even play nice, text is just not as sexy looking as higher resolution screens. Watch the video for full demo!The short/summed up version: it's the new budget king in the 6-8" size. It's screen is a little lower in resolution but still pleasant to look at, it has enough power for most of the typical tablet tasks, and it shares many of the same features as its higher priced brothers such as front and back cameras, b/g/n wifi, and good overall battery life (minus an hour) My favorite size tablet is 8", so if you're looking at the amazon fire lineup, i would take this over the 6" for sure, and would have a hard time picking the 8" fire at 3x the price. If you're not a prime member, it's still a good tablet, if you are a prime member: it's a great tablet. Possible quality control issue: Mine had two dead pixels (not very noticeable, but still will exchange) You can load APKs(enable unknown sources), i loaded antutu and panasonic image app, both work properly.Just some background, i currently own 5 tablets not including this new one, ranging from a nook color, a samsung tab pro 8.4, LG g pad x8.3, an old acer a500 and an off brand 10.1". Suffice it to say: i know my tablets!Main Cons:-2 dead pixels-Front and rear cameras are low resolution, mostly good for video calls only-1 speaker, sounds tinny like a small laptop, Gets decently loud, use headphones!-Text not very crisp (but still decent to read)The rest are overall Positives:-7-8" is my favorite tablet size, 6" is just a bit too tiny, and 9+ can be cumbersome-Uses a slightly slower quad core 1.3ghz processor, for most tasks you won't notice a difference-Screen resolution is OK at 171 pixels per inch, at this price point it's totally satisfactory-Screen uses an IPS panel, meaning good viewing angles and color-The OS/interface overall is a smooth an intuitive experience, very simplified-Advertisements are there, but don't bother me much ($50 version only)-Fit and finish are very good, feels solid in the hand-Comes with 8gb, expandable to 128gb (and cards are cheap!)-A little less battery life than the rest of its brothers (7 instead of 8)-Mostly worth it if you're an amazon prime subscriber (lots of access)-Readability for books is overall good, but still pales in comparison to an e-ink reader such as kindle-Movies look overall great, but look better on my other higher end tablets(of course)-Tested with Panasonic Image app with GH4 wifi control and it works well, very little lagPreface: Let's always keep in mind that this is a $50 tablet, i may be using my $200+ tablets for comparison, but i by no means expect a $200 tablet out of this $50 tablet.The screen:It's a lower resolution, but the important factor is how many pixels per inch are there (PPI), at 171, it's not exciting, but for most people it will be a fine experience, if you put it side by side with a higher resolution screen it's a fairly clear difference, but as someone who owns both, i'm fine with both and expect this at this price. Also realize that some of the larger fire tablets have a comparable PPI. The brightness is good, and the viewing angles are good thanks to it being an IPS panel.The Processor:It downgrades from its brothers to a 1.3ghz quad, which in practice doesn't mean much, it's only when you want to use high intensive games or higher resolution video where you may see some issues. Again, this is a $50 tablet, and at that price it's a very capable processor to have.The overall quality is up to amazon's standards, which is to say: Very good. It's obviously very difficult for other manufactures to match amazon's price/performance/quality, as seen by their overall tablet success. My main quality issue are those two stuck pixels mine has, and it's annoying but simple enough to do a return/exchange to fix that.What it all comes down to though is just one question; Are you and amazon prime subscriber? If the answer is no, and you never intend to be, than it's still a good tablet, but if the answer is yes, than it's a great tablet. Amazon has been seriously beefing up its prime offerings. It's quite insane(in a good way) how many feature you get access to. I've been a prime member for something like 3 years, and yes i've totally drank the coolaid and am a devote believer in its value.Summed up: i could nitpick the crappy front facing camera, or the thick bezel, or the advertisements, but would have to eventually come back to the fact that this is the least expensive tablet of its kind on the market. They scaled it down expertly to be just enough tablet to appeal to a very broad market while keeping the overall quality top notch. Call me impressed and satisfiedIf you have any questions or comments, please do use the comments section and i'll response as best i can :)Antutu Benchmark results:Overall: 23628 (about half the score of a Galaxy note 4, it used the phone scale instead of tablet scale for some reason)UX: Multitask: 3011UX: Runtime: 1896CPU Integer: 1658CPU float-point: 1731CPU single thread integer: 1187CPU single thread float point: 975RAM operation: 1423RAM Speed: 1929GPU 2D: 1637GPU 3D: 6407Storage I/O: 1114Database I/O 660

title: Why I decided to buy the Kindle
body: It came!!!!!!!!!!As I posted earlier, I hope that this tool will allow me to get into reading again, as my COPD sometimes (often) causes problems with my close vision.Wee, it exceeds my expectations. I was able to clearly see the text in every one of the 3 books I ordered. It was easy to read and manage. The books downloaded immediatly after I first turned it on. A few minutes with the manual and I was up and running.I tried the web inteface and was able to view several sites including my emails, and the COPD Information Line web page I work with. I pulled up weather, news, and other than it being in black and white it was greatThe keyboard takes some getting used to but surely is no worse than a PDAI am having a hard time getting used to the next and back buttons. Having to cover on helps. but it has no way I can see to anchor it securely in the cover and easily falls out. I am heading out to get some velcro, which should solve that problem.It came pretty much charged and took only a few minutes for the charge light to go out. When it came on, it was already registered to me. Pretty impressiveI am going to try tonight to download some classic books and convert them with mobipocket creator.I joined the new yahoo forum and an anxious to see how others are doing with itI can see where this will open a whole new world to me.After having read all of the reviews from all of the people who never saw it complaining about the price and what it doesn't do, true it doesn't do everything. True it won't be the answwer for everyone. True, I do have negative input to list1. It won't take out the garbage2. It doesn't do the dishes3. It won't keep my wife from nagging meAwaiting Amazon's answers to these problemsWhat it does do is worth every penny and means the world to me. Thanks Jeff and Amazon staff.I bought one and it will arrive tommorow. I spent a couple long nights reading reviews, which quite honestly left me more confused then ever. I then called Kindle support. I talked to a nice young guy who was more than willing to answer my questions, and probably spent the better part of 20 minutes doing so. (I don't recall his name but Amazon should give him a raise.)I have a genetic disease called Alpha-1 which is trying to destroy my lungs and liver. The end result other than COPD is that on days when my oxygen is not real high, my eyesight suffers.1.On mornings and nights it is difficult to see small print. I used to read 2 to 3 books a week, and now am lucky to read one ever 3 weeks. The ability to increase the font size was a very major selling point.2. I was concerned over only being able to purchase books through Amazon. I was assured by Kindle support that I could download free titles (and other users have confirmed this).3. Since retiring from teaching and now working as a director of the COPD Information Line, I find that I have to do some traveling. Because of my breathing problems I can't carry a lot of weight. Up until this time I carried a portable dvd player but found it distracting in the airports and heavy to carry. This will certainly give me an alternative4. I have to believe in Amazon.com. Today we have no assurance that we will be here tommorow (definitely something my disease has taught me). Amazon has proved their staying power, so I have to go with them.5.I don't have the time to wait 5 or 10 years for a better version. I want to be able to read again now while I still can6. I spend about 4 hours a week in a hospital IV therapy room, and this should make the time go easier.7. As to the price of books, I usually buy mine at Walmart or Sams and they are usually impulse buys. I see it, it looks interesting and I buy it. The prices at the Kindle store are far less than what I have been paying. If a book really strikes me, I will buy it at a yard sale or used store later, but right now I want to read them now, while I still am here (by the way, I ordered the bible, and the Kindle should make that easier to carry around.8. Will it evolve? It has to like everything else. I also honestly believe that there will be an upgrade offer for the people who had enough faith in Amazon to support them from the start. In the meantime, maybe I can once again enjoy reading. THAT IS WORTH A HELL OF A LOT MORE THAN THE $400 TO ME.9.I will enjoy the basic web services offered, and sincerely believe that they will continue to improve through software and firmware upgrades.10 Lastly, to the people who enjoy showing off their expertise by writing reviews without having tried the device, I can only say that if mankind had followed your lead we would still be walking instead of driving and flying. This device is certainly not for everyone, but for me it may well prove to be a godsend. There are many things that I used to enjoy that I can no longer do. The ability to read once again.......pricelessThanks Jeff. This can't heal my body, but it can do wonders for my mind

title: It is exactly what it claims to be!
body: I have had this device for a little over a week now and can honestly say I have no issues with it. I actually ordered the six pack on the release date and then had plenty of time to read several reviews while waiting for my order to arrive. I found the review voted most helpful to truly be the most helpful. I've been rather confused by the number of reviews claiming there are "NO" instructions on how to use the tablet when there is a user manual, instructional video and tutorial accessible by just pressing the help icon. Perhaps all three of those options are not clear enough for some people. I don't know. Anyway, for comparison purposes I have available in my home an IPad, Nexus 7, and Samsung Galaxy smart phones. I purchased these tablets primarily as gifts for family/friends who are not big on tablets and for whom $200 plus tablets would be a waste of money. I also wanted a backup device for myself. At $49.99, this was right on the money. While it is true that this tablet is not going to win the award as the best device in the house, it definitely holds its own. In my experience, I found the screen resolution and brightness to be more than adequate for book reading, web surfing, email, and watching videos--the intended uses for which I purchased it. Also, when compared to my other devices using my home WiFi (40 mbps) there was not any noticeable lag or speed issues. All apps launched quickly and web-surfing was snappy BUT I was not using the Silk browser, which brings me to my main criticism. I agree that that the Amazon Appstore, leaves a bit to be desired. I recommend to anyone reading this to download 1Mobile Market (from their website) to access additional apps, in particular an alternate browser. My personal favorite is the Dolphin Browser which I have been using on my Android devices since 2011. Dolphin Browser, has a really good YouTube shortcut that I've always found to be quite functional, so much so that I rarely even use the official YouTube app on my Android devices. 1Mobile Market is where you can also access Instagram and a better keyboard if the pre-installed one does not suit your fancy. I think most people will be satisfied with the combined selection between the two app stores. I also found side-loading apps pretty smooth and easy but I know this is beyond most people's skill set. With the addition of 1Mobile Market, there really should not be much need for side loading.For a mono rear facing speaker, sound quality is decent, not great and when compared to my other devices, this is an area where I would say the Fire falls behind. I would give the cameras two stars at most for taking photos but I only use cameras on tablets for video calls and videoconferencing so that has not impacted my overall rating. But, in all honesty, I have to say they are not very good for taking pictures and would recommend using another device. All in all this is a very good tablet for $50 and would be good for twice the price. Perhaps some people received units that did not get the best quality control inspection before being shipped. That is really unfortunate because this really is a good little tablet for the price and I highly recommend it for anyone looking for a tablet to handle the basic every day functions.UPDATE: A tip to anyone using 1Mobile Market. To save internal storage space, you should delete any 1Mobile Market downloads after the applications have been installed. To do this go the settings menu, then select storage, then internal storage, and miscellaneous. Then check the box for onemobile_download and click remove. Also, after a couple more weeks with this tablet, I discovered there are a couple of very easy to follow tutorials on YouTube for installing the Google Play store For people who really want the official Google Play Store and the Google Apps that will only run properly with Google Play services both can be installed with a quick visit to YouTube. This can be done without rooting the device or losing of the benefits of the built in Amazon OS. I've continued to use 1Mobile as well because I have found that I still get a "device is incompatible" message with some apps in the Play Store but have nevertheless been able to download and install those same apps from 1Mobile Market. I'm not sure why that it is. I still have not found many, if any, apps available on the Play Store that cannot be found between the Amazon Appstore and 1Mobile. Obviously, the official Google Apps need Play Services to run properly and anyone who purchased apps through the Play Stay will appreciate being able to install it rather than re-purchasing the same apps through another store.With the ability to add the Play Store there is very little this tablet is lacking. Though, I personally would like to be able to customize the home screen or set another home launcher as the default. So far, I have not been able to figure out how to change from the Fire launcher as the default. This prevents full customization which is somewhat anti-Android but I still consider it a very good device and will be gifting it as planned.

title: Many pluses, a few negatives
body: I've now used the Kindle for about six weeks. On the positive side are the portability and ease of use of the device. It's handy and simple. The online store is good and downloading new material is a breeze. Traveling with the Kindle is a joy. No more shoving two or three books into the suitcase for a week's vacation. The screen is completely readable in any light that a book would be readable. The adjustable font is an excellent feature. The built in dictionary is also welcome. I added an SD card with about a megabyte of new age music which adds greatly to relaxing and enjoying a good read.On the minus side, the carrying case doesn't hold the Kindle in place very well. Several of the Kindle edition books I've read are badly edited. Section breaks in some books are not as apparent as in printed editions. Although convenient, the page advance buttons can be hit inadvertently. When returning to reading, particularly if it's overnight, I find myself re-reading the previous page to make sure I've not missed something. I rarely would do this with printed books.My two biggest criticisms are "feel" issues. First, although progress through the Kindle book can be monitored with the advancing ellipses, I miss the sensation of physically moving through the pages of a printed book, anticipating the ending, being able to judge how much further I have to go. At times I feel a sense of irritation because I can't judge the pacing of a story in quite the same way as with a printed book. And, second, I don't go to the book stores much any more! I miss browsing through the stacks, looking at the covers and feeling the books in my hands. Using the online store is simply not the same as going to the local book store, grabbing a few things after some pleasurable minutes walking between the shelves and sitting down with a cup of coffee to peruse the day's discoveries. Alas, convenience has it's cost.Having said all that, I haven't picked up a printed book for pleasure in six weeks and have thoroughly enjoyed reading the dozen or so books I've downloaded onto the Kindle. I would strongly recommend this product to anyone who reads extensively, particularly for pleasure.Follow up a year later:I've had a Kindle for about a year now and am posting this as a follow up. For me it is a great device for entertainment reading. In the past year I've read more than 40 books for this purpose and found the Kindle to be convenient, easy to use and completely enjoyable. It's especially great for travel. My wife (from whom I received the device as a Christmas present in 2007), who opposes all forms of clutter, is more than happy that I haven't added these titles to our overburdened shelving. Also, I'd estimate that I've saved about the cost of the Kindle by purchasing downloaded versions versus hard copy. So, from here on out, the reduced cost is pure savings. The Kindle Store, both through the device and online, is easy to use and has really improved over the past year.I find, however, that reading non-fiction is more difficult than breezing through a mystery or space opera. When reading non-fiction I like to mark up pages with highlighters and margin notes and the Kindle simply doesn't have what it takes in this regard. I've tried about a dozen titles including, particularly, history and social commentary, and have only gotten completely through about 15% of these. The remainder are still waiting to be finished. This could, of course, be due to the quality of the titles or a fleeting interest on my part, but I just don't experience reading non-fiction the same way with the Kindle as with a book.As with my first review, I continue to miss the sensation of physically moving through the pages of a book, enjoying the anticipation of coming to the end. To some degree I've gotten over this by paying close attention to the movement of the dots across the bottom of the reading panel of the Kindle but, still, it's not quite the same.I've had no battery issues as some others have nor have I been concerned about quality construction. I find it easier to use the device without its cover but I use the cover when I travel, for protection. I can't say I've been overly rough but I've not been too careful either and the Kindle is none the worse for wear. I've never gotten into the email features but I put in an SD card with a slew of MP3's for listening while I travel on aircraft and this has worked like a charm. Sure, the songs are played randomly but it's no different than an iPod Shuffle without carrying an extra device. I might say, however, that backlighting would be useful and this, to me, is indeed a shortcoming.So, my conclusion is that this should be rated a 4+. For what I use it for, the Kindle is outstanding and I now wouldn't be without it. But, having said that, it should be remembered it is what it is, a reading device. Those looking for more may well be disappointed. I, however, am not.

title: I love my two Kindle Fire 7 inch 5th Generation tablets!
body: I purchased my first Kindle Fire 7 inch in September 2016. Basically, I really like the tablet but it does have a few glitches. The one that is the most frustrating is it's lack of sensitivity when I touch the screen to select something. In spite of that, I liked other things about the Fire so much that three months later in December 2016, I purchased a second Kindle Fire 7 inch. I had read in the reviews, that some Fires would not charge using the charger that comes with the tablet. My first Fire had a loose charging connection with the tablet, but charged fine. The second Fire I purchased would NOT charge with the enclosed charger even though it fit nice and firmly!! Fortunately, the charger from my first Fire would charge my second Fire. And the new charger for the second Fire, would charge my first Fire just fine!! Go figure! The third thing that I noticed was that sometimes the tablet can be slow in responding to my switching from one function to another, like going from a game to my newspaper subscription. These three things are why I gave the Fire four stars instead of five.Other than those three things, I LOVE my little Fire tabets! I like the size because it's easy to take one with me in my messenger bag. I like the apps that I can get for it, many for no cost through the Underground App store. I'm a Prime member so I use the Amazon Video app quite a bit for movies and TV and I have Kindle Unlimited because I love to read and a lot of ebooks are free to read on Amazon. Because of a discount to Kindle Fire owners, I get the Washington Post for a mere pittance, and I love reading it. I also got some magazine subscriptions for a cut rate price. These were available because of the special offers feature, which doesn't really annoy me all that much. Occasionally I actually purchase some of the deals that pop up because it's something I can use or need.I purchased a green [[ASIN:B018JYMGJG Fintie Silicone Case for Fire 7 2015 - [Honey Comb Series] Light Weight [Anti Slip] Shock Proof Protective Cover [Kids Friendly] for Amazon Fire 7 Tablet (Fire 7" Display 5th Generation), Green]] for my second Fire and a purple Fintie case for my first Fire. I love the cases because they protect the Fires from accidental drops and make it easy to grasp the tablet and they are very colorful. I bought black Fires because I knew I could get any color case I wanted to brighten it up.I also purchased a [[ASIN:B013TMNKAW SanDisk 32 GB micro SD Memory Card for Fire Tablets and All-New Fire TV]] for the first Fire and a [[ASIN:B013TMNNDQ SanDisk 64 GB micro SD Memory Card for Fire Tablets and All-New Fire TV]] for my second Fire because of the discount that was offered. So far on my first Fire, the 32 GB Memory Card is enough. I like that I can put the apps, photos, music, and books on the Memory Card, that way I don't need some much basic device memory.For both Fires I used the [[ASIN:B013EYDCCQ NuPro Fire Screen Protector Kit (2-Pack) (5th Generation - 2015 release), Clear]] for a screen protector because of the discount offered on it. If I had it to do again, I would purchase the anti-glare version because the clear one glaringly shows fingerprints. However, the screen protector was very easy to put on my two tablets. I always am nervous doing this, but these I got right the first time!I did not buy any of the extended warranties or accident coverage because I've had many kinds of tablets before this, and I didn't need any extra coverage. I'm gentle with my tablets. Plus the coverage costs about half of the price of the tablet and I figure it's just about as cheap to buy a new Fire 7 inch when you add in the costs of mailing the tablet somewhere for repairs or replacement. One reason I bought the second Fire tablet was because if the first tablet expires, then I've already got a back up to replace it with. The other reasons I bought it was because of the great Christmas deal on it.It's nice having two because when one is charging up, I can be using the other one. I use them daily. In fact, except for the three problems I listed in the first paragraph, this little Fire works just about as well as my Samsung Galaxy Tab 2 Nook Android tablet that cost about $350! The extras that Amazon sells, like the Prime membership and the Kindle Unlimited membership, help make the Fire super competitive with the Barnes and Noble Galaxy tablet. The Samsung Galaxy does have a much better camera, responds a lot better to touch, however it doesn't charge as fast as the Kindle Fire 7 inch does, nor does it hold the charge as long. I also like the way I can share the same apps and books on my two tablets with ease. I probably won't buy another Barnes and Noble tablet, but will stick to Amazon tablets from now on. If, however, tapping the screen multiple times to get an app to work really bothers you, then maybe the Fire isn't for you. But at $50, it's a great buy!

title: For Just an 'EveryDay Entertain Me Tablet' This is Perfect
body: Of all the tablets I've owned, this is among my favorite. We now own 3. I'd highly recommend this to anyone looking for a tablet for themselves, for their kids, for their spouse, other-half, or for anyone who's looking for a tablet at a great price.I'm not good with technical details, so I'll leave that review to someone who is. But what I do know, is what I like. And I like this. As just an everyday user who doesn't know a lot about specs and technical stuff, I only see things in how they run, not the technical side of how they run. (I leave that to my 4-year-old) :) So this is what I know:This tablet runs really smooth. Do you like Subway Surfers? On all the platforms I've played Subway Surfers on, I've never played it as smoothly as I have on the Fire. You can maneuver all over the board easily with the swipe of one finger. The motions aren't jarring. They don't lag. They don't freeze up and you slam into a train. It's just smooth. It's like that on all games. Just a really smooth response.The graphics are nice. Clear. Bright. I watched Kate & Leopold in the car the other day and it was like watching it on TV. Listening to it through my earbuds brought the sound experience up close and personal.I LOVE LOVE LOVE that if you are a Prime member (if you aren't, try it out. I did years ago, and can't go back.) you can download Prime shows/movies right to your tablet and take them with you anywhere whether or not you have internet. LOVE IT! Love it for me, love it for the kids...SO many great apps to choose from! For me and the kids. We travel a lot for work - and the kids go with us. So I put tons of stuff on their tablets to keep them busy. There is SO much to choose from. Even the educational stuff is fun (bonus for me!) plus I can download Curious George for the road!I'm trying Freetime free for a month - which comes on the Kindle for Kids. Instead of buying the Kindle for Kids though, we decided to go with this tablet for both our middle and younger child. The youngest is 4 and I've decided to just put Freetime on his. (After the Free month, it's $3 a month with Prime). Although, you do get it free for a year with the Kids, and a kid safe case AND a 'replace it no matter what they do to it guarantee'. SO, those ARE things to take into consideration if you have a young child. My hubby wanted to go with the less money route for us, which I understand, but you may decide to take another route. Just do your homework.So, what don't I like? I am a little disappointed in where they put the speaker. It's on the back on the bottom corner. So my son is always putting his fingers over it when we try to watch his Titanic Videos together. It makes it really hard to hear. One minute the sound is there, the next, it's muffled. So that's a bit of a bummer. I think they could have found a better location, but once you realize where it is, you can work around it.I'm also disappointed in the space. 8GB isn't 8GB. Not after ALL of the stuff that comes pre-loaded onto it and you can't remove. You're stuck with it whether you want it or not. So you have absolutely no space what-so-ever to start out with. That means you are forced to buy an SD card. So, right away just save yourself time and disappointment and purchase the SD card to go with it. Buy the one Amazon recommends, here: http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B013TMNKAW?psc=1&redirect=true&ref_=oh_aui_detailpage_o05_s00[[ASIN:B013TMNKAW SanDisk 32 GB micro SD Memory Card for All-New Fire Tablets and All-New Fire TV]]I got the 32 GB and we aren't anywhere close to filling any of them up yet. In fact, we've only used a couple of GB's on each, and each kid has a lot of apps/movies/shows downloaded onto it. You could go with the 16GB, but for the price, I really feel the 32 is worth it. If you can afford it, the 128GB probably is, which is the highest you can go with this tablet. But so far, I'm loving the 32 GB. It really offers the extra expansion that you need - and where Kindle dropped the ball.Those are my two reasons to dock a star. But my only two reasons. I love everything else. You can even put Google Play onto it. Just go visit How to Geek here: [...] and they will talk you through it. You don't even have to Root it. But you DO need a PC.I have an iPad, I've spent the big bucks on a tablet. Yes, I love my iPad. But as just an everyday consumer who doesn't need her tablet to do anything fancy other than entertain her and her kids (and do a little work here and there to pay the bills) - this works just fine for me. I've found I don't need to spend hundreds of dollars on a tablet and this $50 tablet works just fine for the kids and I. Of course, if you're looking for a Premium experience and you want it to do what the expensive tablets do, this one might not be for you.

title: An entire library in my bag
body: I've looked at Ebook readers in the past and not been seduced, perhaps because when I tried reading ebooks on various PDAs (Newton, Palm) I found the experience singularly unsatisfactory. For travel, I'd argue that I always have a laptop, so if I want to read on a device, I can use that (then I'd blithely pack several physical books). Thing is, I wasn't being exposed to electronic versions of books in any way, while the physical ones are everywhere and easy to acquire. Yes, I could seek them out. But I did not. Habits die hard, especially if there's little incentive to change them.I've been an Amazon customer for years -- back when they sent you a travel mug for Christmas just for buying a few books from them during the year. Kindle caught my attention when it was announced, but I ignored it because of the price tag. I'm too prone to early technology adoption and have paid for it in the past (you may have noticed my Newton reference above).As this summer progressed, though, I had a moment of expensive weakness. The wide range of titles availble for it on Amazon -- a place I'm accustomed to browsing -- triggered those same "gotta' have it" neurons that took me to the Apple store for an iPhone. Twice. I haven't regretted those purchases either.But enough about me.Pros:-The Kindle is lighter than most books and can hold hundreds, maybe thousands depending on what size memory card you add. But even without a memory card it holds more than you can read in a few days.-It's much easier than a laptop to pull out and start reading on an airplane.-Purchasing new books and other material is almost too easy when you're within Whispernet range.-If your favorite periodical is available, you can save a few trees with a Kindle subscription.-Sending personal and work documents to a Kindle is very easy and free or very cheap. There's something particularly cool about seeing your own short story looking exactly like a novel by your favorite author.-I'm not one to have a pencil handy when reading fiction, but the Kindle notes feature encourages me to make notes in everything (mostly noting typos!).-Publications are, for the most part, a bit cheaper than the printed versions.-Type size options let you put away the reading glasses.-The screen technology facilitates reading outdoors, unlike any other electronic device I have.-When you plug the Kindle into a computer via USB, it shows up as an external drive. You can drag and drop documents onto it (they have to be in the right format for Kindle to read them). Which means you can store Kindle documents on your computer when you don't need them in hand.Cons:-Reading material that relies on visuals, like some magazines, just doesn't work on the Kindle. Although it does display pictures, there's no color. Even the experience of reading the New York Times, the "grey lady" is disappointing -- only the page one photo is included. Occasionally later stories refer to photos that are not included. Who knew I would miss paging past the ads? I don't mind not getting the advertising inserts, however. But I do wish Kindle could support the crossword.-The page turn effect takes a little getting used to. First time I saw the screen go black, then blank, then build the next page, I thought "oh no, this is going to bug me." But in truth, I'm used to it now.-There aren't many travel books available (yet, I hope). When I travel, one of my toughest decisions is which guide to bring, which to Xerox a few pages from, and which to leave at home (yes, I usually buy several for a destination). Obviously Kindle has the potential to eliminate that problem.-There aren't very many periodicals available, although the list does seem to be growing quickly.-It's very easy to press the page turn buttons by accident. Granted, turning back is not difficult. But it bugs me enough that when I pick it up I have to expend a bit of mental energy thinking about avoiding the buttons -- something that never happens with a real book.-My biggest complaint is the lack of file management. All you can do is change the document sort (by author, by title, etc.). You can't create subfolders and organize by, say, personal, work, fiction, travel, unread, read... You can manage this by limiting the number of documents on your Kindle (keep them on your computer until you want them), but that kind of defeats the purpose of all that memory.Bottom line: I carry my Kindle daily because it's small and light enough not to notice. I buy books on the spur of the moment (maybe not a good thing) and have them in hand within seconds. An ebook reader is not for everyone, but if you've flirted with the technology, it's time to take a serious look.

title: UNSAFE.  Otherwise - Some Limits, but Great for the Price
body: Edit 03/22/2016 - WARNING! AMAZON IS IGNORING YOUR SECURITY!If you believe that the internet is a nice, safe place, and that you can hook into any open VPN hot spot with impunity, then you are a fool. Amazon knows this, and yet they make NO provision for a VPN program of any sort. Yes, it's "difficult" (insert whining sound), but for those of us who chose NOT to share our personal information with any slacker-hacker that happens by, it's essential. There are many good, solid, and *EASY* programs out there, some for free, some pay. Heck, Android has it built-in! Amazon had to intentionally, and with malice aforethought, DISABLE it!I pay good money for the VPN I use on all five of my devices (2xPC, 3xAndroid). But Amazon, of course, won't let me install it on this device. So yeah, goodbye Amazon. Too bad too, because it's otherwise a pretty good device. And yes, Amazon, you've already lost several purchases from me due to this blatant disregard for my security. Better learn fast, 'cos more and more people are starting to learn (the hard way) how important internet security is! (Reader: Don't worry, if you haven't learned yet, you will - one way or another!)---NOTE THAT THERE IS AN AD-FREE VERSION FOR $15 MORE! I really wish I'd known that in advance!Great little device for the price. Severely crippled version of Android though, so if you're expecting an Android tablet, be sure to read on.The "Fire OS" is based on Marshmallow and runs well. That being said, MANY of the options and customization options are removed. You cannot change the Launcher, Keyboard, and so on. Also, you do not have access to Google Play, so apps you may have already paid for, you may have to pay for again if you want it on the Fire as well. (You CAN sideload, if you know how. I can't speak for rooting, but that usually becomes possible on new devices in a few weeks).I did like the kid-friendly features. You can set up "profiles" for your kids, one for 10 years old and below, and one for above that age. The setup is fairly painless. You tap the button, choose the books, movies, shows, and apps you want that child to have access too, make a choice about whether they can use the web or not, and you're done. ALMOST! To be effective, you also have to lock your "Adult" account. I don't know if it prompts you or not, because I locked mine before I started messing with kid features. Once done, you just select the kid's "profile", and boom, the device is locked into the limited subset you've created. The one for under 10 is landscape and more "cartoonish" than the "Teen" one, but seems to operate similarly otherwise. To exit, you just pull down the menu and hit "Exit Profile". If you have multiple kids, I'd suggest putting PIN codes or swipe locks on each one to prevent them messing with each others'. You can also set "daily goals and time limits" (reading, etc.) and limit time by activity (Reading Books, Watching Video, Using Apps, Browsing Web). And there's a checkbox to "Block entertainment content until all daily educational goals are met". It's separate for weekdays and weekends. And when in a child profile, you cannot get into the settings without the Adult account password. You can also manage storage used by each kid profile.Speaking of storage, while the Fire is limited in many ways, it DOES support an SD card. While it doesn't seem to have Marshmallow's ability to treat the SD card as part of the main memory, it does dump almost all media there, so as a media consumption device, you have the ability to store a heck of a lot of stuff. I only have a 64GB card in now, but it says it'll support 128GB.If you use Audible, you'll love that it's integrated, not a separate app, and uses a "library and cloud" similar to the Kindle.If you have Prime, you'll love that you can use it to consume tons of free Prime content - and can also download much of it (but not all) so that you can watch/read/listen while on the road. I've got nine movies, 200+ songs, a number of books and about 20 audiobooks all stored on the SD card at the moment, not counting my personal video files which I copied over using the PC.This is also NOT a full-featured tablet/phone. It has front and rear cameras of reasonable quality, but little else in the area of sensors and such. The WiFi is 2.4GHz only (no 5GHz), no GPS, proximity, etc. Not sure if it has accelerometers or not. The speaker is mono and fine for simple sounds & such, but not for music & movies, so you'll want headphones or external speakers for that.Again, FOR THE PRICE it's a great product. But if you buy it expecting a full Android Tablet experience, you'll be horribly disappointed.

title: A Groundbreaking Device
body: Amazon has produced the breakthrough product for electronic reading with Kindle. It's the first model; has some faults, none major; works and works well. What sets it aside from prior efforts is (i) it is very readable - somewhat different but as usable as most books, (ii) there is a critical mass of content available to make give users access to most new publications, and a broad range of older books, and (iii) the genius of free wireless access.First, the reading experience. The screen is very readable for long periods without fatigue. This is not true of ordinary screens which have a refresh cycle that is fatiguing over long periods. There is less text on the screen than on the page of a typical book which means more page turning, but adjustment to this is easy after a few minutes. The type size is widely adjustable from very small to very large. I find that one size (3) is most readable under normal conditions for me, but the next larger (4) is handy in lower light conditions. The battery life seems excellent. I estimate that is will last for more than a complete average length book, assuming that the wireless feature is not on all of the time. In the hand, Kindle is much more comfortable in the nice, substantial cover that is included. People have complained that it is too easy to turn the page inadvertently because of the long control bars on each side. This is true, but not a major problem and less so when the cover is used. One thing I have found convenient is that Kindle can sit by itself on a table or on your lap without being held, unlike most books whose pages tend to flip over. You only have to tap one of the page bars occasionally to move on. And you never lose your place.I avoided buying earlier electronic books because of the small amount of content available. Amazon has solved most of this with its Kindle store. I estimate that 80-90% of the current books that I've looked for are available, and I'm certain that this will get better in the future with Amazon at the helm. What I didn't realize is that there also is a large amount of free out-of-copyright material out there as well. For example, Project Gutenberg has a huge amount of older texts that are readily downloaded to Kindle. I looked there for authors from Tolstoy to Henry James to Arthur Conan Doyle to Thucydides among many many more. They are all there with a few titles missing, but it's all completely free. Download to your computer and send as an email to your Kindle email address, and in moments they are on your Kindle in Kindle text. The available material is more than adequate now and I'd be surprised if it weren't comprehensive in the future.The free wireless access seals the deal. This means that wherever you have your Kindle (on Sprint's cell system) you have the entire electronic text world instantly available. At a moment's whim, you can look up a book and start reading it within a minute. And there are no ongoing charges for this service. It's free. Furthermore, if you purchase a book from the Kindle store, Amazon keeps it forever in your account without charge. If you erase it from your Kindle, you can retrieve it at any time at will. I don't know how we could ask for more.Complaints. I have very few of importance, however there are a few things it would be nice to see improve in the future. The page turning system could improve - that is less likely to happen inadvertently. I'm not sure of the solution, but they haven't gotten this quite right yet. Navigation within a book could be improved. There are a number of aids to getting around, but the one thing I miss is going to a specific page number (the reference numbers in Kindle are to, I think, line numbers which get pretty large in a normal length book, as the Kindle pages don't conform to book pages. The device is somewhat hard to hold without its cover. As others have mentioned, the corners are a bit sharp. Also, I probably would prefer a slightly larger screen, although the current one is quite adequate. Pictures and charts, etc., either aren't there or don't reproduce well. This is a technological problem that may have to wait for future solutions. Overall, the drawbacks are minor annoyances that are overshadowed by the advantages. I take off one star because this isn't quite perfect.Numerous other reviews have complained that Kindle doesn't do many of the things that their PDA, cell phone or laptop computer can do. Look - this is a special purpose device designed to do one difficult thing really well and largely succeeds. If one buys a Kindle, it's an electronic book, not something else - look at it in that light. Overall, Amazon has produced a real world changing device that I'm sure will improve in the future, but is the start of a new paradigm. My hats off to them for a great start.

title: Love it despite its drawbacks
body: CON: If you are like me and don't read the fine print in the agreements, you will miss the fact that there are advertisements on this Kindle that you cannot get rid of unless you pay $15 to Amazon to remove them. I'm not talking about a small banner across the bottom like some free apps. These are a full page advertisement usually for games right on the first screen when you turn it on or let it go to sleep and then turn back on.CON: The Kindle is a nice size but for small hands like mine, it's hard to hold without it slipping out of your hand. It did not leave my living room where there is carpet until I purchased and received a MoKo case to protect it and to make it easier to grip and hold onto, not to mention to be able to prop it up for reading. The screen was all finger-printed and smudged before I even finished learning my way around it, but i don't like the screen protectors--they get smudged up, too. I don't like that my home screen is cluttered up with so many icons to search through, so I created folders for different apps so only the ones I use most are directly on my home screen.CON: The charger cord was very tight the first couple of times I plugged it into the Kindle, but it very quickly became loose. It takes a LONG time to charge it to 100% battery, and it takes a SHORT time to use it all up. The USB cord is also very short--not even 2'--which means I have to lean over the edge of my recliner and hold the Kindle if I want to read while it's charging. So I ordered a different longer cable that is supposed to charge faster and also give me more distance from the outlet when I do have to use it while charging.CON: I do not like the stock Silk browser mainly because you can't go forward and backward as easily. You can go backward but then if you want to go "right" to the one you were on previously, it doesn't do that. It goes back to the one before the last one--hard to explain! PRO: It does have the option to request desktop site vs. mobile, option to enter private browsing, add bookmarks, share, and print. I could not figure out how to import my bookmarks into Silk from my Chrome browser on my desktop.CON: There are several apps I will never use and there is no easy way to uninstall them and their icons on the home page. So moving them into a "junk" folder is the only way to get them out of my way.CON: When I purchased Prime membership, I was under the impression that the only way you could get Prime free videos, etc. was through the Kindle device itself. That's not totally true. I can get the movies on my laptop and hook my laptop to my HDTV and watch them. There is no way to plug in the Kindle to any device. The only port is the micro USB for charging, and I don't know if that would be able to output to the TV. I do hook the USB to my laptop for copying pictures, files, etc.PRO: Now to the CAMERA: I absolutely love it! It takes 200% better photos than my cell phone and videos, too. They are crisp and clear no matter what kind of light you're in. On my cell phone, I was always moving my hand ever so slightly and would get blurred pictures, but not with the Kindle. The camera alone was worth the purchase price of the Kindle. I haven't take a lot of photos or videos yet because I've only had it a few days, so I don't know how many the Kindle will hold. Time will tell.CON: This Kindle does not come with Google Play Store even though its apps are Android-based. This is frustrating because I have some favorite apps on Google that I cannot find a version to work with the Kindle, including a Google Play Store app. Some APK files I download will install and work fine, and others won't.PRO: Overall, I love this Kindle. It's not big and heavy for carrying in my purse. It is versatile like a tablet and runs many apps just like a tablet. It starts and shuts down very quickly--no waiting. I can get a lot more free books from the lending library and more selection. If I want to watch a movie and I'm not home, I can just find wifi and enjoy a movie. I think you can download movies and save to the device for those times when you're not near wifi, but I haven't fully investigated all its features yet.I'm looking forward to taking this with me when I go away instead of carrying my laptop, and looking forward to great pictures with it.UPDATE: After 2 days I discovered the cord wasn't staying in tight and therefore not charging correctly, and the camera was distorted. So I returned it and they replaced this new one with a refurbished one which I returned as well, got credit, and then bought another new one. So far the new one is working well, but I just got it today. Time will tell.

title: Great Device, But Not A Panacea
body: I like my Kindle a lot, but it's not the complete panacea I had hoped for.Pros:- Very readable display. Ink is very clear and is as readable as a real book.- Small and lightweight; I love that I can carry dozens of books in the space of a single small book. Great for my back and shoulders, since I typically carried a number of books at once in my attache case.- Purchase of new books is instantaneous, and they download very quickly, all from the device itself. Can browse/search Amazon listings and purchase books right from the device. No computer/internet required for any of that.- Uses a cellular connection, so it's easy to connect from anywhere like a cellphone, no wireless internet needed. This service is free, as is the browsing and downloading of books.- Can add bookmarks, highlights, and even personal notes to pages/sections of books. These are browsable on their own screen.- Can do (*very simple*) web browsing.- Cheap subscriptions available to some periodicals (like NY Times, Newsweek, even some blogs), with automatic downloading of the latest issue.- Amazon auomatically saves all your Amazon-purchased content, including bookmarks, notes, etc., so if you lost your Kindle, you could buy a new one and download everything and have it just like it was.- Many Kindle books are cheaper than their real-world counterparts.- Can also store PDFs and TXTs on the device, though it's a bit more laborious to create the files properly and transfer them to the device from your computer via USB.- You can upload some MP3s to it, and listen with headphones while you read. This is great fun. (Shuffle function only!)- Can delete books you're not currently reading, and re-download them anytime, with bookmarks/notes intact.- Accepts an SD memory card for additional space.- Free samples of virtually every book, though occasionally a sample doesn't go much past the table of contents, and in those cases it doesn't really help.Cons:- Page-turning is a bit slow...you have to wait a moment before every new page shows up, which makes skimming through a book or article rather laborious, and disrupts your rhythm if you're a fast reader and you're into a really captivating novel!- Not all books are available, so you still have to buy real books, depending on how mainstream or arcane your tastes are. A lot of my books that I wanted to get rid of after getting the Kindle are not available on the Kindle, so I had to keep the real books anyway. (But there were also a ton I could get rid of, assuming I didn't mind basically buying them again, this time the Kindle version.)- Going back and finding a particular section in a Kindle book doesn't seem as easy as with a real book, unless I've already added bookmarks and notes the first time that I can go back and click to, and even that's a little time-consuming. Kindle does have a search function, and books usually come with a clickable Table of Contents, but still...the Kindle kind of gets in the way when you're using it this way.- I miss out on all the "character" of the book itself, the colorful design and appeal of its cover that makes you want to read it, the feel and weight of it in your hands, the satisfaction of holding/curling up with the book and turning pages and skimming through it.- You can't share your Kindle books with others, or give them to another interested reader when you're done with them.- User-created content is not always structured properly, and therefore displays awkwardly on the Kindle, with weird page breaks, margins, etc. I've had to check out free samples of multiple versions of a non-mainstream book to find the one that's the most legible, and sometimes the only choice is a poorly executed one.I recommend the Kindle, as long as your expectations are a good fit. :-)I think it's terrific for novels and other occasions where you're reading straight through from beginning to end, especially popular, mainstream content. But when using it to store reference materials where your reading is specific and/or non-linear (or when reading more obscure material), it seems a bit clunky and awkward. Others may disagree with me on this point; I'm probably not fully leveraging its capabilities in this way. But with a real book, I can find something I'm looking for in 10-15 seconds. With the Kindle, it feels like it's harder and takes longer, so I avoid doing it, reducing the value of the device to me.I'm a voracious reader, and I thought I would use it for everything, but I found that it fills a rather particular function in my library, and I still have and use lots of real books.I'm interested to see what version 2.0 will be like.

title: Great value tablet - not perfect, but you definitely get your money's worth
body: My first impression as I've had this a day - if you want a nice value tablet, this is the one to get. For $50, you aren't going to do any better. Are there better tablets? Yes, but you are going to have to spend at least $150 to get a better 7-8" tablet.Pros:- Price can't be beat- Expandable storage so you aren't hobbled by a small internal storage- Screen resolution isn't bad, on par with an iPad 2 and original iPad Mini- Headphone, charging port and controls are all located on the 'top' when in portrait mode. It's nice because you can rest it on something in portrait mode and not have cables getting crushed like on most other devices where at least one is located on the bottom.Cons:- The microSD card is a bit difficult to get in an out- With the speaker on the back, it's easy for sound to get muffled if you put it down on a flat surface or depending on how you hold it with your hand (my cheap ASUS tablet has the speaker facing forward, which is much more user friendly)- Screen very prone to fingerprint smudging, might be worth getting a stylus- Videos on SD don't organize by TV show and no method to sort them, but that really seems to be an Android failing with even 3rd party apps unable to do that- Personal documents from Send to Kindle don't show up in the Kindle App (like on iOS and Android or even older Fire tablets), but instead to go a Docs App. Took me 10 minutes to figure that out and only stumbled on it by accident, not something I found on the web.- There is no easy way to natively access video stored on an SD cardFor this, you pay for what you get, and you get a great value. In my limited use this is a great tablet for $50. I had no problems getting video in mp4 format to play on the tablet using the native program, though you will get more options from a 3rd party app. The screen resolution is slightly better than the iPad 2 I also have and I really have no complaints.
