Folks may tout how many pixels something has and how much smoother something looks, but honestly, I haven't noticed (and most folks won't). The only thing more pixels do is eat up battery faster once you hit a certain point.Responsiveness in game play has been pretty good. I haven't played any apps that would stress the system, but it seems like it's at least as good as the cheap ASUS tablet I picked up last year for $100 (which makes sense since this is not cutting edge technology).Not having used the Fire OS before, it took a little bit of getting used to, but overall it's not bad to use. There is much less bloatware than you would find on something like an ASUS tablet (which honestly has at least 20 apps I have no interest in, yet no way to delete) and that term is used loosely - Audibles, Mail, Contacts, Newstand, Weather and such I consider bloatware just because this is mostly going to be for the kids.As I said, video and sound are pretty decent for something so cheap, though I would recommend headphones for a better experience, but the sound is no more tinny than on any other single speaker device lying around my house. If you are going to use it for video, make sure you keep a cleaning cloth around because it picks up fingerprints very well. Video quality is decent and playback seems pretty flawless. I'm not uploading HD because this isn't an HD display and I want to have more video on the card for the kids to watch. On that note, using the native Video app, it just loads them all into one big list - which if you are including a lot of short cartoons, leads to an unmanageable list with no way to sort them. This seems to be an Android failing where it will not recognize tags from video to sort them by show. Guess I got used to having iTunes and iOS do that for me. If you have video in folders, you can organize those and use a 3rd party app to access those folders, but again, season and episode tag info will not be evaluated, so if you have something you want to view in a particular order, add numbers to the file names.For reading, this is slightly heavier than my Kindle touch. This would be great for using in the evenings in a dimly lit/dark room, but probably not so much for outside where the glare would get you. It's still lighter than my iPad and provides more real estate than my phone. It's pretty easy to change the brightness to minimize eye strain.I can't really speak to battery life. It came with 60% and after a total of about 2 hours messing around with it to get used to it, about half an hour of reading and installing a dozen or so apps it was only down to 40%, so it seems reasonable to say you might get around 7-8 hours of mixed use.

title: Kindle and Sony 505 compared-Kindle wins (surprisingly)
body: I never thought I'd like reading a book on an electronic device, despite the fact that I am the quintissential geek in most other things. Well, the Kindle and the Sony 505 eBook Reader have proven me wrong. The text reads like a real book, but I can carry thousands of books with me at any time--assuming I use and SD card. That makes less difference than the fact that I will no longer take up (literally) whole rooms to store my collection of novels I will likely never read but can't face parting with.I have both the Sony 505 and the Kindle and I like them both. I prefer the form factor and 8 level grayscale on the Sony, as well as the fact that it supports folders ("collections"). Frankly, the Kindle is butt-ugly... no other way to say it. (Kinda like the Zune compared with an iPod--both work but one is so much more elegant.) I also appreciate being able to read Sony eBooks on my computer if you want (e.g. if your Reader went belly up in the middle of a book).PDF files display in their native appearance on the Sony (although multicolumn text can be hard to read sometimes) and can be loaded directly, whereas the conversion process for the Kindle, which requires sending a PDF to Amazon for conversion, is not only more cumbersome but the resulting product is often unreadable (formatting, not text size), particularly for multi-column PDFs.I do not perceive any difference in "brightness" or contrast between the two screens, but images look better on the Sony than on the Kindle. No biggie. I have no intention of using either to read photo-laden books. Text readability is equal on the two, IMHO, and that's what counts.I really expected to prefer the Sony overall, but in the end the following features of the Kindle lead me to conclude that I prefer it:Search is wonderful. Have you ever read a book, been introduced to a character early on than not encountered him again for 100 pages, by which time you've forgotten exactly who he is. Not a problem with the Kindle. Just search for the character's name and :::poof::: you can get right back to where you first "met" him. To me this is HUGE. And it is remarkably fast.Whispernet is also very nice. First, because I can browse a brick and mortar bookstore and buy what strikes my fancy on the spot from my Kindle, at a fraction of the price. And, second, because it allows me an alternative to my iPhone (in areas where ATT coverage may be spotty) when I want to check the weather or the headlines on a long trip. And EDVO seems a LOT faster than my iPhone's EDGE network.Five (or is it six?) font sizes on the Kindle gives me lots more flexibility than three on the Sony.The other big plus for the Kindle is the variety and cost of books. Almost everything I have bought thusfar (mostly new release hardcover bestsellers) costs 3-4 dollars less on the Kindle than on the Sony. On the Kindle a newly released novel runs about half of the online price of the same book in hardcover. Over time that is a significant hunk of change. The idea of being able to pay paperback pricing for a novel the minute it comes out is the most compelling feature of the Kindle, followed closely by search (vide supra).The buttons are a mixed bag. On the one hand I like the size, in that they are easy to find and press, but on the other hand inadvertent button presses are still a problem. And, more importantly, the BACK button does not always take me back to where I expected it would. This could readily be fixed in a software update.What could the Kindle improve on? Support folders (called "collections" on the Sony) to allow users to organize their content in a way that is meaningful to them Provide native PDF support, in sizes that permit easy readability yet retain multicolumn formatting Do something about the form factor to make it more visually appealing, more compact (thin mainly), and improve the case (which is next to useless--doubling the thickness of the device and not providing secure hold on the Kindle. Make the corners that rest on my chest when reading lying down a little less sharply pointed. Work on button size and placement to decrease the incidence of inadvertent button presses Go with at least 8-level gray scaleOverall, both the Sony 505 and the Kindle are excellent at what they do, but if I were recommending a single eBook reader to someone, I would favor the Kindle (lower book prices, better selection, search function and Whispernet being the reasons).Do I think eBooks are the way of the future? Yup, now I do. I am a convert.

title: My updated Kindle review? 5 STARS!!!!
body: I am a reading fanatic. I love to be surrounded by books, and to me, books are more than just paper binded together, they deserve respect. My favorite place to go is the bookstore---its my candy shop or toy store. This being said, my husband is getting me the Kindle for an early Christmas present. I feel like a kid again---being this excited over something! I give it four stars because it is a fantastic idea, well thought out and executed with some truly excellent features, but I don't have it in my hands yet. I own a laptop, a PC, and an ipod and I expect them to do what they were made to do, just as I expect the Kindle to do what it was created for: reading print materials. For me, real books are not novels or page after page of print, they are those that are pieces of art in and of themselves---books that are balanced and enhanced by pictures, drawings, maps, and in color. I will never give those up and I don't expect to use my Kindle for that. I expect to use my Kindle to try to satisfy my unquenchable thirst for more and more knowledge that is normally sandwiched between two paper covers. Just like with real books, I will be able to go back and re-read any of those that I want. When I want an "experience" I will go to my bookshelf and dig out my Seneca Ray Stoddard book or my pictoral tour of the Lewis and Clark trail, to complement the material I am reading. There are two kinds of books for me--the ones that you read and re-read and those that compliment those books. The Kindle isn't supposed to take the place of books that are more than just a story and it isn't supposed to look like some super mod chrome plated hunk of metal. When I'm reading a book, I'm reading it to be absorbed in the story, not to look at the paper its printed on and say, wow that's really white paper. My point is, the people who are giving bad reviews on this are expecting it to do everything except wipe their a**. It's just supposed to give you several different reading options---books, magazines, blogs, newspapers, in a compact little package that is unassuming and doesnt take away from what you have it for----reading. It's so interesting how so many people can take something that is so simplistic and pure and want more and more when that isn't the point of the product. Oh sorry Amazon, you've got something there but it doesn't look cool so I'm going to buy the Sony. Well you know what? You people can have it. It's so interesting how in our society the value of something is judged so heavily by it's looks from people right on down to a cutting edge yet simple ebook reader. Great job Amazon, it sounds like a winner and I sure hope I feel that way when I get mine. I'll update my rating after I've played around with it for awhile.MY KINDLE HAS ARRIVED AND IT GETS 5 STARS!!!!I have my Kindle and I am on fire!!! I love my Kindle. I take it everywhere in the case that came with it and I don't worry about it falling out because that little band on the back? Yeah, that is supposed to go around the case and hold the Kindle in. The design is great---for the people that think it looks antiquated, if you look at the Kindle in its case you will realize that it is designed to look like the right page of an open book from the thicker left side (binding) to the tapered right side. It really is ingenious. The eink is fantastic, the screen is easy on the eyes. My navigation buttons don't make any noise like some reviewers had said yet there is a definate "click" feel so that you know you have pressed the button. The black screen that appears for a split second when "page turning" isn't even an issue, it is like turning a page in a book (actually it is faster). Reading with the Kindle in it's case is like reading a book, I hold it just like a book and have even reached to turn the page a few times. The Kindle also plays MP3 files for those people who like music when they read. The slot for the sd card is under the grey cover in back which I really like so that dirt and dust cant infiltrate and it won't accidentally pop out. I love my Kindle. I love that I can tote it around and have the latest news at my fingertips and a selection of books that will keep me reading for a very long time. If your expectations are that it will just be an ebook, you will be pleasantly surprised to find that it is much more than that. If you expect the Kindle to take the place of your pda, blackberry, laptop, cell phone, palm pilot, etc. you will be disappointed and have missed the whole purpose of the Kindle anyway. My only real complaint: I just wish that Amazon had included more free time for me to use my Kindle!!

title: Solid tablet when you consider price; not for everyone.
body: Okay, this device seems to be polarizing. Personally, I think it's a pretty good machine in general, especially when you consider that you can't go for a nice dinner for what you pay for it. However, if you're expecting this to be an iPad replacement, it won't be. If you want that, but don't want an iPad, I recommend the excellent Samsung Galaxy Tab Pro or S lines. However, if you're a Prime member, and if you want a great little tablet to give to kids, supplement a larger tablet, or just don't have a ton of money to spend, this is a great choice to consider.FIRE OS/ADSOkay, unlike most reviewers, I'm going to start with software, as that surprises people the most. While Fire OS based on Android, it is NOT stock Android. For me, there are good things and bad things with that. For accessing Amazon content (the reason Amazon's selling this for $50 or less), the OS works well. There are special tabs on the OS for books, movies, and even shopping on Amazon. I like just swiping to pull up my Kindle books. Another thing that I like is that Amazon seems more likely than other Android device makers to update their OS. I actually had my Fire update its OS a couple of days after I got it. My Android phone has been updated once since it was new, and that was only to remove a serious bug.Fire OS also limits you to the Amazon App Store unless you do some extra work to get Google Play on it. There are far fewer apps in the Amazon store, and they tend to be updated more slowly. However, I LOVE Amazon Underground, a program sort of like Kindle Unlimited that gives you apps for free, and includes all in-app purchases. I was able to pick up Monument Valley and Aaltos Adventure with Underground (both of which you should go put on your Fire as soon as you get it).The other big one is the ads. There are ads. Let me say it again. Unless you pay $15, THERE ARE ADS. However, these ads are on the lock screen only, are static and not noisy, and they go away as soon as you swipe up. I can see people not wanting kids to see them, but an adult really shouldn't have any problem with them.UNDER THE HOODThis tablet isn't going to win any speed awards. It's got a 1.3Ghz Quad-core processor and 1GB of RAM. That's equivalent to pretty much an entry-level phone. That said, I haven't noticed many problems with it. Starting apps has a bit of a lag, but once they get going, they seem to run fine. I was convinced that Asphalt 8, the app I use to test whether a tablet/phone/whatever will be adequate for my gaming needs, would be unplayable. To my surprise, it ran just fine, although without the super-sharp graphics that it has on my Tab Pro.SCREENThe screen is adequate. It's a bit less than HD, but it's small enough you don't really notice. It's clear and bright enough to see, with good viewing angles. I prefer my Tab Pro for watching video, but the screen is just fine for reading or playing simple games.SOUNDThis is the only place the Fire really falls down. The speaker is on the back, which is fine, but it's just not powerful enough (and I'm pretty easy to please when it comes to speakers). You'll need headphones or a Bluetooth speaker if you want to use this.BUILD QUALITYI expected this to feel cheap, but it really doesn't to me (I guess I'm used to cheap phones). Yes, the back is plastic, and the screen isn't Gorilla Glass, but it's well constructed, doesn't creak, and has a nice weight to it.DESIGNI think Amazon was really thoughtful about the design of this tablet. People complain about the large bezel, but I think it's actually helpful when you're trying to hold the tablet in one hand and not tap the screen. I also like the choice to have all of the buttons and jacks on the same side of the tablet, and having the volume buttons aware of which way you are holding the tablet was really smart. Additionally, if you're going to have a plastic tablet, own it and get it in one of the bright colors!SHOULD YOU BUY?So the bottom line. Whether you should buy this really depends on what you want from it. If you have Prime and/or are content to stay in Amazon's ecosystem, and if you aren't expecting a ton of computing power, yes. If you want a ton of apps and a ton of computing power, no, but be aware you'll pay a lot more for the privilege.TL;DRPROS:*It's super cheap*Great integration with Amazon*Well-designed*Free apps!CONS:*It's a bit slow starting things up*Relatively small app selection*Bad speaker

title: Others need to come into the new age!
body: I was very anxious after I ordered my kindle. I ordered it on a Friday and selected two day shipping so it could arrive on Tuesday. I told myself that it was only 4 days away. What I didn't expect to happen was for me to actually be excited and impatient for the wait. I've tried the Sony e-reader and I didn't get nearly as excited after I had purchased that.From the outside, it's appearance may be appaling at first. Don't let that hinder your thoughts on purchasing one though. It has a very nice feel in your hands once you figure out how to hold the thing without turning the pages. There are two design flaws that I see that I hope are improved up in the infamous second edition that will not be out for at least a few years. They could round out the corners so it doesn't eat a dimple into your palms while reading. Secondly, they could rethink the position of the page turners. I do like that they are on both sides, but maybe place them so you could actually grab the sides without screwing up your place. I think that with the second versions of anything, they are a welcomed upgrade. The placing of the SD card is problematic when you want to change them or actually put one in, but in relooking at it's placement, there really isn't a better place for it.So enough with the specs. With that being my only area for complaint, being the design flaws, reading on this is actually more enjoyable than a paper back for me. I don't have to fidget around to find an uncomfortable position for me that supports the book without turning any of it's pages. With the Kindle...I sit in any position I want, not what the book wants. Turning the pages is flawless...albeit slow, but after reading a few pages, you slowly adjust to the fact of it taking time. And after a few chapters, you don't even notice that anymore. The saving the page you left off on feature is awesome as I frequently am doing ten billion things at once and then end up reading the same passage at least 5 times before I realize that I need to stop coming to this page after coming back. And the built in dictionary is awesome as I am hard of hearing and often times do not fully understand what all english words mean even though I speak perfect english. It's awesome that it's right at my fingertips and I don't even leave my page or lose my place.When I took mine out of the well designed box, it started up and I didn't need any time to aquire a tasting for it. I knew exactly how to run it being the techie that I am. I learned how to use all the specs in under ten minutes after just fiddling with it. If you aren't good with technology, I highly suggest reading the on-board user's guide as it explains how to do everything.I took it over to a friend of mine, I am 21 and her and her husband are well into their 50's. They had no problems after changing the font size as I read at the level one. They used setting 3 and 4 and were very impressed. My grandma fell in love with the size 6 font. No longer needing to hunt for the large print books, she is purchasing hers soon. All three of them got aquainted quite quickly with it after a few intructions from me.I have had no problems ordering books and having them delivered right to me in under a minute. I bought 20 books the first day I owned it...and three days later I am finished with 4 of them...still going on my first charge. I did turn my wireless internet off to make the battery last longer.The only reason I hesitate to give it five stars is the design flaws. If you can get past your palms having permanent dimples :-) this is the perfect device for you. I live in a small apartment and face arguments with my boyfriend over space and my love of books. Problem is solved now. So if you are an avid booklover with no space in your house or apartment, this is a very practical solution. You look at the price and think it's a bit much, but think of the money you save on books. With my initial 20 book purchase, I saved almost $200...half of my kindle was paid for right then and there and my boyfriend isn't complaining about space for those 20 books either. I no longer have to justify my must-have book choices. Wish this would have come out about 7 years ago when I started my library!So what are you waiting for? Go buy one! Obviously if you are reading all the reviews you can get your hands on, you know you want one! Just do it! You only live once and regrets will not follow this purchase! You'll thank us all later when you have your own and you can inspire others!

title: Best value for the money in a tablet.
body: To start, this is a very durable tablet. This is not a beautiful-looking tablet, but it is made of durable polycarbonate. If I dropped this onto the floor, I wouldn't worry about it breaking. Even if it does break, just buy another one...they're only $50. I'd cringe if I accidentally dropped my iPad Air...not so with the Fire. The Fire's compact size makes it easy to carry around and use, even with one hand.If you're in the market for a tablet and looking at this, you're probably cross-shopping it with an iPad. After all, while expensive, iPads are the best tablets money can buy. That being said, tablets exist in this weird kind of middle space in between smartphones and laptops. We use our smartphones to stay connected, and our laptops to get stuff done...in this way, tablets by their very nature are "luxury," non-essential devices. However, before you make a decision, consider how you're likely to use a tablet.If you've never used a tablet before, and you're wondering how one will fit into your digital world, buy the Amazon Fire. Even if you find you really don't have a need for it, it's only $50 so you can't really go wrong.Do you use a tablet edit .cad files, create documents and presentations, do a lot of drawing/sketching, and/or do heavy photo and 4K video editing on the go? In this case, a.) I'm very impressed, and b.) buy a 9.7-inch iPad Pro.Then there's someone like me. You mainly use a tablet to quickly triage emails and poke at the web and Twitter while lying in bed or sitting on the couch. When you're on the go, you like to do some reading, watch movies and TV shows, and play some light games. For these types of tasks related to consuming content, buy this tablet. Of course, an iPad will do all of these things better. But for just $50, the Amazon Fire will do all of these things well enough to more than justify its meager $50 price tag. This is a full-featured tablet that does all things most people would want a tablet to do. And again...$50.I'm not going to get into the specs; what you need to know is that this tablet is good enough for most things. Its display and audio are good enough, it can allow you to read books good enough, it plays back both streamed and locally-downloaded videos good enough, handles email good enough, runs your apps good enough, and even plays games good enough.Performance is reasonably snappy. Apps take a few steps longer to load than on an iPad, and some more intensive games can get choppy, but this is by no means a bad experience at all. Games like Sonic Rush, Badland, and even Real Racing 3 run very smoothly, with minimal frame drops. I'm actually surprised at how well this performs in everyday use.The display resolution is by no means Retina, but it is a fine panel for a tablet this cheap. Text is easy to read (if not super sharp), and videos and games look good.The Fire runs Amazon Fire OS 5.0 "Bellini," which in reality is a heavily-skinned version of Android 5.0 Lollipop. It is very easy to use and navigate, and presents the content you own (as well as suggested content for you to buy) on different categories from the homescreen. I have no complaints. The lockscreen ads are a non-issue...this entire tablet is basically an advertisement for how much stuff you can buy from Amazon anyway, so it didn't bother me.There are some issues though. One has to do with the Amazon Appstore. Instagram is inexplicably absent. Same goes for the Asphalt racing games (which I enjoy), YouTube, and Dropbox. On the plus side, you'll find that MOST of your favorite apps are accounted for. This includes Facebook, Twitter, Netflix, Hulu, Crackle, HBO Now, LinkedIn, Yelp, Skype, etc., as well as most of the more popular mobile games. Amazon's Underground store contains a good selection of free apps, including in-app purchases in some instances.There are issues with dead pixels on the display. The initial Fire I purchased had three dead pixels. I exchanged it for a new one, and I count four dead pixels on this display. Just something to be aware of. Also, the cameras stink (expected for $50), but you really shouldn't be taking pictures with a tablet anyway. The front-facing VGA camera is horrible, but it's fine for Skype calls and the like.Overall, this is an excellent tablet for the money that I'd recommend to anyone that needs a solid, cheap tablet to do typical tablety things. Just be aware- you want to have an Amazon Prime membership if you want to get the most out of it.

title: Good tablet, lousy kid profiles.
body: BACKGROUND:We got this as a Christmas present for our kids (all elementary age). We have a lot of educational apps that our kids use, so we thought we'd get this tablet for them. We do not require high performance from our tablets. We previously bought an Asus Memo pad 8 tablet that our whole family has been using. We also have a Motorola Moto G LTE phone. Neither device is top of the line, but both are adequate for our needs (and fun).THE GOOD:(1) The tablet actually arrived before Christmas (delivery was estimated for Dec 30), so Kudos to Amazon for that.(2) The tablet updated fairly quickly, and was pleasantly fast. I had honestly expected it to be slower for the price tag.(3) Most of the apps are able to be installed on the SD card, which saves space on the limited internal memory.(4) Many android devices either do not allow apps to be installed on the sd card, or run poorly with apps on the sd card. We have found no issues with running the apps from the sd card on this tablet. Kudos again to Amazon!(5) We bought the "sponsored" tablet to save money, and we are pleased that the lock-screen advertisements have not been inappropriate - especially since this tablet is for our children.(6) The battery holds its charge fairly well, perhaps better than the Asus.THE BAD(1) One of Amazon's big emphases is its kid-friendliness, and the ability to create kid profiles. Much of the bad has to do with the un-usabilty of the kid profiles for our needs. a) To set up a kid profile, Amazon REQUIRES the age and gender of the child. Entering fictitious info is not a problem, but I still would rather that my children not be profiled at this young age! I would prefer Amazon to make that info optional. b) Once the kid profile is set up, you can share apps and kindle books with the kid profile. However it seems like it has to re-download(?) the apps. When you try to open them in the kid profile for the first time, they sit and load from somewhere for a long time! c) Once the apps are loaded into the kid profile, good luck in trying to organize them. They do not organize. Nor do you have multiple pages on which to place them. Instead they simply scroll in a long (sideways) stream. d) If you have a kindle library like us (over 1000 books, most of which are kids books), you probably have them organized into categories of some sort for easy finding. However, the kid profile does not recognize your categories, nor does it allow you to create new ones. Instead, it tries to place the books into its own "character" categories. It does not make it easy for kids or parents to find the books they are looking for! e) Our kids listen to a lot of audio books, so we thought the kindle fire would be a good resource for that. We downloaded a book from the audible app in the parent's profile (the only way amazon allows you to download an audio book, aside from kindle audio). However try as we might, we could not share that audio book with the kid profile (nor with the teen profile). f) We have numerous audio books of our own from other resources, so we loaded them onto the sd card. But try as we might, we could not access them from the kid profile!2. Downloading from the Amazon App store seems very, very slow. Maybe it was because it was Christmas, but downloads took forever! It was not a wifi problem with the device or our wifi.WORKAROUNDS(1) We gave up on the kid profiles. Using Parental Controls on the main profile, we are able to restrict access to things we don't want our kids getting into (settings, app store, etc), but they are able to access all the content we want them to have. They can see and use our kindle categories, they can listen to audio books, etc.(2) Thankfully, Amazon has not completely locked down this tablet. It IS possible to install the Google Play app without rooting the device. Google Play installed smoothly. (Google Play apps downloaded MUCH faster than Amazon Store apps.) a) One thing to note - you can set amazon store apps to automatically install to your sd card. Anything installed from Google Play must be manually moved to the sd card.CONCLUSIONThis is a good tablet at a great price. Setup may take a while due to the speed of the Amazon app store, and depending on if you want Google Play or not. The kid profiles are perfectly useless for our needs, but the work-around does the job. All-in-all, a great buy!

title: Wonderful device, just needs a little work
body: I'm not sure why anyone would want yet another review of Kindle, but now that I've had mine for a few weeks, I want to make a few comments on it. First, I love it--the rating should really be 4 and a half. But it's not perfect, so I can't give it a straight 5.Good things:--I really love being able to carry this many books around, and I will always have one, whether they improve it or not. I will especially be using it when I travel; it's so wonderful to be able to carry a whole load of books with me, without loading up the suitcase.--Speaking of travel, Amazon has set up Kindle with a universal power adapter, so it can be used in any country (you still need to have a plug adapter for the specific system of each country, but you don't need to worry about plugging it in at the hotel and having it burn up). If you do any traveling outside the US, this is a huge advantage, and I haven't seen this mentioned in too many reviews.--It is so easy to get new books; the downloading process is very fast and very simple (this is also dangerous; I think I'll put this down as a disadvantage, too!)--The leather cover gets a lot of criticism, but I think it works pretty well, and I feel safer using it, to avoid any damage to the Kindle. I did put a couple of pieces of velcro inside the cover to hold the Kindle in place better.--pretty good variety of books available. I have downloaded out of print books I read as a kid, science textbooks for reference, current fiction and non-fiction.--Being able to read a sample first is great. I can then decide to buy the book, put it on a wish list, or forget about it.--It's lightweight enough that it is pretty easy to read in bed, easier than reading the original thick book.--Finding your way around a book is improved by the bookmark system. I have marked and highlighted favorite passages that I like to go back to, and it is fairly workable, but see navigation issues below.Things that need improvement:--The page turning process is a little slow and disruptive. I am a fast reader, and I do have to pause while the pages turn. I don't care about the "flash", but it would be nice to speed up the process. It is not quite fast enough to be totally transparent. Page turning is especially slow if the new page has an image; they take a while to load, apparently.--Speaking of images, don't buy a Kindle book if you especially need to be able to see the figures clearly. The figures are reproduced, but they are not nearly as clear as they would be in a book. I haven't checked to see if there is a function that zooms in in the figures, but that would help. This is a problem if you are reading anything with maps, diagrams, etc. I notice it particularly when reading scientific books. If they could put the figures on a web page for separate viewing, it might help...--As others have mentioned, it is a bit hard to hold without turning the page accidentally. There needs to be a place on both sides of the Kindle that one can hold on to while reading.--Navigation needs to be tweaked a little bit. I often turn back a few pages to consult something that was written earlier, and it is a lot slower with Kindle than it is with a real book. The bookmark process is pretty good, but it could be improved. In old favorite books, I like to just find certain favorite passages (eg, the meeting in chapter 5, the place where the expedition sets out, the discussion I especially liked...). In a physical book, I know pretty much where those passages are, and can riffle through and find them quickly. With Kindle, I have to have already found and bookmarked the place, or I can page through in increments of a tenth of the book, then click through to the right page, but it is a slow process.--I did find one book where the text was slightly garbled: paragraphs from one chapter had been put into another chapter out of order. I have emailed Amazon about it; we'll see if it gets fixed.--Of course, not all books are available. I expect that will improve, though some may never be available for Kindle. However, if I run out of things to read, I can always download War and Peace for 99 cents; that ought to keep me busy for a while!--Oh, I almost forgot, the ease of downloading new books can be a problem for the budget!In summary, I really like my Kindle, and don't regret being an early adopter, but I hope they can make a few improvements for the next round(especially the page turning, folks!)

title: Good solid companion for travel or breaktime.
body: Bought one out of a spur of the moment. Didn't really need or want one, just decided eh, why not? I'll give it a try.Critical details:If I were reviewing it right out of the box I would probably have to rate either 3 or 4 stars. I've been an android fanboy for the past few years and have purchased a couple handfuls of apps on the google play store, so to have a device that is, at it's core, an android tablet.. to not have the Play Store come with and require you to flash it on using ADB, a packaged .BAT and a little bit of computer literacy due to the manufacturer wanting to promote their own digital services? Shameful, I'd say.. but that's just me. If you don't mind taking the time to flash the GAPPS yourself, you don't mind paying a few dollars to get your favorite apps again or if you've not become invested in digital marketplaces yet, then this shouldn't be a problem for you.I don't really read much but I'm glad if I turn into a bookworm I can take advantage of amazon's digital services straight through the tablet. :3, Much info, I know. so helpful. (notsorry)The hardware is fairly decent, but in my opinion it's nothing to really be impressed at, even at the price. I'd call the 50/60 dollar price tag average and appropriate. The screen for me leaves something to be desired at the 1024x600 resolution. The colors are vibrant and beautiful which makes it definitely adequate for watching videos, but the lower resolution means it'll be a little more pixellated than watching them on your laptop, desktop, TV or even your cell phone if you've got a relatively recent mobile device. I'd also like to point out that the Youtube apps on the Amazon appstore are.. severely lacking. it's essentially a mobile browser linked to youtube's site. It doesn't give any options for changing video quality so you're stuck at either 240 or 360p videos which are quite lossy and low quality. After taking the time to get GAPPS and put the legit youtube app on it, 720p video definitely looks much crispier and better detailed.The cameras are.. well.. VGA inner, 2mp outer. that's not very much for a mobile camera, and the photos are fairly potato quality if I try to take them from my desk. Haven't really tried taking any outside in the sunlight, but I don't really plan on using my tablet for my camera anyways.. I already have a 13mp in my phone that cranks out pretty good quality shots so I'll stick with that.The CPU's power surprised me. All of my emulators work great, from PCE.EMU to ePSXe. I've also been having a lot of fun with GTA:SA which ran surprisingly well too. The battery life while playing those games and emulators was excellent, definitely much better than the other devices I've owned. I would've loved to be able to root this thing so I could use Sixaxis with them, but sadly since I've already let the bloody thing do it's updates to 5.3.1.0, I really don't want to mess with reverting back on OS's and brick it.The sound output I will have to give a dishonorable mention.. it's in a bad place. Using a generic ONN case from Walmart, it engulfs the speaker port to where sound output is muffled and quiet. I don't like the idea of my speaker facing away from me anyways, as I'm not trying to broadcast whatever profanity I may be currently viewing on Youtube to the rest of the break room at work. x_x. Of course, that's what they make earbuds and Bluetooth headphones for. The Bluetooth module built in does work decently, but I would like to make note to Amazon that they should add a quick pref widget for that instead of digging in the settings to pair devices or enable/disable BT function.Bottom line, Was it worth it, would I recommend this to other people?Of course it was! I absolutely would recommend this to anyone who just needs a solid tablet for mild internet/multimedia use. It's small and fits nicely in your back pocket, It's fast and right out of the box it comes with everything you really need in a tablet computer; A smooth web browser, multimedia services directly linked to your online account, and your standard suite of apps like the calculator, contacts, email, camera, docs, clock and calendar. The battery life is excellent, especially when gaming so it's an ideal travel companion to keep the kids or whatever you may have as a passenger entertained for a while. If that's all you need, then look no further.

title: Almost perfect
body: My first complaint is about the screen. I knew it was not backlit and was not daunted by that, but the print is dark gray against a lighter gray background. OK in decent light, but the poorer the light you are reading in, the poorer the contrast. The gray background just gets darker. I can read a paper page better in poor light than I can the Kindle screen.My first plus is the variable-size text. In really good light I can read smaller print, thus getting more text per page. In less than optimum light conditions, I can bump up the font size, and I like that. Many books I have picked up in a store, I have put right down if the type is too small. Either on the Kindle or on paper, I can read small print only in very good light.Second complaint is the about the Kindle store. Not all the books I would like are available, and not all of my favorite authors are represented here. I can get Nevada Barr, but not Dana Stabenow. Also, with over a hundred thousand titles, Amazon needs to provide a better navigation system through the store. Scrolling through ten thousand pages is really tiresome, and the only help they give you is a "Sort by"Sfunction that is not all that helpful. There really should be at least a "Jump to" function if you suspect that something you are looking for is lurking somewhere beyond the first few hundred pages.Second plus is the Whispernet connectivity and the speed and simplicity of ordering and receiving the books that you order.Some of the extras that are part of the Kindle world promise to be fun to play with, but I never expected them in a reading device and would not have been disappointed to "just" be able to read books on it. The email function will never take the place of my regular email, but I suppose it might be useful in a pinch. Also, being of an older generation, I never understood the need to carry music with me absolutely everywhere I go. When I first noticed a volume control on the bottom of the Kindle, I thought it was totally redundant. Nevertheless, I do have one mp3 uploaded to my Kindle, and may enjoy having some background music playing softly while I read under a tree or on a park bench somewhere. In order to get a couple more songs on the machine, however, I have to figure out how to convert the m4p files in my iTunes library to mp3 format.Amazon, if you are going to give us hundreds of thousands of titles to choose from and a nifty, wireless way to receive books, and if you have a wide variety of music in a Kindle-acceptable format already online, why not offer the Kindle community the capability of ordering and uploading music selections via Whispernet, just like we get the books. Having to hook up to my computer to upload music is not a real hardship, except for the fact that all my music is in the wrong format and I can't use it on my Kindle.I am sure that future iterations of the Kindle will have backlit screens and more bells and whistles that we may or may not need, but I am glad that I got mine when I did. I have been waiting for a hand-held electronic reading device for 35 years -- I kid you not -- and I did not want to wait a moment longer to get this one.It is expensive, yes, but considering the wireless connectivity that comes with it, it's a bargain, and you may even save enough on the books you order (average $10, versus $25-30 for hardback) that it might even pay for itself eventually -- although, personally, I've been in the habit of waiting for a new book to go into paperback before I bought it. Now I don't have to (at least for SOME of my favorite authors).Oh, and for those who say it is homely, I say phooey. I think it looks elegant. If you want fancy, buy some stick-on rhinestones to decorate it with. I went to a craft store and bought an embroidered, iron-on applique and glued it onto the front of the plain black book cover that the Kindle comes with, not just to dress it up a little, but so I can tell mine at a glance from anybody else's Kindle, on the chance that I run into someone else who has one, which I haven't done so far. But I've only had it two days.Finally, I would like to thank whoever it was who posted a tip somewhere about securing the Kindle into the cover with a little velcro on the back in the bottom corner. The "clip" that is supposed to keep the thing in its cover doesn't work that well.

title: Six weeks and six novels later...
body: I have had my Kindle now for six weeks and have read six novels on it in that time. As much as I love reading I usually only manage one or two books a month. So right out of the gate I will say that one big positive of the Kindle is that it has allowed me to read more. Because I take it with me just about everywhere I go I have all the books on my reading list with me at all times. I have found that I am able to read more than one book at a time, assuming that they are of different genres. So whatever book I am in the mood to read at the time, it is there in my hand.It has been said here already but the e-ink technology is phenomenal. There is no light emitted from the screen so it is as easy on your eyes as a paper page of text. Actually, it is even easier on your eyes due to the fact that you can choose the font size that is most comfortable for you. Reading outside in the light of the bright sun? No problem.I find that the page buttons are actually well placed. Whichever hand I happen to be holding the Kindle in there is a next page button within easy reach of a finger or thumb on that hand. I have read the complaints about the touchy previous page button but I find no such problem when the Kindle is used in conjunction with the Kindle cover. The cover does make it easy to hold and offers that added piece of mind when carrying it around with you. I did, however, think the cover was a bit plain so I purchased a handmade canvas and deer skin Kindle cover off of eBay.The built in Oxford dictionary is a handy little tool. Typically when I read a novel one or two words pop up with which I am unfamiliar. Pre-Kindle I would usually just skip over the word, thus missing out on the opportunity to expand my vocabulary. With the Kindle, however, the definition is just two thumb clicks away.But what about the feel? I must admit that my major concern before purchasing the Kindle was losing that old fashioned "book feel" of the paper against by fingertips, even the smell. But a chapter into my first book the device itself just disappeared and I became engrossed in the story, just as I do with a physical book. In addition to that, it is just easier to read with the Kindle. I no longer have to keep one hand occupied holding the book open. I can lay the Kindle down on the table and read while eating. I can even, for the first time in my life, get a good cardio workout on a treadmill while reading a book. I just crank up the font size and place it on the treadmill reading stand. Reading with the Kindle is just plain more comfortable than reading the old fashioned way.I cannot comment on the Whispernet functionality. I am currently stationed overseas and Whispernet is only available within the US. However, Amazon allows you to download your purchases directly from your online media library to your computer. From there you can connect your Kindle to your computer via a USB cable and download your books. I find it very simple and quick. One downside to this, however, is you cannot download the samples this way. As of this writing, samples can only be downloaded via Whispernet.When I first opened my Kindle I felt the keyboard took up too much space. In fact, since I don't have access to Whispernet I was thinking I would be better off without a keyboard all together and dedicating more of the space to the screen. But now that it is there I actually find myself using the keyboard to take notes in the books I am reading. So while I initially disliked the keyboard, it has now grown on me.The one feature of the Kindle that I could do without is the audio feature. It is a poor MP3 player because you can only use it on random shuffle and the ability to fast forward and rewind does not exist. Amazon should either ditch the MP3 player, thus making the device a little lighter and cutting down on cost, or at least make the MP3 player fully functional.The Kindle is expensive. There is no getting around that. My hope is that they will be able to bring down the cost in the next generation of the Kindle. That being said, I have come to cherish my Kindle so much that, knowing what I know now, I likely would have been willing to pay twice the amount.While there is room for improvement, the Kindle is easily one of the best purchases I have ever made.

title: Unbeatable Price Comes at a Cost
body: Unbeatable Price, Comes at a CostIf you are looking for a simple tablet that will access the internet, check emails, and watch Netflix without jumping through any hoops, then this tablet is for you. If you want something more like say, a standard Google Android device (which is different from regular Android), then I suggest you look elsewhere.As long as you don’t mind jumping through some hoops it is possible to tweak Fire OS to run and look more like Google Android, no rooting necessary. If you want a tutorial on that then feel free to visit ebook reader blog site 'how to install google play on fire tablets the super easy way'. Please note that this is not officially supported by Amazon or Google, however, it is in no way illegal to do so. As far as I am aware this process will only work on Fire OS 5.0 and up, though some people mentioned problems running some Google Play apps later on. These steps worked for me and I cannot guarantee the success rate of anyone else. Good luck!Pros:Perhaps the biggest draw is that this tablet is very inexpensive! If you were lucky enough to catch that $33 deal then you know what I mean.This tablet is on the heavier side, however, it is very durable. You won't have that sleek thin elegant look with higher end tablets, however, you won't have to worry about it breaking after a few drops.The amazon interface is very simple once you get used to the Amazon Launcher, and the limiting customisation makes this a pretty simple-to-use tablet.These tablets have long battery life. and with light use my tablet can last two days on one charge.The tablet is fast as I’ve experienced no lagging, perhaps due to the simplicity and little customisable options that may otherwise slow it down.Amazon offers Amazon Underground to Fire OS users and several apps that would otherwise cost you money are free.This is a family friendly tablet that will allow you to set ‘accounts’ and run a Kindle FreeTime app for children.*This section will explain how Fire OS works, what is available and what is restricted, and how it is different from Google’s Android*Amazon Fire tablets run on Fire OS, which is based on Google’s Android (which is separate from regular Android like you may see on LG, HTC, Samsung, Sony Phones, etc. that run “Google Mobile Services”-they acquired an additional license for access to Google’s Services) however it does not have any Googles services or apps. So that means you won’t have access to Google’s Play Store, Google Docs, Google’s Gmail, Google Drive, Google’s Chrome, etc. This also means you will not have access to most of those 2.6 million or so apps (as of Dec. 2016) from Google’s Play Store. However Amazon does have their own app store and Silk browser, and these Fire tablets come with access to Amazon Underground (apps that would otherwise cost you money are free to Fire OS users).Cons:I’ve already listed most of the cons above which may be a deal breaker for many of you, but I will outline a few more setbacks.The Amazon Launcher is way too cluttered for me! I like a more minimalistic home screen and amazon does not allow you to change the Amazon Fire OS Launcher that comes with the tablet.This tablet will also come with ads on the lock screen, that can only be removed by paying a $15 fee.You have to have an Amazon account, and eventually, a Prime membership to utilise the full potential of Fire OS tablets. However, as a long time user of Amazon Prime, I suggest you invest in Prime anyway (especially if you are a student, or like to shop online).The camera is, well, you won’t want to take any pictures. And the screen resolution isn’t anything to be excited about either.If you do not love Amazon (and don’t want to tweak your tablet) then do not buy this tablet! This tablet is deeply ingrained with Amazon "special offers", lock screen ads, and even some settings options will direct you to their shopping app, which is also a reason why these tablets are so inexpensive. This was a brilliant move on their part as Fire OS is meant to control the experience. Amazon App Store vs Google’s Play Store. Amazon Music vs Google’s Play Music, Amazon Kindle vs Google’s Play Books, etc. Everything is geared toward giving more money to Amazon.

title: Reviewing the reviews
body: I had to delay the writing of this review for a few minutes while I tried to stop laughing. As I looked at the number of imbeciles who have never touched one of these things and are reviewing the concept or actually the presentation of the concept, and what's more, the complete lack of imagination these reviews display. People griping about the price, I just shelled out more than that for a reasonably nice looking bookshelf that will take up valuable floorspace in my house and will be nice looking only as long as I can resist filling it with the crappy looking paperbacks and odd sized hardcovers I have laying around my house. People who haven't touched it griping about the ergonomics. People griping because its not a PC! WOW!This thing, may or may not be a good implementation of the concept, I have no idea until I see and try one. And so I won't review the Kindle - but I saw an interview with Bezos and he described the advantages in detail, and the concept sounds great. Being able to change fontsize, holding and turning pages with one hand. Size, flexibilty to add content or buy books while on the move. Holding mags and newspapers. I travel alot and this sounds great to me. Version one may have issues - or may not, but at some point (maybe now) this thing will be a huge seller. plus, trees all over the world are probably feeling safer....!UPDATE - I now have my own KINDLE!I have not seen too many negative reviews from people that actually own the device. However, there is a single star review that comes up as the negative review that comes up as one of the reviews that were most helpful that may actually be from someone that owns one - though its not totally clear. In any case the reviewers cites ergonomics as all wrong. I have to strongly disagree. The reviewers complaints about button placement are just plain wrong. It takes a few hours to get used to the placement and then you see the advantages.Here is what you can do,Holding the Kindle...Use the Kindle with one hand (either left or right)- No you can't grab it by the buttons- but once you get used the the dead spots on the device you very quickly become accustomed and develop several comfortable positions. And you can actually turn the pages easily with the hand holding the device - true one handed use.Use the kindle with no hands (I often prop it up on my legs and touch it only to turn pages)Screen Operation...Yes there is a flash, but the user that timed it a 2 seconds must have a screwy device. My screen never takes that long to refresh and I find that it consistently refreshes faster than I could turn a page by hand in a normal book. I also found that i began to hit the button a few words before the end of the page getting a screen refresh to happen just as I finish the last word.THe store...Having the device connect via Cell networks was brilliant. I have seen users complain that it should use wireless computer networks and I have to wonder if they actually have the device. Yes I have been in places where I could not get connected, but most of the time its there when I need it.As far as the fringe capabilities (like web search etc) - I did not purchase this device for those functions - I bought it to read books so to me those are the functions that are important and that I am reviewing.What can be improved... In my opinion the device might be tweaked in terms of ergonomics or overall capability, but if it stays like it is I would be quite happy. However, the amount of content definitely needs improvement. While I never have a hard time finding something in the store that I would like to read, I do often find that specific books I would like to read are not there yet. I have heard that Bezos has a goal to have virtually every book currently available in paper form to be available for Kindle. I have also noticed that the number of books on Kindle has increased by about 50% since it's initial release. Also , it would be nice to get more periodical content though the blog list is already pretty impressive. Also - to me, this device would be a huge boon to college students... if the content was there. Having all your textbooks in one little container would be a wonderful thing - also it should limit the outrageous costs for textbooks to some extent.

title: I love my Kindle
body: I was really waiting patiently before buying a Kindle. I wanted to hold one first, but I didn't get a chance (my cousin has one, but I did not get a chance to try his). I did not know if I was going to like it because I heard it really does not accept PDFs, of which I had a lot. I was concerned because it did not seem to support Mac, which I use so I thought I was going to have to use Fusion to get to Windows just to use the thing. Lastly, I kept hearing rumors that a new Kindle was coming out any day.After scouring the Internet for information about a Kindle 2, I decided that it probably would not come out the day after I bought a Kindle. I had just moved (I am a priest, so move every couple of years), and it really hit me how many books I had. I realized that I should start getting rid of some of my books, and thought maybe I could replace them as ebooks. The parishioners at my last assignment gave me a generous gift, so I used the money to buy a Kindle. I am so happy I did.First, the Kindle does look a bit weird. I am not a tech guy, so I do not know if there was a good reason for the angles and lines that it has. I do not find it uncomfortable to hold, but of course I have it in a leather case (not the one that comes with it). The buttons work fine for me except for the two on & off buttons (one for the unit and the other for the wireless). They are on the back of the unit, so I have to unhook one corner from the leather case I have it in just to turn it on and to turn on the wireless. I do not see why they could not put these switches on the top edge of the unit. The rest of the buttons work easily. The software/operating system if fine; although I wish there was a way to jump to a page on the main menu which has the listing of my books, so that I did not have to go through each one just to get to a book at the end.The text on the screen is easy to read. I think I have the text size in the middle, and it is plenty large for me. While not exactly looking like text on paper (the screen is grey-ish), I have no eye strain from it. It is easy to read in standard lighting, and I do not have problems with glare. The pages do not "flip" as quickly as I can turn a page, but I do not find the very slight delay annoying.I have been very pleased with downloading books via the whispernet. I found the entire 3 volumes of the Summa Theologica for $0.99. Can't beat that. The complete works of G.K. Chesterton, Charles Dickens, and Shakespeare for $4.79 each. Plus I have found lots of places to download free ebooks as MOBI files. My favorite is Feedbooks.com. You can get a lot of the classics for free. Feedbooks even tells you how to enter a URL on your Kindle so that you can download their books directly onto the Kindle. I have a 2 GB SD card in, and I well over 200 books on it, and it is not even an eighth full.From the Amazon site I wish I could add Kindle books to my wishlist, so if someone wants to buy me one for Christmas or my birthday, they could. I did find on the Kindle itself how to create a "Save for Later" list of books I might want.I had a concern about interfacing the Kindle with my Mac. It has not been a problem at all. I plug the Kindle in one of my USBs and it and the SD card show up as external drives. I then just need to drag the files I want on the Kindle that I download from another site to the "Documents" folder on either the Kindle or SD card.It took me a while to figure out how to send my MS Word documents to have them converted for the Kindle. I think it was my mistake. Like many people, I have multiple email addresses. You MUST send the file you want converted from the email address that you have your Kindle registered to (I think you can add other email address to your Kindle account to accept email from them as well). Of course this makes good security so that you do not have a bunch of junk sent to your Kindle. I have not used the $0.10 service; it is just as easy to send it to the Free Kindle conversion address. I do highly recommend that you format your Word docs as HTML, as they come out nicer on the Kindle.I have a box of books now that I have replaced with ebooks on my Kindle. It is doing all that I hoped it would, and highly recommend it.

title: Perfect for any Reader
body: I received the Amazon Kindle as a Christmas gift from my parents, and at first I wondered if I was ready to replace reading books in print. After three weeks of heavy use, my entire family has determined that it's the best gift anyone has ever thought to buy me.Like all readers, I'm proud of my ever-growing library of books. To suddenly stop adding to my bookshelves seemed like a giant leap. But as I considered the prospect of reading almost exclusively on the Kindle, I reflected on how I never complained when I no longer bought CDs and instead carried my music around on an MP3 player.And what the iPod has done for music, I'm confident that the Amazon Kindle will do the same to the publishing world.I've given the Kindle a four-star review because no product is ever perfect. The only real complaint I've found with my Kindle is the inability to adjust its brightness; in general, I do find it more difficult to read the Kindle in some dimmer locations. To counter this, however, I've invested in the "Mighty Bright" book light to use in those rare situations. Unlike some users who complain about color screens or insufficient web browsing, I rely on my Kindle for purchasing and reading books. If I want to surf the Internet, I use my laptop or phone (which has full web browsing capabilities).As for complaints that the page-turning takes too long or it's too easy to hit the "Back" button instead of "Page Forward," they're mostly unfounded. The first time I sat down to read, the page-turning required a brief adjustment, but I doubt it takes any longer than physically having to turn a page in a regular book. Also (and maybe it's because I'm left handed and use the "Page Forward" button on the left side of the Kindle more often than the right), but I've only hit the "Back" button once in three weeks. When this happened, I simply used the scroll bar to click on the book from the home screen and it returned to the page I'd been reading. A quick, easy fix.Now, onto the things that really excite me about this product:First, I'm no longer worried that the cost of the Amazon Kindle outweighs the actual cost of the books I purchase. I've created a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet that keeps tabs on how much I save on each book, and it's amazing how quickly it all adds up. It won't take long before my total savings eclipses the price of the Kindle itself. From there on out, then, it's a real money-saver.Second, it absolutely lives up to its quick delivery claim. Friends and family members are amazed by how speedy the wireless delivery works; on average, I'd estimate that purchases arrive within 15 seconds of confirmation (and I'm happy that accidental purchases in the Kindle store are quickly and easily rectifiable). For anyone who quickly finishes one book and is ready to move onto the next, the Kindle is a no-brainer. There's no real need to stockpile books for vacations, weekends, etc. Finish the book you're reading, browse the Kindle store, and start another a minute later.Finally (as a means to keep this review brief), I'm stunned by the electronic ink. Simply put, it's genius. I remember checking out e-book readers from my local library years ago, and they were terrible--I always felt like I was reading a computer monitor, and it wasn't long before I had to take a break. For whatever reason, I expected the Kindle to be harder on the eyes than everyone claims--there's no way it looks like "digital paper," right? Well, wrong, I guess. The display is stunning, and I'm still amazed by it today.In general, the Amazon Kindle is a terrific product with only minor drawbacks. At some point I imagine Amazon will have to lower the price to make it more easily accessible to the general public, but I hope the Kindle catches on. For one, it's a "greener" product (no more wasting paper on excessive Stephen King novels), and it's absolutely one of the most convenient products available today.Judging by the thousands of positive reviews on the Internet--including this website--the Amazon Kindle is a hit. Whether my review changes anybody's mind or confirms what they already thought, I needed to add my two-cents and thank Amazon for making my life a whole lot easier (and more fun).

title: Super Cool But Not Perfect
body: I ordered my Kindle December 3, received it on January 11, and have been experimenting with it ever since. The pros and cons of the device are well-documented in other customer reviews. What strikes me is the passion that this electronic device seems to engender in supporters and critics alike. Clearly, this is not the run-of-the mill consumer product and I don't think it's going too far to say that the Kindle does have the potential to be revolutionary. The Whispernet/wireless access is the "killer app" that is endless fun. Having said that, the Kindle is far from perfect and there is considerable room for improvement. My Kindle has frozen already three or four times and had to be reset with a paper clip. Not a big deal, but I'd like to be free of such computer-like troubles when using the Kindle. The depth of the book selection is rather weak. I tested that in part by looking for 20 or 30 books that were selected by the Chicago Tribune as among the best of 2007. Many of the books were not available yet as Kindle editions. This is understandable as the Kindle is so new. If the product takes off and continues to do well, it's inevitable that more books will become available (think of the sparse movie selection when DVDs first became available). Until then, keep your expectations realistic -- you'll find most of the bestsellers, but often be surprised by what is not yet available. I hope more reference books become available, e.g., the New York Times Guide to Essential Knowledge.I love the convenience of the Kindle, and anticipate that I will really enjoy having it on business trips, keeping a "library" at my fingertips, without the bulk. Having said that, it's hard not to miss some aspects of conventional books, such as having an index, and being able to flip around quickly. I wish the Kindle books had an index where you could highlight page numbers and quickly go to a selection (yes, I know about the Kindle's search features, but it would be nice to be able to use an index nevertheless).As has been discussed ad nauseam, the Kindle is not a particularly elegant design. The plastic feels cheap to me, and the device very breakable. With reasonable care, though, I don't think you need to unduly worry about the Kindle being fragile.I would like the power buttons to be on the front of the device, with perhaps a sliding "lock" feature, as in many MP3 players.As for the infamous cover, I find that it is helpful and stays secure once you get the hang of affixing it.I wish the "select" wheel for menu selections were a bit easier to press, like the select button on many other electronic devices I own. For those of you who have ever developed tendinitis or repetitive stress injuries from mouse or keyboard use, I could see the Kindle causing similar injuries (don't have to worry about that with a book!) but certainly the risk seems low. Nevertheless, the select wheel should not have so much resistance.If you are a devoted newspaper reader, it's hard to believe that you'll be totally satisfied with the Kindle's renditions of newspapers. I compared the Friday print edition of the Wall Street Journal with the Kindle's rendition, and the useful graphic illustrations present in the print version were missing in the Kindle. It also takes much longer to move around from article to article than with the print version. So, I would not be inclined to give up print versions of newspapers in favor of the Kindle. On the other hand, when traveling, I could definitely see buying two or three of the newspapers on the Kindle on the day of travel so that I would have all that reading material with me on the plane (but without all the excess paper). Regardless, as with so many other aspects of the Kindle, individual preferences will vary -- just because I can't see giving up newspapers in favor of the Kindle doesn't mean that wouldn't be a great choice for you.Is the Kindle for everyone? No. But it's certainly a great start, very cool in many respects, and an excellent purchase so long as your expectations are reasonable and you don't ask the Kindle to excel in ways for which it is not designed.

title: I have used ebooks for 10 years, my Kindle 6 weeks:
body: I have used ebook readers for about 10 years, I started with the Rocketbook and then went to Sony, I own both a Sony and Kindle at this time. I use the Kindle slightly more then the Sony, but still use both. My Kindle reveiw: When it works it is great! I had to return my 1st for battery issues. The 2nd has been flawless as far as that goes. The screen is not as pretty to read as the Sony because of the less # of shades, but is easy enough to read. The kindle is smaller then the Rocket book and about twice the thickness of the Sony. But it fits in my purse without issue. I have had no problems with accidental page turns, but I think that is because I went in to it in the habit of holder an ereader, which is different the a book.My positives on the Kindle:1)The amazon store on the Kindle is handy, fast and fun!2)I like the samples most of the time. The amazon has a large varity of books.3) I like the interactive dictionary.4) When the links for footnotes work it is handy and, well, pretty cool :)My negative on kindle:1)The store is flooded with copies of books for 99 cents that you can get free on the net. I mean flooded with public domain books which increases the #'s of books for kindle and makes finding anything soon to be released, or newly released a real challange if you don't go in the search with a title you want.2)Apparently Amazon does not require the books published for the Kindle to be formatted for the Kindle. I've bought about 20 books ($9.99-34.99) and I have had to return 5 because they were not formatted correctly. These are large publisher books, not the free revamped public domain. Many are new hardback releases in the walk in bookstores. Usually the format issues are missing lines or paragraphs, offset lines, sudden appearance of numbers that just run down the page. Text overwritten by other text. Redownloading the book does not help. The errors are in the formatting of the books. At the price 25% of the books not formatted for the device is high. I have bought over 200 books for my Sony (some the same book, publisher, edition ect as the Kindle) and they are formatted correctly for the Sony. So I don't understand why Amazon has such a high failure rate. I bought Robert Fagel's The Aeneid, on the Sony it is formatted perfect, on the Kindle it was not readable. I continue to buy for my Kindle because they allow you to return books within seven days. When you call with format issues YOU HAVE TO REQUEST A REFUND, THEY WILL NOT OFFER IT. I had to read the part in the Kindle manual to one of the Kindle support guys to tell him that I could get a refund because he told my that they don't refund electronic books. Once I gave him the location in the Kindle manual that says Amazon does, he refunded it.3) Kindle support is not much support, they give standard answers while dispensing as little information as possible. I have recieved wrong information from them several times. They can be rude, brisk and seem to not want to be helpful if the problem is not easily solved.4) Do not use NowNOw unless you like spam on male sexual enhancement devices. Amazon appeared unconcerned when I notified them of about 10 spams I recieved ON MY KINDLE from NowNow. They told me that NowNow was contracted through them but not owned by Amazon so they weren't responsible (seriously, that is what they said). They said, both in person and on email replies, that "if I didn't like the answers I have recieved on NowNow to click the link that says it was not useful". This was the solution offed by Kindle support. Sad.I recieved no spam on my personal email about this only on the Kindle.I know this looks like a negitive review on the Kindle, I don't mean it to be. I like the Kindle, I will continue to use it. I would probably buy it again. But it has serious issues Amazon needs to address. Mainly stop selling books that aren't formatted for the Kindle, and getting Kindle Customer support up to par. Also, the Kindle is nice to use, but not as user friendly as the video says. You have to work to get quality material to read and pray you don't have an issue you need customer support for.

title: Very solid product with some small annoyances
body: Upon receipt of the Kindle it quickly because clear that Amazon has taken a page out of Apple's playbook when it comes to packaging. Just opening the package was an experience, which was great since it was a gift for my wife. Of course packaging doesn't make the product. . .Fortunately the Kindle lives up to that first class packaging. I was most concerned about the screen, and it impressed me the most. It is literally just like a piece of paper and nothing like a back-lit computer screen. You almost have to see it to believe it.I bought the Kindle for my wife, and she found it very intuitive to navigate, purchase books, and read after just fiddling around with it for short period of time. She reads probably 10x more than before simply because of the accessibility of the books. The wireless interface is just fantastic, making it simple and fast to purchase a new book. The ability to preview first chapters is also really nice. So overall, I'm VERY positive about the KindleThat said, there are a few irritating things about it as well. First, you have absolutely no real sense of "how far" you are into a book. That might not sound like a big deal, but I personally really like to know that I'm 1/2 way through, or that I've got just another 10 pages until the end of the chapter. There is a basic indicator, but it doesn't help with chapters, and it's one of the only parts of the user interface that's not intuitive. Something about "blocks" and how many "blocks" are left. . .note that those aren't pages, so it's unclear to me what a "block" is.There are also a much larger number of editing errors. Much more than hard copies. I suppose that's a result of the bulk digitization process so this is understandable.Our Kindle has crashed or become unstable in the three times in the month we've had it. Nothing that hasn't been easily resolved by hitting the (quite hidden) reset button on the back or in one case pulling battery. That instability though, is probably the most problematic I see with the Kindle, particuarly for older and/or less technically savvy customers. Amazon need to make more clear in the instructions what to do in the event of a problem. I'm pretty sure my grandparents wouldn't have known what to do with the locked screen for example. They'd have needed to call someone and ask because it's so unclear what to do. So if you give one as a gift to a non-tech savvy person, just explain to them what to do if the device stops responding as expected.Probably my biggest concern is the cost vs. lack of true ownership of the books you "buy". When you go to the book store, you are buying an honest to goodness book that you can read then give/sell to a friend, sell on Ebay. . .whatever you want! That's absolutely not the case with the Kindle, there doesn't appear to be a way to easily transfer ownership of the book that you've allegedly "purchased". What that means is that you didn't really "purchase" the book, but that it's been "licensed" to you. Make no mistake, there's a BIG difference. That would be fine with me, except that for most paperbacks, the discount for "buying" on the Kindle over purchasing in a book store is often around 40 or 50 cents. That's really not very much of a discount!! Hopefully the prices will start to drop in coming years to compensate for the lack of true ownership of the books. Make the price 25% less and I have no problem with this. But the problem is that it remains cheaper to buy a book in the book store, and then sell it when you are done, than buy a book on your Kindle. . .and that's inexcusable in my opinion. The exception to this gripe is hardback books, where the discount is *much* bigger. So Amazon, if you are listening, push that discount down to the larger selection of paperbacks, and do it to compensate for the fact that you aren't actually "selling" a book!So I do have a few grips, but overall, this is a great product that my wife and I are really enjoying. The biggest endorsement is that I'll have to order my own in the next few weeks, because my wife likes hers so much. . .I'm not really allowed to use it!

title: Coolest way to relieve computer vision syndrome
body: I thought the Kindle was all hype, but then I started getting headaches at work.I'm a software designer with no life. That means, among other things, that I stare a a computer screen for 10-16 hours per day. Sure, I hike, camp, and take every chance I get to go be somewhere free of computer screens, but my whole life centers around the computer - especially in winter when it gets dark before I get home from work.My work and hobbies call me to do a lot of research - reading Wikipedia, tech blogs, software manuals, proofing drafts of our own manuals... After a particularly intense week of research, I went home one day, tried to read something on the Web, and had to go lie down and close my eyes for a while. I ordered a Kindle the very next day. I have rarely had a headache since.A word about the free Web access - Is it going to be permanent? I don't know, but it's the best chance at free (or cheap) EVDO access on a device like this. To me, it seems like a very interesting business model. Amazon needs to give the device a lot of EVDO bandwidth to let users browse their site and buy/download books. They're probably using some sort of unlimited plan. It doesn't hurt them that much to throw in Web access for free. I don't know whether it's a model that is going to work for them long-term, but it's not one that most companies would be able to offer, not being large-scale booksellers.I downloaded a lot of free MobiPocket classics to it, too. I would never have been able to stand reading them on a computer screen (the eyestrain again), and it would have cost rather a lot to get paper copies of them all. It's still an expensive device, and I could have read them on a Sony Reader for less, but that does help defray the cost (assuming there are enough classics you've always wanted to read and haven't been able to justify buying). I even found illustrated versions of some of them, which is very nice as long as the illustrations are woodcuts (which are well-suited to the Kindle's screen).Biggest upsides of the Kindle:* Ability to search Wikipedia.* Ability to easily send self a document one has to read that one would otherwise have to print out. They're not currently actually charging the ten-cent charge, but even if they were, I've had to print stuff out at college libraries where it was ten cents *per page.* I'm not going to whine about ten cents per document when the documents can be several hundred pages.* Amazingly easy to read.
