And putting it in the Kindle using your computer is a matter of moving a file from one place to another just like any other computer file. Price: The Kindle itself is a little pricy. But once you get it, Kindle books are relatively inexpensive. They seem to beat the prices of other ebook sellers by a lot. Ergonomics: Oh, this is a simple thing that can be fixed, but, right now, the ergonomics on the Kindle is terrible. Everything else about the Kindle, the weird input sceme, the price of the device itself, etc, can be forgiven by the revolutionary other features, but the buttons and lack of a place to hold the Kindle while reading are a real drawback. There are two next page buttons. One taking up most of the right corner and half of the left corner. There is a previous page button on the left side. So where do I hold the kindle? It's relatively light. That's great. But light or not, I've got to hold it. There is a cover for it that sort of resembles the cover of a hardcover book, but you can't really hold it on that. When you do, it's just uncomfortable. If you hold it on the edges of the Kindle, you push either the next page or previous page buttons. There is a little "aisle" between the halves of the text keyboard, but it's just a space. Moreover, it's a little uncomfortable and not very ergonomic. There is a long and skinny scroll wheel screen next to the main text screen where a cursor moves up and down. When the cursor is next to something on the text screen you want to control or look up, you press it and get various functions. I use the groove that the scroll wheel screen fits in to hold the Kindle while reading. However, no matter what way you hold it, it all feels like you'll drop your $359 device at any moment or you'll accidently turn the page throwing you out of your reading groove. I get the feeling that someone didn't put much thought into ergonomics. When the ergonomics are fixed in the coming generations of the Kindle, this revolution's critics will have little else to be upset about. Getting on the Internet: Good thinking, but it isn't quite there yet. And it's a little slow. Amazon says that the web browser (using Kindle's Whispernet, essentially, cell phone technology, which is free once you buy the Kindle)is expirimental. I guess, it's their way of saying that they aren't there yet. I agree with that. When it gets better and faster, there will be people who will start to wonder why they need their computers at all. Buying books using the Kindle: Awesome. A little slow, but Amazon made sure it was easy so they could sell a bunch of books. It's relatively simple. There is a little thumbnail of the book cover next to each book and they have recommendations for you. You can set up a one touch purchase button and your book is sent to you over whispernet (for no extra charge) in under a minute. Samples: I love Kindle samples. For one touch of a Kindle button, you can have a significant portion of a book sent to you (usually a chapter and a half or sometimes even more) for no money. Like books you buy, it's sent is under a minute. For me, I have book shoping in brick and morter stores. There are usually places to sit down and read the books, but there is so much activity around me that I can't sit and figure out whether the book is good or not. I just want to get what I want and leave. I just can't read in a bookstore. But the sample is a brilliant idea. I can download several samples and lay down in bed or in an easy chair or by the beach and think about whether I want to buy a particular book. If I don't, I can delete it. If I do, it's one click away. But I can take days to think about it or weeks or years. And I can really tell whether I like a book within the first chapter and a half. This will save me, at least, lots of money in the long run, because of my history of putting a book I bought down after reading a chapter and a half. Page numbers: Okay, Kindle doesn't have traditional page numbers. It has a row of dots that are bolded the father into a book you go. The number of dots in bold is relative to the size of the book and where you are in the book. There is also a weird numbering system that I haven't figured out yet. I like the dots, but it's not the same as page numbers. I get the fact that the Kindle screen does not exactly compute to a page of text. Moreover, you can change the text size anyway, which is another tremendous feature, especially for those who need larger text, so that does something to the pagination. However, why can't the Kindle approximate the page number in the print version and, say, add a decimal or something like that. It could say page "375.8 of 500". It doesn't have to be exact. The way it is now sort of feels like being in the wilderness and not being exactly sure how far your car is. It's a little disconcerting. With a print book, you see and feel the amount of pages you have to go. Kindle can approximate pagination better than it does now. Battery Life: Several days. Apparently, once the text or graphic is on the screen, it takes no power to keep it there. That's because it's a different kind of screen. As such, you don't have to worry about charging it all the time. You can litterally read for several days and not have to charge it. I suppose if you actually read for 12 or 16 hours, you might have to charge it after that, but the battery life is by no means a limiting factor. If the battery does go low and you are still reading, then plug it in and continue reading. If you are on a desert island for more than several days, I can see a problem, but other than that, you won't bump up against the battery life. What other gadget can do that? Power and network switches. On the back of the Kindle are a power switch and a network on and off switch. These are physical switches and not toggle buttons, so you can physically see and feel whether the Kindle is on or off or whether the Kindle is able to connect to Whispernet or not. I hate toggle switches, so the switches on the Kindle are a plus. With toggle switches, you don't always know what state you are in. With a toggle switch, is your device just off or is there some major problem with it? Did I hit the button once to on or twice to on and then off? With these buttons, you know. The only problem is that they are on the back. Why not the bottom or the top or the front? It becomes a problem because of the cover. When it's in the cover and the cover strap holds the Kindle, you can't get to the back as easily. You can't just turn it off and close the cover. You have to take off the cover strap and take the Kindle out of the cover to get to the switches. Then you put the Kindle back in the cover and pull the strap either on the Kindle over over the cover. It's more than inconvenient, because you run the risk of hitting the next page button on the right, so you half to hit the previous page button either then or when you return to reading. Maybe you will inadvertantly skip a few paragraphs. In a future version, perhaps Amazon can put these buttons on the bottom (with the power, USB, and sound jacks) or on the top. The Experience: Once you get used to it, you are in the groove of reading just like a print book. Even the ergonomics are not that bad and I expect that Amazon will fix those in a future version of the Kindle. Only you can load hundreds of books on the thing (either in the internal memory or on a SD card), so that you can take your entire library along with you on a trip. And, of course, if you feel like buying some new book, Amazon has over 100,000 Kindle books available for $10 or less and you can get onto the Amazon network anywhere where you can find Whispernet (the Sprint Cell Phone network), which means most places where you get cell phone service in the United States. I'm a little worried that Amazon is going to completely monopolize the book selling industry. There is no need to buy a a new book from anyone else. Amazon is less expensive. For classics that are out of the public domain, you can probably do better than Amazon can, but for the newly published books, I don't think you can. In any case, I'm reading more than I have in a long time. I bring my libary with me and it only weighs 10 ounces. I think this may be another revolution. It is the biggest thing since Guttenberg invested movable type. If only it were easier to hold.

title: The Best Gift I've Given Myself This Year
body: Well, since the gift-giving season is almost upon us, I thought I'd review my Kindle. Right before a big trip this year, I thought I might be doing more travel in 2008 and beyond than I'd been doing for a few years, and I decided to give myself a lovely graduation gift, so I started looking at electronic books. A friend has a Sony e-Reader that he'd let me paw, and I liked it, but he was already having to jump through some hoops to get books, so that seemed like a bit of a negative to me. The Kindle had been out for a while, but I was leary of spending almost $400 without touching it for myself.Still. I figured if I bought it and hated it, I could return it, so I went for it.Wow. I have been overjoyed with this purchase. As my poor long-suffering fiance comments - he's a Kindle-widow. I try to tell him he's a book-widow, but... I LOVE to read. When doing almost anything else for fun, I always have to ask myself, "But, would I rather be reading?" (Hence, my picking up and putting down all knitting and stitching type projects, stamping, jewelry-making, and other other cool hobbies that many of my friends do!). The Kindle, therefore, has made my travel life much easier - I no longer have to put 3, 4, or more books in my carry-on and my suitcase just to see me through a couple of plane flights. Instead, I pack one paperback or magazine for take-offs and landings... and my Kindle, with its over 120 books on it currently.But let's get to the nitty gritty of this review - details!Kindle is available exclusively from Amazon.com and the current price is US$359. It comes with USB capability, so you can upload books, text files, MP3 files, PDFs, and etc. to your Kindle from your computer. I've never used my USB cable. Instead, I take advantage of the proprietarily named Whispernet. When I turn on the wireless access, I can connect to the Web (very minimalist surfing capabilities - I'd rather use my phone's browser most of the time)... and to the Amazon Kindle Store. The Kindle store (neither via browser nor via Kindle)is not great for browsing, although you can search by genre, and the Amazon recommendation engine is at work. On the other hand, if you know what you want to buy -- in under 3 minutes, you can find, purchase, and have downloaded the next book in that series you're reading, the new book by that author, or whatever you're looking for. Your Kindle is tied to your Amazon account, so your credit card on file is automatically charged (which can be dangerous if you're not paying attention to how many books you're buying), but the speed and ease of delivery made me a chain reader for a while when I first got my device!Kindle books are less expensive than most of what you'll buy for a paper book, although just in the last few weeks, the prices have gone way up. For the longest time, I didn't find any books - including those brand-new hardcover bestsellers - that were selling for more than $9.99. Most paperbacks were more like $5.99 or around there. The last hardcover I bought, however, was more. Still, a quick glance through the front page of the Kindle store at Amazon shows that only three or four books on the first few pages were more than that $9.99 standard -- new hardcovers for $11.99 and $14.99. Searching from high-to-low on price shows that there are some multi-thousand dollar text and reference books available, but if you're talking your every day fiction and non-fiction that you might pick up in the airport bookstore -- you'll pay between $5-$10.One of the things that most concerned me when I considered picking up the Kindle was book availability. I do read many of those airport books, but I also like literature, science fiction, fantasy, historical fiction... You get the idea. I didn't want to invest in this and realize I couldn't get the books I wanted! There are currently around 200,000 books available for the Kindle from the Amazon store, about 85,000 of which are fiction. I have been a little disappointed at not finding some books I truly wanted to read on the Kindle, but the selection of books they do have is pretty good. I'm not going to kid you - it's one of my wish-list items that more books in the genres I like most would be available, but I've also found many new authors through trying what I found on my Kindle. I've liked being able to download a sample. It's about a chapter or two of the book (from the beginning) that lets you get a pretty decent page test in before you buy. Once you buy, you do have one quick chance to return (oops, I clicked the wrong one type of thing), but after that, the book is yours. No sharing (or so the rules say - I haven't tried it). It's easy to store books on the Kindle - I think I had almost 80 or 90 before I started wondering what my space was like. I bought an SD card and slid it in the back and feel like I can go on forever now. You can also delete books and redownload from your Amazon account or computer.Battery life is great. Right up until you spend a lot of time with the wireless turned on. Wireless use really whacks the battery life, but since I'm mostly using it to buy and download a book, it doesn't impact me too much. I've gone for a long weekend with fairly heavy book reading with no need to recharge. If I don't turn on the wireless, I've gone almost a week, in fact. The joys of the electronic paper, where the battery power is only being used to turn the pages. Related to this is the backlighting and readability. There is no backlight. This keeps the battery life long and the readability high. Have you noticed that when you try to read on a computer, your eyes get tired? A lot of this is the backlighting. Kindle has none, which means that you can read just fine in bright sunlight (out by the pool, where you can't read a laptop mostly) and in any other normal lighting. I did buy an LED booklight to take for night-time flights, when the overhead light doesn't always do enough, but that's more about me getting old than it is about the Kindle, I think. I find the Kindle very easy to read. Easier in fact than some paper books, as I can up the font size at any time when I'm feeling tired or have been reading too long. Maybe that's a bad thing, but the ability to choose from about 6 font sizes on the fly makes readability pretty good. Screen is clear and electronic ink is so much like reading a printed book that almost everyone I show it to does a double take just on that aspect.My biggest issues with the Kindle have been around how to hold the darn thing. Where the hands in the image are holding it always seems awkward to me and doesn't really work for the lying in bed reading. I often hold it where the left thumb is, but I use my my index and middle fingers (one on each side of the device), with my thumb supporting the bottom. Sometimes I use the top area to hold it. If you try to hold on the left or right wide areas, you'll see that you'd be hitting the buttons that you use to turn the pages. Which means that sometimes, you accidentally turn a page when you don't mean to do so. Not that big a deal, but...Okay, a few more complaints and then we'll wrap it up.The "Back" button (NOT to be confused with the previous page button) does not act in a predictable way. Now, this is coming from someone who prides herself on knowing how to figure out technology and how things work. Sometimes the back button takes you to your book list, sometimes to a previous pages, sometimes to someplace totally unexpected. Either make it predictable or kill it.The book list... hmm, it works well enough when you have only a few books or magazines, but not so well after that. I've got about 27 JD Robb books on the Kindle. If I try to sort by author to find them all, well, some are catalogued with periods between the initials, some with spaces, some with no spaces, some with last name first... So they don't all show up in the same place. If I try to sort by Most Recent, well, it means the most recently touched book - either what you've just been reading, what you downloaded, or what your friend opened while you were showing off your Kindle. While the search works wonderfully, the organization should be improved so the books I've got are easier to sort. They should also have a folder or categorization system so that I can put all my JD Robb books, all my Michael Moorcock books, etc, in their own folders.Now, that issue with the cataloging - where the names aren't standardized. I suspect part of the problem there is the electronic production process. I've noticed that there are a lot of errors (small ones, to be sure) in the electronic books that don't seem to be as prevalent in the paper books. Things like "skirt" for "shirt" or homonyms or other small typo/transcription errors sneak in. They bug me because of my editorial eye and my persnickety nature, and throw me out of the story.Charging for blogs? Okay, so they're delivered more like a magazine or newspaper and it's only the "A Listers" but not worth it for me.Bottom line, if you are a ready who travels, this is absolutely a must-have. If you need to cut down on the number of paper books you're trying to to store in your house - again, awesome. It will never completely replace paper books for me, even though I believe in trying to live a greener life - I just love books. However, I've bought more Kindle books than paper books in the almost 6 months I've had my Kindle and look forward to the improvements that will invariably come in selection and technology to improve it.

title: with Amazon's own goods and services very much thrust to the fore (as is the case with Amazon’s excellent Fire TV Stick
body: Finally, we have a very high performance tablet available for under 50.00$That’s not a typing error - Amazon's very latest Fire 7 tablet will set you back just the meagre sum of $49.99.For that relatively modest price, you get no less than a 7 inch display tablet with a 1020 x 600 screen resolution, a 1.3GHz quad-core processor, 1GB of RAM, VGA front and 2MP rear cameras, and even the added bonus of a micro-SD port.Amazon asserts that the fully charged battery will function for up to seven hours, including reading, viewing movies and playing games. Amazon also asserts that its new Fire 7 tablet is twice as durable as the iPad Air 2, which is quite a bold claim.This particular tablet runs Amazon's own, custom made version of Android, dubbed Fire OS, and running the very latest version, Fire OS 5 'Bellini'. This gives a very different look and feel on the display screen, with Amazon's own goods and services very much thrust to the fore (as is the case with Amazon’s excellent Fire TV Stick, which I review elsewhere).The specifications of this tablet aren’t the best on the market, but at a sub $50.00 price point, I think that the new Fire 7 represents something of a consumer bargain.Budget tablets that pair a wallet-friendly price with anything other than a disappointing array of specifications, and an equally disappointing user experience, are still something of a novelty. However, I think that this new Amazon Fire tablet could be the exception disproving the rule.This 7-inch tablet will (currently) set you back just 49.99$, and while your wallet won’t be lightened too much, your tech-loving mind won't be infuriated up by the tablet's shortcomings. At first glance, and even after some extended use, there really aren't that many drawbacks to this device, and certainly not deal-breaking or unpalatable ones.Obviously, there's no avoiding the fact that this tablet is a no-frills affair, but for £50 you can’t really expect state of the art capability in all areas. Although it’s a little bit chubby at 10.6mm thick and 313g in weight, the new Amazon Fire tablet is nonetheless a perfectly comfortable device to hold, no less or more so than my Amazon Paperwhite e-Reader, which I review elsewhere.The weight of the tablet is well balanced across its 7-inch form, and its matt finish gives a reassuring level of grip. It would be preferable if the screen wasn't bedecked with some oversized bezels, but the modest pricing makes it hard to grumble at this too much – you don’t get a Rolls Royce for Renault Clio money.Apart from the very slightly chunky frame giving the display screen a slightly recessed look, the Fire's 7-inch, 1024 x 600 pixel IPS panel gives pleasingly detailed and surprisingly good results. Given that a tablet’s image quality is usually the first feature on the chopping block when the price is lowered, the excellent screen quality is a really nice surprise.Offering an impressive image quality which is equal to tablets two or three times its price, the Fire's apparently modest 171PPI resolution offers decent visuals by any standard, and truly outstanding results for the cost. There is a degree of noticeable pixellation to text, which sometimes makes reading less than ideal (if your focus is on reading, you’re better off with a Kindle Paperwhite in any event), but image-viewing and movie-watching is perfectly acceptable for casual and leisure use. I’ve just watched Luc Besson’s fabulous thriller Nikita on this little tablet and it was a perfectly enjoyable experience.Gaming looks really good too, which is more important for my 13 year old daughter than me, and thanks to the tablet’s impressively performing internals, the user experience doesn't fall down when playing something like Angry Birds or Candy Crush, etc.The Fire's 1.3GHz quad-core processor is paired up with 1GB of RAM, which offers enough computing punch to tackle all but the most demanding of app-based games and the heaviest of users' demands.The tablet’s power is paired with Amazon's newly revamped Fire OS 5 operating system. This is a cleverly modified version of Android, and the software is almost unrecognisable from the Google platform, wrapped up in Amazon's best efforts to get you to spend money – and if you own and use products like the excellent Amazon Fire TV stick, you’ll know just how zealously Amazon pushes its own products.That said, this is probably one of the Fire's biggest drawbacks, if not the biggest. Although in time you’d probably learn to live with Fire OS, at first it's quite an intrusive interface, that feels like it's pressurising you to go in a certain direction as a consumer. Amazon purchasing options are always very much at the forefront, and despite giving you impressively cut-price hardware, Amazon is very clearly, one way or another, after your cash.Of course, if you don’t mind Amazon pushing it’s own products, and adverts for other sponsored products, onto your lock screen, buying into this product at under 50.00$ is hardly a raw deal.You can’t expect to buy a tablet this cheaply without having at least one or two drawbacks – especially bearing in mind the fact that prices for a market-leading compact tablet like the iPad Mini, currently start north of £300.00, i.e. six times more expensive than the Fire tablet (and not necessarily six times better). In any case, you can pay an extra tenner and Amazon removes the meddlesome adverts for you.The tablet's twin camera collection – there's a 2-megapixel offering around the back and a VGA shooter up front – leaves something to be desired. You get lower quality and quite grainy images that lack depth and detail. It’s not great, but then the camera probably isn’t meant to be a big selling point of this bargain basement gadget.There’s plenty of good news too though. For the first time, there’s a micro-SD slot for adding storage. This was the Achilles Heel of earlier generation Fire tablets, especially those with 8GB internal storage.The new Fire has this 8GB capacity too – but only 5GB is available to you as the user. But you can now provide your own micro-SD card (up to 128GB), or add a SanDisk Ultra Class 10 micro-SD card to your order - 32GB costs 11.99$, 64GB costs 19.99$ and 128GB costs 53.99$ (I bought the 32GB SD card, but the 64GB card seems like the most cost-effective option out of the three, at least in terms of GB per pound).As stated above, this tablet has a 7in screen, and it uses the same IPS technology as an iPad, and so viewing angles, colours and contrast are exceptionally good at the price point.The built-in Wi-Fi is clearly more basic than flagship tablets, but it’s unlikely to make a noticeable difference in general day to day use. The Fire tablet charges via a micro USB, but the downside is that it takes about six hours for a full charge up from a flat battery – very roughly the same time as the battery lasts for browsing the web, reading books and watching videos.Another brownie point is given for built-in Bluetooth for compatible earphones and headphones, speakers and low-energy Bluetooth accessories. Additionally, a mono speaker and microphone are also built-in.You have a choice of several different cases, including Amazon’s own custom fit one for 19.99$, or else non-Amazon produced cases for a few pounds less.Amazon’s Mayday help service is no longer limited to the flagship tablets – it’s now on the 49.99$ Fire tablet too. That means you can get expert help any time, day or night, even on Christmas Day.There’s now even more of a focus on entertainment. So, while you get all the benefits you’ll see on previous Amazon tablets such as the ASAP predictive buffering (which means videos start playing as soon as you tap on them), a new ‘On Deck’ feature automatically downloads videos and the first episodes of Amazon original series – such as Extant, Transparent and Bosch – so there’s always something to watch even if you’re offline. Downloading only applies if you have a subscription to Amazon Prime, or Prime Instant Video, of course.If you own a PS3 or PS4 games system, you can ‘fling’ videos to your TV and use the Fire tablet as a second screen. This also works with the Amazon Fire TV or Fire TV Stick.If you’re buying this tablet for a child, the Fire for Kids app provides a filtered, safe environment where you the parent control which apps and what content they can access and see. You can even apply time limits for different activities such as reading and playing games.FINAL SUMMATION:-PROS: –Impressive screen qualityEasy to set upIntuitive and user friendly operating experienceMicro-SD card portRespectable 7 hour battery lifeCopes easily with movies and gamesThe price is a stealYou can have a fling without needing a divorce lawyerCONS:-Amazon REALLY wants you to buy their productsCamera and video performance is unimpressiveIt’s not great for reading (but the Kindle Paperwhite e-Reader certainly is)Slow 6 hour battery charge up timeThank you very much for kindly taking the time to read through this review. I hope that it helps you to make an informed decision about whether or not this product is a good choice for you and/or your family.

title: Why and how the Kindle changes everything
body: This is less a "pros and cons" review than a hopefully useful commentary about the Kindle compared with other eReaders and what it means for the eBook industry. (I believe that everything has changed with the Kindle's creation.)For many years I have been an avid reader of eBooks using almost every eReading device on the market. So as an early-adopter of techie gadgets I had been anxiously awaiting Amazon's Kindle since its first rumors. So I immediately purchased it both out of curiosity and hoping for a better "next generation" eBook solution. In case you're wondering whether I'm "that" Steve Gibson, I probably am -- I'm the guy who gets Google's first three or four links when Googling my name.I was driven to write this review because it is somewhat distressing and, it seems to me, a bit unfair for the Kindle's average review rating to be dragged so far down by Kindle NON-OWNERS who, judging from their comments, seem to be quite annoyed by all of the positive comments about a device that's expensive, monochrome, not a general purpose media player, unable to leap tall buildings, or in some way less than they were expecting, wanting, or hoping. In contrast to non-owners, the people who actually HAVE Kindle's appear to universally love it, though with very valid caveats. I think of this as "The TiVo Effect" since, for the right sort of user, the Kindle will be life-changing ... but it certainly won't be that for everyone. Although it took me a few days to get completely comfortable with it, I am now hooked.So, for what it's worth, if this posting is discovered by any truly interested pre-purchasers, I hope that the following commentary might place the Kindle in "perspective" and be of some value to you. (And if it is, I hope you'll click the button at the bottom to indicate that, so that this review might be found by more potential buyers ... Thank you!)I have read many novel-length books on my various Palm's, I owned the original Rocket eBook, and I own both generations of Sony's eInk readers, the PRS-500 and PRS-505. So my clear bias is of someone who enjoys technology for its own sake and who loves the idea of reading books on a "device."Amazon's first-generation Kindle arguably has a few warts (see below). So depending upon your needs, budget, willingness to purchase a "first-generation" gizmo that you might regret purchasing and want to replace a year from now, and so forth, you might well decide to wait for the next generation Kindle that will doubtless be even better. But whether you choose to jump aboard now or later, Amazon's entry into the eBook market is a BIG deal -- it forever changes the game. I think there is no doubt that for the first time ever, a substantial number of people who were never captivated by ANY previous eBook system will find themselves reading and enjoying textual content on Kindle's eInk screen.The weird initial love/hate reaction to the Kindle is being compared with Apple's iPod, which was also initially met with striking polarization. We all know how that turned out. :) Although the iPod was far from being the first portable MP3 player, and critics called it a copycat, it was the first portable music player to go mainstream, and it changed the world. I believe that, similarly, the large and tightly interacting collection of Kindle features, that go far beyond those of any other previous eBook attempt, will cause the Kindle to be the first eBook to succeed. By connecting their massive book library, as well as newspapers, magazines, blogs and the Web -- wirelessly -- to a long-battery-life chunk of consumer plastic, Amazon has kicked eBooks into the mainstream.Is the Kindle perfect? Not yet. Is it expensive? Yep. Does it feel like a first-generation product? Absolutely. Will I purchase the next Kindle too? Please let me be first in line!Investing in Kindle's future...From a DRM (digital rights management, aka eBook copy-protection) perspective, my eBook content ownership is already spread around all over the place; from Mobipocket, to Palm eReader, to Sony Connect, and now to Amazon Kindle. Sure, that annoys me a bit, but it's the price one pays for being an early adopter of technology that isn't yet ready for prime time ... as, until now, no eBook system has been. Sony's efforts came the closest, but that all ended for Sony (and everyone else) with the introduction of the Kindle. Existing owners of other eBook formats will certainly continue purchasing content for their devices, but who in the U.S. would purchase a new $300 Sony eReader when for an additional $100 they could have the Kindle ... which is so much more than any of the other "disconnected" read-only devices?In other words, given that Amazon is Amazon, and the fact that they already, right out of the gate, offer so much more than any other previous solution, I feel comfortable now building up my eBook content ownership with Amazon. Sure, I've been wrong before, but this is where I'm placing my bet. I won't be purchasing any more content for Palm's eReader or Sony's. And I like the fact that the content I am purchasing now for this first-generation Kindle will certainly always be readable on whatever future generation devices Amazon's efforts will evolve into.Look Ma, no wires!The huge deal with TiVo was time-shifting and commercial skipping. The huge deal with the Kindle is its wireless connectivity. Being a "traditional" eBook user -- i.e. download into PC and "dock" the eReader to upload -- I didn't 'get' that at first. Now I'm as hooked by that on the Kindle as I am by my Tivo's ability to whiz through endless commercials. The Kindle brings the same sort of freedom and power to textual content that the cell phone brought to voice communications.Sure, I'll purchase eBooks for the Kindle. But I have subscribed to a newspaper and two magazines ... and it is truly a paradigm shift to have their content "just be there" in the morning all by itself. And the periodical content is clean, blessedly free of ads, unnecessary pictures and distractions.An ugly duckling in need of forgiveness?Like many people who worship the infinitely-understated elegance of Apple's iPhone (and many other Apple creations), the Kindle's appearance put me off at first. I was as vocally critical of the darned thing as any of those "one star" reviewers. When the first early photos of it leaked a few months before its release, I thought "No way, what a joke! That must be an early balsa-wood mock-up." Now that weird angular wedggie is sitting here next to me as I type this. And I have forgiven it because something odd happens after using it for a few days: You begin to realize that it really works ... and it works well. (And have you ever tried actually typing on the iPhone's all-screen keyboard?)Did someone say "warts"?The Kindle's screen appears to have slightly lower contrast than Sony's second generation reader, but much more than Sony's first generation offering. Also, the Kindle's fonts are *far* superior to Sony's, extremely legible, in six sizes and with real italics, not just algorithmic slanting. I'm a bit annoyed that the line-spacing is so large on the larger fonts since page changing is an "event", but, again, this is just the first shot.And speaking of page changing, I am not a big fan of the page navigation on this first Kindle. So much of the device is devoted to making page changing easy that it's difficult to pick up and handle the device without inadvertently changing pages. But once you're settled down and reading, the fact that only a thumb-twitch is required is nice. One way or another I'm sure that Amazon will get plenty of feedback about everything ... and the next one will be even better.You want to charge me what??!!There's also been a great deal of confusion about Amazon charging for the conversion and delivery of our own content into our own Kindles. Amazon *only* charges for wireless delivery, the conversion is 100% free. If you eMail your content to YourKindleName@kindle.com it's converted and downloaded into your Kindle for 10 cents. But if, instead, you eMail your content to YourKindleName@free.kindle.com it's converted and a link to the converted file is eMailed to your registered eMail address at NO charge. You can then download it and use your PC's USB connection to transfer the content to the Kindle.Moreover, the FREE MobiPocket v4.2 Creator will convert many formats -- HTML, MS Word Docs, Text, and Adobe PDF into .PRC files -- nicely compressed and encrypted if you wish -- which, when transferred into the Kindle are directly readable. I have converted two large eBooks which I already had in PDF format into native Kindle format and they work perfectly -- no cost and no Amazon involvement at all. And I'm sure that quite soon there will be all sorts of free Kindle content converters popping up all over the place.So I'm glad that I purchased this first-generation device, and that I'm participating in the first real wave of eBook industry creation. None of my other eBook readers offer nearly what the Kindle does. Thanks to Amazon and their Kindle, eBooks have finally happened.

title: UPDATED: Incredible Value for Under $50!
body: [[VIDEOID:58631ccd00b9a1ff824c77b8aac0c363]] UPDATED - After spending quite a bit more time with the device, I would give it a 4.5 due to a few specific gaps that are a bit annoying. However, you are still getting an amazing 7” tablet, with front and rear facing cameras, a gorgeous interface, fairly snappy performance and durability, all for under 50 bucks! I can’t imagine not buying these for myself and my whole family, but not a primary tablet for a techie adult by any means. For background, I have every Kindle, a couple Fires, and multiple tablets from Apple, Microsoft and Samsung. Note that my review with 5 stars considers the value equation, not just performance and how that may or may not compare to other tablets - if you are expecting this to compare to a tablet costing several times more, don't bother. But if you are looking for a great entry level tablet that does most of the things people want, this little tablet definitely delivers the value!PRICING/CONFIG: I prefer this tablet with ads and no accessories to keep the costs down. You have the option to spend more money, but I recommend against it. You can easily see the specs online, so I won’t do you the discourtesy of simply cutting and pasting those here. Here is the price breakdown:• 49.99 base price – what an incredible price point! Or buy 5 and get a sixth one free! This puts it into reach of schools and non-profits.• No sponsored screensaver ($15) – big deal that each time you turn it on it shows you something interesting you might want.• MicroSD card ($19.99 for 32GB) – you probably already have one laying around somewhere. Beyond that, there is memory in the device, and you are using the cloud for storage mostly anyway. If you end up needing this, just buy one off Amazon, it certainly won’t be more expensive. Also, the SD is likely less useful than you might think, since it is currently a bit limited in what can be stored on it.• Amazon Fire Case (24.99) – it is supposed to be 2x the durability of an iPad. No case needed.• Accident Protection (9.99) – who wants to spend 9.99 to insure a $49 device? During your install, they will give you another chance, and you can get the two year for 15.99 if you like. But to me, insurance is for catastrophic things, not a <$50 tablet. UPDATE - after looking into the accident protection, if I were giving it to kids, I might get the insurance. You can get a 1 year pretty cheap as long as you are in the US, and it covers everything from dropping it off a building, running it over with the car, or accidentally hitting it with a baseball bat. As long as you have the pieces, you are good, and there are no back and forth shipping charges to pay.• That is a total of $119.97, or almost triple the price. If you’re buying this device, you’re probably a bit price sensitive – if you aren’t, you’re buying a different device and this review is somewhat irrelevant.GETTING STARTED: Here is some timing for me to get started with this device:• 1 minute – open box and read instructions• 18 minutes – download and update to latest Fire software. If you are giving it as a gift, it is simply good form to turn it on first to get the updates done, even if you are not setting it up under your own name.• Battery started at 65%, so I had plenty of time to connect, download, and play around with the device.• I ordered it without my account being integrated, just as if I were buying it as a gift, unregistered. However, it came pre-configured with all of my information. If you are giving them away, I would double check this.• Tutorials are super simple and quick, and well worth doing if you are unfamiliar with the Fire interface. It is really nice that they integrate your own data into the tutorials.• HINT: While setting up, go to settings, select security, and take care of adding a passcode to your device. If you ever lose it, someone can cause some havoc if you don’t.APPSTORE: Appstore Underground is fantastic. Simple to use, lots of great apps. Nice to know I won’t get gouged here for other fees since everything is included. I have not yet explored the quality of apps available here, so more on that in a later update. Had all my critical apps – LinkedIn, Pinterest, Twitter, Skype, etc. However, as noted, you cant use apps from the Android store, so you have to wait for them via Amazon. Not too big of a deal for me, but others might find it a problem.EMAIL: Email setup was a breeze. In less than 3 minutes, I had my Office 365 account, yahoo account and gmail all set up and flowing perfectly. The interface for switching accounts is one of the easiest I have ever used.CAMERA: The camera and video functionality were easily accessible, and I liked the quality. Not quite the same experience as using the iPhone, perhaps, but it may be that I also don’t have the apps yet installed that make it look so great, so more on that later as I experiment. The camera is 2MP, and the video is 720 HD. If you need something higher res, the Fire HD has 5 MP camera and 1080 HD. But then you don’t have a $49.99 tablet ' Same goes for the added speaker and lack of Dolby sound – this is a single speaker that sounds OK, but it is certainly not the same as the Fire 8 or an iPad. UPDATE: Have played around with the camera a bit more, and added a composite image - the first picture you'll see that it does a very poor job in low light conditions. As the light increases, you get some more detail, and brightly lit is not a bad picture at all, with good detail. This is not the same level of camera you will get on an iPhone or Lumia, so don't expect that, it is fine for casual photo, selfie, Skype, etc.OS: The new OS is fantastic. It is simple to use, even for my fat fingers, and makes sense. Just as advertised, it puts the things you are likely to want to do front and center, makes it easily accessible to find new TV shows and the like, and just overall is a super clean and simple interface. Rotation responsiveness is immediate, unlike many other tablets, and I found the Silk browser on this to be quite snappy in performance. Watching video I found absolutely no lag when I streamed my Prime TV shows.FORM FACTOR: This is light and easy to hold, with smooth, rounded corners that don’t dig into your hands if help for extended periods. It is heavier than a kindle – if you are used to reading on a Kindle, it is not a replacement for that. Given the lower resolution on this device at 171ppi, I will DEFINITELY keep my Paperwhite for extended reading sessions, and would not recommend this tablet if your primary purpose is reading books, and you do quite a bit of that. I included a screenshot, along with comparison to one of our phones, and you can definitely tell a big difference when looking at fine print, and brightness does not compare. I did enjoy the fact that during several hours of use, I never accidentally hit any buttons, something that always drives me nuts on other devices, so button placement is one of the most convenient out there.SD CARD: Note that I saw in another review that you can have all of your music on an SD card, you just have to download it through your laptop first. I have not personally tried this, but I imagine that several things with the SD card will change in future updates to make it more broadly valuable, and Amazon has said this in support interactions. I don't have any view of when that update might be coming, however.OTHER: Have to love all the kid-friendly (or rather, parent-friendly features) that this comes with. Again, buying a pack of these things for the fam is definitely a no-brainer. Charging is not fast - I ran it to zero, then charged for exactly 30 minutes, which brought it to 11%. Doing the math, you're looking at somewhere in the 4.5 - 5 hour range to fully charge.SUMMARY: There is plenty more to say about this, but basic summary is this is an amazing product for the price, and delivers much more than $50 value, especially if you have a Prime account. I have absolutely no hesitation recommending this enjoyable product. This is my go to tablet for just carrying around in my car. I will still keep my Paperwhite for reading, and I have another more expensive tablet for really watching movies, doing actual work, extended email sessions, etc. But this makes a great 3rd device, and I far prefer it vs trying to do similar activities on a phone. It is perfect for Skype, games, Facebook, browsing, watching videos, emergency reading, etc. There is tremendous value in this device.NOTE: There are 3 attached pictures/videos. First is one of the Fire next to my cat to show the nice size. Yes, the cat is huge and I only had seconds before he covered it with his massive body - why do cats do that? The second is a screenshot comparison between the Fire and my phone - big difference in text clarity. The Third is a video showing my 3rd drop test, with no cracks, fractures or other damage. I am now done dropping it, rest assured, it seems quite durable.

title: This is the 5th Generation, so it's pretty obsolete now
body: I am writing this in September of 2020. I got my 5th Generation Fire at Staples back in September or October of 2016. For me, it has been a great device, though it has also had some critical problems.I don't use Alexa, but it is good to know that it has the capacity.The tablet works fine for its purpose. It is an appliance. It displays books and magazines well. It plays HD video. It does not make UHD and 4K stuff look any better than HD, but it is still decent for 7". The catalog of apps for it is diminishing as the media involved and encryption become more complicated. I miss having an eBay app on it. For a while, I tried Facebook Lite. It does not have a functioning version of FB Messenger Lite.It has been a very useful gadget for me. I do not have some way to give it GPS, but I have gotten it to work pretty well with Google Maps (through web browsers) and been able to get good directions and working bus schedules. Most public, free WiFi is still transmitting at 2.4Ghz, so the lack of 5.0Ghz connectivity has not been a problem.It has been great for reviewing YouTube videos when I would set up somewhere to do an electronics repair and wanted a video runthrough for the repair before starting. I have a bunch of camera and computer owners manuals and technical manuals stored on the tablet for quick review in connection with the Adobe Acrobat Reader app.I have not tried any advanced writing. There are some office suite apps available, but this is not the device for laying out your resume, writing a term paper or generating a newspaper. You can do any of this, but with such a small screen and having to connect Bluetooth or similar input peripherals, serious writing is tedious and frustrating.I dislike the camera. I do not get it to focus well, and I have not been able to get it to scan barcodes as I would like. I am sure that it can work, but it has been beyond me.I use the Fire primarily for web browsing, e-mail and Facebook. The Facebook app and the Facebook mobile pages in the web browsers do not display well. The layout is crowded. The pages and the app are not particularly responsive. Facebook Marketplace ads do not display correctly either in the app or in the browser. The pages extend past the screen, and there is no way to scroll over to see the missing parts.E-mail through Microsoft Exchange servers can be problematic. The e-mail app usually connects and works well, but occasionally, the app stops connecting to the mail servers when you switch to a different WiFi network.Memory is generally adequate for the stuff that I do. I put a 32GB storage card in it, and I have been able to get the apps and the operating system to put most things into the on-tablet storage or the microSD card.I have had problems with the MicroUSB socket. The socket is delicate, and the cables that connect to it are delicate. After a very short time, any cable will have had its internals deformed to where it takes effort to keep the cable connected for charging the battery. I have broken enough MicroUSB sockets on phones and other devices that I know not to knock the socket around when a cable or device is plugged in. The location of the socket along the "top" edge of the case is not helpful with that.I do not have a lot of apps loaded on the tablet. I would like to clear up application memory by getting rid of a lot of the Amazon apps, but that is not possible. Removing the "factory" applications requires Rooting the tablet, and that has not been an option since Fire OS 5.3.2.1.In the past few months, the Fire had started developing problems with playing back video in web browser pages. The video frames would be flickering between sizes and resolutions and a portion of the bottom of the video would be something looking like a flickering green bar. That appears to be cured now, but I have not had that extensive of a test of video since the last Fire OS update.Also, every so often, app icons that I had collected into groups on the Home screen would show up back, loose on the Home screen as if they had been dragged out of their group folders, and sometimes with a generic "fire" iconinstead of the apps' original icons. This appears to have been cured with the latest Fire OS update, too.The Fire automatically downloads and installs any updates to the Fire OS that are released for the tablet. Around two weeks ago, the Fire downloaded the latest Fire OS 5.7.6.0 update. The update was very big, and I did not know that the Fire was in the process of downloading it. I just started getting "Low Memory" messages. I tried a bunch of things to free up memory, but none of them appeared to work. When the update finally finished downloading and then installed, I got the first message about the update to the Fire OS. This is not entirely out of the ordinary to get these messages as a surprise. Initially, I was glad, because I had been through a similar situation before and the final installation freed up the "cache" memory that the device had reserved. Then, apps started freezing and crashing. After a bit, the whole device would freeze. Then, the device went into a "boot loop", where it would display the "fire" logo animation but never progress beyond it. I suspect that the download of the Fire OS 5.6.7.0 update was corrupted because of the memory use on the Fire and it never properly updated the electronics with the new operating system.At this point, the 5th Generation tablet was "bricked". I tried looking for solutions, but I could not find one. I went to purchase a used 7th Generation Fire as a replacement. The seller had improperly advertised the device, and that has been returned. I purchased an 8th Generation 8" Fire, and I am in the process of setting that up.In the meantime, I ran across some forum posts with people modifying the 5th Generation Fire by Rooting it through ADB Sideloading, which basically rewrites the electronically programmable chips inside the tablet. all of the forum posts noted that it was no longer possible to Root the 5th Generation Fire after the Fire OS 5.3.2.1 update, but ADB Sideloading would still allow the chips to be rewritten. With that as a theory, I got the drivers and software for an ADB Composite Device Interface and downloaded a fresh set of the Fire OS 5.6.7.0 update and used ADB Sideloading to rewrite the chips in the 5th Generation Fire. It worked. The Fire works again.All of the original information on the tablet was erased in the process of rewriting the electronics. The data on the microSD card was untouched. The tablet has new "splash" screens, and the video playback problem seems to be fixed. When restarting the reprogrammed Fire, I provided my Amazon account information, and the tablet was able to restore from the cloud-based backup. I still had to track down the apps in the Amazon App Store that I had previously downloaded, and for some of the apps that are no longer available from the App Store, I was able to download them through the App Management settings.There are some apps that I was unable to re-download. Everything seems to be working fine now. Better than it has in months. It may be that a backup, fresh OS install and then restoration was what it needed.After that ADB Sideload headache, everything is working for me.I saw someone's advice on how to get APK files to download from Google Play's free listings and install into the Fire tablet, and I may give that a try. If it works out, I will stop back and update.Update: What I found that worked was to enable the Silk Browser to install apps from other sources. It was a security setting. I was able to download a few files that are not Amazon approved for the Fire, and they work. Others would download and install but not run.Today (still in September of 2020), the Fire had discharged the battery to where it turned off. I currently have it plugged in to a cable from a desktop computer, which is a low amperage power source for it. After being plugged in to a power source for the last fifteen minutes, it still does not want to turn on. When I tried the power button, it did not display the "charging" icon or turn on. It may be a result of the power supply being a "low power" source for it.Final(?) update: It is September 29, 2020. The Amazon Fire 5th Generation has not turned back on after the last boot loop and shut down, so it appears permanently "bricked" at this point. I had already received a refurbished Fire 8 8th Generation as a replacement, and am now working with that, but I may get another 7" soon. The 8" is a bit too big and is not easily manipulated with only one hand.

title: I wasn't sure and waited...I love it
body: I was very unsure whether or not I wanted to shell out so much money on an ebook reader. Like most people interested in this, I have an extensive 'real book' library, and am a voracious reader...but I didn't want to drop a huge amount of money on something that wasn't better than real books in most ways.This is the best thing I've ever owned, and let me tell you why.1. LIBRARYI am in the process of replacing my entire real book library onto the Kindle. I store the book files on my computer. I'm in the process of buying a memory chip because I've filled up my Kindle already. You can find many books out there for free, even current books. (Yes, even legally!). I personally love speculative fiction (Sci-fi and fantasy) and TOR books releases a new ebook for free weekly. So it's not difficult to find many resources to build your library. I personally didn't want to pay AGAIN for many titles (as I've bought some books more than once already in real book form, as I've given them away, worn them out, loaned them out without return, etc.) I have the option of using software to scan them in and have text files made of them (many universities and other organizations have such software), finding them for free various places online, rebuying them, or finding them elsewhere. Your mileage will vary...but it's not hard to build a library.Book lovers don't have to be told that if they read a book and love it, to start buying from that author. We do it with public libraries and it's why TOR gives away free books, and I think that trend is going to continue. (In other words, borrowing isn't a bad thing, stealing is! Don't be a jerk. :) )2. THE INTERNET!Free internet access and anyone even remotely saavy can use the kindle to do an awful lot online. You may have to be creative, you may have to change email providers (you can't access all providers through it) but you can use gmail and other free online e-mail. Additionally it's currently free with no plans at this point to charge for it.Wikipedia access is a huge bonus for me.3. LOOK IT UP, MAKE A NOTEYou can make notes 'in your books' without defacing the book. You can back up your entire kindle library on a computer (or disk or data card, etc.) so you can never 'lose' these things.Generally when I run across words or terms I don't understand, I get the jist from the sentence or surrounding ideas. This way I can continue reading and 'get it' without interruption. If I REALLY don't get it, or it's confusing I can look it up. Often I'd intend to look up a name or place or thing like that later...and forget.With the Kindle I can do this instantly without losing my place in the book, and I wish I could adequately express to you what a HUGE difference this is. Without sounding like a snot, sure I'm extremely bright and have a huge vocab. thanks to reading. So I generally don't need to look up things. And I can get the basic idea. Looking up words I 'kinda' know the meaning to but couldn't perfectly nail down has not only added to my experience, and vocabulary, but more importantly in some ways it's added to the depth of my experience with the book.Here's one example. I was reading Philip Jose Farmer's Riverworld (again) and Ultima Thule is mentioned. I 'get' from the structure that it's talking about a northern place, and they use an alternative term 'fools gold' (because in context it's also a mission that won't succeed) as the character is trying to reach this distant point in the Riverworld. But when I looked up Thule, I saw that it was historically (in ancient Rome) refered to the most northern point of the world. So Ultima Thule, in this instance would be the ultimate northern most place, and made MUCH more sense. It also told me more about the character who used that term (and how he knew it) and just added more depth.Sure I didn't have to look it up. And in a real book, I wouldn't have. But using my kindle, it took a simple scroll of the mousewheel to select the sentence, then it looked up key words in the sentence and I selected the right word and there it was. Then I closed it and continued reading, and it took under 20 seconds.How amazing is that. If I tried to do that with a computer and real books, I'd be sidetracked online reading more and more and would 'lose' my spot in the book. If I wasn't too lazy to do it in the first place.4. NOWNOWThere's an experimental service that allows you to ask a question and 3 'experts' will look up answers for your in around 10 mins.This service is the same as someone with good Google-Fu (the ability to do good research online and find things using google, etc.). I have excellent google-fu. But I'm not always near a computer and I don't always have the time. So why not ask NowNow for free?It's also perfect for settling stupid questions. I was watching poker with my husband and we wondered what year Jamie Gold won the WSOP. He got up to 'look it up' and took his usual half hour as he read other things, and then checked his email and etc. Instead Nownow can get the same answer w/in 10 mins. without us leaving the room. (Especially good at resturants!)5. SIZEI'm a voracious reader, and I can't carry tons of books with me when I travel or even I'm out and about. With kindle it's as small as a paperback and lighter in weight, and with a memory card I can carry an entire library with me. The basic memory says it's about 200 books, seems less to me but I haven't counted all the books I put on. It's not enough memory for me, but to expand it will be like $20 anyway. Then I can read whatever I want.6. BUY IT NOWWhen I want a new book I can buy it through Amazon and have it delivered wirelessly almost instantly. The only complaint there is currently their available books aren't that great. I couldn't buy the entire Riverworld series there (which I'm reading now) but I did find it various places online. You won't be able to get every book you want...but you should be able to get most of them.7. EYESI have a neurological problem which affects my eyesight, and cannot be corrected, and changes. So sometimes I can read just fine. Sometimes things are a bit blurry. Kindle allows you to change the text size at any time, and so far I've never had a problem reading a page. THIS IS A HUGE BENEFIT TO ME. You could also use magnifiers with the kindle, though I'd be shocked if anyone had problems with the highest size setting (other than the legally blind who need external tools anyway). The color is also easy on the eyes, though the 'paper' is a darker grey than I'd personally choose, its like a pulp paperback and very easy going on the eyes.There's no internal light, so the light is ambient light. I bought a book light, and use the nova of the light (making sure the bulb doesn't reflect on the page) and that works perfectly for me in low light settings. I bought the LED light recommended for the kindle, the one that clips on. Pretty much any such light would work, but I like that I can move the thing around as the stem is a bendy.OVERALL:I really love it. It's the best thing I've ever gotten. My husband is so happy at how much I love it, and it's constantly with me. Is it perfect? No. I'd change the buttons around a bit. I sometimes hit the page fwd/back/next buttons by mistake when laying down with my kindle. The search function in the library is kinda sucky. The ability to mod. some things, also sucky. I'd love to be able to create my own subfolders and better organize things.But these are all things which I personally think will be 'better' in the future, with software updates. I personally think the 'kindle 2.0' will not be a new machine, but rather new software which we'll ALL get.And even if they didn't fix a single thing, and it stayed this way...it's money well spent for me.I also had a problem with my 'first' kindle with a button being not as flush as I thought it should be. I called their CSR to ask if I could pop it off or what. They apologized and overnighted me a new kindle.The customer service on this thing is AMAZING. The kindle users are sometimes a little fanatical...and you get people who get angry at 'suggested' changes (like those who want better mp3 support and those who scream IT'S A BOOK LEAVE IT ALOOOONE), but we all have in common love for this thing.It's a potential portable Library of Alexandria. Which is why my kindle is named Alexandria.

title: A willing convert into the Kindle Cult
body: I have now been an owner of the Kindle since January 22nd, and I have thoroughly played with the device. I now feel ready to write a review of this product. Before purchasing the Kindle, I had perused the reviews on this fine site and had to suffer through all the cretinous 1-star reviews by people who had not even purchased the device. Worse, they weren't even witty. I rarely write reviews, but I feel the need to restore some balance.The Kindle is a revolutionary device. The Book is a fantastic device, and it's design has not shifted significantly over the hundreds of years. The Kindle is a fantastic step up.First, if you are reading this review, and not yet a Kindle owner, I beseech you to do your research. Thoroughly review the information Amazon provides you. There are discussion boards available too. They will clue you into various issues that may confuse you if you do not watch out for them. So many of these reviews that are negative are by people who did not do their research, so please learn from their mistakes!First and foremost, find out if Sprint's EVDO network is available in your area. Whispernet is a delightful service. But do not assume it's everywhere.For prospective buyers in Elk Grove, CA, Sacramento, CA and San Francisco, CA, I can vouch that it works smashingly. Whispernet works better in my house than my non-sprint cell phone provider. Pity I can't make a phone call with the Kindle.Now, for what I like and some things I like less about the Kindle. First, the good:1. The Feel: Yes, it may take some getting used to, but the Kindle is easy to hold. Yes, you will accidentally hit the buttons. This can be managed, and the simplicity of "turning pages" is well worth the risk. Simply experiment. There are times when holding it in the case is more comfortable. Other times, holding the device itself is more comfortable. My moods change. Regardless, I find this many times more enjoyable than having to use both hands to hold a book. Plus, I like being able to set the device on a table and not have to worry about keeping a book pried open so I don't lose my place. You'll learn to use the keyboard lock feature (which also puts it into a sleep mode).2. Portability: It is very light and simple to carry around. It fits in my purse, and the fact that I have numerous books in my purse does not weigh me down. I can take this device to the gym, and read in comfort. The Kindle does not need a fancy book holder, it just needs the narrow ledge that most fitness machines provide. Today I just spent a few hours waiting for my car to be repaired and the Kindle kept me company there, as well.3. Readability: The six different font sizes are great. I wear glasses, so when I'm at places like the hair salon, where glasses get in the way, I can increase the font and read with no difficulty. It's easy to walk and read, as well, if you're into that sort of thing. Reading in sunlight is sublime. I love having an electronic device that will allow for that! Page turning is just fine. Not too slow, not too fast. I don't find the flash annoying at all. Reading in bed is very easy. And now, if my cat jumps on my chest and scares the bejeezus out of me causing me to drop my book, I no longer lose my place.4. Whispernet: It works great. I've purchased books directly from Amazon and it is very fast. As for the experimental internet feature, I'll describe that later. Buying books from Amazon is so easy, you can do it with one hand while trotting on a treadmill. I can see my Amazon expenses will be rising. I love being able to download samples off of Amazon. Of course, it means better business from me.5. Newspaper Subscriptions: At first, I wasn't that enthused, but I have come to love it. It takes a minute to download in the mornings, and poof! All sorts of news to read. I've partaken of the Washington Post and San Francisco Chronicle. I will likely keep subscribing.6. Highlights and "My Clippings". I find it very easy to highlight an interesting passage and add notes. I love the fact that I can then download it to my pc at home if I want to write something referencing that quote, or save it for later. While note taking isn't quite so easy while on the Elliptical trainer, you can high light a passage with one hand while working out. This delights me.7. The dictionary feature: very n ice. Though I haven't read books that are lofty enough for me to actually need it.8. Adding your own content: I have acquired a bunch of ebooks from Project Gutenberg. That's a fascinating pursuit. And it's easy enough to convert those documents, either by emailing them to your kindle, or using an application like Mobipocket. I've even added my own notes/documents in Word format. It does handle pictures satisfactorily. Of course, I didn't expect high-definition quality.9. Battery Life: I end up charging my Kindle about every 1.5 to 2 days. I don't use the wireless extensively, only enough to download books, get my newspapers, and check email.10. The keyboard: I was pleased with it. It is very easy to use.Some things I would like to see improve:1. The Internet is moderately useful, but not so useful that I'm going to be spending a lot of time using it. It's great for checking email, but it doesn't handle some heavier sites very well. Like Netflix. I think the browser actually just shut down while I was using it. Weather sites, news sites, and email work fine, however. That's okay; this isn't meant to replace my laptop. One of the first problems I had with the Kindle was that something I was trying to do rendered surfing the net useless. I couldn't hit any site. I had to fully reboot the device. Thankfully, I haven't had a problem like that since.2. I hope they allow you to purchase different dictionaries such that you can select a different dictionary for the Kindle to utilize. For example, I'd love to be able to buy French, Latin, or Greek dictionaries and then download books in those languages. That would be great for language learners.3. Since use of Whispernet siphons the battery down fairly quickly, I wish the button to toggle wireless was on the front of the Kindle. Or at least relegated to some function keys, like keyboard locking is.4. Currently, when I decide to buy a book after reading the sample, Amazon downloads another copy of the book. I wish a full purchase of the book would over-write the sample. It would be even cooler if the book you just purchased remembered where you left off reading the sample. But that's over the top. I just think it's kind of less convenient to have to delete the sample passage of the book you purchased.5. PDFs: I do wish this handled PDFs better. I ran into someone with a Sony E-reader, and apparently it will let you read any pdf, and scroll around the document if it doesn't fit the screen-size of the E-reader. Despite this lack of capability, I don't plan on returning my Kindle. If you do have Adobe Publisher or some OCR program, there are ways you can convert some PDFs, but I haven't been too willing to engage in the effort.6. Of course, I wish all of the publishers would hurry up and publish in Kindle format. I really almost hate to read a normal paperback now that I have the Kindle. And I haven't wanted to purchase a regular book since. Sadly, I cannot force their hands.In conclusion, I prefer reading this device to reading normal books, now. My love of books has not dwindled. I will probably still collect more rare hard cover paper books, but most of my reading will now occur on the kindle. It is magnificent and I look forward to seeing how Amazon continues to improve this device.No, I do not regret spending $400 on this. And I will break even soon. Yes, it will make you read more books. I have read two this week, when I usually take two weeks to read one book. And I know this product is not for everyone. But for everyone who loves to read comfortably, the Kindle is for you.P.S. About all this DRM ruckus: get over yourselves. You can loan your kindle to a friend any time you want to. Of course, you won't want to. Otherwise, send your friend to the library.

title: Far superior to the Kindle Fire HD...
body: I have to say that after readings some of the negative reviews claiming that the 5th Generation Kindle Fire is inferior to prior releases, I was concerned that I might be disappointed in the performance of this new 5th Generation Device, versus the one I already have, a Kindle Fire HD, which is probably several years old. But, I agree with the negative reviewers that the internal memory of the Kindle Fire is not adequate, and should have been expanded in the design phase of the 5th Generation device. As it stands now, 5Gb is just a little too close for comfort with respect to those apps that are incompatible for movement to the SD card. For example, the app entitled "Overdrive" will not move to the SD card, and must operate off of the internal memory of the device. I haven't found too many apps that won't move, but it would have been a better Kindle Fire had they designed it with a little more internal memory. The other criticism I have, is that file maintenance can not be performed by simply plugging into the PC, and accessing the files, data, etc...directly. If you're going to use the SD card, you have to remove it, and access it separate from the Kindle Fire in order to put the files, data, videos, photos, etc...on the SD card. They should have designed the Kindle Fire so that you could more easily maintain your data directly from the PC.The negative reviews that you will sometimes read with respect to what I believe is the biggest advantage to the new 5th Generation Kindle Fire, that being the ability to save files, photos, videos, music, etc...to an SD card, are simply wrong. Fortunately for me, I did not pay much attention to the nay sayers with respect to this feature. I suspected those negative reviews may have been from a certain percentage of people that do not know how to set up the device, or do some simple work arounds. And, I was right!With the new Kindle Fire 5th Generation, you can save apps, files, programs, etc...to an SD card, up to 128GB capacity. I have been successful saving all kinds of files to a 64GB SD Card, which is the SD card capacity I just happened to choose for my own needs...including videos, and full length movies..all properly converted to the format that is required for the device to display them, as well as Music, Photos, Word Documents, and anything that an SD card would normally display from files that are put on it, provided there are apps on the Kindle Fire that will read the files. If the device doesn't have the proper apps to read the files, of course they are not going to work.Now, the other REALLY AMAZING THING is this...I suspected that because this device can read an SD card, you could also plug a thumb drive (USB) into the power connection, and the device would read what was on the thumb drive. I have tested a 64GB thumb drive using a short pigtail lead that has the USB connection on one end, which a thumb drive plugs into, and a power connector on the other end, which plugs into the Kindle Fire. These pigtail leads can be found on Amazon.com or, better yet, through Ebay for little or nothing (ie: China). It works absolutely flawlessly!!!That means you can save an unlimited number of files to a thumb drive in whatever capacity thumb drive you want to use (probably up to 128GB, and maybe even more), and save files, music, videos, photos, etc...to it. That means you can not only put up to a 128Gb SD card into the Kindle Fire, but you can also plug a thumb drive into the power connection of the Kindle Fire, and it will display any readable files you put on the thumb drive flawlessly, including videos, photos, and full length movies.The important thing is, you have to have apps on the Kindle Fire that can process the file formats that the you present to it, simple as that. These negative reviewers must be lacking the proper apps to process their files. If you don't have the proper apps to process the files you have, then of course it will not work. But, so far, I haven't found a file that the available apps won't process.The reviews that accuse the new 5th Generation of having a lesser number of apps available compared to what is available through Google Play Store is true. With the new 5th Generation, you are limited to the Amazon App Store, and the Kindle Fire is not set up to access Google Play Store. But, if the app you want is not available through Amazon App Store, then there are many work arounds to this by utilizing an apps that are "APK" downloaders. I have not tested whether an app obtained via APK downloader will successfully load to the card, but I will update this review once I've established that the apps obtained via APK downloaders are either movable to the SD card, or whether they automatically install to the SD card. Some apps are not compatible, and will only install on the internal memory of the Kindle Fire, and can not be moved to the SD card from within your Kindle Fire App Settings. With respect to those apps that can't be moved over to the SD from "settings," you have to monitor how much internal memory you're consuming with said apps that are not compatible, and regulate how many incompatible apps you install on the device because it only has about 5Gb of internal memory. If you begin to use up internal memory, you'll find that apps like your "Wifi" app will begin to display memory warnings, and possibly not function properly if they don't have sufficient memory available to operate. So, in order to get the full benefit of the SD card, try to install or move all the apps, data, and files that you can move, over to the SD card, in order to preserve as much internal memory as possible. I currently have about 3.66Gb of memory left in my internal memory, but I have no less then 56Gb of apps, videos, movies, music, photos, etc...installed on the SD card, and the Kindle Fire works flawlessly with those SD apps and files.To do what I want to do with Kindle Fire, which is mostly loading movies and tv programs onto it, along with books, pdf files, word documents, etc...I have not found the issue of not being able to access Google Play Store a detractor in the least bit. I use ES File to manage all kinds of files. I have downloaded apps directly from their websites when they are not available in Amazon App Store, and loaded them direclty onto the Kindle Fire. Apps obtained via the internet, provided the apps are Android based applications, have all worked perfectly when not obtained through the Amazon App Store. Just remember that if the apps you're trying to use are not for Android devices, then the apps will not work on the Kindle Fire.My conclusion is that this is the best Kindle Fire yet, and with the added storage capacity, and the ability to not only read SD cards with a capacity of up to 128GBs, but the ability to also have files simultaneously avaiable through an external USB drive (thumb drive), plugged into the power connection on the Kindle Fire, the sky is the limit.As far as the resolution of the video files and movies, I have found no difference between the resolution of movies and videos played through my older Kindle Fire HD, versus the new 5th Generation Kindle Fire. If I were to have to select between the Kindle Fire HD that is several years old, and the new 5th Generation Kindle Fire, I would chose the new 5th Generation Kindle Fire over the older one any day.The new 5th Generation will read both the SD card, and a thumb drive simultaenously through the App "ES File." So, all the files you have on the SD card, as well as any files you have on a thumb drive, are all simultaneously accessible, and can be viewed and selected at will. With all that memory capacity, you're not going to need to dismount and remove your SD card often, which is always necessary if you want to load data onto the SD card from your PC. I am totally and completely pleased with the new 5th Generation Kindle Fire. Don't believe the negative reviews...just as I thankfully suspected, the negative reviews originate from those that just don't understand all nuances that are technically possible.

title: The Kindle Rocks!
body: We ordered two Kindles (one for myself and one for my wife) the day that we first read about them. That was back in the second week of December, and they just arrived yesterday (note: we weren't charged for them until they actually shipped).While we've only had them a short time, the bottom line is that we love them! For people like my wife and I, who are voracious readers and heavily into technology, the Kindle is really an awesome solution.The Cons (bad news first):- The price. Because my wife and I read so much, and also because my wife loves to buy new release hardbacks (which are NOT cheap these days), the economics of the Kindle work for us. I believe that it would also be a great investment for students and professionals who can get their reference materials loaded onto the Kindle and take advantage of its search and note-taking features. But it's currently priced beyond what most casual readers (or folks who simply don't have almost $400 to spend) would be willing to pay.- The included cover.
