This is a quibble in the great scheme of things, but I think that they could have done a MUCH better job designing the cover that comes with the Kindle. I personally am so used to holding an open book in my left hand that the most comfortable way for me to hold the Kindle is in the cover. But the cover only holds the Kindle by the left (spine) side, and not firmly enough, in my opinion. They should've had some sort of binding or elastic on the right side corners.The Pros:- The ScreenThe screen is very easy to read except in low light, just as with a "real" book. The ability to change font sizes is very welcome: I'm nearsighted and wear glasses, and I've found that I can hold the unit closer and read with a smaller font, but I can also increase the font size and prop it up on the table when I need my hands for other things (like petting our Siberian cat, Sasha!).The screen refresh when you change pages seems to take roughly half a second (about the time you'd take to turn a page in a paperback, I suppose). There is indeed a dark flash as the electronic ink redraws, but this doesn't bother me - again, I believe this is a matter of personal preference and getting used to it. I've read some reviews where people complained about screen redraws that took more than a second or two: I noticed this also, but only when the Kindle was fetching graphics from items listed in the Kindle store, *not* when paging through content that had already been loaded onto the device. That's really a bandwidth issue more than anything else, and it's not something that bothers me, since the main content I'm interested in are the words in the descriptions, not the thumbnail images (which, by the way, are rendered a lot more sharply than I had expected).- The ButtonsWhile I know a number of folks have complained about the size and position of the buttons, I don't really have any complaints. I think this is largely going to be a matter of personal preference and just getting used to the device. But for me it felt pretty natural.- FunctionalityGranted, I consider myself pretty computer savvy (as is my wife), but to get going with the Kindle is easy as pie. It has an easy to follow section to get you started, and once you get used to the navigation/selection bar with its thumbwheel, using the Kindle is quite intuitive.- The Kindle StoreThis is the main thing that separates the Kindle from other ebook readers. While there are many popular titles that aren't yet available for Kindle, many are - and more are added every day. And one of the greatest features is that you can download a sample to read for free to see if you like the book (or newspaper, magazine, etc., which also have free trials, I believe). That's what I spent most of last night doing: just downloading free samples of the books I was interested in. And the great thing is that these aren't just token samples of a paragraph or two: you get a decent chunk of the first part of the book (one or two chapters) to get a feel for the story and if you like the author, if you hadn't read anything by them before. And the store even has a feature that asks if you bought something by accident, giving you the option to say "D'oh Didn't mean to do that!"Another thing I like and will spend a lot of time exploring is that Kindle really opens up the world to new authors who haven't been able to score with the big publishing houses. There's still the dilemma of marketing and actually letting people know that your book is there for them to read, but at least they have a way to get it on the street other than so-called "vanity" publishers. I look at it this way: a lot of great books that we know were turned down any number of times before a publisher woke up and snapped up the story, and it makes me wonder how many other excellent authors are out there with works we can't enjoy just because they haven't made it through to a publisher (and publishers can only print so many books in a year).- Digital Rights Management (DRM)First, I don't have any worries or concerns about DRM from Amazon. Some folks are worried that there will be changes in technology, formats, etc. - and that will no doubt come to pass. But I believe that Amazon will take steps to ensure that the data I've purchased will be converted or upgraded so I can still use it.Second, a lot of folks are griping about DRM because they essentially want to own the rights to what they buy; in other words, they feel that if they buy a book for their Kindle, they should be able to share it with whomever they please.I'm sorry, folks, but I have to side with the publishers on this one. Having created copyrighted content (instructional DVDs) myself, I have a much better appreciation of the publisher's position than I once did. Let's face it: when you buy a book, you're not paying for the paper the book's printed on, you're really buying the author's words. And every time you share that book without the author getting a royalty, you're denying that individual (as well as the publisher) part of the income they should be getting for their talent and effort and, in the case of the publisher, the expense of actually producing the book.But with DRM, the author and publisher's interests are rightly protected. This also has the beneficial effect to the buyer of - in many cases - reducing the cost of buying the book. Look at what's in the store: many of the new release books there are only $9.99, when the hardback price is $20 or $30.Finally, I suspect that at some point down the road someone will figure out how to *legitimately* buy and sell "used" electronic media protected by DRM. So maybe in the future folks who want to buy new books can do that, but they would also have the option of buying "used" electronic books at a reduced price: they get to save some cash, while the publisher, author, and retailer all get their cut.- Being GreenOver the last couple of years, I've gotten much "greener" than I used to be. We've changed all our bulbs to compact fluorescents; we recycle everything we can; we compost our kitchen scraps; we buy our electricity from a regional wind farm, etc.And the Kindle, I believe, is very green. Yes, there is the production cost in terms of carbon, pollutants, etc. But, particularly for folks who like to read a lot, it can save hundreds of pounds of books - and the trees, processing chemicals, fuel, etc. used to make them - over its lifetime.- Final ThoughtsI have a lot yet to explore on the Kindle, but so far I'm very happy with the purchase (remember, we bought two!). Again, this is probably not something the casual reader is going to want to spend $399 on, but if you read tons of books or need to be able to carry a lot of books around with you (and/or need search and indexing features), I strongly recommend giving the Kindle a try!

title: Another class act from Amazon
body: And why am I so surprised? This is an excellent e-reader and I'm amazed I hadn't purchased it earlier. Shame on me for allowing myself to be dissuaded by so many negative reviews - at least until I realized that most were being written by people who have never tried the device and many who have never even used an electronic reader. I admit that past experience with Amazon's customer service and returns policy took some of the fear out of actually taking the plunge on the Kindle. I have been an ardent (rabid?) customer for at least a decade now and have never found fault with their customer service even when I tried.So, first, a little background from an avowed abibliophobe: I have been an ebook reader for close to a decade now and have used most, if not all of the ereaders out there: PC-based, Apple-based, and Palm-based as well as a number of now-extinct little dinosaurs few would remember at this point. So yes, I've tried (and still use) other devices and formats, and yes, I've blown a king's ransom to support my reading habit. That's me, your mileage may vary, but that's the background I'm reviewing from.First out of the box: Everything needed for an enjoyable read is right there. I emptied the box (and very nice packaging it is too - now proudly displayed on my bookshelf, holding pamplets and manuels that would otherwise be an eye-sore) and turned the machine on. Within minutes the device had located my account ID and downloaded the assortment of samples, blogs, magazines, newspapers and pre-purchased books I had collected at Amazon for my trial run. No glitches, no hic-ups, no problems. Worth the price of admission, at least. If only life in general were so smooth.The device is easy to navigate and surprisingly intuitive. Perhaps someone with less experience with other devices might be a bit lost, but I doubt it. And even for those who haven't looked over the user manual before ordering, the first thing that opens is - wait for it - the user manual! All questions anticipated and answered quite clearly and, again, easily navigated.Now, to brass tacks. The Kindle's "electronic paper" is a revelation. I'm a devout Palm-device user with literally a couple thousand books on my Treo 700p (and on the 8th day God created SD cards) and have played with settings for text-size, screen-colors, etc, trying to find comfortable settings for my tired late-forty-ish eyes (which have been myopic since fifth grade). My job is 45+ hours a week in front of a computer screen which, naturally, doesn't help my vision problems. The Kindle, however, is extremely easy on the eyes and I find I can read much longer with no eye-strain than I have in quite some time. There is, as many have pointed out, no backlight. I am used to reading in bed, lights out, using the backlight on my Treo and, honestly, I anticipated that this lack of backlighting would be a deal-breaker for me. Again, the lack of eye-strain I've experienced with the Kindle's electronic paper concept has made me re-think my attitude. A decent booklight, and I'm set. So far the only real conveniences I miss are the Palm's automatic scroll setting and the auto-off function (in case I happen to actually fall asleep while reading). Perhaps Amazon can work up some type of automatic page turning function (wow, how lazy is that!) and an auto-off or auto-sleep setting that can be user-defined.On to other features: the Basic Web is very nice, allowing the user to enter their own URLs as well as providing a decent assortment of sites already quickly accessible. The addition of the Wikapedia link is much appreciated. It's been a part of my Opera Speed Dial for a while. The on-board dictionary is fantastic. I paid wa-a-ay to much for similar access on my Palm' Add in the out-of-the-box links for Amazon (natch), Google, weather, various news sources (BBC, MSNBC, Tech, Y! Financial and Y! Entertainment), movie times and the yellow pages, and you're pretty well covered.Oh, and did I mention that this web access it FREE? I didn't, did I? Well, let me say it again. It's FREE. Yes, the device is $400 bucks, but it includes FREE web access. In a heartbeat. With no monthly access fees. And it works three times FASTER than my overpriced cell phone service. For FREE. No if, and's, or but's, no $15 bucks worth of additional "taxes" and "access fees." FREE.You did get that part was FREE, right? O.K. then, on to other good stuff:Audible works just as easily on this device as everything else does. The Audible download manager recognized the Kindle and transferred without a hitch. Volume is good both with and without headphones and the words are clear even to these aging ears. The same is true of the music function. True, the Kindle only plays on random which could be a pain with mp3 audio books but don't we all have mp3 players by this point? Really.I wound up answering one of my own early questions about the Kindle and will share with those still awaiting an answer. The Kindle DOES read non-Amazon books, as long as they are mobi format. Of course, via their email transfer service, Amazon will reformat a number of other formats and send them to the Kindle at a very nominal charge - this is explained in the manual. But the multi-format books I have purchased, say from Fictionwise, work perfectly on the Kindle. I use the same credit card for Amazon that I use for Fictionwise, which probably helps (you can, of course, change your credit card settings even on previously purchased books in your account functions at Fictionwise). Just be sure to download, or re-download the books in mobi format. Neither Secure Mobi nor Palm E-Reader formats will work on the Kindle and I assume the same is true of Microsoft Reader and Adobe e-Reader, but you can't have everything and I don't intend on tossing out my Treo any time soon, anyway. I put my mobi format (.prc) books on an SD card and poked that into the slot on my Kindle and the Kindle immediately added them to my book list. Surprisingly, they look better on the Kindle that they ever did in Palm Reader Pro. Perhaps the luxury of actual screen space helps in that area.The leather cover is nice and seems to do a good job protecting the screen while still managing to fold back comfortably and stay out of the way while reading. The "sleep" function is nice and once activated the user is rewarded with various images of authors and interesting inanimate objects and even the occasional reminder of other functions and features. A number of images have actually brought a smile to my face which in my world is always a pleasant and welcome surprise.I've found the email reformat service handy for carrying numerous documents ready for easy access: not-so-frequently called phone numbers and addresses, various reference items I normally relied on my Treo for. Also, as a rabid under-liner and note-taker, the note function and "save page as clipping" function are quite satisfying, especially when both will save to a PC or Mac and be available for editing and reworking. The Kindle's battery-life is excellent and the fact that the battery is actually replaceable is very nice considering the number of Palms and ereaders I've collected which are now little more than expensive bricks since the batteries fried out. The keyboard is easy to use (far easier than my Treo which even with small hands I have to type carefully on) as is the navigation wheel/button.I've shown the Kindle around work to folks who use all manner of technical gadgets and some who just don't see the point of gadgets beyond a cell phone, especially just to read a book or newspaper. All have found something to be impressed with in the Kindle even if just on an aesthetic level.All in all, I must say I'm most pleased with my Christmas gift to myself. Amazon obviously put a great deal of thought into this thing and it is much appreciated. At least by this customer.

title: No, Seriously, 5 Stars!
body: I was given a Fire as a Christmas gift. Mind you, this was one of those gifts I didn't have on my list, but as the holiday drew nearer I sort of hinted at it. Needless to say I was delighted when I unwrapped it, finding my wife didn't just get the hints, she anticipated them, and by several weeks at that!Now based on some of the wildly varying reviews I've read here I wasn't sure what to expect from this critter. And I found a few of them somewhat less than charitable. So here's my own unbiased summing-up, having used it for about a week now. For the record, I consider myself fairly well versed in technology…almost 25 years in IT using, programming, and repairing PCs, Macs and peripherals as well as mp3 players including an iPod touch and a third generation Kindle e-reader. I've also used my wife's iPad and my son's original Kindle Fire. I think have a good basis for comparison.To begin, for just a moment let's forget the price. Okay, it's only $50. Or rather, $35, my Wife The Eternal Bargain Hunter bought it while it was on sale. But--just for now--consider it for what it is ostensibly purposed: a budget tablet/Amazon Prime pathway device.SO, WHAT DOES IT FEEL LIKE? Well, it feels like a tablet, because it IS a tablet. In terms of tactile sensation, the backside of the case does have a bit of a slippery feel to it as opposed to my third generation Kindle, which almost feels rubbery. But the Fire is not cheap-feeling at all; it actually has a good, solid heft to it. But you're going to want a case for it probably and there are lots available. Hardware-wise this is brand new so we have no idea of longevity, but if the Fire lasts as well as it looks, well, I may own it a while.HOW DOES IT WORK AS A TABLET? Quite well, thank you. First of all, the screen is gorgeous. No, it's not a Retina a la the newer iPads, but then it's not supposed to be. It's still darned fine resolution from where I sit. I have no idea what advantage there is to an "IPS" screen, but whatever it is, it works, rendering bright and crisp images with vivid colors. I can Skype and email and check Facebook and play games. Probably I could vidcast too, I'm just not sure the camera is up to it. But otherwise, it does pretty much everything the iPod Touch does, only with a bigger screen. And then there's the access to Prime Music, but that's another matter.HOW FAST IS IT? The next consideration would be the speed of the processor(s). Without actual comparison testing, which doesn't interest me, I'd have to say It's pretty speedy. How fast? Fast enough for my needs, I suppose. I've been watching video (X-Files season 1) and playing games (Real Racing 3, which is fairly graphics-intensive) with no issues at all. Weather radar loads, displays, and refreshes equally well. Probably there are purposes for which the Fire would be too slow, but I haven't found them yet.HOW MUCH CAN I CRAM ONTO IT? Eight GB as a basic storage limitation…eh, not good, BUT…having expandable memory is KILLER. Up to 128 GB? Seriously? I had a 32 GB card in my phone that promptly got reused in the Kindle. Sweet.SO WHAT ABOUT THE SOUND? The single speaker is tiny but sounds okay for what it is. For listening to music or watching videos, put on some headphones or hook up a bluetooth unit. Yes, it does have bluetooth. And oh, that expandable memory...you potentially have GOBS of space for your favorite tunes...and if you care to tote a couple of extra SD cards around, why, you could possibly carry your whole music collection with you. It boggles the mind, it does.WHAT ABOUT THE CAMERAS? Ah, the cameras…well, they're a mixed bag.The front facing one you'll use for selfies and Skype is fine for those purposes; the one in the back (supposedly 2 MP) is near to worthless for indoor pix as near as I can tell as there's no flash. But I don't plan on taking anything but the occasional picture with this anyway. The samples I've seen of outdoor shots look decent, but again, I have a proper camera for that, thank you.WHAT'S WITH THESE "SPECIAL OFFERS"? Some folk have made a big deal about the "special offers" Amazon places on the lock screen, calling them intrusive ads. Your mileage may vary, but to me it's much ado about nothing. They're on the lock screen ONLY as near as I can tell and you'll hardly notice them, but if they're too much for you, why, pay the $15 to have them removed. Myself, I'd rather spend the money on content.WHAT'S THE OS LIKE, AND WHAT'S AVAILABLE APP-WISE? First things first. The Android-based "Bellini" OS is a vast improvement over the original Fire bookshelf and "carousel" approach. My son has one and it's not exactly intuitive. But "Bellini" IS Android for all intents and purposes. If you have an Android phone, you already know Bellini. True, it's missing Google Play, but Amazon's own app store is pretty well stocked, and those in "Amazon Underground" are what they call "really free", which would me to me that you don't even pay for the in-app purchases if you're a Prime member. Check it out. Myself, I'm not sure the lack of Google Play is that much of a dealbreaker; you CAN "sideload" it yourself if you're so inclined. The instructions are all over that Great Fount of Endless Wisdom, the Internet.HOW LONG DOES THE BATTERY LAST? I've heard 6 to 8 hours, depending on usage. That's comparable to most similar sized Android tablets and maybe a little less than an iPad Mini. The Fire comes with a 5W charger/USB cable, but you can get a 9W unit that will charge quicker if you like.I HEARD YOUTUBE DOESN'T PLAY WELL WITH KINDLE…TRUE? Yes and no. I'm not entirely certain this issue is necessarily confined to Kindles, for a start. There's no "official" Youtube app available on the Amazon apps store (Youtube is owned by the Google conglomerate…which also owns Android, so maybe that's understandable if weird.) Still, there are ad-based alternatives (like VideoTube) which will work, but better still, you can just use the built in Silk web browser to access YouTube directly. It WILL work, providing you make sure you're using the desktop version rather than the mobile version. Might this be fixed in the future? I don't know, but as of 12/30/15 this is the way I'm making it work. And speaking of Silk, it's not Firefox, but it's an adequate web browser.CAN I TRUST THE KIDS WITH ONE? Why not? You probably already give them access to your phone or tablet. Heck, they might even have their own. Amazon does offer a kids version of the Fire with a protective bumper, a two year guarantee, and a year's worth of FreeTime, which is Amazon's kid-centric app store. Check it out.DO I HAVE TO HAVE PRIME TO OWN ONE? No, but it definitely helps. I suppose you could load it up with apps and stuff in the free trial period Amazon gives you when you buy one, then just cancel...but seriously, Prime is a heckuva deal if you ask me. Free two day shipping? We probably paid for that with Christmas shopping. AND access to thousands of movies and TV shows? Prime Music? Free apps? And it all goes for $99 a year? Yes, please and thank you.So. That pretty much sums it all up. Now, taking all of the verbosity above into account, let's add back in the factor of price, and the final question:IS IT WORTH IT? For $50? Seriously? You'll spend that much on one night out. A trip to the movies. Myself, I can't see a downside, especially if you're a Prime user. If you're just committed to Apple's iOS, spend the ducats and get the iPad. Or spend the same amount of money (less, actually!) and get yourself FIVE Kindle Fires…at which point they throw a 6th one in for free. Free! A six pack of tablets? Is the 21st century great or what? Now then Amazon, where's my jetpack?

title: The Kindle is Worth Buying Now
body: (Please see the pictures I've posted to illustrate some of the comments I've made below.) [Edit: The pictures are on the Kindle "home" page below the video -- click on the "See Customer Images Link."]With more than 1300 reviews posted here (some of them from people who actually own the Kindle) I won't try to cover old ground in my review, but rather focus on some areas that might not have gotten special emphasisThe Kindle is smaller than it seems in pictures and so are the keys, although neither of these things is necessarily bad. I have small hands and fingers, so it fits me well. YMMV.Even from admirers, the cover has been described as cheap, flimsy, unworthy, etc. This was not my first impression. The outside is black leather and the inside is gray suede. An elastic strap secures the cover closed (and open.) It seems the Kindle was meant to be used with the cover in place. It's much easier to hold that way and the infamous "next page" button problem is much less of an issue.Text is much easier to read than on a backlit computer and, considering how one can vary the font size, easier to read than a book. I find the light gray text background helps rather than hinders, by lessening the amount of glare reflected back to one's eyes.I live in a "five bar" area, so connectivity is not an issue. Most documents come down in seconds. Nothing has taken a minute, although it takes a little longer to establish initial connectivity than I expected.I can read books much faster with the Kindle, perhaps because eyestrain is more easily controlled. In the morning I set the font to the next to smallest, but by late evening I've upped the size a couple of ticks. The Kindle is much lighter to hold than a book, so my hands don't get so tired.I like instant answers. On Christmas morning my son and I were discussing a passage from Huckleberry Finn. I downloaded a copy from Amazon (cost 25 cents) and we searched for the phrase which had us stumped. In just a few seconds we found the passage. Excellent!The Kindle is extremely easy to carry and pack. The cover is padded on the inside so that a soft pillow rests against the screen when it's closed (see picture.) I bought a 2GB card and slipped it in the back. The number of books I can carry is now somewhere north of 1000.While I'm not as dissatisfied as some with the cover, I did manage to damage it permanently. If you'll look at the picture I posted above you'll see the small plastic tab which is supposed to catch the shallow notch in the back of the Kindle. This tab is actually glued on. The glue came unstuck on mine, which meant the tab fell off and could not be replaced. Since the tab didn't work all that well anyway, I attached a couple of Velcro strips which hold the cover firmly in place. By unhooking the upper leather hook I can easily reach the back switches -- more easily than I could when the tab was in place. I strongly recommend the Velcro solution to those of you who are having a problem with the cover. I used heavy duty Velcro, but a lighter weight might also work.When I contacted Amazon the day after Christmas to report the tab breaking off the cover I used the option to have a representative call me back. I was amazed to have the phone ring within seconds. I was quickly speaking with a person who used English as a primary language and two days later I had a replacement cover at no charge. Great job, Amazon. Recognizing the importance of good (and intelligible) customer service is a major step forward. It makes me feel that the price of the Kindle is at least subsidizing quality in areas that consumers seldom think of -- until they need it, of course.The first book I read was Orwell's 1984, which I had not read since college. For only $3.95, I figured it was worth another look. And here's where I found a problem not mentioned by any other reviewers, although I admit I have not read them all. Some books are optically scanned, then optical character recognition, "OCR", is used to convert the scanned page into text. OCR is good, but even when it's 99.999% accurate, errors creep in. Here are some examples of annoying errors: Light becomes fight; the becomes die (most common error); him becomes bun; dirty becomes duty, etc. In only one place did enough errors occur to make the sentence unintelligible, but the point is someone should be proofreading this scanned text.Also in 1984 the chapter titles were messed up. Since you cannot access the table of contents in this book, it's not terribly important, but it's still disconcerting to go from Chapter 4 to Chapter 7, then finish the book back at Chapter 6. None of the text is out of order, just the chapter titles. Again, this may be a result of bad OCR.Some suggestions for the first firmware upgrade:1. The dictionary is excellent, but it wastes one's time looking up obvious words along with the more difficult. Example (from Orwell's 1984): " . . .money nowadays. He even had a job, a sinecure." Ok, I want to look up "sinecure" and I get the definition, but the dictionary also takes time to look up most of the words on the line, including "money", "nowadays", "even", and "job". Even then, the word I'm looking for, "sinecure", is pushed to the second page of definitions, requiring another page turn. The dictionary feature needs to be smarter. If I don't already know the definition of "money" buying a Kindle will remind me. ;-)2. For those who find the large "next page" button too easy to hit, why not allow it to be disabled in the settings menu, or require a double click for a page turn? (I don't really find this an issue with the Kindle in the cover, but a lot of people prefer to use it without the cover.)3. I would also like to see a privacy feature, so that anyone casually looking at your Kindle would not be privy to your reading list. It would be easy to allow password protection of your books.4. Formatting is odd. In some books it's left justified and "ragged right." In others it's fully justified, which, depending on the font, can leave some lines with oddly spaced words. This may be the way Amazon receives the titles from the publishers, but it's not quite as elegant as it could be. I sent two essays to Amazon via my Kindle email, and one formatted full justified and the other ragged right. Go figure.5. I know it's just experimental, but it takes too long to get to the MP3 player. Instead of so much "drilling down" in the menus from where one is reading, why not assign a keyboard button combination to toggle the music on/off? Again, this could probably be accomplished by a firmware upgrade.[Edit: Thanks to D. Stimson for pointing out that Alt-P will accomplish this.]If you got this far, thanks for sticking with me. I'm not always an early adopter, but the Kindle hooked me immediately. If I could award incremental ratings, I would give the Kindle a 4.5 star rating. But since I can't, I'll tilt to a full five stars. That should help make up for the ridiculous one-star reviews posted by several hundred "reviewers" who have never even seen a Kindle. In short, the Kindle will change my reading habits and, perhaps, save my eyesight. It's not quite perfect, but it's a great start for Amazon.

title: Revisiting the Kindle e-Book Reader
body: I confess to having a thing for new electronic gadgets and gizmos. I always have. But I'm pretty fickle about them as well. Gadgets that look cool and act cool, but are too much trouble to use, frustrate my simple mind and typically wind up cast off into a drawer, unused or, if I'm feeling particularly industrious, for sale on eBay.I reviewed the Kindle right out of the box when I got it, but now, after eight months of daily use, I thought it might be time to revisit the device. You know, honeymoon being over and all. So, without further ado:The FabulousInstant access to Amazon.com--Do not underestimate the importance of this feature. It makes owning the Kindle and browsing in the Amazon.com store an experience as satisfying as browsing the aisles of a brick-and-mortar Borders or Barnes and Noble. It also places the Kindle head and shoulders above the Sony Reader.Because the Kindle is wireless, access to the Amazon.com store is always available (and free): sitting in the pediatrician's waiting room, in the airport waiting to board your flight, sitting at the pool or lounging in bed wishing you had something good to read. You flick on the wireless switch, hit the button and you're in the store. Browse new releases, the best seller lists (several of them), or search for your favorite author (by typing on the Kindle's keypad) or genre. Read reviews, download a free sample chapter or two (instantly), or buy the book.There is no other reading device (as far as I know) that can do this. You can also access the Amazon.com store on your computer, buy the Kindle version of the book and, voila, when you turn on the device, your new book magically appears on your device's home page within a minute or two.You can also subscribe to newspapers like the New York Times. (The Kindle "whispernet" deposits the newspaper into your device like an invisible newsboy.) Magazines, including Time and The Atlantic, as well as e-magazines and blogs like Slate and--breaking news--BlogCritics(!) are also available for subscription.Book Price--The current best-sellers and most other hardcover books are $9.99. Paperbacks cost generally between $3.00 and $7.00. If you buy a lot of hardcover books (and I do), you are saving about $15.00 per book. Admittedly, you don't have a physical copy of the book, and for some of us, that's a blessing. The Barnett household is littered with books. We have approximately 2,000 volumes stashed and stacked in various bookcases (shelved two deep in places), on coffee tables, shelves nightstands and other places. The possibility of buying new books without adding to the clutter is (as they say in Yiddish) a mechaiyah. (Insert deep relieved sigh here.)Deleting a book from the Kindle library doesn't erase it, either. The book is simply stored back at Amazon.com for easy retrieval. Unlike storing a finished book in the dark reaches of my bedroom bookcase, I can actually find that biography of Thomas Jefferson when I absolutely must read it again.Selection and Variety--The Amazon.com Kindle store contains more than 130,000 books, and it's not just the best sellers and new releases. A couple of weeks ago, for example, I needed a particular book on Jewish prayer. I was out of town and needed the book for a lecture. I accessed the Kindle store on the device, and crossed my fingers (or to be more to the point, said a little prayer). And there it was. The Kindle store has a very comprehensive selection of all genres.Portability--For me, this is the reason for forking out $350 or more for any electronic reading device. I can carry a whole library of books with me wherever I go. And I do. I typically read one novel and two or three non-fiction books at a time. In hardcover. That's a lot of books to carry around on a daily basis (or especially when traveling).I also like to read at lunch, and to me there is no bigger reading buzz kill than trying to manage silverware and 750-page novel at the same time (especially when the novel is towards the beginning.) Keeping those pages weighted down with salt-shakers, ice-water tumblers, and other at-hand implements is neither easy nor relaxing. With the Kindle, I simply turn it on and prop it up. Pages are turned by pressing very large buttons located both on the left and right of the Kindle. Making it a wonderful lunchtime companion. It's also great to read in bed, for much the same reason. No more unwieldy and hefty tomes to balance while I'm propped up bed. (So sue me, I like really long novels!)Adjustable Type Size--This is a great feature for the middle-aged and elderly amongst us. You can bump up the font size incrementally--all the way up to "I-really-ought-to-get-reading-glasses" size.Keeps your place--Ever fall asleep and lose your place in a book? Forget to bookmark or dog-ear a page? The Kindle always saves your place. If you want to mark a page for reference, there's a cute little dog ear graphic in the top corner of each page. Place an electronic bookmark and the little "corner" turns down just like a real dog-eared page.Battery Life--Like most electronic readers using "e-Ink," the Kindle's real-time battery life is about two weeks or more. If, that is, you use the wireless feature only when needed. Flip it on to download your books or browse in the store and turn off when you're done. The device itself uses very, very little energy.Needs improvement:The Back Cover--Maybe it's just my personal device, but the back of my Kindle keeps coming loose. It's a nuisance, and it should snap in place more securely than it does. Without the included book-cover (which is great), I believe I may have lost the back of the device long ago.Buttons--Don't get me wrong; I love the big, accessible page-turning bars. But accessible location also makes them too easy to flip accidentally.Color--The Kindle would be extra cool if it had color ability. Maybe in its next life; I understand that Kindle 2.0 may not be too far off in the future!Page Numbers--I like to know what page I'm on. I just do. The Kindle tracks your reading progress by paragraph rather than by page. I do understand why, especially if you've bumped up the type size, you can flip page after page without seeming to have made progress. Although I've grown accustomed to this "new" way of judging my progress in the book, I really miss those page numbers.Blurb--Sometimes, especially after I've put down a book for awhile, I forget why I started reading it in the first place, and the back cover (or the blurb) synopsis helps remind me of what the book's about. It's something lacking on the Kindle, and something I greatly miss. It's a little thing, but (for me) it's the one thing that would really make Kindle books "real" for me. Silly, huh?Bottom line: It's still a totally worthwhile electronic gizmo. I rank it in importance after my laptop and my Blackberry (and actually ahead of my iPod - though just slightly).

title: "A letter concerning toleration to all the naysayers, idiots, fools, whiners, and elitist!"
body: Before I proceed on this elaborate rant let me first thank the following people:Melinda Varian,John Newton, Jonathan D. Crain, R. Kolin,P. Laney, Blue Goddess,Patrizia,Janice K.,Soularflair, Cluadia M. Adams, D. Hong, Tammy L. Pickering,P.M.H,Pastor Dan,T. Schiel,Gary Buck, Randy, Eclectic Reader, The Plothole,Legalsea, R. Nielsen.The reason that I have made it a point to single out the folks (if I didn't name you I apologize sincerely) above is because they have tirelessly responded to all the ridiculous comments that people seem to want to post about this device. There have been complaints such as the following.1. There's no way to share books. (This commemnt is blatantly false because up to six units on one account can share a libary)2. Drm is a killer. (Amazon is a predominantly an online bookstore without Drm there would be almost no way for them to earn a profit from the Kindle or it's books, which would mean that they would never build the device)3. There are no books for the device. (Again, false see #4 below)4. Amazon's selection is limited, and that is the only place that I can get books for this device. (If you actually believe that the previous statement is true, then you shouldn't be allowed anywhere near sharp objects). Here is a short list of all the free and/or low cost sites that together contain literaly close to 1,000,000 titles, which are in addition to the 130,000 books, newspapers ect. that are on Amazon.[...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...] [...]5. The next/previous buttons are a pain in the backside. (This can be true if you do not use the required cover included with the device,have suasages for fingers, or have never fingered anything delicately/carefully in your life i.e. a woman's reproductive organs)6.) The cover is flimsy. (This can be true, but their are those that have figured out how to use it by bending the clip to lock the device in place, others such as myself that use $1 or less piece of velcro to secure it in place, there are those that buy M. Edge covers, there are those that use the strap over one of the kindle's corners. All this meaning that it is a small inconvenience that can be easily dealt with.)7.) The internet browser on this thing sucks! (Here it's actually 50/50 becuase you are able to access sites that don't require video ect, but that means that you are often limited to news related sites. The golden rule applies here and that is that this is an Ebook reader not a laptop or iphone)8.) The wireless (evdo)that this device utilizes is horrible. (At first I had some minor problems with this; I live in NJ where there is a signal everywhere and was annoyed to no end by the problem. But once I figured out how to find strong spots in my area (which are everywhere!) I was downloading wirelessly and effortlessly in no time.)9.) The customer service here is unresponsive. (I found that the customer service was very kind, and responsive. That being said my unit arrived in normal working order so I can't knock those folks that have received bad units and are legitimate complainers).10.) Amazon will abandon this device and all its users. (In order to guage A-Z's commitment to this unit just take alook at their numbers as posted on Business Week website. Total gross sales: 10.7 billion dollars, Total net income: just .2 billion. Meaning that by doing things such as subsidizing shipping costs and gutting their prices Amazon was able to lose 10.5 billion dollars of potential profit. To Bezos and Amazon this device is a godsend 1 digitized book can be sold countless times without shipping costs, storage/labor fees, workplace shrinkage, and/or exposure to the natural elements resulting in damage/loss. At a publisher's conference Bezos stated that Kindle owners accounted for 6% of revenue for amazon, meaning that if we are say 50,000 strong we have accounted for $600+million in sales and that is just phenomenal.11.) This machine serves no practical purpose. (If you are so short sided that you can't see the benefit that this unit affords people that suffer from the following conditions then maybe you should switch places with them: the visually impaired, those with arthritis, the folks with nervous system disorders, and those that suffer from learning diabilities.)This unit is also a blessing to our men and women serving our government and business interests abroad by allowing them to download new books painlessly.12.) Their is no way to download when you don't have a signal. (Connect it to your computer and then click, drag, drop in to the kindle document folder,when downloading from different sources. From Amazon it's the same process, unless you just leave books on your device, or you can "one click buy" from the Amazon site in which case its automatically sent to your unit instantaneously as long as you are in the U.S. If you aren't it will wirelessly download when you get back within signal range.)13.) There is a block on how much you can clip/highlight. (From what I gather this is true, but ill tell you from my first hand experience that the bar must be high becuase I have yet to hit it and I am an avid highlighter/ clipper)14.) There are no folders to store content. (Yep this is true there isn't a folder setup, and that is a massive achilles heel becuase I can't organize my collection as I see fit. I would love to have a religion pile, an atheism pile ect.)15.) There is a second unit around the corner. (No one has said that this is the case, the major improvements such as flat and/or color e ink paper is still at least 2 years out, and this unit has an ability to upload updates (page numbers please!!) ala a p.c, or modern video game console meaning that they would tweak it before gambling on a second release).So let me leave you with this story in case you lack my sincerity. I love to read while on the toilet. Yes I have to keep myself entertained while I'm there. Well anyway I always drag a physical book and leave it in the bathroom, The spouse hates this habit like you wouldn't believe! So the other day I grabbed my kindle and used it to read while I took care of business. And while there I even purchased and received Gordon Ramsay's book! And that is the sweetest bit of irony that while i was releasing a brown turtle from captivity in to the wild, I was able to receive a new one in its place.ps. speaking of turds the Sony Prs 505 is an ok machine,but be warned you are not able to wirelessly download, there is no way to swap the battery, the sony bookstore only has around 20,000 titles, you can't highlight/clip, there are only three font sizes and sony as a company has no vested interest in having it suceed. remember betamax? but in the Prs's defense it has twice the gray scale, and i believe that there is a built in dictionary,alongside the ability to create some sort of folders.

title: Kindle will revolutinize the way we read
body: The Kindle will revolutionize the way we read, just like the iPod changed the way we buy and listen to music. I haven't bought a CD in several years; I download all my music and always have my library with me either on my laptop or on my iPod. I predict that the Kindle will do the same for books. I was one of the lucky (and smart) ones who bought a Kindle in the morning on the first day they were on sale. WHAT AN AMAZING DEVICE! I doubt if I will ever purchase a "paper" book again.The methodology and services:1. KUDOs to Amazon for the brilliant and well thought-out internet accessible process. It was registered and I had a dedicated page on the Kindle site from the moment the reader was purchased. I actually selected and downloaded several books before my Kindle had even been delivered. As soon as I turned it on when I did receive it, the purchased books were downloaded automatically within a couple of minutes. Having all my books on the Amazon server is great peace of mind if I should ever lose or break my Kindle. The books will always be there to download again.2. The ability to buy and download books no matter where I am is not a new concept, but the ease by which Amazon has made the process is nothing short of brilliant. And, adding newspapers, magazines and blog subscriptions really sweetens the pot! I travel a lot for business and being able to select and download books it minutes is going to make my traveling much more palatable. No more lugging around heavy books! And, the cost of ebooks on Amazon is MUCH less than any other ebook site. Hey, there are even classic books on sale for 25 cents!3. Integrating a dictionary, wikipedia, and the ability to add multiple bookmarks, highlight and add personal notes makes this device all the more valuable. AND, the highlighting/notes are tracked to the book copies on the Amazon server in your account.4. Audio books can also be purchased from audible.com or iTunes and can easily be downloaded to Kindle.5. While Basic Web is still in experimental mode and can be a little clunky on some websites, it is still amazing that Amazon is offering this internet access for FREE. It's great for a quick look-up, but not to surf for long periods of time. I can research an item when it is fresh in my mind and I don't have to go sit at a computer to do it.6. "Ask Kindle NowNow" is waaaay cool! You can ask ANYTHING, and you will receive 3 answers in 10 minutes! Amazon elves are available 24/7 just waiting to help you out.7. And, if all this isn't enough, Kindle can also play music while you read. It basically operates like an iPod shuffle; you can store music in memory or add an SD card. It won't replace my iPod anytime soon, but having the ability to add a few tunes for enjoyment is a nice touch!The Kindle device:PROs:1. The size is perfect and it weighs less than a pound. It's about the same size as a DVD case. Easy to handle, easy to stow in a purse or carry-on travel bag. Having a nice, white surface without distraction from what is being read is a plus.2. The new e-ink technology makes devices like the Kindle and the Sony eReader pioneers in the development of electronic readers. For the first time, ebooks can be read for hours without eye strain. E-ink is unbelievably amazing! It looks like you are reading PAPER. Absolutely no glare! Sooooo many early comments/ratings were so unfair and, frankly, stupid. For example, "The Kindle doesn't have a brightness control" or "It's bad because there is no back lighting." Obviously, these "informed" individuals should do their homework before they publicly make comments that embarrass themselves....brightness and back lighting are EXACTLY what was intentionally eliminated!!3. There are six different text sizes so that readers can adjust the text to what is comfortable for them.4. The scroll wheel/bar is incredibly easy to use. It automatically positions itself to the most obvious menu choices, such as "close" once you navigate to where you want to go.5. It charges very quickly and stays charged for days if you don't leave the wireless feature active.6. After a few minutes of no activity, there is an automatic screen saver with beautiful or uniquely interesting random pictures.7. The next page bars are on both sides of the screen, making them easy to access for both left and right-handed people. This is also convenient for different reading positions. I like to lay in bed and read. I no longer get aches in my arms from holding a book while I'm laying on my side. I prop up the Kindle with my comforter and only have to touch it to change the page.8. The keyboard buttons are laid out with enough space between them that it is easy to "type" words when on the web.9. Although everything you need to do to manage "reading" is on the Kindle, the added feature of connecting to a computer to transfer documents (for free) is very nice. And, you can email documents to your Kindle through Amazon for a mere 10 cents! And this feature allows you to send the document back to your computer for free.CONs:1. The off/on and wireless switches are on the back. When the cover is on (which is recommended for protection and screen safety) it's awkward to stick your finger in between the cover and the device to turn the switches on or off.2. While I like having the next page bars on both sides of the device for ease of use, it is also a challenge to handle the Kindle. Almost every time I pick it up while it's on, I accidentally hit the next page or previous page bars and then have to change the page back again. I'm learning to grab it with my hand reaching to the center of the screen or on top of the keyboard (which isn't active when in the middle of a book).3. There is no way to manage your "library" other than by title, author, or most recent read. I wonder what I'll do when I have a couple hundred books, magazines, or newspapers loaded. It would be nice if custom folders could be created. Otherwise, I'll eventually need to scroll through pages of titles to find what I want without having to resort to typing in a word search.IN SUMMARY, I've come up with 16 positives and only 3 negatives...which are definitely not "deal breakers." For a first generation device, I think Amazon should be applauded! Superb first release!A comment to all of the one-star whiners...the Kindle is a BOOK READER...it was not meant to get up and fly around the room and out-do computers, MP3 players, gaming devices, video players, cell phones or vacuum cleaners!And one more thing...it is worth every penny of the $399 price tag. If nothing else, it will save trips to the chiropractor for pulled back muscles from lugging around tons of books in a backpack!

title: Is It Worth It? Full Review of My Experience
body: First and foremost, I must stress: This is a $50 tablet. Anybody expecting crystal-clear, ultra hi-def images for $50 - brand new - is kidding themselves. I'm seeing a lot of reviews online that are complaining about the image quality, and again I must state - the thing only cost $50. What the heck do you want?On to the actual review:Initial Unboxing:Nicely packaged. Again I'm seeing complaints that it's "bare bones" packaging. I wouldn't call it that by any means, and it's certainly a much more eco-friendly, lower waste packaging. I don't want some elaborate, highly processed packaging on my device. I want the device, not the box.I did have to update the software right off the bat, which surprised me as this is brand new and released at the same time as Fire OS 5. Even still, the 15 minute process is worth it. I think had I been made aware of this before delivery, I wouldn't have minded at all. So there - you've been notified.Included accessories:A USB charger with cable.Hardware / Specs:MicroSD card slot! Thank you, Amazon! I've been considering a Fire tablet for years, but they kept making them without a MicroSD slot. I have no patience for mobile devices that don't offer this. There's no reason to not include this feature.The quad-core processor and 1GB RAM teamed up with the efficient Fire OS 5 is a good marriage. I've had no lag. I dare you to find a $50 tablet with specs this good.Tactile Impressions:It's nicely lightweight, thin, and well designed to fit comfortably in your hand in either portrait or landscape mode. The material for the back casing is a bit slick for my tastes, but I like to lay on my side in bed while reading, and really any device is apt to slip right out of your hands. I'm going to be picking up a case. If you're considering this tablet, add a case to your order. The tablet itself feels nice and solid, and I wouldn't classify it as "cheap" by any means. Amazon claims this tablet is super-durable. After getting my hands on it, I would call that a plausible claim, but I certainly won't be doing any sledgehammer tests on my own tablet. You'll have to decide for yourself if you believe the durability claims.Screen:Overall I would rate the display as not bad at all. "Not bad" is a lot different than "not good." It's good. it's not amazing, but it's perfectly good. I'm happy. The physical screen itself is nicely responsive, albeit a tad shinier than I would like (I also have this complaint with all of my devices). I will be picking up some low-glare screen protectors. It has a lower resolution than many other tablets available today, but it still does the job. The only reason I could see someone needing to spend hundreds of dollars more for a tablet is if they're maybe an interior designer or artist showing things to clients on their tablet. If you need a schmancy display, upgrade to one of the Fire HD models or look elsewhere.There's a nice brightness to it; my only complaint being that it doesn't get dark / dim enough for my tastes. I like to turn the brightness down all the way when I'm reading in bed so the screen light isn't keeping me up all night, and this could certainly get dimmer to suit my preferences. But, I'm still not being kept up all night.Sound:Single, mono speaker on the back. Works just fine for me. I don't understand people who expect theatre quality surround sound out of every device they own. That being said, if that's what you're looking for, this is not for you. The sound when using headphones is pretty nice, if not a tad bass heavy. But again, it does the job.OS:The Fire OS 5 "Bellini" is pretty slick, and based on Android Lollipop. No doubt you will want to spend a few minutes arranging your icons and whatnot, like we all do with new devices. I'm not a fan of the Silk Browser, which is not bad but I like being able to sync my bookmarks between devices and my desktop computer. I strongly recommend sideloading Mozilla Firefox for Android. That's what I've been using, and it works great on this particular Fire OS.I also spent about 30 minutes grabbing a few apps and sifting through the OS settings, changing things to my personal preferences as I'm very particular.Camera:I can't lie, the camera is not good. I've seen worse, but it's pretty low-res and certainly won't be your camera of choice. However, again I stress that this tablet was $50. I don't really care about the camera. My phone has a great camera. Who wants to wrangle a 7" device to take a picture anyway? If you're a video call person, you may prefer to use your phone or desktop computer. On the other side of the coin, at least for a budget-friendly tablet this has front and back cameras. I've seen loads that have no camera at all, which would be annoying.The Why Behind the Price:So why is it only $50? As you may notice when ordering it, you will be seeing advertisements on the lock screen. You can also pay an extra $15 to stop these ads. We debated it and decided to try the ad-based version first as you can always pay the extra bit later on if you change your mind. I was concerned about this as I really don't like ads - or most ads anyway. They're usually too invasive, loud, colorful, and often don't have anything to do with me and my interests.So far, I actually don't mind the ads. They really do only appear on the lock screen, are whisked away just as quickly as any lock screen image you may have chosen yourself, and do not interfere with my usability experience at all. Here's the real pleasant surprise: so far, I don't hate the ads themselves. The ads I have seen are all for Amazon content - as of yet - and are all based on my shopping history with Amazon. Design wise they have been fairly unobtrusive and simple. It's reminiscent of passing by a professionally designed poster at the bus stop or out in the shops.I imagine we will eventually pay to get rid of the ads, but for now I don't mind.Overall conclusion:I love this tablet! It does exactly what I want it to do, which is act as an e-reader plus a supplementary internet surfing device when I'm not on my computer and need something bigger than my phone. I do play occasional games on it, which work and look just fine, but I'm a PC gamer and quite frankly there is no mobile device that can do what my PC can do. Rather than spend $700 on one of the competition's tablets, I'll put that money into my desktop rig, thankyouverymuch.I cannot possibly see how you could find a better tablet - with this level of internal hardware specs - for this price point.

title: Excellent device, but not without its flaws
body: These guys are a bit pricey ($359.99 for the basic system), but notinordinately so. I've seen a few on e-bay, but decided to get mine fromAmazon. Kindles ship 2d business day, so it wasn't a long wait.The unit came in a box which was designed to resemble a book. Amazonprobably could have saved some bucks there since you'll likely not usethat box again. Included in the box were the kindle, the notebook-likecover, AC Power supply/charger, and the USB cableWhen the unit ships, it is registered to you and your Kindle account (withe-mail addresses) is set up.Pull the plastic strip which masks the battery, plug it in to chargeand wait a couple of hours. Two hours does a full charge and that'sgood for 7 days without Whispernet or 2 days with. I think the bestbet is to leave the Whispernet interface off except when you need it.I also ordered accessories for my Kindle. First was an 8 gb SD card.Amazon says the unit handles 4 gb cards, but my 8gb card works fine andthere are folks who report that 16 gb works fine. The other accessorywas a clip on LED reading light. The Kindle is *not* backlit, so thelight could come in handy.Reading the Kindle is pretty much like reading a book, except that theKindle is lighter. There are two buttons for next Page and one forprevious page. You select the item to read or a navigation menu usinga scroll button just below the window. When you re-open a document,the Kindle returns to the place at which you stopped reading.The unit can hold quite a bit of material. I can put my 1300 volume e-library on my Kindle and still have 7 gb to spare. You can also storeMP3 files in the music folder and play them as you read. Generally, Imove my books to a network drive at home and mainly keep music in that8gb SD card. When I read, I plug in the headphones and press Alt-P tostart the music.A few Pros and Cons:Pro - holds lots of material, more than you want to store on it.Con - the home menu shows *all* of the books on the unit which are inKindle (.azw), mobi (.prc) and Text (.txt) format. Well, those whichhave those extensions. If you have a large number of texts stored onthe Kindle, it is cumbersome to manage. The display ignoresdirectory/folder organization and just shows all the appropriate files.Pro - plays MP3 files. Using a headphone, the sound is pretty clear.With the 8gb card in place, I have 400 songs sitting in place.Con - Through the built in speaker, the sound quality is not so good.Con - The headphone jack is at the bottom of the unit, next to thepower and usb ports. If you are laid back a bit and are holding thekindle on your body rather than holding it up, the headphone jack is inthe wrong place and the plug is going to see unnecessary wear and tear.Con - No play lists and no way to skip or select a song. The unitsimply plays the songs at random.Con - Why didn't Amazon list the keyboard shortcut to play music (Alt-P)or the shortcut to skip a song (Alt-T)?Pro - paging through a document is convenient with buttons on both sidesCon - The right side button is too easy to accidentally trigger.Con - rapidly moving through a text without a bookmark can be a nuisance.Pro - the notebook-like cover protects the Kindle and contains a tabwhich latches the Kindle in place.Con - the on/off switches for Whispernet and Power are on the back of theunit. You have to put your hand between the cover and the Kindle tooperate those switches. This can cause the unit to pop loose from thecover. Then, when you're putting the unit back in place (really asimple operation) that right-side "next page" button is exactly whereyou need to grab the Kindle. You can move quite a few pages before yourealize you did that. I've found the placement of the on/off switches tobe a real irritant.Pro - the screen is easy to read with black text on a light, almostwhite background. You can vary text size for easier reading or, if youwish, more text on the page.Pro/con depending on the user - the screen flashes a bit when you flippages. It was annoying at first, until I realized that it was a goodthing to have a visual cue that the page had changed.Pro - Easy file conversion.Con - Content Manager is a little cumbersome - no surprise on such a unit.To really move files about easily, hook up the Kindle to your PC and usethe PC's file management utilities to move and delete files.Pro - Although this is not documented, you can get a sort-of GPS when using Google Maps. It seems to be based on cell towers rather than the real GPS system, so don't expect pinpoint accuracy - especially if there are tall buildings around.Pro/Con - Although you can load pictures and even do a slide show, that ability is undocumented. Basically, you dump the files into a folder, do an Alt-Z, and then open the folder as you would a book. Rendering of photos is pretty much the quality you would expect of a low-res B/W screen.There are quite a few texts out there (Amazon says more than 140,000 inKindle format). You usually pay $9.99 for a book at Amazon. This canbe a deal if the alternative is the hardback (29.95) version of thebook. You also get immediate delivery. Some individual stories arelisted - usually for a dollar or so. I suspect that the number ofindividual stories inflates the total count of titles. You can read asample of the book without obligation to buy and then buy with a quickclick if you like what you saw.There are some free e-text sites, such as University of Pennsylvania,U of Virginia, Baen Books, Gutenberg, and Gutenberg Australia. Orwellfans can get all of his books at Gutenberg.au. Quite a few of thosebooks on the "must read" lists are available at free sites.Now, having said all that, I guess I should answer the question, "wasit a good value and would you buy it if you knew then what you knownow?" Yes, in a heartbeat. I think it's a big plus for me. I'vereplaced several hundred dead tree editions with e-books - saving space,and getting money for the books at the local used book store and foundthe Kindle pretty easy to use and convenient to tote about.

title: Bang for the Buck!
body: I absolutely love the Fire 7" tablet! I gave it 5 stars because I have been using it every single day since I've gotten it (even though I also have a Google Nexus 7) in order to put it through its paces in order to write a decent enough review for everyone else on here looking to buy this. I am going to be blunt and honest with this review, but as a consumer, you also have to understand that what you're paying for is what you get, which I personally think is great for the money!First off, performance: I have installed apps and games on my device, even watched movies via Prime Video and it has done a great job! I am a huge Hearthstone player and there have been times where it's lagged a bit, but honestly, I get that on my Google Nexus 7 at times and I can't tell if it's a hardware issue or software issue in terms of the game itself, I personally think it's a bit of both. You have to understand though that you are getting what you paid for and the "lag" that occurs is very rare and far in between, it doesn't happen with enough frequency that it would "ruin" the experience, plus it typically only happens around starting up the game for the first time, which is also to be expected with most games on most devices unless you're packing some serious heat under the hood.I will add battery life to the performance section as well; when I unboxed the Fire for the first time, it was somewhere around 70% battery and I had to update the software and all of the typical stuff when first setting up any device. When all was said and done I think it went down about 7% battery if I remember correctly, which was very impressive! I had used it throughout the day and night without charging it and didn't have to until the next day before I went to work. The battery life is killer on this little thing and obviously it'll vary depending on your use of it as to how much you get out of it, but you can watch several movies in one charge (I did so last night via Prime VIdeo, which is also taking into account streaming the movie rather than playing it locally off the device), or you can just use it for light application uses like checking email and what not and get a very long use out of it before having to charge it again, but in comparison to the Nexus 7 it lasts just as long, if not longer because of the smaller CPU. Any time I've had it plugged in and been using it, it's charge fast as well. I'm using an AmazonBasics USB cable I had previously purchased which I believe is a 2.1amp, maybe 2.4, I forget the exact number. Side note: I just went to the page for the product and saw a lot of really negative reviews, but I've been using this cable for over a year now and have zero issues with it and it's on carpet as well, so anyone claiming fire hazard, etc. I'm not sure what their condition was, but have had zero issues and it also supports fast charging, unlike some people who claim that it charges slow; not true.Next, the display: Once again, what you pay for is what you get. During every day use, it's hard to really tell the difference when reading text on sites or typing a message, but going back to Hearthstone as an example, I can tell that the pixel density is lower than my Nexus 7 and quite obviously my computer monitor, but that's for very obvious reasons. The text on the cards is legible, but not as crisp as it is on my other devices. However, being realistic, for $50, the screen I believe is great for the price and when making the purchase and reading the pixel density, I knew what I was purchasing. The screen brightness is on par with about every other adjustable screen you own, I have had no issues with its light source at high or low brightness, though I have not had the opportunity to use it outside yet, I am sure it suffers from the same screen glare as all other glass-faced prodcuts (phone and tablet, apart from e-ink devices).Lastly, the UI (user interface for those who don't know): This is my first time using an Amazon electronic product, apart from an e-ink Kindle. I have owned various Apple and Android products so it's very interesting to compare the 3 OS's. On the Home screen, you can find all of your apps, where you would typically have to open an app folder on Android to display them all, it is more comparable to Apple's approach of having all of your apps on multiple screens, however rather than swiping left and right as you would to navigate iOS, you swipe up and down to navigate the screens on the Fire. When navigating left and right you begin to discover specific tabs that contain detailed pages to display the books, videos, and music you own, either through Amazon or locally on your device. I am pretty keen to that approach; it makes the device very media focused which is typically different than your other devices which are used for a myriad of applications and uses throughout your day-to-day life. It's also worth mentioning that you can expand the memory up to 128GB via a microSD card, so whereas you would generally have to pay more to the manufacturer for more memory, you can swap out your own SD cards for expandable memory and media (really nice for taking pictures with your camera, gopro, etc. then popping out the microSD card and inserting it into your Fire tablet to then view the media on a larger platform).I tried to hit as many points as I could to inform prospective buyers as best as possible. One last thing, the device itself feels nice in your hand and it's worth mentioning that ALL ports and physical buttons are located at the top of the device, so headphone jack, volume rocker, charging port, and power button are all on the top of the device (when it portrait mode), but based on your orientation, the volume rocker will adjust which direction is up and down for the volume, if that makes sense, versus your phone which typically has a dedicated UP and DOWN volume button regardless of orientation. The only port that is on the side of the device is the microSD port, otherwise everything else is located on the top.Friendly reminder when purchasing this product. Expect to get what you're paying for. I am a huge techy and I love this device for what it is, it hasn't disappointed me at all and I highly recommend this for anyone getting into tablets or looking to buy one for a teenager, young child to keep occupied, or just looking to buy a few "in bulk" for the family, presents, or maybe even a small workforce if you're able to publish an app to run on all devices. You can't get a better $50 tablet in my opinion and would highly recommend this to anyone interested!

title: You people helped me so much in making my decision as YOU REALLY GO THE EXTRA MILE in helping people like me come to a very vita
body: 5 Stars so far!This is my FIRST ELECTRONIC DEVICE since my computer died on me approx 8 yrs ago. I'm not tech savvy, therefore, I did a lot of research before making my purchase. I have to THANK ALL THE DEVOTED AMAZON CUSTOMERS for reviewing products so thoroughly!!! You people helped me so much in making my decision as YOU REALLY GO THE EXTRA MILE in helping people like me come to a very vital decision because anything too complicated for my first device would've put me off electronics forever and I'm 50yrs old so it's VITAL that I get in line with 21st Century.For the first 6 days my Fire and I just looked at each other but I'm happy to say we're at the Hand Holding stage because I finally turned it on...then got it hooked up to WiFi and registered. As I'm dealing w/serious health issues and fatigue combined w/lack of concentration I wasn't sure if I was up to getting the process started. I read on some reviews that it took 15-20min to register so that's the reason it took me 6 days to finally do the task, which was no task at all. I don't know what all the fuss was about since all I had to do was push 'Register' and THAT WAS IT!!! I didn't have to fill out ANYTHING! This beautiful little guy already knew my name, email address, EVERYTHING! Thank you, Amazon!!! You took ALL the work out of it for me.Also, it had my Amazon Acct synched with my Tablet so my Audible Book that had been previously purchased was already on Tablet when I hit the Audible App!!! Amazing! All I had to do was press download so that I could save it to listen to 'offline', without internet connection in the future.Limited time prevented me from doing as much as I wanted w/regard to familiarizing myself w/Tablet further but the little that I did was amazingly easy!!!The one thing I noticed though, is that the battery life wasn't what I expected. Using internet the battery went from 100% to 58% within a matter of 2.5hrs and I didn't have screen at full brightness nor was I listening to books, music, etc, nor watching any videos. I will have to investigate further.As for the camera and speaker that most people said was not in line with higher end products... I have absolutely NO COMPLAINTS, well 2 very minor, and in fact, after reading the reviews on camera and speaker I expected very little but knew most reviews cautioned they weren't of the best quality. As I've never ownrd anything other than an Android Smartphone... I can tell you that I am MORE THAN PLEASED with the speaker/sound before headphones are plugged in and VERY HAPPY with Camera and photo quality. I don't know... Maybe I just lucked out but when you have reasonable expectations that are in line with the price of this product then I'm sure you will be PLEASANTLY SURPRISED!!!The battery drainage is the issue so far and I'm finding the touch screen is also not as responsive as I would expect a touch screen to be as I often have to touch a few times or press much harder than you would expect in order for this product to respond.The other thingvis getting used to the keyboard as it seems to be very strange when it comes to input. If you're off a millimeter then it will type a completely different letter or add a space which is frustrating but again, perhaps it takes some getting use to. All in all it seems very sturdy as far as materials in physical make up.If this will be your first electronic device or you're on a limited budget or you want this for your child then I highly recommend as so far it's extremely easy to use, not intimidating at all and makes the whole experience very enjoyable.I would suggest NOT BUYING the SD Card by Sansdisk that Amazon suggests as it's apparently not the correct type for certain types of Video. I purchased a different card after reading a extremely helpful review by a customer for this particular device. I bought the 128g Micro SD Memory Card, important to note is SPEED on card as it helps with video...who knew!?! Thank you to the reviewer whom includes photos of cards and explained how important these things are as SD Cards vary in performance based on needs/speed which it turns out is VITAL for this Tablet. You can look at photos posted here, he posted about difference in memory card and description of importance. I took his advice and ordered from the same company and low and behold the memory card was $5 cheaper than the lower end Sansdisk. Take his advice!I found it... Buy Amplim (company), Ultra-Fast 533x (80MB/S). I'm sure the speed comes in all storage types, I purchased the most storage at 128gb Micro SD Card.vProduct is cheaper and much higher quality or else you may not be able to watch HD movies etc. I'm grateful to learn there's a difference because w/out his review I would've never known the difference and I hope this helps others out there.THANK YOU to ALL the LOYAL ANAZON CUSTOMERS whom take the time to review in detail and share their review and knowledge with the rest of us whom are new to these things!!! Your help has been ABSOLUTELY INVALUABLE TO ME ON THIS PRODUCT, and as always others!!! Many people with High Tech Knowledge, whom also own Higher Tech Devices, gave all the very detailed info on this product in order to help in choosing to buy this product and I'm so grateful you take the time to include, in layman's terms, very elementary and easy information as to help inform those of us whom have no understanding otherwise. Amazon Customers ARE GREAT!!!And of course, THANK YOU AMAZON FOR SUCH A WONDERFUL, STURDY, AND EASY PRODUCT FOR SUCH AN AFFORDABLE PRICE!!! This product is NOT INTIMIDATING and very easy to use! Also, THANK YOU FOR MAKING REGISTRATION PROCESS PAINLESS AS YOU ALREADY DID EVERYTHING FOR ME!!! All I had to do was press 'Register' and it was DONE! I did NOT HAVE TO TYPE ANYTHING IN! Win Win!!! God Bless You... You made my currently, very difficult life so much easier! I'm excited to be able to sit back and relax while I receive treatment for my current health crisis, the main reason I bought product..for an eReader as well as beyond since I wanted something more than an eReader... I can listen and watch all kinds of things and I absolutely LOVE that I could download a Solitaire Card Game for free and be able to play offline, when I'm not connected to or out of range of Internet of any kind. It's right there for me any time...even while I receive treatment. Such a welcome and easy distraction! I LOVE YOU AMAZON! A loyal customer for 12 years!

title: The good, the bad and the neutral. First impressions.
body: I must agree that it is unfair to judge something without trying it. I would therefore have preferred to write this without giving any star rating at all but Amazon does not permit that.After doing quite a bit of research, I have decided to purchase one. I gave Amazon the five-star rating because it has done the REALLY important thing right: it convinced a cynical nerd like me to try their newest toy. My initial impressions include far more "likes" than "dislikes" and, most important of all, I can try it (risk free) for up to 30 days.I will write another review after I have received my unit and tested it. Until then, I wanted to share the things that I considered before purchasing my unit, in no particular order. Perhaps this list will help others to organize their thoughts.LikesThe size and weight of the unit. Large enough to have a usable viewing area (which my iPhone does not) and light enough to hold for extended periods without discomfort.There are already plenty of books available at the Amazon site. None cost more than $10, which is a great price for best-sellers. Over time, the lower cost of reading best-sellers will recompensate me for the initial price of this thing.I can browse the first few pages of a book, to determine whether or not I like it, before I buy it. Almost as good as going to the bookstore!Nice ergonomics for us baby boomers. Large buttons for navigating pages (e.g. forward and backward). Customizable font sizes.I like to retain my books for future rereading and I read a lot. The Kindle's USB connection facilitates storing them (and cataloging them in my PC). Also, Amazon's servers maintain a backup copy of all media that it processes in a so-called Media Library. This will prove to be very handy if something happens to the unit and/or its capacity is exceeded.Visual books, newspapers, audio books and MP3 music in a single unit. Cool!Everywhere access to Wikipedia. I use this reference all the time from my iPhone and the Kindle provides a bigger screen.Everywhere access to other mostly-text web sites.Built-in dictionary. Very handy for books by Anthony Burgess.Each Kindle has its own email address and it automatically filters out emails from senders that are not explicitly authorized. I and other authorized users can send text documents to the Kindle via email.It can be tried risk-free. The Kindle return policy allows you to return it (in like-new condition) within 30 days of purchase. This was a VERY important element of my decision process.I can make annotations, just like I sometimes do in my books. I can clip portions of text (quotes) that appeal to me. Both these annotations and text extractions can be saved to my PC and/or Media Library (at Amazon). I often encounter snippets that I would like to retain. This feature allows me to painlessly save them and, better, find them again when I want them.Storage capacity can be increased by inserting an SD card. I have seen these in up to 8GB, so that's quite a bit of media.Save the trees!The battery is user-replaceable and currently costs $20 in the Kindle store.The power adapter accepts all world currents. A physical outlet adapter may be required.The people who actually have one (testers) are generally singing its praises.Not sureThe screen sounds great. Electronic paper. Low glare and real ink. My eyes will, however, be the final judge.The itty-bitty buttons on the keypad.
