We'll see how my klutzy fingers deal with that.I'll be interested to hear the quality of MP3 music played by this thing.I expect that access to the web (e.g. Wikipedia) will be painfully slow but would be delighted to be pleasantly surprised.Books may only be purchased from Amazon and audio books may only be purchased from Audible. I buy most of my media from them anyway, so I don't anticipate any problems unless they decide to raise prices if/after the Kindle catches on.MP3 music, TXT and a couple of Mobipocket files can be downloaded from any PC via the USB connection. These and other "text" documents (e.g. Microsoft Word or Adobe PDF) may be sent to a Kindle via email, which utilizes a cellular (not WiFi) network. This does not sound too restrictive to me now, but we'll see.Many of Kindle's best features are tied into an analog cellular network that only exists in the USA (i.e. the EVDO extension of CDMA). Media saved in the Kindle is accessible everywhere but all functions requiring network access will only operate in the USA.It had better be possible to upgrade my Kindle to the latest and greatest software release. Further, I want to be able to pick and choose what upgrades are installed and when they are installed. Apple and iTunes "upgrades" have taught me to be wary.DislikesIt's a lot of money. But it's brand new, cutting-edge technology (which always costs more). I expect prices to sink after Christmas.Older books cost more than used paperbacks (at the Amazon site).No support for Bluetooth headphones. This will be missed by those who would like to listen to an audio book or MP3 background music while the Kindle is safely tucked away in a purse or brief case.The handbook is a poor pdf document. You cannot jump from the TOC to the referenced page and it's impossible to determine, simply by looking at a page, what chapter you are looking at. No index.Only a one-year warranty and no offer for an extended warranty. The Kindle's electronic paper screen is new technology and its longevity is unknown. This is an important negative, in my book.No support for PDF. OK, PDF is licensed by Adobe but this nonetheless surprises me. Luckily, DOC, JPG, HTML, GIF, PNG and ZIP are supported. As others have pointed out, it is possible to convert PDF to Mobipocket files, but the conversion appears to be imperfect. I consider this to be an important negative that Amazon needs to address.No WiFi support. On one hand, it's great that the Kindle can access the web and receive email using the standard cellular network(s) but these facilities are MUCH slower than WiFi. I would like to see an implementation similar to my iPhone: WiFi whenever possible and standard cellular when WiFi is unavailable.

title: My ultimate electronic device
body: I was intrigued when I first saw the Kindle announcement, but I always like to do my research before I purchase anything. As I read/watched reviews and product comparisons and read forum discussions and articles I became even more excited about this ebook reader. Yes, some of the other readers out there might look more cutting-edge, but the Kindle has a large, expanding library; Amazon's backing (I've been shopping with them since 1999 and trust them); wireless connectivity via the whispernet; and expandable memory. As a voracious reader and gadget person, this is my ultimate electronic device.I was finally able to place my order on June 6, 2008. Even with the two-day shipping on offer at the time, it seemed to take forever to get here, but when it arrived it was worth the wait. The packaging was very carefully created, even stylish. I appreciate that kind of attention to detail; to me it indicates a quality product.I love electronic gadgets and am very comfortable with computers (they're invaluable to my work as an Information Specialist and writer), so that is my bias and background. It took me mere minutes and a few quick references to the onboard user manual to learn how to work most of the Kindle features. The few areas that I see for improvement were not enough, in my mind, to deduct a star from my review. This is the ebook I have been waiting for.The eInk screen is beautiful! It looks very like reading a page from a magazine. The background is slightly gray, vs. being stark white, but that does not detract from the reading experience. The technology does mean that there is no back light. Thank goodness! I had been reading on a pocket PC and this is so much easier on my eyes. I prefer the method of clipping on a book light when I need it. In some low-light situations simply increasing the font size serves the same purpose. I wouldn't mind some kind of included light that I could switch on at will, but only if they could do it without significantly increasing the weight of the Kindle or reducing the battery life (they would probably have to use a larger battery cell to off-set the light).Yes, it does take a bit of conscious thought at first to avoid triggering the page-turning buttons when you handle the Kindle, but it soon becomes second nature. I would rather have it this way as it allows me to turn pages while reading without even thinking about it. (The large buttons are equally valuable when propping the book up on an exercise machine such as the elliptical; that and increased font size makes it possible to read where I was never able to in the past.) The Kindle really does an excellent job of disappearing when I am reading. I think that is the whole point of the design.I do have a small problem with the back cover (the gray, rubberized portion that hides the battery, SD slot, and reset button). It slides open on its own. Something about the way I carry it in my purse (and, yes, I always leave it in the cover) imitates the motion required to open it. There is nothing wrong with the back cover and it quickly snaps back into place. I just make sure the elastic strap is in place to hold the book cover closed and prevent my Kindle from falling out when I remove it from my purse. I have heard from others who experienced the same problem. The solution is apparently a bit of Velcro or a new book cover/case with corner restraints.That said I'm not quite ready to purchase a new book cover. My Kindle does click into the cover (by means of the little plastic tab) as designed. I think Amazon did a great thing when they included all necessary accessories in a bundle with the Kindle. That avoids a lot of hassle and confusion and prevents the "hidden costs" aspect from being a factor. You know up front that you will have a fully functional Kindle for the price (currently) of $359. Yes, that is a chunk of money, and I didn't spend it lightly, but the investment is worthwile. I will save on books and contain the spread of my already over-large book collection.The main thing on my wish list for Kindle 2.0 is a software upgrade. It should be possible for books to be arranged into folders. Subscriptions and NowNow responses already appear this way. I want manual control over my content so that I can group it to my own liking. For example, I would create a folder for documents related to my writing class and another for the Honor Harrington books by David Weber (an extensive series). Currently, I have to navigate all the way to the end of my 9 pages of content to access "Writing the Novel Proposal," and I look up the next book in the Honor Harrington series online. Others have uploaded text files with book lists for their Kindle, but I don't think we should have to create work-arounds.The ease of moving books and articles to your Kindle is a real plus. You can both email it to your @kindle or @free.kindle address or drag and drop via the USB cord. I tend to grab online articles for later reading and reference on my Kindle.Being able to insert an SD card to increase the available memory was another real selling point for me. I easily transferred my existing ebooks from my pocket PC to my Kindle by means of an SD card. My one call to their customer support center was before I even placed my order, to ask about the accepted types/sizes of SD cards, and they were helpful and polite.One of my favorite Kindle features is being able to download book samples. I use the feature both to remind myself of books I would like to purchase in future and to find out if I really want to read the book. This imitates, at least in part, my quick skimming of physical books in a bookstore. If I decide that I do want to purchase a book, it's only a couple of clicks away.The Kindle is not an all-in-one device, and I don't want it to be. It does what it is meant to do as a book reader and does it well. The extras afforded by the whispernet and the compact library-in-my-pocket make it my ultimate book. Don't even try to get it out of my hands!EDIT - I just had to add a short note on another function I find myself using increasingly. When I'm out shopping, it's great to use the experimental web browser to compare prices. For example, yesterday I compared book and movie prices on Amazon as well as a grooming item from the pet store via Google--both on the Kindle.

title: More Kindle experiences from an actual user
body: I received my Kindle three days ago. Before I go on, let me say that I love it and already prefer it to a mass market paperback or hardback book. In terms of comfort, I'd give it about the same rating as a trade paperback, but after using the Kindle, I'd choose it over all three.Other reviewers have given you an accurate account of how it feels to use the Kindle and the reaction of actual users has generally been very favorable. Rather than duplicate what they've said, I'd like to talk about my initial experiences with it.The first hour or so, the Kindle felt incredibly awkward. I couldn't touch it without pressing a button somewhere. Step one, and the hardest step of all, was learning how to hold it. The next challenge was learning how to navigate the menus. They're straightforward and easy to understand, but even though I'm an experienced computer user, I still got lost at one point. I was in the content manager, which looks a lot like the home page. I could see all my books listed but none of them would open. I had downloaded and read the manual while I was waiting for the reader to arrive. I couldn't figure out how to get back home so I opened the manual on my computer and a quick glance let me figure out where I was and how to get where I wanted to go. For the record, there's a home button on the Kindle keyboard to the left of the SYM key and it takes you right back to the list of books on the reader.The next challenge was wireless service. We have Sprint in my area, but I quickly found the reception isn't very good. I had pre-ordered several books and some of them were on the Kindle when it arrived. I waited for the rest to download, but couldn't get a wireless signal stronger than 1 or 2 bars and it seems to take about 3 bars to receive downloads or visit the Kindle store in anything approaching a useable manner. I finally decided to leave the Kindle on and see what happened overnight. The signal must have been strong enough at times, because all my books and the welcoming letter from Jeff Bezos were there in the morning. I'm downloading books to my computer now. It only takes an extra minute (literally) to transfer them over the the Kindle, so that's more than acceptable.I only had a few hours to use the Kindle the next day. I was still searching for a comfortable way to hold it and so conscious of the device that I had trouble getting lost in the story. But there were moments I did and I could see that it was only a matter of time until I adjusted to the newness and forgot about the device. By the third day, I'd done that. Reading on the Kindle is a great experience and I can't wait to get back to it when I have to go away and do other things.The huge plus is the quality of the display. The text is so close to that of books, I can't tell the difference. What's more, it looks just as good in full sunlight as it does indoors. I think the gray background is just right outside, but a little dark inside. On the other hand, it may be easier on the eyes than a lighter one -- I can't tell that without seeing the difference. I'm willing to bet Amazon spent a lot of time testing this and I trust they chose the gray level they thought gave the best reading experience.A little about ergonomics. The Kindle is heavier than I expected. It weighs noticeably more than a mass market paperback, but it's also noticeably lighter than a hardback. It comes closest to a trade paperback in terms of weight and size. The keys on the keyboard are tiny, but useable. Some people have written about the delay before letters show up on the screen. I just watch they keys and by the time I look up, the word is there.A few other notes:Searching all the books on the Kindlle is fast; it only takes a few seconds to find two words of text in approximately 40 books, some quite long.The choice of font sizes is great. The default was the third setting and that's the one I ended up using. I compared the Kindle version to a paperback copy of the same book. At the third setting, the Kindle had the same number of words per line, but only 16 lines on the screen versus 32 in the printed book. The words per line went up or down by 1 or 2 for each change in font size, and the number of lines on the screen ranged from 26 at the smallest size to 11 at the smallest. 16 lines per screen is fine and it doesn't feel like I have to page forward too often.The Kindle is secure in the cover, but it did help to lift the plastic tab on the back cover slightly (and carefully). I think the tab was meant to catch in the recess on the back of the reader and secure it, but am not sure.For those who think the Kindle is ugly, it's much better in person. I believe the pictures don't do it justice because the right side of the book slopes out and down. If it dropped off sharply like the left side does, it would look more symmetrical. There's a good reason for that design and you'll understand it as soon as you hold the reader. As for style, compare an older Apple product to a newer one. Their looks change radically and what seemed so modern and fashionable a few years looks totally out of date now. Fashion comes and goes; uncluttered, classic design still looks good years later.Finally, how do you hold your Kindle? If Amazon advertised, that's the commercial I'd tell them to make. There are lots of ways. One of the most comfortable is to place my thumbs on either side of the amazonkindle logo and let the book fall back against my open hands. The other is to hold it between my hands about halfway up. When I do that, my left thumb rests on the book cover and lies along the side of the reader, while my right thumb rests naturally on the page forward button. Now I've learned how much pressure it takes to activate the key, I don't have any trouble pressing it accidentally.After some of the negative comments, you might wonder why I'm so happy with my Kindle. The reason is simple: it does exactly what I wanted it to do. It lets me comfortably read lots of book at a lower cost. It will pay for itself in 1-2 years. I don't have the space to keep all the books I buy, so it doesn't matter if Amazon stops supporting the product someday. I will still have read the books and that's what counts.

title: Fragmented Impressions from  a Converted Rare Reader
body: Rather than write the umpteenth glowing review of this exceptional device, I'd like to give you specific views of how and why I use it daily. I am a modest reader--heck, who am I kidding, I read books rarely and magazines only on the throne--and although it'd seem that the most avid readers would be the ones to shell out $400 for the Kindle, I can tell you, this Kindle e-reader has catapulted me into the stratosphere of reader extraordinaire. I read every moment that I can sneak in, and now cannot wait to run home, throw on the jammies, and cuddle with a Mountain Dew and get reading. Not just a novelty, it is so easy to hold, and so clear to read, that I enjoy it much more than reading a hardcover or softcover book. All the people who like the "smell of books, the feel of the page" really should try this device. It has a fully returnable 30 day guarantee, and anyone will find that 10.5 ounces is, simply, much easier on the arms than trying to prop an unwieldy book, turn pages, and suffer. I did not know that I was suffering all these years. No wonder I hated Oliver Twist in junior high. It was the book, it wasn't me. I'm okay. I keep telling myself now, "I'm okay!" Extremely reassuring to the "inner me".Gray screen/eInk: exceptional. I've seen the youtube reviews lamenting the gray back screen rather than white, but once you are reading, you forget all about it, and frankly, the gray is much easier on the eyes. eInk does not flex and "oil" when you touch it with fingers like an LCD display, and as the screen is not touch-screen, you never need to touch or goop it up. It is glorious in its unobtrusiveness.Battery: exceptional. Amazon says 2 days with wireless on, and seven days with it off. I turn on wireless a few times daily to get my newspaper and check out wikipedia, but mostly just use the Kindle on the "on" mode with wireless off. Battery life for me: at least roughly 25-30 hours of reading. Extra battery: 19.99, but unlikely to need one soon, still good to know it is user replaceable.Cover: the book is intended to be read in the cover, quite simply. Please read me review under "Kindle cover" on Amazon for my thoughts on the cover. I love the cover and never read without it and have no issues with errant Next Page button pushes, like those on youtube reviews. Use the cover properly and the book stays well in place. No issues at all with teh cover. None.Quality: I find the reader to be exceptional quality, with one caveat. The scroll wheel is push button type, and the Next Page button is used all the time. They are, if anything on this device, the thing to fail. They will get pushed millions of times over a couple of years. Amazon gives an unconditional one year warranty, and I'd suggest they offer an extended warranty at a reasonable price. This reader is so dependent on the scroll wheel and the Next Page button that, should one of them fail or break off one year and one month into my ownership, I'll be more pissed that a desert rat! After pulling my hair out of my head, one strand at a time, and cursing life up one side and down the other, I will throw down text and video barbs against Amazon that make all those disgruntled Apple MacBook user-sledgehammer youtube videos look like childsplay. Amazon, get an extended warranty going at $19.99 yearly now. I said NOW!Newpapers: Loved my two week Wall Street Journal preview, with easy to read content, but seeing as the info was all about industry and investment, I decided to subscribe to the Washington Post at 9.99 monthly for a less myopic view on news. When USA Today is available, I will stop my hard copy mailed version and get it via Kindle. No issues reading, and although graphics are rudimentary, there are also no ads. Once I am reading, I do not miss the graphics at all.Wikipedia: the internet on this device is horrible, slow and sluggish. Even text-based websites are slow with page loading and I would say not worth even using; do not buy the Kindle for web browsing. All that said, since it is integrated with Wikipedia, that seems to work effortlessly. Click "search" button, then type @wiki and the name of what you wish to search, and boom, in 20 seconds, its there, just like on-line. Same as Wikipedai you all know. Wikipedia has enough content that, if it were published on hard paper, it'd be 2.5 miles long, I'm told. Adore the ability to search wikipedia with the click of a button or two. Could spend hours just doing that. And, on a similar note, the integrated 250k word Oxford English dictionary, that you click while reading to find any word, is fast, effective, and adds to the reading.Silent: I like to read in bed, and my wife likes to sleep. Even the sound of my magazines irks her. Well, this device is nearly silent, even when turning pages. Win-win for wife and me.Font: I change the size of the font with a couple button clicks all the time. I have reasonable vision, but depending on the circumstances, such as low light, if I am tired, et cetera, I change the font more than I thought I would. For instance, when in bed and my arm gets fatigued from holding the book, I lay it down on the covers, lie sideways, and blow up the font, and stare at the reader from about three feet away. Voila, I can still see it. Hugely useable feature.Kindle Store: browse it often, and find it easily used, downloads are fast, and enjoy that Amazon saves my purchased books--forever. And managing content on the Kindle, such as unsubscribing to magazines and newspapers, and trial offers, and getting free samples of every book (I do this often and really have bought and shunned books based on reading one free sample chapter. It works!), is easily done on the device, or on the Amazon website.In summary, although the thing is $399, the EVDO Sprint network functions without a hitch, and for spending hours and hours reading in airports, in bed, in the car, it is a stupendous device. I would read everything on it if it was available. The book list, now over 105k titles, is still spotty, but Jeff Bezos has a goal of getting all published content ever printed on here within ten years (watch the 54 minute Bezos-Charlie Rose interview on Amazon or youtube for a relevant overview).

title: Review of Review
body: I've had my Kindle for almost one year or more than one hundred books. It still works just fine (except for the ridulously long "button" for "next page" on the right side which sticks a little bit). It is as durable as a ball peen hammer and still ranks of one of my top 3 tech purchases of all time. I ran across a review I wrote on CNET last December and found that I still pretty much agree with those earlier impressions. For Kindle 2.0 I'm hoping for: a backlight for night reading, a bigger screen, smaller page turning buttons, a better dictionary, a virtual keyboard to replace the space-wasting physical one and better resolution for drawings and graphs. Also, it is too expensive! I have recommended it to many friends and most don't buy it citing the cost. The Whispernet's almost instant book delivery is painless and addictive. If the entry Kindle purchase price was lower I think Amazon would lock down the e-reader market. Maybe selling a stripped down model with some customizing upgrade options would work. The backlight or color screen or better dictionary would add to the base model cost.If you are a reader, you need a Kindle![..]FWIW, here is my old review from last December:4.5 stars"If you are a "READER" buy it!"by fg4 on December 26, 2007Pros: All you need to read the rest of your life.Cons: Ergonomics not great (for me), B&W, some books too expensive.Summary: I've had my Kindle for 10 days and read several complete books on it, read bits and pieces of a few more and bought quite a few for future reads. The ability to shop a very large selection on the device and download choices practically instantly is somewhat addictive and probably easily anticipated by Amazon.com's marketing dept. I have been a voracious reader for a half century and, sad to say, read a great deal of nonclassic works (aka "trash", beach books, current escapist fiction and so forth). Unfortunately I am almost compelled to buy my favorite authors' books before the ink is dry. This has produced a huge mass of books around the house that I won't be reading again (hey, I love Stuart Woods, but he isn't Steinbeck). My wife, while not actually trying to burn my books, has banned them from the living quarters of our home. I receive ocular daggers whenever I sneak into the house with a Borders or Barnes & Noble bag, although the Amazon.com boxes receive the most fearsome look.So, enter the Kindle: small, space efficient, of trivial mass and letting me buy books at "half price" (as I rationalize with the wife). I am generally an early adopter so I'm used to clunky tech things that will be replaced in 6 months with something twice as cool at half the price. The Kindle has too many buttons! I can hardly pick it up without hitting the "next" "prev" or "back" buttons which occupy the large chunks of space along the left and right borders of the device. Hey Amazon, I don't need a four inch long "button". Remember, we all use cell phones with microscopic keypads and have 21st century manual dexterity. Whether it's a Kindle or a digital camera, we need some unoccupied areas on the surface to grip and hold the device! Also "back" and "previous" seem like the same thing to many folks (like me) but they take you to far different places on the Kindle!If you are a fast reader, you will wish a few milliseconds could be shaved off the electronic page "turning". The screen should be a little bigger (take space away from the buttons and keyboard) and get color please. The B&W text is very easy to read in sunlight or in a relatively bright interior setting. It is useless in dim light--but a backlight or frontlight function would allow reading in a car or in bed. I don't care if this shortens battery reading time; after all, I can plug the Kindle in every day to recharge if I necessary, and my wife hates those little reading lights you clip onto a book (they don't fit very well on your Kindle anyway).The Kindle sucks for newspapers, but I might use it once or twice if I'm someplace with cell coverage, but no computer. Come on Amazon, I pay to subscribe to the online WSJ already--I don't want to pay again to read it on your tiny screen. And, no blog is worth paying to subscribe to! Now, I know you want me to buy and read books, so you don't care that I can't surf the 'net very well with your device--I'm supposed to be reading your books and not wasting Kindle time on the stuff you can't sell to me. Don't worry, in a matter of months there will be some tech workaround or new product that will get around this. Hey, it's got a phone in it--how about letting me making some phone calls. And, mp3s? Wow, very neat to play mp3 background music; it would really be nice if it wasn't hidden in some kind of Nanolike blind shuffle! And, the free Wikipedia is a nice touch, but your free dictionary isn't going to help anyone who managed to squeak past the 8th grade. I haven't found a word yet that I don't know that your pissant dictionary can define-and I'm no Mensa member! I pay to subscribe to the MW unabriged online dictionary; how about letting me use it? When I give your dictionary a line that has a troublesome word, "she took the gun and blew out his cingulate gyrus...", it returns the definitions of "took", "gun" and "blew". Please, I know it's not AI, but the Kindle doesn't have to be flat out stupid! Also, lower the prices (please) on the old mass market "paperbacks"! Who is going to pay you $7.99 for a 9 year old beach book that you can buy for 99 cents at a used book store? Besides if I get sand all over the paper product or drop it in the toilet, I just wipe it off and keep reading--try that with the Kindle!In summary, even though I'm complaining a lot, the Kindle is a great first effort. It is perfect for the voracious junk reader, not so good for a true student and no substitute for a first edition "Leaves of Grass" on your coffee table. I would give it a "10" except it isn't perfect, although if I'll bet Jeff Bezos et al will keep making it better. The Kindle is in the top 3 of my all time best tech purchases (the other two were an Apple II computer and a 628 Kilopixel digital camera bought in the last century.)--fg4

title: absolutely fabulous item
body: I absolutely love my new Kindle. I have it for about 1-1/2 months and I think it is fabulous.I am a very avid reader (about 120 books per year) and when I first saw the Kindle on Amazon.com I was very intrigued. What a neat product!!!.The only problem was the price; $400 is pretty steep to read books. One week later the intrigue of the product won out over the price and I placed my order. The waiting was the worst. Now that I have it I don't want to read a regular book. The printed page is so crisp, clear and easy on your eyes. When reading outside in bright sunlight there is no glare and the printing seems even crisper. WONDERFUL!!!!The ease of purchasing books is great. Your Kindle is already registered, you account with Amazon.com is already set up when you turn your Kindle on for the first time. You need to do nothing but start ordering and reading. I do recommend taking the time to read The Users Guide on your Kindle. There is quite a bit of info that will help you get the most from your new Kindle. Once again WONDERFUL!!!!I have read about 10 books since receiving my Kindle and it is so nice not having to figure out where to store 10 books after they've been read. Great space saver!! Supposedly the Kindle can store up to 200 books (won't know till I get there), but it will be great to read 200 books and not have to figure out what to do with them after I've read them. At the moment I have about 1000 books stored in boxes and it is just such a pain to get them from the attic and take somewhere to sell or give away. This problem will be solved with the Kindle. I very rarely read a book more than 1x, so it just gets deleted and that is the end of that. JUST GREAT!!!*****Other GREAT Features*******The size and weight - "PERFECT!!!**The different font sizes and the ease of changing the text; all right at your fingertips and a "FABULOUS" feature. My grandmother pays big bucks for Large Print books and to top that off they are thicker and heavier to hold while reading. With the Kindle you get the benefit of large print in a very lightweight, small, easy to hold package. FABULOUS!!!!**Ease of page turning - (sometimes too easy) You need to be careful here or you can lose your page very easy. It takes awhile but you will learn how to hold, pick up and lay down the Kindle without losing your page. The page turning buttons are located on each side and they are very touchy, so you need to be careful.**Built in dictionary - this feature is great when you come across a word you just aren't sure of. Just place the curser at the end of the sentence containing the word you want a definition for, press the scroll wheel, select "LOOKUP" and the word and definition are right there. When finished, click "CLOSE" and you are back to reading. VERY SIMPLE!!!!**Purchasing books - so easy and convienent. A couple clicks and you have the book of your choice. Another great feature is the capability of ordering samples of books you are interested in before the actual purchase. The cover of a book may sound good but the first 2 chapters will tell you how interesting the book really is. If the first few chapters don't capture my interest, I won't read the book. I've wasted plenty of money on books that sounded good on the cover only to find out the book was awful. This won't happen with the Kindle.The Kindle has various other capabilities, such as audio books, music, emailing, transferring of PC documents to your Kindle. I have not checked out any of these other features as of yet, so I cannot include an opinion in this reveiw.Even though the Kindle has all these great features, there are a few features that could be improved upon in any future generations of this product.**The case for the Kindle could use some improvement. I think the main function of the cover is to protect your Kindle, which it does very well; if you can get it to stay in the cover. There is a very small clip inside the cover and a slight indentation on the back of the Kindle. You need to line these 2 up at precisely the right angle and tilt the Kindle downward to lock it in place. My first attempt, I broke the clip trying to get it locked in place. Not such a good design feature, could definitely use improvement.**The battery life could be improved upon. I've read mixed reviews on this subject, some people brag about the battery life and others are disappointed. I am one of the disappointed ones. I have had the Kindle fully charged, turned off and not used for a period of 1 week. When I went to use it again the battery was completely dead. So dead that I had to put it on the charger to even turn it on. This has happened 2X. NOT GOOD!!!**Backlighting for night reading would be a fabulous addition to any future generations of the Kindle. I do a lot of my reading at night and the Kindle has no backlighting feature. Amazon recommends the"Mighty Brite" clip-on book light (which I have), but it isn't very easy to clip on to the Kindle. You need to clip the light on till it is stable and there isn't a lot of room on the Kindle to do that. You can't place it on the top because the clip will be in the way of the printed page. You can't clip it on the sides because it presses on the page turning buttons and your pages keep changing. I've figured out that if you clip it on the right side, in 1 certain position you can avoid the page button and the light has the stability that it needs. So, I've solved the problem with some experimenting, but it sure would be easier if the Kindle had a built-in backlighting feature.If Amazons absolute goal was to develop a new, innovative reading device; a device like no other of its kind, than they have greatly succeeded. For persons purchasing a Kindle with expectations of that of a small portable PC, you will be greatly disappointed. The KINDLE IS A READING DEVICE, with a few extra convienences added, it is not a super small, compact PC and should not be expected as such.Overall, I want to give Amazon.com and the Amazon Kindle a "5 Star"thumbs up. My personal opinion is that the "Great Features" of the Kindle outweigh the features that could use some improvement. This is a wonderful item for the avid reader and the best $400 I ever spent.

title: Great First Edition!
body: A couple notes right away:1. I live in an area of Western SD that does not have EVDO. You can download your books and copy them to the Kindle via USB. However, you can not download samples.2. This is regarded as an "electronic device," so, unlike a real book, you can not read it while taking off and landing on airlines. A minor inconvenience.I travel a lot and like to read. I often leave the big books I'm working on at home (i.e., Friedman's "The World is Flat"), just because I'm weighted down enough with computer, etc., when I fly. I often just take paperbacks for that reason. I've been on a couple trips with the Kindle, and I'm glad I paid the bucks to be an "early adapter" of technology. On my most recent trip, weather delayed my flight. On the two day trip, I completed two books and started a third. Most of the time, I buy several books at a time, so I always have one ready to go when I finish the other. But, the spare book I take with me on my trip is usually in my checked baggage - and the rest are at home. On this trip I was able to instantly download another book and keep on going. I'll be moving into an area with EVDO soon, so I'll get the full benefit of the abilities of the Kindle.There are many areas that can improve as time passes, but this was a well thought out product for it's first release. The readability is much better that I anticipated. I'm now glad that it is does not have a back light. You can read it with low light, and adjust the text size for the times of minimal light.It was a hit on one of the flights, with several flight attendants and passengers handling it and asking questions. The $400 price tag made a few gulp. Over time, the features will improve and the price will drop as other manufacturers enter the fray, as well. I don't regret my investment for a moment. If you want to wait, go ahead. But I'm taking a lightweight device that stores lots of books, and my guess is that I won't be buying many books anymore.I'm in agreement with many - there is the annoyance of not being able to set it down, pick it up, or handle it without advancing pages. I can live with it, but there is no need for the height of the page controls - there is a better solution.Graphics will improve with time - and color will be added. For the short term, Amazon needs to focus on pdfs and graphics in books. I read a leadership book with a number of charts that were unreadable. There is no ability to enlarge the graphics as there is with text. A pdf file that I e-mailed was virtually unreadable because is puts the file full page on the screen and you cannot zoom in.There is a confirm action required to delete material, but not to buy material. In showing the unit to someone, they asked about a specific book. I shopped the store and went to download a sample. Because the sample is located right next to the buy action, when I was showing it, as I went to hit the download sample, I didn't directly press the wheel and it slipped to BUY. There was no confirm and it immediately downloaded the book and charged me $9.99. I hit the return to bookstore and deleted the book, but so far, it is still listed in my purchases and my credit card has not been credited for the return. Online, you have to mouse up to the one click, and this is not an issue, but having the sample right next to the buy means there is no room for error. A simple click to confirm would be welcome - especially as using the wheel is new when you are lightly holding the book.I have read reviews from people that would like to see this come with a back light, e-mail, mp3 improvement, and other functions. I have an iPod nano for music - I don't want the Kindle to be an mp3 or video player. I have my smart phone and notebook for e-mail. I would really like to see the focus on being the best book reader you can buy. The battery life without the wireless feature on is excellent and will improve. Going online is nice to follow up on searches, but I don't want to compromise the readability, size and features of the book/magazine side of the Kindle compromised to make this a mini-computer. Let it be about the books.I have tried reading books on my PDAs and I recently purchased an iPod Touch to be able to watch videos on the road. Reading on those devices never worked for me. The Kindle does. I think they could save on keyboard real estate, increase the screen size - and keep the unit size the same - but it is nice as it is.I am excited to watch the growth of the Kindle and other devices that will try to rival it. It will make this an even better product. For me, the ease of use, readability and flexibility of being able to download new books instantly makes this an excellent purchase. My kids always laugh at me for buying the new "toys" on the market, but with the Kindle, they can't wait for version 2.0 to come out so they can get their hands on this one.One final thought about the copy protection. iTunes allows 5 computers to be authorized, Audible has multiple authorizations allowed - even my Garmin maps allow for two GPS units. If Amazon would allow 3-4 authorizations per book, this would be a no brainer - they'd hit a home run. In our family we circulate the better books we read. I know what each of my kids enjoy. I think we ought to have the opportunity to share these books. Other services allow this - I don't see why Amazon can't. If we could trade books among 3-4 users, there would be 3 new sales of the Kindle immediately in my family. We're not talking mass distribution - just the practical ability to share with a couple family members or close friends.Kindle Version 1.0 has been a satisfying purchase. I'm excited to see the growth of this media in the future. I know there are some that long for the physical book in their hands, but this is the future, the same way that mp3 and video has gone. Get over it!Enjoy!

title: Kindle: Amazing New Wireless Reading Device
body: I ordered my Kindle on December 5th and it arrived on January 16th. I've waited to write my review until I've really become familiar with it and how it works for me. I read a large number of books relatively quickly. A significant chunk of my reading time is on public transportation where large hardback books are difficult to carry and handle. On my last two vacations, I've had to take a second suitcase for my books. I live in a large Eastern City with good cell coverage. The Kindle sounded ideal. It has lived up to my expectations.I was surprised at how small it was. Somehow, I thought it would be bigger. I was surprised at how long it took to power up initially. For an thirty seconds, I thought perhaps I was one of those unlucky few with one that was dead out of the box. And then, despite being in the same building as a Sprint Store and in the financial district of the aforementioned large Eastern City, there seemed to be no wireless reception. I turned the wireless switch off and back on again and, well, it's been fine ever since. I think, perhaps, I was a bit anxious.The books I'd purchased in anticipation of my Kindle's arrival downloaded rapidly. The Kindle had enough charge on it to begin reading right away. This has been mentioned before but it's a very nice touch.Overall, I'm extremely pleased. I love the screen. I use the smallest font size most of the time. I increase it in lower light or when I'm tired. I have a borderline need for reading glasses (and lose them constantly) so this is most convenient. I understand some people's desire for a contrast setting as it's what you do to try to make most other kinds of screens more readable. Changing the font size really performs the same functions.I haven't had any real trouble with the cover. I pulled the back off the Kindle while trying to put the cover on the first time but got it sorted out quickly enough. The quality and precision leaves a bit to be desired but it fills it's intended function. I don't fold it all the way back, I just hold it as I would a regular book, sometimes with one hand, sometimes with both. The cover protects the Kindle in my messenger bag. Also, I think, it obscures the fact that I'm not reading a "real" book while on public transportation. As I rarely turn the Kindle or the wireless off, I don't have much occasion to use the buttons on the back. However, I can reach them easily enough even with the cover on. I purchased an SD card which I haven't installed yet but once it's in, I don't foresee removing it very often.Like many people, I have some complaints about the buttons. However, mine seem to be different. I find the Next Page button on the right too hard to push. It's a very long button but seems to have a kind of sweet spot near the center. When I'm holding the Kindle, my thumb really only reaches to just above the Back button. Pressing it there is problematic. I have no such difficulties when holding the Kindle in my left hand. The smaller left side Next Page button pushes easily. But what I really dislike is the noise the scroll wheel makes when I press it. Tactile and audible feedback is, to a point, good interface design but it's just too loud. Forget your reading light waking up your spouse, what about that noise?Battery life has been fine. As mentioned above, I don't tend to turn the wireless off so I only get two or three days. But I'm not likely to be away from an electrical outlet for more than that and, I have to sleep sometime.I have discovered one thing I miss. (And, perhaps it's not fair to blame this on the Kindle or Amazon so it's not a factor in my rating). There is no back of the book, no inside cover flap. I've downloaded a lot of books and samples but I keep now they're just a list of titles and authors. I could go back to the Kindle Store and read the description but I find I keep having the urge to flip over the Kindle and read the description on the back of the book. I would very much like a view option for the home page that includes a brief description of the book. Amazon has this information so it sounds as if it should be possible. I will be sending in a suggestion to Amazon.Overall, I'm please with the availability of content. I was very pleased to discover that the Kindle Store includes a great many science fiction short stories at about fifty cents a piece. This is an excellent use of the selling model. Who would bother to sell an individual short story in printed form? Who would pay to buy it? So, we wait for collections and anthologies. The danger, for me, is that it's just so easy to buy books. I thought One-click and next day delivery was pretty close to instant gratification. This verges on the magical.I am a long time user of many things Apple and a great admirer of their industrial design. I think the iPod borders on revolutionary. But I really don't understand why people think the Kindle has to look as if it was designed by Apple. Yes, it's plastic. I think that was a considered design decision. It makes the Kindle lighter which is of great importance in something I hold upright in my hands for long periods of time.The Kindle is not going to replace my laptop or my iPod or my cell phone or my PDA or my toaster. I knew when I ordered it that it didn't have a color screen, a touch screen or a backlight (and none of the many thousands of books I've purchased have every had the latter two features but I managed to read them anyway). It's not a secure reader for confidential personal or business documents. Just because it might be possible to put a microwave in the glove box of your car doesn't mean that you necessarily should. I am less of a fan of convergent devices than many. The Kindle is a very good book reader. That's amazing enough.I was reading the other night, deeply engrossed in the story, reached up to turn the page... No, I mean I really reached to turn a the physical page. The Kindle does disappear.

title: Theres nothing to think about. You cant get a better tablet for $50
body: This is the 3rd 2015 7" Fire tablet I have purchased. I bought 2 at Christmastime as gifts. Amazon was sold out of them during Christmas so ,oddly enough,I bought them at ToysRUs. The ones I purchased at Christmas came with a NICE black leather case, a 32GB microSD card, an excellent screen protector and cleaning kit, all packaged in a nice box with a free month of Netflix and a Free trial of Amazon Prime. ALL OF THIS WAS ONLY $60.OO each. As I said I bought 2. I set them both up as they were gifts for children. I installed tons of games on them off Amazon's Truly Free Apps. I also sideloaded Google play store on each of them just because that was all these kids have ever seen at school and I wanted them to have some familiarity. I also added my own screensavers as they were too young to know about Amazon shopping, but I didn't want to set it in kids mode because the parents might use the tablets too in the children's sleeping hours,and I set them up on the assumption that they would use them. The parents did,became prime members and now each have their own tablets where before this they were afraid of spending money on something they knew nothing about. You just CANNOT GO WRONG paying such little money for such a great tablet. The only thing I hate is that this most recent one I bought didn't even come with a screensaver AND it had SOMEONE ELSES INFORMATION ON IT ! Now I know how to do a factory reset and was not gonna make my little boy wait several more days to get a new tablet because his sister was getting a new acer tablet that day too and both were delivered by Amazon. Anyway, I did the factory reset and began setup of the tablet. I went to sideload Google play store onto the tablet only to find out an update had been done on this specific tablet and it wood no longer take the sideload app.(Sideloaded NOT ROOTED) Anyway, I won't root a device because it voids the warranty. So, I have a friend who works on one of the big android websites. She fixed up a packet for me to sideload Google play onto my sons Amazon Fire tablet. It is available online if you look around. And anyone can install it with no experience. You won't mess it up. But there's no need to add Google play, unless you just want it. Now, with my son, the ONLY reason I wanted Google play on there for him is because I had purchased about $1200 worth of educational apps for him over the last 2 years that I would have had to purchase again through Amazon and that would be stupid. They are both android. My son has Downs and knows his way around an android tablet or an ipad. He got his first iPad at age 4 and his first android tablet was a Toshiba Thrive the year they came out. But if I had not already owned so many Google apps that were for his use , I would have just used Amazon apps. That's the only thing with Amazon Fire tablets are hard for some people to overlook, exactly this, if you are heavily financially invested in Google Play apps then you are not gonna be happy with a Fire tablet-none of them. But if you are just getting a first time tablet for someone or you just want a reader or even want to buy your family a nice gift, but them EACH a Fire tablet. At the 50 price tag (and occasionally 39.00) you cannot go wrong! Personally I have a Fire Phone AND Fire tablet. I don't regret either. The Fire phone is the stupidest, best made phone I have ever owned and the 2015 Fire tablet is stunning display Super fast setup, Nice little camera and video for getting a picture that you don't have your camera handy for.. Just great all around. As far as my Fire phone I'm just sad I bought it and then Amazon just decided not to support it anymore. So I don't know if it will ever get another update. I think they will stay in the tablet market tho. I'm sure no tablet besides apples overpriced orchard collection sells as many as Amazon sold with this new line. They are sleek,sturdy,great little tablets that cannot be beat for 50 bucks by anyone ! Apple can't do it, Samsung can't, ASUS can't, no one can put a 50 dollar tablet out right now that would steal Amazon's Fire tablet business.The only thing is people who owned the old one will put those for sale on eBay and craigslist etc and make the new ones look bad. People think they are getting the new one and they get the old one.Amazon should have named them Fire2 or something to set th apart. A friend of mine bought one like that and I knew as soon as I saw it that it was the old one. EBay gave her money back and I sent her straight to Amazon. But I see $50-$100 tablets a chain stores that come in all these colors and say they have this and that and they are cheap, plastic, with lousy cameras and terrible speakers and just plain junk that isn't worth the box they come in. I don't know why anyone shops around when they are looking for an inexpensive or a first time user tablet. In fact I don't know why these aren't used in primary and Jr high schools at this price. They can handle apps guess you are a hard core gamer or you need a special tablet for graphic arts. Other than that Amazon's 2015 Fire tablet line has something to suit everyone's needs at AwESoMe prices. By the way, for kids there is a really neat little foam case with built in handle and stand that fits the 7inch tablet. Just search for it here on Amazon. Its available with Prime and can be shipped to you right along with your tablet. Its available in several great colors too and really keeps that tablet in place so you don't have to worry that your child drop it or bump it. Just be sure to get a screen protector.BTW, NOTE TO AMAZON: You really should throw a screen protector in the box with all your tablets. A lot of people don't know what to buy and don't realize how important they are. I really enjoyed the all in one packs that I bought st Christmas with EVERYTHING included from a case to the sd card. But including an inexpensive screen protector would save lots of first timers screens.Amazon's beautiful Fire screen is worth that little extra for your customers, isn't it?

title: A Portable Library
body: I've had my Kindle for several weeks now, and am quite pleased with it. It's nice to be able to carry a good-sized library with me while traveling or just going someplace where I might have some time to fill. The Kindle is the size of a DVD movie case, and that will be much better than traveling with a stack of books as I usually do. Also, I expect that this device will help alleviate a serious problem with book clutter in my house.I've added a [[ASIN:B000PO3CTM 4 GB SanDisk SD Memory Card]] to my Kindle, which should be enough to hold several thousand of books.A few specific comments:DISPLAY: The electronic ink display is quite sharp and easy to read, even in bright sunlight. The display has a matte finish, so no problems with glare. There is no backlight (this is not possible with this technology), but a [[ASIN:B000VTWFKS booklight]] works quite nicely. There is a kind of "flicker" when you change pages, but I didn't find this to be a problem. Also, the display is black and white -- color is not yet available with electronic ink technology.BUTTONS: The buttons give a solid "click" when pressed. It takes a short time to get used to holding the Kindle without hitting the buttons on the side, but using it while in the cover seems to fix this.ILLUSTRATIONS: The Kindle can display illustrations -- only in black and white (grayscale), but with very high resolution. Illustrations must be sized to fit the Kindle screen, so complex illustrations or figures with small lettering may not reproduce well. The quality of illustrations will depend on the publisher, so it's probably a good idea to download a sample of a book if it contains important illustrations. Technical books containing fairly simple figures and graphs tend to work fairly well.FOREIGN CHARACTERS: The Kindle is able to display a few foreign letters: mostly vowels with acute accents, grave accents, and umlauts. The current version should be able to display texts in French, Spanish, or German just fine. However, I have quite a few books in Esperanto that I would like to read, and Kindle cannot yet display the six accented letters of the Esperanto alphabet, even though Web browsers can display these letters. To expand the available languages, I would like to see the characters from the Unicode "Latin Extended A" character set added.EQUATIONS: Yes, the Kindle can display equations in technical books. I've downloaded a few books on mathematics and physics, and the Kindle can display even complex equations just fine. The equations can even scale with changing font size, so they aren't just images if they've been done correctly. Greek letters are available both in equations and within running text. The quality of equations depends on the publisher, though, so I'd recommend downloading a sample of a technical book before buying it.FOOTNOTES: If a book contains footnotes, it's possible to see the footnote by "clicking" on it using the scroll wheel. It's up to the publisher to link the footnotes in this way, though, so not all books will have these "linking" footnotes.COVER: I've found, as have others, that the cover didn't hold the Kindle very securely. The upper-left and lower-left corners fit into leather "brackets", while the back is supposed to be held by a plastic clip that fits into a groove in the back cover. It's this clip in the back that doesn't seem to hold securely -- and even when it is secure, it tends to make the back cover slide off. However, this is all easily fixed by attaching two 5/8" Velcro circles on the back of the right-hand side.BOOK SELECTION: I'd guess that I've been able to find about 1/3 of the books I was looking for available in Kindle format. At present the selection is pretty broad, but not yet deep: you're liable to be able to find at least one book in just about any subject, and many popular books (along with quite a few somewhat more obscure books) are available. There are still a number of notable omissions ("The Grapes of Wrath", "The Good Earth", and "Johnny Tremain", for example), but I expect this will be remedied in time. While not all books I'm interested in are currently available in Kindle format, the selection is large enough to keep me in reading material for quite a long time.NICE FEATURES: You can easily look up an unfamiliar word in the built-in dictionary just by clicking on the line of text containing the word. This is much handier than using a separate dictionary. The Kindle automatically saves your place in each book, but you can also set multiple bookmarks. You can change the font size in any text to suit your needs.OTHER FEATURES: You can highlight text and make your own notes (analogous to writing in the margin). I don't read books this way, so I haven't used these features myself. There is also an experimental Web browser and MP3 player for music and audio books, but I haven't used these features much either.SUGGESTIONS FOR FUTURE VERSIONS:(1) As mentioned earlier, an expanded character set (Unicode Latin Extended A) would be useful.(2) Subdirectories. The Kindle keeps all its books in one top-level directory, which can be sorted by title, author, or most recently accessed first. Twelve book titles are displayed on each page. I already have a large number of books on mine, so it would be nice if future versions had even one level of subdirectories so that I could organize them into e.g. "Technical", "History", "Reference", "Fiction", "Finished", etc.Overall, this is a very impressive product. I'm actually finding myself reading more than I was before, probably because the Kindle makes it so convenient to have a large library of books available whereever I go. I highly recommend this product to every avid reader.

title: Re-KINDLE-ing My Love of Reading.  :  )
body: First and foremost, I'd like to say that I am a Kindle owner. It's important because I believe the rating on this wonderful device is being brought down by people who do not own the device and/or haven't even tried the device through someone else. That's my biggest problem with the reviews thus far. This is an ingenious new project and I'd like it to get the recognition that it deserves. This is by far the best e-reader that I have owned and wouldn't trade it for a thing. Try it, I guarantee that you'll love it just as much and it will change the way you read forever. I mean, really, what do you have to lose? Amazon has a wonderful thirty (30) day return policy and will refund your full purchase price.Secondly, the things that are being used as negatives or cons are so off the wall. "Amazon is trying to dictate what you read", "It's ugly", "It's too expensive", "I know another reader that's much better"...my point on that is then go and post about that reader, but don't bring down this life changing device.For me, there isn't a thing I don't like about my new Kindle...It's helped me rekindle my love of reading and I couldn't be happier. I no longer have to go to the bookstore, drive there, find a parking spot, go in and pray that they have my books in stock or it will be off to another book store and when you're an avid reader, this is a real pain.I love the fact that if I want to I can carry up to two hundred books in my purse and with the SD Memory Card, loads more. I am a book-a-holic...If I want to read it, I buy it, and this little device just made my life two hundred percent easier...I cut out all of the above and just type in the names of the books I want, and in one click it's on my Kindle in less than thirty seconds. And the list of books that I still want to read is long...amazingly long.So far, every single book that I have gone to get has been available on the Kindle. No worries here. I will look forward to their growing library and am so glad that I signed on with Amazon long term with my kindle. This is NO different from owning an Ipod...so I don't particularly care about books that can only be downloaded into my reader. That's right, I did pay for the book and while I can't take it down to the used book store for money...(how many of us have time to do that anyway), I do get to read books that come out first (no waiting for the paper back to come out), are on the best seller list, at a fraction of the hardcover book cost, and I can hold so many. Try that with regular books. So this file sharing system means nothing to me. I look at it as the Kindle is to books as what the Ipod is to Music. I pay for my music and I pay for my books.This device makes my reading too easy and I find myself lost in a book over and over again. I take my tea and my snuggly warm pink blanket and just relax on the couch or in bed.The pages are easily readable with many sizes of lettering to choose from to fit each person's needs. Amazing. I love the fact that all the books that I buy are backed up on my computer so if I want to re-read a book. It's still right there, even if erased from my kindle.Travelling with my kindle is wonderful as well. I don't need to have the whisper net on to enjoy books that I have already downloaded and if I am out of the country, I just simply upload them from my lap top. Easy as can be.I love to simplify my life with new and cutting edge gadgets. This one is remarkable. I love everything about it and everything it can do.The fact that my morning paper awaits me as I wake up or my magazine subscription reaches my kindle before it hits the stands is amazing.Yes, it's true, there is no color...how many colored paperbacks or hardcovers do you own...maybe magazines, but remember we are first here with this Kindle and by the time the next one comes out, I'm sure a few corrections will be made. It's very easy on the eyes and I can read for hours on end. It's easy to hold and while I've read other reviews, I don't find that I turn pages by mistake. It definitely takes a little push to do that. Maybe they're holding it there when they walk? I'm not sure. The keyboard is easy to see and use. As is the Kindle itself. All buttons and levers are explained in the booklet, but are pretty much self explanatory.If you do have a question, which I have in the past, you can just contact the Kindle Support Team. They are available every single day, even holidays. You can do this by email or by calling Kindle Customer Service at 1-866-321-8851. There is even a button in the "contact us" section of Amazon where they will call YOU back...right away. No joke! These have to be some of the nicest, dedicated and well-informed customer service representatives that I have ever come across (and I shop a lot!).If I had to pick one negative, it would be the ill fitting cover that comes with my beloved Kindle. But then again...it's a device and not really a book. So, any cover might be hard to fit. I'm sure new ones will be out in the future to make it easier and I only use the cover to attach my light.Thank you Amazon, I'm so glad I waited for this revolutionary product to become available to me. I love it whole heartedly. I'd recommend it to any one. It's easy to use and simply a joy to have around. I can now shop for books and read just about anywhere. I feel fortunate to be one of the proud owners of this device.I am one satisfied customer of MANY. So please, if you do not own a Kindle, wait until you do to make a review worth reading to help someone trying to decide if they too would like to be a kindle owner. In my opinion, WHO WOULDN'T?? : )Happiest of holidays and a Blessed New Year to all. May 2008 be really great!!

title: For $49, this is an affordable powerhouse compared with higher price alternatives.
body: I have used tablets for a while, starting with a Dell Android. I currently have and frequently use my iPad Mini, Samsung Galaxy Tab 4, 7-inch, and the new Amazon Fire 7-inch. The iPad is the longest and most used tablet that I still used today. But as I'm going on three years of use, I'm looking for a cheaper replacement for diversity and productivity.On the side, I develop iOS and Android apps. I really enjoy my iPad, as I can use it for work/productivity/network capabilities, my hobbies in photo and video editing, entertainment like streaming video and games. The Fire brand is designed with kids in mind and parental controls balanced with entertainment, and it delivers and delivers better than other tablets.I have a three year old, and one on the way, and my three year old loves to use my devices (phones, computers and tablets). While there are plenty of options for parental restrictions (software, apps, native functions), I find that the Amazon Fire offers the best (and peace of mind) parental controls. I can share my contents and libraries with my family--this performs better than my previous Barnes & Noble Nook HD 7-inch. I can set limits of content and time of usage for my kids. I can use it to watch my shows/movies whether it's Amazon Prime Videos, Netflix, Hulu or HBO Now, and the wife makes use of it for videos, music and games.Everything is fast, and unlike previous models, you can use a MicroSD card for additional space (and just about every app can be fully installed on the SD card). The camera isn't all that great, but I don't use any tablets to take pictures, as I use it more for video chatting with family/friends, which it is just fine/perfect for that usage.While this will not (anytime soon) replace my iPad in terms of productivity and my hobbies, I will say it has replace the iPad's function as a entertainment source for all my family, and for a parentally control device for my kids. However, once my iPad quits (or breaks), since I use an iPhone, I wouldn't be replacing it--unless for some reason my eyesight gets bad and I need an iPad to see, but then again, I can't justify the cost of an iPad. (I just may have to buy the Fire for my family, which would cost me about $196: way cheaper than the iPad and Samsung.)The sponsored contents are to help drive down the cost of the tablet and services, and it does not affect your usage whatsoever. I only see the sponsored content when I wake up the device to unlock, and that's it. I personally ignore most advertisements. So, I don't (personally) see a justifiable reason to pay extra to remove the sponsored contents. However, if you did/do buy the sponsored content Fire, you can log on to your account on Amazon.com and pay the extra $15 to remove the content. (I'm cheap, so I won't be doing this.)For $49, this is an affordable powerhouse compared with higher price alternatives. As for cheaper low-end tablets that cost about the same as this Fire, this Fire supersedes them beyond reproach.Some of my cohorts have argumentative point that you need 4G (cellular data) capability on tablets to be fully-functional. I will say my experience using the modern tablets, currently, if you have a tablet with 4G capability you will pay more. You are paying for the extra device on your account, and perhaps different data cost. I have Verizon, and using my iPhone as a hotspot versus using my iPad Mini 4G, the data usage are the same. Without going further into the subject. I have used the Amazon Fire 7-inch on a recent road trip. My family and I have made plenty of use. Movies, TV shows, games, social media, and reading. Everything was smooth and fast.I have been with Amazon since 1997, and I do more shopping with Amazon.com than any other brick & mortar stores in the last seven years. Having a one-stop shop, that I don't have to leave my current geographic location, all on a small tablet is absolutely awesome!PROS:- Cost vs Value vs Usage--you cannot beat this price for the powerhouse unit this is ($49!).- Everything Amazon is in one place--A one-stop shop.- MicroSD card slot- Kids books and magazine/newspapers are vivid and alive compared to ebook readers (of course, it's a tablet).- I got 8 hours of multiple usage, 1 hour more than advertised!- The large bezel is great for holding the tablet without regards to accidentally pressing the screen (which is also great for toddlers).EQUIDISTANT:- The mono-speaker is great, but if you have screaming kids, washer going on, or you want to hear the surround sound/FX, etc., headphones or external speakers would be best for those conditions.- Cameras are good enough for video chatting, but pictures and selfies you'll need your phone, which is most likely better.CONS:- Screen brightness isn't low enough for bedtime reading and isn't bright enough for bright outdoor usage. But typical indoor usage the screen is awesome and clear.- Placement of Power/Wake button (for me) needs to be on the side, because sometimes I would inadvertently press it cause my activity to pause, or to wake up the device when stored away.- Heavier than the iPad Mini and Samsung Galaxy Tab 4 7-inch. While comfortable to hold, long-term hold activates my carpal tunnel syndrome.- The Silk web browser isn't all that great, but it does the job for standard web browsing. (No options for better alternatives).- Common cross-platform apps generally cost more than it would on iTunes or Google Play. (Ex: one game app is about $3 on other stores, but on Amazon it was $7) But this is understandable.

title: For me, this is a great product!
body: I'll start by saying that I am a "gadget" junkie but take my reading experience very seriously. I would not say that I am a 20 book-a-year reader, but I do love the experience of relaxing with a good book and a beverage and just escapting the day-to-day humdrum. I also commute to work and do much of my reading "on the go". Vacations are a big opportunity to catch up on my reading also. The Kindle has made all of the reading opportunities easier for me. I was an E-Book owner and just hated the device. Then I tried the PDA approach and it was even worse. the screen was just too small. I feel the Kindle has solved ALL the problems I had with both the E-Book and my PDA...and then some!1 - EInk - WOW - Easy on the eyes, clear sharp and I don't fell like I am reading something "electronic". 6 different font sizes means there is a size for everyone. I use the third largest size and it is very comfortable on my eyes. The "flash" after the turn page is something that I got used to after the second day. There is about a one second delay in the page turn but I just adjusted my timing and hit the page forward button a little before getting to the end of the last line on the page so my reading was not interrupted. Not such a big deal.2 - Back light - Kindle does not have a back light. I thought that would be my biggest concern and as it turns out, I find that the screen is so crisp and bright that I don't need a light at all under most lighting conditions. I did test one of my book lights on the Kindle and find that the glare off the screen is minimal because of the "satin" finish of the screen. I think it will be just fine.3 - Weight - Very light weight, however I tend to read with mine in the cover that comes with it for several reasons (more below), so it does add a very little bit of weight. But the Kindle is so light, that with the cover is it still lighter than any hardcover book and about the weight of a paperback. I can have all different titles at my fingertips rather than lugging books and magazines and newspapers back and forth to work everyday. My tote bag is way lighter now that I have a Kindle and my back is so happy for that!!4 - Page Forward & Back Buttons - OK, I would have to agree with most reviewers that the buttons are super-sensitive and probably in a bad position...if you are not using the book cover. If you keep the Kindle in its cover and fold the front cover to the back while reading, it give you a "lip" to hold onto down the left hand side and therefore keeps your fingers from randomly hitting the buttons. I am sure this will be addressed in the next version, but it is not something that would make me send this one back, or not buy it at all.5 - Key Board - Great feature, but I think it could have been covered with a flap. I think that would give the user more space to hold the unit if they are not using the book cover. It really has no function during the reading process unless your are putting the unit to sleep. Everything else, I believe, can be done with the "roller" control unless you are listening to background music and want to pause or foward with out moving out of the book you are reading.6 - MP3 Player - Ahhhhhh...what a great little feature! This is probably one of the top three features (along with the EInk and wireless capability) that really spoke to me and said "Try Me". I can not read when a TV is on, or there is a conversation going on around me, or when there is music on with lyrics...I get too distracted. Unfortunately, many of the opportunites I take to read are in public (commuting, office visits, salon visits, vacations where there is constant music being played at the pool) and the music and conversations going on around me are not conducive to my reading experience. With the Kindle I can have my own "reading specific" music (instrumetal only) right at my fingertips without having to pull out another device. If a conversation gets too loud on the bus to work I just put my headphones on and ZONE out! The MP3 download process was very easy...just use a USB plug-in and drag and drop. The unit also has a small speaker on the back of it for easy listening with out ear phones.7 - SD Slot - 2 Gig is enough to hold more than enough books and music!! No more stopping in the airport to buy extra books just in case the one you have turns out to be a dud!! You have your whoooole library with you at all times.8 - Wireless - Can I get an AMEN?!?!?! Unbelievably convenient and easy to use. Don't like what your reading? Just heard of a new title you MUST have? Shop for your title directly from the unit and have it delivered DIRECTLY TO YOUR UNIT in about 20 seconds!! Sure, there is nothing like strolling thorugh Barnes & Noble on a quiet Sunday morning and just browsing the books, and most of us will continue to do that. But, when you need instant gratification this device delivers!9 - Cost of books - This is a personal issue for each one of us. For me, I feel that I am going to spend money on a book anyway so it does not bother me that the cost is $9.99 (sometimes lower/sometimes higher). I am still saving money because I tend to buy the "larger" paperbacks because they are easier on the eyes and they sell for several more dollars than the e-books. Your titles are kept for you on Amazon.com.10 - Battery Life - Does it ever konk out? Mine hasn't yet...even using the music player it is still on its first charge.I am still learning my way around my Kindle but these are my initial impressions after using it for about one week. I hope this helped.

title: Updated Kindle review 4 months later...
body: I keep reading bad reviews posted by folks who obviously DONT own a kindle. I decided to respond to one that seems to sum up most of them..."The product has considerable drawbacks, any one of which is a deal-breaker.1. Black and white display "Um, its a BOOK reader, designed to look and feel like reading a book. How many books you know of have multi-colored fonts on every page?"2. No WiFi, uses EVDO instead like the cell phone network (EVDO is slower than WiFi, and WiFi works in more places in urban environments)"Again, your off your rocker on that one. If your stealing WiFi from some poor shlub who neglected to set WEP or WPA security, then shame on you! But most places simply do not have WiFi thats free and open to use. I live in Maryland and the only place that has it reliably is McDonalds, and it costs per hour. EVDO works simply everywhere, even in my basement where I dont get cell service."3. Integrated book purchasing system locking you to a provider "Yeah, this proves your NOT an owner. You can buy from MOBI or any other dealer that supports sending the unencrypted-non-DRM books thru amazon. MOBI supports this directly and even flags books that are NOT kindle compatable. Also you can convert into MOBI format (which the kindle will read just fine) about anything you desire and USB it in or have amazon send it in."4. Difficult/impossible to read non-DRM or free books (Project Gutenberg) I already own in text and PDF formats "Again, get a FREE copy of MOBI creator and change the format to MOBI, upload and read. Of course you know that all the other ebook readers out there have the same issues, and some are actually more limiting than the kindle is due to NO MOBI support at all."I mean, really, are you guys joking? Other than the e-ink display, this thing looks like technology that has already failed in the marketplace. Twice. "Failed is a hard term when the numbers dont support it. If a product is truly failing, the price would come down, not up."Look, if I'm going to pay $400 for it, I should never have to pay for it ever again. If it's tied to a service, then I have to pay for that service. Note that there's nothing wrong with *including* a service. But if I MUST use that service to make the thing work, then it fails."at $399, the kindle is smack in the middle of ebook reader prices, which go as high as $799. To make an analogy for you: If you buy a bookshelf lined with gold that cost you $399, you would expect your local bookstore to keep it filled with your choices for free because you own the bookshelf. Thats how poor it sounds when you demand free books for life."On the other hand, if this was:a) Color (E-Ink technology has not advanced this far yet)b) WiFi (Lock out more than half the US from using it)c) Using standard technologies like a web browser (Firefox, upgradeable too) (Buy a laptop already, this is an EBOOK reader)d) Capable of reading common formats (PDF) without paying a dime (It is)e) $400 (Sure, amazon can raise the price if you like)"Then I'd absolutely buy one right now. No question about it. ""So, Amazon, call us back when you make these adjustments. Because that's the minimum feature set required to make such a device worth owning."They called me, and now I have a kindle thats worth owning :)UPDATE TO REVIEW:I wanted to take a moment from my normal diatribe to update my review a bit. First, the negative.....THE CASE BLOWS. Im not kidding, it really does. While it is a top quality case, soft lining, sttched soft leather and good quality overall, the design of the case is what gets me. The Kindle is in no way clipped or locked into the case, it is basicly free-floating. There are no clips, bands or any sort of attachment on the outer edge, only on the 'spine' and thus if not secured with the elastic baqnd, your kindle will fall out of the case and smash on the floor. This is very important when handing your kindle to non-owners (the 'OOH, can i see that' crowd) whom have no idea about the case design and fully expect it to be mounted inside.SOLUTION: Velcro to the rescue ! I placed a velcro dot on the back of the kindle, and the matching part on the inside of the case, problem solved :)I also wanted to mention that I have located a plethora of free software to convert all types of ebook formats to PDF, which I can then easily send to amazon to translate (for free of course) or just run through Mobi Creator. While Most of what I have is already in PDF format, I found it quite easy to move my collection over to my kindle.Update:This is my second review on the kindle, mind you this review is specific to how the kindle uses its battery power.The manual states you should put it in sleep mode when not using, as it will do that by itself after about 10 minutes of inactivity.
