This is a good thingand under normal circumstances the battery will last about a week with no wifi use.The problem comes in when you have a SD card inserted. I have a 2 gig card in place and a lot of things on it. It appears that the power consumption to run the SD card is never truly shut off, even in sleep mode so after but 2 days my Kindle is exhausted of power, even though it was not used at all.I highly suggest storing books on your SD card and using it to access them when you need, but move them to the kindle memory for reading, rather than just leave the card in place.

title: Love the Kindle, Photos and comments don't do it justice
body: I was very pleasantly suprised when it arrived. It was smaller than I imagined and looked dramatically better than the pictures do online. Not sure what it is but it looks a lot better in person. The hundreds of people critiquing it without using one are really flying blind, probably more so with this product that most others I have seen. My thoughts having used it for a day:- Ergonomics: Very good. I have clicked next page accidentally a couple of times, but the convenience of the buttons far out way that concern. I find it easier to use with the cover on and the cover folded over the back. I hold it with my left hand towards the bottom and my thumb resting in the empty space with easy access to the next page button. I tried the right hand but because it is the thinner side it feels heavier overall in that hand. The scrollwheel works really well and I find very easy to use (the silver looking marker is very cool). Most menu choices respond quickly, occasionally there is a delay depending on the type of command chosen. The keyboard so far has been easy to use. Have to get used to the space bar being on the left, but again, not something i see as an issue as i am already typing very quickly on it. Screen is great, very easy to read and very crisp. I don't turn it off, i put it into sleep mode which brings up a screen saver image (just for fun i guess). Going in and out of sleep mode is quick and easy, though a couple of times i tried to get out it took longer so i tried again, which ended up putting it back into sleep mode.- Cover: The cover is not the most secure fitting, but it seems to stay on well enough, with the strap on i don't see it falling out without some effort, but time will tell.- Shopping: No surprise here, shopping on the kindle is great, bought my first book and it downloaded in about 20 seconds, new york times in less than 10. browsing and searching are very easy and the shopping site is very responsive considering it is over the air. Tried a sample chapter and it worked exactly as advertised.- Wireless: Wireless signal is very strong at my house. At work it is much weaker but the store still works well, just a little slower. When putting the book into sleep mode it appears to turn off the wireless (good thing for battery). When unlocking it the kindle takes a minute or less to regain the signal.- Dictionary: Dictionary is great, you click on a row, select lookup and get the definitions. This feature is really helpful for me. It does take a few seconds for the lookup to finish, but still much faster than a paper dictionary. The summary definitions it gives are sufficient to get the definition, but you can click through for more detail. No way to lookup a word outside of selection a row (fine for me).- Home page: content is listed in order that it was touched, you can change the order though. There is no grouping options other than to show only books or only periodicals. Shows you a progress bar under each book on the home page (nice touch).- Content management: adding/removing is done on a separate screen. When i bought a book my wife (who also got one) was immediately able to see the book under her content management page and it said `amazon' under it instead of `kindle'. She can select the book and download it to her kindle. You delete books by checking them off and then choosing remove selected from the menu.- Bookmarks: Very easy to use, though it does add an entry to your clippings file which shows up on your home page. I find that a bit annoying as i just want it to store the bookmark, i don't need a separate record of it outside of the book.- Web browser: very rudimentary but i was able to go to some sites, definitely much faster than my iphone, but not nearly as usable.- Amazon nownow: this is very cool, i have sent some very random questions and have gotten back multiple answers for each: who won the dallas game last night, need a good Italian restaurant in `my town', who is the leader of Uzbekistan. They were all answered accurately and with a lot of supporting detail within several minutes.- Newspaper: subscribed to the new york times. It is very simple and stripped down. This works well for me as i don't have time to read the whole paper anyway. There are some articles missing but overall fine for me. It breaks it up into the usual sections with about 5 article headings that include a short summary, the rest of the articles are just the heading, you have to click through for more detail. It doesn't remember if you read an article or not, links look the same in either case. I like it and will use it, but i think the cost is too high for what you get, 5-7 dollars a month would be better. Other important point on periodicals, you cannot share them as you can books. If my wife wants to read the paper on her kindle she has to buy or subscribe herself. I think this is a major issue with periodicals if the expectation is that a family would own more than one kindle. Who would every by two newspapers for a household? Why would i do it on the kindle then?I am very happy with it so far, will see how it goes as time goes on. Hope this helps anyone considering. If you are on the fence and not sure if you will like it or if it will work out i am pretty sure it will.So far i have shown it to several people and they are all very impressed and very surprised by how much better it is than it appears on the web.Peter

title: The Ipod Nano is blasphemous!!!
body: Gone forever are the days when a family can sit half comatose in their living room watching a big screen television! Now, thanks to the Ipod Nano, we can get our television shows on our own handheld Nano and not only mentally and emotionally separate ourselves, but physically too. Damn, you Apple ("I love you Apple, really.")! Whew!!! Now, that I have gotten that out of my system, (well, having read many reviews, this does seem like the place to post a "review" of a product I've never owned or even used for reasons that have nothing to do with the quality of the product itself and serve only the purpose of showing how smart I am) onto the Kindle (which I do own).First, I think it looks pretty cool. The screen is amazing and the wireless capability works great. I don't think the cover looks cheap at all. I do wonder why they chose to design it the way they did. It works. It does hold the Kindle in place. I think the problem for most is that it doesn't seem like it will. It doesn't leave you feeling confident that your Kindle won't fall out. I think that will just be a mental hurdle to overcome because it does, in fact, work (and in future versions, hopefully they'll create a cover that leaves people feeling more secure). But really - it works!!!The Kindle is a dream to read in and out of the cover. Here's one of the things that I love that I could never do with a paperback or in most cases, even a hardcover. I can sit in my lounge chair with my feet up on the ottoman (legs bent) and I can gently lay the Kindle right on my lap (propped up by my legs) and read. Don't even need to hold it. With paperbacks, you have to hold it open. With hardcover books, can get the page to stay open, but often it's not comfortable because of the size of the book. Sometimes books are so large (like Harry Potter - which admittedly is not available on e-book, but from what I've heard it's because of the author, not Amazon) that it's a physical feat just to hold the book for a couple of hours at a time. With the Kindle, I just gently place the 10 oz. unit on my lap and read, only touching the Kindle to press the next page button.I'm not claiming that having to hold a paperback or hard cover book is some great travesty, or that Amazon has relieved me of some perilous burden of life. Point is this product improves my experience of reading, and ultimately, despite what those who so vigorously attack it say, the Kindle does what it does and it does it pretty well.It allows you to read an e-material comfortably and easily. ("My God Bezos, what have you done! You should be ashamed of yourself"). If that's what you are looking for, then give it a shot.And guess what?If you don't like it, you can send it back.***UPDATE***It's been a couple of more weeks of use and wanted to give an update.1.) I think the cover is fantastic. The Kindle doesn't slip out. It makes transport of the Kindle very easy and secure. Also makes reading very comfortable.2.) No issue at all with the buttons. I've read it in bed, at my desk, on a couch, and it is much more comfortable and easy to read (for me) than a book.3.) Power issue. I am having a battery issue. With wireless off, Kindle is only holding a charge for 2 to 3 days. Called customer service. They were great. They "pinged" my device, took off some type of log files, contacted me a day or so later and said there is a problem with my device. They are sending another one (which will arrive who knows when), but I can keep the one I have until it arrives. The only inconvenience is having to charge a little more often. No biggee. Very pleased with their response.4.) Have asked a couple of questions using Now Now. Actually have received different answers from the 3 people on one question. In fairness the question doesn't seem to have a consistent answer anywhere I go. ("Are w and y vowels? Some people I know were taught that both are, some taught only y and some taught that neither are. Thought I'd throw the question at the Kindle team to see what they came up with. 2 of 3 said both are. 1 said only y. Apparently, it's a mystery of the universe).5.) I think the "back" button was a great addition. I haven't browsed the web on the Kindle very much, but sometimes navigating the Kindle to go from point A to point B seems like it can take more steps than it should. With the "back" button though, you just jump back to where you want to go. For example, if I'm reading a book and then go to a newspaper. To get back to the book would entail going to the Home menu, scrolling down to the book selection, clicking the scroll button to take you back to the book. With the "back" button, just click twice fast and you're back. Really I'm not that lazy, i.e. either route is not that difficult. Just commenting that I think the addition of that button was a good move and it has some function beyond browsing.6.) The one thing I haven't used yet is to have my own documents sent to the device. I keep meaning to email support or even ask Now Now. I want to know what Amazon does with the documents that you email with them. Not the conversion that they do, but what happens to the original file I sent them after they've converted it for me. Do they keep it? Do they trash it? Clearly, it would be silly to send them something highly confidential. Still, I'd like to know what happens to my files.

title: Why I love my Kindle
body: Top 7 things I love about my Kindle:1 - I can now read on the treadmill, elliptical and bike easily when at the gym. With the adjustable font size, large page turn button on the right and no need to turn a physical page I can even read while running on the treadmill not just walking. I just tap the page turn button which given it's size is an easy mark to hit and off I go. Some people have complained about the page turn buttons being too big and easy to hit, I'm glad I bought this model b/c if the next one has smaller buttons to hit it might affect my ability to use this wonderful device while on the treadmill. My workouts go by so quickly if I'm reading a good book that I sometimes run for longer than an hour before I realize I've gone over my target time.2 - Instant access to the book you want to read. I find that I read books that are in a series so being able to have the next book download immediately after you finish the previous one is the coolest thing ever. No more waiting for the next book to arrive because the first one was so good you finished it in one day you can just keep going and going without missing a beat. The download speed is so fast it's beyond description, I've literally never had to wait regardless of book size. Usually by the time I can navigate back to my home page the book is there so it downloads faster than I can get to it.3 - Multiple books in one small neat little package (and it keeps track of my place in each book!). I love to read and my interests are all over the place so I tend to read different types of books at the same time. Fantasy, History, Finance/Investing, Fluff, you name it so being able to pick what I feel like reading at that moment is priceless especially when traveling. Ever been stuck on a long plane ride with a book you just couldn't stand to read at that time and wish you had something else, the kindle solves that problem. It's a must have for anyone who travels by air on a regular basis.4 - Adjustable font size. I know I briefly mentioned this in my top one reason but it deserves it's own mention. I have no serious vision problems so before I got the kindle I thought it was a neat feature that I would never use. Not true, I change my font size around to suit my situation, I use the standard size 3 font for most things but when reading in bed without glasses or contacts I find that if I bump it up to size 4 it's more comfortable to see. I also bump it up to size 6 when working out b/c this makes it easier to focus on the words while moving. This has allowed me to read my kindle while it's resting on the arm of my couch or on the counter, etc. because I can adjsut the font to make it easy to see no matter the situation.5 - Book Samples - This is to books what the itunes sample is to music. Publishers don't realize how much this could help (or I suppose in some cases hurt if the book wasn't well written) the sale of a book. I can't even count the number of books that I have gotten hooked on and bought that I would have otherwise never purchased or tried. The sample is free and is usually the first chapter although one book gave me four chapters, which I read and of course by that point I had no choice but to buy it, I was already four chapters in at that point. With a sample there is no risk of shelling out $10 to $20 on something you might not want to finish once you start reading.6 - Battery life is amazing. I bought the kindle for the sole purpose of book reader, I don't want music, web browsing (other than the book store) color, backlighting or any other features that I have on one of my fifteen other gadgets. Just like I wouldn't expect my paper books to function as anything other than a book, that's how I want my kindle to function. With that said I only turn the wireless access on when I want to browse for and download a new book so my battery lasts a really long time. I spent one entire weekend reading a really good book and several nights after for a total of 30 or more hours of reading and still didn't come close to killing the battery and that includes leaving it on one whole night b/c I fell asleep. I like being able to go away for a few days and not have to pack yet another charger to plug in somewhere.7 - Book Price. I always buy my books, I never use the library and I rarely borrow books from others b/c I always forget to return them (I've been told that's called stealing). I also never really share the books I read with anyone else (few of my friends like what I read, I'm not selfish I swear). That being said everything that I have wanted to read that has been available for the kindle has been cheaper in some cases by more than half. You would think that I could cut back on my book budget but I just buy more books now.I know a lot of reviews talk about the page turn buttons, the appearance, the file organization, etc. These things had me a little hesitant to be an early adopter but I'm so glad I did. I have read more since I got my kindle than I did before and it has been great. I rarely turn the tv on anymore because the kindle has made reading any and everywhere so much easier. Try holding a real book with a dog on your lap while drinking a cup of coffee and see if you are even able to turn the page, it's not possible but it is with the kindle.

title: Two Fires Make MANY Folks HAPPY!!!!!
body: I've been working steadily with two of these for almost 3 months now. One is for use by my handicapped stepdaughter as her music source and the other is for me to use as a mobile workstation - I'm a database administrator. These are the first "smart", touchscreen devices I've ever had or used and I'm a 64 year old Geezer who has rarely texted, so ti's been quite a learning experience, but I must say that this new 7in. Fire tablet is all I could have hoped for. Below are the details of how I set up each of these Fires.****** For Music ******* My stepdaughter is 35, somewhat autistic, has OK, but not good fine motor skills, and lives in a group home. She HAS to have music to listen to constantly. She lugs around a boombox and cases full of CDs, which get dirty and/or scratched and then she gets upset that her songs won't play right. We were looking at how to switch her over to an MP3 player or some other digital format, but the iPods, MP3 players, smartphones, etc. were all too small for her to manipulate. We didn't even consider a larger tablet, which she could have controlled, because they are too expensive and she tends to bump and drop things a lot.When this new 7in. Fire tablet came out in September, especially the $99 Kid's version with the "no questions" 2 year warranty, we thought this was our chance. We have a Premium Spotify account and created Playlist folders and Playlists of all her favorite artists and albums. The beauty of Spotify is that, with the Premium account, you can download up to 3,333 songs to your device for playing when you don’t have WiFi, which is her case frequently. We added a 32GB SD card to her Fire and discovered that you have to install the Spotify app AFTER you install the SD card or it will download music to the meager 4 or 5GB of usable internal storage instead of to the add-on SD card.We added the I Heart Radio app so she could also stream some of her local stations and she went crazy adding a bunch more from around the country. We also have an Amazon Prime account and added some Prime Music playlists for the few albums that aren’t on Spotify. We, like most other folks, can’t get Amazon Music to download to the add-on SD card, even though I applied the same tweak (see below for my “techie” Fire setup) that worked on my Fire. With the addition of a set of Bluetooth headphones and a Bluetooth speaker, she’s set up to play music all the time. The only cords she needs are those OTG ones for charging. I put small paint dots on the USB connectors, the charger, and her Fire so she could easily align and plug them in the right way without frustration. Did the same thing for mine since I seem to always try it the wrong way first. Very simple convenience.I did some day-long power tests last week and found that the fully-charged Fire will last about 9 hours constantly streaming Spotify (Shuffle All Songs), but with very little display use - always “sleeping”. Power only lasts 5 or 6 hours with lots of busy display. YMMV.Another beauty of the shared Spotify account is that she can email me from her workshop and request a new artist or album and I can pretty quickly add it from my PC. (No way she could use the Fire's keyboard to type a search in.) The new stuff immediately shows up on her Playlists. WE ARE ALL LOVING THIS!****** For Techie Use *******I administer about 30 Oracle databases on about 12 Linux and HPUX servers, so I need to have ssh terminal sessions and use an sftp-based file manager. The apps I got for those. as well as a few others are:Cisco AnyConnect to support VPN to my organization. Works GREAT.vSSH for ssh terminal sessions. Very nice. I can switch from session to session easily.ES File Explorer for local files and remote file management over sftp. I’m still learning all the ins and outs of this app. It’s VERY full-featured. Nice file editors.Bookmark Folder to manage all the work-related, technical, and personal bookmarks for the Silk browser. Really simple and flexible - perfect for me.To be able to get Android apps that aren’t in the Amazon store, I followed the directions herehttp://www.howtogeek.com/232726/how-to-install-the-google-play-store-on-your-amazon-fire-tablet/Now I can find and install apps from the Google Play store. It also added a choice to Settings to allow you to choose to download Amazon music to the add-on SD card. The same procedure didn’t work on my stepdaughter’s Fire, though. Don’t know why. I could have done something else, since I tried quite a few tricks I found online.WiFi availability when I’m out and about can be a problem, but I have Time Warner Cable at home and they have public WiFi hotspots all over Austin, where I live. Of course using WiFi at a car dealership while I’m waiting on maintenance is nothing new, but standing on a random street corner checking in at the office is. TWC hotspots are nice. This app helps find those and others Wi-fi finder.Yesterday I got this for Christmas "QQ-Tech Mini Bluetooth Keyboard W Touchpad for Android OS Windows" and am very happy with it so far. Not as good as a full sized keyboard and mouse, but a heck of a lot better than the Fires keyboard.Hope this hodgepodge has been helpful.

title: Amazing product for so little cash
body: For $50, I wasn't expecting much at all, what can you really expect to get for $50 from a tablet? Well, apparently quite a lot. Amazon is practically giving them away, and it's a steal for any purchaser, but it's for a reason. This is pretty much a mobile Amazon Store that you can take with you anywhere. It also has built in direct connects to things like Audible and HBO Now. Having owned this for a few months, I would recommend it to anyone needing a wifi Tablet that doesn't want to spend a lot of money.Pros:*The first thing the device does is update to the latest Firmware, I suspect this is actually them attaching your account to the device because when it comes up, it has your account loaded with your name and all your media (see below)*Small, lightweight tablet with great picture and an ok speaker. I tend to use it with headphones at work anyway.*The 7" size really is great. I also have a 10" tablet that feels bulky compared to this.*It's a speedy response to about everything I want.*Instant access to Amazon Prime Music and any media you have on Amazon (movies, kindle books, audible books, etc) at the touch of a button*Easily able to setup email boxes on the device*Amazing battery life. I use it to listen to music or books at work and I can go 2-3 days between charging it*Simple interface that mimics most Android smartphones*It feels sturdy in my hand, but not too weighty or bulky.The MEH portion: (things about it I don't like or hate, but other people may have opinions about)*The screen is very glossy, so reading a book on it can be difficult if the lighting is reflected.*The screen can get dirty quick. I'm a photographer, so I take a lint free cloth to it and it takes about 20 seconds to get clean again*It really is just a mobile Amazon store wrapped up in a pretty package, purchasing anything from Amazon is a snap!*The Unlock screen/screensaver is a crummy commercial based on your Amazon utilization. Most the time it's irrelevant products, but there have been a few that caught my eye, and I've made 2 purchases from the advertising.*Volume buttonsare 'gravity based'. No matter which way you hold the tablet 'down' or 'left' is always volume down and the opposite is volume up. It's strange at first, but quickly learned.*Wifi only connection to the internetCons:*The screen can be slightly unresponsive at times. I find when the tablet is laying flat, the very corners of the screens don't like to register taps. This can be a problem when trying to advance a song or pause a song/movie. I simply pick it up and it works fine.*It is a $50 mobile Amazon store. It is sooooo easy to buy things because everything is attached to your Amazon account and they have your purchasing info on file. Want to try Audible.com? Press one button and it's yours. No filling out anything, you literally press one button and you're on a free month trial. It's so easy to buy things I sometimes wonder if I've bought something without knowing it and will be charged later.*Locked into the Amazon App store. Yes, you can get Google play on the device really easy, but many non-techy people would struggle with this task. (see link below)*You will need wifi where ever you are in order to access online libraries. You can download to the device itself but...You will only have 5 gigs of space left of the 8 gigs because of the OS. You can put in up to 16 gigs of extra memory but you can't download any Amazon based library items to the external memory (no movies, books, music, etc downloaded directly from Amazon can be put on the external card).All in all, if you want a tablet, buy this one NOW. It is worth every penny and works great. Some others have complained about power/charging issues, dead pixels, etc, and I've experienced none of the bad things they have experienced. I've not had to contact Amazon about any issues with the device at all.I have a 10" tablet I bought last year for 4 times the amount I paid for this one that pales in comparison to the Fire tablet. I was hesitant to get it because I already had a tablet, but it was only $50, so I tried it. My old tablet is no collecting dust because this one is so much better. Electronically, the two tablets are almost exactly comparable, but the bigger screen on my old device makes it laggy in comparison, and having access to all my movies, music and books immediately through wifi makes it downright awesome. Everywhere I go there is wifi, so it's not an issue. Maybe on a long drive or flight it could be an issue, but I have already downloaded a few things to the small space allowable for downloading and it's just not an issue for me.The link for adding Google Play to the device (and to turn off the ad driven screensaver):https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j-glxYTY8O8&index=1&list=WLSeriously, if you want a tablet and have Amazon Prime, there's no reason NOT to buy this tablet. If you don't have Amazon Prime, well, I don't know how it works because I have Amazon Prime. I believe it gives like a free month or something of Amazon Prime when you buy it, so it may be worth it for that.

title: Every Avid Reader's New Found Treasure...Priceless!!!!
body: I originally purchased the Kindle for my husband as a unique view on the traditional 1st Anniversary gift (something paper). He travels a lot and this seemed like a perfect thing for him to take on the road with him. As it happens, my "Perfect" gift was on the way to him when I received mine from him....A KINDLE!!! Now each of us have our own separate Amazon accounts for purchasing items (mostly for Christmas shopping, etc) so neither of us realized that we had each gotten one another the exact same thing. Now, I am writing this review to speak for both of us...neither could of asked for a better gift. My husband is a super "techie" and loves all new devices, etc. He owns everything that people stand in line for...the more hype, the more he has to have it. So, this was right up his ally, both to give AND to receive. He fell in love with it, though it took a couple of hours. Yes, the buttons can be troublesome at first as you get used to them and learn just how to hold your Kindle...but this is not some major flaw in the product. It takes adjusting to and learning to make it fit you hand...it does indeed become an extension of yourself as you are reading...you hold it in your palm which it is designed to comfortably fit and the buttons no longer present a problem once you adjust your self to it. But more importantly, you can read with it sitting in your lap and it is about a million times easier to sit and read than an actual book. I read like crazy. I am a writer, and all writers that I know have about 6 books going at a time. I have hard cover, soft cover, used and brand new..and I have yet to find a book that I could place in my lap and read without having to adjust it with great effort to prevent it from flipping pages and then having to take the time to go find my place again....and holding a hard cover book of any size in the palm of your hand is TIRING!! They are heavy, bulky, and generally uncomfortable. All of this is eliminated with the Kindle. Yes, you get yourself used to the "next page"/"previous page buttons and sometimes will accidentally go forward or back..but finding where you were is a breeze when you are going through that "adjustment" period...a couple of clicks of the buttons either way and there you are....nothing like having to flip back through a ton of actual pages. And being able to place it in the cover and throw it in my purse when I am traveling as opposed to having to pack an arm load of books for a trip is such a difference. Not only do I have every book that I am reading right there to decide between in one lightweight device, I also do not have to decide which book to carry on the plane, and don't weigh down my luggage anymore...which these days can cost a fortune! No more deciding between my books and extra shoes or my beauty products!! :) The Kindle has become a part of me, and I no longer go anywhere without it! My husband quite frequently uses the 10 cent downloading of Word documents, etc. So that he is not traveling with a briefcase full of the reports and project materials that he needs to read and research...and that often times become bulky and down right impossible to keep organized when you need to scatter through them while sitting on a plane traveling to find what you are looking for. And, best of all, both of our Kindles are registered together..so one purchase and we both have the book to read...we have created a library of books that we each can share and make selections from...even from multiple states apart. The very last point that I would like to make...we have moved four times over the course of our lives together and another move is eminent, seeing as how we are living in two completely different States as of now. The amount of clutter, weight, and overall tediousness of packing and repacking boxes upon boxes of old and new books is one of the single most tiresome parts of moving. There are books that we have read, but would never want to get rid of, there are those half read books, and then there are books that we have read, will never read again, but pack and move anyway because they are there on the shelves. No longer will this be the case. Yes, we have those priceless books that we will always have in our library...but the rest...we no longer have to trouble ourselves with. When every book you have altogether weighs less than the normal paperback...well, you just can't beat that! I want to buy one for every single book enthusiast that I know. And everyone that I meet, asks about it and has immediately wanted one. This is a definite winner. The complaints that are out there are from people that no understanding of the concept of this device. It is not meant to be a dvd player, or even a MP3 player, though it has that capability. It is meant to be a book....ALL books, in one easy to carry device. The extras are a bonus, but you don't buy a book in color unless you are under the age of 7 years old. I think the fact that the pages LOOK like pages is what makes it so remarkable. If you want to view pictures in color, watch a movie, or listen to music then use your iPod or get yourself a PDA...but if you want to READ a book, then purchase yourself a Kindle!! You will never regret it!! And it gets a whole lot more attention than my new iPhone!! :)

title: A Steal, With a Few Limitations
body: Admittedly, I can be cheap (frugal?) sometimes.That's why I bought this tablet to replace my (formerly) top of the line $230 Google Nexus 7 that I broke. Does this live up to those high standards? No. However, for $50, I am NOT complaining. I'll try to compare the two as I go on, to see if you think you should buy this or a more high end tablet.BUILD QUALITY: The Fire is built pretty well. The back is smooth plastic and it doesn't seem to scratch. The camera is recessed, which is pretty nice too. A small speaker in the corner rounds out the rear of the device. The front is okay looking as well. A camera placed in the center is the only obstruction on the front of the device. The screen takes up most of the front but there is a bit of bezel, more than the Nexus. Note that there is no auto brightness sensor. The glass does not feel quite like glass, but better than many budget phones I have tested. Overall, the build is nice and similar to the Nexus 7, but about double the thickness. Best of all, an SD card slot!!! I have 10 movies stored on my 64gb Samsung SD card and still have plenty of space on the devices hard drive. Some apps and music can also be stored on the hard drive as well.SCREEN: This tablet has an adequate 171 ppi. Looking at it alone, it looks good (but the brightness cannot go very high or low). However, this pales in comparison to my old tablet, with a 323 ppi. When you look at the Fire next to an iPad or Nexus, the difference is noticeable. However text is clear and easy to read, and movies show up pretty well (no HD).USER INTERFACE: The Fire uses a modified version of Google's Android operating system. Aside from menus, notification bars and the first home screen, the Fire is unique. Even on the home screen, it's different from Android: it scrolls up and down versus side to side. The rest of the interface is purely Amazon: you have your Video section, Music, Books, Games, etc. Be aware that only Amazon Video, Music, etc will show up on their respective screens. Netflix and Spotify will be in the Apps section, not the Video or Music section. Another thing to know is that you mainly access the Amazon App store. Most apps are on there, but I really miss the official YouTube app. You can side-load some apps off your computer, but the Google run apps require Google Play Services which I don't think you can download on this device (correct me if I'm wrong). All of the pre-installed apps work great, except the water app. Very basic. Just download the Weather Channel App.CAMERA: This is the worst part about this device. My phone has an average camera and I don't own a professional camera, so I used my Nexus's 8mp camera to take my nicer shots. That is out of the picture (pun intended) with the Fire. The back 2mp camera is horrible and takes grainy and underexposed shots. It's "best" in close up situations. The front camera is worse, VGA (!). Don't expect to take any Instagram ready selfies on this...stick to your phone.BROWSER: This uses Amazon's Silk browser. I hated Silk on the first gen Kindle Fire. But now, it's basically the mobile version of Chrome. I actually like some features more, like the side menu. The only improvement would be to allow switching from Private to regular...once you close a Private tab its closed, no switching back and forth.MUSIC: Both Amazon MP3 and Spotify work well on the device. Sound through headphones is great, but the mono speaker...not so much. My Nexus had great sound through dual Dolby speakers, just like the Fire HD 8 and 10. However, the speakers on this Fire suck. It sounds washed out, and some tones just don't register. I mean, it's okay for late night Beyonce jam sessions with friends or showing someone a short video, but don't expect to watch a movie with those speakers.GAMEPLAY: I don't play that many games, but the few I have work well. Minecraft is fast and smooth, but not as nice as on an iPad or my Nexus. Goat Simulator is also fast but glitchy (I think it's the game not the device). Simple games like Candy Crush or Where's my Mickey? show up well. However, the worst visuals I have had in a game was a slots game. Everything was stretched and the colors looked so faded (I played this same game on my Nexus 7 and it was fine).BATTERY: The battery charges fast enough, usually 3-4 hours from 10%. It's faster if you put the device in airplane mode. The battery life is okay. Some games like Minecraft and Goat Simulator drain the battery so fast, but I can watch a two hour movie in airplane mode and only go to 89%, from a full charge.SUMMARY: I firmly believe this is an amazing value. It cost about 80% less than my Nexus, and I think it does about 80% of the same tasks well. While it doesn't have super high end specs, it works well for the average consumer. If you have a nice phone and a computer, this is a good supplement. Where this lacks they excel. However if you intend for this to be a primary device (i.e. using as a computer) this is not a good choice. If you're on the fence, I say buy it and see how you like it. If you don't love it, return it and buy a device that would better suit your needs. But for me, it's perfect.

title: A wonderful transformative experience for readers.
body: I received my Kindle six weeks ago and admittedly loved it from the first moment. I wanted to let it sit for a while before writing a review and now that I have my finished reading my 5th Kindle book I feel ready to weigh in:Hots:1) Carry dozens of books in one sleek form factor.2) Buy books on impulse for a low price and get them immediately via wireless (download a whole book take about a minute in an area with decent signal strength). Wifi is promiscuous and grabs a connection without any configuration or user intervention all over town. I live in NYC and have successfully poached Wifi all over - in the train station, in the cafes, at my office desk, in the park etc... No WEP keys or any fiddling at all. I don't know how they do it.3) Screen is lovely. Everyone who sees it is appreciative of the paper white non reflective non backlit screen.4) Great selection of books - especially new books. I know that this is a work in progress. I went through my want list and more than half the titles were available on Kindle. It's a glass half full kind of thing. I also found that almost half the titles on my want list were not available - but I'm into all kinds of weird stuff. All in all I was ver impressed with the depth of the offerings.5) The ability to search the whole Kindle for any bit of text you think of then have pages of lovely little contextual text clippings to help you pick which of the hits you want to visit.6) The ability to surf the web via "Experimental" from the main menu. This is fantastic given the fact that the WiFi is so promiscuous. The downside is that the browser is somewhat primitive and doesn't support pop ups. Some sites don't work all that well - but many many work fine. it's a fine way to get the news with your morning coffee if you opt to not pay to subscribe to a real newspaper.7) The social and cultural factor. People are fascinated with the Kindle. Reading in public often sparks a conversation. You may view this as a positive or a negative - but there's no denying that the Kindle has magnetism.8) The experience of reading - very similar to a book. Once you've selected your preferred type size you just flip pages and go. Simple, intuitive, familiar. Nice.Nots:1) No page numbers! Relative location numbers are necessary because the number of "pages" a book on Kindle has is dependent on the type size you pick. Without absolute page numbers around you don't know where to go when a figure in a book suggests you "see page 189". The lack of absolute page numbers makes indexes useless. If someone did the work to make each index item a live link that would be fine. The reality is that all the Kindle books I've seen so far have had their indexes rendered useless.2) All books look the same on Kindle. There's only one type face and only one layout. The subtle artistry of font selection and book layout is lost. You still get the cover, but with Kindle you usually only see the cover once - unlike with a real book where you see it every time you touch the book - subtly influencing your experience.3) The page advance buttons are large and well placed, but these positive attributes makes it easy to hit buttons by accident. I don't know how they will resolve this. You don't want to make it hard to advance pages. The screen flashes black for an instant when changing the displayed content.4) The Kindle screen is greyscale with a small number of available shades. This means that pictures don't look great. Books with tables or figures suffer by having the graphic elements presented in small greyscale unzoomable images. For example I have workout books on Kindle and the exercise regimens are very hard to read because they are so tiny to fit onto the small screen.5) Awkward tiny keyboard - this is true of virtually all portable devices.6) There's no cursor or mouse pointer on the main screen of Kindle (a consequence of the E-ink screen technology). You can only select but using the click wheel and associated little selection square on the right hand edge. It's more fiddly than a real cursor. I'd hope they fix this in future versions. That being said, the selection method works pretty well in practice and doesn't interfere too often.All in all I feel the Kindle is an AMAZING first effort. The strengths far outweigh the weaknesses. Having the Kindle transforms your relationship with books both for better and for worse. You can get books immediately. Carry dozens without thinking about it. You can easily search among books and hop, skip and jump. Make bookmarks, grab clippings etc... You can surf the web and grab a limited list of periodicals. My main concerns are the longevity of the device (I give my rough treatment) and the long term issue of the proprietary book format. I have faith in Amazon, however - and believe they will honor the format and make compatibility going forward a promise they will keep. Bravo - Amazon has transformed book retailing again - and changed the medium itself; expanding its possibilities.

title: This is easily the BEST tablet you can buy for under $100.
body: As it would be very difficult to review this product without keeping in mind the price - $49.99. With that said, value plays a HUGE part in reviewing this tablet. But even at $100 this would perhaps still be a 5 star item but I would never know that because I would not own one. So for me, price was the biggest factor in purchasing the 2015 Fire 7". So I will simply list out my opinions on each of the categories I felt important to list.- *PRICE* - If this acted like a typical $50 tablet (and my children have had a few) it would not matter how cheap this was because no one would take it seriously. But as an adult using this it has the feel of the "real deal" and not some knock off garbage made by someone who doesn't care if it lasts because they are simply in it for a quick buck. Amazon is in it for the long haul with this tablet and it shows. HINT*** this tablet is supposed to go on sale on Nov. 26th for the extremely low price of $35! If it is worth is at $50 (and it most certainly is) then it is DEFINITELY worth it at $35. Get one - or two - or five or six. At this price it will beat the "Buy 5 get the 6th free" deal they are running now by $50 and you don't have to buy 5 to get a discount.- Features - There is nothing missing from what I would want in a tablet. With that said, I like the new OS and the way things are laid out. It's extremely easy to find settings and make whatever changes I wanted to make and if I couldn't figure it out in 1 minute or less...just remember, Google is your friend, people. Front facing camera is all I cared about but this has a rear facing as well so that's a plus. It also has an expandable memory slot that I will be using in the future and this was a clincher for me since the 8 GB of internal memory would be frustrating if it were not expandable. The best way to explain how each feature is compared to a tablet 3-5 times the price would be to think of every aspect of it (cameras, speed, screen quality, etc.) and assume it will be around 20% less nice than the boutique ones.- Build Quality - Usually with cheap tablets the screen is the the first place to suffer. The screen is solid and responsive and I had any complaint whatsoever I would say that it is a fingerprint magnet. Comparing this to a higher quality item I would say it just plain old gets dirty faster and is harder to clean. The finish is obviously not high quality but not a show stopper, just worth mentioning since I noticed it.- Apps - I really like Amazon's app store and when comparing it to Google Play I would say it has 80-90% of the apps that you could find on Play. This doesn't bother me and loading apps from Google is possible if you really just HAVE to have an app that's only available there. So far I have not had the need. I ESPECIALLY like the "Actually Free" selection of apps. Actually Free means not only is the app free to download but there is aren't supposed to be in-app purchases. The worst thing that you might have to deal with is ads popping up between levels on games or at certain intervals in other apps. Not at all enough to bother me as you can't blame them for needing to make money somehow.- Size - I guess size is size when you are getting a 7" but since most of my experience prior to this was on the Kindle Fire HD 8.9 2nd Generation, this thing felt like a phone when I first started using it. Now that I'm used to it I would still prefer an 8" I think but I bet my wife would not agree. Because it's smaller when using the Kindle book app in landscape view it's doesn't "split" the page like you were reading an open book with 2 pages and a center line like the 8.9 inch did (I don't know if they just did this on the smaller versions or if they did away with it altogether for sure) and I REALLY liked that it did that. So that is one thing that bugs me just a tad.- Other thoughts - I like that the accessories for it are constantly growing. I think a case is necessary with a tablet but supposedly these are made to withstand a pretty good fall. But without a case it's not the best for viewing a movie. Without Amazon Prime I probably wouldn't own one of these either, so get Prime to fully enjoy it. The volume is pretty quiet and not very rich and may possibly be this tablets worst feature, in my opinion. But it's workable as long as there is not a lot of noise wherever you happen to be using it. The Fire Stick is also supposed to go on sale on Nov. 26th for $25. So, for $60 you can get a tablet and a fire stick which pretty much turns your TV into a smart TV. Coupled with the tablet you can do all sorts of neat things like screen cast or mirror and control videos on the TV while you do other stuff on your tablet.So, if you are on the fence because you are not sure if the build quality will be up to par, stop worrying. But if you are spoiled with a high quality tablet already and cannot see yourself downgrading then I guess you will just have to go with the HD 8 or 10 and I betcha you will be very pleased.
